They discuss the hypothesis that Bradwood was playing chess against himself when he was killed. Professor Yardley proposes two unverified theories: either the killer, Crocetta, was the expected visitor whom Bradwood thought was a friend, or there were two separate visitors that night—one expected and one being the enemy, Crocetta. Ellery rejects the idea that there was only one visitor, arguing that the truth lies in the specific arrangement of the chess pieces and the psychology of skilled players.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Ellery analyzes a checkers game found in Breda's study to determine the circumstances of Breda's murder. While Stolings previously observed Breda playing alone, Ellery argues that the board's state proves Breda was playing against a real opponent. The game was highly unbalanced, with Black holding a dominant advantage of two kings and two pieces against Red's two weak pawns. Ellery concludes that a master player like Breda would never practice an intentionally lopsided game, implying the presence of a visitor.

By examining the board, Ellery determines Breda was playing as Black and sitting in the chair closest to the desk. A red pawn had reached the black king's line but had not been crowned. This missing red pawn was found near Breda's body at the totem pole, and a red mark was found on Breda's palm, indicating he was holding the piece to crown the opponent's pawn when he was interrupted.

Ellery connects this to the crime scene, noting a bloodstain on the carpet behind Breda's chair. He posits that Breda was attacked from the front while attempting to crown the pawn, causing him to fall backward. This suggests the murderer was Breda's opponent. Ellery refutes Dr. Yadley's suggestion that an accomplice or Crosetti attacked Breda from behind while an innocent visitor played chess; Ellery argues that Crosetti would not have attacked in front of a witness, nor would he have left a witness unharmed. Therefore, the murderer must have been the person playing the game with Breda.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Ellery Quine, Professor Yadley, and Yale discuss the murder of Brad. Ellery deduces that the killer, Crosetti, must have been someone Brad knew well, regardless of any disguise used, because Brad would not have invited a complete stranger into his unprotected home to play chess. Based on this, Ellery realizes that Crosetti is not actually a cripple; rather, he used a childhood disability as a consistent physical feature to mislead the police, abandoning the limp once he sensed danger.

Ellery then reviews the sequence of crimes, noting that the imposter Crocetta killed the first victim, Clinton, due to a family feud, mistakenly believing he was killing Andrew. After discovering a note from Bradley indicating that Van (Tervil) was still alive, Crocetta's motive shifted toward finding Van to complete his revenge. Ellery realizes that Croasdale has been waiting for the opportunity to find Van through Megalas. He concludes that Croasdale intends to kill Megalas once he obtains this information.

Realizing the imminent danger, Ellery urgently sends a telegram to the Sheriff of Aroon, West Virginia, instructing him to organize a force to guard Old Pete's shack and keep Old Pete safe until Ellery arrives. He also requests that the sheriff report Croasdale's return to Crummett. Ellery attempts to contact Officer Vaughn to coordinate further, but learns that Vaughn and District Attorney Eisam have already driven off in a car after receiving a telegram.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Following a phone call, Ellery Quine decides he must travel to West Virginia to prevent a fourth murder. He believes the killer, Crocker, is heading toward the mountains to find a hiding place and has likely already killed Meagal to obtain information. Because a severe storm prevents him from hiring a plane through Minola Airport, Ellery decides to drive his old Duesenberg sports car. He instructs Professor Yadley to stay behind and attempt to contact Vaughn and Eisam.

Ellery begins his journey at one o'clock, driving through New York City, the Holland Tunnel, and various towns in New Jersey and Pennsylvania. Despite the heavy rain and poor road conditions, he drives at high speeds, eventually reaching Harrisburg and later Hollidaysburg. After a night of rest at a hotel, Ellery continues his drive through Pittsburgh. By 11:30 a.m. the following day, he arrives in Aroo Village, West Virginia.

Upon arriving at the town hall, Ellery enters Judge Lutten's empty office. A man in a blue gingham shirt follows him and informs him that the judge is not there. Ellery searches the judge's desk and discovers a yellow telegram addressed to Sheriff Redden in Aroo. The telegram instructs the sheriff to assemble a police force to guard "Old Pete's shack" and to report Crosetto's return to Krutem. It further instructs that if the situation at the shack has already occurred, the police should find Crosetto's tracks without disturbing the scene.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Ellery Queen discovers that his telegram to Sheriff Redden was never delivered because the sheriff and Mayor Matt Hollis left for a week-long fishing trip in the Ohio River. The caretaker of the office had signed and shelved the telegram. Driven by fear, Ellery drives his Duesenberg to a remote location, carrying a loaded semi-automatic pistol. He ascends a path toward a clearing, noting the silence of the forest. Upon reaching a fence, he observes footprints in the damp earth and finds that a door is slightly ajar. He enters the building, encountering a "terrible sight" inside, and closes the heavy door behind him.

Returning to the town hall, Ellery encounters a doorman who provides him with a note left by a tall, gangly man described as looking like "old Abe Lincoln." The note, signed by Yadley, explains that Yadley traveled to the area via private plane to find Vane and Isam. Yadley reports seeing a limping man, presumably Crozack, who rented a car at Yellow Bay and traveled to a hotel in Stowellville, Ohio. Yadley instructs Ellery to go to Stowellville immediately. Ellery realizes the note was left by Yadley, not Abraham Lincoln, and instructs the doorman to give the note to incoming officials from New York, specifically Eames and Vance. After giving the doorman a hundred-dollar bill, Ellery speeds away.

Meanwhile, Officer Wain and District Attorney Esam arrive in Bradwood with a representative from the U.S. Attorney's office, escorting Percy and Elizabeth Rein. After receiving a report from Professor Adler, the officials realize they must pursue the suspects.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
After receiving news that Professor Yadley had flown southwest from Minola's airport, Vain, Easom, and Escham travel to Arroyo. Upon arrival, a caretaker provides them with a note from Yadley, which is accompanied by a hasty message from Ellery Quin. The notes indicate that Yadley visited a messy shack and warns that there are mysterious footprints near the shed.

Vain and Easom proceed to the shack, noting two sets of footprints made by the same shoes: one set leading from the fence to the door and another forming an arc. Inside, they discover the headless body of the schoolmaster, Andrew Tewell, hanging from a log like a trophy. The body is dressed in fake mountaineer clothing, with its legs nailed together. The scene is described as a slaughterhouse, with a large capital "T"—the mark of Crosetto's revenge—written in blood on a table.

Vain discovers a bloodstained axe used to sever the head, a pair of shears, and a discarded, cut roll of bandage. He also finds an almost empty, unlabeled blue glass bottle of iodine that had been knocked over. Vain initially theorizes that the killer, Crosetti, injured his wrist and replaced a bandage in haste. However, Escham suggests that the wrist wound might actually belong to the victim, Ran, rather than the killer. To resolve this, Vain removes the nails and clothing from the headless corpse to examine the body for wounds.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
After discovering that Croce was the one injured by nail holes in the palms and feet, Wain and Escham return to Arroyo. Wain contacts District Attorney Krümmit, who promises to keep their unauthorized handling of a corpse secret and arranges for Captain Pickert and a coroner to arrive. The pair then travels toward Stowberville, Ohio.

On Tuesday, a multi-state pursuit involving cars, trains, and planes begins. Ellery Queen arrives in Stowberville and receives a note from Yardeley at the Ford Stowberville Hotel. The note reveals that a man matching a specific description checked in at midnight and left by taxi at 7:30 a.m. heading for Chinsville; the man had a bandage on his wrist and had abandoned a limp. Ellery identifies this man as the killer and contacts the Chief of Police in Cheswick, using the alias Ellery Quincannon to request assistance.

Ellery travels to the Clayton Inn in Cheswick, where Police Chief Hardy informs him that Yardeley’s contact reported the suspect changed his route to Columbus instead of Chinsville. At the Seneca Hotel in Columbus, Ellery finds another note from Yardeley stating that the suspect, referred to as "K," took a 1:00 p.m. train to Indianapolis. Yardeley intends to catch a plane to intercept him. After learning that a plane has already been rented for him at the airport, Ellery flies toward Indianapolis. Meanwhile, Wain and Escham arrive in Columbus and fly to Indianapolis via a plane arranged from Chillicothe. As they travel, Ellery reflects on the murder of Andrew Van, feeling confident in his progress.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Ellery Queen calculates that Professor Yadley might catch the fugitive, Crocker, in Indianapolis. However, upon arriving at the Indianapolis airport at 8:30, Ellery receives a note from Yadley. The letter explains that Yadley missed Crocker, who had already flown to Chicago on a 7:15 flight. Yadley suggests that if Ellery reaches the Chicago airport by 8:45, he should coordinate with the police to arrest the man.

Ellery calls the Chicago police commissioner, identifying himself as the son of Detective Richard Queen. He requests that the police follow the tall, dark-skinned man with a bandaged wrist without alerting him, suggesting they only arrest him when he attempts to leave Chicago. He also asks the police to assist Professor Yadley upon his arrival.

Ellery flies to Chicago and lands at the airport, where he is met by a group including Professor Yadley, the Chicago Police Commissioner, and his father, Detective Richard Quincannon. Quincannon explains he traveled from New York after being notified by a police chief in Cynthiana that Ellery was his son. The Commissioner informs Ellery that Crocker landed around 8:45 without suspicion and has since taken a taxi to the Rockford Inn, where he is currently being monitored by four police cars. Shortly after, Detective Vane and Prosecutor Easom arrive in Chicago. The group gathers in a room at the Rockford Hotel, where Vane expresses frustration at the fugitive's ability to stay ahead of them.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
At the Rockford Hotel, Ellery Queen, Professor Yardley, Commissioner, and others wait for the capture of a man registered as John Chase from Indianapolis. Detectives report that Chase, believed to be Vilga Crosetto, intends to flee to San Francisco and travel across Asia. Ellery reveals he knows the man's true identity, prompting a coordinated police action on the sixth floor. A waiter is used to approach Room 643 under the guise of providing service. When the door opens, the group subdues the man. Ellery identifies the occupant not as Crosetto, but as Andrew Van, the headmaster of Arroyo.

Later, while traveling back to New York on the "Twentieth Century Limited," Ellery explains his reasoning to a skeptical group including Officer Van, Isam, and Professor Adair. Ellery asserts that while the first three murders appeared inconsistent, the fourth murder provided the missing piece of the puzzle. He notes that the initial investigation in Arroyo suggested Crosetto was a mad fanatic seeking revenge for his family, a theory supported by the motive of a seized inheritance. However, Ellery explains that the key to the case was found in a shabby cabin in West Virginia. He reflects on how the investigation initially struggled with the significance of the letter "T" left at crime scenes and admits that a hunch he had seven months ago regarding the Wilton Court murder might have solved the case earlier had he pursued it.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Ellery explains the details of a fourth murder case to Professor Yardley, Inspector Quin, Vorn, and Escham. He describes a scene at a shabby little house where a corpse was found nailed to a cross-shaped form. Ellery notes that the victim had been dead for approximately fourteen hours. Outside the house, which has only one door and a window sealed with barbed wire, there were two rows of footprints made by the same pair of shoes, indicating one person entered and exited. The footprints were left after 11:00 PM, following heavy rain, coinciding with the time of the murder. The footprints suggest the killer is lame.

Ellery identifies two key pieces of evidence found on the stone floor: a used bandage roll and a large, opaque, unlabeled blue glass bottle of iodine. Since the victim had no wrist wounds, Ellery concludes the killer injured his own wrist, likely while using an axe or struggling with the victim. Ellery points out a logical inconsistency: the medicine shelf contained two labeled bottles of disinfectant (iodine and red medicine), yet the killer chose an unlabeled, non-transparent bottle. This implies the killer already knew the contents of the unlabeled bottle.

Ellery deduces that only the owner of the house, Andrew Tewell (aliases Andrew Van and Old Pete), would have such prior knowledge. Therefore, Andrew Van is the killer, and the body nailed to the wall is not Van, but a victim he killed. Ellery further explains that Van attempted to deceive investigators by dressing the victim in his own ragged clothes to make it appear as though Van himself were the deceased.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Ellery deduces the true identities of the corpses involved in a series of murders. He first confirms that the bodies of Brad and Meagal are indeed themselves, based on a specific mole on Brad's thigh and a hernia diagnosed by Temple and found on a corpse on the *Helen*. Ellery concludes that the corpse hanging from the mast was not Croscot, as Van would have simply claimed self-defense if innocent. Instead, the corpse must be Clinton, whom Van falsely claimed died seven months ago at the Arojo intersection. Ellery realizes that Croscot was actually the person killed at Arojo, meaning the "devil" the police sought for months was already dead.

Ellery identifies Van as the killer of Croscot. He eliminates Meagal due to geography, Brad due to an alibi involving a chess champion, and Kline due to his low intelligence and lack of understanding regarding the "T" symbol. Ellery reconstructs the plot: Van (referred to as Fagan/Fann) lured Crosetti to Arojo via an anonymous letter. Crosetti, seeking revenge, used a car from Crocker to reach the crossroads but did not bring a suitcase because he did not intend to dismember a victim.

The mastermind, Vance (or Andrew Cullis-Tweed), orchestrated the murders to appear as Croscot's revenge. Vance used Clinton—whom he had hired from a Pittsburgh orphanage—to disguise his own identity. By arranging murders to look like a fanatic's work, Vance intended to appear killed by an avenger while actually escaping with his life savings and money from his brother, Stephen. Ellery concludes that the decapitations and the "T" symbols were not the work of a fanatic, but a calculated method to prevent the identification of the corpses by removing their heads.

title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Ellery explains the complex motives and methods of Andrew Van der Veer (also referred to as Andrew Gregg Trevor or Van), who orchestrated a series of murders to claim an inheritance. Ellery reveals that Van beheaded Croscot to prevent body identification and to mislead investigators into believing the victim was Van or Cling. Van utilized a "T-junction" concept to create a pattern of irrationality, necessitating the beheading of Brad and Megal to maintain the illusion.

Regarding the final murder, Ellery and Escham discuss footprints near a shabby hut. Ellery deduces that the killer's footprints entering the shack were deeper than those leaving because Van was carrying the body of Klein from a nearby cave. Van had intended to create the illusion that the limping Croscot had entered the cottage, but he failed to account for the weight difference affecting the footprint depth.

Ellery clarifies that Van's reappearance at the crime scene was a calculated move to claim his $5,000 inheritance from Breda's will, which required him to be legally alive and identified by Megal. Van also used a note to suggest he was being hunted, making his "return" seem like a victim of a vengeful killer. Officer Quinn provides the specific motives: Van killed Tom because Tom took a woman he loved, and he killed Megal because Megal had controlled and withheld family money from him.

Finally, Ellery explains that the appearance of the innocent Striker at various crime scenes was not a coincidence but a result of Van's manipulation of Croscot's movements. Ellery concludes by deciding to write a book titled *The Mystery of the Egyptian Cross* to cover his investigation expenses.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Muto and Mutsuji travel through a desolate, wintry landscape toward the Inari Shrine, passing through the northern Kyoto Basin near Mount Danwa. They drive through the territory of the Matsuzaki family, characterized by a half-collapsed, elliptical iron gate and a neglected, tree-lined avenue. Their destination is Inkyou Castle, also known as Sparrow Castle, a Gothic-style mansion with blue conical roofs and three spires. Mutsuji explains that the castle's name derives from a prose poem by Luigi Righetto, in which a sparrow personifies death coming for children at dawn; he notes that Righetto was himself executed for murdering women.

Upon arrival, the pair observes several Kyoto Prefectural Police vehicles, including a gray forensic department car, parked by a fountain. Mutsuji concludes that a murder case has likely occurred. As they approach the mansion, they are greeted by Detective Tsubaki, a straightforward man in a trench coat who has previously helped Mutsu thrive within the police force. The group prepares to meet with a man named Mr. Itou.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Detective Jiro and Kumotsubashi reunite after three and a half months, having last seen each other during an arson case. Jiro, acting on a request from Mr. Mikage Itou, arrives at a location to investigate what appears to be a murder case involving Mr. Itou. Jingu informs Kumotsubashi that Mr. Horikawa and his team were the first to arrive at the scene.

The group enters a spacious entrance hall characterized by a high ceiling, a golden chandelier, and a pervasive red color scheme. The floor, staircase, wallpaper, and furniture are all various shades of red, creating a twilight-like atmosphere. The hall functions as a museum of ancient art, displaying expensive artifacts such as "Eryx's Vessel," "Lacan's Holy Grail," and "Al-Rus' Mermaid."

As the group ascends the red-carpeted stairs toward the second floor, where the crime scene is located, they examine a pair of Italian Renaissance-style decorative armors standing guard. Mukai points out a significant anomaly: while the left armor is complete, the right armor is missing its iron boots, leaving the mounting pipes and leather buckles exposed. The group discusses the potential significance of this imbalance and the unfastened buckles. Inspector Tsujimura, a member of the wartime generation, expresses a desire for the case to be straightforward, while others suggest that the contradictions and puzzles will eventually lead to the truth. The group proceeds toward the second floor, intending to meet the members of the Kumihara family later.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Chief Inspector Tsujimura and a group of investigators arrive at the Kyūgajō mansion to examine the crime scene in Itou's room. The room is a spacious, antique-furnished suite located at the end of a long, dim corridor. Upon entering, the investigators encounter Detective Horikawa, Detective Horiwell—a talented investigator and right-hand man to Saito Village—and Muto.

The investigators discover the body of Itou, an elderly man wearing a flashy blue woolen robe. The corpse is in a bizarre state: it is decapitated, with a bloody, dark cut where the head should be, and the feet have been severed. Muto explains the significance of the feet by demonstrating that the victim was wearing decorative medieval iron armor boots. Because such decorative armor is designed for aesthetic harmony rather than practical use, the boots are too small for human feet to fit inside; therefore, the feet had to be removed to put them on.

The identity of the victim is confirmed by a silver engagement ring on the right ring finger, which is embedded in the dry skin and engraved with the name "Ito" in Roman letters. While a housekeeper is expected to attest to the ring's ownership, the investigators note that the victim's head has not yet been found.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Following the discovery of a headless corpse, Wakisaka suggests using the concept of a set to narrow down the search for the missing head. Drawing a parallel to Edgar Allan Poe's "The Purloined Letter," Mukai posits that the head might be hidden in a conspicuous location due to psychological blind spots. Mutsuzaki reveals he had already seen a head wearing a hat on the entrance hall's coat rack, which explains his previous optimistic attitude.

Detective Nakamura rushes in to report that while he found a head on the coat rack as predicted, it does not belong to Ito, but to someone else. Mutsuzaki concludes that they have been set up. An old man named Hayashi, the younger brother of the deceased Miri Hayashi, appears and identifies the head as belonging to Hayashi. Despite the gruesome nature of his brother's death, Hayashi remains composed and detached. He recognizes Kurosu, noting that he is the person Ito hired.

During the investigation, Horikoshi provides details regarding the discovery of the body by the housekeeper, Kubo Haruko, who is currently resting due to her mental state. Tsuchimikado questions Higuchi about the whereabouts of a man named Uma. Arai notes that Uma claimed he would stay in the city overnight, a claim supported by Yoshino, the housekeeper. However, it is pointed out that Tsutsuji is approximately 100 kilometers away across the Danbo Plateau. The passage concludes with Mukizuki intensely questioning the old man about the location of Ito's actual head.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Woodside asks Inaba for advice on where the killer might have hidden a head. Inaba suggests the location is "Hell's Gate," a room in the mansion. Jiban, the son of a neighbor, leads the group—including Kaname, Mutsu, the narrator, Detective Horikawa, Kumagai, Kumozawa, Wakamatsu, Wakuzaki, Tsubaki, Detective Toriyama, Sugayan, and Kanou—through a dark, narrow, and unsettling passage toward the room.

The group arrives at a thick, ivory-colored marble door featuring exquisite carvings of the torments of hell, including naked bodies and an inverted Eastern Orthodox cross. Kumozawa observes the artistry, while Wakamatsu suggests the door was not originally intended for use as a door. Wakuzaki uses an oil lamp to inspect the upper part of the door, discovering small, soybean-sized holes recessed into the carvings. Detective Toriyama attempts to open the door, but finds it locked. Sugayan produces a key and successfully unlocks the door.

The narrative then shifts to a prologue set the previous day. Yuki Hikari, the narrator, visits an office where Mukai and Kumazawa are present. Kumazawa has opened two envelopes addressed to Murakami Yusuke: one white and one light brown. The brown envelope is covered in a white powder used for fingerprint extraction. The letters have no sender. It is noted that Murakami's father runs the Murakami Detective Agency, an elite and reputable firm in the Kyoto and Kansai regions.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Mochizuki is a highly talented detective at the Agency for Faith, known for his ability to logically process intelligent crimes. He maintains a close friendship with Inspector Jinguuji of the Metropolitan Police Department. Due to his reputation as a "genius," Mochizuki previously received a massive volume of private commissions, though he has recently begun rejecting most of them because they involve trivial disputes among the upper class. He only accepts cases that genuinely interest him.

The narrator, Hagio, a mystery novelist and long-time acquaintance of Mochizuki since their university days, visits him and observes a peculiar letter. The letter is written on high-quality, light yellow stationery with a floral pattern. The handwriting is light and appears to have been written quickly with a fountain pen. The content is brief and high-handed, consisting of a single sentence: "Come to my residence at 10:00 a.m. on December 2nd for consultation."

The sender is identified as Tomijiro Itô. This name is associated with the Ito Group, a massive corporation with roots in the Meiji-era Ito Silk Factory that grew into a large zaibatsu. The group is currently represented by Kinmiri Heavy Industry. The letter is linked to the mysterious Kinmiri family and their reclusive lifestyle at "Crow Castle," a legendary and secretive mansion in Kyoto. Hagio suspects the letter might be related to the recent death of Mukayama Tadashi, the chairman of Mukayama Seisakusho. Mochizuki expresses interest in the letter, suggesting it is significant.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Mutsu presents a brown paper envelope containing a threatening note made of mismatched newspaper clippings. The note warns that "Death is lurking in the city of Sōyama." Murakami observes that the sender either knows him well or knows nothing about him, and the narrator agrees to accompany Murakami, jokingly expressing a desire to meet the author.

The scene shifts to a dark, cylindrical stone tower that resembles a giant well. The space is windowless, illuminated only by an ancient, unlit oil lamp and faint light from high arrow slits. Inside, a gruesome discovery is made: the decapitated head of Ito lies near a headless corpse. The cut is so precise that the head and body appear to belong together. Kusaka suggests the body might belong to Haya, and a detective searches the corpse's pockets, finding only a black leather wallet, a room key, and a floral handkerchief. In the corner of the room stands a dark brown guillotine with a glowing, one-meter-wide blade.

As Hodai leads a pale Kikumura away to call the forensic team, the group discusses a potential lie told over the phone by someone imitating Yamazaki. Mukai uses a lamp to reveal ancient script inscribed on cobblestones in the center of the room: "Mene, mene, tekel, upharsin." Mukai explains this is a biblical quote from the Book of Daniel regarding the fall of a kingdom, suggesting the events are the work of an "invisible hand of God." Woodside proposes that the location is a secret room, noting that the marble door is the only entrance and can be locked from the outside. The narrator concludes that the situation appears to be a locked-room mystery.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Kumotsumi and Tsubaki discuss the motive behind the killer's theatrical actions, specifically the decapitation of victims and the creation of a locked room. Kumotsumi suggests the killer is performing for an audience. Mochizuki points out a key held in the left hand of the deceased Uma. Detective Jinguji examines the object, noting it is a luxurious, dull brass key with a complex shape and a carved lily on the handle.

Mochizuki also directs attention to Kusanagi’s corpse, which is surrounded by shriveled orange or mandarin seeds. While Jinguji suggests the killer might be mentally ill or a fanatical logic enthusiast, Mochizuki posits that the killer is a highly confident person, possibly a narcissist, who may be intentionally leaving misleading trails. As the forensic team arrives, the group is instructed to investigate the source of the oranges.

In a Victorian-style second-floor reception room, the narrator waits with Matsuzaki. Mutsuji teases the narrator, suggesting that the case is just beginning and that the killer might be willing to reveal themselves if found. Mochizuki asserts that the locked room was likely an inevitable part of the killer's aesthetic design. While the narrator questions the next steps following Itoh's death, Mochizuki remains calm, appearing to wait for something specific. The scene concludes when Kanai enters the room; he appears pale and unhealthy, looking like a weak scholar, and gazes at the group with unease.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Jiban meets with Mutsuzaki, Yasuoka, and Kumotsu to discuss the investigation into the deaths of Ito's father and Umezu. During the conversation, Mutsuzaki pretends to be ignorant of the case details, while Jiban probes the others for information. Mutsuzaki suggests that the killer belongs to the Inagaki family, a sentiment that Yasuoka appears to share.

Jiban reveals his true motive: he wants to hire Kumotsu to find the killers because he does not trust the police. Kumotsu agrees to the assignment, and a room is arranged for them on the third floor. The group also discusses the mysterious "Hell's Gate," a room containing a guillotine that the elder Ito used for solitary, nocturnal activities which he sarcastically referred to as "worshiping gods." It is noted that the Inagaki family members, such as Ushijima, are largely uninvolved in the family company since their grandfather's retirement.

The discussion is interrupted by the arrival of the police chief and Detective Horikoshi. The police officer announces that they have found both of Ito's feet. When asked where they were found, Mutsuzaki speculates they might be in Ito's desk drawer, which leaves the chief stunned by the accuracy of the guess. The passage concludes with a description of Ito's room, which features exquisite, century-old Flemish-style furniture and is where Ito spent most of his time, except for his rituals in the "Gate of Hell."

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
In an ornate, antique-style room, a detective examines a desk featuring an asymmetric, crooked vase. Tsubaki observes that the desk is where a foot was hidden. Mutsu notes that the killer likely did not intend to hide the foot, as it was placed in a bottom drawer covered by documents. Among these documents is a high-quality white envelope addressed to Mr. Motoyama of Goryokuchō. Goryokuchō is described as a famous, highly marketed private detective and one of the "Kyoto Odd Men."

The investigators observe that the envelope's seal remains intact, suggesting the killer had no interest in its contents. The group discusses why Itoh commissioned two different detectives—Mutsuken and a detective from the Higashinari Detective Agency—simultaneously, speculating that the matter may be exceptionally serious. Jiroguchi suggests collecting fingerprints from the letter before opening it.

The investigation moves to Umayo's room, which is adjacent to Ito's room. The police chief uses a key taken from Umayo's suit to unlock the door. Unlike previous rooms, Umayo's room shows no signs of disturbance or new bodies. However, the investigators discover an extensive collection of approximately fifty to sixty enlarged, framed photographs of horses covering the walls. The presence of these photos suggests Umayo had a deep obsession with racehorses rather than gambling. Mutoh mentions a specific horse named "Reed's Uma," noting its age, while Juchū expresses contempt for the subject of horse racing.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Mutsuji and Sugiyama discuss Hirabayashi’s attachment to a racehorse named Reed, noting that Hirabayashi enjoys the thrill of racing regardless of performance. They mention that Hirabayashi has been depressed since the death of a horse named Rydeber, which died from a broken leg. Kusanagi suggests a complex family dynamic regarding the deceased, implying a relationship that outsiders cannot easily penetrate.

As detectives search Ai-ma's room, Wakatsuki describes the room as a "singularity" in mathematical terms, suggesting that because the killer did not use the room as a primary stage, the lack of activity itself may be a clue. Mishima shifts the focus to a set of new stereo speakers and a record left out on a player. Mochizuki identifies the LP as a recording of Dvořák's "American" String Quartet and Smetana's "Death and the Maiden." While some characters suggest the music is a thematic clue related to the victims, others, like Matsuzaki, argue it may be a coincidence or an attempt to find meaning where none exists. A Mozart "Requiem" is also noted, though it is not the record playing.

The investigation moves to a guest room on the third floor, where the police question Akiyoshi, Yan, the housekeeper, and other staff. After three hours of questioning, the police make no significant progress. No one has an alibi for the time of the crime between 3:00 and 4:00 a.m., and no new information regarding the motive for the murders or the victims' bizarre actions is discovered.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
The detective attempts to question the Yoshinobu family, but their detached and indifferent demeanor makes the interrogation ineffective. The Inspector becomes exhausted by the lack of urgency shown by the family members. During the process, the housekeeper, Yoshiko, provides two significant testimonies. First, she observed Yoshinobu writing a note the previous night, which may be the letter sent to the detective in Higashiyama. Second, she clarifies the situation regarding the "Hell Gate," the windowless stone room where the horse corpse was found. While it was initially suspected that a spare key held by Yasu was used to lock the room, Yoshiko testifies that the wall-mounted safe containing the spare keys has been covered in undisturbed dust for years, suggesting the spare key was not used.

Kumotani bets 125,000 yen that Yoshiko is not mistaken. Mukai suggests that the killer’s provocative attitude implies a locked-room mystery, though the detective questions the possibility of a third key. Kumotsubo suggests a connection to Prokofiev's opera *Love of Three Oranges*, specifically the suite titled "Hellish Scene," though the detective remains skeptical of this connection.

Nakamura delivers a forensic report stating that the victim, Ito, and the horse died between 3:00 a.m. and 3:30 a.m. The cause of death for both was the severance of the spinal cord. Forensic evidence indicates the decapitation occurred while the victims were still alive, leading Tsujimura to describe the killer as a monster.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
During an investigation into the murders of Ito and Uma, Kumotsuma and the police discuss the gruesome nature of the crimes. It is confirmed that Ito's feet were severed approximately thirty minutes after his death. The victims' necks were cut cleanly, suggesting the use of a highly skilled blade rather than a blunt instrument like an axe. Nakamura reports that the victims were likely knocked unconscious before being decapitated. While a guillotine was considered, there were no signs of its use. Kumotsuma suggests the killer is hiding the weapon to create an effect and signal that more murders will follow.

Regarding the crime scene at "Hell's Gate," fingerprints on the door handles belong to Ito and Umezawa, suggesting Umezawa entered the room of his own accord or was lured there. While blood from both victims was found in "Hell's Gate," a significant amount of Ume's blood was also discovered on the carpet in Ito's room. Kumagai notes a suspicious detail: Uma's left arm experienced a spasm while he was holding a key, despite him being right-handed. This inconsistency is highlighted as a potential major factor in the case.

The investigation establishes that Ito was killed in his room between 3:00 am and 3:30 am, while Uma was killed at "Hell's Gate." The scene at "Hell's Gate" presented a "locked room" situation, as the door was locked and the key remained in Uma's hand. As the investigators settle into a guest room on the third floor, Mochizuki suggests that the murders are part of an ongoing series and may be related to a delegation Ito intended to pass on.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
During a discussion regarding the murder of Ito, characters analyze the motives and methods of the killer. Woodstock and Woodin debate whether Ito’s actions were careless or if he intentionally called the police to restrain a killer. Woodstock presents a threatening letter received the previous day, noting that the short interval between the commission letter and the threatening letter suggests the killer was aware of Ito's actions. Mutsuzaki suggests the killer might be targeting him personally rather than the police.

The group explores a hypothesis regarding Hayashimizu, referred to as "the horse." One theory suggests Hayashimizu attempted to create an alibi by visiting Shimizu, intending to kill Ito, but was instead killed by the true culprit. This theory posits that the "Hell's Gate" scenario was originally intended for Ito, and the killer improvised the decapitation and the presence of Hayama's body to confuse the investigation.

Wakisugi challenges this hypothesis by questioning the significance of Ito's severed foot, the blood of Hayama found in Ito's room, and the mechanics of the locked room. While one participant suggests the killer manipulated the safe to hide tampering, Wakisugi argues this is an unreliable explanation. The discussion concludes with skepticism regarding the killer's methods, specifically whether the locked room was achieved through an antiquated trick like using a needle and thread, especially given the placement of the key in the victim's left hand despite Kenzo's habitual right-handedness.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Mochizuki and the narrator discuss the inconsistencies in the death of Yasuko (Ito). Mochizuki argues that if the crime scene were part of the killer's plan, Yasuko should have been killed at "Hell's Gate" rather than her own room. He suggests the killer may have been interrupted by Uma, though he admits his deduction might be incomplete. Mochizuki expresses a somber, philosophical view that deductive reasoning is insufficient for understanding the case, comparing it to using Newtonian mechanics to control nuclear physics.

The group is notified by a stern housekeeper that dinner is ready. Arai invites the investigators to dine with the family. The dining room in the Inoue household is described as an extravagantly decorated, massive space featuring a vaulted ceiling with religious art, a Raphael painting of the Virgin Mary, and a large chandelier. The decor is a "smorgasbord" of various Western and Southern European styles.

The dinner guests include Akiyama, Kouichi, Akiyoshi, Fujiko, Shin'ichi, Yuzuha, Muto, the narrator, Satsuki, Yuki, and Arai. The two daughters of the victim, Hidemasa, are notably absent. During the meal, Arai introduces the attendees. Akiyama asks Wakaba about the progress of the investigation, to which Wakaba responds cryptically. Tensions arise when Yuki sharply demands that the killer be found quickly to prove his innocence, prompting a reproach from Wakaba. Despite these underlying frictions, the meal proceeds in a heavy, largely silent atmosphere of indifference.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
The next morning in the city of the Crow, Xiang Yue wakes up after a restless night to find his room empty. Wakatsuki has already departed for Kyoto, leaving Xiang Yue alone in a spacious, soundproof room with cold breakfast. While listening to Schubert on the radio, Xiang Yue reflects on the psychological effects of sensory deprivation.

A loud, persistent knocking at the door leads Xiang Yue to discover two identical, beautiful girls: Miyuki and Kanae. Although Mutsu previously stated they were approximately twenty years old, they appear to be fifteen or sixteen, dressed in matching pink outfits with frilly aprons and flowered hats. They possess a youthful, innocent aura and gray eyes that show no emotion regarding the deaths of their father and grandfather.

The twins interact with Xiang Yue in a synchronized, "stereo" fashion that he finds physically difficult to endure. After a brief misunderstanding regarding his name, the girls ask if he has found the killer of their father and grandfather. Xiang Yue admits he has not, noting their unsettling lack of grief. The conversation turns to the identity of the killer; one girl suggests their great-grandfather is responsible, leading to a heated argument between the sisters. The encounter concludes with the two girls engaging in a physical shoving match in front of a stunned Xiang Yue.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Miyuki and Kanae, two twenty-year-old twins, engage in a physical altercation that the narrator, an assistant to Detective Watari, unsuccessfully attempts to stop. The housekeeper, Yasu, intervenes by scolding the twins and ordering them to stay inside the house. Yasu's practiced handling of the situation suggests she has managed the twins' behavior many times before.

Seeking fresh air, the narrator descends a red-carpeted staircase and notices two large, masterfully painted portraits hanging on a lily-patterned wall between the first and second floors. The portraits depict a man with a rugged face and a monocle, and a beautiful woman in a ball gown holding a Chinese fan. The narrator notes the paintings appear to date from the construction of Crow Castle, spanning the late Meiji to Taisho eras.

Yukiye, an adopted daughter of the Inari family, descends the stairs wearing black mourning clothes and a lace hat. She identifies the subjects as her adopted grandparents, Fukuda and Hina. Yukiye explains that a painter from Paris was specially invited to create the works. The narrator reflects on the woman in the portrait, noting her foreign features and her reputation as a "heroine among women" and a business leader who met Mochitsuki in Harbin in 1918. Rumors suggest she may have been a Russian noble or a bandit leader. Yukiye refers to the woman as "the rose," evoking the concept of the "black rose of Le Mei Ta," which is said to maintain eternal beauty.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Yuki and Yuzu engage in a philosophical discussion regarding darkness and light. Yuki suggests that even pitch-black darkness eventually fades to purple, noting that even the Snow Queen Selene found peace in a world without light. Yuzu remarks on the melancholy of the night and the inability of flowers to bloom in the lightless city of Xiangyuan. Yuzu suggests that the "white raven" is no longer a raven and warns the narrator not to think too much about the matter before walking away.

The narrator, Kouki, later finds Mizuho sitting in the courtyard of the Inari household, which resembles a small botanical garden. Mizuho, wearing a white dress, appears pale and describes a sense of something "terrifying" within the city. She suffers a coughing fit, attributing her poor health to the changing seasons. Mizuho expresses deep trust in Mr. Kimitsuki, whom she compares to a god, and believes he will rid the household of its terrors. Kouki attempts to encourage her, suggesting that trust brings redemption.

As Kouki leaves, he encounters Mukae, who mocks his gentlemanly behavior. Kouki expresses concern that Mizuho seems to be on the brink of death. Mukae and Muto discuss the underlying reasons for her condition, revealing that Mizuho is the daughter of a mistress. Muto explains that the Kaga family only recently acknowledged Kaguya, following a relationship twenty years ago involving Kanou and a woman he nearly ran away with.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Mukai and the narrator discuss the past, noting that Yasunobu failed to withstand pressure and that Mist-drawn is currently in a state of psychological fragility, potentially harboring a desire for revenge. During their conversation, a blue hat drifts through the courtyard, and Woodjutsu remarks on the color scheme of Kurogane City, noting the "gray" appearance of the Minagorou family, which is linked to the presence of mercury in Inaba.

In the afternoon, the police chief and his team conduct a thorough search of "Hell's Gate" for hidden rooms or escape routes. The search is unsuccessful, with a detective concluding there is no space for even a mouse to fit. Following the search, the narrator speaks with Kusaka and Mutsuji. Kusaka notes that Itoh visits the room every night, suggesting the police chief is looking in the wrong place.

Mutsuji dismisses the physical details of the crime—such as how the room was constructed or how the body was mutilated—as superficial. He argues that the "atmosphere" of Crow Castle is the true key to understanding the killer. He points out a paradox in "Hell's Gate": the door features a carving of a suffering human denying righteousness, yet the center of the room contains a salvation message representing God's response. Mutsuji suggests the killer's intention aligns with the "other side of Nirvana" and hints that the killer may not be a conventional human. Later, Kanou visits the group. While Kanou remains reserved, Mukai abruptly confronts him regarding his concern for his daughter.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Kaneko is shocked when Mukai mentions Mizuho, a topic not previously discussed. Sugata (Muto) reveals his motive for the visit, explaining that he intended to claim Mizuho but was prevented by her mother's death. He discloses that Mizuho's mother was Maria Kuchera, a foreigner he met in America. This revelation suggests that Mizuho is of mixed race, which would have caused intense opposition from the xenophobic Kagura family and the Kishijiro bloodline.

Sugata expresses his inability to protect Mizuho himself due to family opposition and asks Shizu to take on the responsibility. Although Shizu suggests Tsukimi might be a better choice, he reluctantly agrees to protect her while he travels to investigate the case. After Kaneko leaves, the group discusses the assignment. Mutsuji plays "Death and the Maiden" on an amplifier while performing string tricks. The conversation shifts to the nature of the investigation and the identity of the killer.

The group speculates on the motives of Kaname, suggesting he may have been torn between his inheritance and his mistress. Mukutsumi proposes a theory that the killer could be the mistress, implying that if she discovered Kaname's true intentions, the situation could turn deadly. The passage concludes as Hina announces dinner time and the narrator notes that Kumozawa is missing at 10:00 AM.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
In the morning at the Kurojishi mansion, the narrator reflects on Mutsumi's prediction of a second murder case and his desire to escape the gloomy atmosphere of Kurokawa City. While walking in the corridor, he hears rhythmic hammering and discovers Yamada, a long-term laborer who works at the mansion twice a week, repairing a cracked lily-patterned handrail. Yamada expresses a lack of goodwill toward the sisters Kanae and Miyuki, blaming them for the damage, and voices his fear regarding the unidentified murderer, urging the detective to catch the killer so he can work in peace.

The atmosphere becomes tense when Keiichiro returns from outside in a bad mood. A confrontation ensues between Keiichiro and Jinma (referred to as Jouma/Jirou), with Jinma defensively claiming he is not "burying a body." Yuzuha intervenes to defend the detective, descending the stairs with Yuki. Amidst the awkward silence, Yuzuha suddenly takes the narrator's hand and leads him outside, an action that leaves both the narrator and a stunned Jinma speechless. After Yuzuha apologizes for her brother's temperament, she shifts the conversation to Mr. Kimitsuki. The narrator agrees with Xuyan's assessment that Kimitsuki is a highly intelligent and capable reasoner, noting that praising a friend is a virtue.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
After leaving the hall of the Inoue mansion, Xuyan (Yuzu) asks the narrator, Mr. Kouyama, who "Brutus" is, a question the narrator perceives as destructive. As they walk along a path toward a lake, Yuzu questions the narrator's strange behavior in the hall. She claims his brother is "already crazy" and tells the story of Val Selan, a carpenter poisoned by his wife who became perceived as insane and lived in a state of delusion before being selected as a saint. Yuzu uses this allegory to discuss subjective truth and the nature of existence.

When Wakamizu approaches, Yuzu leaves abruptly. The narrator asks if her comments referred to Selene or Shizuma, but receives no answer. The narrator then speaks with Mutsuzaki (Mukai), who observes that Yuzu is a clever woman. Mutsuzaki jokes about being manipulated by the murderer, suggesting there is an eight-eighth probability that a woman is the killer, though he quickly dismisses it as a joke to ease the narrator's unease.

As the narrator and Mutsuzaki return to the entrance gate, a piercing scream and the sound of Mahler's music signal a new tragedy. Inside, a dark brown box carved with scenes from the Kalevala, once cherished by Aku-ōn, is stained crimson with blood. The scene is described as a macabre parallel to Salome dancing with the severed head of John; Aku-ōn’s own severed head has been placed upon his prized box. Tsubaki and Kumiko observe the head of Akibas, noting that this new murder involves decapitation and adds new elements to the ongoing mystery.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Following a scream from Harumi, the narrator and Mutsuji run toward the second-floor corridor. There, they encounter the servant Shima, who is stumbling and incoherent, attempting to convey that something has happened to Aoyama. The narrator finds the housekeeper Yamauchi slumped against a door in a state of terror. Looking where she is staring, the narrator discovers a white head placed on a box, which appears to be floating against the dim wall.

Mukutokun observes that the murderer has taken advantage of them, noting that Arai is unlikely to be the killer and may instead be used as a decoy. Jirogawa points out that the killer frequently leaves body parts missing, such as a head or a body. While the head was found in a room with little blood, Detective Horikoshi’s team finds no clues in the closets, suggesting the decapitation occurred elsewhere.

At 12:20, Detective Nakamura reports finding a body in a first-floor storage room. The room, used for food storage and kept at 4-5 degrees Celsius, is a bloody crime scene. The headless body of Akibabe lies in an "X" shape on the white floor. A maid named Miyuki discovered the body while preparing lunch. Mutsuji suggests the killer may have timed the discovery to create a dramatic effect, potentially intending for the body to be found first to lead people to the head, or vice versa. The group concludes that the victim likely died from a beating before being decapitated.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Detective Horikoshi and Muto discuss a previous case where a victim was decapitated while still alive, suggesting the killer believed the person was already dead. Following this, questioning of relevant personnel takes place on the third floor of the building where the first head was discovered.

Mr. Yashiro (also referred to as Yamada or Mount) is the first to be questioned. He explains that he was following Mr. Kuroiwa's instructions to repair the stairs. He finished around noon and went to report it with Lady Haru. Yashiro describes discovering Arai-san's head, which initially looked like a prank because the face was painted white. He notes the door was unlocked. Shanda, who entered the room with Yashiro, recalls hearing Daya Grandmother scream after he entered.

The estimated time of death for Arai-san is between 10:00 and 10:30. Yashiro provides an alibi, stating he worked on the stair railing continuously from before 10:00. This is supported by the testimony of a housekeeper and maid, who noted he did not stop hammering for more than a few minutes, effectively ruling him out as a suspect. Yashiro reports that the only people who used the central staircase during his work were Jumaru-san, who carried a black travel bag, Kiri-drawn-san, a detective, and Yuki-san.

Muto argues that Yashiro's presence on the stairs makes him an unpredictable witness for the killer, but the narrator suggests they should not rely solely on his testimony. The passage concludes with Muto questioning who among the people not mentioned by Yashiro might be suspicious.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
During the investigation into the Arai murder, the circle of suspects initially appears to include nearly everyone. Wooden Shinsuke jokingly suggests himself as a suspect, but the investigation shifts to the housekeeper, Kubo Haruko. Haruko, appearing pale and exhausted, testifies that she was in the kitchen with the maid, Hina, preparing lunch. Hina corroborates this, stating they were in the kitchen from approximately 10:10 until 10:40, during which time they could hear Mr. Yamada repairing the stairs. This establishes alibis for both Haruko and Hina.

The investigation into the Inagaki family relatives, however, yields no solid alibis. Gan-ya claims to have heard nothing due to soundproofing; Mizuki claims she did not notice Akiyama passing through the courtyard; and Shizuka claims he was walking in the outer courtyard for an hour without explaining a bag he was carrying. Detective Jiroh expresses frustration at the lack of progress, but Muto suggests that the investigation has gained a new direction regarding the victim's side. Muto posits that the killer may have been aware of Mizuki's presence in the courtyard and suggests a startling possibility: that the person who might have overlooked something is Arai himself.

The narrative also provides historical context regarding the Oyama family and Kurenaga Castle. After World War II, the mansion became a villa. Twenty-five years prior, the former president, Oyama Motoharu, and his wife, Hinata, moved there to live in seclusion. They hired Yashiro as a housekeeper to care for them, as their three sons were stationed in various cities.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Following the death of Hinata-ko, Motsu (Motoshige) experienced declining health and loneliness, eventually calling his long-estranged sons back to Inabu Castle. Motsu passed away one month prior to the current events, an event Mukai compares to the decline of the Ashikaga shogunate disrupting a long-standing equilibrium. Following Motsu's death, Yodo, Uma, and Kouzu were also killed. 

Discussions regarding the Mikage family tree reveal a missing woman named Uzuki, identified as Mutōmochi's youngest daughter. It is noted that she ran away with a man thirty years ago, an act of "selfishness" that led Dōshō to sever all ties with her; her cousin Kujaku had chosen true love, while Kusunoki had not obtained what he wanted.

In a discussion regarding the recent murders, Mochizuki proposes a new suspect: Hiruko or her descendants. This possibility suggests the killer might be an outsider, which would disrupt the established investigation and include everyone from the protagonists to Detective Tsujimura and the police as potential suspects.

The group later visits Yoshino, who is listening to a melancholy piano quintet by the Russian composer Mikhail Ivanovich Medtner. Kumagai explains that Medtner was a figure "erased from history" because his compositions were destroyed by the Bolsheviks following the Russian Revolution. It is noted that Medtner was a political figure rumored to have plotted with Rasputin. The conversation concludes with a mention of Sergei Prokofiev, who lived in Japan during his exile.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Kanayama reveals that Medvedev, a political prisoner fleeing the Red Terror, lived in the Inagaki family's Kujō residence during the pre-war period. While staying there, Medvedev composed a dark piece titled "Imakagami," which he dedicated to Kanayama's grandfather, Tomomichi Imai. Because Medvedev's name was omitted from a recording made thirty years ago, the work was credited to Imai. Kanayama explains that Medvedev died six months after arriving in Kurosawa City; while his death was attributed to drowning in a pond behind the house, the police suspected he was murdered by a communist. Kanayama suggests Medvedev may have been introduced to the Mochizuki family through his grandfather or great-grandfather's service in Manchuria.

The conversation shifts to the inheritance of the late Oyama, which Sugiyama estimates to be approximately 500 billion yen, consisting largely of land in the Northern Mountain and Mirai Group stocks. The will cannot be opened until fifty days after Oyama's death, a date falling on the thirteenth of the current month. Sugiyama expresses concern that he may have lost his inheritance rights because his father and uncle were killed before the will could be opened.

As the group observes Mistuho reading in a pavilion in the courtyard, it is noted that Kaname has been watching over her from a distance for five months. Following her reading, Mistuho heads toward a church located at the western end of the estate. Kanayama explains that his family converted to Orthodox Christianity after returning from China, a rarity in Japan.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Kaname explains that Medvedev was placed in a stone coffin in a mortuary located about 100 meters away from the current location. Kaname notes that his father disliked Orthodoxy and the nearby church, though the church could not be demolished while his grandfather was alive. 

Muketsumi, accompanied by Jiban, Sugawa, Kojima, and others, requests to visit the church. They walk through a dark passage that is described as being symmetrical to "Hell's Gate," which lies in the opposite direction. Mukae suggests the corridor represents a physical form of the transition through death, where the path taken is determined by karma. Sugawa explains that the church consists of a tower entrance leading to a small building containing a chapel with an altar, a cross, an organ, and statues.

Upon entering the church, the group hears the sound of an organ playing Bach's "Passepied." They find Kiri (also referred to as Misty or Mistuho) playing the organ in a small, simple room featuring stained glass windows of Mary. The altar features a cross with three wedges in each direction, mirroring the carvings on the "Gate of Hell." 

After Kiri finishes playing, the group interacts with her. She admits that playing the piano helps her forget "all sorts of things." Muketsumi asks if she left the premises on November 28th, but she denies being anywhere for the past week. After the group departs, Kaguya remains and tells the narrator that her worries have worsened since the death of Yuan. Kaguya also mentions that her grandfather does not like Misty.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Following a conversation with Misty, where the narrator attempts to address her perceived isolation and the destiny of the Tsubaki family, Misty leaves in a state of distress. The narrator then considers the problem of when Maria will ascend to heaven.

In a new chapter, the narrator speaks with Mutsuji (Muto), who is preoccupied with playing with a needle. They discuss a threatening letter sent three days ago. The narrator notes that because there is no local post office, the sender must have traveled to mail it. Mutsuji identifies that besides the staff, only Yutaka and Kouji have left the area. Since the letter had to be sent shortly after one from Itou, and only Jouma, Sugai, Yutaka, and Kouji can drive, the narrator narrows the suspects to Jouma or Sugai. Mutsuji, however, views the letter as a "bottleneck" rather than a simple clue and keeps his specific hypothesis as an "ace card" from the Inspector.

Later, the narrator encounters a haggard and agitated Jinma (Shizuma) in the hall. Jinma criticizes the narrator's leisure following the death of Aunt Cui and Aaru. The narrator observes that Jinma’s usual arrogance has been replaced by a fearful bravado. In a subsequent discussion with Muto, the narrator reflects on fate, comparing themselves to the goddesses Clotho and Atropos. Muto rejects the concept of fate, suggesting that only "Death" lurks in the mansion. The passage concludes with Muto revealing that Jinma loves Yuyan, suggesting that the narrator's fatalistic views might overlook more positive underlying truths.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Following a conversation with Wakamura regarding Shizuku and Shibumi, the narrator loses the opportunity to seek answers when Shizuku is found dead the next morning. Tameichi (also referred to as Jingma/Shizuma/Jumpei) is discovered as a decapitated, naked corpse in a recently renovated bathroom in Kurosawa City. The bathroom features light brown tiles with lily patterns. The faucet was left running, creating loud noise that likely masked the crime and caused water to overflow, potentially washing away bloodstains. The detective determines the time of death was between 12:00 and 1:00 AM, noting that Tameichi likely died from a blow to the back of the head.

The discovery was made by Yuzuha, who fainted upon seeing the body. The investigation faces a bottleneck due to a lack of solid clues and the soundproofed nature of the rooms in Kyokuto City, which allows the killer to operate undetected. Jirou makes unsettling comments regarding the aesthetic of the corpse, leading to a tense exchange with the detective.

The investigators visit Yūka (Yuki), who is recovering from the shock of finding her brother. Yūka explains that she felt an indescribable unease and ran toward the bathroom after hearing running water. She notes that her brother's room door was unlocked, which is unusual as it is typically locked. Yūka also states she heard no sounds of a shower the previous night. The passage concludes with Mutsu questioning Yūka about why she entered the room that morning, a question that leaves her bewildered.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Yuzu informs the detective that she saw her Uncle Aoba walking toward her brother Kei's room after midnight the previous night. Although the corridor was dark and she only saw his profile, she is certain it was him. The detective expresses disbelief, noting that Inaba is dead and questioning the timing of such a sighting. Yuzu expresses guilt, wondering if she could have saved her brother if she had checked his room sooner.

Meanwhile, Wanli Hua presents an ancient, fragile book to Mutou, which she found inside a table at a church. The book is bound with ropes, features torn pages, and contains text in both Latin and English. Mutou identifies the volume as the "Lecanova Book," an apocryphal gospel of the New Testament that focuses on the humanity of Jesus during his crucifixion. 

Following this discovery, Kanae notes that the book is similar to one her grandfather used to carry when visiting a "jail" (which Kanade suggests may be the "gate of hell"). Kanae describes her grandfather wearing a black cloak and carrying a strange tree branch, appearing to perform a secret ritual. Xiao Yan reflects on the possibility that Itou, who was the first victim, might be the murderer using a scapegoat, or that the killer utilized the discovered book. Mutou and Kaneko depart to investigate further, acknowledging that new facts are emerging.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Mutsu-tsune discusses the "Book of Raknawa" (or "Le Cenacol Book"), an occult scripture published in Japan thirty years ago that serves as a defamation of Jesus Christ. The book posits a "Jesus twin" theory, suggesting that Mary gave birth to twins and that Jesus used teleportation to appear in two places. It claims the figure crucified at Golgotha was the brother, while the elder brother intended to resurrect to establish Christianity, portraying Judas as a faithful servant killed to ensure silence. Mutsu-tsune reveals a rose-patterned washi paper bookmark left in the book, likely by the murderer.

This text connects to a monochrome woodcut painting in Kaname's room, which depicts two Jesus twins being crucified. Mutsu-tsune suggests the book and the painting are metaphors for the murders of Ito and Uma, and that the killer's motive involves a "rebellion against God."

During a final explanation to detectives Motegi and Horikawa, as well as the narrator, Mutsu-tsune presents a hypothesis regarding the underlying phenomena of the case. He notes that while decapitation is a surface phenomenon, the killer's specific behavior is distinctive: the severed heads and limbs were not hidden. Mutsu-tsune argues that if the killer's goal were a "replacement trick," the heads would have been concealed. Instead, the killer's decision to leave the body parts intact and discoverable suggests that decapitation itself is the direct intention, rather than a means to hide an identity.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
During a gathering, Mochizuki presents a theory regarding the killer's motives for leaving the heads of Ito and Yokozawa undiscovered. He argues that the killer did not hide the heads because they were not afraid of a search, but rather feared that a search would lead investigators to a specific "sanctuary" containing fatal clues. Mochizuki rejects conventional search locations like the house, lake, or forest, suggesting instead that the killer utilized the concept of "hiding the tree in the forest."

Muto and Mutsuji expand on this, hypothesizing that the killer used the Tomimura family's embalming room to hide the remains within a coffin. They suggest the killer wanted to prevent anyone from opening the coffins to maintain the illusion that they were empty. Following this logic, the narrator reveals that Takao died a month prior. Mutsu concludes that if Kazuo Mochizuki did not actually die but instead woke from a coma in a coffin, then Daisuke Inagaki is the perpetrator of the serial murders.

Mochizuki further explains that the deceased Tomita was likely the "ghost" Yuzuha saw, as his appearance could be mistaken for Akagi's in the dark. Mochizuki introduces an antique book titled "Lekkunarawa," discovered in a church, which he believes inspired the double decapitation of Ito and Ushiwaka. Finally, Mochizuki addresses the "Hell Gate" room, suggesting that the owner of the house might possess a third key. He explains that a previous error occurred when Otoya mistakenly moved Hayato's corpse into the "Hell Gate" instead of Ito's, resulting in Hayato's left hand holding the key.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Following Muto's hypothesis regarding the Mikumo incident, a group including Kogen, the police chief, Horikoshi, and others decides to open a coffin in the underground mortuary of Kurokawa City. Kogen agrees to the request, partly because he is a primary suspect and seeks self-preservation. Amid heavy snowfall, the group descends a wide staircase into a dark, pungent underground room containing several coffins, including those of Takao and Kimiko.

They focus on the stone coffin of Tadao, which features a crystal lid engraved with "TAJIMA·I." As the grave diggers and Detective Horikoshi lift the lid, the group expects to find Takeda. Instead, they discover the corpse of Mutsuji, who had died a month prior. The discovery causes chaos and psychological distress among the witnesses, including Mochizuki and Woodmore. Mutsuji’s body has been cut into two halves, mirroring the state of previous victims, suggesting the work of a serial killer.

In a separate narrative segment, a man named McArthur, dressed in a tailcoat and top hat, arrives at a desolate house. He initially mistakes the narrator for Kumigami and then for Shimomura. Upon learning that Jinmao Jingma passed away the previous day, McArthur’s demeanor shifts, and his gaze becomes sharp as he asks if Jingma was killed.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
McCarty Eel, a private detective from Osaka known for solving the North Kii Arson Murder Case, arrives at the mansion. Koumatsu, identifying himself as a friend of Wakaba, observes McCarty’s detached demeanor and suspects he may be of Slavic descent. Koumatsu notes that the police chief, who is arriving to report autopsy results, will likely clash with McCarty. Detective Torizuka confirms McCarty's reputation as a capable but odd character who is highly selective regarding cases. There is tension regarding the absence of Matsukaze and the high intelligence of the killer, leaving McCarty as a potential source of hope.

Toriumi reports the autopsy results for Otomo (Tōshi Mori), revealing that he died approximately thirty days ago from arsenic poisoning rather than natural causes. The poisoning was chronic, causing internal damage. It is noted that Mochizuki's head was severed three days after Tōshi Mori's death, likely inside the coffin, which explains the lack of fingerprints. The police chief concludes that the murderer meticulously planned the crime a month ago. Furthermore, Mochizuki's lawyer, Downs, reveals that the will distributed the inheritance equally among relatives, contradicting Wakizashi's previous theory. Following this report, Jinguji expresses a desire to meet McCarty.

Koumatsu visits Yuzu, who remains in a state of grief and aloofness. Despite her composed voice, she admits she is not feeling better. Koumatsu observes that Yuzu possesses feelings for Kyousuke that exceed sibling affection. When asked if she likes Kei Maru, Yuzu answers affirmatively while staring at a Van Gogh painting.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
After a tense exchange with Xiyuan (Yuzu) regarding their relationship, Hagoromo (Kouzuki) agrees to meet her on the other side of the lake after sunset. While returning to his room, Hagoromo encounters MacArthur, who provocatively claims to be the best detective in Japan, surpassing Wakimura. MacArthur mentions he has spoken to Kanou regarding the disappearance of a client, though Hagoromo suspects Muto has simply abandoned the client to hide in the mountains. Before retreating to his room, MacArthur issues a cryptic warning to Hagoromo to pay attention to "the twins."

Later, around 4 pm, Jia arrives in a panic to report that Kaina and Wanli are missing. The household is thrown into chaos; Kaname attempts to maintain authority while Yashiro (Daya) is overcome with distress. MacArthur appears, having been eavesdropping, and claims to have an answer regarding the situation. He commands Shinobu and Shizuka to follow him, dismissively treating the emotional distress of the others, including Yasuko (Yuri) and Daya, with cold indifference.

Hagoromo and Kaname follow MacArthur through the courtyard and into the outer garden. They walk a significant distance toward a large, calm lake. MacArthur stops and points to the center of the lake, where a small white wooden boat with the number "2" written in blue on the bow is floating.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Jian Yan and McArthur observe a small boat in the middle of a lake that appears to be filled with corpses. McArthur reveals he checked the boat earlier but left the scene intact to preserve evidence for the police. After instructing Mr. Sugiyama to contact the police chief, Jian Yan and McArthur board a different boat to investigate. McArthur explains he had previously untied the original boat to let it drift, a move intended to prevent the killer from changing their mind.

As they approach the second boat, Jian Yan discovers two identical naked female corpses wearing scarlet spring robes. The twins, Kanae and Miyuki, have been beheaded, and blood has stained the peony patterns on their clothing. McArthur insists they return to shore immediately to avoid disturbing the scene.

Hours later, Detective Kubo and forensic investigators arrive. The detective determines the murders occurred between 1:30 and 2:30 pm and concludes the twins were beheaded in the boat. The housekeeper, Auntie Daya, and other witnesses, including Kaname and Yayoi, are unable to distinguish between the two sisters due to their identical appearances. Hirukawa discovers a note in one of the victims' sleeves containing a crude sketch of a lake, an arrow pointing to a dock, the time "2:00," and the word "secret." The clumsy, left-handed handwriting suggests the killer used the note to lure the unsuspecting twins to the lakeside. Amidst the investigation, Jian Yan encounters Yuki-draw and later discusses the cruelty of the event with Mifumi.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
After a tense exchange where the narrator questions whether Misty’s search for the divine is merely an escape from fear, Misty asks if the narrator is a god. Unable to affirm or deny this, the narrator feels regret for being unable to save her. Misty then decides to go to the church to play Bach.

The narrator enters a house where Detective Hidewell is tidying up and Detective Tsujimura is frustrated by a lack of leads. Detective McArthur is behaving provocatively, lounging in Wakatsuki's chair and irritating the Chief Inspector. McArthur reveals that he discovered the headless corpses of the twins, Mirei and Kaina, on a ship after a walk by the lake, though he had known about them since after two o'clock. He dismisses the idea that the murders were for inheritance, suggesting the twins were simply in the way.

McArthur narrows the suspects to Kaji, Kiri, and Yuzu. He notes that Kiri was the only one upstairs during Akiba's murder, which implies Misty could be the killer through process of elimination. The narrator finds this suggestion difficult to accept. As the narrator follows McArthur to understand his intentions, McArthur mocks the detectives' emotional limitations and their tendency to change attitudes once a case is solved. When questioned about his ability to solve the case, McArthur claims he can do what Mutsuzaki cannot and offers the narrator a hint: that their perspective needs to be broader.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
McCarty walks toward Misty's room, dismissively telling the narrator that he is a "rational creature" who has forgotten the concept of emotion. Later, around 10:30 p.m., Kajiya visits the narrator's room. They discuss Mrs. Hina, who is resting after the loss of the twins, Mutsu and Kiri. Kajiya, appearing sensitive and on edge, shares memories of his deceased mother. The narrator attempts to find a copy of the record "Death and the Maiden" to play for him, but it has gone missing. Kajiya then asks the narrator to protect the "mist drawing," expressing a desire for redemption and a hope that "that child" is saved before he finds his own resting place.

The following morning, the body of the housekeeper, Yasuko, is discovered in her room behind the restaurant. Her severed head is placed on top of the previously missing "Death and the Maiden" record on a low table, while her body is discarded near the entrance. The narrator notes that unlike previous victims who were members of the Inagaki family, Yasuko was a servant, leading the inspector to suggest she may have known something unfavorable to the murderer. During the investigation, Chief Inspector Hatohara discovers an unremarkable engagement ring on Yasuko's right index finger. The ring is engraved with the Roman letters "SHIITSUKI." Upon seeing this, the narrator exclaims that the housekeeper was actually "Kurogane."

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Jirouchou reveals that Yashiro is actually Yuri, the daughter of the runaway Daiju, and possesses the blood of the Ichiyas. This discovery connects thirty years of history, as Yashiro had lived as a housekeeper under the name Tsukiyomi. It is suggested that Mochizuki knew Tsukiyomi was Daya and forced her to avoid the Kono surname, leading to a life of hidden identity and atonement. The group realizes the killer likely knew Tsukiyomi's true identity, as she had changed so much over three decades that even her brothers could not recognize her.
