McArthur arrives at the mansion accompanied by Kaname. When the narrator whispers to McArthur that "Yamamoto is Hikari," McArthur reacts with shock, dropping his walking stick and turning pale. Despite this emotional blow, McArthur later hosts a meeting in the first-floor lounge for six attendees: Kikuchi, Kuroi, Yuzuha, Detective Tsujimura, Detective Horikawa, and the narrator.

McArthur begins addressing the case, specifically the murders of Ito and Uma. He argues that in a locked-room mystery, the "why" is more important than the "how." He begins a lecture on the six categories of motives for creating a locked room to help identify the killer. He first rules out the motive of disguising the crime as a suicide, noting that Ito's head and Hama's body were found together in the room. He then rules out the motive of framing a specific person, noting that there was no indication the killer intended to point the finger at the deceased maid, Yashiro.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
McAtar provides a detailed analysis of the motives behind locked-room mysteries, categorizing them into six types: hindering prosecution, vanity/games, professionalism, accidental occurrence, and others. He dismisses several categories for the current case, noting that the locked room was not created to prevent prosecution since the murders of Aoyagi and Shizuma were sufficient for charges. He identifies the most likely current explanation as the fifth category—vanity or a game—but then introduces a seventh concept: "situation construction."

McAtar argues that the decapitation and mutilation of the victims were not the primary goals, but rather "decorations" designed to create a specific environment. This "situation construction" was intended to make it appear natural for the investigator, Mutsu, to stay at Kurokawa Castle to investigate the Ito-Hitomi murder case. By creating an incredible crime, the killer ensured Mutsu's involvement, thereby allowing the real culprit, Wakizaki, to maintain control over the investigation.

Regarding the first double homicide, McAtar asserts that Woodmatsu acted with an accomplice. He suggests that the threatening letter Woodmatsu showed to Hasegawa was a forgery designed to provide a motive for his visit to the Inari house. Furthermore, McAtar explains the locked room in "Hell's Gate" as a simple deception: the accomplice locked the door from the outside using Ito's key. When the body was discovered, Woodmatsu—who was the first to find the key—placed it in the left hand of the deceased, Hama. This explains why the right-handed Hama held the key in his left hand, as his right hand was pinned beneath his body.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
McArthur presents a persuasive theory regarding a series of murders, beginning with the death of Ito. He asserts that Okumura committed the murder while an accomplice provided an alibi. According to McArthur, Okumura lied about the order in which the head and body were discovered to make the accomplice's alibi appear fortuitous. He explains that Okumura killed Ito and decapitated him on the first floor, while the accomplice remained on the second floor. To move the head upstairs, Mukai threw it from the first-floor garden into a second-floor room. The head was painted with white lead powder to disguise bloodstains from the throwing process and to create confusion. McArthur identifies Kanai as the accomplice, noting that Kanai invited Kumagai into the house and used him to deceive the witness, Kagayama.

McArthur further claims that Kumagai killed Shima, as Kumagai could enter the house without suspicion. He then accuses Jiban of being responsible for the subsequent twin murders and the death of Yūko, suggesting Jiban acted on behalf of Wakasugi to avoid suspicion. The motive for Kanai's actions was to seize control of the Mikage family's power by eliminating obstacles, including Ito, Uma, Akiyoshi, Kizuki, Mukumizu, and the twins.

Finally, McArthur connects the case to a historical mystery involving Medvedev and the Inamura family record, "Imakagami." He suggests that Medvedev may have been killed to embezzle hidden Romanov family funds entrusted to him and Tomonori Motomu. He implies this historical theft may be linked to the current events and Kumagai.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
McArthur concludes a lengthy deduction by suggesting that Kumagai might be the great-grandson of Medvedev. He posits that Medvedev had a mistress in Japan who was pregnant when his body was found, and that this descendant might be seeking revenge against the Inagaki family. Detective Jouji is visibly affected by this theory, but McArthur clarifies it was merely a reliable scenario for investigation.

The meeting is interrupted by Kuwabara, who enters the room clapping. Kuwabara (referred to elsewhere as Wakuzukin or Mukagawa) refutes the accusation, stating his background is clear and that Medvedev’s actual great-grandchild is female. Despite McArthur’s attempt to suggest Kuwabara might be motivated by Kanai's inheritance, the group loses confidence in the detective. Mutsu dismisses the meeting, leaving a defeated McArthur behind.

Following the meeting, the narrator reunites with Matsuzaki, who has returned from a mountain retreat at Mount Kumashira. The narrator recounts the recent murders of Mirei and Kanae, and the revelation that Yuzuha is Kikyo. Mutsu reflects on the case, noting that while he felt powerless, the experience provided him with a sense of personal growth. Mutsu also warns McArthur to be careful in the future, noting that McArthur's intelligence makes him dangerous.

Later, Mutsu takes the narrator to National K University Hospital. Mutsu reveals he has an appointment with Professor Nakatani, a world-renowned pioneer in Japanese neuroscience, though he does not immediately disclose his specific motive for the visit.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Muji and the narrator arrive at Professor Nakamura's room on the fourth floor. Muji requests confidentiality and enters the room, leaving the narrator to wait in the hallway for thirty minutes while reading Ellery Queen's "The Dutch Shoe Mystery." When Muji emerges, he indicates the meeting went well but remains secretive. They return to Kyoto City after nine o'clock, where the narrator observes the eerie, greenish-white glow of the city lights.

The narrator visits Ophelia, who is in a weakened state and speaks cryptically about everything crumbling the next day. Afterward, the narrator encounters Mr. Sugiyama (referred to as Kanai), who appears unsteady, possibly from drinking. Kanai entrusts the narrator with a "mist drawing" and expresses a desire to protect someone. He notes that Mukae believes the case will be solved the following day.

In a subsequent conversation, a talkative Kaname reveals a pattern regarding the Mirai family: women who enter the family tend to die early. He suggests that the family structure lacks women and that this is not merely coincidence. Kaname mentions that his grandmother, Lady Hinata, lived a long life, which broke the superstition, but he suspects that Setsuko was brought into the family as a foster daughter to serve as a substitute to appease this perceived fate.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Following a heavy conversation with Kanai regarding the symbolic nature of a woman's presence, Kou leaves Kaname and decides to take decisive action regarding Yuzu. Despite his own mental burden, Kou feels a premonition that drives him to visit Yuzu's room. He finds her wearing a black dress and, with a harsh but determined attitude intended to prevent a tragic ending, insists she leave the city of Kurage with him.

Kou drives Yuzu in a Piazza car to Arashiyama, where they walk along the south bank of the Arashiyama Gorge near the Katsura River. The landscape is cold and desolate, marking the transition from autumn to winter. After two hours, they visit a coffee shop in Sita. During their meeting, Kou confronts Yuzu about her grief and her connection to the Miura family. He challenges her claim that she is mourning Shizuma, arguing that she lacks blood relation to the family and that her sadness is actually a form of self-pity and an attempt to escape reality.

Kou accuses Yuzu of using Shizuma as a puppet to facilitate her own escape from fate, claiming her love is merely a dream. He asserts that while Shizuma resisted fate, Yuzu is attempting to flee from it. As Yuzu enters a state of hysteria and her logic collapses, Kou slams his hands on the table to suppress her emotional outburst, drawing the attention of other customers in the shop.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
After a tense conversation in a café, the narrator proposes marriage to Yuzu, explaining that it is the only logical solution because he understands her and possesses what she seeks. Yuzu calmly accepts the proposal.

Returning to a room where Kumozawa is listening to a record, the narrator learns that Detective Jouji is on his way, signaling the potential resolution of the case. Mukai provides a beige business envelope containing a report from Cleo of the Mukimizu Detective Agency regarding McArthur. While the narrator initially wonders if McArthur is a suspect, Mukai clarifies that he is not the killer.

The group, including Detective Tsujimura and Detective Kuroda, heads toward McArthur's room. Upon entering, they discover a gruesome scene: McArthur has been murdered, and his severed head is resting on a table next to a "tailless evening gown." The victim had been dead for less than an hour.

Mukai reveals a significant discovery from the reports: McArthur is actually the son of Matsuri and Rokushu Miyuki, making his real name part of the Dragon Tree Lai family. This connection explains McArthur's intense reaction to the news regarding Yashiro's Tsubaki lineage. The Chief Inspector, Muto, expresses regret for his optimism and his failure to lead the case, noting that he felt forced to listen to his opponent's explanation. As the group follows Muto toward the church, there is a sense that a final, tragic act is unfolding.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Mukaijima (also referred to as Mochizuki) stops the group to explain the killer's motive and the pattern behind the recent murders. He reveals that the various "unnecessary" props and circumstances of each crime were intentional challenges meant to demonstrate the killer's superiority. By listing the specific elements of each murder—the iron boots on Itô, the locked room and kumquat seeds with Umay, the white face of Akagi, the nakedness of Shizuki, the coffin of Hozumi, the twins Kanae and Michiyo, the LP record of Yasuko, and the hat—he identifies a theme. The killer has been performing perfect imitations of the "nationality series" by Ellery Queen, specifically: *The Dutch Shoe Mystery*, *The Chinese Orange Mystery*, *The French Powder Mystery*, *The Spanish Shawl Mystery*, *The Greek Coffin Mystery*, *The Siamese Twin Mystery*, *The American Gun Mystery*, and *The Roman Hat Mystery*.

Upon entering the chapel, the group discovers a final tableau: Kaken’s body is bound to a Russian cross, with his head placed on the altar. Mukaijima declares that "The Mystery of the Egyptian Cross" is complete. Mutsu then begins to explain the failure to identify Mizuki as the murderer sooner. He notes that while Itô’s severed feet were hidden in a desk drawer under an unopened envelope addressed to the Higashiyama Detective Agency, the killer showed no interest in the letter. The police chief observes that the killer did not open the envelope, suggesting the killer had no motive to uncover the contents of the commission Itô was preparing.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Mochizuki presents a new hypothesis to the police chief and the detective regarding the murders. He initially considered that the killer might be illiterate due to the sparse, simple hiragana used in the letters left by Kanae and Mikari, which only contained the words "secret" and "2 o'clock." However, the killer's ability to imitate detective Kiyoshiro Kishiro and their use of the Latin/English "Lecchonawa Book" (a Bible translation) proved they could read. Mochizuki concludes the killer is not illiterate, but rather "illiterate in Japanese," meaning they can only read English. This points to Mizuho, a foreigner who lived in America until five months ago.

Mochizuki further resolves the mystery of the threatening letter sent to Hagino. He determines that the murderer did not send it, nor did a third party. Instead, Yudu sent the threatening letter to Hagino to manipulate her temperament and ensure she would come to the Inari house. This is supported by the fact that Yudu had spilled glue on his desk while preparing the letter.

Mochizuki identifies the murderer as Kiri, describing her as a genius who treated the mansion and its inhabitants like pieces in a crossword puzzle. He begins to recount the Ito-Hyoma murder case, noting that Ito had approached them with a request. He explains that around 3 am, Mukemizu struck UMAYama from behind with a blunt object, causing him to faint. Mukemizu then moved the unconscious UMAYama into Ito's room, intending to carry out an execution based on the "Lecchonawa Book" and a painting in Kanayan's house, acting as if she were a reincarnation of God.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Kumozawa explains a miraculous sequence of events involving Mukemizu (Mistuhide), Itô, and Utsugi (referred to as Haya/Utsunomiya/Ushio in various contexts). Mukemizu intended to kill two men sitting on a carpet, Itô and Utsugi, by decapitating them simultaneously with a large knife. However, an anomaly occurred during the strike. As the blade passed through Itô's neck, Itô's head remained on the blade's surface for a fraction of a second. The blade then continued its path, cutting off the head of a nearby person, Haya. Due to Newton's third law and the extreme speed of the event, Itô's head was pushed off the blade and landed vertically on the cut surface of the body.

Kumozawa posits that because the decapitation took less than a hundredth of a second, Itô's brain cells and sensory nerves remained alive. He suggests that the cold temperature of the blade temporarily paralyzed the cells, and the temperature difference between the nerves created an electromotive force that allowed the neural fibers of Itô's head to connect with the nerves of Utsugi's body. This created a temporary "Frankenstein" entity: Utsugi's body controlled by Itô's consciousness.

Upon waking in this new body and seeing Maki holding a knife, Itô was gripped by extreme fear. He instinctively fled into the "Gate of Hell" and locked the door from the inside. Because Itô was left-handed, he used the left hand of Utsugi's body to lock the door, causing the right-handed body's left hand to spasm. Eventually, exhausted and unable to survive without connected blood vessels, Itô died, causing the head to separate from the body and creating the illusion of a locked-room crime scene.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Wakisugi explains the complex series of murders orchestrated by Mistuho. In the Ito-Uma case, Mistuho originally intended to theme the crime after "The American Gun Mystery" using a specific record and a horse. However, when Ito accidentally created a locked room, Mistuho improvised, changing the theme to "The Chinese Orange Mystery." She threw orange seeds through a small hole in a marble door to match the theme. She also decapitated Ito, placing his head on a hat rack and leaving bloodstains on the carpet. The record intended for the original theme remained in Uma's locked room because Murata could not enter.

Regarding the Akatsuki murder, Akiba avoided suspicion by hiding stolen items in her skirt, a technique used by American pickpocket gangs. To kill Jouma, Akiba wore oversized clothing and placed Tamekichi's head on her own head to mimic a scene from an Aeschylus tragedy, luring Yuzu to open Tamekichi's coffin.

In the case of Fujiko, the plan involved McArthur, Tomioka's son. McArthur attempted to team up with Fujiko to claim Tomioka's property, but Mistuho killed him instead. Because McArthur wore a top hat, Mistuho adapted the theme to "The Mystery of the Roman Hat," a theme originally intended for Yūen. 

Wakisugi reveals Mistuho's motive as a twisted Oedipus complex. Driven by a desire to transform Kaji into a "God" after being disappointed by the reality of her father Kanayanagi, Mistuho transitioned from a believer to a figure like the Virgin Mary. She sought to eliminate other Mikumo family members to grant Kaji absolute power and bloodline legitimacy.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Muto explains to the group in the church that Mistuho’s fanatical ideals transformed Kaji into a symbolic "god." To achieve eternal brilliance and live forever in Mistuho's heart, Kaji had to undergo a "purification" through death. Muto characterizes this as a holy war committed by the mist-drawn to fulfill their faith. Following this discussion, Muto reveals that Fujiko is at the "Hell Gate." He compares her situation to the ending of the "Japanese Thrush Mystery," noting that she chose suicide to complete a narrative epic and escape divine laws.

The group enters "Hell's Gate" and finds a guillotine in the center of the room. Fujiko lies on the device, her hand gripping the knife used in ten murders. A prophecy etched beneath the guillotine is fulfilled as her severed head radiates a cold light. Despite the violence, Fujiko appears serene, having found redemption and becoming a "god."

After the case concludes, the narrator feels a sense of emptiness and returns to the central hall. He locates a hidden passage behind the tilted portrait of Ochiba. Descending a staircase and passing through an iron door, he enters a basement corridor resembling a prison. He eventually reaches a stone room containing the coffin of the deceased Domo. While sitting on the coffin and smoking, the narrator realizes a murderer is hiding behind the coffin in the corner. He addresses the silent figure, acknowledging that they performed a great job and remained unsuspected.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
The narrator confronts a silent figure, revealing that the housekeeper, Nishimura, is actually Lady Hinata, who is ninety years old. The narrator explains that the real Nishimura was placed in a coffin two years ago, allowing Hinata to assume her identity. Hinata used heavy makeup, long bangs, and her resemblance to her daughter, Tsukiyomi, to maintain this deception. Hinata had kept Tsukiyomi locked in the basement for over twenty years and orchestrated a dramatic replacement involving her daughter.

The narrator details Hinata's elaborate manipulations to frame Mistuho for the crimes. Hinata utilized a scheme involving the "Country of Origin" book series, knowing that a non-Japanese person like Mistuho would not have known that the original title of a specific book had been changed to "The Gate of Life and Death" due to the Pacific War. Hinata also poisoned Oyama with arsenic while disguised as Nishimura, and later manipulated Mitsutaka into opening Oyama's coffin to view a decapitated corpse, thereby preventing anyone from suspecting Oyama was still alive.

Furthermore, Hinata manipulated the detective Wakizaki by forging a letter from Aoi to prompt his investigation. She planted false clues, such as a foreign LP record and citron pips, to guide Wakizaki toward the false conclusion that Fujiko was the murderer. Finally, the narrator reveals that the "locked-room mystery" of the "Hell Gate" was an illusion; Hinata simply used a spare key kept in Yuki's room. The perceived impossibility of the crime was merely a result of the investigators overcomplicating a simple act of theft and murder.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
An investigator presents a detailed deduction to a hidden figure in a coffin, revealing that the daya (maid) orchestrated a series of murders to manipulate the investigation. The investigator explains that the daya maintained the appearance of a mere witness while using Sanbei to create false alibis. Specifically, regarding the Yama murder, she damaged a handrail to manipulate Inagaki and used Sanbei to misreport the timing of events, making it appear she lacked the time to kill Inagaki. She also used a makeup-covered head to deceive Mountbatten, imitating the "French Powder Mystery" to confuse the identity of the victim.

The investigator further reveals that the daya incited Kaname to claim a painting, summoned MacArthur to the location to ensure his death, and kept Yuzuha alive as a potential scapegoat to protect herself. By arranging these clues, she successfully manipulated Mochizuki into acting as her spokesperson, leading him to conclude that the "fog drawing" was the killer.

After the hidden figure finally speaks, acknowledging the reasoning, the investigator launches a second attack. She connects the killer's choice of Ellery Queen's national series to her true identity. The investigator reveals he knows her history, linking her to Perm and Manchuria. He concludes that the piano quintet "Imakagami" held by the Taima family is actually a requiem composed by Medvedev for family members killed during the Russian Revolution, implying the killer is a descendant of those victims.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
Kougetsu Joushou confronts Daya=Hana=Anna-Stasia, revealing her true identity as a Russian princess of the Romanov bloodline. He explains that she killed the composer Medvedev because he knew of her existence. Driven by a desire to hide her assimilation into Japanese culture and prevent her lineage from being viewed as "degenerate," she planned a massacre of the Kuragin family after economic liberalization reforms threatened to resurface Medvedev's work, "Imakagami," and expose her secret.

Kougetsu reveals that he is the biological grandson of Anna-Stasia and the twin brother of McArthur (Ryuujin Raijou). He explains that his mother, Tomiko Akiyoshi, was a twin who secretly gave birth to him to protect his identity. To prove his lineage, he presents a gold cross and a talisman left by his mother. He clarifies that his intention in marrying Yuzu—Medvedev's great-granddaughter—is not for the Higuchi family fortune, but out of love, despite having the legal standing to claim the inheritance himself.

Kougetsu delivers a final psychological blow, labeling Anna-Stasia a "failure" and a "puppet" rather than a god or monarch. He accuses her of being a disgrace to the Romanov name and tells her not to resist him, as it would bring shame to her descendants. After delivering this revenge for his mother's death, Kougetsu leaves the location, predicting that the broken Anna-Stasia will likely take her own life rather than lose her remaining dignity.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In 1782, Captain Roger Umpleby built a house on a headland on a small island off the coast of Loch Cardip, designed to withstand harsh sea conditions. Following his death, the property passed to distant relatives. By 1922, Arthur Umpleby renovated the Georgian-style house and transformed the island into the Captain's Flag Hotel by adding a concrete causeway, tennis courts, a terrace, and a private bay with a pontoon. The hotel underwent further expansion in 1934, adding a cocktail bar and dining room.

Hercule Poirot is currently a guest at the hotel, relaxing on the beach in a white suit and Panama hat. While lounging on the terrace, he is subjected to the monotonous rambling of Mrs. Gardner, who is knitting while her husband, Mr. Odel Gardner, rests nearby. Mrs. Gardner discusses how Mr. Kyles of Cook's Travel Agency recommended the hotel for its scenery and sanitation. She expresses her admiration for Miss Dunn of Rossmond's and reveals she knows of Poirot's reputation through Miss Robson, who spoke of his work on the Linnet Ridgeway murder case in Egypt.

Other guests present include the blunt Miss Bustard, Colonel Barron, who is watching swimsuit models, and Major Barry. When Mrs. Gardner suggests Poirot might be there for professional reasons, Poirot insists he is only there to relax. The conversation shifts to the people sunbathing on the beach, whom Poirot jokingly compares to bodies.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Colonel Barry, Miss Bustle, Mrs. Gardner, Mr. Gardner, Hercule Poirot, and the vicar, Stephen Lane, engage in a conversation regarding modern social habits and the nature of the seaside. Mrs. Gardner discusses the casual nature of modern youth and expresses her concern that her daughter, Erin, might grow excessive body hair if she sunbathes too much. Poirot compares the sight of bodies lined up to a mortuary or meat on a butcher's slab, a comparison that draws indignation from Mrs. Gardner.

As the Gardners depart for the hotel, Stephen Lane joins the group. The conversation shifts to physical activities, such as walking and rowing, and the physiological causes of seasickness and acrophobia. Miss Bustle mentions that Mrs. Redfern suffers from severe dizziness at heights. Christine Redfern joins the group after swimming, noting that her pale skin does not tan but instead develops rashes. Miss Bustle suggests that Poirot play a joke on Mrs. Gardner by telling her a murderer is staying at the hotel, but Poirot notes she would likely believe him.

The discussion turns to the likelihood of crime in such a peaceful location. While Miss Bustle believes the area is too tranquil for murder, Poirot argues that evil exists everywhere, even under the sun. He explains that the seaside provides a perfect cover for enemies to meet, as the tradition of August vacations makes the presence of any individual appear natural and inconspicuous. Stephen Lane concludes by reflecting on Poirot's words, citing the concept that the human heart is full of evil and madness.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While discussing the existence of evil, Hercule Poirot and a group of onlookers at the Pirate's Flag Hotel observe the arrival of Elena Marshall (formerly Eleanora Stewart) on the beach. Elena, a former stage actress described as having a commanding, magnetic presence, attracts the attention of almost everyone present. Patrick Redfern, a well-muscled man, is visibly drawn to her, eventually walking to her side on the beach.

The onlookers react to Elena with varying degrees of hostility and fascination. Colonel Barry recalls a similar red-haired woman in Simla, India, who caused domestic chaos. Emily Bruste labels Elena the embodiment of evil, while Christiane Redfern calls her a "beast." The vicar, Stephen Lennard, becomes agitated, insisting that Elena is a menace and the incarnation of evil. Amidst this scrutiny, Poirot notes one significant exception: Elena's husband, Marshall, a calm man reading a magazine, does not look up at her.

Later, Poirot is joined by Rosamund Darnley, a woman of exceptional beauty and owner of a famous London clothing company. Rosamund expresses her discomfort with her current location, noting she had visited two years prior. She reveals to Poirot that she has been troubled by a painful personal vision that reminds her of her past.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a conversation with Hercule Poirot, Rosamond Daneley reflects on her "too British" childhood, characterized by country life, financial hardship, and a sense of regret regarding past choices. Despite her success as an artist and businesswoman, she expresses a sense of incompleteness due to her single status, noting that she would prefer a family over her current material wealth.

Poirot observes a sudden change in Rosamond's demeanor, noting that she became preoccupied with the past following the arrival of Captain Kenneth Marshall and his family. Rosamond confirms that Marshall is a childhood friend she has not seen in fifteen years. She describes Marshall as a good but foolish man who repeatedly marries problematic women. She recounts his history of marrying Eleanor Stewart, a woman involved in several scandals: first, she was accused of murdering her husband with arsenic (though she was acquitted); second, she was a notorious third party in a high-profile divorce; and third, she was the beneficiary of Sir Roger Erskine's fortune.

Rosamond characterizes Eleanor as a "gold digger" and a "man-eater" who targets men to exploit them. She expresses concern that Eleanor is currently targeting Patrick Redfern, a man who is devoted to his wife, Christine Redfern. Rosamond believes that while Christine is a reasonable person, she will face a "rude awakening" when confronted by Eleanor's predatory nature.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Sixteen-year-old Linda Marshall experiences intense self-loathing and frustration while looking in a mirror, feeling unattractive and socially aimless. She harbors a deep, burning hatred for her stepmother, Elena, whom she views as a "beast" and a bad influence on her family. Linda observes that Elena’s presence has changed her father, Kenneth Marshall, and feels that Elena’s existence makes her life dull. Linda recalls her arrival on the island, where she met Rosamond Dunnery, a woman she respects because Rosamond treats her like a normal person. Linda imagines an ideal life where she and her father could enjoy the island's caves and beaches alongside Miss Dunnery, free from Elena's presence.

Meanwhile, Kenneth Marshall confronts Elena in her room as she prepares for an evening out. Kenneth questions whether Elena's insistence on visiting the island was motivated by her previous acquaintance with a man named Redfin. Elena claims she had no idea Redfin and his wife were coming, attributing her interest in the location to recommendations from the Larrins. Kenneth accuses Elena of wanting to "stir things up" and ruin the relationship of the young, in-love couple. Elena denies direct involvement, suggesting that while others may be attracted to her, she is not responsible for their feelings. When Kenneth asks if she admits Redfin is infatuated with her, Elena dismisses him as a fool and tells Kenneth that he should know she only loves him.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After dinner, Patrick and Christine Redfern walk to a location near "Sunshine Cliff," a series of stone recesses carved into a cliffside. Christine confronts Patrick, accusing him of choosing this specific location only because Mrs. Marshall was coming there. She claims Patrick has fallen in love with Elena Marshall and warns that Elena is a bad person who will hurt him. Patrick dismisses her concerns as jealousy and paranoia, insisting he was merely making small talk. He urges Christine to be reasonable, and they return to the hotel. Hercule Poirot, having eavesdropped on the argument from a nearby corner, tells Hastings that the signs of a coming murder were already present.

Elsewhere, on the eastern side of the island, Rosamund Darnley and Kenneth Marshall sit on the grass overlooking a bay. Rosamund reminisces about their past before abruptly suggesting that Kenneth should divorce his wife, Elena. She cites Elena's bad reputation and the fact that she does not get along well with Kenneth's daughter, Linda. Kenneth becomes angry at the suggestion, expressing his disdain for the ease with which people enter and exit marriages. He asserts that marriage is a responsibility and that he intends to honor his commitment to Elena "till death do us part." Later, while driving a narrow road back to Redfern Bay, Horace Blatt—a tall, balding man with a red face who enjoys being the center of attention—nearly hits Christine Redfern, forcing her to press herself against a mountain wall to avoid the car.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After nearly running over Christine Redfern, Mr. Blatt insists on driving her back to the Pirate Flag Inn. During the drive, Blatt complains about the dullness of the hotel guests, including a British man, a vicar, an American couple, and a foreigner with a ridiculous mustache. Christine identifies the foreigner as the detective Hercule Poirot. Upon arriving at the hotel's garage, Mr. Bret expresses suspicion toward Poirot, questioning why a famous detective is present if not investigating a case.

In Ledcari Bay, Linda Marshall browses a souvenir shop and selects a book titled "The Marriage of William Ash." She is interrupted by Christina Redman, who explains she only stopped at the shop to avoid walking the promenade with Mr. Blatt. As they walk, Linda expresses a sense of internal chaos and anxiety to Christina.

Later, in the hotel bar, Horace Bret confronts Poirot, doubting his claim that he is merely on vacation. Bret suggests Poirot belongs in French seaside resorts like Deauville. Bret discusses his own successful life and his attraction to the romantic names of the island and hotel. He mentions his past sailing and notes that Redfern is often preoccupied with Marshall's red-haired wife. Bret warns Poirot to be careful of Marshall, implying he is not as nice as he appears, but he stops speaking when Patrick Redfern enters the bar. Patrick discusses his childhood sailing and mentions rumors of a secret passage leading to a "fairy hole" or cave located behind a stone embankment on the other side of the bay.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Horace Blatt discusses Devon legends regarding fairies and goblins with Patrick Redfern. Redfern explains that local folklore suggests goblins can lead people astray, though Blatt suggests such incidents are likely due to intoxication. After Blatt departs for dinner, Redfern tells Hercule Poirot that Blatt is a hard worker who prefers solitude and is obsessed with developing the area into a city.

Poirot advises Redfern to stay away from women to maintain a peaceful life. This provokes Redfern's anger, as he defends his marriage and blames women like Mrs. Gardner and Mrs. Bruste for causing trouble. Later, Poirot finds Kristine Redfern sitting outside in the damp evening. Kristine expresses her resentment toward being pitied by others as a "poor little woman." Poirot attempts to comfort her, discussing the importance of virtue and wisdom, and assures her that her husband loves her. Kristine breaks down in tears before asking to be left alone.

As Poirot returns to the hotel, he discovers Elena Marshall and Patrick Redfern in the bushes. He overhears Redfern professing his infatuation with Marshall, while Marshall responds with soft, seemingly affectionate words. Poirot observes that Marshall's expression appears self-satisfied. Shortly after, Marshall approaches Poirot, making casual conversation about the pleasant weather.

On the morning of August 25th, Linda prepares for the day at the Pirate Flag Hotel. After a moment of intense reflection in her mirror, she dresses in a swimsuit and bathrobe. As she descends the stairs toward the sea, she encounters her father.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Early in the morning, Linda rows a small boat across a submerged causeway to reach the mainland, where she visits a grocery store to buy items, including several candles. Upon returning to her hotel room, she meets Christine Redfin. Christine invites Linda to join her at Oyster Bay at 10:30 to sketch. Linda agrees, noting a subtle sympathy between them fueled by their mutual dislike of the same woman. As Linda leaves the dining room, she encounters Rosemond Dunn, who observes that the weather has improved since the previous day. Rosemond criticizes Christine’s mismatched green-and-yellow beach outfit, comparing her unfavorably to Elaine Marshall.

Hercule Poirot leaves the hotel earlier than usual and encounters Elaine Marshall on the beach. Elaine is pushing a white raft into the water and asks Poirot not to tell anyone her location because she wants to be alone. Poirot is skeptical of her request, suspecting she is meeting someone. Shortly after the raft disappears, Patrick Redfern and Kenneth Marshall walk down the beach. Marshall asks Poirot if he has seen his wife, noting she is not in her room. Meanwhile, Patrick Redfern sits near Poirot, appearing increasingly agitated and impatient as he waits for Eleanor to appear. 

As the morning progresses, various guests like the Gardners and Miss Bustle appear on the beach. Marshall finishes a swim and mentions he must return to work. During a conversation with the Gardners, Marshall is asked about Linda's whereabouts; he claims she is likely wandering the island. The passage concludes with Patrick Redfern still sitting on the beach, looking toward the hotel in disappointment.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Brutus joins a group including Mrs. Gardner, Mr. Gardner, Hercule Poirot, and Miss Bustle. Mrs. Gardner suggests a trip to Dartmouth to visit Prince Town Prison and discusses her belief in the pyramids' prophecy and the influence of radio waves on human sanity. During the conversation, Miss Bustle mentions that someone threw a bottle from a hotel window, nearly hitting her. Mr. Gardner retrieves a light purple yarn for his wife, noting he found it in a closet rather than a dresser.

Patrick Redfern, appearing despondent and agitated, asks Miss Bruste to go rowing around the island. As they row westward past Sunlight Cliff, Patrick notices an umbrella on the cliff, which Miss Bruste identifies as belonging to Miss Dunn. Miss Bruste suspects that Patrick’s motive for rowing is to search for Mrs. Marshall, who has not appeared that morning.

As they round the southern tip of Elfin Bay, a small, shaded area, Patrick spots a figure on the beach. Despite Miss Bruste's objections, Patrick rows toward the shore. They discover Elena Marshall lying face down in the sand with her arms spread out, wearing a white backless swimsuit and a green hat. Miss Bruste realizes the scene is unnatural because the cliffs block all sunlight from reaching this part of the beach, making Elena's sunbathing pose impossible. Patrick realizes Elena is dead and, after touching her stiff body, reacts with shock.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After discovering the strangled body of Eleanor Marshall, Emily Bruster and Patrick Redfin decide not to touch the scene and wait for the police. To avoid being alone on the beach with a potential killer, Emily rows a boat to Ladekapi Bay to seek help while Redfin remains to guard the body.

At the scene, Detective Inspector Colgate and Dr. Nisden conduct an examination. Dr. Nisden determines that Eleanor was strangled by someone with a strong grip and likely caught her off guard. Based on the discovery time of approximately 11:45, Dr. Nisden estimates the time of death occurred between 10:45 and 11:40. Emily identifies the deceased as a guest from the Pirate's Flag Inn.

Police Commissioner Colonel Weston meets Hercule Poirot at the site. Poirot reveals that he was the last person to see Mrs. Marshall alive; he helped her push a raft into the water around 10:15, after which she paddled alone toward Elven Bay. Poirot also discloses that Mrs. Marshall had requested he keep their encounter a secret. The investigation considers the possibility of an affair between Mrs. Marshall and Redfin, noting that Redfin may have followed her to the area. Meanwhile, the police question Mrs. Castle, the proprietor of the Black Swan Inn, regarding the nature of the guests at her establishment.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At the Pirate's Flag Hotel, Mrs. Castle expresses concern to Colonel Weston and various inspectors regarding the reputation of her inn following the strangulation of Mrs. Marshall. She explains her methods for keeping unauthorized people off the island, noting that the property is cut off by the tide and protected by a gated jetty and impassable rocks. However, Colgate suggests that intruders could use small boats to land at a cove. Mrs. Castle identifies her staff, including George, who manages the beach and guests, and William, the gardener. She also lists the hotel employees: four maids, a barman named Henry, a cook with two assistants, and a chief waiter named Albert with three assistants.

Colonel Weston then interviews Kenneth Marshall, the second husband of the deceased Mrs. Marshall (formerly Helen Stewart, a retired actress). Marshall states he and his wife had been married for four years. He claims that while his wife's beauty and acting career likely attracted envy and jealousy from other women, he knows of no men who bore her ill will. He mentions that she had met a Mr. Redfern once at a cocktail party but is unsure about other guests. Marshall reports that the last time he saw his wife was around nine o'clock that morning; she was in her room opening mail, which she noted consisted of bills. He adds that she typically eats breakfast in bed and usually does not come downstairs until near eleven o'clock. Hercule Poirot observes that if she had appeared at ten o'clock, it would have been unusual.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation into the murder of Eleanor Marshall, Superintendent Weston questions Kenneth Marshall regarding his wife's activities and her relationship with Patrick Redfern. Marshall claims he found his wife's room empty after breakfast and subsequently searched for her on the beach. Weston notes that hotel guests observed Eleanor and Redfern becoming inseparable, a fact Marshall dismisses as gossip, claiming he does not interfere with his wife's habits. Hercule Poirot argues that understanding Eleanor's personality is essential to identifying her killer, noting that Marshall has provided very little information about her character.

When asked to account for his morning, Marshall states that after swimming, he returned to the hotel at approximately 10:40. He claims he spent the hour from 10:50 to 11:50 in his room writing emails. To support this, he offers three unstamped letters containing financial documents and lists as evidence, suggesting the sound of his typewriter might have been heard by a nearby maid. He then notes he played tennis at 12:00 with Mrs. Redfern, Miss Darnley, and Mr. Gardner.

Weston further inquires about Eleanor's inheritance. Marshall states that Eleanor had no close relatives and had not written a will, which would leave her entire estate to him. He reveals that Eleanor became a wealthy woman after receiving a bequest of approximately £50,000 from her late friend, Sir Roger Erskine. This revelation prompts Inspector Corrigan to question Marshall's assertion regarding the lack of a will.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following the departure of Kenneth Marshall, Colonel Weston, Inspector Cargraves, and Hercule Poirot discuss the suspect's behavior. Cargraves notes that Marshall’s calm and collected demeanor might make a jury distrust him, as he does not outwardly show emotion regarding his wife's murder. Poirot suggests that Marshall is "clammed up" and believes the husband knows something. Poirot cites seeing Mrs. Marshall and Patrick Redfern together at Sunlight Cliff the previous night, noting that Marshall appeared suspiciously expressionless afterward.

The group discusses Marshall's alibi, which involves the use of a typewriter. Cargraves suggests the alibi's validity depends on whether a maid in the adjacent room heard the typewriter. Chief Inspector Kogorov proposes that the killer is either a hotel guest or an outsider, noting that while Marshall is the primary suspect due to potential motives of jealousy and money, Elena Marshall likely had female enemies. However, Poirot points out that the coroner, Nisden, determined the cause of death was strangulation by a man with large, strong hands, which complicates the theory that a woman committed the crime. Inspector Cargot suggests a theory involving a jealous former lover, "X," who may have followed Elena to the island by boat.

Weston then reviews the hotel register to identify potential suspects. He eliminates Major Corn and his family, as they were on a sea trip that morning. Polo provides descriptions of other guests, such as the outgoing, middle-aged Gardners from New York.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation, Polly, Colonel Weston, Hercule Poirot, and various inspectors discuss potential suspects. Polly describes the Redfins, noting that Patrick Redfin is a skilled athlete infatuated with Eleanor Marshall, while his wife is a quiet, intelligent woman. They also discuss Horace Bratt, a wealthy and verbose man who appeared uneasy, and Reverend Stephen Lane, described as a highly strung fanatic. 

Polo reveals that Eleanor Marshall asked him to keep her morning seaside departure a secret. He observed that both Eleanor's husband and Patrick Redfin were searching for her at the beach, leading him to conclude that Eleanor went to meet a mysterious third party. Poirot argues that Eleanor, who thrives on male admiration, would not have been alone, suggesting she met someone from outside the hotel.

Colonel Weston decides to question Linda Marshall, Eleanor's teenage stepdaughter. Linda, appearing frightened, recounts her morning activities: she swam, had breakfast, and went to the bay with Mrs. Redfin. She notes that Mrs. Redfin returned to the hotel at 11:45 to prepare for tennis. When questioned about her family dynamics, Linda claims she gets along well with her stepmother, Alyona, and that her father does not argue with anyone. However, Linda suggests that Mrs. Redfin might have wanted Eleanor dead because of her husband's affair, though she maintains that Mrs. Redfin is not the type of person to commit murder. Poirot concludes the session by musing on the physical impulse of sudden, violent anger.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After questioning Linda, who provides no useful information, Colonel Weston and Hercule Poirot discuss her role in the investigation. Poirot and Weston conclude that Christine Redfin is cleared of suspicion; Poirot believes she lacks the impulsive nature and physical capability to commit the murder, while Corrigan notes that Dr. Nesden described the killer as having large, strong hands, whereas Christine has small, delicate hands.

The investigation shifts to Patrick Redfern. Under questioning by Weston and Poirot, Patrick admits he has been "madly in love" with the deceased, Mrs. Marshall, for three months. He confesses that he met her several times and that his wife, Christine, was unaware of the extent of their relationship. Patrick claims that while he was infatuated with Mrs. Marshall, he truly loves his wife. Colonel Weston suggests that the affair could provide a motive for murder, specifically that Mr. Marshall might have killed Mrs. Marshall upon discovering the relationship. Patrick denies thinking this was possible, describing Marshall as a quiet person.

Patrick reveals that he and Mrs. Marshall usually met on the beach in the morning to go out on a raft, but she did not show up this morning. He mentions that they sometimes met in the afternoon at Gull Bay to avoid crowds. When asked about other locations, Patrick notes that they avoided Fairy Cove in the mornings because it is too busy with people taking boats out, though they sometimes walked on the island after dinner. The passage concludes with Weston noting that Patrick has provided no information regarding why Mrs. Marshall went to the Fairy Cove that morning.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Colonel Weston, Superintendent Clegg, and Hercule Poirot discuss the murder of Elena Marshall. After questioning Patrick Redfern, Weston identifies three potential culprits: an unknown killer, Elena's husband, or Redfern's wife, Christine. Redfern vehemently denies Christine's involvement, arguing she lacks the physical strength to strangle a woman as strong as Elena and could not have used the ladder to descend the cliff to the beach. Weston admits that while the physical evidence is difficult, they must establish motive and opportunity. Following Redfern's departure, Weston reveals he only accused Christine to provoke a reaction from her husband, though Poirot notes Redfern's defense was as convincing as an alibi. Clegg suggests that Elena's husband might have pre-planned the crime by preparing three letters.

Miss Christine Redfern is then interviewed regarding her morning activities. She states that she visited Elena's room after 8:30 AM, at which time Elena claimed she was going swimming. Christine then had breakfast and met Elena at 10:30 AM to go to a cove on the eastern side of the island. Christine claims she spent time drawing while Elena sunbathed. At 11:45 AM, Christine left the cove to prepare for a tennis match, noting that she heard Elena splash into the water as she climbed the cliff path. Christine then played tennis with Mr. Marshall, Mr. Gardner, and Miss Darnley. When Poirot questions her reaction to the news of the death, Christine admits she was not surprised, implying she had a specific opinion of Elena's character.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Christine Redfern describes the deceased Eleanor Stewart as a useless, unintelligent parasite who was frequently involved in "dirty affairs" such as extortion and jealousy. Colonel Weston questions Redfern on her use of the word "extortion," leading her to admit that she accidentally overheard a man with a muffled, unrecognizable voice extorting Mrs. Marshall three days prior while playing bridge.

Following Redfern's departure, Inspector Cargraves, Colonel Weston, Poirot, and Detective Inspector Colgate discuss the possibility that Mrs. Marshall was meeting a blackmailer at Fairy Bay. They note that the location is ideal for a secret meeting because it is deserted, accessible only by a vertical steel ladder, and contains a hidden cave known as the Fairy Cave. Weston narrows the potential suspects staying at the hotel to Mr. Gardner, Major Barry, Mr. Horace Blatt, and Reverend Stephen Lane, noting that the latter three were out during the morning.

Colonel Weston summons the American couple, the Gardners, for questioning. Mrs. Gardner claims she and her husband were on the beach all morning. She expresses her disdain for Mrs. Marshall, calling her dim-witted and uncivilized, and suggests that Mr. Marshall should have married the intelligent and successful Rosamond Dunnely instead. She further remarks that everyone could see how much Dunnely loved Mr. Marshall.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation into the death of Mrs. Marshall, Mrs. Gardner suggests that the deceased was a strange woman and speculates that her death might lead to a reconciliation between her husband and a former childhood sweetheart. Mr. Gardner notes that Mrs. Marshall spent much of her time with a young man named Redfern, while describing Mr. Marshall as a reserved man.

Major Barry, who claims to have extensive knowledge of human nature from his military service in the Himalayas, compares the case to a past incident in Shimla where a man choked his wife during a rage. He suggests Mrs. Marshall was a woman who easily seduced men, including Redfern. When questioned about his whereabouts, Major Barry states he went to Saint-Louis to make a phone call at the post office regarding a horse bet, but claims he got lost in the alleyways on his way back. Colonel Weston and Chief Inspector Colgate discuss the possibility that Barry used a boat to travel to Elfin Cove to commit the crime and return, using the "getting lost" story as an alibi. The police decide to have Inspector Corghetti investigate Barry's vehicle route.

Later, Emily Bostwick expresses a lack of sympathy for the deceased, labeling her a "thoroughly bad woman." She claims Mrs. Marshall had a history of ruining those she was involved with, citing a cousin's connection to a scandal involving an inheritance and a young man who engaged in unsavory stock market dealings to fund his relationship with her. Bostwick concludes that Mrs. Marshall's history makes the investigation of her death straightforward.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Emily Bustard suggests to Hercule Poirot, Colonel Weston, and Inspector Cochran that Eleanor Marshall’s murder was likely committed by a former lover, arguing that the victim's own character is the best clue. Poirot agrees, noting that Eleanor is the best clue to her own murder. While Inspector Cochran suspects Bustard due to her apparent grudge, Poirot notes she was under his supervision until she went out to sea with Mr. Redfern.

Rosamond Dane, owner of the fashion company Rosamond's, provides a statement to the investigators. She explains that she was at Sunlight Cliff from 10:30 until 11:40 that morning, reading and occasionally looking at the sea, but she did not see Eleanor Marshall or any boats. Rosamond, an old acquaintance of the Marshall family, expresses that she did not like Eleanor due to her intolerant nature. When questioned about potential blackmail or affairs, Rosamond expresses surprise at the idea of blackmail but suggests that Kenneth Marshall might be too foolish to realize the extent of his wife's behavior. She concludes that the killer is likely a man because Eleanor was strangled.

Later, while searching Eleanor’s bedroom, Inspector Clegg and Colonel Weston discover a bundle of letters. Poirot examines Eleanor's extensive collection of hats and clothing. Among the items, the men find a letter from "J.N." to Eleanor. In the letter, J.N. expresses intense, mad love for her and mentions departing for China. He thanks her for a check that prevented his prosecution and expresses a desire to buy her emeralds. Inspector Clegg suggests investigating whether J.N. actually went to China, noting that his obsession with Eleanor could provide a motive for murder.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Inspector Courgette examine Kenneth Marshall's small room, which contains a desk with an open typewriter and a letter dated the 24th with a Leedham Bay Post Office postmark from that morning. Colonel Weston then leads the group to Linda Marshall's east-facing room. While Weston searches for weapons or hidden items, Poirot discovers remnants of something recently burned on the mantelpiece, including a block of wax, scraps of green paper with the number "5" and the words "… and row…", a needle, and burnt animal hair. After examining a bookshelf containing various titles, Poirot finds a small brown leather book hidden behind "The Younger Son" and "The Marriage of William Ash," which prompts him to reflect on a realization.

The group continues through the rooms of the Redfords and Rosamund Dane. Near Rosamund's room, Poirot observes a balcony and a path leading down a rock staircase to a beach, noting that one could traverse the island without entering the hotel. Weston suggests Kenneth Marshall might be involved due to his suspicious attitude, though Poirot remains cautious.

The investigation moves to the testimony of Gracie Nalarkot, a maid. She reports that Kenneth Marshall requested she clean his room quickly around 10:10, and she heard typing from his room until approximately 10:50. She arrived at Miss Darnley's room at 11:00, coinciding with the church clock striking. Gracie also notes that Linda Marshall's unusual behavior included being downstairs by 10:00, despite her habit of eating breakfast in bed. When Poirot presses Gracie for her opinion of Mrs. Marshall, she becomes uneasy, prompting Inspector Courgette to intervene.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation, Colonel Weston and Hercule Poirot question Gracie regarding Mrs. Marshall. Gracie describes Mrs. Marshall as an actress-like woman who is often rude and unpredictable. She reveals rumors of an affair between Mrs. Marshall and Mr. Redfin, noting that Mrs. Marshall seems to fear her husband, Mr. Marshall, despite his quiet nature. Regarding the morning's events, Gracie explains she delivered approximately six or seven letters to Mrs. Marshall's breakfast tray, which the woman later tore up and threw in the trash.

Poirot inquires about the cleaning of Linda Marshall's room, which Gracie cleaned around 9:15 AM after she went to breakfast. Gracie notes that Linda Marshall returned to her room at 9:45 AM and left again at 10:30 AM. Poirot also establishes that while Mr. Marshall and Mr. Redfin went swimming before breakfast, Mrs. Marshall's swimsuit remained dry.

When asked if any bottles were missing from the rooms, Gracie admits she could not tell if anything was gone from Mrs. Marshall's room due to the large number of items kept there. Poirot explains his reasoning to Weston: a bottle was recently dropped from an east-side window, nearly hitting Miss Bustard. Poirot deduces that someone threw the bottle into the sea to hide its identity rather than using a wastebasket. Finally, Gladys Nairne reports a "strange" occurrence: the sound of someone taking a bath at midday, which she noted to a colleague.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Grattan departs, Hercule Poirot and Weston discuss the significance of a bath, with Poirot noting that strangulation requires only strength and a murderous nature, leaving no blood or weapons behind. Colonel Gage then presents evidence regarding a letter typed by Mr. Marshall. Gage explains that the letter, dated the 24th, contains specific numbers from a recent correspondence and was typed on a machine matching the envelope's postmark, suggesting Marshall could not have prepared it in advance.

Rosamund Darnley then arrives to correct her previous statement, claiming she was not at Sunray Cliffs all morning. She states she returned to the hotel around 11:15 AM to retrieve sunglasses and saw Mr. Marshall typing in his room at 11:20 AM. Inspector Corelette concludes this clears Marshall, as the timeline aligns with the maid's testimony and the time of the victim's death. However, Poirot questions why Darnley volunteered this specific evidence. Detective Colonel Coghlan suggests she may be covering for a lie regarding her whereabouts, and Inspector Korgheit notes that Mrs. Gardner implied Darnley might have a motive due to her fondness for Marshall. Weston dismisses this, insisting the killer is a man.

The investigators discuss exploring Elven Cove and the fairy cave to find potential hiding spots. While Poirot declines to travel by boat due to seasickness, Mrs. Castle interrupts to report that two unidentified wealthy individuals arrived for lunch around 1:00 PM. Finally, the vicar, Mr. Stephen Lane, arrives and expresses his feeling that there is evil around them.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation, Stephen Lane discusses his views on evil with Hercule Poirot and Colonel Weston, characterizing the deceased, Eleanor Marshall, as an embodiment of evil and a "witch." Lane provides an alibi, stating he spent the day walking in Saint Pere and visiting its church, where he signed the guest book. When Poirot notes that Marshall was strangled rather than "brought down" by wickedness, Lane denies knowing who committed the act.

The group travels to Elfan Bay, where Chief Inspector Philip has collected various items from the beach on a rock, including scissors, ropes, and a fragment of a high-quality pipe. Poirot observes that Mr. Marshall had previously mentioned a missing pipe. When Poirot asks if he smokes a pipe, Stephen Lennon admits that he does.

At the beach, the Chief Constable explains that the body was found where Lennon had been standing and notes that the tide timing suggests the body arrived at 10:45. Patrick Redfern then leads the group to a narrow fissure between boulders at the foot of a cliff, which serves as an entrance to a cave. Weston, Poirot, and Lane enter the cave. While Weston finds nothing suspicious and notes the air is fresh, Poirot detects a subtle fragrance in the air that he recognizes as a perfume used by two specific people. The passage concludes with Poirot spotting something on a protruding rock above them, prompting Weston to suggest they check it.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While investigating a cave, Lane retrieves a deep green iron box labeled "sandwich" from a high rock crevice. Inspector Philip examines the contents, finding small containers for salt, pepper, and mustard. While the salt and pepper containers are filled with salt, a rectangular flat box contains a bitter, white crystalline powder that Philip identifies as a drug.

At the hotel, the police commissioner, Colonel Weston, and Hercule Poirot discuss the implications of the drug. Weston suggests the victim, Mrs. Marshall, may have been killed for stumbling upon a drug cartel, while Poirot doubts she was an addict due to her healthy appearance. Horace Blatt, a local man, arrives at the hotel and joins the discussion. Blatt claims he was sailing alone toward Plymouth at 9:45 a.m. and suggests the murder was a crime of passion. He provides character details about Marshall, noting he is a "dark horse" who has previously been involved in physical altercations.

When questioned about whom Mrs. Marshall might have been visiting at Fairy Bay, Blatt suggests Redfin, Gardner, Barry, or a clergyman, whom he describes as a hypocrite. Blatt also expresses his dissatisfaction with the unfriendly atmosphere at the local establishment, the Pirate's Flag Inn. Poirot observes that Blatt appears "tense." Meanwhile, Inspector Cargraves notes that it takes three minutes to walk from the hotel to the elevator leading to Fairy Bay, suggesting a person could reach the area quickly once out of sight of other guests.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Weston and Cargraves discuss the logistics of the crime scene, noting that an average person could traverse the path to the beach in fifteen minutes. Cargraves details the smoking habits of various individuals, noting that Brat, Marshall, and the vicar smoked pipes, while others smoked cigarettes or cigars. They also confirm that hotel staff members, including Henry, William, and George, have alibis that would prevent them from seeing anyone approaching from the dyke path. Weston mentions a new discovery: a sandwich box found in a cave.

Kenneth Marshall visits the investigators to inquire about the funeral arrangements. Inspector Coghert returns three letters to Marshall, which the investigator uses to suggest Marshall's innocence by proving his typing speed. Weston notes that Marshall was seen typing until 10:55 and was again seen by Miss Darnley at 11:20, effectively eliminating him as a primary suspect. Shortly after, the pathologist, Nisden, arrives to report that the autopsy revealed the presence of diacetylmorphine (heroin), suggesting a drug deal is involved in the case.

Following the autopsy, Marshall walks with Rosamond Dunnley. Marshall expresses feeling the weight of public scrutiny and media misinterpretation. Rosamond advises him to face the gossip head-on, though Marshall remains passive. They discuss Poirot, with Rosamond questioning the detective's competence. Later, an agitated Linda approaches them seeking news from the police. Marshall remains vague about the investigation's progress. Rosamond eventually takes Linda aside to comfort her, suggesting that Linda should try to move past the shock, noting that Linda did not like the deceased, Eleanor.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Rosamond confronts Linda, who expresses intense, troubling feelings regarding the death of Elena. Rosamond urges Linda to forget the incident and keep her thoughts to herself, insisting that a murderer must have sneaked onto the island to kill Elena. Linda reveals that her father previously married a woman who had been tried for murder, suggesting her father might not view murder as entirely wrong. Rosamond firmly denies this, asserting that the police have an unshakable alibi for Linda's father. Linda declares she will never forget the situation and runs back to her hotel.

Hercule Poirot questions Christine Redfern regarding Linda Marshall’s movements. He notes a discrepancy in Christine's previous statement about Linda going swimming. Christine admits she was surprised when Linda claimed to have been swimming because Linda was carrying a package of candles. When the packaging broke, Linda appeared uneasy and embarrassed. Poirot inquires about a bright green tear-off calendar, which Christine recalls seeing but cannot place. Poirot then shows Christine a book titled *Witchcraft and the History of Invisible Poison*, which Christine remembers seeing Linda quickly hide in a village bookstore. After confirming that Christine did not use her bathtub that morning, Poirot moves on.

Poirot approaches Mrs. Gardiner, who is distracted by a jigsaw puzzle due to the tension of the inquest. Poirot assists her by correctly placing a puzzle piece, explaining that the tip of a black cat's tail is white. Mrs. Gardiner expresses her desire to understand how Poirot solves cases, to which Poirot compares his method to assembling a mosaic where every odd-shaped piece must fit perfectly.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot continues his unofficial investigation by gathering information from various individuals. After discussing the nature of puzzles with Mrs. Gardner, Poirot visits Kenneth Marshall. Marshall, who is busy typing, reacts impolitely to the interruption. Poirot asks if Marshall took a shower after typing but before playing tennis on the day of his wife's murder; Marshall denies this, stating he only showered an hour prior.

Poirot then meets Mr. Gardner at the bar. Mr. Gardner expresses suspicion that the police are withholding information from the family and expresses a desire to take his sensitive wife away from the hotel. When Poirot asks for his opinion on the late Mrs. Marshall, Mr. Gardner calls her a "complete fool."

On the sun terrace, Rosamund Darnley approaches Poirot, noting that he is conducting his own investigation. She suggests that the murder of Eleanor Marshall was committed by a dissatisfied man from her past—a vain and sensitive person who may have followed her to the island and used the foggy weather the day before the murder to hide his arrival at Fairy Bay. Poirot counters that a murderer would likely not risk being seen crossing the hotel in daylight. He then questions Rosamund about whether she took a bath that morning, noting that no one seems to have taken a bath. Poirot also observes that Rosamund wears Jaeger No. 8 perfume, the same brand used by Mrs. Marshall. He concludes by asking Rosamund if she went into the fairy cave on the morning of the incident.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot questions Rosamond Dane regarding the crime. Rosamond suggests that Eleanor Marshall, who used Chanel No. 5, might have entered the fairy cave on the day of the crime. Poirot counters by noting that Rosamond previously claimed she did not leave the beach, yet he reveals that Kenneth Marshall saw her returning to his hotel room via a mirror above his desk.

Later, Poirot approaches Linda Marshall on the beach at Swan Bay. He challenges her polite claim that she liked her stepmother, noting her clear dislike for the deceased Elena Marshall. When Linda attributes the murder to a "ruthless madman," Poirot expresses doubt. He mentions "the candle," which causes Linda to react with terror before she flees up a path.

Meanwhile, Inspector Cornish reports to Commissioner Weston regarding Elena Marshall’s finances. Cornish has discovered evidence that Elena was being blackmailed, as her funds significantly decreased through untraceable cash payments and bearer bonds. The investigation focuses on three men: Major Barry, who had large, unexplained bank deposits attributed to horse racing; Reverend Stephen Lane, a former priest with a history of psychiatric treatment for delusions regarding "female devils" that might provide a motive for murder; and Horace Blatt, a businessman with high, unexplained income who is suspected of either extortion or drug trafficking. Finally, it is noted that Kenneth Marshall’s company is facing collapse, providing him with a motive to benefit from a £50,000 inheritance following his wife's death.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Commissioner Weston and Superintendent Corgherget discuss the investigation into Eleanor Marshall's death. Corgherget notes that a letter signed "J.N." found in her room was sent by a young man in China, a person previously mentioned by Miss Bruce. Weston considers the possibility of drug trafficking and recalls a connection to Reverend Stephen Lane, who entered a mental institution the previous Easter. Weston links this to two unsolved cases in Surrey: a young woman found near Bagshot and "The Mysterious Murder in the Wild Woods."

Meanwhile, Hercule Poirot sits on a cliff overlooking Elphiston Bay, reviewing a collection of disparate clues, including Chanel No. 5 perfume, nail clippers, a pipe fragment, a green calendar, a girl's watch, and drainage water. He seeks to connect these facts to confirm the presence of evil on the island. 

Inspector Cornthwaite approaches Poirot on the cliff to discuss unsolved strangulation cases. Corgherget reveals details regarding the death of Alice Corigan, who was found strangled in Caislin's Marsh. This case is geographically linked to the death of Nellie Parsons, a maid strangled in Marybush Wood, and both are near Whitecliff, where Mr. Lennon serves as vicar. Although Alice Corigan's husband, Edward, was initially a suspect due to her life insurance, he was exonerated by a solid alibi involving a train journey and a meeting at the Scone Tea House. The police believe the same killer murdered both women, but the perpetrator was never caught. Poirot concludes by stating his intention to use a small, inconsequential lie as a test to expose a larger pattern of deception.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses his investigative method with Inspector Cargraves and Inspector Colgate, explaining that he looks for patterns in expert crimes. He notes a similarity between the death of Alice Corey and the case of Nellie Parsons, specifically that the husbands in both instances had airtight alibis.

Later, Colonel Weston informs Poirot of his decision to hand the case over to Scotland Yard. Weston believes the investigation is linked to drug smuggling at Elves Bay, involving Horace Blatt. Weston posits that Blatt uses a boat with a red sail to transport drugs, potentially hiding them in the Fairy Cave on a rocky outcropping. He suggests the drugs might be exchanged via a sandwich box placed in a lady's swimming bag during lunch. Weston theorizes that Eleanor Marshall was killed because she witnessed Blatt hiding goods in the cave. While Poirot expresses doubt regarding the certainty of proving Blatt's guilt, he decides to take action by arranging a picnic.

Poirot persuades a hesitant group to join the outing to Dartmoor to lift their spirits, including Emily Bryce, the Redfuns, the Stephens-Lennon-Gardners, Rosamond Dane, Linda, and an enthusiastic Mr. Bratt. Major Barry refuses to attend. Although Rosamond Dunn initially intends to stay behind because Linda has a headache, Mr. Burt and Mr. Bratt insist she join the group. The party departs in three cars, with the Gardners and Poirot traveling together.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a group outing, Mrs. Gardner criticizes Mr. Bret for his intrusive behavior and for distributing unrequested photographs of the group. The party, guided by Hercule Poirot, visits a grotto and a fairy hole before walking to a riverside picnic spot. While crossing a narrow wooden bridge, Emily Bristow becomes dizzy and has to be assisted by Poirot and Patrick Redfern. The picnic provides a temporary reprieve from the atmosphere of suspicion surrounding a recent murder.

Upon returning to the hotel near Ladekapi Bay, the maid, Gloria Naraqot, informs Mrs. Redfern that Linda Marshall will not wake up. Poirot and others find Linda in her room with an odd complexion and barely audible breathing. Next to her, Poirot finds a note in student handwriting where Linda confesses to killing Elena and expresses regret. 

Dr. Nesden arrives and informs the group, including Mr. Marshall, the Redferns, and Rosamond Dane, that Linda's chances of survival are slim. The maid testifies that she saw Linda taking a bottle from Mrs. Redfern's bathroom cabinet. Mrs. Redfern admits the bottle contained her potent sleeping pills, which she had previously given to Linda. 

When Colonel Weston expresses disbelief regarding the note, Poirot challenges Christine Redfern’s alibi. Although Christine claimed to have been with Linda until 11:45, Poirot points out that her sense of time was unreliable. Christine admits that during her walk back to the hotel, she was preoccupied with thoughts of leaving the area without telling her husband, causing her to walk in a trance-like state and lose track of time.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals his investigation into the murder of Eleanor Marshall. He first presents evidence found in the room of Eleanor's stepdaughter, Linda Marshall, including melted wax, burnt hair, and a book on witchcraft. Poirot suggests Linda may have performed a superstitious ritual using a wax effigy and a needle to express a desire to kill. He notes that Linda's alibi relies on time evidence she provided herself and suggests she could have traveled from the mountain to the beach to strangle Eleanor. Poirot also notes Linda's physical strength and her mother's past murder trial.

When Kenneth Marshall insists his wife is innocent, Poirot explores other motives, including blackmail, drug trafficking, or religious fanaticism. He proposes that Kenneth might have committed the murder with an accomplice, suggesting a partner could have typed a forged letter in his room to provide an alibi. Poirot exposes that Kenneth and Rosamund Darnley lied about their whereabouts and the positioning of a typewriter to cover their actions.

Finally, Poirot turns his attention to Patrick Redfern. He argues that Eleanor Marshall went to Elfrida Bay to meet a lover, believing it to be Patrick. Poirot asserts that Patrick killed Eleanor after she emerged from a hiding spot. He reveals that the "body" seen by witnesses was actually Patrick's accomplice, Christine, who was painted black and wearing a paper hat to impersonate the corpse. Poirot identifies Patrick and Christine as Edward and Christine Corey, noting they were recognized by police from a guest photograph following their involvement in the previous murder of Alice Corey.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Patrick Redfern, driven by rage, attacks Hercule Poirot by attempting to strangle him. Following this, a narrative reflection explores the nature of evil and the characters involved in a recent crime. While Reverend Lane and others believe Elena Marshall is the source of evil, the narrator argues that Elena was merely a victim of her own perceived vulnerability and the designs of men. Elena was a woman whom men often targeted for exploitation, including her husband, Mr. Marshall, who mistakenly tried to protect her after a previous marriage involving a woman of similar traits.

The narrator identifies Patrick Redfern as the true source of evil, characterizing him as a predator who exploits women for money. Redfern likely deceived Elena by promising investment opportunities, similar to how he previously manipulated a woman named Corey. Redfern is assisted by his wife, Kristin Reedman, an actress who maintains a facade of fragility and intellectualism to hide her true nature as an energetic former physical education teacher.

The crime was a meticulously timed operation. Redfern and Reedman staged fake arguments to create a sense of domestic tension. On the day of the crime, Redfern prepared by unlocking a patio door to suggest an early swim and hiding a green Chinese-style hat to implicate Elena. He had also arranged a secret meeting with Elena at Fairy Bay, instructing her to hide in a "fairy hole" if she sensed anyone approaching.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Kristen Redfern orchestrates a complex deception to frame Linda Marshall for the murder of Elena Marshall. To create an alibi and manipulate time, Kristen tampers with Linda’s watch, setting it twenty minutes fast. She also applies brown sunscreen to her body to disguise her skin tone and later throws the empty bottle out of a window. To frame Kenneth Marshall, the conspirators had previously broken one of his pipes near Elven Bay.

During the execution of the plan, Kristen uses a white swimsuit and loose clothing to hide her tanned skin. After resetting Linda's watch to the correct time, Kristen climbs a cliff, hides her clothes, and uses a green hat with a red wig to pose as a corpse on the beach. When Patrick Redfern and Emily Brust arrive by boat, Patrick discovers the "body." Fearing the killer is nearby, Emily rows back to the bay to find the police, leaving Patrick alone.
