Kristen then destroys the hat and wig, hiding the pieces in her swimsuit before returning to the hotel to wash off the sunscreen and change into a tennis outfit. She burns the remains of the hat in Linda's fireplace alongside a calendar page to make it appear as though Linda was performing a witchcraft spell.

While the deception unfolds, Patrick Redfern goes to the fairy hole and kills Elena Marshall by clutching her neck. Hercule Poirot explains to Rosamond Dunnley that while Patrick had a motive to steal Elena's money, his presence on the beach provided an alibi. Poirot concludes that Kristen and Patrick intended to make the child, Linda, a scapegoat by exploiting her belief in magic and her guilt over a ritual she performed.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot deduces that Patrick and Kristen Redfern conspired to murder Elena Marshall. He concludes that Elena was hiding in a fairy hole, evidenced by her perfume, while a decoy—likely Kristen wearing brown sunscreen to alter her appearance—lay on the beach at Fairy Bay under a Chinese-style hat to mimic a corpse. Poirot identifies a mistake in the killer's plan: the loss of scissors used to cut an identical green hat. He notes that Patrick killed Elena by strangulation after 11:45, a time manipulated by the killers. He breaks Kristen's alibi by proving she had two opportunities to tamper with Linda Marshall's watch: once in Linda's room and again after Linda exited the water. Poirot also dismisses Kristen's claim of acrophobia as a lie. Comparing the case to the Alice Corey murder, Poirot suggests Patrick uses "playing with time" as a signature method and may be a serial killer.

In separate conversations, Poirot explains his reasoning to Mrs. Gardner and comforts Linda Marshall, who admits to feeling relief after destroying a wax figure of her stepmother. Meanwhile, Kenneth Marshall confronts Rosamond Dunn, revealing he suspected her of murder because of her past violent behavior and her attempt to provide him with a false alibi. Kenneth expresses his disdain for Elena, describing her as a pitiful, helpless woman. The tension between Kenneth and Rosamond shifts toward their relationship; Kenneth demands that Rosamond abandon her profitable clothing business to move to the countryside with him, threatening that he will not be with her otherwise. Rosamond sarcastically accepts, noting that her dream of living in the countryside is coming true.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In the early spring of 1946, Linnie "Lyn" Marchmont returns to White House, a Georgian-style home in the decaying village of Wormsley Valley, after completing her service in the Women's Royal Naval Service. While she finds comfort in the familiar surroundings and the absence of military routine, she experiences a growing sense of restlessness and dissatisfaction. Lyn observes that her family's financial situation has declined due to post-war taxes and rising costs, making her realize that their previous stability was entirely dependent on her wealthy, childless uncle, Gordon Claude.

During breakfast with her mother, Adela Marchmont, the conversation turns to the recent death of Gordon Claude in a foreign land. Lyn reflects on how Gordon had acted as a provider and protector for the entire family, including her cousin Rolly Claude. Rolly and his friend Johnny Vavasu had started a farming partnership with the implicit understanding that Gordon would provide financial support if they proved their capability. Lyn’s own future path seems set by the expectation that she will marry Rolly, a plan she has desired for years.

The family's sense of security was recently disrupted by Gordon’s unexpected marriage to a woman he met while traveling from South America to New York. Adela expresses surprise at the union, noting that Gordon had always seemed content with his familial ties. As Lyn contemplates the implications of her uncle's death and his sudden marriage, she recognizes that the financial cushion that once guaranteed their future has been compromised.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Lynn discusses the late Gordon Croud with her mother, Adela Marchmont. Adela describes Gordon's widow, Rosaline (also known as Mrs. Andeh), as a neurotic and clumsy woman who suffered from trauma following an air raid. Rosaline is currently living in Frognack with her brother, David Hunter, an Irish man whom Adela describes as rude and impolite. Adela expresses suspicion regarding Rosaline’s motives, noting that she traveled from South America during the war, leading to rumors that she was seeking a wealthy husband.

Adela reveals that Gordon’s marriage to Rosaline invalidated his 1940 will, which had previously ensured the family's financial security. Consequently, Rosaline inherited everything. This development leaves Adela in financial distress; despite Gordon's previous promises to provide for the family, Adela is facing an overdrawn bank account and mounting bills for house repairs and war damage insurance. Lynn suggests that Rosaline has a moral obligation to help, but Adela refuses to ask for favors, especially given the potential influence of Rosaline's brother.

In a separate setting in Wormsley Valley, Francis Claude, a forty-eight-year-old woman known for her ability to afford and manage servants, observes her husband, Jeremy Claude. Jeremy, sixty-three, appears unusually expressionless during their meal, a change that Francis immediately notices. A fifteen-year-old girl serves them, appearing highly sensitive to Francis's reactions.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Francis Claude, the daughter of the Earl of Trentham, married the lawyer Jeremy Claude after he used his shrewdness to settle her father's bankruptcy. While the marriage appeared successful and Francis played the role of a loyal wife and mother to their son, Anthony, the couple maintained a relationship characterized by reticence and a lack of verbal intimacy. Following the death of Anthony during his military service, Francis became more efficient and emotionally hardened.

While drinking coffee in a room overlooking their Georgian-style house's garden, Francis observed Jeremy exhibiting a characteristic habit of stroking his upper lip, a gesture indicating inner turmoil. Despite his initial denial, Jeremy eventually admitted that something was wrong, telling Francis that she had "made a bad deal." Francis initially suspected financial trouble, a concern rooted in her childhood experiences with her father's unpredictable economic status and the presence of bailiffs. She understood that for Jeremy, money represented security and identity, whereas she viewed it more cynically. As Jeremy appeared increasingly frail and sorrowful, Francis questioned whether the company was failing or if they would be forced to sell everything.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jeremy Claude confesses to his wife, Francis, that he has embezzled a trust fund and needs money quickly to prevent the crime from being discovered. He expresses deep shame, believing Francis married him for stability and a life free from scandal. However, Francis laughs at his sentimentality, explaining that she married him because she fell in love with him, specifically citing his intellectual seriousness during a dinner years ago when they were wealthy.

Francis remains calm and pragmatic. She notes that her own family has a history of covering up misdeeds—such as those of her father, her cousin Charles, and her cousin Gerald—and asserts that while she is not perfectly virtuous, she is loyal. To solve the financial crisis and prevent Jeremy from going to jail, she suggests borrowing money from Rosalind, the widow of Gordon, whom she calls "the enigmatic Rosalind."

Jeremy hesitates, explaining that the funds are intended for Rosalind's living expenses. He reveals that under the law of intestacy of 1925, if Rosalind were to die, the money would pass to Gordon's nearest relatives, which would include Jeremy, Leonel, Adela, and Morris's son, Rolly. Francis realizes that this would mean the money would eventually come to them, and a chilling realization settles over the room as she processes this possibility.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jeremy Claude and Francis discuss their financial desperation, contemplating whether they can borrow money from a younger relative. Francis expresses a ruthless determination to secure funds, even suggesting she might rob a bank.

In a separate setting, Rolly Claude and Lynn MacIntyre discuss the economic shifts following the war. Rolly notes that while farmers profited during the conflict, the era of large-scale farming is changing. He mentions that Mrs. Gordon intends to spend a large sum of money on a fur coat in London. Rolly describes Mrs. Gordon to Lynn, noting her beauty and perceived innocence, though he suggests she may appear slow due to her carefulness regarding her thick accent and literary references. He also mentions her brother, David, whom he views as potentially underhanded.

Rolly and Lynn discuss their personal lives and the "topsy-turvy" nature of post-war roles. While Rolly stayed home during the war, Lynn served in Egypt, North Africa, and Sicily, experiencing combat. The tension of their differing wartime experiences leads to a discussion about marriage. Despite Lynn feeling a sense of disappointment regarding Rolly's perceived indifference and emotional guardedness, they agree to marry in June and settle at Longshu Residence. Lynn reflects on how the war has fundamentally changed her identity.

At a party hosted by Uncle Lionel and Aunt Kathy, the guests observe the family dynamics. Dr. Claude is described as a man struggling to suppress anger, maintaining a forced politeness. His brother, Leonel Claude, is a thin man with gray hair who lacks the composure of a lawyer; he is characterized by an irritable, unpredictable nature and a passion for historical herbal research. He finds it difficult to tolerate his wife's capricious personality.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At a family gathering intended to celebrate Lynn Marchmont's return, Lynn interacts with various members of the Claude and Croud families. Aunt Kathy, a woman interested in séances and Egyptian prophecies, greets Lynn and discusses the effects of her time in Egypt. Rosalyn Croud, the widow of Gordon Croud, arrives with her brother, David Hunter. Lynn observes that Rosalyn is beautiful but dressed in a way that suggests she does not know how to wear expensive clothes. David Hunter is described as an arrogant, thin, and rude young man whom Lynn perceives as reckless and unreliable.

During the gathering, David makes contemptuous remarks about the wealth Gordon Croud spent on his modern house in Wormsley Valley, Froebank. As the group moves to a dilapidated dining room for dinner, Lynn senses a palpable, intentional hostility and malice in the atmosphere, which she interprets as a consequence of the aftermath of war. She realizes that the local population seems to harbor a deep hatred toward outsiders.

Sitting beside David, Lynn engages him in a conversation about this malice. David compares modern hostility to the tangible witchcraft of the Middle Ages and suggests that even if the family hated him and Rosalyn, they could do nothing about it. He asserts that "nothing is safe." Lynn finds herself drawn to David’s dangerous worldview. The meal is interrupted by Aunt Kathy’s attempts to discuss spiritualism, which Dr. Lionel Claude sternly dismisses as superstition.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a conversation, Lyn admits to holding a grudge against Rosalyn and others regarding "unjust wealth." David Hunter questions Lyn about her marriage to Rolly Claude, whom he calls an idiot, but Lyn defensively refuses to explain. David then recounts Rosalyn’s history, describing her as a "fool with luck." He explains that after a failed acting career and a marriage to an intellectual Nigerian official in Cape Town, Rosalyn received a life insurance payout following his death from fever. She later married Gordon Claude in New York, but he was killed by a bomb only two weeks later, leaving her with a large house, jewelry, and a substantial income. David reveals his own motive: an Irishman who fought for England and joined the Allied Commandos, he followed Rosalyn to London after hearing of her marriage and wealth.

Later, at breakfast in the English countryside, Rosalyn expresses an urgent desire to return to America, complaining about staying near the Claude family. David expresses his pleasure in seeing the Claude family suffer from jealousy and resentment, labeling them "little fleas." He claims the family hates them and suggests they might even try to poison her. While David insists they are staying in the countryside for her health and to avoid the trauma of the London bombings, Rosalyn expresses suspicion. She asks if he is staying because of a woman she encountered one night, a girl who served in the Women's Royal Naval Service.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
David Hunter denies to Rosalind that he has feelings for Lynne Marchmont, describing her as an arrogant and obnoxious girl. However, after leaving the room, David mutters to himself that Lynne's return will ruin their plans, revealing that he is intentionally taking paths to encounter her. Rosalind, meanwhile, expresses her superstitions to David, warning him of a mysterious stranger and a girl who will bring trouble.

Later, Adela Montgomery visits Rosalind to seek financial assistance. Adela is facing a crisis due to an overdrawn bank account, unpaid taxes, and house repair bills following the death of Gordon Croud. Adela had previously been hesitant to ask for help and was confused by Lynne's recent opposition to borrowing money from Mrs. Gordon. While waiting, Adela observes Rosalind's seemingly naive and childish demeanor, wondering if such behavior was an affectation that once attracted Gordon.

When Rosalind mentions David, Adela seizes the opportunity to ask for help. Despite her internal embarrassment and contempt for her own desperation, Adela explains her financial situation. Rosalind, appearing startled and innocent, offers to ask David for help, but Adela pushes for an immediate check. Rosalind locates a checkbook and writes a check for five hundred pounds. Adela accepts the check, which is written by Rosaline Croud, feeling a mixture of relief and contempt for Rosalind's perceived naivety.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Mrs. Montague leaves with a check for five hundred pounds, David confronts Rosaline about her decision to give the money away. David argues that Rosaline is wealthy due to the death of Gordon Clode, who died without a will, and warns her not to give any more money to the Claude family. He notes that Rosaline's sense of guilt is an inconvenience.

David then questions Rosaline about Lynn Marchmont's whereabouts, learning she went to Longleigh to see Rolly. Irritated by Lynn's relationship with Rolly, David intercepts her on a staircase halfway up the hill. He mocks Rolly and questions Lynn's upcoming June wedding. Lynn passionately asserts her love for Rolly, but David dismisses her feelings as an imagined ideal. During their tense encounter, David notices a woman crossing a door above Frobenk. Lynn identifies her as Francis.

David informs Lynn that her mother, Adela Marchmont, has already visited and received five hundred pounds. He further reveals that Aunt Kitty owes money to a medium and that the doctor has also come to them for funds. Distressed by the news, Lynn runs back toward the farm.

David enters the house through the French doors and finds Francis Claude speaking to Rosaline. Francis explains that Gordon's death has left Jeremy in a desperate financial situation and asks for assistance. She cites various reasons, including government regulations and mortgage loans, to justify a request for a million pounds. David, recognizing her storytelling ability, remains skeptical of her claims regarding Jeremy's economic bind.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a confrontation, David rebukes Francis and others for treating Rosalind as a "money tree." He reveals that while Rosalind can use the income from her assets, she cannot touch the principal and must pay approximately £1,906 in income tax. David accuses the group of mocking and hating Rosalind behind her back while begging her for money. Francis responds by stating she hates David for living off his sister, despite David's claim that he is protecting Rosalind from greedy people. Francis exits through a floor-to-ceiling window after issuing a vague threat.

Following the argument, David warns a terrified Rosalind that several people are eager to see her dead. He promises to protect her from threats, including the De Guise family, but urges her to be careful if he is not present. David also notes that Rosalind’s sense of conscience may complicate their future.

In a separate scene, Lynn asks Rolly for a five-hundred-pound loan to help her mother, Adela, who has borrowed money from David Hunter. Rolly refuses, citing his own financial struggles with a new tractor and government bureaucracy. He suggests that Rosalind should help the others since Gordon died without a will. Lynn expresses resentment toward David, whom she believes has Rosalind under his thumb. Meanwhile, Adela reveals she used a check from Rosalind to pay off debts to Arthur, Boddaghan, and Nabvors, expressing relief at being debt-free despite Lynn's bitterness regarding their continued reliance on Rosalind.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Lynn questions Mrs. Monte regarding a rumor that a woman might use a fake brother to manipulate a man named Gordon. Mrs. Monte explains the theory that women seeking money and position often use a man from their past, posing as a brother, to gain access to unsuspecting targets like Gordon. Lynn angrily defends the woman's character, insisting she is likable and not a manipulator.

A week later, a tall, bronze-skinned man with a backpack arrives at Wormsleychurch station and heads toward Wormsley Valley. Meanwhile, at the Long Row, Rolly Crouse is visited by Rosalyn Crouse. Unlike her usual expensive, pretentious city style, Rosalyn wears a rustic, striped country dress that highlights her Irish heritage, jet-black curls, and blue eyes. She speaks with a softer Irish accent and discusses her past, claiming she had a farm in Ireland before her career on the stage. Rolly, noting her knowledge of dairy products, finds her appearance innocent and naive, comparing her to a young calf.

Rosalyn expresses interest in the farm, but when Rolly offers her tea, she becomes alert and insists she must return home because David is returning on the 5:20 train. Rolly observes her hurrying toward Frockbank, noting her apparent fear of David, whom he perceives as the central figure of the household. As Rolly stands by the gate, a tall, broad-shouldered man in a felt hat with a backpack approaches and asks for directions to Wormsley Valley. Rolly directs him through the field toward the village and offers advice on local accommodations, suggesting Stagg or Bellis and Motley.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Rolly encounters a tall, tanned stranger with blue eyes and a beard who asks for directions to Froebel's, a white house on a hill. Rolly identifies the resident as Mrs. Gordon Clout. The stranger, who appears to have a colonial accent, departs toward the Wormsley Valley, leaving Rolly feeling a sense of familiarity regarding the man's face. Later that evening, Rolly visits the Stagg Saloon and asks the bartender, Beatrice Lippincott, about the stranger. Beatrice consults her guest ledger and identifies the man as Enock Yadon (later referred to as Enoch Yate) from Cape Town.

The following morning, David Hunter and Rosaline are having breakfast when David opens a letter addressed to him. The letter, written by Enoch Yate, claims to have news regarding Lieutenant Robert André that may shock "Mrs. Claude" (Rosaline). Yate, who is staying in Stagg, invites David to visit him that night to discuss the matter. David, unsettled by the letter's vague wording and the use of quotation marks around "Mrs. Claude," perceives the message as a potential threat. To protect Rosaline, David instructs her to pack her bags and immediately move to an apartment in London, telling her to avoid all visitors. That night, David goes to the Stagg to find Yate. Beatrice Lippincott informs him that Enock Yate is staying in Room 5 on the second floor.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Miss Beatrix Lippincott leaves her shop assistant, Lily, to arrange linens, David Hunter enters Room 5 to meet a man identifying himself as Inok Jacobson (also referred to as Enock Yatton or Judd). David observes that the man appears worn-out and elusive. During a meeting involving whiskey, the man reveals that he knew David’s sister Rosalyn’s first husband, Robert André (also referred to as Robert Andorra), very well.

The man suggests that André did not actually die in Africa as believed, but instead faked his death to manipulate local natives and reappear under a new name. He implies that if André were to reappear, it would reveal that Rosalyn’s current marriage to Gordon-Claude is bigamous, potentially invalidating her inheritance and Gordon-Claude's prenuptial agreement. The man claims André is alive but in poor health and requires expensive medical treatment.

David initially accuses the man of extortion, but the man threatens to go public with the information to the Claude family. To protect his sister and her status, David agrees to pay. After the man demands two million, David refuses, noting Rosalyn cannot touch her principal investment. The man lowers the demand to one million. David agrees to the payment on the condition that it be made in cash. He instructs the man to bring the money to Stagg on Tuesday night at nine o'clock. David leaves the room in a state of intense anger.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a rented apartment at the Sheep's Pool Court in Mayfair, David Hunter and Rosaline discuss a situation involving extortion. David expresses frustration over being blackmailed, noting that the value of emeralds Rosaline sold to Greer-Trex on Bond Street for four thousand pounds has since doubled. David fears they will be "bled dry" and expresses resentment over a "secret struggle" regarding André, who they previously believed was buried in Africa. To manage the crisis, David plans to pay five thousand pounds to Mr. Inoke Yadon on Tuesday night, intending to prevent Yadon from approaching the Klopp family. David adopts a defiant attitude, telling Rosaline to follow his instructions exactly as part of his "Enoch Yate Plan."

Meanwhile, Rolly receives a letter on extravagant stationery from Beatrice Lippincott. The letter invites him to Stag to discuss matters regarding the passing of his uncle and the handling of his estate. Rolly, who has known Beatrice since childhood and recalls rumors regarding her past, decides to visit her. At the pub, Beatrice leads Rolly into a private room. She informs him that the gentleman Rolly had previously inquired about, Mr. Yates, was visited the following evening by a Mr. Hunter. Beatrice explains that she noticed a connecting door between Room 4 and Room 5, which is usually hidden by a large wardrobe, but noted that the door was slightly open during her duties.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Beatrice informs Rolly that she overheard a significant conversation while in Room 4 at Stagg. After she recounts the details, Rolly thanks her and leaves. Realizing the information involves the entire Claude family, Rolly decides to seek legal counsel from his uncle, Jeremy Claude. He travels to Jeremy's house on High Street, choosing to wait in the study rather than join Jeremy and Francis for dinner to keep the matter confidential. While waiting, Rolly observes items in the study, including a briefcase belonging to the late Sir William Jessamy and photos of Francis, Lord Edward Trenton, and Jeremy's deceased son, Anthony. In the dining room, Francis and Jeremy discuss Rolly's visit; Francis expresses her suspicion regarding the relationship between Rolly and Lynn. When they attempt to check on Rolly, the maid Edna informs them that he has already left.

Meanwhile, Lynn Marchmont experiences growing unease and dissatisfaction. She reflects on an awkward encounter with Rolly involving a request for £500 and feels a sense of boredom regarding her relatives. During lunch, Lynn argues with her mother about the cost of hiring a gardener. Later, Lynn meets Aunt Cassie, who cryptically suggests that good news is coming and expresses concern for the mental state of Lynn's uncle, Leonard Claude. Lynn observes that Leonard has changed and suspects he may be using drugs for stimulation. Seeking clarity, Lynn decides to go for a long hike to escape the valley and contemplate her life, noting a sense of nostalgia for the order and purpose she experienced during her military service.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Linn sits alone in a grove on a hillside, contemplating her potential marriage to Rolly. While she loved him before her enlistment, she feels she has changed significantly over the last four years, whereas Rolly remains the same. She worries that marrying him and moving to Long Willow would mean a life of stagnation and a loss of freedom.

Her reflections are interrupted by David, who emerges from the bushes. David is in a hurry to catch a 9:20 train from Froebel to London to return to Rosaline. During their encounter, Lynn expresses contempt for Rosaline living in a serviced apartment, prompting a bitter outburst from David. He accuses Lynn of becoming "tame and tractable" like everyone else who seeks safety after the war. In a moment of intense emotion, David kisses Lynn and claims her, asserting that Rolly Claude is an idiot. He then departs, promising to call her once he reaches London.

Returning home, Lynn feels confused and agitated. She reflects on her mother, Adela, who has borrowed £500 and views Rolly as a "safe and reliable" choice. Lynn feels resentment toward Rolly, believing that if he truly loved her, he would have provided the £500 she needed rather than being "rationally and practically" maddening. As she waits anxiously for David's promised phone call, she struggles to reconcile her attraction to David's volatile nature with the serene, predictable image of Rolly.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Lynn Marchmont receives a phone call from her Aunt Kathy, who sounds weak and claims to have made a mess of a meeting at a college. Kathy mentions her phone is broken and she is calling from a public booth. After the call, Lynn is interrupted by another caller seeking Miss Lynn Marchmont of London, but the connection is poor and the caller tells her to hang up. Shortly after, a private call from London comes through. It is David, who speaks with urgency and excitement. He tells Lynn he intends to leave Britain entirely, explaining that his previous claims of wanting to leave were a pretense to hide his desire to stay in the Vale of Worms. He tells Lynn that they have no future together, calls himself a scoundrel, and suggests she marry a man named Rolly instead. David ends the call abruptly after cursing.

At a location called Stagg, a maid named Gladys discovers the body of a guest, Enoch Yaton, in Room 5. Yaton is lying face down with a smashed head. Gladys alerts Beatrice Lippincott and Dr. Lionel Crowd. Upon inspecting the body, Dr. Crowd becomes authoritative and instructs Beatrice to call the police. Beatrice warns Gladys against speculating that it is murder to avoid libel. Later, Superintendent Spence takes a statement from Beatrice regarding a conversation she overheard. While Beatrice hopes the death might be a suicide, Spence notes that the victim was struck on the back of the head with steel fire tongs. Spence reviews Beatrice's testimony, noting that while it contains some excitement-driven embellishments, the core of her account is sinister and intriguing.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Sergeant Spence and Chief Inspector Grieve meet with Mr. Rolly Claud at the police station to discuss the death of a man in Stagg. The man, who registered the name Enoch Yates, was found with a smashed watch face, a gold lighter engraved with initials, a gold lipstick tube, and heavy steel fire tongs with rust-brown stains.

Rolly explains that he visited the deceased after learning information from Beatrice Ripplecote regarding the status of a man named Robert Andes, who was supposedly dead in Africa but might still be alive. Rolly had first visited his uncle, the lawyer Jeremy Claude, around 8:20 PM. Deciding not to wait for the cautious lawyer, Rolly went to Stagg to confront the man himself.

Rolly arrived at the man's room around 8:30 or 8:40 PM. He attempted to imply the man was involved in blackmail, but the man remained amused. The man claimed he had information to sell and was expecting a "client" that night who would pay for proof that Robert Andes was dead. Rolly, assuming the client was David Hunter, became angry and left the room around 9:00 PM. Rolly expresses regret for his arrogant behavior, noting that the man seemed ecstatic about his upcoming meeting.

When Spence shows Rolly the steel tongs, Rolly notes there was no fire in the room during his visit. Spence reveals that medical evidence shows the victim was struck from behind and then hit from above with the fireplace tool. Rolly expresses a desire for the man to still be alive so he could have obtained the information. When Spence presents the gold lighter and the lipstick tube, Rolly admits he does not recognize them.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Spence discusses the investigation with Rolly Claude and Inspector Grieve. Rolly notes that the deceased is the only lead to finding André, making the search difficult. Spence suggests that media coverage might prompt André to emerge. After Rolly departs, Spence examines an expensive gold lighter marked with the initials "D.H." and instructs Grieve to trace it through Bond Street shops.

The investigation focuses on a broken watch found at the scene, which points to 9:10. While Grieve suggests this indicates the time of the crime, Spence expresses skepticism, noting that criminals sometimes smash watches to create false alibis. Forensic evidence places the crime between 8 pm and 11 pm. Chief Inspector Graves reports that a gardener, Edwards, saw David Hunter exiting a side door at Flambrook around 7:30 pm, contradicting the belief that Hunter was in London. Spence also considers an unknown lipstick found under a wardrobe in the Stagg hotel, noting the room had been vacant for three weeks.

Spence visits an elegant building near Shepherd's Market to investigate. After speaking with an uniformed staff member, Spence learns that both Mrs. Gordon Claude and David Hunter have been staying there since the previous Saturday. The staff member confirms they were both present the previous night and suggests checking room service records for breakfast deliveries. Spence proceeds to the fourth floor and visits apartment nine, where David Hunter admits him into the residence. There, Spence meets Rosaline Claude, who appears tense.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Spence visits David Hunter and Rosaline Claude to investigate the murder of a man at the Stagg Inn. The victim, who used the name Enoch Yates, had his head bashed in. Spence reveals that Yates claimed to be a friend of the late Robert Anderson (also referred to as Robert André or Robert Andhay) and suggested that Anderson was still alive. David, maintaining a calm and defensive demeanor, dismisses these claims as a hoax and asserts that Robert was his sister's first husband who died of blackwater fever in Africa.

When Spence questions David about his whereabouts between seven and eleven the previous night, David refuses to answer, citing a dislike of being coerced. Spence warns him that he will be called for formal questioning. To advance the investigation, Spence requests that Rosaline identify the body, noting that a witness heard the deceased mention Robert. Surprisingly, Rosaline agrees to go. David supports her decision, and Spence arranges for a Daimler rental car to transport them.

On the way to the morgue, Rosaline is visibly terrified and pale. She whispers to the sergeant that she is fearful due to a past trauma in London where everyone in her house died during an air raid, leaving her as the sole survivor. David encourages her to be brave, telling her the deceased will look peaceful.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Spencer presents a deceased man, claiming to be Enoch Yates, to Rosalyn Claude. Rosalyn denies ever knowing the man, prompting Spencer to privately suspect she is a skilled actress. Spencer informs Mr. Claud that the widow's testimony should settle the matter with the jury, though Mr. Claud remains skeptical.

Hercule Poirot, having read a newspaper report about the death of Enoch Yates in Wormsley Valley, becomes interested in the case. The report notes that forensic evidence shows the man died from skull fractures and that the police are seeking information regarding Yates, who reportedly arrived from Cape Town. Poirot recalls a conversation involving Major Porter and a recent visit from Mrs. Leonie Claude, which links the case to his previous observations.

Rowley (Rolly) Claude visits Poirot to discuss his uncle, Gordon Claude, who married a widow named Andréa Deauville before his death. Rolly suspects that Enoch Yates is actually Robert Andeh, using an alias. He notes that the name "Enoch Yates" is a literary reference to a character who returns to find his wife remarried. Rolly explains that Beatrice Lippincott overheard a conversation suggesting Andeh was in the area under an alias to avoid trouble for David Hunter. The deceased man possessed no identification documents, only personal items like clothing and a toothbrush. Rolly also mentions that David Hunter, Rosalyn Claude's brother, claimed to have met the man and given him five pounds after receiving a letter stating the man was a friend of the supposedly destitute Robert Andeh.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Rolly (Rowley) Claude discusses the identity of a deceased man with Hercule Poirot. Rolly expresses distrust toward Rosaline Clude, noting that if the dead man were André Guerin, Rosaline would have no financial motive to deny knowing him. Rolly asks Poirot to find someone who can definitively identify the body, as Guerin had no living relatives in England. Poirot uses his precise memory to recall a name from a previous encounter at a club: Captain Porter. After sending a note through his servant George to the Garter Club, Poirot arranges a meeting with Colonel Porter for that afternoon.

Poirot and Rolly meet Colonel Porter at his dilapidated home on Cromwell Road. Poirot observes that the Colonel appears to be in financial distress, noting the absence of a valuable carpet that had been recently removed. During the meeting, Poirot shows the Colonel a newspaper photograph of the deceased, identified in the press as Enock Yate. Upon inspecting the photo, Colonel Porter emphatically identifies the man as Robert Andrei.

Later, Rolly calls Lynn to invite her to Rolly's Grove, claiming to have good news. When Lynn arrives at the field, Rolly tells her that they are "on a roll" and mentions that he has been working with Hercule Poirot, whom they had previously heard of during the war.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Rory informs Lynn that he consulted Hercule Poirot, who identified a man named Potter as the best friend of Robert Andraud. Potter confirmed that the deceased man at Stagg was indeed Robert Andraud. Rory celebrates this discovery, claiming it defeats the "con artists" Hunter and his sister. He explains that because Rosalind was not married to Gordon when her first husband was alive, she cannot claim Gordon's money, meaning Rory will receive a quarter of the inheritance. Rory suggests providing Rosalind with a living allowance, though he expresses contempt for David, accusing him of being a murderer.

Rory believes David killed André to prevent blackmail and to secure the inheritance. However, Lynn counters his timeline by noting that David caught the 9:20 train to London, which she knows because he called her from there. Despite her defense, Lynn privately contemplates whether David's strange behavior and haste suggest he killed André before catching the train. She fears that David will eventually be hanged.

In a separate scene, David instructs a distressed Rosalind to remain calm and stick to their story: that the dead man was not her husband, Robert Andraud. Rosalind expresses guilt, fearing divine punishment for taking money that does not belong to them. David uses fear to manipulate her, telling her that only her admission of the truth could lead to his execution. He advises her to act "a little stupid" during questioning and informs her that he has hired a criminal lawyer, Guy Thorn, to protect her during the interrogation.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At the Chateau de Vronk, David promises Rosaline they will move to the south of France or America once their current situation concludes. He encourages her to take bromide tranquilizers prescribed by Dr. Klopp to manage her anxiety. Despite the impending interrogation, David feels no regret regarding his risky lifestyle and whispers to Rosaline that he did not kill the deceased.

An inquest is held at the grain market, presided over by Coroner Mr. Peppermarsh and Constable Spence. Attendees include the Kloes family, Mrs. Marchmont, Lynn, and a worried Major Porter. Dr. Leonine Clode testifies that the deceased, identified as Inoak Jadon from Cape Town, was found face down with a head injury caused by fire tongs. The forensic officer notes the attack was brutal, involving five to six blows to the base of the skull, some of which were post-mortem. The victim was approximately forty-five years old and in good health.

Beatrice Lippincott testifies that Jadon arrived on Friday but failed to provide his ration book, claiming he would look for it. She also recounts a conversation she overheard, which she reported to Rolle Claude. Guy Soames questions whether Jadon explicitly identified himself as Robert Underdahl, to which Beatrice replies he did not. Rolle Claude testifies that he met the deceased at 8:55 PM and that Jadon claimed he could not be proven wrong without his cooperation, laughing about Robert Andrae being alive.

David Hunter is called to testify. He denies Beatrice's version of events, claiming Jadon only asked for financial assistance and never mentioned Robert Andrae being alive. David attributes Beatrice's testimony to the errors of an eavesdropper. He denies visiting the deceased on Tuesday and asserts that his sister has become a target for parasites since inheriting her late husband's money.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an inquest, David Hunter is questioned by the coroner regarding his whereabouts on Tuesday evening. When presented with a golden lighter found near the body, Hunter claims it is his but asserts he lost it on Friday morning. Mr. Guyson suggests the coroner might have left his own lighter at the deceased's location on Saturday, but the coroner moves on.

Captain George Douglas Potter, formerly of the Royal African Rifles, takes the stand and identifies the deceased as his friend, Robert André. However, Rosaline Crowd (Mrs. Gordon-Claude) testifies that she does not know the body and insists it is not her husband, Robert André. Despite her firm denial, the jury returns a verdict of premeditated murder against David Hunter.

Following the inquest, the coroner, Superintendent Spence, the county police chief, and Hercule Poirot meet to discuss the case. The police chief notes that André had previously planned to fake his death using the name Enoch Yates. Spence expresses concern regarding the lack of concrete evidence, noting that the English countryside is deserted in the evenings, making it difficult to find witnesses. He reveals that the primary evidence against Hunter is the golden lighter found under the body, though it bears no fingerprints. Additionally, the deceased's watch stopped at 9:10, which aligns with testimony that André was expecting a client. Spence concludes that the only people with a motive are David Hunter or his sister.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Spence and Hercule Poirot discuss the motives and circumstances surrounding the death of André. Spence argues that since the victim was a stranger to the Wormsley Valley, the only people with a motive are the Claud family, who would benefit from an inheritance if Robert André were deceased, or Rosalind and David Hunter. Spence notes that David Hunter was present in the Valley on the day of the death, arriving at Wormsley Hicks station at 5:30.

The men discuss the testimony of Beatrix Lippincott, whom Poirot believes is a sincere witness. They consider Rosalind Hunter's financial situation, noting she traveled to London to sell jewelry, which Poirot suggests could be evidence of her paying a blackmailer. While Spence views this as potential evidence of murder, Poirot argues it suggests an intent to pay rather than kill. Spence characterizes the type of person capable of such a crime as someone who thrives on wartime excitement but lacks respect for human life in peacetime.

Poirot reveals his personal interest in the case, linking it to a past experience involving a wife remarrying and a blackmailer being silenced. He contends that the case is unusual because "everything is wrong." He points out the oddity of André's actions, such as sending his sister to London before visiting Inoke Yatton. Poirot also observes the dependency of the Claud family on their patriarch, Gordon Claud, questioning how the family will react now that their "tree" has fallen. He concludes by noting that Gordon's wife's testimony regarding the recognition of her husband still needs to be examined.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a cynical discussion with an inspector regarding the motives for a woman to pretend not to recognize her husband, Hercule Poirot walks through a marketplace in St. Agatha. He observes the local architecture, noting the contrast between a small Roman Catholic church and the imposing Protestant St. Mary's statue. Inside the Catholic church, Poirot encounters Rosaline Clowes, who is sobbing. 

Rosaline expresses terror, claiming that David Hunter has been taken by authorities and that people want her dead. She insists David is innocent of the murder. When Poirot questions her about the lies she has told and asks if the deceased man was her husband, Robert Andrich, Rosaline becomes defensive and suspicious. She insists the dead man does not look like her husband and refuses to describe her husband's appearance before fleeing the church. Poirot, seemingly satisfied by her reaction, leaves the church and meets Rolly Claud and Lynn Marchmont at a building called Stagg. Rolly expresses gratitude for Poirot's recent assistance, though Poirot notes that Lynn appears strained. During their conversation, it is revealed that Lynn and Rolly have been engaged for six years, though Lynn has been serving overseas in the Royal Navy Women's Service.

Despite Rolly's annoyance, Poirot announces his intention to stay in the Wormsley Valley for leisure. Lynn privately asks Poirot if he believes David Hunter is guilty, to which Poirot responds by asking for her opinion. After the couple departs, Poirot visits the home of Dr. Lionel Clowes (referred to as Dr. Lionel Croude). He is greeted by Auntie Casey, who appears melancholy and unwelcoming, eventually inviting him in to speak in a hoarse whisper.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Clout asks Hercule Poirot to keep her visit a secret from her husband, Rolly Clout, specifically regarding a private matter they discussed. She expresses her belief in a higher purpose behind events, such as the coincidence of Robert Andraud being in the Valley of Wormesley. Poirot informs her that he intends to stay in Stagg for a few days, noting he was "guided" to St. Agatha by her own guidance.

Aunt Cassie discusses the upcoming marriage of Rolly Clout and Lynn Marchmont, noting Lynn's cleverness with numbers. She expresses concern that Lynn might become restless due to Rolly's limited outlook following his time on a farm during the war, potentially leading her toward someone more adventurous like David Hunter. She quickly denies any romantic understanding between Lynn and David, noting they often argue.

Dr. Leonnel Claude joins the conversation, appearing tired and haggard. He questions Poirot's decision to stay in Stagg, suggesting Poirot feels something is wrong with the case. Poirot agrees, expressing suspicion regarding the murder weapon. He suggests that while a pair of bloody tongs was found near the body, the wound might have been caused by a different, sharper object like a brick. Dr. Claude acknowledges the possibility of medical error and preconceived notions, noting that the victim was found face down on an Axminster carpet.

Aunt Katey enters with tea, and Dr. Claude leaves the room in dissatisfaction. Aunt Cassie laments Dr. Claude's poor mental state following the war and his inability to retire due to the death of Gordon, who died without a will. She finds comfort in the spirit world and the fact that Captain Porter successfully identified the murdered man as Robert Andrich.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot enters an empty hall at an inn and observes the quiet atmosphere of the guest area. He visits room number five, which appears recently cleaned and polished, lacking its usual Axminster rug. Upon examining the room's furnishings, including a marble fireplace, Poirot wipes his finger along the right side of the fireplace surround and finds it blackened, while the left side remains clean. He also notes a window overlooking a garage roof and a narrow alley that provides a discreet shortcut.

Seeking warmth, Poirot returns to the "guest-only" room, where he encounters a formidable, elderly woman with iron-gray hair. The woman expresses xenophobic views, criticizing foreigners, the quality of the inn's food, and the changing social morals of young women. She complains about the presence of soldiers and pilots, as well as the lack of proper modesty in women's clothing.

The conversation shifts when the woman reveals she witnessed a woman in an orange scarf and makeup exiting room number five late at night, after ten o'clock. The woman claims she saw this individual laughing and chatting with a man in the room. This event occurred the night before the man was murdered. The old lady expresses her disapproval of the woman's behavior and mentions she has already complained to Miss Lippincott about such indiscretions.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot questions a woman regarding a man shouting at a girl, but she refuses to report the incident to the police and leaves in anger. Poirot then speaks with Miss Lippincott, who identifies the woman as Mrs. Ledbetter, the widow of Reverend Ledbetter. Poirot inquires about a young woman who visited the victim on Tuesday night; Miss Lippincott does not recall her, but Poirot provides a description of a woman wearing an orange scarf and heavy makeup who was seen talking to Jardine in Room 5 at 10:15 PM.

Poirot meets with Inspector Spence, who presents a piece of evidence: a deep cherry red lipstick found under a wardrobe in Room 5. Poirot notes that the shade suggests a woman with dark brown hair. Spence reveals that several women, including Rosalind Clay and Lynn Marchmont, use similar lipsticks, but he suspects another woman may be involved. 

Spence discusses David Hunter’s alibi. Hunter claims he walked a circle around White House through Dunn's Wood, Bats Hill, and Long Ridge before catching a train to London. His arrival at Shepherd's Court at 11:00 PM was confirmed by Mrs. Gordon-Claude, and he made a long-distance call to Lynn Marchmont at that time. However, Spence notes that the victim, Yatton, was alive at 9:50 PM, and the killer likely tampered with a watch to suggest a 9:20 PM death. Poirot expresses interest in the timing of Hunter's walk, the encounter in Madge Wood, and the phone call between Hunter and Marchmont. Later, Poirot visits Jeremy Claud, observing a portrait of Gordon Claud and a photograph of Lord Edward Trenton.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot meets with Jeremy Claude to discuss the possibility of a lost will belonging to Gordon Clyde. Poirot suggests that documents thought to be destroyed might be decipherable through new methods, specifically mentioning a file in a safe. Jeremy disputes this, claiming there was no safe at the Sheffield Terrace house and that Gordon kept important documents in his office. Poirot asks for permission to investigate the Air Raid Prevention Bureau records, which Jeremy grants, though he expresses skepticism regarding Poirot's success.

Francis Claude, Jeremy's wife, joins the meeting. She appears ill and expresses concern for Rosalind, who is alone at Frognack following David's arrest. When Poirot mentions Colonel Porter, Francis reacts with curiosity and suspicion. After Poirot departs, Francis and Jeremy privately admit they do not believe Poirot's stated reasons for returning to London or his theories about a new will.

Poirot travels to London to investigate the Sheffield Terrace incident. He confirms the house was destroyed and that three servants—Frederick Gems, Elizabeth Gems, and Eileen Corrigan—were killed during the air raid. Poirot intends to contact the servants' relatives for information and seeks to interview Colonel Porter regarding his observations during the air raid. However, upon arriving at Porter's house, Poirot finds a police presence. He learns from a local inspector and Chief Constable Greville that Colonel Porter has committed suicide by shooting himself with his own revolver, allegedly due to depression and the pressure of testifying in the investigation.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot investigates the second-floor room where Colonel Potter was found dead in an armchair with a gunshot wound to his right temple. Greves notes that the death was assumed to have occurred a few hours prior, though no one heard the shot. Poirot observes that the room is orderly, but finds it highly unusual that a meticulous man like Potter left no suicide note or letter. The landlady, encountered on the stairs, provides a lengthy account, claiming Potter had been depressed and pacing following a coroner's inquest in Wormsley Valley regarding the murder of a friend. She mentions he had been struggling with money and refused food.

Later, Poirot meets Francis Claude at Stagg. Francis confesses that she is related to the man posing as Inoke Yates, identifying him as her distant cousin, Charles. She reveals that her family was in desperate need of money because her husband faced financial ruin and potential imprisonment. Francis admits that she conceived a plan to blackmail David Hunter by exploiting a rumor that Rosaline Claude's first husband might still be alive, which would invalidate her inheritance. 

Francis explains that she recruited Charles to impersonate "Robert Andrae" to facilitate the scheme. While the plan initially worked and Hunter agreed to deliver money, the situation turned violent. Francis states that Charles was murdered by the "dangerous" David Hunter. Poirot concludes by questioning if Francis then manipulated Colonel Potter into identifying Charles as "Robert Andrich" to further her objectives.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an interrogation, Francis Claud denies involvement in bribing Major Potter to falsely identify the deceased as Robert Andrich (also known as André Hercule). Francis admits to wanting a share of Gordon's money through unconventional means but denies fabricating evidence to strip Rosalind of her status. Poirot informs Francis that Major Potter committed suicide that afternoon. Poirot explains that Potter, facing financial hardship and the pressure of repeating his lies under oath, chose suicide rather than facing the consequences of his false testimony.

The following morning, Poirot meets with Inspector Spence. Poirot reveals that the man claiming to be Inoke Yadon is actually Charles Trenton, a known swindler with a history of using high-status appearances to facilitate theft. They discuss the dismissal of the case against David Hunter, noting that a witness, Jimmy Pierce, saw a woman in a tweed coat and an orange scarf near a phone box on the night in question. Poirot suggests that the woman's appearance—including heavy makeup and slacks—might be a disguise used by a local resident, such as Lynn Marchmont or Madame Jeremy, to avoid recognition.

Poirot concludes that the case is fundamentally flawed because the deceased's character does not match that of André Hercule. He notes that the man in Stoke was a blackmailer, whereas André was an eccentric, chivalrous man. Poirot expresses concern regarding the behavior of David Hunter and Rosalind Claud, noting that Rosalind appears to be afraid not just for her property, but for her life.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses the Clout family inheritance with Inspector Spence, noting that because Robert André Hercule died in Africa, Rosaline Clout stands as the barrier to the family inheriting Gordon's money. Poirot suspects Rosaline, noting that despite her unstable mental state, she is physically strong and has a long life ahead of her to covet the inheritance.

After leaving the police station, Poirot is accosted by Aunt Caroline (also referred to as Aunt Emily), who laments the tragic, materialistic life of the late Colonel Porter. During a clumsy encounter where she drops a codfish and a can of golden syrup, she mentions seeing a familiar face of someone deceased while seeking change for a phone call. Nearby, Mrs. Leonine Clout joins a line at the Peacock Candy Store.

Poirot walks toward White House, reflecting on the path Charles Trenton took before his death, where he encountered Rosalind Clode. At White House, Poirot finds Lynn Mutchmont resting under an apple tree. Lynn expresses her desire for David Hunter to be found innocent, suggesting the police are prejudiced against him. Poirot clarifies that it is the jury, not the police, who hold prejudice. He reveals he has been gathering information through gossip, including the movements of a man named Jato and the unusual happiness Rosalind Clout displayed while at a farm with Rollei Clout. Lynn admits that her family has all attempted to get money from Rosaline and confesses she is uncertain about her potential marriage to Rolly Crouch, a decision complicated by David Hunter's sudden appearance in the woods.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot engages in a psychological discussion with Lynn, who claims she has changed after serving in the Women's Royal Naval Service and living abroad. Poirot disputes this, suggesting she has always been a person who seeks to escape her current circumstances. Lynn admits she has struggled with her feelings for David Hunter; while she is drawn to him, she admits she has never fully trusted him due to rumors that he was merely Rosalind's boyfriend. She notes that David's commanding officer praised his bravery, but she remains uncertain if he is responsible for the current case.

Lynn reveals that on the night of the murder, she received a call from her Aunt Kitty, who used a public phone booth because her own phone was broken. She also received a call from David, who stated he was leaving her life because he felt he was no good for her. Lynn expresses a collective sense of ill will toward Rosalind, noting that the family feels a desire for her death, though she attributes this to the harm Rosalind has caused through jealousy and resentment. Lynn describes Rosalind as looking like a ghost and being terrified at Frognack, refusing help from family members including Aunt Frances, Aunt Cassie, and Lynn herself. Lynn mentions that Rosalind seems to listen to David and prefers receiving supplies from Rolly Claude.

Poirot decides they must visit Frognack. Upon arriving at the estate, which was established by Gordon Claude, they are met by a maid. While waiting for the maid to deliver a message, Poirot observes that the living room at Frognack is filled with high-quality, tasteful items, yet lacks any personal touch or evidence of Rosalind's individual personality.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While discussing the nature of sin and luxury with Lynn Marchmont at Frognack, Hercule Poirot is interrupted by a frightened maid who reports that the lady upstairs, Rosalind, cannot be woken and feels cold. Poirot rushes to Rosalind's bedroom and finds her lying on a four-poster bed. After checking her pulse, Poirot informs Lynn that Rosalind has been dead for some time and died in her sleep.

Poirot discovers a small white box by the bedside containing three remaining pills labeled "Take one before bedtime." On a desk nearby, he finds a piece of paper with untidy handwriting in which Rosalind expresses feelings of wickedness and a need to find peace, ending the note abruptly with a dash. David Hunter, Rosalind's brother, rushes into the room and accuses Lynn and others of murdering Rosalind for her money. When Poirot suggests Rosalind may not have been murdered, David points to her healthy heart and nervous problems as reasons to doubt a natural death.

Inspector Spence arrives and proposes an alternative theory: that Rosalind may have killed a man named Yadon in a fit of madness while in the Wormsley Valley, rather than being in London as David claimed. In a subsequent discussion at the police station, Spence admits he had focused on David's alibi while overlooking Rosalind's. He suggests Rosalind might have attacked Yadon in a rage and notes her recent dazed behavior. Poirot clarifies that the pills found by Rosalind's bed were merely harmless bromide used for calming nerves.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses the death of Rosalind Claude with Inspector Spence, noting that she was murdered rather than committing suicide. Poirot reveals that a call was made from a public telephone booth at Wormsley Vicarage to the Sheepfold apartment at 10:15 PM on Tuesday, implying someone was present in the flat. He argues that the woman seen in an orange scarf was not Rosalind Claude, which shifts the motive and identity of the killer. Poirot classifies the three deaths in the case differently: he views the death of Enoch Dorrington (Charles Trenton) as an accident where no one intended to kill him, one death as a suicide, and Rosalind Claude's death as a cold, premeditated murder. He suggests there are two distinct murderers. Poirot leaves Spence to wait for a letter from overseas.

Poirot then visits Madame Claude to question her about her husband’s morphine addiction, which she explains began during the war due to overwork and neuralgia. He asks if she met anyone at the public telephone booth on the night she called Lynn Marchmont. Madame Claude recalls borrowing two pence from a woman exiting the booth. She describes the woman as looking like an actress wearing an orange scarf, noting that the woman's face seemed familiar, as if she were someone who had already passed away. Meanwhile, Lynn Marchmont decides she must go to the Longrein Residence to speak with Rolly face-to-face, feeling that a storm is approaching and that she is about to say goodbye to her current way of life.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Lynn decides to leave her current life to marry David Hunter, despite feeling reluctance about uprooting herself. Recalling David's earlier explanation that he married her in London to prevent her from hesitating, Lynn travels to Long Willow House during a storm to inform Rolly Claude of her decision.

In the kitchen, Lynn tells Rolly that she loves David and that there is no longer an "us." Rolly reacts with intense, animalistic fury, expressing resentment over his stagnant life on the farm compared to the adventures Lynn has experienced. He reveals his deep bitterness regarding his past, including watching a friend die in war and seeing a girlfriend leave for military service. Rolly declares that if Lynn will not be his, she will be no one's, and he confesses to having killed two people. He then attempts to strangle Lynn.

Hercule Poirot interrupts the assault by coughing at the door. After Rolly releases her, Poirot manages the tense situation by providing Lynn with a cold, wet handkerchief for her throat and instructing Rolly to make coffee. Rolly admits to Poirot that he was attempting to kill Lynn and confirms that he killed Charles Trenton. As David Hunter enters the room, bewildered by Lynn's condition, Poirot maintains control of the group. He instructs everyone to sit and listen as he prepares to speak about crime.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot questions the motives behind the crimes committed by the Claude family, noting that their sheltered upbringing left them unprepared for the loss of security. He suggests that the family members may have harbored murderous thoughts regarding Rosaline Claude. Rory admits that he once considered killing Rosaline when she visited his farm, noting he could have made it look like an accident, but he did not act on it.

Poirot reconstructs Rory's actual crime: a killing committed in a rage. Rory explains that after visiting his uncle Jeremy, he discovered a photograph revealing that Jeremy and Francis had attempted to defraud one of Rosaline's relatives. Furious at this betrayal, Rory returned to Stagg, confronted a man in room five, and punched him in the jaw. The man fell and hit his head on a marble hearth, dying instantly. Rory discovered a lighter with the initials "D.H." on the body, identifying the deceased as David Hunter. To cover his tracks, Rory moved the body, cleaned the scene, and tampered with the man's watch to set the time to 9:10.

Rory further reveals that he attempted to manipulate the investigation. He later found a man who had intended to provide false testimony regarding the identity of the deceased; finding the man had committed suicide instead, Rory destroyed a note intended for the coroner stating that the deceased was not Robert André Devereaux. Rory confesses his motives were to obtain money to bribe Lynn and to ensure Hunter was convicted, though he expresses confusion as to why the case against Hunter was ultimately dropped.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals that David Hunter orchestrated a complex deception to divert suspicion from himself after discovering his blackmailer, Jardine, murdered at Stagg. To create an alibi, Hunter disguised himself as a woman using his sister's clothing and makeup, then staged a performance in Room 5 to be seen by a local gossip. He further manipulated his timeline by using a public telephone to coordinate a long-distance call with his sister, Rosalind, making it appear as though he were in London.

Poirot exposes the truth behind the identity of "Rosalind Claude." The woman was actually Eileen Corrigan, an Irish maid who had survived an air raid alongside Hunter. Hunter had convinced her to impersonate his deceased sister to secure a life of wealth. When the blackmail threatened this deception and Eileen’s conscience began to cause a mental breakdown, Hunter murdered her by replacing her prescribed bromide sleeping powder with morphine. Upon Poirot presenting a photograph proving Eileen's true identity, Hunter flees the scene. Superintendent Spence arrives to confirm that Hunter has been apprehended.

In a later scene, Lynn approaches Rolly Clout at his farm. Rolly, burdened by guilt over the deaths of Jardine and Colonel Potter, expresses his belief that he is a criminal. Lynn, however, declares her love for him, dismissing his legal troubles as manslaughter and arguing that the police will remain focused on Hunter for the murders. She insists that she is "home" with him, despite the risks.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot is unsettled when his highly efficient secretary, Miss Lemon, makes three mistakes in a simple letter. This uncharacteristic error leads Miss Lemon to reveal that she has been distracted by her sister. Miss Lemon explains that her sister, a widow who previously lived in Singapore, moved to England six months ago to work as a dormitory supervisor at a girls' boarding house located at 26 Walnut Street on Chestnut Street. The house is owned by a capricious woman of Greek descent named Mrs. Nikolai.

Miss Lemon describes her sister as a practical woman who is well-suited to managing the diverse, international student population. However, the sister has become increasingly anxious because items have been going missing from the dormitory in an unusual manner. Although Miss Lemon has not contacted the police, she is troubled because the thefts do not seem to follow ordinary explanations. Miss Lemon admits she lacks the imagination to speculate on the nature of the crimes, even after consulting medical literature.

Poirot, noting that Miss Lemon's distraction is affecting her work, decides to involve himself in the matter. While he tells himself he is only intervening to ensure his secretary returns to her perfect efficiency, he is secretly motivated by a sense of boredom and curiosity regarding the unusual thefts. He references a classic Sherlock Holmes line about celery and butter to signal his engagement with the mystery.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot arranges an afternoon tea with Miss Lemon’s sister, Mrs. Haberdashery (also referred to as Mrs. Hubbard/Mrs. Harbottle), to discuss a series of unusual thefts. Mrs. Haberdashery provides a notebook containing a list of missing items, which includes an evening dress shoe, artificial jewelry, a powder box, lipstick, a stethoscope, earrings, a cigarette lighter, old flannel pants, a light bulb, a box of chocolates, a torn scarf, a canvas backpack, boric acid powder, and Epsom salt. Notably, a diamond ring—belonging to Patricia Lean—was previously found in a soup bowl belonging to Valerie Hobhouse.

Poirot finds the random nature of the list intriguing, comparing it to a memory game called "The Three-Headed Woman." He notes that while many items are of little value, the diamond ring and the stethoscope are significant. Mrs. Haberdashery explains that the ring was Patricia Lean's mother's engagement ring; its return in Valerie Hobhouse's soup dish was initially dismissed as a prank. 

Poirot begins to analyze the victims and the items. He learns that Patricia Lean is a quiet, plain, and respectable girl studying archaeology who is not wealthy and has recently quit smoking. Valerie Hobhouse is described as a clever, dark-skinned woman with a sharp tongue who works at a beauty salon called "Sabrina Goddess." Poirot observes that while some items might tempt a vain, cash-strapped girl, the stethoscope—which belongs to a pleasant young man named Mr. Bateson—suggests a thief who knows how to pawn professional equipment.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot investigates a series of odd missing or damaged items at a dormitory. He reviews a list of items that seem unlikely to be stolen, including Mr. McNab's old flannel pants, light bulbs, borax, bath salt, and recipes. However, he identifies more suspicious items: a torn canvas backpack, which likely belongs to either Leonard Betson or Colin McNaughton, and a delicate green silk scarf belonging to Valerie Hobhouse that was cut up. He also notes the disappearance of one evening shoe belonging to Sally Finch, an American Fulbright scholar. Poirot suspects the destruction of the scarf and backpack was motivated by malice.

To investigate the shoe, Poirot instructs Miss Lemon to have Mrs. Hubbard report the loss to the lost property office at Baker Street Station or the Lambeth Police Station, suggesting she claim it was lost on a train or in a taxi. Poirot also pressures Mrs. Harbottle to provide information regarding the students' personal relationships, temperaments, and potential jealousies, suspecting she is protecting someone she likes.

Later, Mrs. Habberd returns to 26 Mountnut Street, where she is greeted by Leonard "Lennie" Bateson. Lennie makes gruesome jokes about "chopping up a lovely corpse" and mentions a reaction from a girl named Sylvia. He is joined by Nigel Chapman, a lanky, long-haired young man. Before Mrs. Habberd can settle in, she is informed that Mrs. Nicolaitis wishes to see her.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At 26 Nutmeg Street, a residence for students, Lenn Bates and Valerie move through the shared living spaces. Mrs. Haberdash goes to visit Mrs. Nicolaitis in her heated living room. Mrs. Nicolaitis expresses outrage over rising food bills, accusing Mrs. Haberdash of being robbed or deceived by the Italian chef. Mrs. Haberdash defends the costs as necessary for nutritious meals and explains that she utilized discounts from Lumpson's. 

During their argument, Mrs. Nicolaitis expresses a desire to prevent Sally Finch, an American Fulbright scholar, from leaving the residence, though she admits she does not know the real reason for Sally's departure. Mrs. Nicolaitis also makes discriminatory remarks regarding non-white students and accuses Nigel Chapman of being a communist. Mrs. Haberdash defends the students and dismisses the accusations.

After leaving the confrontation, Mrs. Haberdash is approached by Elizabeth Johnston, a law student from the West Indies. Elizabeth leads Mrs. Haberdash to her top-floor room to reveal that her thesis has been ruined by spilled ink. Elizabeth notes that the ink is still wet and specifies that it is green ink, which is not her own. Elizabeth identifies Nigel Chapman as someone who uses green ink, though she expresses doubt that he is the culprit. Mrs. Haberdash promises to investigate the incident. Following this, Mrs. Haberdash heads toward the door of Sally Finch's room.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a cozy room, Mrs. Haberdash discusses the recent misfortune of Elizabeth "Black Beth" Johnston with Sally Finch. Sally, who is planning to leave the house due to a series of unsettling incidents—including disappearing shoes, a cut scarf, and a tampered backpack—expresses her suspicion that someone "not ordinary" is living in the house.

Mrs. Haberdash moves to the student common room, where she finds Valerie Hobhouse, Nigel Chapman, Patricia Lane, and a frightened girl in a raincoat named Celia. Mrs. Haberdash informs Nigel that someone used green ink to sabotage Elizabeth Johnston's essay, noting that Nigel is known to use green ink. Nigel denies the accusation, claiming Elizabeth is a role model to him, and discovers his ink bottle is nearly empty. Nigel then threatens Celia, questioning her alibi, though she claims she was at the hospital all day. Valerie suggests that the situation has escalated beyond mere pranks and requires action.

In a separate setting, Hercule Poirot examines a silver evening shoe found on Baker Street, which confirms his suspicions. Miss Lemon provides him with a letter from her sister, Mrs. Haberdash, and facilitates a phone call. During the call, Mrs. Haberdash explains that the house is currently quiet and mentions that Miss Bartlett, an explorer, recently gave a lecture. Poirot arranges to visit the house that evening to give a lecture on detective theories and criminal cases. That night, a notice is posted in the common room announcing Poirot's lecture. After dinner, Mrs. Haberdash introduces the elderly, black-haired Mr. Poirot to the students.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a dinner of vegetable soup and spaghetti, Poirot sits with Mrs. Harbottle and a young woman named Celia Austin. Celia, a pharmacist at St. Catherine's Hospital, reveals that she is not a student but is staying at the residence. She provides Poirot with a detailed list of the other residents, including Indian students Chandra Lal and Gopal Ram, Dutch student Miss Reinecke, and the Egyptian political fanatic Ahmed Ali. She also identifies Nigel Chappman, Patricia Lane, Lenn Bateson, Valerie Hobhouse, Colin McNab, Sally Finch, Genevieve Marycourt, Rene Harlow, Jean Thompson, Archibald Booth, and Elizabeth Johnston. Celia notes that Nigel tends to stir up emotions and that Lenn is easily irritated, while Colin is more light-hearted. Poirot observes that Celia appears to be in love with Colin, though Colin ignores her.

During the meal, Nigel Chappman discusses crime as a form of creative art and suggests police officers have sadistic tendencies, a comment defended by the anxious Patricia Lane. Celia mentions having danced with Nigel previously at Cambridge University during May Week.

After dinner, the group moves to the public lounge for coffee. Poirot delivers a forty-five-minute speech, using a story about a soap manufacturer in Liège who poisoned his wife to illustrate the importance of preventing murder. Following the speech, Colin McNab challenges Poirot, asking why he is really there. Poirot admits that his hostess confided troubling matters to him. This admission causes Lancelot Bates to react with anger, Nigel to smirk, and Celia to react with shock, claiming she was right.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Habert suggests that M. Poirot advise the group on recent events and recommends calling the police. This sparks an animated discussion, with Genevieve protesting the idea of involving the police. During the debate, Poirot presents Sally Finch with a pair of evening shoes, explaining that he deduced the missing shoe had been taken to Baker Street station's lost and found. He reasons that a thief would likely hide a single shoe on public transport during rush hour rather than attempting to destroy it or sell it. While Nigel and Patricia defend Nigel against accusations of the theft, Poirot observes the agitated Len Bateson and the other students. Poirot notes his delicate position as a guest and states he will only discuss the "nuisance" if the group invites him to do so. After Archibald Bletchley suggests a democratic vote, Sally Finch and Nigel urge Poirot to speak. Poirot then officially recommends that Mrs. Haberdashers or Mrs. Nicoletti call the police immediately.

Following the suggestion, Poirot is escorted to a private sitting room by Mrs. Haberdashers. She expresses anxiety and agrees with his recommendation, though she wishes he had been more discreet to avoid a scandal. The conversation is interrupted by Colin McNaughton, a graduate student in psychiatry and psychology. Colin criticizes Poirot’s methods as outdated and narrow, arguing that crime should be understood through psychological motivation rather than just legal guilt and punishment. After a brief disagreement with Mrs. Haberdashers, Colin begins to explain his perspective to Poirot, using the theft of Sally Finch's shoe as an example of a "Cinderella complex."

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a discussion regarding the psychological motives behind a series of thefts, Colin McNab uses the story of Cinderella to suggest that the perpetrator acts out of feelings of disappointment, jealousy, and inferiority, seeking recognition. He argues that the stolen items—such as powder boxes, lipsticks, and jewelry—are not sought for their value but represent a desire for attention or a manifestation of internal complexes. Poirot challenges Colin by questioning the significance of other missing items, such as a stethoscope, a recipe, and boric acid, and eventually confronts Colin about his own missing flannel pants, causing him visible unease.

The tension is interrupted when Silvia Austin enters the room. She confesses to the thefts, claiming she does not know why she did them and expressing fear that she is a bad person. Colin immediately adopts a protective, fatherly role, reassuring Silvia that she is merely "sick" and promising to take responsibility for her. He successfully pressures the group to stop involving the police. However, when Poirot presents a list of specific incidents, Silvia denies involvement in several, including the theft of the stethoscope, the spilling of ink on a paper, and the damage to a canvas bag. She admits to cutting a scarf and returning a ring, but claims she knows nothing about the backpack, light bulbs, boric acid, or bath salt.
