After Silvia and Colin leave, Mrs. Hubbard expresses relief that the matter is resolved. Poirot suggests the situation is a "modern love story" driven by psychological complexes rather than lofty ideals. While Mrs. Hubbard concludes the incident was a hoax and a waste of time, Poirot remains thoughtful and silent.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot expresses his belief that the investigation is not yet over, noting that unexplained problems remain. He asks Mrs. Hubbard to call Patricia Lane so he can examine a stolen ring. Patricia, an archaeologist in her early thirties, hands Poirot her mother's out-of-fashion diamond engagement ring. During their conversation, Patricia expresses regret regarding her relationship with her deceased parents and explains her serious nature. She defends Nigel against accusations regarding the incident involving Miss Johnston, suggesting someone used green ink to frame him. Patricia reveals she met Nigel a year ago at the Chateaux de la Loire, where she cared for him while he suffered from pneumonia. Poirot, finding Patricia's personality somewhat monotonous, takes the ring for further examination, promising to return it the next day and warning her to be careful.

The following morning, Mrs. Hubbard finds the breakfast atmosphere tense due to the discovery that Elizabeth Johnston's paper was ruined. Mr. Chandra Lal interprets the incident as an act of racial oppression, leading to a heated argument involving Gene Tomlinson, Valerie Hobhouse, and Nigel. Nigel mockingly claims responsibility for spilling the ink to protect Patricia, but Patricia clarifies that she was the one who borrowed ink from him the previous day. The argument escalates into a political debate involving the Oxford Group and the Mau Mau Uprising, prompting Nigel to leave the room in frustration. Patricia follows him outside, drawing a sneer from Valerie. Meanwhile, Genevieve, a French girl, reacts with a sudden scream after receiving a translation from Rene.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a dispute regarding a missing powder box, Colin McNaughton silences a group of arguing students by banging on a table, causing a jar of orange marmalade to shatter. Colin defends Sylvia, arguing that her actions were not stealing but the result of an emotional crisis, and that she requires sympathy. Genevieve insists the behavior was stealing, but Lenn Bateson interrupts the argument to leave for college. Other students, including Mr. Chandran, Rene, and Mr. Akibombo, also depart.

Mrs. Haberdash remains behind with her daughter, Valerie. Valerie reveals that she suspected Sylvia’s motives, suggesting Sylvia's actions were a way to get Colin's attention. Valerie also urges her mother to ensure Celia returns Genevieve's powder box to maintain peace. Celia enters the dining room in tears and attempts to compensate Mrs. Haberdash for the broken items and losses by writing a check. Because her pen is out of ink, Celia uses Nigel's green ink from a shelf to complete the transaction.

The Italian servant, Jerome, informs Mrs. Haberdash that the Countess Nicolletti is in a state of agitation. Mrs. Haberdash finds the Countess pacing her room and shouting. The Countess accuses Mrs. Haberdash of calling the police to her respectable dormitory. Mrs. Haberdash denies this, explaining that no one called the police; rather, a private detective who had been a guest for dinner the previous night had visited to give a lecture on criminology. The Countess counters by citing her past troubles with the police involving forged documents and a murder case.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Nikoleteis confronts Mrs. Haberdash, accusing her of betraying secrets to a friend and failing to manage the students' disruptive behavior. Mrs. Nikoleteis demands that a student who admitted to various wrongdoings be expelled, lamenting the ruined reputation of the student home. Mrs. Haberdash retreats to the kitchen, where Maria, appearing tense and uncooperative, refuses to make Italian seasoning and instead offers to make a pale, English-style stew.

That evening, Mrs. Haberdash attempts to restore peace by gathering the students. During dinner, Len Bateson assists Celia by escorting her into the room. The atmosphere shifts when Mr. Akiyama makes a comment about Silvia's skill at stealing, causing Sally to feel suffocated. Amidst the social tension, Colin McNaughton announces his intention to marry Silvia after his studies. While some students offer congratulations, others engage in a debate regarding marriage and age. Celia expresses her belief that the recent incident involving ink on a paper and a backpack will be resolved by the following day.

The following morning, Miss Lemon arrives late to meet Hercule Poirot, breathless and flustered. She informs him that a student named Celia has committed suicide, likely via a morphine overdose, leaving a note behind. Poirot, noting he had anticipated something might happen, instructs Miss Lemon to file his letters while he heads to Chestnut Street. Upon arriving, Jerome recognizes Poirot from the previous evening and conspiratorially informs him that Celia died in her bed. Jerome adds that she had been engaged to Mr. Collins, a tall man who smokes a pipe, just the night before. Jerome offers to notify the mistress once the police have finished their investigation.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Jerome leaves, Hercule Poirot examines the students' belongings but finds little of interest. Upstairs, Inspector Sharp questions Mrs. Habberd regarding the death of Celia, a twenty-three-year-old pharmacist at St. Catherine's Hospital who reportedly committed suicide by taking morphine. Sharp notes that a suicide note in Celia's handwriting was found, and a fingerprint on the paper confirms her identity. He suggests her death may have been driven by romantic distress involving Colin McNaught, a self-centered postgraduate student at St. Catherine's with whom she was recently engaged.

Mrs. Habberd reveals that Celia had previously been stealing small items from the house, a behavior she suggests might be kleptomania. She notes that Celia's theft was discovered after Poirot gave a lecture on stealing, which prompted Celia to confess. Although Mrs. Habberd confirms the handwriting on the note is Celia's, she expresses a growing, intuitive sense that something is wrong.

Jerome interrupts the questioning to inform Mrs. Habberd that a gentleman is waiting to see her. The man is identified as Poirot, who has a small beard. Sharp observes Poirot in the public lounge, where the two men recognize each other from a previous case at Crest Hill. Poirot claims he is there because Mrs. Habberd is the sister of his secretary, Miss Lemon, but Sharp challenges him, questioning his true motive for attending the crime lecture and investigating the mystery.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Inspector Sharp discuss the suspicious nature of the thefts attributed to Celia Austin. Poirot explains that the thefts lacked a consistent pattern and appeared to be an intentional attempt by Celia to mimic a criminal persona to attract the attention of Colin McNab, a clever psychologist with whom she was in love. Poirot suspects that someone may have manipulated Celia into adopting this behavior.

The investigation shifts when Mrs. Haberdashere arrives with a discovery that challenges the suicide theory. She notes that the suicide note was written in blue-black ink, whereas Celia had filled her pen with green ink that morning. Mrs. Haberdashere realizes the note was actually a torn fragment of a letter Celia had written the previous day. Sharp concludes that someone found the letter, tore a piece from it, and used it to falsely suggest a suicide, thereby committing murder.

Later, while having tea provided by George, Sharp informs Poirot that he intends to question the students. Poirot suggests that Mrs. Haberdashere, due to her rapport with the youth, would be a helpful resource for judging character. Sharp also mentions plans to meet the unlikable mistress of the house. Regarding the cause of death, Sharp reports that hyoscine morphine was stolen from the poison cabinet at St. Catherine's Hospital. The drug was an older, less common type stored on a top shelf, making it unlikely to be noticed immediately. Sharp notes that several people had access to the cabinet, including three pharmacists, three senior pharmacists, a laboratory assistant, and a cleaning woman.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Sharp and Poirot discuss potential suspects and visitors to the pharmacy where Celia Austin worked. Sharp notes that many people pass the pharmacy, including those visiting the chief pharmacist or the production department. Recent visitors mentioned include Patricia Lane, who met Celia at the pharmacy before a planned cinema outing, and Elizabeth Johnston, a fluent English-speaking girl with dark skin who previously inquired about jobs and medical prescriptions. Other frequent visitors include the doctor, various young men seeking aspirin, and students connected to St. Catherine's Hospital, such as Leonard Bettson, Colin McNab, and Jean Thomlinson. Sharp observes that the routine nature of these visits makes it difficult to track specific movements or suspicious inquiries regarding the poison cabinet.

Sharp explores the theory that someone may have manipulated Celia into stealing. He identifies Leonard Bettson, Nigel Chapman, and Valerie Hobhouse as students with the knowledge to suggest such an idea. Poirot suggests that while a medical student from India is preoccupied with politics, other individuals possess various motives or temperaments that could lead to murder, including Leonard Bettson, Valerie Hobhouse, Nigel Chapman, Patricia Rye, Sally Finch, Jean Thomlinson, Elizabeth Johnston, and Colin McNab.

Following his discussion with Poirot, Sharp interviews various students, including a French girl, a Dane, and an Egyptian, but finds no useful information. He then speaks with Mr. Akibombo, a young man from West Africa. Akibombo suggests the death might be a family feud and asks for Celia's hair and nails to perform a traditional method from his home country to find the truth, which Sharp declines.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Archibald departs, Inspector Sharp interrogates Nigel Chapman regarding the death of Celia Austin. Nigel, a twenty-five-year-old student of Bronze Age and Medieval History at the University of London, was born in Nagasaki. He claims to have no romantic interest in Celia, despite seeing her daily, and notes that she disliked his sense of humor. Nigel recounts that the group last saw Celia at dinner, where Colin McNaughton announced their engagement. While the group later moved to a public lounge, Nigel claims he did not personally serve Celia coffee and denies any involvement in her death or the theft of items from the house.

When questioned about green ink found on Mr. Johnston's paper, Nigel suggests someone used his ink to frame him due to "resentment" among the residents, though he becomes evasive when pressed for details. Inspector Sharp also considers the possibility that Celia’s thefts were a psychological issue or a ploy to gain Colin's attention.

The Inspector then questions Leonard Bateson, who admits to pouring coffee from a pot into a cup and placing it next to Celia, but denies adding morphine. Bateson claims he was distracted by an argument and did not see her drink it. Bateson becomes angry, insisting Celia must have poisoned herself. Sharp counters this by revealing that the suicide note was actually a piece of a letter Celia had written earlier that morning, suggesting the note was a forgery.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Sharp conducts interviews with Leonard Bateson and Valerie Hobhouse regarding the death of Celia Austin. During his questioning of Bateson, the officer explores whether Celia’s recent thefts were a calculated attempt to gain the interest of Colin McNaughton, a man obsessed with psychological abnormalities. Bateson denies having any psychological knowledge that could have influenced Celia's actions. Regarding the green ink spilled on Elizabeth Johnson's paper, Bateson suggests Nigel Chapman might be responsible because he holds grudges and may have been angered by Elizabeth's habit of correcting people. Bateson also notes that Colin McNaughton and Gene Thompson have been annoyed by Elizabeth.

Sharp then interviews Valerie Hobhouse, a long-term resident of the dormitory. Valerie describes Celia as a likable but not very bright girl. She expresses suspicion toward Silvia, noting that Silvia appeared self-satisfied when Sally Finch's shoe went missing, which disrupted Sally's plans for a party. Regarding the destruction of Elizabeth's thesis, Valerie shares her intuition that Patricia Lane might be responsible. She explains that Patricia is protective of Nigel Chapman, her love interest, and might act impulsively to defend him from suspicion. Valerie also suggests that someone might be attempting to frame Nigel. Finally, when discussing how Celia might have ingested morphine, Valerie suggests it could have been placed in Celia's coffee while it was cooling, though she notes that using powder instead of a pill would make the act even more difficult to execute unnoticed.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Hobhouse provides Inspector Sharp with an account of an evening, noting that most boys had gone out, Celia and Gene Thompson went to bed early, and she and Sally stayed up late. Following this, Sharp questions Patricia Lane regarding the destruction of Elizabeth Johnston's paper. Patricia expresses her belief that Celia was responsible and defends Nigel Chapman against suspicions that he destroyed the paper. Patricia describes Nigel as a shy, moody person whose rude and sarcastic behavior stems from an unhappy childhood and a strained relationship with his strict father following his mother's death. Sharp observes that Patricia appears to be in love with Nigel and considers whether her feelings might provide a motive for murder, though he notes that the engagement of Sylvia Austin and Colin McNaughton might rule out such motives.

Sharp then interviews Gene Thompson regarding the death of Sylvia Austin. Sharp suggests the poison used may have been obtained from St. Catherine's Hospital, where Gene visits the pharmacy to see Millicent Keller. Gene denies any involvement or contact with the poison. The conversation shifts to the possibility of theft; Gene maintains that Sylvia's actions were a simple lack of honesty rather than a psychological compulsion. Gene expresses a negative view of Colin McNaughton, labeling him an atheist and a communist, and suggests he supported Celia because of a lack of respect for property. Gene also claims that a man named Poirot was the one who found Sylvia out, a claim Sharp disputes, noting that Poirot only suggested calling the police.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation into the death of Cecilia, Inspector Sharp questions Gene Tomlinson regarding the possibility of morphine poisoning. Gene suggests that someone may have poured ink on Elizabeth Johnston's paper, though Sharp notes that the suspect denied doing so. Gene also posits that Mr. Archibald might be responsible due to jealousy.

Gene recounts a discussion from two weeks prior involving herself, Nigel Chapman, Colin McNaughton, Len Bateson, and Patricia. During this argument about methods of murder, Nigel claimed he could acquire drugs without being noticed. To prove his point, Nigel later presented three items: scopolamine tablets, digitalis tincture, and hyoscine with a St. Catherine's Hospital label. To avoid leaving evidence, the group destroyed the substances by throwing the pills and hyoscine powder into a fire and pouring the digitalis tincture into a toilet.

Inspector Sharp summons Nigel Chapman to discuss these events. Nigel admits to the wager and the illegal acquisition of drugs. He describes his first method for obtaining drugs: following a general practitioner in the countryside and stealing medicine from a parked car. He successfully stole scopolamine hydrobromide from a doctor's medicine chest. Nigel then describes his second method, which involved using Cecilia to help him. He had her write a digitalis tincture prescription to mimic a doctor's handwriting, which he then used on hotel stationery to obtain the drug from a busy London pharmacist by using a blurred signature of a remote doctor.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an interrogation with Detective Sharp, Nigel Chapman describes his third method for obtaining poison to prove a point. Nigel explains that he disguised himself as an intern doctor by wearing a white coat and a stethoscope, which he had stolen from Len Bateson and subsequently pawned. He entered Celia’s pharmacy while a new employee was working the window, successfully posing as medical staff to obtain pills. Nigel claims he used these three methods—stealing from a car, forging a prescription under the name H.R. James, and the pharmacy disguise—to acquire scopolamine hydrobromide, hyoscine hydrochloride, and digitalis tincture.

Nigel asserts that he kept the scopolamine and hyoscine in a closet drawer under his socks and eventually disposed of all the poisons by burning them and flushing them down the toilet, a plan he discussed with Pat Lean. Detective Sharp suggests the possibility of spying, noting that someone could have entered Nigel's room, replaced the poison in the bottles with something else, and returned them. Nigel admits he lives with Len Bateson and that while girls are generally not allowed in the boys' dorm, Pat Lean visits his room to mend his socks. Nigel maintains his innocence regarding the murder of Silvia, acknowledging that his word alone is insufficient. Following the interrogation, Len Bateson and Colin McNab confirm the details of Nigel's bet and the disposal of the poison. Detective Sharp then speaks privately with Colin McNab regarding the poisoning of Colin's fiancée.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Sharp questions Colin McNab regarding Celia Austin’s psychological state and their relationship. McNab attributes her behavior to an unfortunate childhood and notes that while he was subconsciously attracted to her, he does not intend to marry her soon due to financial instability. McNab believes Celia was well-liked and suggests her death may not have been due to personal grudges.

The investigation continues with Sally Finch, who expresses fear regarding the atmosphere of the house. She identifies Mrs. Nicolaites as a "cruel" figure and suggests that Mrs. Silvia may have been killed because she intended to reveal truths about someone. Sally provides a critical observation: she saw Celia leaving the house through the front door around 10:00 PM to meet someone outside, despite Celia having previously said she was going to bed. Sally notes that the male servant, Jeronimo, locks the doors at 11:00 PM, but anyone with a key could have entered before then.

Finally, Elizabeth Johnston is questioned. She doubts Celia destroyed her academic paper, suggesting Nigel Chappell is too smart to use his own ink, and posits that Westlake might know the culprit. Elizabeth reveals that on the night she died, Celia mentioned she did not take down a light bulb and expressed suspicion that someone’s passport might be forged. This mention of a forged passport prompts Sharp to reflect on past police records involving foreign students at Nutmeg Lane, including a deported West African student and a search for a mixed-race man connected to a murder.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Sharp questions Elizabeth Johnston at a dormitory, inquiring if she senses anything wrong at the location. He mentions that Sally Finch, an American, believes something is strange. Elizabeth dismisses Finch’s observations as American neurosis and paranoia. Sharp uses flattery to engage Elizabeth, noting her high intelligence compared to other residents like Nigel Chapman, Leonard Betts, and Valerie Hobhouse. Elizabeth maintains that the hostel is respectable and denies any subversive activities, suggesting that Celia’s death was a personal matter involving a romantic entanglement. After Elizabeth leaves, Sharp informs Sergeant Cobbs that he intends to return the following day with a search warrant to conduct a thorough investigation, suspecting that Elizabeth knows more than she reveals.

In a separate scene, Hercule Poirot receives a phone call from Mrs. Haberdashere. She reports that Inspector Sharp has returned to the hostel with a search warrant and that Mrs. Nicoletti is distraught. Poirot realizes he made a mistake by not requesting a chronological list of missing items earlier. Mrs. Haberdashere explains that her previous list—which included a high heel, bracelet, diamond ring, powder compact, lipstick, stethoscope, boric acid, light bulbs, and canvas backpacks—was organized by perceived importance rather than time. Poirot instructs her to write down the events in the exact order they occurred.

On Saturday morning, Inspector Sharp returns to Chestnut Street with a search warrant. He seeks to speak with Mrs. Nicolaitis, who protests the search, fearing her students will leave and cause her bankruptcy. Mrs. Nicolaitis insists the death was a suicide, while Sharp maintains it is a murder investigation.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Sharp conducts a search of Mrs. Nicoletta’s sitting room, despite her vehement objections and claims that the law does not apply to her. When Sharp attempts to search a locked cabinet, Mrs. Nicoletta refuses to provide the key, which she has hidden under her blouse, and insults the officers. Sharp orders an officer named Cob to bring a hammer, and the cabinet is forced open. Numerous empty brandy bottles roll out of the cabinet. During Mrs. Nicoletta's subsequent rage, Haberdasher cleverly replaces the bottles in their original positions.

Mrs. Harbottle attempts to comfort the agitated Mrs. Nicoletta with a sedative. Mrs. Nicoletta expresses intense anger toward the police, whom she compares to the Gestapo, and complains about the damage to her property. She dismisses the recent murder in the house, claiming "Little Silvia" actually committed suicide due to a broken heart. Mrs. Nicoletta expresses anxiety that her private cabinet has been compromised, though she remains evasive when Mrs. Harbottle asks for the true reason for her unease. Mrs. Nicoletta accuses Mrs. Harbottle of being a spy but insists she cannot leave because of the ongoing murder investigation.

Meanwhile, Hercule Poirot arrives at 26 Walnut Street and is greeted by Jeronimo. Jeronimo describes the intrusive police presence and the frustration of the staff, specifically Maria. Poirot inquires about a missing light bulb from several months prior. Jeronimo recalls that the bulbs were taken around the time the police arrived to investigate an African student who had fled to the house to avoid consequences for his involvement with a woman who cheated on men for financial support.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot follows Jerome to Mrs. Habert's room, where she provides him with a written timeline of recent events. Mrs. Habert explains that Mrs. Nicolaitis was upset by a police search that resulted in the discovery of numerous empty brandy bottles after an officer broke down her cabinet door. 

While reviewing the timeline, Poirot notes that a canvas bag was listed as the first incident, occurring before the jewelry thefts. Mrs. Habert recalls that the bag belonged to Lennon Bates, which was eventually found behind the boiler cut into pieces. This incident coincided with the police questioning an African student regarding "immoral income," an event that also occurred on the same day a lightbulb went missing from the public lounge. Jerome had previously reported that all spare bulbs were missing, forcing the use of candles.

Poirot investigates the nature of the damaged property, viewing a backpack in Colin MacNab's room that is nearly identical to Lennon's destroyed bag. He observes that the bag would require significant strength and malice to cut, rather than simple scissors. Mrs. Habert mentions that Valerie's scarf was also found cut into pieces. 

After discussing the timeline of thefts, which began around late February, Poirot examines the view from the balcony. He identifies the location of the boiler room near the coal shed and notes the layout of the house, observing that Colin MacNab's room, Nigel Chapman's room, and Lennon Bates' rooms face the back garden, while the girls' rooms are located nearby.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After discussing a backpack with Mrs. Habberd, Poirot travels to a store on the main road to investigate its origin. Although Mrs. Habberd misremembered the name as Maberry or Kyles, Poirot identifies the shop as Hicks, which sells outdoor and sports equipment. Using an exotic accent, Poirot adopts the persona of a man buying a canvas backpack for a nephew going on a hiking trip. He purchases a sturdy canvas backpack identical to the one found in Colin's room. During the transaction, the shopkeeper mentions that many students from a nearby dormitory on Chestnut Street shop there.

Upon leaving, Poirot meets Inspector Sharpe, and the two meet at a sandwich shop to discuss the investigation. Sharpe reports that while a student named Chapman had three poisons in his possession, Sharpe doubts his guilt due to his lack of ill will toward Celia Austin. Sharpe also reveals that Elizabeth Johnston was a Communist, a discovery that surprises Poirot. Additionally, Sharpe notes that Patrica Lane had a handkerchief stained with green ink in her drawer, suggesting she may have been involved in an incident involving Elizabeth Johnston's papers. Sharpe also mentions that Leonard Bateson's father is a patient at Longveil Asylum and notes that Ahmed Ali possessed sexual books and postcards. Sharpe further describes finding subversive pamphlets among Chandra Lal's belongings and a large number of empty brandy bottles in Mrs. Nikolaitis's private cabinet. Despite these findings, the search for illegal passports yielded nothing. Poirot concludes that the investigation must return to the backpack as the starting point of the events. Meanwhile, Mrs. Nikolaitis is seen ascending the stairs after an argument with Jerome and Maria, where she makes disparaging remarks about Italians.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Nicolaitis instructs Mrs. Hubbard to have a cabinet door repaired and a brighter light bulb installed in the hallway, insisting the police cover the repair costs. Mrs. Nicolaitis expresses fear that someone is following her, though Mrs. Hubbard notes her employer has a habit of exaggerating. After Mrs. Nicolaitis leaves the house and walks down Walnut Street, she enters "The Queen's Necklace" pub. While drinking brandy, she broods over the police discovering her "secret stash" and worries that Girolamo and others will spread this information. After being interrupted by an acquaintance, she leaves the pub and attempts to walk home. Feeling dizzy from the alcohol, she stumbles near a mailbox and collapses. A clerk alerts Officer Bote, who finds her on the ground and concludes she is merely drunk.

Meanwhile, Hercule Poirot examines four canvas backpacks, including one purchased from Mr. Hicks. Using a paring knife, he slices them open to inspect their construction, noting that the Hicks backpack contains a wavy hardboard lining. Poirot calls Inspector Sharpe to request the specific dates and times of past police investigations on Chestnut Street. Sharpe provides three instances: an investigation into seditious pamphlets on December 18th, an investigation into Montague Jones on February 24th, and an investigation into William Robinson on March 6th. Poirot concludes the call and reviews a revised list of items provided by Mrs. Habberton, which includes various personal belongings such as a stethoscope, a diamond ring, and boric acid.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot visits Valerie Hobhouse at 26 Walnut Street on a Sunday to discuss the investigation. Jerome leads Poirot to Valerie’s spacious, tastefully decorated room, which contains furniture Poirot notes is not original to the residence. Valerie, a buyer and stakeholder for a beauty salon called Sedna Goddess who travels frequently to Europe, appears tired and anxious. She mentions having been questioned by Inspector Sharp the previous day.

During their conversation, Poirot reveals that he has borrowed the engagement ring stolen from Patricia Rene to have it examined by a jeweler. He discloses a significant discovery: the large gemstone in the ring is not a diamond, but a white zircon. While Valerie suggests that Patricia may have replaced a lost diamond with a cheaper stone due to financial reasons, Poirot proposes a different theory. He suggests that the diamond was deliberately removed and replaced with a zircon before the ring was returned.

Poirot accuses Valerie of stealing the diamond. He explains his reasoning by analyzing how the ring was "discovered" in her soup bowl. He notes that the soup was brought to the table covered with a lid, meaning only the person serving the food or the person eating it could have placed the ring inside. Poirot concludes that Jerome, the server, did not do it, and instead accuses Valerie of staging the discovery of the ring in her own bowl for her own amusement and sense of humor, thereby exposing her involvement.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot confronts Valerie regarding the theft incident involving Miss Silvia. He deduces that Valerie orchestrated the theft—which involved Silvia stealing items and tearing a silk scarf—to help Silvia gain the attention of Colin McNab. Valerie admits to this, explaining her motive was a mix of "stupid goodwill" toward Silvia and a desire to tease the conceited Colin. Valerie further confesses that when Silvia stole a genuine ring belonging to Patrice, Valerie took it to prevent trouble. Driven by a gambling addiction and debts from a club in Mayfair, Valerie admits she replaced the diamond in Patrice's ring with a zirconia and sold the original diamond. Poirot advises Valerie to confess her actions to Patricia and return the diamond.

The conversation is interrupted by Mrs. Haberdash, who announces that Mrs. Nicoletti has been found dead on the street. Although she was thought to be drunk, she is deceased, and an autopsy is scheduled for Tuesday. Mrs. Haberdash notes that Mrs. Nicoletti had recently become unpredictable and expressed feelings of being unsafe.

Later, at Scotland Yard, Poirot meets with Inspectors Wilding, Bell, and Sharpe. Inspector Japp joins the meeting to discuss the broader context of the investigation. Japp reveals that there has been a significant influx of narcotics, primarily heroin and some cocaine, into the country over the last eighteen months, managed by smuggling rings with bases across Europe.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses a smuggling operation with Inspector Waddington and a Bell Inspector, dividing the problem into drug distribution, methods of entry into the country, and the identity of the mastermind. The Bell Inspector notes that while drug distribution channels include nightclubs, pharmacies, and various service providers, the authorities are struggling to track the increasing rate of drug entry. Smuggling of precious stones from South Africa, Australia, and the Far East is also a concern.

Poirot theorizes that a flaw in the shipping process allows for the transport of high-value, small-sized items like heroin, cocaine, or gems. He suggests that the smugglers use unwitting carriers, specifically students, to avoid suspicion. Poirot explains a specific scheme involving high-quality, durable backpacks sold on the market. These bags feature a slight difference in stitching at the bottom and a thick, removable lining used to hide contraband. A group, potentially led by a student or someone posing as one, uses these bags to smuggle goods across the English Channel. The student replaces the tampered bag or lining upon returning to Britain to avoid customs detection.

Poirot links this scheme to Walnut Street, noting that the backpacks there were suspiciously cheap. He deduces that a backpack was cut into pieces and thrown into a boiler room garbage bin because a smuggler, fearing police surveillance following an unrelated visit to a dorm supervisor, felt compelled to destroy the evidence. Poirot believes the act of cutting the bag was a desperate attempt to prevent the bag from being traced back to illegal substances.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot suggests to Inspector Waddington, Constable Bell, and Superintendent Sharpe that a smuggler may have stolen light bulbs from a hall to avoid being recognized in bright light, forcing the use of candles. This theory leads the group to discuss connections between various student clubs and societies. Superintendent Sharpe identifies Mrs. Nicolaitis, the Mountbatten Street manager, as a person who ran several such clubs. Poirot notes that while Mrs. Nicolaitis provided financial support, she likely used upright individuals, such as his friend Mrs. Haberdashere, to manage the locations, suggesting Nicolaitis may have been a puppet rather than the mastermind.

Sharpe reveals that the police are investigating Nicolaitis's background and finances, noting she was a forceful woman. It is disclosed that Nicolaitis is dead, and the police suspect murder rather than natural causes, pending an autopsy. Poirot speculates that Silvia Austin may have witnessed significant events—such as the use of fake passports, the destruction of a backpack, or the removal of a light bulb—without realizing their importance. Sharpe suggests Nicolaitis may have been killed because her alcoholism made her unstable and likely to testify against her accomplices.

Later, at a breakfast table, Nigel, Lenn Bateson, Gene Thomlinson, Renee, Ahmed Ali, Mr. Akibumbo, Valerie, Colin McNabb, Patricia, and Sally Finch discuss the situation. Mr. Akibumbo defends religious items found in his room during a police search. The group debates the cause of Nicolaitis's death; while Gene suggests alcohol poisoning and others mention her alcoholism and the brandy bottles found in her room, Colin McNabb notes he saw her enter "The Queen's Necklace" on Saturday night. The conversation concludes with speculation regarding whether she was murdered for money.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an outdoor lunch in Regent's Park, Archibald tells Sally Finch that he has been distracted by the events on Chestnut Street. He expresses interest in boric acid, noting that Mr. Chandra Lal previously used a white powder of it to wash his eyes from a small white bottle that had gone missing.

In a separate conversation, Gene asks Valerie for advice regarding a matter of conscience. Gene reveals that he accidentally rummaged through Nigel's briefcase a few weeks prior and discovered a passport with a name like Stanford or Stanley, though the photo was of Nigel. Gene suspects Nigel has a double identity and wonders if Nigel killed Celia because she discovered the fake passport. Valerie dismisses this, explaining that Pat told her Nigel had signed a contract to change his name for money. Offended by Valerie's skepticism, Gene leaves. Afterward, Valerie tells Sally that she believes Gene is terrible and suspects she might be a murderer. Valerie also expresses her belief that Mrs. Nick was likely killed and suggests there is a "nutcase" among them.

Later, Pat approaches a frantic Nigel, who is searching for lost notes. Pat confesses that while mending his socks, she found a bottle of morphine in a drawer. Nigel claims he had previously told her he obtained it from a hospital and intended to dispose of it after a certain bet, noting he had not yet obtained a "third thing."

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Pat reveals to Nigel that she replaced the poison in a small bottle with baking soda to make it look identical, hiding the actual poison in her handkerchief drawer for safety. She intended to return the poison to the hospital via Silvia once Nigel won a bet. However, Pat confesses that the poison has disappeared; she noticed it was missing even before Silvia died. Nigel admits that he was the one who originally stole the poison to impress others, and he fears being held responsible for Silvia's death.

Nigel conducts a frantic search of Pat's room, looking through her clothing and drawers for the "baking soda." During the search, Sally Finch enters the room. Sally borrows a stamp from Pat's desk to mail a letter, which Pat had actually written to Nigel's father. Nigel discovers the letter and tears it into pieces, expressing intense anger.

Pat attempts to reconcile Nigel with his father, the renowned chemist Arthur Stanley, who is reported to be in critical condition. Nigel reveals his motive for the hatred: he believes his father murdered his mother by intentionally giving her an overdose of barbiturates so he could marry another woman. Nigel explains that he changed his name and left to avoid his father after revealing he knew the truth. He asserts that his father's mistress left him because she discovered his crimes.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a common room, Genavieve announces to a group of students that she is certain Nigel Chapman killed Silvia. She claims she overheard Nigel in Patricia's room explaining that his father was a murderer and why he changed his name. Other students, including Mr. Chandra Lal, Gene, and Ahmed Ali, speculate on Nigel's character and motives, while Leonard Betson reacts angrily to their theories.

Meanwhile, at a police station, Nigel provides a statement to Inspector Sharp. Nigel explains that Patricia Lane is in a state of mental chaos and cannot remember when she last saw a morphine bottle. During their conversation, a constable receives a call from Patricia. She sounds agitated and claims to know who took the bottle from her handkerchief drawer, but the line goes dead before she can name the person.

Inspector Sharp and Nigel travel to 26 Walnut Street. Upon entering Patricia's room, they discover Patricia Ryan dead on the floor. Sharp determines she was killed by a blow to the back of the head with a marble paperweight wrapped in a wool sock. Nigel identifies the paperweight as a "Luzern Lion" and becomes distraught, noting the sock belonged to him.

Sharp begins questioning Jeremiah to establish a timeline. Jeremiah states that between 6:00 and 6:35, Nigel, Mrs. Habberd, and Valerie Hobhouse were absent. He notes that Nigel left at 6:00 looking agitated and Valerie left at 6:00 dressed semi-formally. Miss Sally returned after 6:00 to post a letter. Sharp notes that Patricia's call occurred at 6:08, leaving Nigel, Valerie, and Mrs. Habberd as the primary individuals who cannot be accounted for.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In Mrs. Haber's room, Inspector Sharp and Chief Constable Cobbe question Mrs. Haber regarding the death of Patricia Lane. Sharp theorizes that Patricia attempted to call the police from the common room around 6:08 PM but stopped when she recognized someone entering. Sharp suggests the killer, whom he believes is a woman due to the familiarity required to enter Patricia's bedroom and the likelihood of a female eavesdropper, followed Patricia to her room and engaged her in conversation. Mrs. Haber notes that the house walls have poor sound insulation, mentioning that Gene often eavesdrops and that Ginny Tomlinson overheard Nigel Chapman tell Patricia that his father killed his mother. The potential suspects include Ginny, who lives in the adjacent room, and Elizabeth Johnston, who lives on the other side of Patricia's room behind a thin partition wall. Sharp reveals he discovered two hairs between Patricia's fingers.

The investigation is interrupted by Archibald Bum (also referred to as Arkady Bogomolov or Akibumbo), who reports a medical incident. He explains that a week prior, he felt indigestion and asked Elizabeth Johnston for baking soda. Elizabeth informed him that there was some in Patricia's drawer and went upstairs to retrieve it. Archibald took the small bottle and swallowed what he believed to be a spoonful of baking soda. He subsequently suffered intense stomach pain. After consulting a chemist, he discovered that the substance was not baking soda, but a spoonful of boric acid.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Akibumbo suggests to the inspector that Miss Silvia’s death was staged to look like a suicide. He posits that a person living in the adjacent room could have jumped across the windows facing the chimney to enter her room. Mrs. Habberd identifies Nigel Bateson as the neighbor. Akibumbo reveals that Chandra Lal had previously reported that Nigel Bateson took his boric acid. This leads to the realization that someone took morphine from Nigel’s drawer, replaced it with boric acid, and Patricia Lane subsequently switched the boric acid for baking soda, mistakenly believing it was morphine. Sharp shows Mrs. Habberd two red strands of hair found in a package, which serves as a clue pointing toward Lennie Bateson.

Later, Hercule Poirot and Inspector Sharp discuss the case, noting that everything is now in order. Sharp prepares to go to 26 Walnut Street while Inspector Cobbe handles that location.

Inspector Cobbe and Detective Constable McRae arrive at a beauty salon in Mayfair owned by Mrs. Lucas to search the office of Miss Valerie Hobhouse. After searching the safe and desk, Cobbe discovers six blue leather-bound passports taped to the bottom of a desk drawer. The passports belong to various identities, including Mrs. Da Silva, Miss Irene Frank, Mrs. Olga Cohen, Miss Nina Le Meur, Mrs. Gladys Thomas, and Miss Moira O'Neill, all featuring photos of dark-skinned women using different hairstyles and disguises. Two additional foreign passports for Madame Mahamoudi and Miss Sheila Donovan are also found. Cobbe concludes that these identities were used for smuggling and money laundering through a Mayfair gambling club to avoid taxes, suggesting that Silvia may have seen one of these fake passports dropped on Mountbatten Street.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In Mrs. Haberdashere's sitting room, Inspector Sharp discusses the investigation into Valerie Hobhouse with Hercule Poirot. Sharp explains that Hobhouse used Celia Austin’s kleptomania as a ruse to facilitate a sophisticated smuggling operation involving drugs and gems. While Sharp notes that they lack concrete evidence to charge Hobhouse with the murder of Celia, Poirot reveals a different truth regarding the death of Patricia Lane.

Poirot explains that the phone call made to the police station at 6:08, which appeared to be from Patricia Lane, was actually made by Valerie Hobhouse from a pharmacy telephone booth to create an alibi. He asserts that Nigel Chapman is the true murderer of Patricia Lane. Poirot argues that Chapman, who possessed morphine and exhibited reckless, overconfident behavior, killed Patricia by hitting her from behind. He notes that Chapman was able to "recognize" the fake call because he was the one who answered it immediately after committing the crime. Chapman had attempted to stage a double bluff using green ink and planted Lennie Bates's hair in Patricia's fingernails, but Poirot views these as the mistakes of a self-centered man.

Following this, Poirot meets with Mr. Endicott, a lawyer, to discuss the recent death of Arthur Stanley. Poirot inquires about the death of Arthur's wife several years prior, which was ruled an accidental barbiturate overdose. Endicott notes that Arthur provided evidence regarding his wife's confusion. Poirot concludes the meeting by asking Endicott's opinion on whether Arthur Stanley was the type of man who would kill his wife to marry another woman.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot visits Mr. Endicott, the executor of Arthur Stanley’s will, to discuss Arthur's son, Nigel. Poirot reveals that Nigel, who changed his name to Nigel Chapman, became involved in a smuggling ring involving drugs and jewels alongside Valerie Hobhouse. Poirot explains that Nigel’s criminal activities were nearly exposed when a police investigation near Cambridge caused him to panic and destroy evidence. This panic led to a chain of events where Nigel murdered Celia because she knew his true identity. Nigel also killed Patricia Lane, who had pressured him to reconcile with his father.

Mr. Endicott produces a sealed letter from Arthur Stanley. The letter reveals that Arthur knew Nigel had murdered his own mother by overdosing her on sleeping pills after she discovered Nigel had forged her signature. Arthur had instructed Endicott to investigate Nigel and either destroy the letter and a confession if Nigel proved upright, or hand them to the law if he was a criminal. The attached confession is signed by Nigel Stanley, admitting to the murder of his mother on November 18, 1950.

In a separate scene, Valerie Hobhouse provides a voluntary statement to Inspector Sharp. She admits to being an accessory to Nigel's crimes, including the murders of Celia and Mrs. Nick (whom Nigel drugged), but maintains she did not intend to kill. She expresses a desire to bring Nigel to justice. During the interrogation, it is revealed that the deceased Mrs. Nikolaitis was Valerie's mother.

Elsewhere, Sally Finch and Lennon Bates discuss the case, with Lennon expressing concern that Nigel may have tried to frame them. Sally mentions her intention to marry Lennon. The narrative concludes with Poirot finishing correspondence with Miss Lemon.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
On September 9th at 2:59 PM, Colin Lamb was walking west on Wilbraham Crescent, searching for house number 61. After passing several houses and Diana's Cottage, he reached the end of the road at Albany Avenue. As he walked, a young girl rushed out of house number 19, screaming and colliding with him. The girl was visibly shaken and claimed that a man was lying dead inside the house, noting that there was blood present.

Lamb guided the girl to an iron railing to rest and then entered house number 19. Inside the living room, he encountered Millee Pema-Chhoe, a blind woman who remained remarkably calm. She informed Lamb that she had returned from shopping and sensed someone else in the house. Upon investigating a sound, she discovered a man's body behind the sofa. She described finding the man's hand, noting it was cold and lacked a pulse.

Millee Pema-Chhoe instructed Lamb to bring the girl inside to the dining room to wait for help, stating she would brew tea for her. Lamb settled the girl in the dining room and proceeded to a phone booth approximately fifty yards away. He called the Croedin Police Station and requested to speak with Inspector Hatchet. The call was answered by Dick Hakast, who recognized Lamb.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Inspector Dick Hakast arrives at 19 Wilbraham Crescent to investigate a murder. A man has been found dead on the floor, likely stabbed approximately thirty minutes prior. The house is owned by Miss Mirella Pembleton (also referred to as Miss Pema, Miss Pemah, or Miss Permshaw), a teacher at the Allenburg Institute who teaches blind script to disabled children.

Hakast interviews Hilary Webber, a stenographer from Cavendish Document Printing Agency, in the kitchen. Webber explains that she was sent to the house at 3:00 p.m. by her employer, Miss Martin, specifically at the request of Miss Pembleton. Webber discovered the body after turning a sofa and admitted to touching the corpse, which left her hands covered in blood. She noted a strange discrepancy with the house's clocks: while the cuckoo clock struck three, other clocks were nearly an hour ahead. Webber claims she entered the house because Miss Pembleton told her to come in, despite the two not having spoken previously. Webber, who lives with her aunt Mrs. Lott, appeared visibly shaken and fearful during the questioning.

After dismissing Webber to the dining room, Hakast prepares to interview Miss Pembleton. As he approaches her, she demonstrates her blindness by feeling her way to a chair. Before the interview begins, Miss Pembleton questions Hakast about the identity and presence of Colin Lum, a man who had been present at the scene.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Hakast interviews Miss Pemahox, a teacher at the Allenberg Institute for Disabled Children who is losing her vision and has studied Braille. Hakast explains that Colin Lamm, an ocean biologist and friend of his, was present because he happened to be passing by when Miss Weaver screamed about a murder. Hakast provides a description of the deceased—a man approximately sixty years old, between 5'9" and 5'10", with gray hair, brown eyes, a thin face, and a strong chin, wearing a dark gray suit—but Miss Pemahox cannot identify him.

Hakast questions Miss Pemahox regarding a potential connection to the Cavendish Typing Agency and a Miss Sheila Weber, but she denies knowing the name or having a phone to make such a call. When asked about her whereabouts during the estimated time of death (1:30 to 2:45 PM), she states she was at a post office on Albion Road and a fabric store called "Field and Lane," returning home when her cuckoo clock chimed three times.

A conflict arises when Hakast claims several clocks in the living room are fast by over an hour, including a Dresden porcelain clock, a gilded French clock, a silver travel clock, and a clock labeled "Rosemary." Miss Pemahox, who relies on touch to "perceive" objects, insists these items do not belong to her and were not in the room when she left at 1:30 PM. A fingerprint technician reports that the leather travel clock and silver clock have no fingerprints, and all the clocks in the room stopped at 4:13 PM. The technician also notes that the fingerprints found in the room likely belong to women.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Hakaste examines a small pile of items on a table near a deceased man, which includes a wallet containing seven pounds and ten shillings, a silk handkerchief, digestive pills, and a business card for R. H. Kouri, Esq. of the Great City Small Town Insurance Company, located at 7 Danver Street, London. Hakaste questions Miss Pemuko, who denies having any appointments with insurance companies, specifically stating she has never heard of the Metropolitan District Insurance Company and has no need for life insurance. She also denies knowing anyone named Kirby.

To assist the investigation, Miss Pemuko, who is visually impaired, agrees to "feel" the dead man's face to identify him. She traces his hair, eyes, ear, nose, mouth, and chin, but concludes she does not recognize him. After the fingerprint examiner leaves and the body of Mr. Kouri is moved from the room, Hakaste reviews the facts with Miss Pemuko. She confirms she had no appointments, no insurance consultations, and no request for stenographer services from the Calverdish Document Service. She notes that when she cleaned the house that morning, only a cuckoo clock and a grandfather clock were present. She explains her schedule, noting she was at Allenberg College from 10:00 to 12:15 and returned home at 12:45 before leaving again at 1:30.

Hakaste questions her regarding four clocks that appeared in the room with their hands set to 4:13, but she claims the time means nothing to her. After dismissing Miss Webber and Mr. Collins, Hakaste instructs that the four new clocks be packed carefully for transport. He then departs with Colin Lumb, heading toward the Cavendish Manuscript Society on the Strand.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Harkness and Colin discuss a girl who discovered a body and expressed extreme terror. Harkness explains the peculiar layout of Wilbraham Crescent, noting that the street is built back-to-back, requiring a detour through Albany Road to reach the second half. Colin is searching for number 61, which Harkness identifies as the home of an architect named Brand. Harkness describes Brand as a dishonest architect who uses inferior materials to commit commercial fraud. He notes that Brand recently escaped bankruptcy after his Canadian wife inherited a large sum of money following the deaths of her grandfather and her father in an air disaster. Harkness mentions that the domestic tax department previously investigated Brand's sudden wealth but found nothing.

Harkness and Colin then travel to the Cavendish Agency on Wangfujing Street. After passing a photography studio and coal merchants, they enter the agency and are led by a receptionist to the office of Miss Martin-Dell, a woman in her fifties with reddish-brown hair. While Harkness questions her, Colin observes her collection of autographed photos from various mystery and romance novelists. Harkness asks about Sheila Webb, an employee who is currently out on business. Miss Martin-Dell states that Webb has been with the agency for a year and may be at the Heron Hotel for a five o'clock appointment. She adds that Webb previously worked at a firm in London, possibly a property firm, and has good references stored in her safe.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Inspector Dick Hackett investigates Miss Martindale at her Documentary Agency regarding a suspicious incident involving Sheila Webber. Miss Martindale describes Webber as a careful, average-speed typist who lives with her aunt. Miss Martindale explains that a woman identifying herself as Miss Pemaroo called the agency at 2:45 p.m. to request Webber's stenography services at 19 Wilbraham Crescent for a 3:00 p.m. appointment, claiming Webber had served her before. However, Miss Pemaroo later denied making the call.

Hackett reveals that when Webber arrived at the residence, she entered the living room as instructed and discovered a murdered man who had been stabbed. Miss Martindale expresses disbelief at the connection between the call and the death. During the inquiry, Miss Martindale explains that she has run the agency for fifteen years, providing research services—such as legal knowledge, poisoning processes, and foreign details—to authors, having previously served as Miss Pembroke's secretary and worked for horror novelist Gary Greggson.

As the investigators depart, the receptionist, Irene, complains about a broken high heel lost in a street grate. The narrative then shifts to O'Neil Cortin and his mother, Mrs. Cortin. While O'Neil plays with a metal rocket model, he informs his mother that a police car has arrived at their house. Mrs. Cortin initially accuses him of lying or being influenced by a local gang led by Alf, but she eventually goes to the door to confront the two men knocking.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Inspector Hakaste visits Mrs. Cording (also referred to as Mrs. Corton/Cottington) to investigate a murder at 19 Wilbraham Crescent. Mrs. Cording, who works for a blind woman named Miss Millechamp (also referred to as Miss Pemerton/Pemarhu/Pembroke), explains that she typically works there from 9:30 or 10:00 until noon. Hakaste informs her that a man was found stabbed to death in the living room of the residence.

The detective asks if anyone visited the house that morning. Mrs. Cording denies seeing anyone, specifically noting she would not admit a man claiming to be an insurance agent. Hakaste mentions a man in a dark suit who left a card under the name Mr. Corley. Mrs. Cording suggests the name sounds Indian, though Hercule Poirot clarifies it is not. The body was discovered by a stenographer who had arrived at the house by mistake.

Hakaste questions Mrs. Cording about the clocks in Miss Millechamp's sitting room. She identifies a grandfather clock and a cuckoo clock but denies the presence of a silver traveling clock, a gilded clock, a ceramic clock, or a leather clock labeled "Rosemary." Hakaste notes that four clocks at the scene are running an hour and a half fast. Mrs. Cording mentions that Miss Millechamp occasionally buys items like carpets and curtains at auction markets, though she does not usually buy small antiques.

Later, Dick Hakaste meets with Colin Lumb, an oceanographer. They discuss the upcoming trial of Rackin, who successfully smuggled important documents out of a secure location by having them photographed. Colin explains that the operation involved a flawless plan where a man used an identical coat to switch places with Rackin to avoid suspicion.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Colin discusses the investigation of Rackin with Hakast and Dick. Colin explains that Rackin’s activities involve a sophisticated organization between the Portsmouth Naval Base and London, driven by a motive of money rather than political ideals. Colin admits to using Rackin to leak valuable intelligence. Colin also reveals his personal investigation into "New Moon Street" and various moon-themed pubs and crescent-shaped streets in Portbury, motivated by a sketch found in the wallet of a deceased boy named Hambley. The sketch contains the number "61" and the letter "M." Dick informs Colin that number 61 on Wilbraham New Moon Street is occupied by an architect and offers to help Colin investigate the area and check for foreign residents the following morning.

The next day, Hakast is angry because a leather travel clock marked "Rosemary" has gone missing from a collection of clocks. Hakast explains that during the process of moving a body and packing the clocks, a subordinate named Edward reported only finding three clocks instead of four. Colin recalls that Miss Pemahyu briefly returned to the house alone to retrieve forgotten gloves. Hakast suggests that she may have taken the clock and implies that Colin is being too protective of her due to romantic feelings. Hakast warns Colin that the girl is deeply involved in the murder case and has her own problems.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hakaste and the narrator discuss the investigation into a murder at Pema's house. Hakaste suspects Hillary Weber, who discovered the body, noting that her lunch break aligns with the estimated time of death. The narrator questions the suspicion of Weber, given her apparent distress, and notes that Pema is a blind woman who worked as a math teacher before finding employment at the Allenberg Institute. Hakaste reveals that the investigation into a man named Kirby has yielded nothing; his business card, name, address, and the Big City Small Town Insurance Company appear to be entirely fabricated. The autopsy indicates the victim was killed with a sharp instrument, such as a kitchen knife.

The investigators consider seeking clues from neighbors in the upper-class residential area of Wilbraham Crescent. Hakaste notes that number 18 is occupied by Mr. Waitehouse, an assistant at a law firm, and his sister, Edith. At number 18, James Waitehouse expresses concern to his sister about recent suspicious activity in the neighborhood, but Edith dismisses his fears as sacrilege. Edith discusses rumors that the victim may have been an accountant from Allerton College investigating financial discrepancies. She defends Miss Pembroke’s character, rejecting the theory that she strangled the man. After James leaves for work, Edith, feeling agitated by the possibility of danger, places a nine-iron from her golf bag near the front door for protection.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Hazard informs Miss Watrous (also referred to as Miss Walterhouse, Miss Watkins, and Miss Houseworth) that policemen are waiting in the dining room. Miss Watrous descends the stairs to meet Detective Chief Inspector Hakaste and Mr. Huckleberry, who is accompanied by Constable Lamb. The detectives are investigating a murder that occurred next door at Miss Pemberton's house between 1:30 and 2:30 PM.

The detectives present Miss Watrous with a photograph of a respectable-looking man, believed to be a stranger who visited Miss Pemberton. Miss Watrous claims she does not recognize him. When questioned about her movements, Miss Watrous states she was in her garden before 1:00 PM and later sat in her living room reading *The Times*. She recalls seeing Miss Pemberton walk past her front door, which faces the street, while she was sitting near the window.

Miss Watrous explains that after lunch, she performed various tasks, including doing crossword puzzles, washing dishes, and writing letters. She notes that she heard a scream from next door, which prompted her to look out the window. From her vantage point, she observed a young man and a girl at the front gate; the man appeared to be arguing with the girl before making her sit against a doorpost and then entering the house. Miss Watrous did not see Miss Pemberton return to the house before this incident. She only realized the gravity of the situation when she heard police sirens and learned of the murder.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an interrogation, Inspector Hakaste and Inspector Rune question Miss Wat Tyler regarding a man murdered who referred to himself as "Curry." Miss Wat Tyler denies knowing anyone by that name and notes that she and James have insurance policies with the "Mutual Insurance Association." She mentions that James Watkinson was at "Gatesford and Swindham's firm" on Hay Street during the incident and usually has lunch at "The Three Feathers." As the detectives depart, Colin Lumb observes a golf club in a baseball bag, which Miss Wat Tyler claims she does not know how arrived there.

Outside, Hakaste and Colin discuss the interrogation. Hakaste suspects Miss Pembroke might have made a phone call at 1:58 PM, despite her denial and a discrepancy in her walking route, though the motive for the call remains unclear.

The detectives then travel to 20 Wilbraham Crescent, a property named Diana Cottage. The house is poorly maintained, featuring a bright turquoise door and a pungent odor. Upon entering, they encounter Mrs. Hemm, an elderly woman wearing an orange fur scarf that is actually a live cat. The house is filled with several cats and the smell of animals. Mrs. Hemm, who is defensive following a complaint from the "Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Association," claims she lives for her cats. When Hakaste attempts to question her about the murder of a man at number 19, Mrs. Hemm shows little interest, initially confusing the matter with a neighbor's dog.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Hakast and Colin interview Mrs. Hamm regarding her whereabouts during the afternoon of the murder. Mrs. Hamm states she was home between 1:30 and 3:30, spending time grooming her pets and attempting to lure her kitten, Arabella, down from a tree. Colin notes that her description of feline behavior is accurate. Hakast requests to see her backyard, which is overgrown with thick shrubs and laurels. Mrs. Hamm mentions that Mrs. Lyme’s two sons, whom she describes as "hoodlums" with air rifles, live in the south end of the crescent and their gardens are adjacent to hers. Hakast observes that the dense vegetation makes the gardens feel isolated, noting that the victim did not live in the house at number 19, where a blind woman resides, but rather came there to be murdered.

The investigators then travel to number 61, a property located behind Mrs. Hamm's house that is connected to number 19 at one corner. They meet Mr. Jotham Brand, a friendly, wealthy man living in a luxuriously furnished home. Brand discusses the local rumors regarding the murder, noting conflicting reports about whether the victim was strangled, stabbed, or beaten. Hakast reveals that the victim left a business card in his pocket and shows Brand a police photo of the deceased. Brand claims he does not recognize the man and has not had any visitors for sales purposes. When Hakast suggests questioning Brand's wife, Valerie, Brand hesitates, noting she is unwell. Brand then comments that the photo of the murdered man does not look unpleasant, remarking that the victim looks as if he is merely sleeping.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Hakast meets Valerie Brand, the wife of Josea Brand. Valerie is described as having a pale complexion and a weak, melancholic voice, appearing to enjoy her state of illness. Hakast shows her a photo of the murder victim, an insurance agent. Valerie expresses curiosity but incorrectly assumes the man was strangled; Hakast clarifies he was stabbed. Valerie claims she does not know the man and asks if he had any connection to their neighbor, Miss Pembroke, but Hakast states Miss Pembroke did not know him.

Regarding the neighborhood, Josea Brand explains that while he is not a real gardener, he employs Mr. McNaughton from number 63 to maintain their garden. When questioned about the time of the murder, between one and three o'clock, Josea states that he and Valerie were having lunch together around one or one-thirty. He notes that their dining room window faces the street, so they could not see the garden, and that Valerie usually rests upstairs after lunch. Josea reveals that the couple's lifestyle improved a year ago after Valerie inherited money from an uncle she had not seen in twenty-five years. He expresses interest in traveling to Greece, though Valerie prefers to stay in England due to her health and social connections.

After leaving the Brands, Hakast and Coling discuss the case. Meanwhile, at 62 Wilbraham Crescent, Mrs. Rymes deals with a commotion in her kitchen caused by her sons, Bill and Ted, who were using jars as bowling balls. The boys engage in a fierce wrestling match, prompting Mrs. Rymes to push them out of the kitchen so she can clean up the broken ceramics.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Rimsay (also referred to as Mrs. Lymes, Mrs. Lymsey, or Mrs. Limehouse) is cleaning up broken porcelain and reflecting on the upcoming return of her two energetic children, Bill and Ted, to school after a five-week holiday. Her reflections are interrupted by Bill, who informs her that a detective has arrived to discuss a murder that occurred the previous day at the home of Miss Pembroke, a blind woman who works at Allenberg College.

Detective Hackett arrives at the house accompanied by a man who takes notes. After the detective authoritatively dismisses the children from the room to allow for a private conversation, Mrs. Rimsay denies any knowledge of the incident at number 19 or the neighbors on New Moon Street. Detective Hackett asks if she was home between 12:30 and 3:00 p.m. the previous day; she confirms she was, though she left before 3:00 p.m. to take the children to the cinema. The detective shows her a photograph of a man named Mr. R. H. Corey, whom she does not recognize.

The detective notes that the children's gardens are adjacent to the neighbors' and expresses interest in speaking with them, suggesting children often notice things adults miss. This leads to a discussion about Mrs. Blackham at number 20 and her fourteen cats. Before departing, the man taking notes suggests that Mrs. Rimsay hire an au pair to assist with her household chores.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Rymes discusses her husband, an engineer who traveled to Sweden in early August, with Hakaste and Colin. She notes his interest in electric trains, which run through their hallway. Following the conversation, Hakaste asks the children, Bill and Ted, to show them the garden at 62 Wilbraham Crescent. The garden contains various plants and scientific models, and from the space between an apple and pear tree, the rear of Miss Pemshaw’s house at number 19—the murder site—is visible.

The children explain that they did not see or hear anything during the murder because they were watching a movie. They mention that Miss Pemshaw is blind but moves easily through her garden. Colin discovers a hosepipe near the pear tree and realizes the children have used it to spray things. Bill and Ted reveal they can access Miss Pemshaw's garden by passing through a hole in the barbed wire fence that connects to Mrs. Hamm's garden.

Huckleberry (Hakaste) suggests the children may have found clues while exploring. Bill and Ted present a collection of items found in a knotted handkerchief: a cup handle, a pottery shard, a broken sponge, a rusty fork, a coin, a clothespin, a piece of colored glass, and half a pair of scissors. Hakaste takes the glass, while Colin examines a non-British coin.

As they walk, Colin speculates that Mr. Rymes's frequent travels and the family's circumstances might serve as a cover for something else. Hakaste then suggests visiting the MacNorton house, where retired professors live, noting its garden also connects to number 19. Upon arriving, they are met by a young woman who reacts with fear when Hakaste identifies himself as police.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hakaste and a policeman visit the home of Mrs. McNaughton to investigate a murder case involving Miss Pemmican. After being led into the living room by a young woman named Greta, the officers meet a worried Mrs. McNaughton. She mentions hearing a scream the previous day but assumed it was caused by the children of Mrs. Lymes, who frequently make loud noises imitating spaceships or atomic bombs.

When Hakaste shows Mrs. McNaughton a photograph of the deceased, she believes she recognizes the man. She recalls a man who previously visited her home to sell encyclopedias or vacuum cleaners, noting that he had annoyed her husband, Angus, while Angus was planting bulbs. She suggests the man might have been driving a bakery truck, though she cannot confirm if she saw him the day of the murder.

The officers then go into the garden to speak with Angus McNaughton. Angus explains that when he heard the scream, he was working in the garden and assumed it was the Lymes children. He notes that the children often jump fences and chase cats, and mentions that their father, Mr. Rimmers, is an engineer who is frequently abroad, such as in Sweden. Mrs. McNaughton estimates the scream occurred around 2:30 PM. Angus, who was busy making compost and fixing a water pipe, leads Hakaste to his compost pile near a shed by the fence separating his property from house number 19. Hakaste asks if Angus saw anyone in the garden of number 19 or peering out its windows, but Angus shakes his head.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After an unsuccessful interview, Detective Hakaste and Detective Blue (Colin) discuss a witness who claimed to see a shadowy figure in the garden of number 19. Hakaste dismisses her claim as wishful thinking, noting she could not clarify her observations. Hakaste also expresses suspicion regarding Mrs. Black, noting her strange lack of awareness regarding her neighbor, Miss Pema, and her indifference toward the murder case.

Upon returning to the station, Colin informs Hakaste that he is leaving for London to give a report. Colin also mentions he intends to consult a private detective and friend of his father, Hercule Poirot, for stimulation. Hakaste expresses surprise, as he believed Poirot was dead.

Meanwhile, Inspector Hercule works through daily files. He receives a report from Sergeant Grey stating that no one recognized Mr. Kuri’s photo at transit hubs. Laboratory analysis of a suit reveals the tailor's label was removed, leaving it unclear if Mr. Kuri or the killer intended to hide an identity. Hercule notes that the deceased's dental records are the most promising lead for identification.

Later, Hakaste continues his investigations. He notes that Ms. Sheila Webber had an appointment with Professor Puddy at the Heron Hotel. Hakaste then walks toward 14 Palmerston Road to visit Mrs. Rotten (also referred to as Mrs. Rudd/Rutter), the aunt of a person involved in the case. On his way, he encounters a vaguely familiar girl with blue eyes and fair skin but fails to place her. At the residence, he meets Mrs. Rudd, a disheveled woman who expresses frustration with reporters. Hakaste enters her home, where he picks up several envelopes from the doormat before handing them to her.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Hackett visits the home of Mrs. Rotten to conduct inquiries regarding a murder case involving her niece, Hilary (also known as Sheila) Webb. While waiting in the messy living room, Hackett observes letters addressed to Mrs. Rotten and Miss R.S. Webber. Mrs. Rotten explains that Hilary is currently working late for Professor Puddy, a man described as pleasant but lacking a sense of time.

Hackett discusses the upcoming trial, noting that Hilary only needs to state her discovery of the body. He mentions that the identity of the deceased is still unknown, though a business card found in the victim's pocket suggests the man may have been considering insurance. During the questioning, Hackett clarifies Hilary's full name as Rosalind Sheila Webb, though she prefers Sheila. Mrs. Rotten provides details about Hilary's previous life in London, noting she lived at 17 Caldington Grove near Fulham Road and worked for a real estate company called Hopgood and Trent before joining Cavendish Document Printing last November.

When Hackett asks if Hilary is an orphan, Mrs. Rotten confirms she is and that she is the child of Mrs. Rotten's sister. However, Mrs. Rotten claims she does not know the occupation of Hilary's father. Hackett suggests that Hilary may have been deliberately lured to the scene of the crime by someone with a grudge or someone attempting to frame her. He speculates the perpetrator could be a rejected suitor or a jealous former roommate from London. Mrs. Rotten defends Hilary's character, though she considers the possibility of a non-serious attempt to frame her.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Hakast (also referred to as Hercule or Inspector Hastings) interviews Mrs. Ruddock (also referred to as Mrs. Routon, Mrs. Rudd, Mrs. Luton, Mrs. Ruddle, Mrs. Rutt, and Mrs. Rotten) regarding the parents of Hila Weber. Hakast suspects that the motives for the current incident stem from the parents' past. Mrs. Ruddock reveals that Hila is the illegitimate child of her sister, Anne. Anne was a respected school teacher who became pregnant by a man named Mr. Weber, who either could not or would not marry her. To protect her career and reputation, Anne sought a clean break, giving Mrs. Ruddock half of their mother's inheritance to raise Hila. Anne subsequently moved away, possibly to Australia, to continue her teaching career, and has since had no contact with Mrs. Ruddock or Hila.

Mrs. Ruddock, a widow who previously ran a candy store in Lincolnshire before moving to the area to work for the clothing retailer "Roscoe and West," states she does not recognize a man in a photo shown by the detective. Hakast reflects on the deceased, reconsidering whether the man was a "good man" rather than the "bad man" he had previously assumed based on a false name found on a business card.

The conversation concludes with Mrs. Ruddock noting that Hila’s colleague, a girl Hakast previously encountered on the street near Cavendish Stationery, came to visit Hila but left because she had another appointment. The colleague claimed she needed to ask Hila for advice regarding an unimportant matter.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After leaving Mrs. Luton, a detective ponders the name "Rosemary," which Mrs. Luton explained was chosen by her sister, while "Sheila" was named after their mother. 

Colin Lamb travels through the streets near Covent Garden to a crowded, dimly lit bookstore near the British Museum owned by Mr. Solomon. Lamb is researching seaweed and seeks books on ocean biology and fossils. He navigates through narrow aisles and various floors, passing through a "treasure" storage area on the second floor before reaching the third floor. There, he enters a room where he encounters a middle-aged woman, whom he calls "Nanny," who informs him that a man named Mr. Collins was unhappy with his progress.

Lamb enters a smoke-filled room to meet Colonel Beck, a group leader. Beck expresses frustration regarding the lack of progress in Lamb's investigation into "Wilbraham Crescent." Lamb reports that he has been investigating houses on New Moon Street and mentions a discovery: a murder occurred on Wilbraham Crescent, though the victim's identity remains unknown because a business card found in their pocket contained a fake name and address.
