Lamb requests permission to investigate specific individuals, identifying a house called "Diana's Cottage" located at 20 Wilbraham Crescent in Crofton, ten miles from Portbury. He explains that the cottage is inhabited by a Mrs. Hemm, who spends her time caring for eighteen cats.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The narrator discusses potential suspects near a murder scene with Colonel Beker. He identifies Mr. Lyme, an engineer living at 62 Wilbraham Crescent who travels abroad and has a wife and two sons. Beker compares Lyme's cover to a man named Pendleton, who used various wives as disguises. The narrator also mentions MacNorton, a retired Scottish professor at number 63 whose garden adjoins the murder site, and a blind former schoolteacher living alone at number 19. The narrator suggests that the proximity of these gardens would make it easy to dispose of a body. To support his investigation, the narrator presents Beker with a muddy Czech coin discovered in the back garden of number 19. Beker teases the narrator about his motives and a girl involved in the case, but the narrator remains focused on attending a trial in Crofton.

Later, Colin Lamb visits Whitehall House to see his friend, Hercule Poirot. After being greeted by a servant named George, Lamb enters room 203, where Poirot is sitting by an electric fire with tea and books. Lamb explains that he uses the alias "Lamb" to protect his father's identity. The two men discuss the aging process and gardening before transitioning to a conversation about Lamb's father, whom Poirot admires for his unique, trap-setting investigative methods. Poirot concludes the meeting by congratulating Lamb on his recent success with the Larkin case.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a visit, Poirot explains to Colin that he has been seeking intellectual stimulation through research and problem-solving. He recounts a minor mystery involving three dry orange peels found on his umbrella stand, which he solved by discovering that a new cleaning lady had violated rules by bringing her child to work. Poirot also mentions applying his methods to real-life unsolved mysteries, such as the Charles Bravo case and the case of Constance Kent, claiming he found clues to the motives and culprits.

Turning to literature, Poirot critiques various crime novels. He praises "The Case of the Living Dummy" for its atmosphere and character studies, and "The Adventures of Arsenè Lupin" for its vitality and humor. He describes "The Secret of the Yellow Room" as a masterpiece of logical reasoning. Regarding contemporary authors, he criticizes Ariadne Oliver’s early works for being unrealistic and her portrayal of Finnish characters as inaccurate, though he notes her later improvement in describing police procedures. He discusses Cyril Quin, noting that while the plots are repetitive and revolve around intricate alibis like train and bus schedules, he enjoys unraveling them. In contrast, he dismisses horror novelist Gary Gregson for creating chaotic, unbelievable plots. 

Discussing American writers, Poirot praises Florence Eckle for her methodical pacing but criticizes the frequent, unnecessary inclusion of alcohol consumption in American crime fiction. While he dismisses the hardboiled genre's focus on violence, he concludes that American crime novels are often more cunning and imaginative than British ones, citing Louisa O'Malley as an example.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While searching for books, Hercule Poirot discusses the academic writing of Louis O'Malley and expresses his reverence for Sir Arthur Conan Doyle, specifically praising the character of Dr. Watson. He briefly mentions his friend Hastings, who is currently in South America.

The narrator approaches Poirot to seek help with a murder case that lacks logical sense. The narrator details the events that occurred at 19 Wilbraham Crescent. Poirot listens intently and concludes that the case is "remarkable." Although the narrator wants an immediate solution, Poirot explains that the case is likely simple because it appears so complicated. Poirot experiences a sense of déjà vu, noting that the details of the case follow a pattern similar to a previous case involving a soap-boiler in Liège, though he clarifies that the specific elements—such as clocks, a fake insurance agent, or a blind woman—do not match this instance.

Poirot asserts that the identity of the deceased will eventually be established by the police through various scientific and investigative methods. He advises the narrator to go back and "chat" with the neighbors rather than simply asking them if they saw anything suspicious, suggesting that clues are often hidden in casual conversation. The narrator reveals he has already transcribed all neighbor conversations in shorthand. Finally, Poirot suggests the narrator speak to a specific girl whom the narrator had previously held in his arms when she rushed out of the house in a panic.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot instructs his companion to investigate a girl who may be connected to the case. Poirot suggests that because a specific phone call requested her presence at 19 Wilbraham Crescent, her involvement is not a coincidence. He advises the narrator to visit a blind woman, go to a printing shop under the pretext of printing a manuscript, and befriend the girls working there to gather information. Poirot concludes the conversation by quoting "The Walrus and the Carpenter" from *Alice's Adventures in Wonderland*.

During a trial in Crofton, Miss Weaver and Miss Martin provide testimony regarding a phone call received by the Cavendish Typewriting Agency. Miss Martin testifies that at 1:49 PM, a caller claiming to be Miss Pembroke requested a stenographer, specifically Miss Weaver, to go to 19 Wilbraham Crescent. Miss Pembroke denies making this call. Inspector Hatch testifies that he discovered the deceased's body at the residence immediately after receiving the call.

Dr. Riggs provides medical evidence, estimating the time of death between 1:30 PM and 2:30 PM. He concludes the victim was stabbed in the heart with a thin, sharp knife, possibly a French cooking knife, while in a coma induced by chloral hydrate. The victim had not eaten solid food for at least four hours prior to death. The coroner adjourns the trial for fourteen days, rescheduling it for September 28. Following the proceedings, Irena Brent of the Cavendish Typewriting Agency attempts to locate Sheila, who reportedly left the court with a young man. Irena approaches a policeman to ask for Detective Hakast, who is seen discussing matters with the coroner and the county police chief.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Eina, feeling confused and unable to make sense of a recent conversation, walks from the grain market to Albany Road. She heads toward Wilbraham Crescent to visit 19 Wilbraham Crescent, the site of a reported murder at Miss Pemaroo's residence. Onlookers gather at the house to speculate on the crime, discussing rumors that a man broke in through a back window to steal silverware and was killed with a kitchen knife. Bystanders note that the homeowner is blind and that a girl involved in the incident is in a state of shock. Ina Brent, caught in the crowd, stares at the house and begins to contemplate the murder until a voice startles her.

In a separate narration, Colin Lamb observes Hilary Webber leaving an inquest room following her testimony. Colin approaches her and suggests brunch at a Chinese restaurant, but Hilary declines, stating she must shop before her work shift begins at 2 pm. After they agree to meet later, Colin reflects on his deep feelings for Hilary.

Later, Colin visits Dick Hakast at the police station. They discuss the inquest and medical evidence. Colin reports that an expert believes the murder is a "simple" case because the perpetrator's plan was overly complicated. The expert suggested interviewing neighbors. Dick reveals that the murder weapon—a recently sharpened kitchen knife—was found at the house of a "cat lady," though it had been wiped clean of fingerprints. They discuss the theory that the victim was first anesthetized elsewhere and then transported to number 19, possibly from a nearby house with an adjacent garden.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hakast and Colin discuss the possibility that a killer used a car to transport a body or commit a crime near number 19. They theorize that Miss Milla Pemmerho may have killed a strong man by anesthetizing him in a drink, then cleaning the scene and disposing of the knife in a neighbor's garden. Colin notes that she specifically called the Cavendish Typewriting Office to ask for Miss Hill Weaver.

Colin presents Hakast with a postcard received by Sheila. The card features the Central Criminal Court and contains the address for Miss R.S. Webber in Sussex, the word "Remember!", and the time "4:13," which matches the time shown on an alarm clock from the crime scene. While Colin believes Sheila is telling the truth about not knowing the card's meaning, Hakast remains focused on the connection. Colin also reviews files regarding the deceased, noting that while many people claim to recognize him, the letters are largely unreliable.

The investigation is interrupted by a phone call informing Hakast that Ina Brent, a girl from the same office as Hilary Webb, was found strangled with her own scarf in a phone booth on Wilbraham Crescent. Miss Walters, a neighbor from number 18, discovered the body. Later, a young officer named Pierce informs a frustrated Hakast that Ina Brent had approached him before her death, stating she wanted to speak with Hakast because she had "some doubts." Hakast regrets missing this opportunity to hear her concerns.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Pierce informs Hakast that Ila Brent approached him as people were leaving the trial. She had been looking for someone and asked to speak to the officer who provided evidence that day. Because Hakast was busy with the county police commissioner, Pierce advised her to leave a message or return to the station. Ila claimed her business was not important, though she appeared puzzled and remarked, "I don't know if what she said is true." Ila was later found strangled in a phone booth.

Hakast reflects on Ila's possible motives, wondering if she had seen or heard something related to Hilary Webb's testimony. Hercule Poirot investigates the possibility that Ila went to Wilbraham Crescent to visit someone. He notes three potential addresses: number 20 (Mrs. Blackham), number 19 (Miss Pembroke), and number 18 (Miss Watkins). Poirot considers that the murder weapon, a knife, was found at number 20, and wonders if Ila suspected Miss Pembroke of having a relationship with Hilary Webb or if Miss Watkins, who found the body, was involved.

Hakast calls Colin Lumb, who confirms he had lunch with Sheila Webb at a Chinese restaurant on Market Street at 1:00 PM, following her shopping trip. This contradicts the fact that Ila Brent died between 12:30 and 1:00 PM. Later, Poirot visits Miss Pembroke at 19 Wilbraham Crescent. She claims she was at home around 12:30 PM and denies that Ila Brent came to see her, expressing anger upon hearing of the murder.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Hakaste (also referred to as Detective Hercaster, Hardcastle, or Hacker) conducts several interviews regarding the murder of a nineteen-year-old stenographer found in a telephone booth. Following an interrogation of Miss Pembroke, who denies seeing the girl or any suspicious activity, the Inspector visits Miss Wutherspoon (also referred to as Miss Walters, Miss Watkins, or Miss Whitehouse). 

Miss Wutherspoon claims she discovered the girl after attempting to use a phone booth because her own phone was making a strange buzzing noise. When questioned about reports of the girl visiting her front gate, she suggests the girl might have merely been delivering a flyer. She then suggests the girl may have actually been visiting Miss Pembury, noting that a stenographer had reportedly visited Miss Pembury on the day a man was killed. The Inspector suspects Miss Wutherspoon may be attempting to deflect suspicion by redirecting the investigation toward No. 19.

The Inspector then visits the Cavendish Typewriting Agency to see Miss Sheila Webber. He is met by Miss Martin (also referred to as Miss Martindeer or Miss Martindale), who berates him for the lack of police protection. She argues that a person with a "perverted psychology" is targeting shorthand typists, noting that Miss Webber was previously traumatized by being sent to discover a corpse, and now a girl named Iena has been murdered. Miss Martin expresses concern that the killings are part of a "mad scheme" and mentions rumors regarding clocks, which the Inspector dismisses as a matter of trial postponement.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Hakast (also referred to as Hercule Poirot) attempts to investigate the death of Ina Brent by questioning her colleagues at her office. Miss Martin, the supervisor, suggests that the office staff might have more information, as they are prone to gossiping. Hakast speaks with three typists: Janet, a blonde woman; Melanie, a dark-skinned woman; and a third smiling girl. 

The women explain that they realized something was wrong when Ina failed to return to the office at two o'clock. They had previously accompanied Ina to a courthouse, but she disappeared after that. One girl recounts learning of the death from a local shopkeeper who mentioned a girl found dead in a phone booth. The colleagues note that Ina had recently seemed preoccupied, making mistakes and acting uncharacteristically distracted. They also mention that Ina did not appear to have a boyfriend. When Hakast asks if Ina was close to Sheila Weber, the women state they were not particularly close. They inform him that Sheila Weber is currently at the Curlew Hotel with Professor Puddy.

Hakast travels to the Curlew Hotel and interrupts Professor Puddy, who is narrating a story to Hilary Weber. Despite the professor's irritation at the disturbance, Hakast insists on speaking with Sheila Weber. He notes a hint of worry in Hilary's eyes as he identifies himself and informs the professor that the matter concerns the death of Ina Brent.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an interrogation, the detective informs Hilary Webber that Ina Brent was found strangled in a phone booth around 12:30 p.m. following a speeding incident. Professor Puddy, who had been working with Hilary, expresses shock at the bizarre nature of the death. The detective reveals that Ina had gone to Hilary's aunt's house the previous day to see her, but Hilary claims she was unaware of Ina's motives. Hilary explains that Ina was often indecisive and prone to worry, citing an instance where Ina accidentally sent an incomplete manuscript to Mr. Armand Levin.

The detective suggests the murder might be connected to an event on September 9th involving a man on Wilbraham Crescent. He notes that the phone booth where Ina was killed is located on that same street. Hilary denies any connection to the location, stating she has avoided Wilbraham Crescent since her previous involvement there, and becomes visibly distressed, pleading with the detective to find the truth.

In a separate narrative, Colin Lamb reports to Colonel Beck in London. Beck informs Colin that Mr. Rimsay, a building engineer living at 62 New Moon Street, is acting suspiciously after receiving a mysterious assignment from a company with no traceable history, which prompted a sudden trip to Romania. To investigate, Beck provides Colin with a new identity as Nigel Trunch, a botanist studying rare plants in the Balkan Peninsula. Beck also mentions other suspicious individuals, including a retired mathematics professor, Mr. MacNorton, who resigned for health reasons but remains energetic and has cut off contact with friends.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After taking a ten o'clock flight, the narrator visits Hercule Poirot, who is drinking blackcurrant. The narrator informs Poirot that they are leaving the country soon and provides updates on the Crooked House case, including the news of another murder and a specific postcard. Poirot notes a pattern involving the numbers "4, 1, 3" (fourteen thirteen) and suggests the postcard is missing a bloody fingerprint. While Poirot refuses to name the killer, he claims to know the identity of the deceased man. To confirm his suspicions, Poirot instructs Miss Lemon to have a lawyer, Mr. Endicott, inquire about marriage records at Somerset House.

Meanwhile, ten days after September 20th, Inspector Hercule Hachette struggles with the unidentified deceased man's identity. Despite numerous leads from people claiming to recognize the man in a newspaper photo, investigations into the man's clothing and various local businesses have yielded nothing. Hachette receives a letter from a woman named Melina Rival, who believes the man is her long-lost husband, Harry. 

Inspector Grey introduces Mrs. Rival to Hachette. She appears to be around fifty years old due to heavy, casual makeup, with dyed reddish-brown hair, wearing a black coat, skirt, and white shirt. Mrs. Rival claims she last saw her husband about fifteen years ago on a train, though she notes they were married only three years ago. She states that Harry is an insurance agent, a profession she suspects may have been an excuse for him to be away from home for long periods.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Hakast accompanies Mrs. Rivallion to identify a body. Upon viewing the deceased on a gurney, she confirms the man is her husband, Harry, noting that although he has aged significantly, his identity is certain. 

Back at the police station, Mrs. Rivallion reveals that Harry used the name Castleton, which she suspects was not his real name. She describes him as an elegant, upper-class man who used his respectable appearance to deceive women. She explains that Harry frequently engaged in patterns of behavior where he would propose investments to women, often after getting them emotionally involved or engaged. Mrs. Rivallion notes that she refused to invest her money in him, both due to a lack of trust and a desire to maintain financial independence.

Mrs. Rivallion recounts that their marriage ended fifteen years ago after Harry claimed he had gotten a schoolteacher into trouble and needed to leave immediately. At the time, she gave him ten pounds and has had no contact with him since. She also reveals that her own name, Melina Rivallion, is not her real name; her true name is Flossi Gapp. She currently works odd jobs, including occasional acting.

When Hakast presents a business card found in the deceased's pocket for an insurance agent, Mrs. Rivallion acknowledges the profession but suggests Harry likely used many different names. Regarding the motive for Harry's presence in the victim's neighborhood, she dismisses the idea of fraudulent insurance scams, suggesting instead that he was likely pursuing women or perhaps engaging in light blackmail by exploiting women's desire to keep their pasts secret.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation, Hakaste presents several clocks to Mrs. Melina Rival. She expresses interest in a gilded clock and notes that the man she married, Harry Castleton, appeared to be a respectable upper-class gentleman, which led her to fall in love with him. When asked about a connection to the name "Rosemary," she recalls a redhead named Rosalie but cannot confirm a link to her husband. She also notes a clock's hands pointing to 4:13. Mrs. Rival agrees to provide identification for a trial in two days, stating she married Castleton over twenty years ago at St. Michael's Church in Dunbrooke. After she departs, Detective Inspector Gray arrives, and Hercule Poirot observes that Castleton’s respectable appearance is likely his modus operandi and suspects multiple women are seeking revenge against him. Poirot specifically ponders a clock inscribed with "Rosemary."

Later, Colin Lamb meets with Poirot, who is annoyed by renovations in his apartment building. They discuss the identification of the deceased in the Crocetta trial as Harry Castleton. Poirot notes that the wife, Mrs. Melina Rival, has been cooperative. The conversation shifts to a girl named Ina who died in a phone booth on Wilbraham Crescent; Poirot critiques Lamb's failure to gather specific details about her movements and the location of a grille where her heel became stuck. The following evening, Lamb meets with Hakaste at his house. Hakaste reveals that Harry Castleton was a man who appeared respectable but made a living by marrying or engaging wealthy, gullible women to gain control of their savings before disappearing.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
An investigator discusses the deceased man's history with a colleague, noting that the man was a successful con artist who frequently changed his identities. He had operated under names such as Harry Castleton, Raymond Blair, Lawrence Dalton, and Roger Byron, though investigators could not prove these were the same person. The man had disappeared for fifteen to twenty years before dying on a carpet in Miss Pemaroo's living room. His wife, who identified the body, used the alias Melina Rival but her real name is Flora Gapp, a former actress and socialite. Investigations into the clocks found at the scene revealed that a gilded clock and a Dresden porcelain clock were purchased at the Pombal Market, while a silver clock was bought from a silversmith in Bournemouth by a woman in a green hat. When asked about a missing fourth clock, Huckleberry refuses to answer.

In a separate narrative, Colin Lamb, using the alias Douglas Weesby, meets Hilary Webber from the Cavendish Typewriting Agency at the Golden Daffodil Café. Hilary expresses intense fear of a detective who suspects her of murdering a man and Ina. She denies staging the scene or imitating Miss Pemaroo's voice on a phone call. Colin attempts to reassure her but suggests she may be hiding something. He tells her he is acting out of affection and believes her terror at the crime scene was genuine. The meeting concludes when Colin asks Hilary why she stole the clock inscribed with "Rosemary," a charge she denies.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Colin Lamb confronts Hillary (also known as Rosemary Shirley) regarding her presence at a murder scene at 19 Wilbraham Crescent. After Colin points out that she lied about returning to the house to retrieve gloves on a warm September day, Hillary admits she went back to take a clock and shoved it into her bag. She claims she did this because the clock's name, "Rosemary," is her own name.

Hillary explains that the clock had been with her her entire life, but she had recently intended to take it for repairs. She recounts that when she entered the house at Wilbraham Crescent, she discovered a dead man. While stunned, she placed her clock on a round table by the fireplace, and her hands became covered in blood. She fled the scene to avoid being seen. Hillary believes someone is framing her, noting that a postcard sent from Old Bailey Street—containing the words "remember" and the number "4:13"—suggests the sender knows she took the watch/clock.

Hillary speculates about her mysterious parentage, noting that her aunt provides inconsistent stories about her parents' deaths in an accident. She wonders if the dead man was her father and if she was lured to the scene as part of a plan to make her appear guilty. When Colin asks where the clock is, Hillary reveals she threw it into a neighbor's trash bin.

Later, Colin checks out of the Clarendon hotel and visits the police station, where he speaks with Hakast. Hakast advises Colin to stay away from the situation and reveals a letter from a woman describing a specific physical feature of her husband: a scar on the back of his left ear caused by a razor.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a discussion with Hakaste regarding a letter from Melina Rivale and the confirmation of a scar on a deceased man's ear, the narrator (Lime) visits Mrs. Lamech at 62 Wilbraham Crescent. Lime clarifies that he is not a detective involved in the recent phone booth murder case, but is instead investigating her husband, Michel. 

Mrs. Lamech reveals that Michel plans to move to Moscow, a decision she does not fully support due to her moderate leftist views and her desire to raise their children as British. While she expresses uncertainty regarding whether Michel is involved in the Larkin case, she remains vague about his specific plans. She mentions her intention to find work, perhaps in secretarial duties, though Lime warns her against working at Cavenish Typewriting and Stenography due to the misfortune affecting its employees.

As Lime leaves, he encounters Mrs. McNaughton, who is carrying a heavy shopping bag. After helping her to her kitchen, Lime discovers that the bag, which she claimed contained cornflakes and watermelon, actually contains three bottles of whiskey. He concludes that this explains her unsteady gait and cheerful demeanor. Finally, while walking toward Albany Road, Lime meets Mr. Brand, who inquires about the progress of the Rivale case and the identification of the body.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After parting ways with an acquaintance at number 61 Albany Road, the narrator walks toward Wilbraham Crescent, reflecting on the lack of witnesses regarding the death of Mr. Corry (Castleton). The narrator mentally lists several unresolved questions, including when Corry was anesthetized, where he was murdered, and how his body arrived at number 19. The narrator considers visiting Miss Pemaroo, Miss Waterhouse, or Mrs. Humble, noting that Mrs. Humble’s tangential speech might contain useful information.

Upon arriving at number 19, the narrator finds the street desolate and quiet. After a brief, futile attempt to converse with an orange tabby cat sitting on the doorpost of number 20, the narrator observes that the neighborhood lacks the "gossipy" residents typically helpful to an investigation, as many elderly people have moved to nursing homes or hospitals.

While observing the large, modern apartment block, the narrator sees a flash of light from a third-floor window. Using a small bird-watching telescope, the narrator identifies a child with a long braid looking out from the window. After an old Rolls-Royce passes by, the narrator approaches the apartment building. Avoiding the empty doorman station, the narrator takes the elevator to the third floor. Finding door number 77, the narrator decides the number seven is lucky and presses the doorbell to see who responds.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Colin Lamb visits a residence to return a silver-white fruit knife he found after it was dropped out of a window. He is met by Ingrid, a tall, blonde Norwegian woman who is currently cooking and has limited English proficiency. Ingrid leads Lamb to a living room where he meets Geraldine Mary Alexandrina Brown, a highly intelligent nine or ten-year-old girl with a plaster cast on her leg.

During their conversation, Geraldine reveals that her father works at the Cattin's Mill Filament Factory in Harebridge and that her mother died in a plane crash when Geraldine was an infant. She explains that Ingrid has lived with them for two weeks and is teaching Geraldine Norwegian. Geraldine describes her habit of watching the neighborhood through a high-quality telescope, which she uses to observe neighbors and record details in a notebook. She assigns nicknames to the residents, such as "the Countess Kalarabas" for a woman living in a house with messy trees.

Geraldine explains that her leg injury occurred when she was nearly run over by a bus. She also mentions that the house next door is occupied by a blind woman. The conversation turns serious when Geraldine reveals that the caretaker, Harry, told her about a murder case in that house. Geraldine claims she witnessed the aftermath of the event, noting that she heard a scream and saw someone run out of the house, which signaled that something had happened.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Geraldine, a ten-year-old girl, recounts her observations of a murder to the narrator. She describes a young, beautiful woman named Sheila Weber running out of a house screaming and grabbing a man who looked like the narrator. This man then entered the house, which is occupied by Miss Pemukh (also referred to as Miss Pembroke). Geraldine notes that Miss Paxtaville, a neighbor from number 18, observed the scene from her steps. Following the incident, the man made a call from a roadside phone booth, and police arrived to carry a dead person away in an ambulance. Harry, the apartment caretaker, informed Geraldine that the victim was a man named Mr. Corry. Geraldine also mentions a separate murder case that occurred later at a phone booth.

The narrator questions Geraldine about the victim's arrival. Geraldine believes Mr. Corry was in the house all along, as she did not see him enter through the front door or garden. She describes the victim as an elderly man, approximately sixty years old, with a trimmed beard and a dark grey suit. Geraldine provides a detailed timeline of the day, noting that Miss Pembroke left around ten o'clock and the housekeeper, Mrs. Crab, left around noon. Geraldine explains her high level of observation was due to an argument with the cook, Ingrid, which led her to watch the street through a telescope. Finally, Geraldine notes that instead of the usual Southdowns Laundry, a new service called Snowflake Laundry had delivered clothes that day.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The narrator questions Geraldine about a laundry delivery made to No. 19. Geraldine consults her notebook, noting that at 1:35, a large basket of laundry was delivered while Miss Pemmican was out. She describes the driver as an ordinary, older man who drove the wrong way down the street, carried the basket to the back door, and wiped sweat from his face before driving off. Before leaving, the narrator advises Geraldine to keep a mysterious fruit knife that does not seem to belong to anyone.

Later, Mrs. Rivale enters the "Pavilion of the Peacock" bar in a distressed state. She complains to the bartender, Fried, about being subjected to deception and evasion, stating she can no longer tolerate being treated poorly by people who do not tell her the whole truth. Fried consoles her and eventually advises her to go home to avoid the approaching rain.

Upon arriving at her house, which smells of cabbage, fish, and eucalyptus, Mrs. Rivale is informed by a voice from the corner that a gentleman is waiting for her upstairs. She enters and finds Detective Chief Inspector Hakast sitting in her home. Hakast explains he traveled to London for work and wishes to discuss "small matters" regarding Harry. Although Mrs. Rivale defensively claims her business with Harry is over and that she has already provided all the information she can, she eventually agrees to listen to the detail Hakast wishes to discuss.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Chief Inspector Hakaste interrogates Mrs. Rivard regarding the identity of a man in a newspaper photograph. Mrs. Rivard claims the man is her husband, Harry Castleman, whom she married on May 15, 1948. She asserts that Harry has a distinctive scar behind his left ear, caused by a razor accident involving a dog in late 1948. She maintains that she has not seen him since she sent him away in 1951.

Hakaste challenges her testimony, noting that forensic examinations indicate the scar tissue is only five or six years old. He points out that if the scar is recent, the man cannot be her husband, as Harry did not have it when he left her in 1951. Hakaste warns Mrs. Rivard that providing false information could lead to charges of perjury. Although she initially reacts with indignant defiance, claiming she is merely performing her civic duty, her demeanor shifts to fear and anxiety once Hakaste leaves.

Mrs. Rivard goes to a post office and uses a phone booth to call an individual named Flo. During the call, she reveals that she was instructed to lie about the scar and had previously accepted money to do so. Despite her fear of being an accessory to a crime or facing jail time, she agrees to meet the person the following day because the offered sum of money is large enough to improve her life. She decides to proceed with the deception, planning to use excuses such as memory lapses or mistaken identity to evade police scrutiny.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a conversation with Colonel Baker, in which Baker expresses concern over Colin Lamb's low spirits and warns him against becoming defeated by the chaos of the world, Colin reads a newspaper report. The report states that Madame Melina Rival collapsed at Victoria Station due to exhaustion and died in the hospital after being discovered to have been stabbed.

Colin calls Hakastad, who reveals that he had previously visited Madame Rival. Hakastad explains his theory that Madame Rival had been paid to falsely identify a corpse as her long-abandoned husband, Harry Castleton, to protect certain business interests. Hakastad notes that after Colin's visit, Madame Rival made a phone call and subsequently traveled to Crofton during rush hour, where she was likely stabbed by a killer hiding in the crowd. Colin informs Hakastad of a witness report regarding a large laundry basket delivered to 19 Wilbraham Crescent by a man in a car from Snowflake Laundry on September 9th, before hanging up to handle his own affairs.

Five days later, Colin arrives in Crofton and stays at the Clarendon Hotel. Upon waking, he receives a thick, expensive envelope containing a note that reads: "Curlew Hotel, 11:30, Room 413 (Knock three times!)" Noticing the room number matches the time, Colin proceeds to the Mallard Inn. He goes to Room 413 and knocks three times as instructed. Upon entering the unlocked room, he finds Hercule Poirot, who surprises him by revealing he arrived in a luxury Mercedes-Benz.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dick arrives at a location, claiming he is there because his apartment requires renovation and his doctor suggested the seaside air. He reveals he specifically requested room 413. He informs the narrator, Colin, that he has called the county police commissioner and is waiting for Hercule Poirot.

When Poirot arrives, the group sits together. Dick attempts to discuss the investigation, but Poirot interrupts, claiming he is also there because his London apartment needs renovation. Poirot suggests that Colin brought him the case not to solve it, but to challenge and mock him by providing a problem that cannot be solved simply by sitting in an armchair.

Poirot claims to know who killed the man at 19 Wilbraham Crescent and who killed Ingrid Bret, though he admits he has not yet found the evidence to prove it. He explains that "being certain" means that once the correct method is found, the facts will fall into place. Poirot compares the need for a detective to the need for a hunting dog that can track and please its owner. He describes a "dog" with extraordinary abilities—a metaphor for a source of information—that possesses an extraordinary memory and can repeat specific, accurate details of conversations and events as if it were present at the scene.

Poirot and Colin discuss the inconsistencies of the case, noting that newspaper reports are often inaccurate. They review the facts of the death at 19 Wilbraham Crescent: a middle-aged man was found dead, carrying a business card for an R.H. Curry of a non-existent insurance company. The investigation notes that a stenographer, Miss Hillar Webber, was requested by a Miss Mylees Pemmerton to go to the residence at three o'clock, where she discovered the body.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses his methodology for solving a seemingly fantastical murder case with Colwyn. Poirot explains that the case's absurd and dramatic elements—resembling detective novels by authors like Agatha Christie or Dixon Carr—serve as distractions. He compares the situation to a woman hiding her aging face behind heavy makeup, jewelry, and furs; similarly, the strange designs of the crime distract from its essence.

Poirot asserts that to solve the murder, one must identify both the motive and the victim. He decides to treat the victim, Mr. Corry, as an ordinary, wealthy, and respected middle-aged man to narrow the search. He notes that while various people could be suspects—including Mrs. Hemm, the Brandt couple, the MacNodon couple, Miss Watrous, Miss Pemmerton, and Sheila Webb—the investigation must focus on why an ordinary man would need to die.

Poirot highlights a crucial observation made by the neighbor, Mrs. Hemm, who remarked that Mr. Corry "came here to die." This realization, combined with the fact that the victim had no identification, no wallet, and torn clothing labels, suggests a deliberate attempt to obscure his identity. Poirot notes that the identity of a man named "Curry" via an insurance agent's card may be a temporary measure. He further observes that the victim's lack of known associates and the absence of dental records suggest he may have been a foreign traveler whose disappearance went unnoticed.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals that a flaw in the killer's plan has allowed him to identify the murderer of Ingrid Brent. Using a poem about a missing horseshoe nail to illustrate how small details cause large failures, Poirot explains that Ingrid Brent's broken shoe heel led to her discovery of a lie. On September 9th, Ingrid's heel became stuck in a grating, causing her to return to the Cavenish Typewriting Agency earlier than expected. While there, she realized that Miss Martindeer’s testimony regarding a specific phone call at 1:49 p.m. was false, as no such call occurred.

Poirot deduces that Miss Martindeer followed Ingrid to Wilbraham Crescent to silence her. While Ingrid was in a phone booth, Miss Martindeer strangled her with a scarf. Poirot identifies Miss Marple as the killer, describing her as an efficient and unimaginative conspirator. 

The motive stems from a fraudulent inheritance scheme involving the Brand couple. Poirot explains that the Brands used a previous marriage to claim a large inheritance from a Canadian uncle, despite the current Mrs. Brand not being the rightful heir. To prevent the truth from being exposed by a visitor from Canada, the Brands conspired to murder him. Poirot concludes that Miss Martin and Mrs. Brand are sisters and the masterminds behind the plot. He further suggests that the killer attempted to evade suspicion by posing as a wealthy tourist and disposing of the victim's passport to misdirect the investigation.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot explains to a companion that the complex case involving mysterious clocks was a plot orchestrated by Miss Marple. Using the unpublished manuscripts of the deceased author Gary Greggson, Miss Marple acted as a mastermind, manipulating local characters to follow the plots she discovered in his notebooks. Poirot reveals that the clocks' specific times—5:14:54, 5:07:07, and 5:05:05—formed the password (514547) to a safe hidden behind a replica of the Mona Lisa, which contained the Russian royal crown. Poirot provides contact information for Mr. Endebie, an international lawyer in Canada, to assist with further investigations.

Later, Colin Lamb visits Miss Mina Pemukho at her home on Wilbraham Crescent. Colin reveals he has uncovered her role as the headquarters of a criminal plot, noting how she used braille to secure records and how information was transmitted via Lymes. Despite her blindness, Colin recognizes her intelligence and danger, disarming her when she attempts to use a knife against him. He informs her that specialized personnel will arrive in two hours and offers her a chance to leave. Colin expresses a desire for her to become his mother-in-law, though he admits he does not trust her. He subsequently announces his intention to return to marine biology in Australia.

A letter from Richard Hakast to Poirot confirms that the body identified as Harry Castleton was actually Quentin Dugastlin. The letter notes that Valerie Brand (née Montreux) is the niece of the late Henry Montreux and that her sister, Miss Martinchel, was involved in the crime. Subsequent notes from Richard Huckleberry reveal that Mrs. Brand confessed, claiming her sister and husband orchestrated the scheme and that she was only informed at the last moment. Additionally, Miss Martin was identified as the woman who purchased the two clocks.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Aeneas Oliver stays with her friend Judith Butler while they help prepare for a Halloween party for children aged ten to seventeen. The party is hosted by Mrs. Royana Drecker, who refers to the event as a "High School Entrance Exam Party" because the children are graduating from Elm Tree School, though Miss Whitaker notes that entrance exams have recently been abolished.

During the preparations, Oliver observes the decorations, including pumpkins and gourds, and reminisces about seeing many pumpkins in America during Thanksgiving. As women arrange furniture and decorations, Oliver discusses the timing of Halloween and All Saints' Day. When a bowl of apples is brought in for an apple-biting game, thirteen-year-old Joyce identifies Oliver as a writer of murder stories. Joyce suggests Oliver create a murder mystery game for the party, but Oliver declines, mentioning a previous unsuccessful attempt.

As the group prepares various games, including tug-of-war and a "dragon game," they decide to use the library because its old carpet can handle moisture. Mrs. Hargreaves brings a green plastic bucket for the games, while Miss Lee suggests a zinc bucket would be more stable. Oliver, who has a dislike for plastic, observes the guests from a chair while eating an apple. She mentally categorizes the attendees, including Miss Johnson (the organist's sister), Royana Drecker, and several children such as Beatrice, Kathy, Diana, and the assertive Joyce. The passage concludes with a discussion about hand mirrors and the possibility of seeing future husbands, as well as Joyce expressing her preference for murder stories with a "strong blood scent."

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a gathering, Joyce claims she witnessed a murder when she was very young, though others like Miss Whitaker, Mrs. Dreck, Anne, and Cassie dismiss her story as nonsense. Joyce explains she did not contact the police because she only realized the event was a murder months ago after hearing someone else mention it. When Beatrice asks for details regarding the victim and the killer, Joyce refuses to answer because she finds Beatrice annoying.

As the group prepares for an apple-biting game, a debate ensues regarding whether a galvanized pail or a plastic bucket is better; they conclude the metal pail is superior despite the plastic bucket being more attractive. Mrs. Oliver, feeling guilty after being teased about her love for apples, seeks privacy in the "Apple Orchard" house. On her way, she encounters a young boy and girl, approximately fifteen and twelve years old, embracing against a door. After Mrs. Oliver asks to pass, the lovers reluctantly separate and angrily call her selfish. Mrs. Oliver enters the house and locks the door behind her.

Later, Ariadne Oliver discusses the complexities of parties with Judith Butler. Judith suggests that Roya Drake is an excellent organizer. At 7:30 PM, the party begins, featuring decorations of red, blue, and yellow pumpkins. Roya Drake announces a program including a broomstick competition, cutting cornbread in a greenhouse, an apple-biting contest, dancing, a mirror ritual in the small study, dinner, and a dragon hunt. During the broomstick competition, Roya explains her motive for using small brooms is to ensure prizes are distributed more evenly among the children. The festivities continue with the apple-biting game and a performance by Mrs. Goodbody, a local cleaning woman playing a witch. The witch performs a ritual for Beatrice, instructing her to hold a mirror and wait for the lights to go out to see the face of her future husband.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At a party organized by Royana Drake, a light beam and a mirror reveal a figure with a large beard that Beatrice compares to the rock star Eddie Prescott. Royana explains that Nick and his friend Desmond, an experienced photographer, staged the effect with lighting and costumes to entertain the guests. Following a dinner of various snacks and cakes, the party concludes with a "dragon hunt" featuring a dish of burning grapes on a green tablecloth. As the party ends, Judith notes the need to clean up the mess.

In a London apartment, Hercule Poirot is disappointed when his butler, George, informs him that Solomon Levy cannot visit to debate the Cunningham Road Public Baths murder case due to a severe cold. Poirot receives a subsequent telephone call from his friend, Ariadne Oliver, who sounds breathless and highly agitated. Despite knowing that Mrs. Oliver's visits often involve long sessions of venting grievances, Poirot invites her over and prepares lemon barley tea and white wine for himself.

Mrs. Oliver arrives at the apartment in a state of extreme distress, wearing a soaked fisherman's rain hat and an oilcloth raincoat. She repeatedly expresses a sudden, intense hatred and fear of water, claiming she had never previously considered what water could do. Poirot observes her agitation with interest as George helps her remove her wet clothing.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Oliver meets with Poirot to report a murder that occurred the previous night at a Halloween party in Woodleigh, a residential area near London and Manchester. Mrs. Oliver explains that she was visiting a friend, a widow named Judith Butler, and had been invited to provide ideas for the children's party.

The party included traditional games such as biting apples and "Catch the Dragon," which involves burning raisins. Mrs. Oliver reveals that a girl named Joyce, approximately twelve or thirteen years old, was found dead in the library. Joyce had been murdered by someone pushing her head into a heavy, zinc-lined bucket filled with water and floating apples, causing her to drown. Mrs. Oliver notes that the use of a heavy bucket rather than a plastic one might have prevented the incident.

Mrs. Oliver expresses deep distress and a sudden aversion to apples. She clarifies that she did not know Joyce personally but was present because of her connection to Judith Butler. She suggests that the crime may be linked to her own identity, noting that a child at the party recognized her as an author of murder mystery books. Poirot attempts to gather facts regarding the victim's development and the upcoming police interrogation, while Mrs. Oliver implies that the circumstances of the death are more complex than they initially appear.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Oliver meets with Hercule Poirot to discuss the murder of Joyce, a boastful and self-important girl found dead in a library. Mrs. Oliver explains that during the party hosted by Roya Delek, which was attended by approximately twenty-five to thirty people including various mothers, school teachers, and young people, Joyce had made a startling claim. Earlier that day, while preparing for the party, Joyce insisted she had witnessed a murder a long time ago, though she had not realized it was a murder until she suddenly reflected on it. Although the adults and children mocked her and did not believe her, Mrs. Oliver now suspects Joyce was telling the truth and was killed because she was a witness.

Poirot observes that the murder was a cruel, well-timed act involving holding the victim's head under a bucket of water. He theorizes that someone who heard Joyce's claims earlier that day might have known the identity of the original killer and murdered Joyce to prevent her from revealing the truth. Poirot suggests the killer might be someone protecting a family member.

Following the discussion, Poirot travels to a house called "The Crowned One" on a hilltop. There, he encounters Superintendent Spence, who is tending to his garden. Poirot reveals that he found Spence's location through a Christmas card the Superintendent had sent. The two men share a past connection involving a previous murder case.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot meets with Spence on a glass-enclosed terrace in Woodleigh Village. Spence explains that he moved in with his sister, Elsa, after her husband passed away and her children moved abroad. While sharing spiced ginger beer, the two men discuss a recent murder case involving a child whose head was found in a water bucket. 

Poirot attempts to enlist Spence's help, arguing that despite Spence's long retirement from the police, he still possesses a detective's perspective and access to rumors. Spence expresses skepticism, noting that modern social dynamics and the tendency of women to defend "bad boy" partners make investigations difficult. 

The conversation shifts to their shared history. Poirot reveals he was asked to investigate the current case by the mystery novelist Ariadne Oliver. This prompts a recollection of a past case involving the murder of Miss Macquet, which Spence had previously asked Poirot to solve. They briefly reminisce about a difficult individual from that old case, described as a man suited to being a mortician. 

Poirot clarifies that Mrs. Oliver was staying at the house of a woman named Mrs. Butler, a widow whose husband was a pilot. Poirot explains that Mrs. Oliver traveled to London to find him because she was deeply uneasy about the incident and wanted him to intervene. Although Spence acknowledges that he also sought Poirot's help, he insists that he cannot assist with the current investigation.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Superintendent Spence discuss the murder of a thirteen-year-old girl. Poirot describes a cruel method of killing involving a trick to bite an apple, which suggests a perpetrator who might have a history of child abuse. Spence notes that the local community is unstable with high population turnover, though some residents like Miss Emery and Dr. Ferguson are long-term inhabitants. Spence identifies two potential suspects from the party age group: Nicholas Lanchester, a seventeen or eighteen-year-old man, and a person named Desmond, who was previously held in custody due to a psychological report. Spence also considers the possibility that an outsider entered through an open door or window.

The investigation is driven by a claim made by a girl named Joyce. Poirot recounts to Spence that Joyce, during a conversation about Mrs. Oliver’s books, mentioned having seen a murder once. While Mrs. Oliver and Spence initially view this as a tall tale or exaggeration, Poirot argues that the girl's death shortly after making the claim suggests a causal link. If Joyce had witnessed a crime years prior—perhaps an accident she did not realize was intentional—the discussion of murder may have triggered her memory, prompting the killer to act. Spence notes that the party included various adults, such as Dr. Ferguson and the parish priest, as well as approximately fourteen children. Poirot suggests the murderer may be someone who appears unremarkable and is now acting out of shock because they have been discovered.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After piquing the interest of Inspector Spence, a retired high-ranking official from the Criminal Investigation Department, Poirot departs to meet Mrs. Oliver at a Georgian-style red-brick house named "Apple Orchard." Poirot intends to use Spence’s connections to the local police to gather information and plans to involve Spence's sister, Elspeth, who is well-acquainted with the local community.

Upon arriving at Apple Orchard, Poirot meets Mrs. Oliver. He explains his decision to stay in a hotel rather than at Miss Judith Butler's house, noting that a detached perspective allows for more comprehensive observation. Poirot outlines his investigative plan: he intends to examine the crime scene of the thirteen-year-old girl's death, speak with the victim's mother, meet with the local inspector, the local doctor, and the school headmaster. He also schedules a meeting for six o'clock at the Spence house to discuss the case with Spence and his sister.

Mrs. Oliver introduces Poirot to Mrs. Dreyfus, a tall, athletic, and efficient woman in her mid-forties who organized the social event where the murder occurred. Poirot observes that Mrs. Dreyfus appears to be suppressing emotions, suspecting she feels a sense of inadequacy or anger that a tragedy occurred during an event she arranged. Mrs. Dreyfus greets Poirot and expresses her hope that he can help, while noting the local stir caused by the child's death and commenting on the increasing frequency of such crimes in the countryside.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Dribb shows Hercule Poirot the crime scene following the death of a young girl named Joyce. Mrs. Dribb suggests that an outsider, possibly a person from a mental institution, may have interfered with the orderly event. She explains that the police believe the crime occurred during a game of "Dragon Hunt" played in the dining room. Poirot then inspects the library, where a bucket with plastic sheeting underneath was used; Mrs. Dribb notes that she had added extra water to the bucket during a game of "bite the apple." Mrs. Oliver declines to enter the library to avoid painful memories.

Poirot questions Mrs. Dribb about Joyce’s character, suggesting that the victim's temperament might be a motive for the crime. Mrs. Dribb describes Joyce as a thirteen-year-old girl who was not very likable and liked to show off. She reveals that Joyce had claimed to have witnessed a murder, but Mrs. Dribb dismissed this as nonsense and boasting intended to attract attention from her friends and the celebrity guest, Mrs. Oliver. Mrs. Dribb notes that Joyce's sister had silenced her when she made the claim. When Poirot asks if any murders have occurred in the Woodley community, Mrs. Dribb recalls a distant case involving a truck driver and a buried girl but insists Joyce's claims were untrue. After Poirot departs with Mrs. Oliver, he muses that the location lacks the atmosphere for a murder and contains no one worth killing, though he wonders if someone might want to kill Mrs. Dreyfus.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot and Mrs. Oliver discuss the character of Roya Drake, a dominating woman who effectively runs the community, and the circumstances surrounding the death of Joyce, a thirteen-year-old girl. Mrs. Oliver notes that while some locals speculate the death was a rape, there were no signs of sexual assault. Mrs. Oliver then introduces Poirot to Mrs. Reynolds, Joyce's mother, who is described as a slow-minded woman dressed in black mourning clothes.

Mrs. Reynolds expresses her grief and suspicion toward the death, suggesting that a man might have broken in through a window to kill her daughter by holding her head underwater. She dismisses the possibility of a female killer. When Poirot asks about Joyce's claims of witnessing a murder, Mrs. Reynolds believes her daughter was merely joking or mixing things up, as she does not believe Joyce could have seen such an event. Mrs. Oliver adds that other children at the party likely laughed at Joyce or thought she was making things up to avoid encouraging her.

Poirot attempts to determine if Joyce had witnessed anything suspicious in the past, noting that a thirteen-year-old might claim to be "too young" to report something. Mrs. Reynolds, who was not at the party due to feeling unwell, mentions her other children: sixteen-year-old Anne and eleven-year-old Leo. Poirot subsequently speaks with Leo in the garden, where the boy is working on an airplane model. Leo dismisses Joyce's claims, stating that she frequently boasts and says anything to get attention.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Leopold tells Hercule Poirot that Joyce Reynolds likely fabricated her claim of seeing a murder to gain attention, noting that Beatrice and Kitty laughed at her story. Poirot then questions Anne, Joyce's sister, who confirms she heard the claim but did not take it seriously. Anne explains that Joyce was a habitual liar who frequently exaggerated stories, such as a trip to India, to impress others. Anne notes that while there have been deaths in the area, no unusual murder cases have occurred in Woodley for several years.

Later, at Scone Manor, Poirot dines with Inspector Spence and Elspeth McKay. Elspeth characterizes Joyce as a "master of deception" who told outrageous stories to seek attention. While she acknowledges that Joyce might have seen something, she remains skeptical of her credibility. When Poirot inquires about potential victims from the last three years, Spence provides a list of names, including Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, Charlotte Belfield, Janet White, and Leslie Firrel. 

Poirot becomes interested in Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith. Elspeth mentions a "call girl"—clarified to be a foreign student—who lived with an old lady and disappeared shortly after the woman's death. Although Spence suggests the student ran off with a man, Poirot questions the lack of rumors regarding the man's identity. While the death of Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith was attributed to regular heart trouble, Poirot questions why Spence placed her at the top of his list of potential victims.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot discusses the death of Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, a wealthy widow of a shipbuilder who lived in Stone Quarry House, a Victorian mansion featuring an underground garden created from an abandoned quarry. While her death was attributed to natural causes—specifically heart failure following exertion in her garden—Poirot investigates potential foul play. 

The discussion reveals a controversy regarding a codicil to her will. While her previous wills left most property to her nephew and his wife, the Drakes, this new codicil left everything to a young nurse from Central Europe. The Manchester law firm Fuller, Harrison, and Leedbetter identified the codicil as a forgery. It was noted that the nurse had experience mimicking handwriting, having previously written letters for Lady Lintin-Smith. Following extensive questioning by the lawyers, the nurse fled the country, leaving her belongings behind. There is a suggestion that the nurse may have served the victim a drink that tasted strange or bitter before her death.

Poirot also inquires about Charlotte Belfield, a sixteen-year-old shop assistant found dead with multiple head injuries near a mining area. Two young men, Peter Gordon and Thomas Heddle, were identified as potential suspects. Peter Gordon is an unemployed man with a history of petty theft and associations with delinquents. Thomas Heddle is a shy, anxious stationery store worker with a stammer. Although jealousy was considered a possible motive, both men had alibis: Heddle’s mother claimed he was home with her, and Gordon’s friends provided an alibi, though the credibility of these statements was questioned.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot, Spence, and Eleanor discuss past murder cases to find connections to the recent death of Joyce Reynolds. They first examine the case of Leslie Firrell, a law firm clerk at Fullerton, Harrison & Ladbate who was stabbed from behind near the Green Swan Inn eighteen months ago. Firrell had a criminal record for forgery and possessed a large amount of unexplained cash. Suspects included the innkeeper Harry Griffin, his wife, or members of Firrell's gang. Poirot dismisses this case as unlikely to be related to Joyce.

They then discuss the death of Janet White, a teacher who was strangled on a shortcut two and a half years ago. Her roommate, Nora Ambrose, noted that Janet had been receiving threatening letters from an unnamed man who had broken up with her. Poirot finds this case more relevant, theorizing that Joyce might have witnessed a murder in the past—perhaps Janet's—and only realized the significance of what she saw as she grew older. Poirot suggests that Joyce was killed because she claimed to have seen a murder while preparing for a Halloween party.

Spence provides a list of eighteen people present during the party preparations, including Mrs. Dreyfus, Miss Whitaker, Rev. Charles Cotterell, and the Reynolds family. Spence notes that the list may be incomplete as people frequently arrived and left. Finally, Poirot meets with Dr. Ferguson, a Scottish doctor, who discusses the cruelty of the child's death and the dangers posed by mentally deranged individuals in society.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses the murder of Joyce Reynolds with the family doctor, who was Joyce's physician. The doctor explains that Joyce was a healthy child who suffered occasionally from biliary disease but had no unusual medical history. When Poirot suggests that Joyce might have been killed because she "said too much," the doctor notes that while a witness's testimony can be a motive, the killer's motive might simply lie in a disturbed or evil mind. The doctor posits that the killer was likely present at the Halloween party, potentially as a guest, a servant, or someone who entered through a window after checking the latches.

Poirot then visits the Elm Tree School to meet Miss Emery. He explains that he is investigating the murder at the invitation of Mrs. Ariadne Oliver. Miss Emery describes Joyce as an ordinary child with a compulsive habit of lying to attract attention and boast to her friends. She suggests that Joyce’s claim of witnessing a murder earlier that day was likely an exaggeration of a mundane event, such as a car accident, intended to impress others or Mrs. Oliver. Miss Emery notes that most children at the party were between nine and fifteen years old and associated with the school. She also mentions a previous unsolved crime at the school: the strangulation of a melancholy twenty-four-year-old teacher named Miss White, which occurred a year or two prior.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After discussing the acceptance of murder with Miss Emlyn, Poirot is introduced to Elizabeth Whitaker, who is suggested as a person of interest regarding the death of Joyce Reynolds. Poirot explains he is investigating privately through a friend rather than the police.

Whitaker, a guest at the party, describes the evening's events. She recounts that during the final program—a "dragon hunt" game involving burning grapes—she stepped into the hallway to escape the heat. There, she observed Mrs. Dreckly (referred to elsewhere as Mrs. Dribb) emerging from a first-floor washroom carrying a large, heavy vase of flowers and autumn leaves. As Mrs. Dreckly stood at the staircase landing, she appeared to be staring intently toward the library door, which is located directly opposite the dining room door. Whitaker notes that Mrs. Dreckly seemed suddenly startled, as if she had seen something unexpected, such as a door opening or a person appearing. This shock caused Mrs. Dreckly to lose her grip, shattering the vase and spilling water on the hall floor.

Whitaker believes Mrs. Dreckly was frightened by something she saw near the library, though Mrs. Dreckly did not mention being scared at the time. Poirot observes that Mrs. Dreckly is not easily frightened and suggests that someone may have been in the library and retreated inside upon hearing the vase break, only to slip away once the guests were leaving. Whitaker concludes by detailing the party's sequence of games, which included a broom race, a balloon race, a mirror trick involving reflections, an obstacle race, a flour and sixpence game, dancing, and dinner.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an interrogation, Elizabeth Whitaker, a mathematics and Latin teacher, tells Hercule Poirot that she did not know Joyce well but remembers seeing her clumsy while cutting flour. Poirot asks about Janet White, a teacher from two years prior. Whitaker becomes visibly unsettled, questioning if the incident is related to the current case. She reveals that Janet White was strangled one evening on her way home through a wood near a mining area. Poirot inquires about Nora Ambrose, noting that Janet and Nora were close and both were considered "too free." Whitaker explains that Nora left school to work in the north of England following the incident, which the police never solved.

Poirot later visits Stonehaven House, where an elderly woman informs him that the Colonel and Mrs. Weston are in London. He proceeds to Stonehaven Wood to visit the Quarry Garden, an underground garden created from a derelict quarry by a gardener named Michael Garfield. As Poirot admires the meticulously arranged autumnal landscape, he reflects on the connection between a forged will, a missing girl, and the garden's creation. He contemplates whether the garden's design reflects the specific vision of his patron, Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, or the artistic execution of Michael Garfield, noting that the garden appears to grow freely despite being strictly planned.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While walking through the hillside gardens of Stone Court Manor, Hercule Poirot observes the meticulous design of the landscape, which features rare shrubs and well-maintained paths equipped with comfortable seating. Poirot reflects on the beauty of the scenery and recalls a conversation regarding a murder that occurred years ago in an old quarry near the site. He notices a silhouette in the bushes and encounters a tall, slender, and exceptionally handsome man in his late thirties or early forties.

The man introduces himself as Michael Garfield, the architect of the garden. Garfield explains that the current owners, a retired Colonel and his wife, purchased the unprofitable house at a low price. Garfield reveals that the late Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, who financed the garden, left the property to him in her will, though he subsequently sold the house and the Stone Mine Garden.

During their conversation, Poirot questions Garfield about his motives and his relationship with his former patron. Garfield admits that while he promised to follow Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith's instructions and views, he actually implemented his own ideas by presenting them as her own plans. He compares this professional tactic to selling a brown egg to a child by framing it as a premium rural product rather than just an egg. Garfield asserts that to be a successful artist and businessman, one must skillfully sell their own ideas through the guise of a client's blueprints.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot meets Michael Garfield in an underground garden, where Poirot praises the aesthetic beauty of the location. During their conversation, Poirot notes that Ariadne Oliver, a best-selling author, helped bring him to the area. Poirot mentions that apples from a Halloween evening party brought him there and remarks that attending a murder party is an unpleasant experience. When discussing a child named Joyce, Garfield dismisses her as insignificant and boring. Poirot concludes the conversation by noting that murders are typically motivated by interests, fears, or love.

After Poirot departs, he is met by a twelve-year-old girl named Miranda, who is waiting to escort him to tea with Mrs. Butler and Ariadne Oliver. Miranda identifies herself as the daughter of Mrs. Butler and the niece of Ariadne Oliver. As they walk through the garden, Miranda describes her habit of observing nature and people from trees. She reveals that the garden was once a quarry where a woman's body was found. Miranda discloses that the victim, Joyce, died by drowning, a fact she learned from overhearing the cook, Mrs. Pelling, and the cleaning lady, Mrs. Mundt, discussing how Joyce's head was dunked in a bucket of water. Miranda explains she was not at the party due to a fever and a sore throat. Miranda then leads Poirot through a hedge and past a cucumber frame to a small bungalow, where she announces she has brought him safely.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot visits Mrs. Oliver (Ariadne) at her home, where he is introduced to her friend, Judith Butler. Judith, a woman with golden hair and sea-green eyes, met Ariadne in Greece after pulling her from the sea. During tea, served by Ariadne's daughter, Miranda, the conversation turns to the murder of Joyce Reynolds. Mrs. Butler questions why Joyce, who was known for lying, would suddenly claim to have seen a murder, but Poirot confirms that Joyce was indeed murdered.

The group discusses biblical stories from the Apocrypha, specifically the tale of Judith and Holofernes and the story of Jael, who killed Sisera by driving a nail into his head. Miranda expresses her distaste for such violence, stating that if she had to deal with enemies, she would prefer to use a drug to allow them to die peacefully in their sleep. 

After Miranda leaves to wash the dishes, Mrs. Oliver expresses her anxiety regarding the local atmosphere and the safety of children, urging Poirot to find the killer so the community can feel at ease again. Poirot and Mrs. Oliver move into the garden to sit on a stone bench among the Elizabethan queen roses. There, Mrs. Oliver asks Poirot for his impression of Judith.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot and Mrs. Oliver discuss Judith, a charming widow whose husband, a pilot, died in a car accident. Mrs. Oliver notes that Judith performs clerical work and has one child, Miranda. Poirot expresses interest in investigating the deceased and the missing, specifically a girl exchange student who may have been involved in legal trouble. 

Poirot shows Mrs. Oliver his notebook, which contains a "Death List" including the wealthy Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith and Miss Janet White, a school teacher and law firm employee who was killed with a knife and had a history of forgery. He also notes "Missing Girl's Cry" and the word "forgery" regarding a potential addition to a will that favored the exchange girl. Poirot mentions the word "elephant" might be connected to the case. He informs Mrs. Oliver that he has an appointment at the firm Fullerton, Harrison, and Readbate in Manchester to discuss the forgery and speak with people involved in the preparation process.

At the law firm, the senior partner, Mr. Jeremy Fullerton, receives Poirot, who is accompanied by Inspector Henry Largent and retired Inspector Spence. Fullerton recalls a past case where Spence had insisted the wrong man was convicted, a case eventually solved by a Belgian detective. Fullerton reflects on the murder of a child at a gala, noting he has a sharp idea regarding the killer but remains uncertain among three suspects: three idle young men.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot interviews Mr. Fullerton, the legal advisor for the Dray family and the late Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, regarding the death of a young girl named Joyce Reynolds. Fullerton provides background on the Dray family, noting that Hugo Dray became lame due to polio, and discusses the wealthy Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, who died of heart disease two years prior. 

Poirot shifts the inquiry to one of Fullerton's former employees, Leslie Ferrell (referred to by Fullerton as Leslie Firr), who was stabbed to death years ago near the Green Swan Inn in Woodley. Fullerton explains that while the police suspected the motive was jealousy involving a married woman or other girls Leslie was involved with, no one was caught due to a lack of evidence. Fullerton offers an alternative hypothesis: Leslie may have been killed by a gang after attempting to leave their illegal counterfeit transactions.

Poirot suggests that a child might have witnessed the murder of Leslie Ferrell but remained silent due to fear or the desire to keep a secret. Fullerton expresses skepticism, arguing that rumors are common and that children would likely tell their mothers. Poirot maintains that children often process traumatic or confusing sights in silence until a later event triggers their memory. He implies a potential connection between the death of Joyce Reynolds and the unsolved murder of Leslie Ferrell, though Fullerton remains dismissive of such a link.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a conversation with Hercule Poirot, a legal professional discusses the disappearance of Olga Seminova, a foreign nurse who cared for Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, the aunt of Hugo Drake. While Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith had previously intended her estate to pass to Hugo and his wife, a codicil written three weeks before her death diverted a substantial portion of her wealth to Olga. However, handwriting experts determined the codicil was a forgery. Olga had previously been tasked by Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith to imitate her handwriting for private letters, and she attempted to use this skill to pass off the forged document. Following a lawsuit regarding the document's authenticity, Olga disappeared.

Jeremy Fullerton, a lawyer, reflects on his past encounter with Olga two years prior. He recalls her as a passionate, resilient woman from Central Europe. Although he felt compassion for her difficult life, he remained a proponent of the law. Fullerton recalls a meeting where Olga sought his legal representation, claiming Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith left her the money because the woman pitied her suffering and disliked her relatives. Olga argued that Mrs. Drake was overbearing and that her aunt deserved to enjoy her wealth. Olga defended her actions, claiming she was merely being kind by bringing Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith forbidden foods like chocolate and butter, despite medical advice. Fullerton refused to represent her, noting his existing duty to the Drake family.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Olga Seminova, an exchange student, argues with Mr. Fullerton regarding a disputed will left by the deceased Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith. Olga claims that Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith wrote the document herself, noting that the mistress had previously made Olga practice imitating her handwriting to maintain Victorian social standards for private letters. Olga asserts that the cleaning lady and a gardener named Jim witnessed the signing.

However, Mr. Fullerton reveals that an expert handwriting analysis concluded the codicil was not written by Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith. This suspicion was initially raised by his associate, Small Cole, who noted the handwriting did not match Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith's older files. Mr. Fullerton believes Olga’s greed led her to create a will that excluded all relatives, including a long-term beneficiary nephew, in favor of Olga herself. While moved by Olga's tragic past of loss and injustice, Fullerton maintains that the evidence is against her. He advises her to "come clean" to avoid harsher consequences, suggesting she might receive probation due to being a first-time offender and a non-fluent foreigner.

In response, Olga expresses feelings of persecution and contemplates fleeing Britain using fake papers or disguises, claiming she has saved enough money to disappear. Mr. Fullerton dismisses her plan as childish, warning that an arrest warrant would find her. Following this exchange, Hercule Poirot arrives at an estate, where he is told Mrs. Dreyfus will soon arrive. While waiting in the living room, Poirot observes a meticulously maintained garden filled with asters, chrysanthemums, and roses.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot observes that the garden is meticulously maintained, suggesting that Mrs. Dreyfus may be a hindrance to Michael Garfield. Following a church committee meeting, Poirot speaks with Mrs. Royana Dreyer, a domineering woman. Poirot mentions that Miss Elizabeth Whitaker, a math teacher at Oakwood School, suggested Mrs. Dreyer might have witnessed something significant during the recent party.

Poirot inquires about a vase full of flowers. Mrs. Dreyer recalls that she found a glass vase containing wilted autumn leaves and chrysanthemums on a staircase table without water. She took it to the bathroom to fill it, but found no one there. She then explains that she accidentally dropped and shattered the wedding present in the hall. Miss Whitaker assisted her in sweeping the broken glass behind an old clock.
