While Mrs. Dreyer maintains the breakage was merely a result of her own clumsiness and fatigue, Poirot suspects she was actually frightened by something.

When Poirot asks if she saw anyone leaving the library, Mrs. Dreyer firmly denies seeing anyone. Poirot remains skeptical, suspecting that her determination to deny seeing anyone stems from a protective instinct to exonerate a young person she cares about. Dr. Drake suggests that Miss Whitaker might have seen someone entering the library shortly before the vase incident, potentially a child or a young boy, and that Mrs. Dreyer's reaction was an attempt to avoid being unfair to a person she only partially identified.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses the murder of a child named Joyce with Roya Dreyfus (also referred to as Mrs. Drake/Dreyer/Dreck). Roya suggests the perpetrator is likely a teenager acting out of senseless destruction or revenge, a sentiment she believes the police share. She argues that such crimes are often the result of difficult circumstances, such as broken homes, and advocates for remedial education rather than just imprisonment.

Roya recounts the death of her husband, who was paralyzed by polio six years ago. He was killed in a traffic accident involving a stolen Volkswagen Beetle that had been taken from a man named Mr. Watkinson in Manchester. She maintains that the accident was manslaughter caused by irresponsible youth rather than premeditated murder. Poirot counters this by asserting that Joyce’s death was premeditated murder, as someone intentionally held her head underwater. He suggests the motive might be self-preservation, noting that Joyce had boasted shortly before her death that she had witnessed someone committing a killing.

Roya suggests Poirot consult Miss Emmeline, the headmistress, who she believes has psychological insight into the students. Poirot also inquires about an exchange student who served as a companion to Roya's aunt, Lady Llewellyn-Smith. Roya reveals that the girl, who was friendly with a lawyer in Manchester previously involved in a forgery case, disappeared after Lady Llewellyn-Smith's death. Roya suspects the girl forged a codicil to her aunt's will.

After the conversation, Poirot walks to Helpsley Cemetery, a modern, tidy cemetery located near a small, centuries-old church.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While visiting a cemetery, Poirot reads the gravestone of Hugo Edmund Dreyfus, who died on March 20, 19xx. An elderly gardener approaches Poirot and discusses the deceased, noting that Mr. Dreyfus had been a former athlete who became a cripple due to polio. The gardener describes the widow, Roya Alabella Dreyfus, as a bossy, highly active woman who managed many village associations and spent years caring for her husband. The gardener suggests that because she has no children and has completed all her local responsibilities, she will likely move abroad to a place like Greece, Spain, or Portugal to find new things to manage.

Poirot asks the gardener for the location of Nicholas Lansham and Desmond Holland, who the gardener explains live past the church in the third house on the left, boarding with the Brandes. Poirot subsequently visits the two young men, aged eighteen and sixteen, who had assisted Mrs. Dribb with preparations for a Halloween party by climbing ladders, hanging lights, and placing golden pumpkins. Poirot notes that while they are well-mannered and dressed in expensive, modern clothing, they are the type of suspects the police and the gardener might target due to their age. Poirot engages them in conversation, seeking their astute views on the case, while observing their mannerisms and the work they performed during the party preparations.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Desmond and Nicholas, two young men helping at a party, show Hercule Poirot distorted photographs they created using makeup and different hairstyles to act as "imagined husbands" for the girls. They used lighting and mirrors to project these images, a setup that pleased Mrs. Drecker. Desmond notes that during the afternoon, several people were present, including Mrs. Drecker, Mrs. Butler, a school teacher named Miss Whitaker, Mrs. Flettbard, and Miss Lee, the pharmacist. The Reynolds children were also there, including the victim, Joyce Reynolds, her sister Anne, and a boy named Leopold, alongside Beatrice Adler and Kathy Grant. Men present included the vicar and a stuttering assistant vicar.

When Poirot asks about Joyce Reynolds, Nicholas mentions a rumor that she claimed to have seen a murder through extrasensory perception shortly before her death. Desmond and Nicholas deny hearing this directly, as they were busy installing lights and decorations. Desmond suggests Miss Whitaker as a suspect, citing her perceived sexual frustration and a past incident involving a strangled teacher. Nicholas suggests that Miss Emery might know more about Whitaker's character. Desmond also speculates that the assistant curate might have killed Joyce in a ritualistic manner involving apples.

Later, Poirot speaks with Miss Goodbody, a pleasant woman with a witch-like face. She explains that she writes poems using the girls' baptismal names, which are read aloud by girls imitating spirits during the mirror illusion. She observes the changing fashions of the young people and reflects on historical clothing styles.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot speaks with Mrs. Goodbody, who clarifies that while she uses a crystal ball for seeing, she does not use a Ouija board. Mrs. Goodbody discusses her belief in people who are "born" evil, citing a cruel girl she knew in Yorkshire. Regarding the Reynolds family, she describes Mr. Reynolds as unremarkable and Mrs. Reynolds as anxious. She characterizes the daughter, Ann, as smart but self-satisfied, and the other daughter, Joyce, as a frequent liar who tries too hard to appear intelligent. She describes the son, Leo, as a highly intelligent boy interested in physics and math, but warns he is a trickster who likely uses secrets to extort hush money from others. When Poirot asks about a foreign girl who supposedly ran away, Mrs. Goodbody implies she will not get far.

Later, Mrs. Oliver is approached by Mrs. Liman, a cleaning lady who previously worked for Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith. Mrs. Liman seeks advice regarding a legal matter from the past involving a codicil to Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith's will. Mrs. Liman explains that the codicil left all the money to the foreign girl who cared for Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith. This caused suspicion among relatives, specifically Mrs. Drake, who wanted to invalidate the document. Mrs. Liman notes that lawyers suggested the foreign girl might have written the codicil herself.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Lyman confesses to Mrs. Oliver that she witnessed Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith write a codicil to her will. Mrs. Lyman explains that while she was working for the wealthy widow, she, a gardener named Jim, and a foreign girl named Miss Olga (possibly Miss Orlanda Seminova) were present when Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith wrote several lines with an ink pen and signed the document. Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith then had Mrs. Lyman and Jim sign the paper as witnesses. Mrs. Lyman observed the widow hide the signed document in an envelope inside a Victorian-era book titled *The Search for All Mysteries* on a bottom bookshelf.

Driven by curiosity, Mrs. Lyman admits she searched for the book the following day while Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith was in Manchester. She discovered the envelope and read the document, which bequeathed the widow's fortune to Miss Olga in recognition of her care during the widow's illness. Mrs. Lyman returned the papers to the book to avoid detection. 

Mrs. Lyman notes that rumors later circulated claiming the codicil was a forgery. Although a lawsuit was initiated, it was dropped without a trial, and Miss Olga fled to Central Europe. Mrs. Lyman initially dismissed the situation but became unsettled following a recent murder involving a child pushed into a bucket of water. She now suspects Miss Olga may have killed Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith to secure the inheritance and has approached Mrs. Oliver for help, emphasizing that she only read the document and did not steal it.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Harriet Lymon (formerly Harriet Lyman) informs Mrs. Oliver that she and a man named James Jenkins witnessed Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith sign an addition to her will, confirming the signature is authentic. Lymon notes that Jenkins moved to Australia a year and a half ago and is no longer local. Lymon expresses guilt for remaining silent about the will and mentions that a child named Joey witnessed Miss Olga killing Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith. Lymon observes that Miss Olga appeared smug regarding a potential inheritance but fled suddenly when the police arrived. Mrs. Oliver advises Lymon to tell this information to Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith's lawyer and promises to speak for her.

Later, Hercule Poirot joins Mrs. Oliver. While discussing the case, Poirot realizes the importance of "dates," noting that the roots of the present murder lie in a past event. He explains that a murder occurring years ago, witnessed by a girl, led to that girl being killed four days ago. Poirot mentions that his investigation has yielded facts arranged by date and notes that no one believes the testimony of Joyce Reynolds. Poirot also discusses the possibility of a forged will, suggesting that while people suspect the foreign exchange student, there was another forger in the village. This forger, whom Poirot does not know, died approximately two years ago after being killed by a knife following a dispute involving jealousy and a girl. Poirot suggests that these seemingly independent incidents may be interconnected.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Mrs. Oliver discuss the importance of dates and timelines regarding a suspected forgery. Poirot considers the possibility that a foreign girl forged a will to become a beneficiary. He asks Mrs. Oliver if she can accommodate two guests at her London apartment for prudence, though she expresses reluctance to host people. During their conversation, Mrs. Oliver explains her method of writing fiction, noting that she observes strangers to create characters like "Constance Cannaby" but avoids using people she actually knows. She mentions her acquaintances, Judith Butler and her child Miranda, noting they seem to be involved in an "interesting play."

Mrs. Oliver reveals new information: the document in question might not be a counterfeit. She explains that Mrs. A. Jones Smith may have legitimately included a codicil in her will leaving her money to an illegitimate daughter, witnessed by Harriet Liman and James Jenkins. Poirot notes that while a real document may have existed, a forged codicil is also present, which lawyers intend to challenge in court. Mrs. Oliver theorizes that the beneficiary might have forged a new codicil after the original was destroyed during a dispute with relatives.

To verify facts, Poirot uses Judith Butler's telephone to call Mr. Fullerton, the lawyer involved at the time. Poirot discovers that the forged codicil presented at probate was not witnessed by Mrs. Liman, but by Mary Doltie, a former employee of the Llewellyn-Smiths who has since passed away. The other witness remains James Jenkins, who went to Australia.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Oliver and Hercule Poirot discuss the existence of both a forged and a real codicil regarding Lady Llewellyn-Smith's estate. Mrs. Oliver suggests the real codicil might be hidden in a book titled "In Search of All Mysteries" at Stone Court, though she notes that most of the estate's contents were sold after Llewellyn-Smith's death. Mrs. Oliver questions if Mrs. Lymon was coerced into lying and expresses interest in the disappearance of Olga. Poirot reveals his assistant in London is gathering information and expects news from Bosnia, which may help him learn if Olga has returned or find letters she wrote to a confidante. Mrs. Oliver also mentions a strangled schoolteacher previously noted by Elizabeth Whitaker, speculating on the possibility of murder.

Later, Pollock (referred to as Poirot in the text) walks through a path in a stone quarry garden that appears to have been widened by someone larger than Miranda Butler. He reflects on the garden's eerie, pagan atmosphere and the certainty that the will sent for examination was a forgery, noting that the lawyer Mr. Fulleton is too capable to suggest legal action without evidence. While walking, he encounters Michael Garfield sketching in the garden and Miranda Butler standing by a nearby stream. Poirot observes Michael's skill at pencil sketching and engages him in conversation. Michael explains that he draws to record things before they disappear, specifically mentioning Miranda as a subject he wishes to remember. Miranda approaches, claiming her presence by the stream is a coincidence and not a planned modeling session.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miranda tells Mr. Polo and Michael Gaffield that she is searching for a legendary wishing well in the forest near the old quarry. She mentions that Grandma Goodbody, whom she calls a witch, claims the well was sealed up years ago because it was dangerous, possibly after a child named Kitty fell in. Michael Gaffield tells Miranda that while there is a regular well in White Hill, the legend of the wishing well is likely a story made up by Mrs. Goodbody. He reveals to Poirot that there was actually a wishing tree—a beech tree on a hill—that people circled to make wishes, but it was split by lightning six years ago.

After Miranda leaves, Poirot and Gaffield discuss Gaffield’s restlessness. Gaffield, a designer of houses and gardens, expresses dissatisfaction with his work and a desire to move, possibly to Greece, because clients like Drayton insist on impractical or showy designs rather than following his professional suggestions. 

The conversation shifts to the sudden death of Leslie Firle, a junior lawyer from a Manchester firm. Gaffield explains that Leslie was killed with a knife, and local speculation suggests it was due to a jealous affair with a woman named Sandra. Poirot inquires about Olga, suggesting a connection between her and Leslie. Gaffield confirms that Leslie and Olga were seen together often, though Olga is cautious and has not confided in him. Gaffield notes that Leslie was a charming man who was involved with several women, which caused upset among his girlfriends. Poirot expresses interest in investigating the events and "unsavory things" that occurred before Leslie and Olga met.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses a theory with Michael Gaffield regarding a forged will belonging to the deceased Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith. Poirot suggests that Leslie Ferrell, a man with a prior forgery charge, may have forged the will to benefit a close female friend who stands to inherit a fortune. Poirot suspects Ferrell used his relationship with the girl to facilitate the crime, potentially using funds from her employer, Olga Seminova, to fund the scheme. Gaffield, preferring to maintain a peaceful atmosphere in his woods, urges Poirot not to investigate or expose the truth.

Later, Poirot visits Elspeth McKay, who informs him that her brother has been summoned to the police station by Tim Largheon. Elsa Petheridge provides Poirot with a letter from a foreign office regarding Olga Seminova. The letter reveals that Olga’s family is dead and her last correspondence, sent eighteen months prior, mentioned a young man she hoped to marry. Poirot concludes this supports his theory that Leslie Ferrell may have manipulated the situation.

Upon returning to his hotel, Poirot finds Roya DeRoc waiting in the lounge. She is visibly anxious and distraught. She reveals that a child has been killed: Leopold Reynolds. Roya explains that his body was found on a country lane; it appears someone pushed him into a nearby stream and held his head underwater. Poirot draws a connection between this death and a previous incident involving someone named Joyce.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Dreyfus confesses to Hercule Poirot that she witnessed Leopold, the ten-year-old brother of the deceased Joyce Reynolds, exiting the library with a strange expression. She initially withheld this information because she believed Leopold had killed his sister. However, she now realizes Leopold was likely shocked to find Joyce dead and was attempting to slip away unnoticed. Dr. Dreyfus explains that she remained silent to protect the child, believing he was too young to be held morally responsible. She further reveals her theory that the killer drowned Leopold in a stream to prevent him from testifying, as he had either seen the murderer or evidence of the crime. Poirot notes that Leopold had recently been receiving large amounts of pocket money, suggesting someone was paying him for his silence.

Following this, Poirot consults with Inspector Spence and Inspector Raglan before driving to the Oak Tree School to visit Miss Emery. He informs her that he knows who killed Joyce Reynolds and seeks her validation. Poirot presents Miss Emery with a piece of paper containing four words; she agrees with the first two but finds the latter two a stretch due to a lack of evidence. Poirot connects the concept of "water" to Leopold's death by drowning. He asserts that Leopold's death was not an accident but a consequence of his decision to risk his life for money. Poirot concludes that the killer is using murder as a means of self-preservation and intends to return to London to develop a plan of action to uphold justice and prevent further killings.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses his difficulty in determining the method and motive of a case with Miss Emery. He asks for her opinion on the trustworthiness of Nicholas Lansham and Desmond Holland; Miss Emery describes them as fundamentally flawless and trustworthy. Poirot then departs to visit another person.

In a supermarket, Elspeth MacInnes and Mrs. Cartwright discuss activity in the quarry woods. Mrs. Cartwright notes that lumberjacks are digging deep around tree roots on a steep slope, and police are present to keep people away from the potentially dangerous trees.

Ariadne Oliver receives an urgent telegram instructing her to bring Judith Butler and Miranda to her house immediately for an urgent surgery. Although Judith is hesitant to leave her home and expresses concern about leaving Miranda with the Reynolds family—noting that the Reynoldses have lost two children—Ariadne insists they must go. Ariadne reveals that the telegram was a message from Hercule Poirot. She suggests the "surgery" mention might be a red herring and plans to take them to London for the ballet. After Miranda briefly leaves to say goodbye to a friend, the group packs and departs for London, planning to stop at the Black Boy Inn at Haverham for lunch.

In London, Hercule Poirot meets with Inspector Timothy Largely, Superintendent Spence, Chief Constable Alfred Richmond, and a prosecutor from the Crown Prosecution Service.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot meets with a prosecutor, Inspector Ruggles, and other officials to discuss a disappearance. Poirot asserts that the motive is simple and claims he possesses irrefutable evidence, including a real estate transaction document involving wealthy clients in the West Indies and the Mediterranean. He further claims that within 24 hours, he will provide a witness who saw a murder. Poirot expresses fear for this witness's safety, describing the antagonist as a ruthless and potentially insane individual. While the prosecutor remains skeptical of Poirot, calling him a charlatan, the police commissioner and Inspector Lugg express confidence in his abilities.

Meanwhile, Mrs. Oliver and Judith Butler are having lunch at the Black Child restaurant with Miranda. When Miranda fails to return from the restroom, Judith searches the premises and the garden but cannot find her. Elsewhere, Inspector Goodwin informs Eustacia Mackay that a body has been found in a well; he confirms the victim was stabbed to death, indicating murder rather than suicide.

In reality, Miranda has not been murdered. She slipped out of the restaurant through a garden door and entered a car driven by a man with gray hair and a beard. The man drives her toward the Canterbury Hills to view the Double Axes and the Canterbury Stone Circle. Later, Mrs. Oliver calls Poirot in London to report that Miranda is missing and mentions a sighting of her entering a car with an old man. Poirot informs her that a body has been found in a well, though Mrs. Oliver is unaware of this discovery.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At the Canterbury Stone Circle in November, Miranda is accompanied by a man who discusses the necessity of sacrifice to create beauty. He leads her to a stone carved with a double axe and offers her a golden goblet filled with peach-flavored liquid, telling her to drink so she will always be happy. As Miranda prepares to drink, Nicholas Lanser intervenes, pulling her away from the man and a knife. Lanser informs Miranda that there is no sacrifice ceremony and reveals that a missing exchange student was actually found dead in a well. He identifies the man who brought Miranda to the circle as the person responsible for the girl's death.

In a subsequent meeting involving Poirot, the Chief Constable, and other officials, Miranda is questioned about what she witnessed nearly two years prior. She explains that she previously saw a man and a woman carrying a body in the quarry garden. At the time, she saw blood on a scarf and a knife, but she initially believed it was an accident. She later witnessed the same pair in March, shortly after Easter, discussing a Greek island and a signed agreement. Miranda identifies the two individuals as Mrs. Dreker and Michael. She admits she did not report the incident because Michael had convinced her that the act was a necessary ritual sacrifice.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot explains to Mrs. Oliver that Roya Drake is the killer. He reveals that Drake, acting like a "Lady Macbeth," orchestrated a scene to explain why she was wet after drowning Joyce Reynolds. Drake dropped a vase of flowers to splash herself with water, creating a witness in Miss Whitck to validate her appearance. Poirot notes that Drake and Michael Garfield were partners in crime, motivated by Garfield's obsession with creating a beautiful garden on a Greek island using the Llewellyn-Smith estate.

The motive involved a complex scheme regarding a will. Garfield and Drake intended to inherit the estate by using a forged codicil that was designed to be easily debunked, thereby ensuring the original will—which favored Drake—remained in effect. To protect this plan, they eliminated Olga Seminova, who knew about the genuine codicil, and Leslie Farrar, who was involved in the forgery. Poirot discovered Olga's body in a wishing well in the Quarry Woods after hearing a nursery rhyme reference. He also deduces that the death of little Leopold was a move to silence him because he knew too much.

Poirot describes Garfield as a "Lucifer" figure—a man obsessed with beauty who was willing to sacrifice anything. Judith Butler reveals she is the mother of Garfield's daughter, Miranda, having fled him years ago due to his cold nature. Poirot explains that Garfield planned to sacrifice Miranda in a ritualistic ceremony at the Canterbury Stone Circle to achieve his "new Eden," a plan symbolized by a drawing of her titled "Iphigenia." Ultimately, Garfield dies by his own hand at the stone circle, and Miranda survives.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Captain Arthur Hastings travels by train through the Essex countryside to Styles Manor, which has been converted into a luxury hotel. He is returning to the estate following an invitation from his old friend, Hercule Poirot. Hastings reflects on his past, noting that his first encounter with Poirot at Styles years ago led to a lifelong friendship and a career uncovering murderers. He also recalls his late wife and the changes to the estate, noting that John Cavendish has died and Lawrence has moved to South Africa.

Hastings received a letter from Poirot, who is currently suffering from worsening arthritis and has returned from Egypt for treatment. Poirot invited Hastings to stay at the manor alongside the Franklins and Hastings' daughter, Judith. Hastings describes Judith as an eccentric, intelligent science graduate who works as a secretary for a doctor specializing in tropical diseases.

Upon arriving at the estate, Hastings observes that while the manor's structure remains unchanged, the paint has faded and the grounds are less maintained. He is met by Mrs. Lettice, the wife of the colonel who owns the estate. Mrs. Lettice is a warm but firm businesswoman who manages the hotel operations. She mentions Poirot's eager anticipation of Hastings' arrival and comments on Judith's scientific work, which she views as unladylike. Hastings concludes his arrival by inquiring about Judith's whereabouts.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Captain Hastings arrives at a large house via the 5:40 train. He is greeted by Mrs. Lettrell, who mentions that Dr. Franklin rents an underground laboratory on the property for experimental animals, and by her husband, Colonel Lettrell. During their arrival, they encounter Mr. Norton, a bird watcher, and Sir William Boyd-Carters, a tanned, decisive former provincial head in India and skilled hunter. Sir William, who is currently angry with contractors and architects, expresses his familiarity with Hastings through Poirot and makes a comment about Hastings' daughter, Judith.

Lestrade leads Hastings to his room, which has been updated with modern fixtures and partitioned spaces, though Hastings finds the cheap modern décor disappointing. After being interrupted by a sharp cry from Mrs. Lettrell calling for the Colonel, Hastings proceeds to visit Poirot.

Hastings finds Poirot significantly changed by age and arthritis; the detective is now thin, shriveled, and confined to a wheelchair. Poirot has been using hair dye to maintain a black appearance, which Hastings finds comedically out of place. Despite his physical decline, Poirot maintains a sharp mind and a warm affection for Hastings. Poirot complains about the poor quality of the food, the lack of seasoning, and the inadequate modern amenities at the establishment, such as the lukewarm water and thin towels.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a conversation, Hastings expresses concern regarding Poirot's financial stability due to wartime investments, but Poirot reassures him that he is wealthy. Hastings reflects on the past with nostalgia, but Poirot counters by reminding Hastings that his own memories of that era are romanticized; Poirot recalls that Hastings was actually struggling with injuries, depression, and romantic complications.

Poirot then reveals his true motive for being at their current location: he is there to catch a murderer. He explains that while his physical agility has declined, his mind remains sharp, and he intends to rely on Hastings for the more active aspects of their investigation. Hastings expresses skepticism, noting that the current residents—including the Letruffes, Norton, Boyd Carrington, the Franklins, Judith, Allerton, and Miss Cole—all seem like nice people.

To prepare Hastings, Poirot provides a briefcase containing news clippings and a case summary detailing several tragedies. Hastings reads through five specific cases:
1. Leonard Ezzington died of arsenic poisoning; his wife was acquitted due to circumstantial evidence but later died of a sleeping pill overdose.
2. A frail spinster named Miss Sharples, cared for by her niece Frieda Kre.
3. Edward Riggs, a tenant farmer, killed his wife and her alleged lover, Ben Craig, with his own gun, claiming he had a "blank mind" and was sentenced to life imprisonment.
4. Derek Bradley died from cyanide-laced beer; his wife was convicted of murder and executed.
5. Matthew Ridgfield was killed by a head injury; his daughter Margaret claimed responsibility to free her sisters, but was sent to Broadmoor, where she died.

After reading the summary, Hastings is left feeling confused.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Hastings discuss five seemingly unrelated murder cases involving different motives, such as jealousy, money, and selflessness. Poirot points out that despite their differences, each case has only one clear suspect. He reveals that a mysterious individual, referred to as "X," was present near the victims or locations in all five instances, despite having no motive and being hundreds of miles away during the actual crimes. Hastings concludes that X is the real killer.

Poirot then informs Hastings that X is currently present in Styles Manor and predicts that a murder will soon occur in the house. When Hastings suggests Poirot can prevent the crime, Poirot explains the difficulty of prevention. He outlines three methods: warning the victim, which often fails due to disbelief; or using cunning to intervene at the exact moment of the strike to catch the murderer in the act. Poirot notes that they currently do not know who the target will be because the motive remains unknown. Hastings considers whether the motive is revenge, but Poirot refuses to reveal the identity of X to prevent Hastings from reacting visibly when they encounter the suspect.

The conversation is interrupted by the arrival of Hastings' daughter, Judith, who is described as tall with a serious, tragic air. Poirot teases her about her scientific work, noting her fingers are stained with methylene blue.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Judith and Poirot debate the necessity of marriage before Dr. Franklin enters the room. Dr. Franklin, a tall, thin, and absent-minded man, briefly discusses work and his intention to read to his wife, Barbara, before departing. Judith expresses disdain for Mrs. Franklin, criticizing her for reading sensational novels and obsessing over her illness. During the conversation, Poirot and Hastings discuss Judith's father's preference for women with reddish-brown hair.

Hastings observes that Poirot’s longtime valet, George, has been replaced by a clumsy man named Curtis while George attends to his sick father. As Hastings retires to his room, he experiences a moment of doubt, wondering if Poirot’s claims of an impending murder at Stile's Manor are merely a product of the detective's aging mind and a desire to chase criminals. However, Hastings ultimately decides to assume Poirot is correct and that a murderer, referred to as "X," is present in the house.

At dinner, Hastings is introduced to Miss Cole and Major Allerton. He immediately dislikes Major Allerton, perceiving him as a handsome but dissolute rake who indulges in gambling and drinking. Despite the disapproval of Colonel Lestrade and Boyle Carrington, Allerton remains popular with the women, including Judith. As the guests sit for dinner, Hastings remains alert, attempting to identify which man in the house is the killer.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Captain Hastings mentally evaluates potential suspects for a murder, considering Lieutenant Colonel Lutterell, Mr. Norton, Boyd Cardington, Franklin, and Colonel Alton. He also considers Miss Cole as a possible female suspect, noting her tense and witch-like quality. While reminiscing about Cynthia Murdoch, Hastings is interrupted by Judith, who confronts him about his strange behavior during lunch.

The conversation shifts to a past murder involving a kind, generous woman named Mrs. Inglescott, who was killed with cyanide. Hastings explains that her stepchildren felt like prisoners due to her control over the family wealth. Judith expresses intense indignation toward such "tyranny," arguing that the elderly should not enslave the young. She becomes agitated, comparing such people to "vampires" and praising a woman named Margaret Ritchfield, who killed her cruel, controlling father and turned herself in. Hastings reprimands Judith for defending such dangerous notions.

Judith then informs Hastings that Mrs. Franklin wishes to see him in her bedroom. Upon arriving, Hastings finds Barbara Franklin resting on a sofa bed, accompanied by Mr. Franklin and Boyd Cardington. Mrs. Franklin introduces her nurse, Miss Crawford, a tall, beautiful young woman with reddish-brown hair and notably long, white hands. Miss Crawford remains silent during the introduction, merely tilting her head.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a room with Mrs. Franklin, Barbara Franklin accuses her husband, John, of being a "slave master" to their daughter, Judith, regarding her workload. Judith attempts to deflect the tension by asking John about a "stain" in the lab, prompting John to lead her away to continue their work. 

In the sickroom, Mrs. Franklin expresses guilt over her inability to pay attention to John's work and her physical revulsion toward his scientific subjects, such as rats and guinea pigs. Boyd Carrington, an old playmate of Barbara, comforts her. He reminisces about their youth and mentions his past in Burma, where his wife died. Boyd expresses his belief that Barbara’s illness is rooted in emotional issues caused by her marriage to Franklin, whom he feels does not appreciate her. He suggests that Barbara needs a woman's help to improve her living conditions, and the nurse agrees to assist.

Later, the narrator seeks out Poirot to discuss the investigation. The narrator expresses frustration over a lack of progress and mentions Norton as a potential suspect, though notes Norton is too low-key to be certain. Poirot offers a hypothetical discussion on how suspects can hide in plain sight, using the example of a butcher whose bloodstains would not arouse suspicion. The conversation shifts to the character Alison; the narrator expresses disdain for him, while Poirot observes that villains often possess an attraction to women because people have a natural aversion to excessive safety and seek thrills through danger.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Blackthorn suggests to Poirot that the killer, "X," can be identified by finding the person who knows everyone. Poirot warns him that the investigation is more complex because the guests at the inn are not strangers; the Lestrade couple, who own the inn, have invited friends and acquaintances, including the Franklins, Norton, and Miss Cole. Poirot refuses to reveal more details to protect Blackthorn from the cunning and ruthless killer.

The next morning, Blackthorn speaks with Poirot’s servant, Curtis, who reveals that Poirot’s heart disease has worsened significantly in recent months. Later, Blackthorn joins Norton, Colonel Lestrade, and Mrs. Lestrade for a game of bridge. Mrs. Lestrade plays aggressively and uses her peripheral vision to peek at opponents' cards. She also speaks harshly to her husband, making him nervous and causing him to play poorly. After the game, Norton remarks on how the Colonel has become a "pushover" under his wife's bullying.

While walking in the hall, Blackthorn notices the garden door is open. Norton informs him that Blackthorn's daughter, Judith, is outside with Alton. Disturbed by this, Blackthorn retires to his room but cannot sleep due to anxiety regarding his children's safety and his late wife's absence. Seeking sleeping pills, he encounters Alton in the dim corridor. Alton, wearing a repulsive smile, invites Blackthorn to his room to discuss his insomnia.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a conversation with Alton, the narrator observes him retrieving a potent barbiturate called Slumberwell from a small bathroom closet. Alton admits the drug is difficult to obtain without a prescription but claims to have a way around it. When the narrator asks if Alton knows Ezekiel, Alton becomes cold and defensive, instead pivoting the conversation to Ezra Pound, whom he describes as a drug addict. The narrator returns to his room, suspecting that Alton is "X" despite having openly voiced his suspicions.

At Styles Manor, the narrator observes that Hercule Poirot is physically weak and dependent on Curtis for mobility. The narrator resolves to act as Poirot's "eyes and ears." The narrator also visits Dr. Franklin’s laboratory, where Franklin conducts research on alkaloids extracted from "god's judgment beans." Franklin explains that these West African beans were used by tribes to distinguish the guilty from the innocent; he has discovered that one variety contains a bioalkaloid that counteracts the toxins of the other. During a discussion on morality, Franklin expresses a chilling view that 80% of humanity should be eradicated to improve the lives of the remaining population.

Seeking to test his suspicions regarding Alton, the narrator attempts to probe Judith's feelings toward him. However, the narrator's clumsy approach fails to elicit the desired information, as Judith reacts poorly to the questioning.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a tense argument with his daughter Judith, who rebukes him for interfering in her personal life and her friendship with Colonel Alton, Captain Hastings is approached by Nurse Crawford (also referred to as Nurse Cavell and Nurse Craven). The nurse expresses her disdain for Mrs. Franklin, claiming the woman is unintelligent and manipulative, suggesting she feigns illness and weakness to control her husband, Dr. Franklin. The conversation shifts to the heavy atmosphere of the location, which the nurse attributes to a past murder involving an old lady. Hastings reflects on whether a premeditated murder can leave a lasting psychological mark on a place.

Boyd Carrington, a man of strong and rational personality, joins the group and engages in lighthearted banter with the nurse regarding Judith’s work in the laboratory. After the nurse departs, Carrington invites Hastings to visit Nayton. Hastings consults with Poirot, who encourages the excursion, noting that Carrington is Hastings's "type."

During the trip to the grand estate of Nayton, Hastings enjoys the company of Carrington, who shares anecdotes from his time as a magistrate in India and his experiences in East Africa. The conversation turns to Poirot; Carrington expresses deep respect for Poirot’s character and professional achievements. Despite Poirot's physical decline, Carrington maintains that his mental faculties remain sharp, asserting that physical incapacity does not necessitate mental decline.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Blackstone and Boyd Carrington discuss the nature of crime and impulse before touring Nayton Manor, a Tudor-period estate. Boyd explains that his uncle, Sir Evelyn, was a recluse who left Boyd a large inheritance. Boyd shares his personal grief regarding his late wife, who died of alcoholism, and a missed romantic opportunity with a much younger woman. As they walk through the manor and its gardens, Boyd discusses local residents, including Colonel Lethbridge and his business-minded wife, and mentions Norton, a man who prefers birdwatching to hunting.

As days pass at Styles, an atmosphere of unease persists among the guests. Blackstone expresses frustration to Hercule Poirot about being kept in the dark regarding the identity of "X." Poirot reprimands Blackstone, explaining that the investigation is not a game of guessing identities, but a mission to save lives. Poirot clarifies that the primary objective is to determine who the next victim will be. When Blackstone suggests there must be a connection between the victim and X, Poirot explains that the genius of X lies in the fact that the connection is so subtle that it remains undiscovered.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot and Blackstone debate the presence of a killer at Styles Manor. Poirot asserts that the presence of "X" in the house implies a murder is imminent, using metaphors to explain that certain signs inevitably lead to certain conclusions. While Blackstone remains skeptical and suggests waiting, Poirot argues they must predict the outcome by identifying the potential victim who meets the specific conditions of X's criminal methods. Poirot instructs Blackstone to use his senses to observe the residents, though Blackstone resists spying due to his sense of gentlemanly honor.

The following day, Blackstone suggests involving Boyd Carrington, whom he believes is resourceful and intelligent enough to help. Poirot adamantly refuses, insulting Carrington as an arrogant, talkative man with a terrible memory. This prompts Blackstone to recall an instance where Carrington repeated a story to Poirot that Poirot had originally told him, confirming Carrington's poor memory.

Seeking clarity, Blackstone retreats to a dilapidated pavilion on a hill to analyze the residents' motives. He considers Colonel Lestrade but finds it unlikely he would commit a violent murder during a bridge game. He investigates financial motives, wondering if Mr. Norton, Miss Cole, or Colonel Lestrade might inherit from the wealthy Boyd Carrington. He also contemplates whether Dr. Franklin might poison his wife, Mrs. Franklin, and considers if the attractive Judy might be implicated in a crime due to perceived romantic motives.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Captain Hastings contemplates potential motives for the murder of Alton, wondering if Miss Cole might act out of jealousy. While observing Dr. Franklin walking toward the house in a depressed state, Hastings is interrupted by Miss Cole. Hastings joins Miss Cole and Elizabeth Coleridge at a Victorian gazebo, using the opportunity to observe the reserved and suspicious Coleridge.

During their conversation, Miss Cole reveals that Dr. Franklin is unhappy because his wife prevented him from pursuing a prestigious tropical medicine research opportunity in Africa. She suggests that Mrs. Franklin is a selfish, long-term bedridden patient who manipulates her husband to get her way. Miss Cole clarifies that she learned these details from Hastings' daughter, Judith, who is a dedicated scientist with a disdain for Mrs. Franklin. Hastings expresses concern that Judith’s intense scientific dedication seems unnatural for a young woman.

Miss Cole then provides insights into the other guests: she has known the Lutterer couple for a long time, describing Colonel Lutterer as a nice man and his wife as an aggressive, talkative person. She describes Norton as a kind, sensitive, and introverted man with a keen eye, though he lacks special talents. Miss Cole concludes by reflecting on the oppressive nature of the hotel, noting it is filled with failures, people beaten down by life, or those nearing death. This prompts Hastings to reflect on the shared sense of hopelessness and faded dreams among the guests at Styles, including the ambitious but stymied Dr. Franklin, the shy Norton, and the bedridden Bore.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While reflecting on the past, the narrator observes that Styles has always been a "house of misery." He recalls that his friend John and his wife were miserable, Lawrence Cavendish was gloomy, Cynthia was frustrated by her lack of independence, and Ingersoll married a rich widow for money. The narrator tells Miss Cole that everyone is unhappy, including Judith. Miss Cole notes that Sir William is different because he is a successful man from the outside world rather than an invalid.

Miss Cole then reveals her true identity, explaining that "Richfield" is her mother's maiden name and she was formerly known as Matthew Richfield. She describes her childhood as being like a prison due to her temperamental father. She becomes emotional while discussing her sister, Maggie, who confessed to a crime and turned herself in to the police. The narrator privately believes that Maggie did not commit the crime, though he decides not to tell Miss Cole yet.

Later, around six o'clock, Colonel Luttrell (also referred to as Lethbridge or Letreil) arrives carrying a hunting rifle and dead pigeons. He appears distracted and out of sorts. As the group walks toward the house, they see Mr. Norton and Boyd Carrington on the veranda. Colonel Luttrell offers to buy the men drinks, but as he goes inside to fetch a bottle, his wife, Daisy, interrupts him. She angrily berates him for his lack of business sense and his habit of giving drinks away, eventually seizing the wine bottle and locking it in a cabinet. After Daisy storms out, a visibly aged and upset Colonel Luttrell returns to the group, claiming the whiskey is out. The encounter leaves the group, including Norton and Carrington, feeling stunned and awkward.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While observing Lettrél's wife, Daisy, gardening, the narrator experiences feelings of resentment toward her. Nearby, Norton, Boyd Carlington, and Lettrél engage in conversation. Carlington shares an exaggerated story about an Irish sergeant who accidentally killed his brother while hunting, which lightens the mood. Following the story, Norton praises Carlington’s success, leading Lettrél to suggest such success is often due to luck rather than merit. The conversation becomes awkward when Norton makes a clumsy comment about a wife bullying her husband, prompting a stern, unemotional defense from Lettrél.

The tension is interrupted when Lettrél attempts to shoot a pigeon but misses. He then spots movement in the tall grass, which he believes to be a rabbit eating saplings. He fires his gun, but the shot is followed by a woman's scream and a gurgling sound. The narrator discovers that Lettrél has accidentally shot Daisy in the shoulder while she was securing saplings to stakes. Norton, nauseated by the sight of blood, runs to fetch medical help. Nurse Craven and Dr. Franklin arrive to treat Daisy, who is moved inside to a bed. Dr. Franklin informs the narrator that the bullet missed vital organs.

A dazed and distraught Lettrél is found in the smoking room, claiming he mistook a reflection for a rabbit. Later, the narrator discusses the incident with Judith and Alton. Alton reacts with callousness, suggesting the shooting might have been a "nice way out" for the Colonel or even intentional. This cynicism causes the narrator to doubt the accidental nature of the event. When the narrator later discusses the matter with Boyd Chilton, they both reflect on the earlier conversation regarding the possibility of Daisy provoking Lettrél.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Captain Hastings recounts his habit of providing Hercule Poirot with detailed daily accounts to keep him connected to the outside world. During a conversation, Miss Creevy interrupts to inform Hastings that Mrs. Lestrade has woken up and wishes to see her husband. Hastings finds Lt. Col. Lestrade in a morning room, where the Colonel appears frightened and physically shaken. Hastings supports the unsteady Colonel as they go upstairs to the bedroom. Inside, the couple shares a tender, affectionate moment, which leads Hastings to feel ashamed of his previous speculation that the shooting was intentional.

At dinner, Hastings observes the guests: Mrs. Franklin appears well in a pink gown despite Dr. Franklin's troubled demeanor; Alton and Judith go to the garden after the meal; and Boyd Carlington chats with Mrs. Franklin. Elizabeth Cole remains engrossed in a book, appearing somewhat uncomfortable around Hastings. Later, Hastings finds Colonel Lestrade in Poirot's room, where the Colonel nostalgically recounts falling in love with his wife at a dance.

Hastings later discusses the incident with Poirot, admitting that both he and Boyd Carlington had suspected the shot was intentional. Poirot remains reserved but notes that things are not always as they seem and that genuine affection can exist behind animosity. As Hastings prepares for sleep, he experiences a sudden realization regarding Poirot's alert gaze. He contemplates a theory that a third party, "X," might have fired a second shot simultaneously with the Colonel's shot at a rabbit, masking the murder as an accident. He concludes that if the Colonel were to "confess" to an accidental shooting, the police might not perform the ballistic tests necessary to prove a second weapon was involved.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hastings realizes that the current incident follows a pattern seen in previous cases involving a figure known as "X." In these cases, individuals like the tenant farmer Riggs or Maggie Richmond believed they had committed murders that they actually had not. The next morning, Hastings shares this realization with Poirot, who confirms the connection but refuses to disclose the specific motive or details to protect Hastings from the culprit. Poirot warns that a murderer will likely strike again, noting that the recent shooting was only avoided by luck.

Hastings remains suspicious of the people at Styles and attempts to test his theory regarding Captain Alton. He questions Judith about her whereabouts the previous night, specifically asking if Alton was with her. Judith becomes angry and defensive, claiming she went to town alone to buy stamps and that she and Alton only met near the house briefly before running into Hastings. Hastings observes the interaction between Judith and her employer, John Franklin, noting Franklin's arrogant and superficial treatment of her, which contrasts with Alton's excessive attention.

Hastings fears that Alton may be "X," noting that Alton was not with Judith when the gun went off and that Alton is a rational man lacking moral boundaries. Further information from Boyd Caldwell reveals that Alton is married to a devout Roman Catholic who refuses to divorce him, a fact Caldwell suggests makes Alton appear more reliable than he truly is. Meanwhile, at the hospital, Mrs. Franklin expresses resentment toward the attention being given to Lieutenant Lestrade's wife.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a conversation with Hercule Poirot, Barbara Franklin expresses feelings of being a burden to her husband, Dr. John Franklin, suggesting she wishes to "end it all" so he might be free. Captain Hastings relays this to Nurse Crewe, who dismisses the remark as non-literal. Later, while sitting by a beech tree near the laboratory, Mrs. Franklin adopts a different persona, presenting herself as an adoring wife. She describes Dr. Franklin as a "saint" who is dangerously dedicated to scientific advancement, specifically citing a risky gas experiment he once performed on himself and expressing fear that he might attempt his current "bean experiment" on himself to obtain human data.

After Mrs. Franklin leaves to accompany Boyd Carleton to look at a house, Hastings observes that she seems to enjoy playing various dramatic roles. Nurse Clyde arrives to return Mrs. Franklin's forgotten handbag and gloves, prompting Hastings to remark on Mrs. Franklin's perceived irresponsibility and lack of consideration for the staff. Poirot responds cryptically by noting Nurse Clyde's red-brown hair.

The following day during lunch, Hastings, Judith, Boyd Cartwright, and Norton discuss the ethics of euthanasia. Hastings expresses concern that the practice could grant too much power to a patient's relatives, while Norton argues it should only be used for incurable illnesses at the patient's willing request. Boyd Cartwright challenges the concept of a patient's true willingness to die, recounting a story of a cancer patient who was given morphine by a doctor but ultimately chose not to take a lethal dose, using the anecdote to argue that people ultimately prefer to stay alive.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a discussion, Judith argues that those who are mentally capable have a responsibility to make decisions for those weakened by illness. She suggests that if a life is unhealthy or useless, it should be allowed to end painlessly, asserting that only those who contribute to the community deserve to survive. Boyd Carrington agrees with the principle that only those with value deserve to survive, while Norton challenges Judith, claiming she lacks the courage to act on such theories. After the gongs sound, Judith leaves the group in anger, followed by Boyd Cartwright.

Norton approaches Hastings to express concern regarding Judith's frequent contact with Alton, a man with a bad reputation. Norton shares a story about a similar independent girl who committed suicide via a barbiturate overdose after being manipulated by Alton. Hastings, feeling a sense of foreboding, becomes convinced that Alton is the murderer and that Judith is falling in love with him.

Later, Boyd Carleton also advises Hastings to warn Judith about Alton. Struggling with his own fears and indecision, Hastings eventually finds Judith sitting alone in the rose garden looking miserable. He approaches her and warns her that Alton is not worth her affection, noting that he is a married man and that their relationship will only lead to sadness, shame, and regret. Judith responds with a heartbreaking, sad smile.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a heated argument where Judith declares that a specific man means everything to her, she expresses intense anger toward the narrator for interfering in her life and retreats. The narrator, left feeling disappointed and helpless, is later joined by Elizabeth Coleridge and Norton. To soothe the narrator’s distress, they take a walk to observe nature. During the walk, Norton uses a telescope to look at a distant grove of trees but suddenly stops, appearing pale, anxious, and bewildered. When the narrator imperiously demands to see through the telescope, Norton attempts to claim he merely misidentified a bird. The narrator snatches the telescope and sees only a flash of white, possibly a girl's dress, disappearing into the trees.

Upon returning to the villa, the narrator encounters Mrs. Franklin and Boyd Carrington. Mrs. Franklin, appearing radiant after a shopping trip to Tedcastle, discusses the local drought and expresses concern regarding John’s recent headaches and his worries about an experiment. She also questions Norton’s frightened appearance. Shortly after, Curtis wheels Polo through the doorway; Polo appears alert and asks what is happening, but Mrs. Franklin dismisses the tension by attributing it to an approaching storm.

The narrator follows Mrs. Franklin to her room in the east wing, where Nurse Craven is examining Boyd Carrington’s hand. After Mrs. Franklin requests service and Boyd departs, the narrator heads toward the room shared with Poirot. Outside Alton's room, the narrator encounters Judith. The narrator drags her into a room to confront her about visiting Alton, calling him "scum." Judith responds with indifference and sarcasm, stating she does not view him as a saint, while the narrator continues to protest her contact with him.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a confrontation in which Judith asserts her independence and refuses to obey him, the narrator feels powerless and alone, noting that her mother is deceased. Following dinner, the narrator observes Judith and Alton kissing behind the house. Norton attempts to intervene, advising the narrator to accept the situation and give up, but the narrator pretends to comply to hide his true intentions.

The narrator overhears Alton in a pavilion planning to take Judith to London the following day, claiming he will be visiting a friend in Ipswich to hide their meeting. To prevent this, the narrator decides to sabotage Alton. He goes to Allerton's room and enters the bathroom to access a bottle of sleeping pills. He carefully replaces eight of the sleeping pills with aspirin to ensure the bottle looks unchanged, then returns to his room. He tests a mixture of whiskey and the pills, noting they dissolve easily and taste only slightly bitter. His plan is to offer Alton a drink and surreptitiously spike his glass with the lethal dose of sleeping pills.

While waiting for Alton to return, the narrator is interrupted by Curtis, who brings him to see Bello. The narrator feigns a headache caused by the weather to explain his absence and avoid suspicion. After drinking some chocolate provided by Polo, the narrator returns to his room. He leaves his door slightly ajar to listen for Alton's arrival and whispers to his late wife, stating that he is doing this to save Judith.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After falling asleep in a chair while waiting for Alton, Hastings wakes up feeling dazed and nauseous. He realizes he had previously intended to kill Alton by replacing half of his sleeping pills with aspirin. Feeling remorseful, Hastings cleans himself up and confesses his plan to Poirot. 

Poirot rebukes Hastings, explaining that the plan was foolish and would likely lead to Hastings's arrest. Poirot notes that because of Hastings's known dislike for Alton—which Boyd Carlington and Norton could testify to—and the fact that Alton's daughter, Judith, is the target of Alton's unwelcome advances, Hastings would be the primary suspect. Poirot also mentions that a key to the house had previously gone missing, suggesting the house is not as secure as Hastings believes. Hastings suggests the house's dark history and past murders might be affecting the inhabitants' mental states, a sentiment Poirot acknowledges. Regarding the situation with Judith and Alton, Poirot advises Hastings to do nothing and to trust Judith, noting that she is strong and capable.

The following morning, Hastings is relieved to see that Judith has abandoned her plan to go to London, instead going to the laboratory with Franklin. This leads Hastings to believe she rejected Alton's invitation. Meanwhile, Alton leaves for Ipswich. Boyd Carlington reports receiving bad news via letter and dealing with construction difficulties. Additionally, Mrs. Franklin is reported to be suffering from exhaustion and various physical ailments, which the others attribute to her delusional disorder.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Nurse Crevlin and Dr. Franklin dismiss Mrs. Franklin’s complaints of illness as attention-seeking behavior, noting her normal temperature and pulse. While most guests do not take her minor illness seriously, Boyd Carlington feels intense guilt, believing his previous day's activities neglected her fragile health. After unsuccessfully attempting to give her chocolates, Boyd joins the narrator and Norton in the smoking room to eat a chocolate box in a solemn atmosphere.

As the weather shifts from heavy rain to a pleasant afternoon, the atmosphere at the manor changes. Elizabeth Coleridge plays Bach and Mozart for Polly in the sitting room. Judith returns from the garden appearing pale and vacant, while Dr. Franklin appears stressed and distracted. However, Franklin suddenly experiences a moment of clarity, telling Norton that he has realized he can take a "shortcut" to simplify his processes.

Later, the Lettré couple appears unusually harmonious, with Mrs. Lettré joking gently with Colonel Letraille. This reconciliation prompts a group discussion regarding marriage and divorce. The narrator and Norton support the idea of divorce for personal happiness, whereas Boyle Carrington and Dr. Franklin oppose it; Franklin argues that marriage is a contract and a responsibility that must be honored. Amidst the conversation, the narrator observes a potential connection between Elizabeth Coleridge and Norton. Suddenly, the narrator is struck by a profound sense of unease and fear, a feeling noticed only by Boyd Carlington, who asks if the narrator is alright.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The narrator expresses a deep sense of foreboding and unease to Franklin. Judith emerges from the house with a serious expression, prompting Norton to compare her to the Jewish heroine who decapitated Holofernes. Judith moves to sit with Franklin, noting that Mrs. Franklin has invited the group to her room for coffee.

The group gathers in Mrs. Franklin's room, where she prepares coffee with the assistance of Nurse Creven. The attendees include Franklin, Boyd Carlington, Elizabeth Cole, Norton, and the narrator. While the group discusses a crossword puzzle, debating clues regarding Tennyson and Shakespeare, Judith walks out onto the terrace. She calls out that she sees shooting stars, prompting Boyd Carlington to lead most of the group, including Mrs. Franklin, out to the balcony to make wishes.

Left alone with the newspaper, the narrator becomes overwhelmed by a memory of a tropical night and Cindy, leading to silent tears. To hide his distress from Judith when she re-enters, he pretends to study a book of Shakespeare. After the group disperses, Franklin expresses satisfaction with his evening and goes for a walk. The narrator later encounters Franklin and Judith in the hallway; Franklin asks Judith to walk with him, but she declines. After the narrator parts ways with Franklin, he goes to Bello's room and finds Judith. She kisses him and asks for his forgiveness regarding an incident involving "Uncle Heracles."

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a conversation with Judith, the narrator notes that Mrs. Franklin fell seriously ill and died the next morning. Although initially thought to be aconitine poisoning, a coroner's inquest two days later determined she died from curare poisoning and other alkaloids from poisonous beans, likely ingested between 7 pm and dawn.

During the inquest, Dr. Franklin testified that his laboratory door was usually locked, with keys held by himself and his assistant, Miss Hasting; his wife occasionally borrowed a key. He discovered his concentrated curare solution had been replaced with water. He noted that while his wife had been depressed, she had recently been in good spirits and he did not believe she would commit suicide.

Nurse Crawford, who had cared for Mrs. Franklin for two months, testified that the deceased had expressed suicidal ideation previously, feeling like a burden because Dr. Franklin had turned down an overseas job to care for her. On the night of her death, Crawford observed that Mrs. Franklin was unusually excited and agitated. She noted that Mrs. Franklin had many opportunities to ingest poison via her food or drinks, such as Burgundy wine or milk, and could have disposed of a bottle in the trash or medicine cabinet.

Sir William Boyd Carlington testified that Mrs. Franklin was a selfless woman who felt troubled by hindering her husband's career. Judith testified that she had no knowledge of the poison being taken and that Mrs. Franklin seemed her usual, slightly excited self. Finally, Hercule Poirot testified that Mrs. Franklin had expressed a desire to "end it all" during a conversation the day before her death. He also stated that on June 10th, he saw Mrs. Franklin exiting the laboratory holding a small bottle in her right hand.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following an inquest into the death of Mrs. Franklin, the coroner concludes that she died of curare poisoning. Based on witness testimony regarding her severe depression and Hercule Poirot’s claim that he saw her carrying a small bottle from the laboratory, the jury delivers a verdict of suicide due to mental abnormality.

Hastings later confronts Poirot in his room, questioning the truth of his testimony. Poirot admits that while he did not lie, he intentionally shaped his statements to ensure a suicide ruling. He reveals to Hastings that he believes Mrs. Franklin was actually the victim of a premeditated murder. Poirot intends to continue investigating the case secretly to catch the killer, whom he refers to as "X."

Following the funeral at Styles, Hastings is approached by an old lady who suggests that a similar murder occurred at Styles Manor twenty years prior, involving a wife whom everyone believed was treated well by her husband. This encounter leaves Hastings unsettled by the suspicion cast upon him.

Later, Hastings learns from Curtis that Poirot has suffered a severe heart attack. Unlike his usual behavior of seeking immediate medical attention for minor ailments, Poirot refuses to see more doctors, claiming he has already consulted specialists including Dr. Branco, Dr. Dash, and a cardiologist referred to as "R." Poirot expresses a fatalistic view regarding his declining health, noting that a body cannot be repaired like a machine.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a heart attack, Hercule Poirot informs Hastings that he will not see a doctor, despite Hastings' concerns and a visit from Curtis. Poirot declares that this will be his last case, noting that he is facing a cunning murderer referred to as "X" whose methods he cannot decipher. While Poirot's physical health is failing due to paralysis and heart issues, he maintains that his mind remains sharp. Hastings, however, privately doubts Poirot's mental acuity following the deaths of Mrs. Franklin and the near-death of Mrs. Littler.

The next day, Poirot agrees to see Dr. John Franklin, a researcher rather than a general practitioner. After examining Poirot, Franklin informs a worried Hastings that Poirot’s physical condition is "terrible" and that his time is running out. Franklin notes that Poirot is aware of his condition and is driven by a sense of responsibility to finish his work. The only available treatment is the use of nitroglycerin vials for potential attacks.

Franklin expresses a detached philosophy regarding death, stating that medicine's true purpose is improving human life rather than merely delaying the inevitable. He reveals that he has been hired for a position in Africa and will depart in ten days. Franklin also discusses his personal life, admitting to a lack of grief over the death of his wife, Barbara, describing himself as a selfish and stubborn man who moved on from their marriage quickly.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Franklin explains to Hastings that he previously declined a job opportunity in Africa solely for financial reasons to maintain his wife's standard of living. When Hastings questions his lack of grief regarding his wife's suicide, Franklin reveals he does not believe she committed suicide, though he expresses a firm refusal to investigate or care about what actually happened.

Following the funeral, Stephen Norton appears preoccupied and distant, exhibiting inner turmoil. He approaches Hastings to discuss a moral dilemma using the metaphor of accidentally reading a private letter or looking through a keyhole. Hastings eventually realizes that Norton is referring to something he witnessed through a telescope while on a grassy hill with Hastings and Elizabeth Coleridge. Norton admits he saw something through the telescope while looking for a woodpecker that he considers "spying." While Norton claims the discovery is not directly related to Mrs. Franklin's death, he suggests it might help explain certain things.

Hastings suggests that Norton consult Poirot for advice. Although Norton is initially skeptical due to Poirot being a foreigner, he eventually agrees to seek Poirot's counsel. When Hastings informs Poirot of the situation, Poirot becomes grim and warns that the information Norton witnessed is "very dangerous." Poirot instructs Hastings to arrange a casual, inconspicuous meeting with Norton for that evening and asks who else was present during the incident, to which Hastings responds that Elizabeth Coleridge was there.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Captain Hastings conveys a message from Poirot to Norton, noting Norton's hesitation when Hastings asks if he has told anyone else about his situation. Later, Hastings encounters Elizabeth Cole on a grassy hill. During their conversation, Hastings expresses his sadness regarding bad news about an old friend, implying the potential end of Poirot's life. Elizabeth discusses her own loneliness and her relationship with Stephen Nott, but she becomes distressed when discussing her sister, Maggie. Elizabeth believes her sister is a criminal or a murderer, but Hastings asserts that Maggie did not kill her father and promises to prove it.

Hastings then meets Boyd Carleton near the mansion. Carleton, who is moving to Nayton, expresses his dislike for the house's ominous atmosphere and suggests that Barbara Franklin's husband, Franklin, might be responsible for her death. Carleton notes that Franklin is indifferent and obsessed with his career. Carleton reveals that Cleven Nurse, who has unexpectedly returned to stay for the night, provided him with information and has long disliked Franklin. Hastings remains skeptical, reminding Carleton that Nurse's evidence helped secure a suicide verdict and that Poirot saw Mrs. Franklin holding a laboratory bottle. As Alton approaches, Hastings reflects on the difficulty of investigating a suspect who lacks a clear motive. Finally, Hastings notes that while he has total faith in Poirot's eventual victory, Poirot himself has expressed uncertainty by mentioning the possibility of something happening to him.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot informs Hastings that his life is nearing its end and that a clever individual known as X might attempt to hasten his death for personal benefit. Poirot reveals that he has made arrangements for Hastings to take over his role, leaving physical clues in a locked briefcase that will lead Hastings to the truth, even if that truth is difficult to face.

Following a pleasant dinner at Stile's Manor involving Mrs. Letreul, Dr. Franklin, and Nurse Crewe, the group plays cards. Hastings visits Poirot with Norton, who intends to lend him a book. Later, Hastings observes Boyd Carlington appearing preoccupied and uncomfortable by the carefree atmosphere, eventually leaving after hearing distant thunder. During the night, Hastings is awakened by a noise and sees Norton returning to his room in striped pajamas, notably locking his door. This action causes Hastings to feel unease, as he recalls the mysterious disappearance of Poirot's room key.

The next morning, Hastings finds Poirot looking exhausted and ill. Poirot reveals that Norton reported seeing two people, but he refuses to disclose their identities to Hastings immediately, stating he wants to remember the details first. Poirot claims the case is essentially over but remains melancholy. After breakfast, Hastings walks in the garden and encounters an energetic Boyd Carlington. They notice that while other windows at the manor are open following the storm, Norton's window remains shut. Concerned that Norton might be ill, they decide to check on him.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a maid reports that Norton did not answer her knocks, Hastings and others find his bedroom door at Styles Manor locked. Lieutenant Lethbridge and Lestrade's wife oversee the forcing of the door, revealing Norton dead in his bed. He has a small hole in the center of his forehead and is holding a pistol; the door was locked from the inside, the windows were closed, and the key was in his pocket. While the jury eventually rules the death a suicide, the autopsy doctor notes that suicide victims rarely aim at the center of the forehead.

Hastings informs Hercule Poirot of the death. Although Hastings identified the man by his distinctive pajamas and limp, Poirot argues that these traits could be faked and concludes that Norton was murdered. Hastings suspects Norton was killed to prevent him from revealing something he had seen.

Following these events, Poirot dies of a heart attack. While officially ruled a death by natural causes, Hastings suspects murder, questioning if someone deliberately removed Poirot's life-saving nitroglycerin. Hastings, acting as the executor of Poirot's will, opens a briefcase left for him. He discovers that the files regarding "X" have vanished, likely due to X's interference. However, he finds two books: a small edition of Shakespeare's *Othello* and John Irving's *John Ferguson*, the latter marked at the third act. A piece of paper falls from the book containing a note from Poirot: "Go to my servant George." Hastings intends to find George to help decode the clues before burying Poirot.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a funeral, the narrator sits with his daughter, Judith, to make plans for the future. Judith reveals her intention to leave England for Africa with Franklin, stating she is going as his wife rather than his assistant. This revelation causes the narrator great distress and confusion. He begins to question the true nature of Judith and Franklin, wondering if Judith possessed murderous intent, drawing a parallel to the character in *Othello*. He contemplates whether the "X story" was a smokescreen created by Poirot to protect Judith or to hide a tragedy involving the Franklin couple.

The narrator travels to Eastbourne to visit George, Poirot’s former servant. During their conversation, George reveals that Poirot himself sent him away temporarily to care for his father, though George does not know the reason. George also mentions that Poirot has been wearing a wig due to hair loss. When the narrator presses George for reasons behind Poirot's actions, George suggests Poirot may have wanted to hire Curtis, a man who previously worked as an assistant at a mental institution. This leads the narrator to a new realization: Curtis, rather than a guest at Styles, may have been the mysterious "X" all along.

In a postscript, Colonel Hastings notes that he received a sealed package from Poirot’s solicitors four months after Poirot's death. The package contains a manuscript from Poirot, who explains that he is writing to reveal the truth about the second "Styles Incident." Poirot asserts that he has left enough clues for Hastings to discover who killed Norton and provides a warning that the truth regarding the death of Barbara Franklin may be shocking.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At Stiles Manor, a narrator addresses Blackstone, explaining that he summoned him to act as his "eyes and ears" to witness a specific sequence of events. The narrator reveals that he previously withheld the identity of "X" to facilitate a specific realization. He explains that X is not a direct murderer but a "catalyst"—a third substance that triggers a reaction between others without participating in the crime itself. Using literary parallels, the narrator compares X to Iago from *Othello*, a character who manipulates others into committing violence while remaining above suspicion, and to Cruit John from *John Fergusson*, who uses psychological suggestion to lure others into killing.

The narrator confesses that he came to Stiles because he intended to commit a murder himself to achieve a greater goal of saving the innocent, though he compares his hesitation to Hamlet. He then reveals that X is Stephen Norton. Blackstone had correctly suspected Norton due to his inconspicuous nature. The narrator describes Norton’s background as a man with a domineering mother and a history of being mocked for his aversion to blood and violence. This trauma led Norton to discover a sense of power through psychological manipulation.

Norton is identified as a sadist and an addict of "secondary violence" who uses his compassionate, listening nature to manipulate the hidden desires of others, effectively destroying their will to resist murderous impulses. The narrator notes that Norton’s crimes extend beyond five known cases; he has manipulated Ezelington, Rigs, Frieda Kre, and Margaret Ritchfield by using psychological stimuli to influence their actions and perceptions.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
An unnamed narrator explains to Hastings how Norton has been systematically manipulating people at Styles Manor to stir up conflict. Norton first targeted the relationship between Franklin and Judith. While Franklin is a principled man whose devotion to his work makes him resistant to external pressure, Norton successfully exploited Judith’s vulnerabilities. By mocking her lack of courage regarding her desire for a "useless life," Norton goaded her. Judith had previously flirted with Alton as a desperate attempt at comfort, but she held no romantic feelings for him. Judith also misled Hastings into believing Alton was her lover to avoid his pity.

Norton also targeted the Lutterell couple. He manipulated Lutterell’s feelings of inadequacy and resentment toward his wife. During a bridge game, Norton orchestrated a situation where Lutterell’s wife intervened during a moment of tension, and Norton then exacerbated the humiliation by bringing up a shooting incident and prompting the senile Boyd Carleton to tell a specific story about an Irish soldier. However, this plan failed to destroy the couple's bond.
