She has been invited to tea by Mrs. Marina Greg, who sent a handwritten note mentioning a previous meeting in California. As Lady Bunty approaches the house, she observes the improvements made to the property and feels nostalgic for the iris beds she once loved.

Upon entering the library, Lady Bunty finds Mrs. Greg and her husband, Jason Rad (also called Goldie), seated at a tea table. Mrs. Greg expresses her deep desire to settle in the English countryside permanently, viewing the house as her true home. While Jason Rad offers affectionate support, he privately considers the Victorian house ugly and the location inconvenient, doubting whether Marina will remain enthusiastic about the move for long. Mrs. Greg greets Lady Bunty warmly, acknowledging the coincidence of their meeting and apologizing for the changes made to the house, which previously belonged to the late Colonel Bunty.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The Countess de Bennington (also referred to as Mrs. Bantling) welcomes Marina Gregg and her husband, Jason Rad, to the house. The Countess explains that she sold the property after her husband passed away and has since enjoyed traveling to visit her four children and nine grandchildren located in Kenya, South Africa, Texas, and London. Jason Rad, described as having an irregular, clown-like appearance but a friendly voice, offers the guests tea and pastries prepared by an Italian chef.

During the visit, Mrs. Trent observes Marina Gregg, noting her striking, Greta Garbo-like beauty and her ability to project a magical aura. Marina expresses a desire to settle into the house permanently, though the Countess privately doubts Marina's ability to settle down. The conversation is interrupted by Ella Jelinsky, Jason Rad's confident, businesslike secretary, who arrives to inform him that Mr. Bartlett has an urgent matter.

After Jason Rad leaves with Marina to attend to a request, Ella remains to converse with the Countess. Ella discusses the local real estate and the practicalities of rural living, such as the difficulty of finding plumbers and the lack of supermarkets. The conversation shifts when Ella asks about the house's "atmosphere" and specifically inquires when a murder took place. Despite the Countess's initial denial that a murder has occurred there, Ella insists she has heard rumors of a murder occurring by the fireplace.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Bantry and Miss Jelinsky discuss a past murder involving a girl who was brought into a room after her death. Mrs. Bantry explains that the body was discovered in the library by a maid during early morning tea service. Miss Jelinsky requests that the matter not be mentioned to Marina Greg, noting that the movie star lives in a "bubble" and has been unwell for the past two years. Miss Jelinsky describes Marina as having extreme mood swings and expresses doubt regarding her long-term happiness at the house, though she notes that keeping Marina happy is important to a "genius" associated with her.

At Gresham Hall, a St. John Ambulance fundraising event draws an unprecedented crowd of local residents curious about the "movie people" and their renovations. Guests observe the new swimming pool and the redesigned interior, which features dark oak and Spanish leather. Mr. Sampson, a 96-year-old resident, predicts scandalous behavior will occur at the estate. Lady Bantling arrives late and observes the successful turnout and the well-maintained gardens. 

Hilliary Preston, an employee of Mr. Radley, approaches Mrs. Bantry and invites her to the third floor to meet Mr. and Mrs. Radley and their "special friends." Mrs. Bantry follows Preston, passing Mr. and Mrs. Alcock MP, up a staircase cordoned off with red rope. At the top of the stairs, Marina Greg and Jason Radley greet a group of elite guests in a lounge area where the butler, Giuseppe, is preparing drinks. A man in uniform begins introducing the guests.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At a gathering hosted by Marina Greg, various guests arrive at a renovated Victorian-era house in Bermuda. Mr. and Mrs. Ackroyd join Jason and Blackie Preston for drinks, while a bewildered clergyman arrives and is attended to by Miss Jelinski. The Bucher couple also arrives; the clergyman praises Mrs. Bucher's past diligence as a secretary for an organization. During the arrivals, Heather Badecock delivers a tedious speech, and Mrs. Alcock expresses discomfort and suspicion regarding vodka.

Amidst the social interactions, Marina Greg experiences a moment of intense, frightening shock while staring at a wall near the stairs, though she quickly regains her composure and offers Jason a Kir Royale. Seeking to see the house's renovations, Mrs. Bantry, Mrs. Alcock, and Mrs. Blunt take a tour of the bathrooms. They attempt to peek into the bedrooms, but find all the doors have been locked by the owner. While walking back, Mrs. Bantry observes Mrs. Mivet and Shelley chatting cheerfully outside.

The atmosphere shifts when a commotion is heard near the stairs. Miss Jelinski hurries past the group, noting that the bedroom doors are locked and that someone is seriously ill. Jason Radcliffe is seen on the phone, and Miss Jelinski reports that Heather Badecock's condition is very bad, suggesting she may be suffering from a heart or brain attack, or an epileptic fit.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Bantling informs a man that she does not know a woman living in the local residential development, though she notes the woman's husband is a fair, inconspicuous middle-aged man. Nearby, Eira Jelinski enters a bathroom to search for ammonia solution, a stimulant. Mrs. Bantling encounters Jason Rad, who informs her that the woman is dead.

The following morning, Miss Knight serves breakfast to Miss Marple. Miss Knight mentions that Mrs. Bantry called at eight o'clock to speak with Miss Marple, but Miss Knight delayed the message until after breakfast. While Miss Marple eats, Miss Knight complains about a young woman named Shirley singing loudly while vacuuming.

Thelma (also referred to as Sheila/Shelley) visits Miss Marple to discuss an incident at Gosington Hall. She reports that a woman named Mrs. Badcock died during a fundraising event for St. John's Ambulance. According to Thelma, Mrs. Badcock drank something, felt unwell, and died five minutes later. Although Mrs. Badcock was in perfect health, a doctor ordered an autopsy because the cause of death was undetermined. Thelma describes Mrs. Badcock as a kind but meddlesome person who often shared her herb-filled savory cakes with others. Thelma also notes that Mrs. Badcock's husband appeared devastated, though she suggests he may have simply been a man who lacked the courage to stand up to his wife.

Miss Knight informs Miss Marple that Mrs. Bantry has gone out, prompting Miss Marple to prepare for her arrival. Mrs. Bantry soon arrives at Miss Marple's home, out of breath and stating she has much to tell her. Miss Knight leaves the two women to talk.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Bantry and Miss Marple discuss the sudden death of Heather Badcock, which occurred the previous day. Mrs. Bantry reveals that Heather arrived full of energy but collapsed and died fifteen minutes later. Mrs. Bantry learned of the event through a chain of gossip starting with Old Briggs, who overheard Dr. Sutherford calling the police station while tending his garden.

Miss Marple rejects the possibility of suicide, noting that Heather was a kind person. She compares Heather to a woman named Alison Wilder, describing a specific type of self-centeredness where an individual is unselfish but lacks a sense of self-preservation or awareness of how their actions affect others. Miss Marple suggests that Heather may have been murdered because she was unaware of a danger or perhaps because of mistaken identity, though she clarifies that Heather was not the type to extort anyone.

Dr. Haydock arrives and visits Miss Marple, mentioning that rumors in St. Mary Mead already suggest the death is a murder case, even before the autopsy. He notes that the autopsy is scheduled for the following day. After the doctor leaves, Mrs. Bantry reveals she was present at the house when the death occurred. She explains that a slender young man, possibly Marina Gregg's secretary, brought her into the house to a reception area set up on a landing where a dressing room and bedroom had been removed to create an alcove.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Bantry recounts a gathering to Miss Marple, describing the attendees, which included Marina Gregg, her husband, public relations secretary Eileen Jelinski, a vicar, Mrs. Sandlerford, Colonel and Mrs. Corrington, the county magistrate, and others from a movie studio. During the event, Heather Badcock, the secretary of the St. John Ambulance, spoke bluntly to Marina Gregg about meeting her years prior. Mrs. Bantry notes that during this time, Marina Gregg appeared to freeze with a shocked expression, staring fixedly at an Italian painting—a reproduction of Bellini's Madonna—on the wall. While Marina was distracted, several people ascended the stairs, including the town mayor and his wife, a young man with a mustache, and a girl with a camera. Jason Radcliffe eventually nudged Marina back to her usual charming self and served champagne to Marina and Mrs. Baskerville. Shortly after, a guest felt unwell.

An autopsy revealed that Heather Badcock died from four grains of a benzodiazepine-related drug, though the method of administration remained unknown. Following a two-week investigation, Inspector Frank Cornish visited Arthur Badcock. While driving Arthur home to Arlington Lane, Cornish observed Arthur's neighbor, Mrs. Mary Bain, who had moved there six months prior following her husband's death. Cornish noted Mrs. Bain's alert, cautious gaze and suspected she had a prior connection to the police. After she served them tea and departed, Cornish continued his questioning of the agitated Arthur Badcock regarding the autopsy results.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Chief Inspector Cornish and Detective Constable Cornish interview Arthur Badcock regarding the death of his wife, Heather. The detectives reveal that Heather died from an overdose of a drug sold under the brand name Camon, which is used to treat anxiety and depression. Badcock insists Heather was a happy person who never required medication and had not seen her doctor, Dr. Sim, since moving to the area.

Badcock recounts that during a charity gala at the Goschington Estate, Heather and he had tea and cake in a courtyard tent before being invited to a private reception on the upper floor of the house. There, they met the hostess, film star Marina Gregg, and her husband, Jason Radcliffe. Radcliffe served Heather a Daiquiri cocktail. While Heather was chatting with friends from the St. John Ambulance, someone touched her elbow, causing her drink to spill on her and Miss Gregg. Miss Gregg then handed Heather her own glass, claiming she had already had enough. Heather drank from the glass, walked a short distance, and then collapsed and died after complaining of feeling "off."

In a separate discussion, Chief Inspector Cladrock and Mr. DeMortimer Clegg discuss the case. It is noted that the victim, Heather Badcock, was the local secretary for the St. John Ambulance and had organized the gala. The event was hosted by Marina Gregg and Jason Radcliffe at Goschington Manor to support their new film company. The police believe the case is significant due to the high-profile nature of the attendees and the bizarre circumstances of the poisoning.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Demott Cluttercuck and his superior discuss the suspicious circumstances surrounding the poisoning of Heather Bardwell. The crime occurred during an afternoon event where a prescription drug was administered via a cocktail, a risky method given the presence of twenty to thirty people. While the local detective, Conish, noted that Heather's husband, Mr. Bardcock, appeared tense, he concluded the couple seemed loyal. Demott decides to take Tidler, a movie enthusiast, to investigate the case.

Detective Moore (also referred to as Dermot Clark) visits Miss Marple to inform her that Scotland Yard has taken over the murder case. Miss Marple reflects on Heather's character, noting that while she was a good housekeeper, she was not particularly considerate, which may have left her husband lonely. Miss Marple suggests that the detectives visit Lady Bunty, who lives in a cottage at Gosington Manor and attended the party. She advises them to mention "Charlotte's Daughters" to Lady Bunty to prompt relevant information.

Later, Demott Clark meets Mrs. Bantry. She observes that while there is no official evidence regarding how the poison was administered, the community treats it as a murder. However, she finds it difficult to imagine a motive, stating that although Heather was an unpleasant, overly emotional, and over-the-top woman, she does not believe anyone would want to kill her.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Inspector Clouseau, acting on a request from Miss Marple, meets with Dermot Clarke to investigate the matter of "Charlotte." During the discussion, Mrs. Bantry and DeMott evaluate the motives regarding the death of Mrs. Badcock, concluding that she had little wealth to inherit and no known enemies or blackmailing tendencies. Dr. Mortimer suggests she may have known something harmful to others, but Mrs. Bantry remains skeptical.

Mrs. Bantry describes a specific expression she witnessed at a reception held on a landing platform. She calls this the "Charlotte look," referencing Tennyson's poetry, and describes it as an expression of profound, chilling horror. She observed this look on Marina Gregg. While Heather Badcock was talking to Marina, Marina appeared to be staring past her, over her shoulder, toward the hallway or a painting, as if she had seen something terrible. Mrs. Bantry recalls several people present at the event, including the mayor and his wife, a red-haired man she thought might be a reporter named Calbraith, an imposing dark-skinned man with a glamorous actress, General Barnstaple, the Grays from the farm, and a pretentious girl from London with a large camera.

In a separate inquiry, Cunish reports to Mr. Clutter that Mrs. Badcock had no enemies, no suspicious affairs, and a good marriage. Regarding her husband's potential motives, Cunish notes he works at Bidell Russell, a real estate agency, alongside colleagues Flore West and Miss Glendale. Connington adds that a widow living next door, described as an attractive woman with "gypsy beauty," has been taking care of the husband and appears determined to marry him.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Commander Klatdock arrives at Goschington Manor, where he is introduced to Blackley Preston, an assistant to Jason Rad. Preston expresses the company's full cooperation with the investigation and suggests that the deceased, Mrs. Badcock, may have suffered an allergic reaction. Dr. Mortimer Cragdon clarifies that Mrs. Badcock was healthy and died from consuming six times the usual dose of camphor, a drug Preston admits to using himself to calm his emotions. Preston notes that camphor is available in most medicine cabinets throughout the house.

As they move to the staircase, Preston explains that guests were led up in small batches by himself and Ella Jelinsky. Preston recalls that Mrs. Badcock arrived with her husband, who wore a large official necklace, and a Mrs. Bantley. He does not know who served them drinks, noting that several people were responsible for service. Preston also mentions the presence of reporter Jim Galbreath, various photographers, and the unexpected arrival of Adwick Fenn, a prominent figure in the entertainment industry who has known Marina Gregg for years.

Klatdock inquires about the drinks and the staff responsible for serving them, noting that any of the thirty people present could have potentially poisoned Mrs. Badcock. When Klatdock requests to speak with Marina Gregg, Preston refuses, claiming she is in a state of deep depression and is under the care of a private physician. Klatdock reviews a medical certificate provided by Preston regarding her condition.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Blackley Preston informs Chief Inspector DeMott Caddock that Marina Gregg has been under the care of Dr. Morris Gilchrist for several years due to recurring illness, though she regained her health about a year ago. Caddock requests to meet Dr. Gilchrist, who is brought to him by Preston. The two men move to the doctor's private bedroom to speak privately.

Dr. Gilchrist explains that Marina Gregg cannot receive visitors or participate in the investigation because she is suffering from nervous tension; he warns that questioning her would cause her to become hysterical. He notes that while she is currently depressed, she likely possesses a strong desire to speak with Caddock within the next forty-eight hours. Gilchrist provides a psychological profile of Gregg, describing her as an excellent but emotionally unstable actress whose moods oscillate between ecstasy and despair. He mentions that her life has been marked by pain and unhappy marriages, though she currently finds refuge in her marriage.

When Caddock inquires about her reaction to the recent tragedy and the death of Heather Bardcock, Gilchrist remains non-committal, suggesting Gregg might react with sincere sympathy, dramatize the event for herself, or react for other unknown reasons. Regarding medication, Gilchrist reveals that Gregg relies on a drug called Camon, which is also commonly used by others in the industry, such as Eira Jelinsky and Harley Preston, to provide calm and energy. Gilchrist concludes by stating he is weighing his conflicting responsibilities: his professional duty to maintain patient confidentiality and his duty to take action if he suspects a patient is in danger.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Gilchrist meets with Detective Chief Inspector Cloak to provide confidential information regarding Marina Gregg. Gilchrist reveals that following a recent incident, Marina suffered a mental collapse. While he was providing her with sedatives, she told him, "The drink was meant for me, doctor." When Gilchrist later attempted to revisit the topic, Marina denied saying it, claiming he had misunderstood her due to her state of consciousness. Gilchrist notes that while he cannot confirm the truth of her statement, Marina genuinely believed the drug was intended for her. Gilchrist also confirms that Marina has not shared this belief with her husband.

Following this, Mr. Blackley Preston leads Mr. Demott and Detective Chief Inspector Cloak to the second-floor room of Jason Rad. Demott observes that Rad is a difficult, unreadable man with a profound intellect and a clown-like face. During the interrogation, Rad explains that he and his wife, Marina, did not know Heather Bardock prior to the incident. Rad claims Heather had only met Marina twelve or thirteen years ago at a garden party in Bermuda, where Heather had requested an autograph. Rad dismisses the idea that Marina was distracted during their recent encounter, suggesting any perceived lapse in attention was merely fatigue. 

Cloak then confronts Rad with the possibility that the poisoning of Heather Bardock was an accident and that Marina was the intended target. Rad agrees with this assessment, though Cloak notes that Rad had not mentioned this theory to Inspector Cornish or during the inquest.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation, Jason Radcliffe explains to Chief Inspector Craddock and Detective Dumot why he remained silent regarding his suspicion that his wife, Marina, was being targeted for poisoning. Radcliffe admits he holds a personal theory that someone intended to administer a fatal dose of poison to Marina in a public setting, which would make prosecution difficult due to the presence of many strangers. He chose silence to protect Marina’s fragile mental state; he describes her as an actress who seeks constant security and happiness but has faced repeated tragedies, including the birth of a son with intellectual disabilities eleven years prior, which led to her hospitalization in a sanatorium. Radcliffe notes that Marina had recently regained a sense of calm and optimism, and he feared that informing her of the threat would cause her to collapse again.

When questioned about potential suspects, Radcliffe refuses to name anyone, stating that while the evidence of an attempted murder is factual, it is unbelievable to him that someone would hate Marina so much. He recounts the events at his home: he poured two glasses of gin from a pitcher, one for Marina and one for Mrs. Bardok. During a period when guests, including the mayor and representatives from a movie company, were milling around, the drinks were left on a table. Radcliffe believes the poison was added to Marina's glass during this time. The attempt failed when Mrs. Bardok accidentally dropped her glass, and Marina insisted that Mrs. Bardok drink from her own glass instead. Mrs. Bardok died four to five minutes after drinking the liquid.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Demott Craddock interviews Jason Rad regarding the potential poisoning of his wife, Marina Greg. Jason explains that he initially assumed Marina had suffered a sudden medical emergency, such as a heart attack, rather than being poisoned. When Demott questions if Marina had enemies, Jason notes that while envy and jealousy are common in their social circle, he does not believe anyone has the motive or potential to be a murderer. He mentions that while Marina had occasional affairs, they did not cause long-term animosity. Regarding economic motives, Jason states that while he and a replacement actress might benefit from her death, the impact is not definitive. Demott requests a list of everyone present in the alcove during the incident, and Jason directs him to his secretary, Ella Jelinsky.

Demott then meets with Ella Jelinsky, who provides a typed list of guests. Ella explains her role is to manage Marina’s social life and oversee the house. She suggests the incident might have been an accident rather than murder, theorizing that a guest may have accidentally poured a massive, lethal dose of a prescription drug into a glass, which Marina then mistakenly drank. Ella notes that Marina is often confused and frequently takes "Carmen," a substance used by others in the house. While Ella describes Marina as a great artist with extraordinary charm, she characterizes her personally as a "nightmare" to work for.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective DeMott Craddock interviews Eleanor Jelinsky regarding Marina Gregg’s mental state. Jelinsky explains that Marina is prone to extreme emotional shifts and is highly sensitive to topics involving mental institutions or children. Marina’s only child, whom she had with her ex-husband Isidore Wright, is described as "a bit crazy," and the memory of this child haunts her. Jelinsky notes that Marina’s conflicts usually stem from contract issues, jealousy, or suspicion, but she does not appear to fear anyone in particular.

Later, at the Blue Wild Boar pub, Craddock meets with Detective William "Tom" Tidler. Tidler reports that while there are no major rumors at the movie studio, staff find Marina annoying but charming, and they view her husband, Jason Rad, as a great director or producer. Craddock expresses suspicion regarding a lethal drug that Marina believes was intended for her, noting that she only informed her doctor of the matter rather than her husband. Tidler provides details on Marina’s past marriages: her first was to a realtor, her second to a foreign count or prince, her third to an actor named Robert Trask, and her fourth to a playwright named Isidore.

Craddock decides to consult Miss Marple, an astute elderly woman living near the church, to gather local information and help identify "suspect X" from a group of twenty people. Meanwhile, Miss Marple visits Mrs. Jamison at her hair salon, "Diane's Stylist," to request old issues of movie magazines for her own investigation.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple discusses her interest in celebrity gossip magazines with Shelley, explaining that she studies them to better understand human nature, which she believes remains consistent across different social spheres. Their conversation turns to the recent tragedy involving Heather Badcock. Shelley notes that while the police have not officially declared it a murder, the circumstances do not suggest suicide. Shelley describes Badcock as a high-energy "busybody" and observes that while her husband, Arthur Badcock, appeared depressed at a recent party, he now seems more lively. Shelley suggests the death might have been an accident involving a contaminated drink, a theory Miss Marple finds plausible but remains cautious about.

Detective Chief Inspector Cridland (referred to also as Mr. Domett Craddock, Dermot, and Cumberland) visits Miss Marple to share information regarding the case. He provides a list of individuals present on the landing at the time of Badcock's death. While many locals and officials have been ruled out, the investigation continues to focus on husbands. Both Cridland and Miss Marple suspect that the intended victim of the crime was actually Marina Greg, rather than Heather Badcock. Cridland, motivated by the reputation of Miss Marple’s former "Tuesday Night Club" for solving mysteries, seeks her assistance in analyzing the witnesses and their locations. Miss Marple shifts the focus of the investigation toward Marina Greg’s adopted children—a local child, a foreign refugee, and an American child—seeking information on their current whereabouts.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple discusses her theory with Detective Dermot Craddock and Detective Dermot Coddle regarding the children of Marina Gregg. She suggests that the children, who were sent away after enjoying luxuries, may have developed resentment due to feelings of rejection. She expresses interest in discovering their current ages and locations, noting that magazines suggest they are now adults.

Craddock reviews a list of potential suspects. He notes that Jason Rad, Marina's husband, is a prime suspect because he attempted to hide that his wife was the intended victim to avoid terrifying her. Marina is described as neurotic and prone to anxiety attacks. Craddock also mentions Ella Jerinsky, the efficient secretary, and explores the possibility of a motive involving her feelings for Marina's husband. Other individuals mentioned include local photographers, newspaper reporters, a former wife of Marina's husband, and Adwick Fenn, a former close friend whose unexpected appearance caused a stir. Additionally, a young man named Blackie Preston is noted for being evasive about the events of the day.

In a separate discussion, Inspector Cornish and Craddock evaluate the suspects present on the landing when Marina Gregg displayed a "frozen expression." While Cornish briefly considers the corrupt mayor, he is ruled out. The Glass couple is also excluded as they never left St. Mary Mead. The remaining suspects include the American stranger Adwick Fenn, the American film star Laura Bruster—the ex-wife of Marina's third husband—and a London photographer named Margaret Bens. Craddock decides to interview the reporter and travel to London to investigate Fenn, Bruster, and Bens.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a discussion regarding the attempted poisoning of Marina Gregg, investigators debate potential suspects. Koenig suggests Lola Bruton is suspicious, but Demott Delacroix argues that the difficulty of poisoning Marina's drink unnoticed makes it unlikely for a high-profile guest like Laura Broust. Crocker suggests the butler, Joseph, or someone in the catering company could have easily administered camphor. Mr. Cragdock notes that the secretary, Ella Jelinsky, or a young man named Blackley Preston had the perfect opportunity to act in public without drawing attention. He also mentions that Marina's husband, Jason Rad, remains a suspect, though Conness observes that Rad appears devoted to her.

Following a phone call announcing that Marina is ready for questioning, Demott Delacroix goes to Goschington Manor to meet Ella Jelinsky. Demott observes Ella's efficient, "poker-faced" demeanor and theorizes she might have a motive involving her love for Jason Rad. He speculates that Ella could have slipped the drug into Marina's cup during a moment of distraction, but the plan failed when Heather Baddock accidentally spilled her drink, leading Marina to offer her untouched cup to Baddock, resulting in Baddock's death. When questioned about the catering company from Basingstoke, Ella claims Jason Rad insisted on hiring a local firm. Demott notices Ella's red-rimmed eyes, which she attributes to hay fever.

Detective Chief Inspector Claddock eventually meets Marina Gregg in a private sitting room. Despite her recent illness, Marina appears vital, beautiful, and clear-eyed. She apologizes to Claddock for her previous behavior and her slow recovery.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Marina Gregg tells Detective Claddock that she believes she was the intended victim of a poisoning attempt because her drink had been tampered with, though a different woman ultimately consumed it. She reveals that she felt compelled to speak after being advised by Jason Rad, Gilchrist, and Ginx. To support her claim, Marina presents a note found on her dressing table after a bath that reads, "Don't think you can escape next time." She suggests the note could have been left by someone in the house or someone who climbed onto her balcony.

Marina discloses that this is not the first threatening note she has received. Three weeks prior, a handwritten note saying "Get ready to die" was sent to her movie company. On the day of a cocktail party, a gardener delivered a note stating, "Today is your last day alive," which Marina claims was delivered by a man on a bicycle wearing glasses. She previously dismissed these as jokes or religious harassment.

Detective Claddock and Detective Morton express skepticism regarding Marina's claims of ignorance, suggesting her celebrity status provides many potential motives for enemies. Jason Rad joins the conversation, defending Marina and claiming they have no enemies, though he views the situation as a joke that turned dangerous. Finally, Detective Clutter questions Marina about a witness report claiming she looked frightened while greeting the Baskervilles. He specifies that the witness saw her looking toward the stairs, where several individuals had recently appeared, including Mr. and Mrs. Glass, a reporter, Adele Fenn, and Laura Bruste. Marina denies being frightened.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation, Dermot Crawford questions Marina Greg regarding her distracted behavior during a reception, specifically her failure to respond to Mrs. Baskerville. Marina claims she experienced a "cold scene," a psychological blankness caused by fatigue and the mechanical nature of her acting profession. Crawford expresses concern for Marina's safety, noting that someone is sending threatening letters and has already attempted to poison her. He suggests the perpetrator is closely related to the house and may have a mental disorder. Jason Rad, Marina's husband, urges her to disclose any suspicions.

Detective Chief Inspector Craddock questions Marina about her reaction to the unexpected arrival of Adwick Fenn and Miss Laura Brushter. Marina admits she was happy to see them. Craddock reveals that Laura Brushter is the ex-wife of Marina's third husband, Robert Truscott. Marina acknowledges that Laura had once made a drunken threat involving a gun, but insists the conflict is ancient history. Jason Rad defends Laura, stating she had no opportunity to poison Marina's drink because he was standing close to her. Regarding Adwick Fenn, Jason describes him as a prominent figure in the American entertainment industry and an old friend.

Elsewhere, Mrs. Bantry is weeding her garden when she observes Ella Jelinecki emerging from a phone booth. When Mrs. Bantry greets her, Ella appears startled and explains she was using the phone due to a problem with her home line. After Ella leaves, a suspicious Mrs. Bantry decides to satisfy her curiosity and uses the phone to call an American number.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Bantry calls Gossington Hall and attempts to probe the recipient about whether their phone is malfunctioning. When the recipient expresses confusion, claiming the phone is fine, Mrs. Bantry claims her hearing is failing. She then calls Jane (Miss Marple) to report that a secretary had gone to a public phone booth to falsely claim the hall's phone was broken, suggesting the secretary wanted to make a private call without being overheard.

Donald McNeil, a red-haired man, speaks with investigators Dermot Clogg and Crawford. McNeil denies having a motive for the murder of Heather Badekirk or the attempted murder of Marina Gregg. He admits he was at the scene and witnessed Heather Badekirk gasping for breath before she died from poison, but claims he saw nothing else. McNeil reveals he had previously gone upstairs to get drinks, during which he saw fashion photographer Margaret Bens and Lola Bristow. He notes Lola had dyed her hair a "Fiji style" rust red and was accompanied by an unknown, important-looking American man. McNeil also mentions that he was Marina Gregg's first husband, though his identity was previously considered insignificant.

At Scotland Yard, Inspector Clodd reviews files and telegrams before heading to the Savoy to interview Lola Bruce. Lola, who is dressed sumptuously, defends Marina Gregg and dismisses the poisoning attempt as a mistake. She admits to a past connection involving a man named Rob, noting that Marina had fallen madly in love with him twelve years prior while Lola was struggling with neurosis and her marriage. Lola explains she married Eddie Groves following her divorce from Rob to maintain stability for her children.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Constable Craddock interrogates Laura Brouster regarding her past threats against Marina Gregg. Craddock reveals that Brouster had previously threatened to kill Marina and admits that Brouster once shot and killed Eddie Groves during an argument. Brouster dismisses the accusations, claiming her words were blown out of proportion and arguing that it is unrealistic she would travel to England specifically to poison Marina’s cocktail. She describes Marina as a fickle woman who struggled with personal issues, including failed attempts to raise adopted children. Craddock notes that an obvious attempt on Marina's life had occurred, though an unknown lady was mistakenly killed instead.

Later, Craddock visits Adwick Fenn at the Duchester, Suite 190. Fenn discusses Gosington Hall, noting its Victorian style, and speculates that the drug in the cocktail was intended for Marina. Fenn suggests that Lina Brown might have had a motive involving a film role, though he posits the poisoning might have been intended to scare Marina rather than kill her. Craddock confronts Fenn about rumors that he was once obsessively in love with Marina and that she feared him. Fenn admits to a past obsession but denies making threats, claiming his previous interference with Marina's film career was a commercial decision to protect his investment. He dismisses Marina's fear as childish and maintains that he moves on quickly from disappointments.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Adwick Fenn and Chief Inspector Cuddlecock discuss a potential poisoning involving Marina Greg and Heather Bardwell. Fenn suggests that multiple people could have been involved in the act, but he refuses to speculate on specific suspects.

Detective Superintendent Cumberland, accompanied by Detective Dermot Klatzko and Detective Constable Craddock, attempts to contact photographer Margaret Bens. After failing to reach her by phone, they visit her studio in a dead-end alley off Tottenham Court Road. Inside, they meet Johnny Jethro, a thin young man who assists Margaret with her scheduling. Jethro explains that the studio phone was unplugged to prevent distractions. Craddock informs Jethro that they are investigating a murder in St. Mary Mead, near Marchbournem, involving poison in a cocktail, and that Margaret was an eyewitness. Jethro offers to drive the detectives to Margaret's current location at the Kitts' house in Hampstead Heath.

Upon arriving at the residence, the detectives observe Margaret directing a photo shoot involving two girls, Jane and Elsie, in an attempt to capture natural poses. When Craddock introduces himself, Margaret—described as thin and skeletal with thick black hair—acknowledges him. Craddock explains he wishes to ask questions regarding the incident at Goschington Manor near Marchbarn, where Margaret was reportedly taking photographs. Margaret confirms she remembers the event well but asks if the detective is Constable Coney.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Demot Craddock, a detective from the London Police Force, meets with Margaret Buns (also referred to as Margaret Bens or Margaret Bunce) to discuss the murder case involving Marina Gregorovna. Demot explains that the county police commissioner transferred the case to him because it is considered a transatlantic matter. Margaret, a photographer, drives Demot back to her studio at Tooting Court Road.

During their discussion, Margaret explains that she was hired to take exclusive photos of a reception where Marina Greg and Jason Rad were hosting guests. Margaret had positioned her camera on the stairs to capture people ascending and to photograph Marina and her guests. She confirms she could see Marina clearly and occasionally saw Jason Rad, but she did not see Mrs. Bardell or witness her drinking the fatal beverage. Margaret recalls photographing the mayor and noted that Adwick Fenn and Lola Brouste were walking up the stairs behind the mayor.

Demot asks Margaret if she noticed Marina appearing uncomfortable or displaying an unusual expression. He mentions that various witnesses described Marina's expression differently, with some saying she looked startled or frozen. Margaret produces a glossy photograph she took of Marina. In the photo, Marina is being held by a woman whose back is to the camera, but Marina’s eyes are fixed on something to the left with a completely expressionless face, showing neither fear nor pain. Demot concludes that Marina had seen something significant. Margaret reveals she has not sold the photo to newspapers because she finds it unethical to profit from peering into someone's mind. Demot notes that Margaret is straightforward, as he already knew from his files that she was trained in America.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an interrogation, Detective Constable Craddock questions the famous photographer Margaret Bunsen (also referred to as Miss Maple or Miss Benes). After discussing her training at Reingold Film Company under Andrew Quill and her residence in Seven Quays, Craddock confronts her with the truth regarding her past. He reveals that she was adopted by Marina Greg and lived with her for four years.

Margaret admits the truth, explaining that Marina Greg adopted three children from different locations: herself, an infant named Rod, and an older boy named Angus. She describes their life in America as a grand performance where Marina acted as a "mother" for public image while actually treating the children as temporary substitutes. This facade ended when Marina became pregnant with her own child, a child who was later born with a disability. Margaret expresses deep resentment toward Marina for making the children feel loved only to discard them once she had her own biological child. She notes that Rod moved to the American Midwest to run a farm, while the whereabouts of Angus, who once expressed a desire to kill the unborn baby, remain unknown.

Margaret clarifies that she traveled to Britain to take photos of Marina out of curiosity, not because of any arrangement. When Craddock asks if she attempted to poison Marina, she denies it. However, Craddock reveals that Marina Greg is still alive, suggesting that while Margaret did not kill her, someone else has committed a murder.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an interrogation, an unnamed woman denies attempting to poison Marina or kill Mrs. Badcock, despite the Inspector's suggestion that she intended to kill Marina. She claims to know nothing about the culprit but sarcastically suggests that many people—including a black-haired secretary, Mr. Blackley Preston, servants, or movie company employees—could be hiding their true identities.

At 16 Aubrey Street, Shelley Beck discusses her frustrations with her neighbors with her husband, Jim Beck, while he assembles a model. Shelley expresses resentment toward a neighbor, Miss Knight (whom she refers to as Miss Maple), for meddling in her business and offering unsolicited dietary advice. Shelley criticizes the local atmosphere of the new housing estate, noting that neighbors like the Hartwells and the Barnabys are constantly complaining or making noise. She also mentions the breakup of Lily Price and Harry, whom she considers a bad person.

The conversation shifts to local gossip regarding Arthur Badcock and Mary Bain; Shelley suggests Arthur's current state of mind may not be genuine, though Jim warns her that his wife has just died. Annoyed by Jim's lack of attention, Shelley decides to go to Glady Dixon's house to borrow a dress. After leaving her home, she passes a neighbor's house and heads toward number sixteen on Blenheim Lane, where she enters the open door and calls for Glady.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Shelley visits Gladys, who is making a paper pattern in a small bedroom. Gladys mentions feeling indigestion after eating. The conversation shifts to news from the movie company, where Gladys reports that Marina Greg returned to the set and caused trouble by claiming her coffee tasted off. Gladys notes that she serves Marina coffee in a unique ceramic cup, but Mr. Rad settled the matter by pouring the coffee down the sink. Shelley suggests that Marina may be the target of assassination attempts, citing threatening letters and a previous incident where a marble statue fell from a pedestal in a room called "Short Brown," nearly hitting Marina.

Gladys expresses a desire to quit her job at the movie company's restaurant, fearing that those intending harm often strike bystanders. She recalls an incident involving Heather, noting that it seemed Heather may have spilled her navy blue nylon taffeta cocktail dress on purpose. Gladys expresses interest in the dress and plans to visit the mansion to speak with the Italian butler, Mr. Zucco, though Shelley suspects Gladys merely wants an excuse to see him.

In a separate scene, Miss Marple discusses her knitting with Dr. Hedlock. The conversation turns to the murder case. Miss Marple shares her conclusion that if the cocktail was tampered with, the administration of the drug must have occurred in a way that no one witnessed, despite there being eighteen to twenty people present. Dr. Hedlock suggests the drug might have been placed in an eye-drop bottle beforehand.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple discusses three possibilities regarding why no one reported seeing someone add something to Marina Gregg's glass, despite twenty people being present. She suggests the first possibility is that an observant person saw the act but was too unintelligent to realize its significance. The second possibility is that someone, accustomed to people taking medicine or pills during meals, added a substance to a glass they mistook for their own, making the act appear unremarkable to onlookers. The third possibility is that someone witnessed the event but remained silent, potentially due to blackmail. Miss Marple notes she lacks evidence for the third option unless another murder occurs.

Meanwhile, Ella Jelinsky completes a phone call where she mischievously whispered "I see you..." to the recipient, feeling a sense of intoxicating power. After passing Madame Blanc in the garden, Ella returns to her office to find Jason Rad visibly distressed. Jason reveals that he secretly saved a sample of the coffee Marina Gregg complained about and sent it for analysis. The results confirm the presence of arsenic. Jason notes that while Marina was wrong about the coffee tasting bitter—as arsenic is tasteless—her intuition was correct. Jason expresses concern over the threat letters Marina has been receiving and the danger posed by the arsenic. He questions the reliability of the servants, specifically mentioning Giuseppe, while Ella maintains that the servants are upstanding and that tampering with food without being noticed is impossible. Jason remains deeply unsettled by the situation.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jason Radle and Ella Jelinski discuss the late happiness of a woman, whom Jason believes was like a child when content. Ella expresses cynicism regarding lasting happiness and notes that Giuseppe has gone to London to visit a sick relative in Soho. Jason expresses intense concern for Marina, stating she is in danger. After Ella leaves the office to retrieve nasal spray for her hay fever, she enters her bathroom and uses a spray bottle. She realizes too late that the liquid has a bitter almond taste, indicating she has inhaled cyanide.

In a separate investigation, Frank Koenig informs Clutter that Miss Bristow was not in London on the day in question. Clutter and Koenig discuss the possibility that the butler, who is also in London, could have slipped cyanide into Eileen's inhaler. Later, Claddock calls Adwick Fenn to inform him of Ella Jelinski's death by cyanide poisoning. Fenn suggests that Jason Radle might have information regarding her motives. Fenn then reveals that Ella had previously called him, claiming she saw him put pills in a drink, implying she was attempting to blackmail him. Fenn identifies the caller as Ella because the voice sneezed, a symptom of her hay fever. Claddock informs Fenn that his whereabouts will be investigated. Claddock, Clark, and Cuney discuss whether Fenn’s explanation is a "grand deception" involving a fake alibi.

Meanwhile, Joseph returns to Gosington Manor after midnight. As he enters his suite and turns the key in the lock, he feels a hard object against his back and is ordered by a voice to raise his hands and remain silent.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Luigi Mazzoni is shot twice and falls forward. Nearby, Bianca hears what she believes to be a gunshot but dismisses the sound and returns to sleep.

Later, Miss Knight arrives breathless to inform Miss Marple that the Italian butler, Giuseppe, was shot the previous night. Miss Knight attributes the violence to "American hooligans." Miss Marple suspects that Giuseppe was killed because he was attempting to blackmail someone. Miss Marple also notes that Giuseppe had traveled to London the day before his death.

Miss Marple pulls Shirley aside to discuss the matter privately. Shirley reveals that her friend Gladys, a restaurant worker and acquaintance of Giuseppe, had intended to ask him about something "odd" she had observed. Shirley mentions a rumor that Mr. Rad's secretary suffered a heart attack and may be dead, but the primary focus remains on Giuseppe.

Shirley explains that Gladys was a worker at the party involving the death of Mrs. Baskerville. According to Shirley, Gladys observed something strange regarding Mrs. Baskerville and a cocktail: Gladys claimed that Mrs. Baskerville intentionally spilled a cocktail on her own skirt to ruin it. Shirley finds this detail ridiculous and suspects Gladys may be withholding the true nature of what she saw. Miss Marple expresses concern that Gladys did not report her observations to the police, suggesting that the significance of the event may be misunderstood or that crucial facts are missing.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Knight brings a drink to Miss Marple, criticizing the housekeeper, Mrs. Baker, for leaving a vacuum cleaner in the hallway. Miss Marple defends the housekeeper, noting that Miss Knight had called her in to talk. Miss Knight mentions having to redo a bedspread and promises to ensure Mrs. Bickerdyke remains quiet while Miss Marple rests.

Elsewhere, a handsome American man asks an elderly lady for directions to sixteen Blenheim Court to find Gladys Dickson. The lady informs him that Gladys is likely working at the Blackwoods Film Company restaurant. The man explains he is looking for her because she did not come to work, and he intends to find her at Gosington Manor, where a butler was shot the previous night. The lady also mentions that Mr. Rad's secretary died of a sudden illness.

Inspector William (Tom) Tidler visits 16 Blenheim Terrace to find Gladys Dickson. A fifteen-year-old girl informs him that Gladys left town suddenly that morning for a sponsored vacation, though she provided no address. Tidler later reports to the police station that Gladys called in sick at the movie company for a week. He also notes that Marina Gregg is in a state of mental disorder, claiming her coffee was poisoned. While rumors suggest Mr. Rad kept some coffee for chemical analysis that proved it was poisoned, Rad denies the coffee was suspicious, claiming Marina's perception is skewed by stress and threatening letters. Rad shows Chief Inspector Credlock two printed threatening letters: one stating "You won't have to wait long, get ready yourself," and another featuring a skull and crossbones with the message, "This is you, Marina." Rad suggests a local who hates the acting profession might be responsible. Rad expresses desperation to remove his wife from the situation due to the deaths of Eira and Mr. Zucchini. Marina, resting on a chaise lounge, asks her husband if the visitor was Detective Clark.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Marina Greg expresses intense fear and paranoia to her husband, Jason Rad, following the deaths of Ella and Giuseppe. She believes an unknown enemy is targeting her, citing a suspicious incident at a movie company where she suspects her coffee was poisoned. Marina expresses suspicion toward Ella, believing Ella hated her due to her love for Jason, but she acknowledges Ella is dead. Jason attempts to comfort Marina, promising to ensure her safety, though Marina notes his expression becomes strange, like a "clown laughing at something extremely sad," when he swears to care for her until the end. To calm herself, Marina requests a yellow pill.

The following day, Chief Inspector Craiglockhart visits Miss Marple, feeling disheartened by two murders occurring within twenty-four hours. Miss Marple provides him with whisky and soda to help him relax. During their conversation, DeMortimer Craddock (referred to as Dermot) and Miss Marple discuss the investigation. Miss Marple suggests that the murderer is often the most obvious person, such as a spouse. While Dermot argues that Jason Rad is truly in love with Marina, Miss Marple notes that while Jason is a wealthy man with no obvious motive to kill his wife, investigators must look "around the edges" of the case. She concludes that while the investigation might naturally focus on the husband, the true culprit remains difficult to find.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple discusses her observations with Mr. Cladock and Coddle, noting that her reading of gossip magazines has provided insight into human nature and scandals. She suggests that the person attempting to kill Marina Greg is someone with an abnormal psychology, drawing a parallel to Alfred, the son of Mrs. Pike, who is now in Ferewyse Asylum. 

The conversation turns to the deceased Italian butler, Giuseppe, who traveled to London on the day of his death. Coddle reveals that Giuseppe arrived in London at 11:30 a.m. and deposited £500 in cash into his bank account at 1:33 p.m., without visiting any relatives. Miss Marple posits that this sum might be an installment of a larger payment, suggesting the killer could be someone of humble background working at the movie company, such as a servant or gardener, or perhaps a hired assassin. Cladock notes that Adwick Fenn, Lola Brutus, and Margaret Bens all went to London that day and were unaccounted for during the hours Giuseppe was in the city.

Miss Marple expresses a suspicion that the motive involves the children Marina Gregg adopted. This leads to a discussion regarding how childhood trauma and intense emotions can manifest in adulthood, illustrated by Claridge’s visceral reaction to jellied pudding rolls following his mother's death.

After Miss Knight enters to serve tea and expresses concern that discussing murder might upset the elderly Miss Marple, Detective Mortimer assures her that Miss Marple is composed enough to handle the topic. Once Miss Knight leaves, Miss Marple asks Coddle to review the party guests and specifically inquires about the photographs taken by Margaret Bens on the stairs. Coddle produces one of the photographs for her to examine.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple examines a photograph featuring Marina Gregg, Jason Radcliffe, Arthur Badcock, and Mrs. Badcock. Miss Marple observes that Marina Gregg has a shocked, stunned expression in the image. Dermot provides notes regarding what Mrs. Badcock said to Marina Gregg during the photo session. While accounts from Mrs. Bantling, Jason Radle, and Mr. Badcock vary slightly in phrasing, they all convey that Mrs. Badcock was a great fan who had once gotten out of bed while sick (either with chickenpox or the flu) to obtain an autograph from Marina Gregg many years prior. Miss Marple connects this to a mystery regarding why Mrs. Badcock destroyed a new dress.

Miss Marple then reflects on a housekeeper at the Loriston house who frequently muddled telephone messages due to poor grammar, noting that such mistakes can lead to confusion. She also reveals that she personally sent Gladys Dixon, a seamstress, to Bournemouth with money for a vacation to ensure her safety.

Two days later, Miss Knight informs Miss Marple that Miss Conway, who is described as unstable with a poor memory, has been staying at the Bellgrove Hotel in Lyndhurst. Miss Knight suggests Miss Conway may be suffering from dementia rather than amnesia. Following this conversation, Miss Marple calls Dr. Hedgcock to request a meeting. When the doctor arrives, Miss Marple asserts that she is healthy and strong, arguing that she does not require a permanent live-in caregiver and only needs someone to visit daily for tidying.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Haddock advises Miss Marple that living alone is risky due to her age and a touch of bronchitis. He suggests she replace her current caretaker, Miss Knight, with an old servant, noting that her nephew, Raymond, would pay a reasonable wage. After Dr. Haddock leaves to treat patients with common diseases—specifically measles, whooping cough, and suspected scarlet fever—Miss Marple becomes suspicious of his mention of these illnesses.

Miss Marple calls Mrs. Bantry to clarify a detail regarding Heather Badcock. Mrs. Bantry recalls Heather telling Marina Greg a story about having chickenpox, but Miss Marple questions if the illness was actually whooping cough. Mrs. Bantry dismisses the idea, suggesting it might have been hives instead. Following the call, Miss Marple considers whether her former parlour maid, Loyal Florence, could be persuaded to return to St. Mary Mead to care for her, though she doubts Florence would leave her cottage.

Shelley enters to collect dishes and attempts to comfort Miss Marple regarding the influence of Miss Knight. Shelley then proposes that she and her husband, Jim, move into the empty rooms near the kitchen—formerly the gardener's quarters—to care for Miss Marple. Shelley promises that Jim can assist with plumbing and carpentry, while she will learn housekeeping. Although Miss Marple doubts Shelley's domestic abilities, she is drawn to Shelley's eager and lively nature. Miss Marple agrees to consider the proposal, stating she will arrange for Miss Knight to move to a hotel in Lyndhurst to care for a Miss Conway, and requests to speak with Jim to discuss the details.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Shelley informs Miss Marple and Xie Li that Arthur Badcock is at the police station to assist with an investigation. Xie Li reveals a rumor from Mr. Upshaw that Arthur Badcock was previously married to the actress Marina Gregg before her career took off. Following this news, Miss Marple becomes restless and determined to take action. She instructs Miss Knight to call Inspector Englebert (whom she refers to as Inch) and begins searching for a medical book to consult a specific page regarding a hint provided by Dr. Hedlock.

Miss Marple calls the Reverend to inquire about a reception held when Mrs. Badcock died. She asks if he remembers a conversation between Mrs. Badcock and Miss Gregg regarding a chance encounter in Bermuda. The Reverend recalls that Mrs. Badcock mentioned having a mild form of measles at the time. 

Driven by this information, Miss Marple takes a taxi driven by Roberts to Goose Green House to see Mr. Jason Rad. Upon arrival, she is met by a confused, frail man and a polite young man with an American accent named Blackley Preston. Despite Preston's attempts to block her access to Mr. Rad, Miss Marple insists on waiting. Preston eventually returns with Dr. Gilchrist, a man in tweeds who recognizes Miss Marple through his friendship with Dr. Hedlock. Miss Marple maintains her resolve to see Mr. Rad.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Gilchrist informs Miss Marple that Mr. Rad’s wife passed away in her sleep due to a suspected overdose of sleeping pills. While the doctor suggests it may have been an accident, he notes the possibility of suicide or foul play, though he cannot confirm the cause. Despite the news, Miss Marple insists on seeing Jason Radcliffe.

Dr. Gilchrist facilitates the meeting, telling Radcliffe that an elderly woman is determined to speak with him. Before they meet, Miss Marple speaks with Blackleigh Preston, Radcliffe's public relations employee of two and a half years. Upon meeting Radcliffe, Miss Marple explains she is there to prevent an innocent person from suffering. She reveals that Arthur Badcock is being questioned by police regarding the death. Radcliffe is confused, believing Badcock does not know his wife, but Miss Marple clarifies that Badcock was actually his wife's first husband, formerly known as Alfred Beadle, a property developer.

Miss Marple requests to stand at the top of the stairs where Radcliffe and his wife used to greet guests. She observes the location and a painting titled *The Smiling Madonna* by Giovanni Bellini. She notes that Mrs. Brentley previously mentioned the wife's expression "froze" while looking at this painting. Miss Marple asserts that the situation is simple, though Radcliffe remains confused. Detective Chief Inspector Demetrius Craddock arrives at the house. Miss Marple insists on discussing the matter at the stairs, identifying it as the scene of the crime related to the day Heather Badcock was poisoned. She suggests the incident was an unavoidable consequence of Heather Badcock's character.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple explains the motive behind the death of Heather Badcock to Jason Radcliffe and Detective Constable Caddle. She reveals that Heather Badcock had previously contracted measles and, despite being ill, attended a celebration to meet the movie star Marina Gregg to obtain her autograph. During that encounter, Heather unknowingly infected the pregnant Marina Gregg with measles. This infection caused Marina's child to be born with mental and physical defects, a tragedy that haunted Marina for years.

Miss Marple asserts that when Heather Badcock met Marina Gregg again and boasted about her bravery in attending the event while sick, Marina acted impulsively. Marina had previously ingested camphor, a dangerous drug, into her own drink. To ensure Heather consumed it, Marina intentionally pushed Heather's arm, causing Heather to spill her own cocktail. Marina then insisted that Heather drink from her own cup after cleaning the mess. To cover her tracks, Marina attempted to frame herself as the intended victim by staging threatening letters and self-poisoning incidents.

Miss Marple suggests that Jason Radcliffe had known the truth about the measles all along and had been attempting to protect Marina from further harm or legal consequences. Following the explanation, Detective Inspector Tidler interrupts the conversation. Miss Marple then visits Marina Gregg, who lies dead on a bed. Miss Marple muses whether Marina died from a self-administered overdose of pills or if someone else provided them, while Jason Radcliffe reflects on Marina's beauty and suffering.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a three-day boat journey from New Zealand to Plymouth, twenty-one-year-old Gwendraith Ridd decides to forgo a train trip to London to instead explore southern England by car. Her goal is to find a permanent home to share with her husband, Jervis, who is currently abroad on business and may not return for several months. Although they are both orphans, Jervis encourages Gwendraith to select a house that features a small garden and is located near the south coast.

A week after arriving, while traveling through Dartmouth in a rented Daimler, Gwendraith spots a white Victorian villa for sale. She stays at the Royal Clarence Hotel and returns the next day to view the property. The house features a long, narrow living room, French doors leading to a terrace, a rockery, and views of the sea. During the viewing, she meets the owner, the Honorable Mrs. Hargreaves, a melancholy woman who is preparing to move to Kent following the death of her husband, Colonel Hargreaves, from pneumonia.

While Mrs. Hargreaves discusses the Colonel's final illness, Gwendraith mentally renovates the property. She envisions modernizing the kitchen, adding bathrooms, and redecorating the rooms, which are currently painted in uneven shades of yellow-brown. She notes the house has approximately seven bedrooms, including an attic and a circular bedroom with projecting windows. Gwendraith feels a strong sense of belonging to the house as she reviews the property details and asking price.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While inspecting a property named "Mountain Slope Villa," Gwendolyn experiences a sudden, inexplicable sense of fear. She asks the owner, Mrs. Hengrave, if the house is haunted or if anyone has died there. Mrs. Hengrave responds icily, noting that while people have died in the century-old house, the previous owner, Miss Elvosi, was in good health. Mrs. Hengrave explains she is in a hurry to move to Kent to be with her own kind. After touring the neglected garden and terrace, Gwendolyn decides to make an offer. Following a stroll through Dartmouth, Gwendolyn learns the real estate agent has accepted her offer, and she sends a telegram to Jules to announce the purchase.

A month later, Gwendolyn moves into the villa, furnishing it with high-quality items from her aunt's estate. While renovations are ongoing, she hires Mrs. Cochran to manage the kitchen. Mrs. Cochran, a well-mannered woman who disapproves of Gwendolyn's friendliness toward lower social classes, serves her breakfast in bed. Gwendolyn settles into a small, round bedroom that Mrs. Cochran suggests may eventually serve as a nursery. While exploring the room, Gwendolyn discovers a locked closet that has been painted shut for years. Later, Gwendolyn goes to the garden to check on Foster, the part-time gardener. She insists he carve a path through the rockery, which requires removing various bushes. During their interaction, Foster tells Gwendolyn that it looks as if she is "back in the past," leaving her questioning his meaning.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Foster discovers original steps leading toward the lawn that had been previously filled in. He discusses landscaping with Gwendalyn, noting that while the shrubs will provide a "deep view" for the living room window, they will block light and include expensive, old jasmine that cannot be transplanted. Gwendalyn inquires about the house's previous residents. Foster recounts that before the Hendricks, the house was occupied by Miss Elvosi, a Low Church believer, and a black pastor. He also recalls Mrs. Findlay, an aristocrat who lived there for many years, planted the magnolias and privet hedges, and eventually died abroad before being buried in the churchyard. Foster expresses dissatisfaction with the constant "change" in the area, noting how a local hospital became a girls' school.

Gwendalyn returns inside to write a letter to her cousin, Joan West, the wife of novelist Raymond West, declining an invitation to stay in Chelsea because she must oversee workmen at Deermeadow. Gwendalyn grows frustrated by the lack of a door between the living room and the dining room, which forces her to walk through a drafty hall. She consults Mr. Sims, an architect and interior designer, about installing a door. Despite her suspicion regarding his tendency to exceed budgets, Mr. Sims agrees it is a simple improvement. His foreman, Taylor, inspects the wall and reveals that a door originally existed in that exact location but had been blocked up. This discovery causes Gwendalyn unease, as she realizes she has repeatedly and instinctively walked toward the exact spot where the hidden door once stood, leading her to wonder if she possesses psychic abilities or if the house is haunted.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn requests that Taylor open a sealed cabinet in her upstairs bedroom. That night, she feels uneasy and fearful in her house, eventually finding comfort only in her own bedroom. The following day, after lunch served by Mrs. Coker, Gwendolyn opens the cabinet and discovers that the room's current floral wallpaper is different from the original wallpaper preserved inside the cabinet. She realizes the wallpaper inside the cabinet is identical to a pattern she had recently imagined for the room. Disturbed by the possibility that she can see the past or experience temporal anomalies—referencing John William Dunne’s theories—she sends a telegram to Raymond West in Chelsea, requesting to visit him.

Upon arriving, Gwendraith (Gwendolyn) feels nervous around Raymond and his wife, Joan, due to Raymond's unusual mannerisms and their elite cultural discourse. Raymond outlines a schedule of cultural events, including ballet at Sadler's Wells and a play at the Little Whitmoor Theatre. He also introduces the idea of Aunt Jane (Miss Maple), a woman who enjoys solving puzzles and was involved in a murder case. Gwendolyn meets the elegant Miss Maple and attends the Majesty Theatre with Raymond, Joan, an artist, and a lawyer. During the play, a terrifying climax involving a character's death causes Gwendolyn to scream and flee the theater in a panic. She runs blindly toward the Strand and eventually hails a taxi in Piccadilly to return to the house in Chelsea, arriving in a state of visible trembling.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After returning home in a state of distress, Gwendolen retreats to her bedroom, where she lies shaking and terrified. Miss Marple enters the room and provides Gwendolen with hot water bottles and strong, sugary tea, which helps calm her. Downstairs, Raymond and Joan discuss Gwendolen's condition, with Raymond questioning if her reaction was merely due to the play, while Mr. Maplewood suggests there is more to the matter.

The following morning, Gwendolen expresses fear that she is losing her mind and suggests seeing a psychiatrist. During breakfast, she receives a telegram from her husband, Jules, stating he will return from Dalhousie via plane within a week. This news causes Gwendolen further anguish, as she feels she cannot return to her life or the house associated with her recent experiences.

Gwendolen confides in Miss Marple, recounting her terror during the previous night's play. She describes a sudden, vivid vision of a woman named Helen, who appeared dead and strangled with a green face, accompanied by a voice and the sight of non-human, monkey-like hands. Gwendolen is disturbed by the fact that she does not know anyone named Helen and fears she is experiencing delusions or seeing hauntings. Miss Marple suggests that Gwendolen's experiences—including a hidden garden path, a bricked-up door, and detailed knowledge of wallpaper—might be suppressed memories from her own life. Gwendolen explains she was born in India to a British officer and raised in New Zealand, but she possesses only fragmented memories of the period between the two locations, including a man in a white military uniform and a nanny named Southie.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple suggests to Gwendolen that her recent experiences are not supernatural, but rather repressed childhood memories. Miss Marple notes that Gwendolen has confused two different journeys and points out that Gwendolen’s intimate knowledge of her current house—such as the wallpaper patterns, the mahogany bathtub, and the layout—matches a childhood home in England. Miss Marple posits that Gwendolen’s memory of seeing a woman named Helen being strangled is a suppressed trauma from when she was a child. She explains that Gwendolen’s description of looking through the gaps in the railings, rather than over them, is a behavior characteristic of a child.

To investigate, Miss Marple advises Gwendolen to contact her Aunt Alison to confirm if she ever lived in England. Ten days later, Miss Marple meets Gwendolen and her husband, Jules Reed, at an inn in Mayfair. Gwendolen presents an airmail letter from Aunt Alison (Mrs. Danby) that confirms Miss Marple's theory. The letter reveals that Gwendolen’s mother, Margaret, died in India after marrying Colonel Harryd. Following her mother's death, the Colonel intended to bring Gwendolen to England, but he instead married a young woman and later separated from her. Eventually, the Colonel sent Gwendolen to live with her relatives in England via a British nursemaid. The letter also mentions that the Colonel passed away in a nursing home about a year after Gwendolen arrived in England.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Alison Dumbie sends a letter to a recipient, expressing hope that they will be reunited with Charles or Giles soon and stating she will explain her own situation in a future correspondence.

In a separate setting, Miss Marple discusses a letter with Jules and Gwendolen. Jeeves reveals that his new home, Mount Edgecombe, is the site of a first-degree murder that occurred in the foyer approximately eighteen to twenty years ago. The victim was a beautiful woman whose only known detail is her Christian name. Jeeves expresses a desire to investigate the mystery, but Miss Marple becomes visibly distressed and warns the newlyweds to stay away from the matter. She argues that because the crime was never reported or suspected by the community, digging into the past could disrupt their peaceful lives and bring them pain. Despite her warnings, Jeeves remains determined to investigate the murder that happened in his own house.

Following her return to St. Mary Mead, Miss Marple appears absent-minded and exhausted, causing concern among her neighbors and her maid, Evelyn. She eventually summons her long-time friend and physician, Dr. Hedock. While the doctor attributes her weakness to the draining atmosphere of London, Miss Marple uses the pretext of needing restorative sea air to request a professional prescription for travel. She reveals her intention to go to the small town of Dartmouth, prompting Dr. Hedock to question her motive. Miss Marple responds by posing a hypothetical scenario regarding the discovery of an old, unreported murder.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple discusses a revived murder case with Dr. Hedlock. Dr. Hedlock warns her against getting involved, advising her to "let the sleeping murder sleep" because killers who have successfully covered their tracks may react dangerously if an investigation resumes. Miss Marple explains her motive is not personal curiosity but a desire to look after two young, inexperienced children involved in the matter. Dr. Hedlock expresses concern that her involvement in Dillesham might be a "death sentence."

Upon arriving in Dillesham, Miss Marple visits her friends, Colonel and Mrs. Bantry. She finds the Colonel with a gun and a Spanish hound, while Mrs. Bantry is gardening. During their conversation, they discuss the Sanders, a former cook and her husband who used an inheritance to open an inn at Dillmouth Seafront, number 14. Mrs. Bantry encourages Miss Marple to stay there for the sea air and good food.

In a separate scene, Giles and Gwendolyn discuss a potential crime scene at a mountain villa. Gwendolyn recalls seeing a figure with non-human gray claws, but Giles remains skeptical, suggesting she is misremembering. Gwendolyn considers that the event might have been a childhood nightmare, noting that no one in Dillmouth has any record of a murder or disappearance occurring in that house. Giles reluctantly agrees it might be a dream, but he maintains his disbelief, arguing that one cannot dream specific lines from "The Duchess of Malfi."

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jules Reid and Gwendolyn attempt to recover Gwendolyn's suppressed childhood memories. Jules suggests that Gwendolyn's vision of a monkey paw might have been a nightmare triggered by the trauma of seeing a corpse. Gwendolyn remains unable to recall details about her father or a woman named Helen, noting that her memories feel increasingly distant. Jules mentions that his search of parish death records yielded no one named Helen near the relevant age, only an elderly woman named Ellen Page.

Gwendolyn recalls that Foster, the gardener, stated the Elvewis and Findson-Wife families lived in the house before the Hengrave family. Jules hypothesizes that Gwendolyn's father may have rented the house and its furniture, prompting a visit to real estate agents. At Calbreath & Pendlebury, the company through which Gwendolyn purchased her house, Mr. Pendlebury explains that their records do not go back far enough to show a lease for a Captain Haroldy. He suggests speaking to the retired chief agent, Mr. Calbreath, who lives at Calcutta Lodge on Westerton Road.

At Calcutta Lodge, Gwendolyn and Jules meet Mr. Calbreath's daughter. She notes that her father's memory is scattered due to a stroke. Gwendolyn attempts to connect the house's name to her father's time in India. They are eventually introduced to the elderly Mr. Calbreath in his study. While he does not initially recognize the name "Hardie," he recalls a soldier and a man who once swindled him. Upon Gwendolyn providing the name "Hardie," Mr. Calbreath remembers Colonel Hardie, describing him as a nice man with a very pretty, young, blonde wife.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn and Jules (Jiles) interview Mr. Calbraith to gather information about a woman named Helen. Mr. Calbraith describes her as looking anxious and mentions a house called St. Catherine's Villa, which was once rented by a soldier and later auctioned to the Elwes family, a group of sisters who were strongly anti-Catholic. Following this lead, Jules uses his office to request marriage records from the Kennington Registry Office. He discovers a marriage certificate from Friday, August 7th, between Kellvin James Harrydi and Helen Spenceley Kennedy. This discovery puzzles the pair, as they believed Helen had divorced and moved away.

While Gwendolyn is walking along the seafront in Deal, she encounters Miss Marple, who is visiting the seaside for health reasons. Gwendolyn informs Miss Marple about their investigation and the newspaper advertisements they placed seeking information on Helen Spendlove-Haliday, née Kennedy. Miss Marple observes Gwendolyn’s underlying anxiety regarding the case.

Later, Jules and Gwendolyn receive a response to their advertisement from a professional named James Kennedy, M.D., of Highcliffe Manor in Woodley Porton. He identifies himself as Helen Spengler Kennedy's brother and requests information regarding her whereabouts. Jules and Gwendolyn travel to Woodrailton to meet him.
