At Highcliffe Manor, they encounter a stern, dark-skinned woman before being received by the elderly, piercing-eyed Dr. Kennedy. Giles presents a rehearsed story, claiming they are searching for Gwendolyn's childhood acquaintances. Initially cold and hostile, Dr. Kennedy's demeanor shifts instantly when Gwendolyn identifies herself as the daughter of her father's second wife, using her maiden name, Hardy. Dr. Kennedy recognizes her, asking if she is "Gwenith."

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn (Gwenith) and Jiles visit Doctor Kennedy to discuss Gwendolyn's past. Gwendolyn reveals she has purchased a mountain-side villa in Dealmore, which Kennedy identifies as St. Catherine's Manor, the house where Gwendolyn previously lived. Gwendolyn explains her motive for returning is a sense that the place feels like home, despite her hazy memories.

Gwendolyn seeks information regarding her father and her sister, Helen. Kennedy recounts that Helen returned from India on the same ship as Gwendolyn's father, Kelvin Harley. Although Kelvin was a single father at the time, they married in London and lived in Deal. However, less than a year later, Helen ran away with another man, possibly a married Roman Catholic. Kennedy notes that Kelvin did not want a divorce. Regarding Gwendolyn's father, Kennedy suggests he sent her to New Zealand due to pressure from her mother's relatives following the breakdown of his second marriage.

When Gwendolyn asks about her father's death, Kennedy claims his health was poor and he died in a nursing home on the East Coast two years after leaving Dilworth. Kennedy avoids providing the specific location of the nursing home or the site of the grave, dismissively arguing that focusing on the past is a mistake. Kennedy further reveals that he was Helen's half-brother and had helped her through past troubles before she married Walter Fane in India. Kennedy expresses a desire to contact Helen, but Gwendolyn and Jiles state they do not know her whereabouts. Gwendraith concludes that they hoped to contact Helen to learn more about her father.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After an initial meeting where Dr. Kennedy vaguely confirms that a sanatorium involved was a tuberculosis facility, Jiles and Gwendolyn depart, feeling that the doctor is withholding information. Despite Jiles's hesitation to continue the investigation, Gwendolyn insists on pursuing the truth regarding her father, Calvin Harley.

The following afternoon, Dr. Kennedy calls Jiles to arrange a visit. While waiting, Gwendolyn speaks with Old Foster, who describes Dr. Kennedy as a good but dull doctor who stopped practicing fifteen years ago due to ill health. Foster also mentions that Dr. Kennedy's sister was considered "wild" and ran off with another man.

When Dr. Kennedy arrives at three o'clock, he reveals that the facility Calvin Harley entered was not a tuberculosis sanatorium, but a mental institution. He explains that Calvin suffered a mental breakdown due to extreme stress after his wife, Helen, left him for another man. Dr. Kennedy asserts that Calvin’s belief that he had strangled Helen was merely a "bewitching delusion" caused by pathological fantasies and unsettling dreams. 

Dr. Kennedy recounts a specific incident where Calvin arrived at his examination room and claimed, "I killed Helen." Gwendolyn notes that when they inspected the bedroom, there was no body and no sign of a struggle. Dr. Kennedy explains that he found a note from Helen in a living room wastebasket, in which she stated she was leaving him for the man she loved. Dr. Kennedy concludes that Calvin saw the note and, in a state of mental instability, hallucinated the murder.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
A narrator recounts the history of Kelvin Harry and his sister, Helen. After Helen disappeared, Dr. Kennedy searched the house and found Helen's packed suitcase and clothes, but no body. Kelvin, believing he had hallucinated strangling Helen, agreed to enter a private sanatorium in Norfolk for treatment. Two years later, Kelvin died at the sanatorium. Following her disappearance, Helen sent two letters: one from Biarritz, France, stating she was going to Spain, and a second six months later from Florence, addressed to "Miss Kennedy," stating she did not want a divorce but would not grant one without proof. Kelvin chose not to divorce her. Gwendolyn, who is searching for news of Helen, reveals she heard her father confess to strangling Helen.

Jiles and Gwendolyn discuss the inconsistencies in the case. Gwendolyn claims she saw a body in the foyer, whereas Kelvin told Kennedy he strangled Helen in the bedroom. Jiles argues that if Kelvin had killed Helen and buried her, he would not have confessed the crime to Dr. Kennedy, a man he was not close to. Jiles notes that the lack of a death record in the parish register proves Helen did not die of natural causes. Jiles suggests the letters might be forgeries designed to mimic Helen's handwriting and reinforce Kelvin's belief that she had run away. Jiles concludes that the pattern of staged departure—including the packed clothes and timed letters—is typical of a calculated murderer, but this contradicts the behavior of a passionate killer who would confess impulsively. Jiles posits that an unknown factor, "X," is involved in the orchestration of these events.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jules and Gwendolyn discuss a theory regarding the death of Helen Haliday. Jules proposes the existence of an unknown factor, "X," representing a person who has not yet appeared. He hypothesizes that Helen was preparing to run away with a lover, and when he discovered her note, he strangled her in a fit of rage. Jules suggests that Helen did not actually die but instead regained consciousness and fled, which explains why Kelvin believed he had killed her and why her belongings were missing. Gwendolyn, recalling her childhood observations, remains skeptical, insisting she saw Helen dead.

The arrival of Miss Marple for tea interrupts their discussion. After some polite social chatter, Gwendolyn decides to disclose their findings to Miss Marple. Gwendolyn asks if her father might be involved in the case. Miss Marple suggests it is a possibility, noting that such strangulation cases often involve husbands. Miss Marple also acknowledges Jules's "X" theory, agreeing that an unknown element may exist. Gwendolyn then announces plans to visit the Salt Marsh Sanatorium in Norfolk, where her father passed away, to search for clues.

At the sanatorium, Jules and Gwendolyn meet Dr. Penrose. Gwendolyn explains she was raised in New Zealand and only recently learned the details of her father's past. Dr. Penrose reviews her father's medical records, noting that Colonel Harrydie suffered from intense delusions or hallucinations. He reveals that the Colonel was extremely agitated and insisted he had strangled his second wife out of jealousy. Dr. Penrose admits that without Dr. Kennedy's assurance that the wife was still alive, he would have believed the Colonel's claims.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Penrose informs Gwendolyn Reed that her father, Colonel Kelvin Haldi, committed suicide by overdosing on sleeping pills he had secretly stockpiled. Dr. Penrose clarifies that while Haldi suffered from delusions—including the belief that his wife, Helen, was poisoning him—he was not a murderer. The doctor suggests Haldi’s conviction that he had killed his wife stemmed from a psychological desire for punishment after finding a note from her stating she was leaving him. Dr. Penrose provides Gwendolyn with an old black notebook containing Haldi's case notes, which had been preserved by a Dr. McQuail.

While traveling back to London by train, Gwendolyn reads the notebook. The entries reveal Haldi's mental instability and his suspicion that Helen was hiding something or had a lover. He records a memory of finding a note in the dining room followed by "darkness," leading him to believe he strangled her. He expresses a desire to end his life to prevent scandal and protect Gweneth, whom he mentions is in New Zealand. Gwendolyn, moved to tears, notices a headline in a passenger's newspaper asking, "Who is the man in her life?" which mirrors the suspicions in the diary.

The narrative shifts to Miss Marple, who is walking through a seafront square and entering an embroidery shop on Fore Street. While purchasing turquoise yarn, Miss Marple engages in polite conversation with the shop assistants, noting that she has not been to the area in nineteen years.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple visits a fabric shop in Deal to gather information. She inquires about Finchson's house, which was previously rented by Captain Harry Daly and his wife. The shopkeeper identifies the former cook as Edith Peaget, who currently works at Windy Lodge in Dilmouth. The shopkeeper also provides details regarding the Feen family, noting that Walter Feen is a senior partner at a law firm and had previously traveled to India. Miss Marple mentions that Walter Feen was once engaged to Miss Kennedy, but she broke the engagement to marry Colonel Hardy. The shopkeeper adds that Colonel Hardy's second wife left him for someone else, potentially a summer visitor, which left Major Halliday a broken man. Miss Marple learns that Edith Peaget's sister is the wife of Mr. Montford, a confectioner, and that a message could be left for Edith there.

Later, Miss Marple meets Gwendolyn and Giles at the Cat's Prowess coffee shop. Gwendolyn presents a black notebook containing writings from her father, composed while he was in a nursing home. After reviewing the notebook, Miss Marple expresses uncertainty about whether the pair should continue their investigation, suggesting that while leads like former servants may still be found, the pursuit might ultimately be unproductive. However, she admits a feeling that something intangible makes the risk worthwhile. Giles proposes a theory regarding Helen Halyday: that she did not die as believed, but instead fled with a lover. He notes that this theory fails to explain why Helen never contacted her brother, Dr. Kennedy, or why Kelvin Halyday remained convinced he had strangled her. Giles suggests Helen's total disappearance is highly suspicious and may have driven Kelvin to suicide.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a discussion regarding the death of Helen Kennedy, Jervis proposes a hypothesis to explain the discrepancies in the case. He suggests that Calvin Harriott did not kill his wife, but was induced to believe he did. Jervis posits that an unknown individual, referred to as "X," anaesthetized Harriott with a high concentration of anesthetic in his whisky. While Harriott was unconscious, X strangled Helen in the hall, moved her to a bed to simulate a love affair, and then moved the body. This theory accounts for Harriott's belief that he committed the crime after waking up in a state of confusion and jealousy.

The group discusses potential motives for X, focusing on men from Helen's past. Miss Marple reveals that Helen had previously been engaged to a lawyer named Walter Feen, whom she traveled abroad to marry before breaking the engagement. Miss Marple, noting her own habit of gathering local gossip, mentions that Feen is now a senior partner at a law firm and lives with his mother. Gwendolyn suggests that Helen may have encountered another man while traveling abroad, possibly a married man of social standing. Miss Marple supports this, suggesting that a passionate but impossible romance could have caused Helen to break her engagement to Feen and return home. Gwendolyn concludes that Helen's subsequent relationship with her father was not a passionate affair, but a mutual consolation between two people sharing personal troubles.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn, Jiles, and Miss Marple discuss the mystery of a married man who may be a third "X" in a complicated history involving Helen and Walter Fane. Miss Marple suggests that Edith Pettigrew, a former cook at St. Catherine's Manor who lives in Dilham, might provide information. Gwendolyn decides to make a new will to get close to Walter Fane, despite Jiles's warnings.

In another location, Lily Kimball discovers an old newspaper advertisement asking anyone who knows Helen Spenceley-Harriet (née Kennedy) to contact Reed and Hardy in Southampton Street. Lily recalls serving Mrs. Harriet at St. Catherine's Manor, which was rented from Miss Finsbury and her husband. Lily speculates to her husband, Jim, that Mrs. Harriet may have been murdered by her husband, noting that a suitcase, handbag, and clothes went missing. She recalls that a Swiss nanny named Léonie saw something out the window during a chaotic night involving a doctor and a man sleeping in a dressing room. Lily believes Mrs. Harriet might have run off with a married lover, a theory she once shared with Edith, though Edith did not believe her.

Gwendolyn visits the law office of Walter Fane, a dignified, weary man of fifty. The office is an old-fashioned, Victorian-style space belonging to a senior partner. While discussing the signing of her simple will, Gwendolyn mentions that she and her companion have purchased a mountain villa that was formerly known as St. Catherine's Villa. Upon hearing this, Walter Fane removes his spectacles and looks down at the table.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn Reed meets with Walter Fane to discuss making a new will, as her previous relatives in New Zealand have died. During the meeting, Gwendolyn attempts to probe Fane's past by asking if he knew her mother, Meg Harley. Fane denies knowing Meg, explaining that he was once engaged to Helen Kennedy, who eventually married Colonel Harrydyke. Fane reveals that Helen traveled to India to marry him but changed her mind and met Gwendolyn's father on her return journey. Fane notes that Gwendolyn's father and Helen lived in Dalhousie for a time. Fane recalls carrying Gwendolyn on his shoulders when she was a small child and mentions that he left India after an unsuccessful attempt to grow tea to join his family's legal firm. He invites Gwendolyn and her husband to meet his mother for tea and schedules a follow-up meeting for Thursday at eleven o'clock.

Later, Gwendolyn discusses Fane with Jules by the seaside. She argues that Fane is too quiet, gentle, and upright to be a murderer, though Jules suggests that quiet individuals like Liz Burton or Wallace can also be suspects. Despite her hesitation, Gwendolyn recalls Fane's strange, unfocused gaze when Saint Catherine's Villa was mentioned.

In a separate scene, Gwendolyn visits Mrs. Montford's drawing room and meets Edith Peggitt, the sister of Mrs. Montford. Edith reminisces about Gwendolyn's childhood, recalling how Gwendolyn used to mispronounce "raisins" as "pouf-gan" when visiting her. Edith also mentions a former nanny named Léonie.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn, Jules, and Jeeves interview Edith Pargeter regarding Gwendolyn's childhood and the disappearance of her stepmother, Helen Kennedy (also referred to as Mrs. Harleyde or Lady Hildred). Edith recalls Gwendolyn's former nanny, Léonie, and a maid named Lily Abbot, who used to play games with Gwendolyn. Edith describes Helen as a kind person who was essentially a child herself, noting that Helen's brother, Ken Kennedy, was much older and reclusive.

The conversation shifts to Helen's sudden disappearance, which caused Colonel Hildred to collapse. Jules and Jeeves explain they are investigating for legal reasons regarding her status. Edith reveals that Lily Abbot had long suspected an affair between Helen and an unnamed man whose name starts with "E" or "H." This man and his wife, who were summer visitors from Northumberland staying at the Royal Clarence Hotel sixteen years ago, allegedly had an interest in Helen.

Edith discloses a significant discovery: Helen had planned to move to a house in Norfolk and had even invited Edith to join her. This move was kept secret by Helen, who seemed to have a sudden aversion to Dilham and appeared afraid to stay there. The passage concludes with Edith recalling a moment when Lily Abbot reported hearing the mysterious lady and gentleman talking in the sitting room after returning from the garden.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Edith Pettigrew recounts a past incident involving Lily and Lady Harriet. Lily claims that Lady Harriet once expressed intense fear of a man, stating, "I'm afraid of you!" Lily believed this indicated a serious threat. On the night of the incident, Lily went to the movies and took a child named Leonie with her, leaving the house under the assumption that the master and mistress would return soon. 

After the movies, Dr. Kennedy found Edith in the kitchen and asked Lily to check the mistress's bedroom to see if any clothes were missing. Lily returned excited, claiming Lady Harriet had run off with someone, possibly an officer or a chauffeur. However, Lily later became convinced that Lady Harriet had been murdered by the master. Lily noted that the missing clothing was inconsistent; for example, a silver-gray ball gown was taken without its matching belt, bra, and slip, and gold-embroidered shoes were taken without their silver-banded counterparts. Lily theorized the master killed her and buried her under the basement floor to stage a disappearance. Edith initially dismissed this after seeing the master walking down a hill with a golf club at the time of the alleged incident, concluding that someone else must have been responsible.

In a separate setting, Joram (using the name Mr. Reed) visits the Royal Clarence Hotel to manipulate the receptionist, Miss Nelcourt. He claims he is looking for an old guest register to settle a bet with his wife. Miss Nelcourt provides the register, which contains names of celebrities and royalty. Joram finds the record he seeks: Captain Sidney Erskine and his wife stayed at the hotel from July 27 to August 17. After copying the entry, Joram returns to a hillside villa and informs Gwendolyn that he believes he has found the person he was looking for.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jules and Gwendraith plan to travel to Anstey Hall in Dys, Northumberland, to investigate clues regarding Erskine. Jules has written to Kennedy to request Helen’s letters and a handwriting sample. Gwendraith suggests locating Lily Abbott, a perceptive former servant from St. Catherine's Manor who once worked in Torquay, believing she might provide information. Giles agrees to place an advertisement for Lily.

In Dartmouth, Miss Marple visits Mrs. Fenn, a woman she knows through a mutual friend, Eleanor Fern. During their conversation, Mrs. Fenn discusses her three sons: Gerald, who works in Singapore; Robert, who is in the army; and Walter, a homebody who lives at home and works at her firm. Mrs. Fenn reveals that Walter once traveled to India to become a tea planter because he was in love with Helen Kennedy, a woman she considered unsuitable and promiscuous. After Walter went to India, Helen wrote to him claiming she would marry him, but upon arriving at the docks, she rejected him to pursue a married man instead. Mrs. Fenn notes that Walter maintains extreme self-control and buries his emotions deeply. Miss Marple probes into Walter's temperament, leading Mrs. Fenn to recount a childhood incident where Walter, after his brother Robert broke a model airplane he had meticulously built, attacked Robert with a fire stick while screaming that he wanted to kill him.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple converses with Mrs. Fyne regarding the history of a woman who failed to marry Walter Fyne. After the failed engagement, the woman married a widower and moved to St. Catherine's Villa near the hospital, but she eventually left him for another man. Mrs. Fyne notes that Walter remained friends with the woman, often visiting her and playing with the child from her marriage. That child has since grown up and is now part of the Reed family. Mrs. Fyne also mentions Jackie Avesford, a former office clerk who pursued Helen Kennedy to improve his social status and now runs the Avesford Water Lily Carriage Company. During the conversation, Walter Fyne enters; Miss Marple observes him as a refined, quiet, and somewhat boring man.

Meanwhile, Gwendolyn and Jules visit Anstey Manor, a desolate white house on a barren hill, to investigate Colonel Richard Erskine’s past romantic affairs. Gwendolyn is motivated by her memories of Helen Kennedy. Upon arrival, they meet Colonel Erskine, a man Gwendolyn finds attractive and distinctive, unlike the unremarkable Walter Fyne. They also encounter Mrs. Erskine, Janet, a robust and haggard woman who watches Gwendolyn with suspicion. Gwendolyn engages the Colonel in small talk about real estate and her desire to live in the countryside, while the Colonel describes the area as isolated. He reveals that his family has resided at Anstey Manor for nearly three hundred years, dating back to the reign of Queen Anne.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a visit to the Erskine residence, Gwendolyn and Jiles meet with Colonel and Mrs. Erskine. Eustace Erskine discusses his two sons—one in the military and one recently graduated from Oxford—and expresses pride in the sacrifices he made for them. Gwendolyn observes a sense of darkness in the atmosphere and notes Mrs. Erskine's apparent jealousy. When Jiles mentions they are looking for a house in Deal, Mrs. Erskine reacts coldly, stating they lived there years ago and disliked the "lazy life." A moment of tension occurs when Jiles identifies a nearby house as St. Catherine's Cottage rather than the name Mrs. Erskine suggests, causing a visible reaction from the couple. Mrs. Erskine then excuses herself to the garden, where the Colonel tends to flowers.

After leaving, Gwendolyn and Jiles discuss their mission. Gwendolyn reveals she left a ring near some forget-me-nots to facilitate a pretext for returning. Jiles expresses guilt over Gwendolyn performing the "dirty work" of investigating, while Gwendolyn argues that a woman can ask personal questions more easily than a man. She expresses doubt that Colonel Erskine is the killer, believing he is a good man who loved Helen.

The following morning, Jiles monitors Anstey Manor while Gwendolyn returns to the Erskine garden under the pretext of searching for her lost engagement ring. After finding the ring near some larkspurs, Gwendolyn sits with Colonel Erskine and confronts him about his past relationship with her stepmother, Helen Kennedy (later Helen Harley). Erskine remains composed but tense, acknowledging that Gwendolyn is likely seeking information regarding certain letters.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendraith (Gwendolyn) questions Colonel Erskine regarding the disappearance of her stepmother, Helen. Erskine reveals that he and Helen had a serious romantic connection after meeting on a ship to India, though they agreed to part ways because he had a wife, Jenny, and children. Years later, after his father died, Erskine and his wife traveled to Deal for a holiday. While staying at the Royal Clarence Hotel, Erskine encountered Helen again.

Gwendraith notes that Helen left Dillworth suddenly one summer night, leading many to believe she eloped with Erskine. Erskine denies this, stating that his last encounter with Helen was on a beach around nine o'clock the evening before he and his wife departed. During that walk, Helen told him to leave immediately. Erskine speculates that Helen ran away to avoid him because she realized he still loved her.

When Gwendraith asks if Helen was afraid of her husband, Erskine recalls that while Helen's husband seemed cheerful, Helen had appeared worried. Erskine suggests that jealousy can be a terrifying, hidden force. The conversation is interrupted by the arrival of Mrs. Erskine. Erskine's demeanor becomes rigid and tense. Gwendraith informs Mrs. Erskine that she found a ring Mrs. Reed had dropped in the garden. As Gwendraith departs, Colonel Eustace walks her to her car while Mrs. Erskine calls out to Richard regarding an important telephone call.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolen (Gwendy) approaches the Erskine residence and overhears a heated argument through the French windows between Mrs. Janet Erskine and Colonel Erskine. Janet accuses the Colonel of infidelity, specifically mentioning a blonde woman named Harriet from Dilworth. In a state of distress, Janet threatens to commit suicide by jumping off a cliff. Gwendy retreats to the driveway, requests a servant to move Mrs. Erskine's car, and returns to her hotel to meet Jiles. Gwendy informs Jiles that the Colonel was not the man who ran away with Helen and that he claims Helen was still alive when he left her that night.

Later, Miss Marple is weeding the garden when Dr. Kennedy arrives to see Mrs. Coke. Miss Marple introduces herself, noting that she is helping Gwendolen with the gardening. Dr. Kennedy, appearing anxious and worn out, reveals that Charles Reed has asked him for letters written by his sister, Helen, to determine if they are forgeries. Dr. Kennedy admits his previous certainty regarding Kelvin Harley's guilt is wavering; while he once believed Harley strangled Helen and staged her disappearance with a suitcase and letters, he now finds it hard to believe Helen would remain silent for years if she were alive, yet finds it equally strange that no one reported her death if she were dead. He leaves a sample of Helen's handwriting—a plant order—and a second letter for the Reeds to examine. Dr. Kennedy expresses fear that if Harley is a murderer, he is a cold-blooded, premeditated killer. Miss Marple concludes the encounter by asking Dr. Kennedy if Helen was afraid of anyone, specifically mentioning a young man named Avril.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Marple questions Dr. Kennedy regarding a young man who previously dated Helen. Dr. Kennedy dismisses the idea of a romantic connection, stating the man was merely an ordinary employee at the Fein and Wachtel Law Firm who left after an indiscretion involving revealing his employer Mr. Walter Fein's affairs. Miss Marple remains puzzled by this information.

Elsewhere, Lily Gowan discusses a newspaper advertisement for a maid position at Dulcote St. Catherine's Cottage with her husband, Jim Gibb. Lily suspects the ad might be linked to a potential inheritance from Mrs. Harley or a reward for information regarding a murderer. She recalls details involving a woman named Lorraine and a man's car. Jim, growing angry and bored, warns Lily not to meddle in such matters and leaves. Driven by greed and a desire for luxury items, Lily decides to write to Dr. Harry, the brother of Lady Hildy, to seek advice, though she hesitates to mention details about Leonnie or a car.

The following morning, Miss Marple visits Jiles and Gwendolyn. She explains she has hired an additional gardener, an old man named Manning, to assist Mr. Foster. Miss Marple reveals that Manning previously worked for Dr. Kennedy and identifies the young man who dated Helen as Avery. Jiles notes he has obtained a handwriting sample of Helen's from Dr. Kennedy to send to an expert. The group meets Manning, a shrewd, grumpy hunchback, in the garden. As Manning discusses the history of local gardens and former residents like Mrs. Findlay, Jiles asks him if he indeed used to work for Dr. Kennedy.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a conversation with Jiles and Gwendolen, an elderly man named Manning recalls the history of Dr. Brent, who now lives in Crossby House. Manning discusses Miss Helen Kennedy, the sister of Dr. Kennedy, who returned to the area after marrying an officer from India. Manning recounts a malicious incident where someone cut the tennis net at the house into pieces. Following this, Miss Helen suffered an injury when she tripped on a rake in the middle of a dark driveway; the resulting wound on her foot would not heal, preventing her from dancing.

Jiles inquires about Jackie Averycliffe, a former employee of the law firm Feen and Worchman, whom Manning describes as a "low-class character" and a friend of Miss Helen. Manning denies that Averycliffe was responsible for the net, attributing the destruction to sheer stupidity or venting. Manning also mentions Walter Fein, a man who was devoted to Miss Helen but was not loved by her; Fein eventually went abroad and returned to become the head of his firm.

Later, Miss Marple joins Gwendolen and Jiles. Miss Marple reflects on the nature of unrequited love and jealousy, using the case of Mrs. Erskine and her husband to illustrate how unreturned affection can lead to mutual suffering. She cautions that feelings are not always reliable indicators of guilt, noting that people often act contrary to their established characters due to deep-seated motivations or blood ties. Miss Marple emphasizes the importance of the crime scene and suggests that finding the address of J.J. Avery, the owner of the Water Lily Carriage Company, would be a straightforward task.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jiles Reed and Gwendolyn prepare to visit J.J. Avery, the owner of the Water Lily Carriage Company, after Miss Marple warns them that he is a shrewd man. While preparing to leave for Exeter, Jiles receives a call from Dr. Kennedy regarding a strange letter from a woman named Lily Kimball. Dr. Kennedy explains that Lily, a former maid in his house, sent a letter containing a clipping and expressing doubts about a past event involving a woman named Mrs. Harriet. Lily mentions that she saw a fancy car outside and suspects Mr. Kimball might be involved, but she fears police involvement. Dr. Kennedy arranges for Lily to visit on a Thursday so they can all speak together.

During the conversation, Dr. Kennedy reveals that he has heard the name Avery before. He recalls that Jackie Avery was once fired from a firm for attempting to steal confidential information to sell for money. Kennedy notes that Avery showed signs of persecution delusion and emotional instability following his dismissal.

Jiles and Gwendolyn proceed to meet J.J. Avery at his luxurious new home. Avery is described as a plump, middle-aged man with shrewd black eyes. After Jiles discusses a potential business trip for a boys' club, Avery questions why they wish to meet privately. Jiles explains that Gwendolyn wants to reconnect with her stepmother, Helen Harley (formerly Helen Kennedy). Upon hearing the name, Avery reacts with sudden excitement, claiming to remember "Little Miss Helen Kennedy" from eighteen years ago, though he states he has had no news of her since.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn and Gwendraith visit Jackie Avelick to seek information regarding Helen, who fled Dartmouth eighteen years ago. Gwendolyn reveals that they suspect Avelick may have had feelings for Helen. Avelick clarifies that their relationship was merely a youthful flirtation and that he was not brave enough to pursue her seriously. He explains that Helen’s brother, a rigid doctor, prevented them from seeing each other. Avelick admits he visited Helen two or three times in Dartmouth to check on her, but he refuses to help the group further. During the conversation, Avelick reveals he was previously fired from the Fein and Wachman Law Firm due to a setup by an enemy, though he does not name the individual.

After the group departs, Gwendraith briefly returns to the house to retrieve a forgotten shawl, witnessing a sharp exchange between Avelick and his wife, Dorothy. As Gwendolyn and Jules drive away, Gwendolyn connects Avelick’s presence to a previous mystery involving a "mysterious man in a luxury car" mentioned by Edith Pargette and Lily Kimball. She suspects Avelick was at the scene of a crime on a specific night. Their realization is interrupted when Avelick approaches them from behind a hedge, identifying his eye-catching yellow luxury limousine as "Little Goldfinch."

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a brief encounter with a pleasant man named Mr. Avery, Gwendolyn and Jiles express unease, wondering if he overheard them referring to his yellow car as the "Yellow Peril." Later, Jiles receives a report from a handwriting expert which confirms that a letter sent from abroad was indeed written by Helen. This discovery confuses Gwendolyn and Jiles, as it suggests Helen left the house and wrote the letter, contradicting previous theories regarding her death. Miss Marple suggests that the handwriting analysis is significant because someone made a mistake, implying the letter's authenticity narrows the list of suspects.

Gwendolyn, Jiles, and Miss Marple travel to Dr. Kennedy’s house to meet Lily Kimball. Dr. Kennedy, appearing pale and nervous, explains he sent his housekeeper out to prepare for Lily's arrival. He expresses confusion over the case, questioning why Kelvin insisted he killed Helen if the letters were not forgeries. Gwendolyn posits that Helen might be responsible for her own situation. While waiting for Lily, Dr. Kennedy looks at an old photograph of himself with a student girl, appearing accusatory toward the group.

Meanwhile, Lily Kimball travels by train, disembarking at Mucking's Siding. As she ascends a steep path toward Woodrey Camp, a figure emerges from the woods and startles her. Following a brief period of implied struggle in the secluded woods, a wood pigeon flies away. Back at the doctor's house, Lily fails to arrive. Dr. Kennedy expresses frustration and concern, noting that she should have arrived by now given the directions he provided.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Kennedy, Giles, and Gwendolyn initially believe that a woman expected for a 4:35 arrival has simply changed her mind or missed her train. After Kennedy calls the train station and confirms no such passenger is arriving, Officer Rast of the Longford Police Department calls to report that the expected woman, Lily Kimball, has been found dead.

Officer Rast informs Kennedy that Lily was strangled and her body was discovered by a hiker in a wooded area near Midgehams Junction. Although Kennedy had sent Lily a letter providing directions to his home via a specific train route, Rast reveals that Lily arrived much earlier than expected. She departed Cumbria at 1:30 PM and alighted at Midgehams Junction, the station before Woodruff, rather than following Kennedy's instructions.

During an inquiry at the police department, Kennedy, Giles, and Gwendolyn recount a history involving Mrs. Harryd, Kennedy's sister and the stepmother of Giles and Gwendolyn. Eighteen years prior, Mrs. Harryd disappeared from the family home, an event previously attributed to elopement. Lily Kimball, formerly Lily Abbot, had been the household's housekeeper at that time. Lily believed a murder had occurred in the house, a suspicion that persisted despite Colonel Harryd dying in a mental institution under the delusion that he had killed his wife.

Officer Rast suggests Lily may have been attempting to meet someone else or was involved in extortion. Lily's husband, Mr. Kimball, provides testimony stating that Lily was obsessed with crime stories and frequently claimed the husband had killed the wife and hidden the body. He dismissed her theories as imagination. Following the investigation, a terrified Gwendolyn returns home to be cared for by Jules under Kennedy's instructions.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn is distressed by the realization that a woman was murdered eighteen years ago. While Jules attempts to comfort her, Miss Marple arrives at the manor and joins Gwendolyn, Mrs. Coleridge, and Jules. Miss Marple asserts that recent events unequivocally confirm that Helen Harley was murdered at a young age.

Miss Marple proposes that Helen's body is not in the basement, as Edith Pettigrew previously claimed, but is instead buried at the end of the terrace. She explains that the relocation of the garden steps to the end of the terrace provided a pretext for digging, which would avoid suspicion from the gardener. Miss Marple notes that Lily Kimball’s letter mentioned a Swiss girl named Leonie, who saw something suspicious from the first-floor children's room window—likely the digging of a pit—but Leonie never reported it to the police.

Jules reconstructs the crime, suggesting the killer strangled Helen in the hall, moved her to a bed, and waited for Kelvin Harley to pass out from drugged whiskey before taking the body to the terrace to bury it. This implies the killer remained lurking near the house all night. Miss Marple advises that they must not trust anyone's word and should instead rely on confirmed facts. Jules begins to re-examine the suspects, specifically questioning the testimony of Erskine. While Gwendolyn believes Erskine is ruled out, Jules suggests that his claim of a mutual parting from Helen might be a lie to hide a motive of obsessive love and potential threats.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jules, Gwendolyn, and Miss Marple discuss three potential suspects in the murder of Helen Haridy. 

The first suspect is Erskine. Jules reconstructs a theory where Erskine, who had an attractive quality toward women, met Helen on the beach. He may have insisted she run away with him, but when she refused, he strangled her in a rage. Jules suggests Erskine then buried the body and attempted to frame Kelvin Haridy. This theory accounts for the fact that Helen had expressed fear of a man and that Erskine admitted to seeing her again.

The second suspect is Walter Feen. Gwendolyn describes Feen as a repressed, obsessive man who was deeply hurt when Helen abandoned him in India to marry Kelvin Haridy. Although he appeared gentle, he had a history of violent rage as a child. Helen had reportedly told him, "I've always been afraid of you," and planned to move to Norfolk to escape him. Jules notes that Feen lived within walking distance of the scene and suggests he might have killed her if she changed her mind about running away with him, potentially to avoid a scandal.

The third suspect is Jackie Averycliff. Gwendolyn notes that Averycliff suffered from paranoia and held a grudge against Helen after a failed fling. He blamed Feen for losing his job. Jules posits that Averycliff may have been Helen's secret lover. This theory is supported by Lily Kimball’s letter mentioning a fancy car parked outside that night, which was identified as Averycliff's. Jules suggests that if Helen decided not to run away with him, Averycliff might have killed her and taken her suitcase and letters to make it appear she had fled abroad.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn and Jules Reed discuss the potential motives regarding Jackie Avery and letters that may have caused Helen to pretend she had gone abroad. Mrs. Cochran informs them that Mr. Fenn visited earlier that afternoon, claiming Gwendolyn was expecting him, as was a second man in a large yellow car. Jules calls Fenn, who claims he received an urgent call from the office regarding a large sum of money, but denies being the caller. Jules then calls Avery, who reveals that a man impersonating Jules called him to request an urgent meeting regarding money. Gwendolyn suggests that either Fenn or Jack Afrik is attempting to create a false alibi or frame the other. Jules suspects Fenn, but Miss Marple suggests another possibility.

Jules calls Colonel Erskine in Northumberland to verify his whereabouts. Gwendolyn uses a pretext about the Linscott Blake Mansion to engage him in conversation. Erskine reveals he is at home performing housework because his wife is visiting her sister and the maid is away. Gwendolyn concludes that Erskine is ruled out as a suspect for Lily Greenbank's murder, though Miss Marple remains grave and worried.

Later, Gwendolyn reflects on the chaotic morning. Detective Inspector Primer and Inspector Premerle arrive to investigate the murder of Lily Kimber. Jules directs the inspectors to the terrace and the nursery. Jules then encourages Gwendolyn to share her private observations with Premerle. Gwendolyn describes her inexplicable sense of familiarity with the house, claiming she remembers the wallpaper and layout from her own childhood.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn Reed makes a statement to Inspector Primer, claiming she saw a dead woman with golden hair and a purple face in a hillside villa hallway, identifying her as Helen. Gwendolyn suggests that someone framed her father for the murder of Lily Kimber, noting that Dr. Penrose and Dr. Kennedy believed her father was incapable of such an act. After Jules Reed is sent to the garden, Gwendolyn shares her speculations with the Inspector, including how Walter Fane and J.J. Avery received impersonated calls from Jules yesterday afternoon. Inspector Primer notes that Lily Kimber was murdered between 2:20 and 2:45 PM and emphasizes the need for verification rather than speculation.

In the garden, Inspector Primer meets Miss Marple, who is gardening. He acknowledges her reputation and her involvement in the case, specifically regarding the location of Mrs. Harriet's body. Miss Marple compares the deep roots of bindweed to the long-standing nature of the murder case. A policeman then announces that they have found a body.

As the discovery is made, Jules returns pale to inform Gwendolyn. Mrs. Coker comes into the garden and, upon seeing the remains, suffers a severe physical reaction. Gwendolyn provides her with brandy, but Mrs. Coker becomes unresponsive with terror and is eventually taken to the hospital. A forensic pathologist notes that Mrs. Coker might have died without medical intervention. Inspector Primer questions Gwendolyn about her recent consumption of alcohol, and she explains that she and Jules had been drinking whiskey instead of brandy. Following the police departure, Jervis eats lunch and joins the others, leaving Gwendolyn alone in the house.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn reflects on the murder of Lily Kimball, suspecting that either Walter Fane or Jackie Afflick tampered with the brandy and used a prank phone call to implicate the other. Gwendolyn considers Walter Fane, a quiet man who may have been driven to violence by past humiliations involving a woman named Helen. She then shifts her suspicion to Afflick, theorizing that his arrogant demeanor masks a cruel nature and an inferiority complex. She believes Afflick killed Lily because she threatened him and now intends to kill Gwendolyn and Jervis to protect his secrets, potentially framing Walter Fane in the process.

While contemplating whether Richard Erskine’s wife might be involved due to a suspicious man's voice on the phone, Gwendolyn is interrupted by Dr. Kennedy entering the house. In a moment of intense psychological terror, Gwendolyn hallucinates or perceives Dr. Kennedy as the killer, accusing him of murdering Helen and Lily. He approaches her, claiming that her interference has brought back the memory of Helen and that he must kill Gwendolyn just as he killed Helen. As he reaches for her throat, Gwendolyn is paralyzed by fear in the empty house. The tension is broken when Miss Marple runs up the stairs and accidentally sprays soap water into Dr. Kennedy's eyes while attempting to spray aphids on his roses.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
On the terrace of the Imperial Hotel in Torquay, Miss Marple explains the truth behind the past crimes to Gwendolyn, Jules, and Giles. She reveals that Dr. Kennedy was a madman driven by a sinister, possessive love for his half-sister, Helen. After Helen married Kelvin Harleywood in London to escape her brother's strictness, Dr. Kennedy pretended to accept the marriage. However, when Kelvin informed his brother of the marriage, Kennedy went insane. He strangled Helen in the hallway of St. Catherine's Villa and then executed a wicked plan to frame Kelvin. Kennedy manipulated Kelvin into believing he had killed his own wife and had suffered a mental breakdown, eventually convincing him to enter a mental institution. To maintain this deception, Kennedy forged Helen’s handwriting in letters and attempted to poison Gwendolyn with brandy.

Miss Marple further explains that Kennedy murdered Lily Kimball to protect himself. He lured Lily to a meeting at the crossroads, strangled her, and replaced her original letter with a fake one to make it appear as though she were blackmailing him. This murder was triggered because a girl named Leonie had seen Kennedy digging a hole in the garden and had told Lily. Kennedy had also attempted to frame others, such as Jackie Averycliff, by manipulating perceptions of a car seen at the scene. Ultimately, Kennedy confessed his crimes to the police before dying. Gwendolyn reflects on the sinister atmosphere of the house and the tragic death of Helen, who died in her prime.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Anthony Ked, a quick-witted tour guide for the Jazzy High-End Tour Group, meets an old friend, Jimmy McGregor, while managing a group of tourists in Bravaio. The group, consisting of seven women and three men, is currently bored and waiting for a drive to Matopos. During their brief interaction, Jimmy remarks on Anthony's tendency to find trouble and his perceived lack of fear regarding death. Anthony later manages the group's needs, which include consoling an irate gentleman, assisting an elderly woman with postcards, and flirting with Miss Taylor, a young and frivolous tourist. While leading the group to a café, Anthony tells Miss Taylor about their first meeting in Africa seven years prior, claiming they rescued Jimmy from being eaten by a tribe.

That evening, Anthony visits Jimmy in his room. Anthony expresses dissatisfaction with his current job and asks Jimmy for employment opportunities. Jimmy offers Anthony a job located in Britain, though he explains he is declining it because he intends to travel to the interior of the country to mine for gold. Jimmy mentions a connection to Heslovsky, prompting Anthony to describe his knowledge of the Balkan Peninsula nation. Anthony notes that Heslovsky is a place of frequent riots and revolutions, following the assassination of King Nicholas IV seven years ago, which transformed the country into a republic.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jimmy (James McGregor) informs Anthony of a mysterious package he received containing the memoirs of Count Stapfitch, the former Prime Minister of Hesoslovakia and a powerful political figure in the Balkan Peninsula. Jimmy recounts that four years ago in Paris, he rescued the Count from a group of thugs. Following the assassination of King Nikolai IV—whose reign ended in a republic after a popular uprising against his marriage to a commoner, Empress Farajia—the Count had settled in Paris. 

Two weeks ago, Jimmy received the manuscript along with a letter promising £1,000 if it is delivered to a London publisher by October 13th. Because Jimmy has already booked a trip to Cape Town to travel through the interior of Africa, he cannot complete the delivery. Anthony agrees to take the job for a quarter of the reward (£100), and the two men agree that Anthony will travel on the ship *Granada* using Jimmy's name.

As they finalize their plans, Anthony expresses skepticism regarding the logistics of the delivery, questioning why the Count's estate would send a manuscript from France through Africa to England rather than mailing it directly. Anthony muses that the publication of such biographies often causes trouble, suggesting that the deceased Count may have intended to stir up controversy from beyond the grave.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jimmy and Anthony discuss the mysterious circumstances surrounding Count Stapleton. Jimmy reveals that while intoxicated, Stapleton claimed to know the location of the Light Mountain Diamond. Anthony notes that the diamond is famously secured in the Tower of London. Jimmy further discloses that Stapleton mentioned the thugs who attacked him were from "Viktor's court." Anthony identifies Victor King not as a royal, but as a notorious jewel thief and con artist who used that name as a codename in Paris. Anthony suspects a connection between the criminal underworld, the potential restoration of the Heslovich monarchy, and the discovery of oil in Hessia. Anthony expresses an intention to travel to Heslovia to invest in oil mines and potentially pursue political influence.

Jimmy then asks Anthony for a favor regarding a package he received from a man named Pedro, a Hessian whom Jimmy had rescued from a river in Uganda. Before dying of a fever, Pedro gave Jimmy an oilcloth package containing letters from a British woman who was blackmailing another woman. Jimmy wants to return these letters to the intended recipient to prevent further fear, but the only identifying information is the name "Chimney Cottage." Anthony recognizes Chimney Cottage as a prestigious British mansion frequented by royalty and diplomats. Jimmy requests that Anthony travel to England to deliver the letters personally, fearing that mailing them might be dangerous or that the recipient might already be dead. Anthony agrees to assist.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jimmy and Anthony examine a packet of letters to find clues regarding the whereabouts of a woman named Virginia Ruff, who may have stayed at the Chimney House. Despite their efforts, the letters yield no information. Jimmy expresses a deep concern for her, and Anthony promises to remain in Britain if he cannot find her.

Edward Alastair Brent, the ninth Earl of Carrington, is a man who feels overshadowed by his brother Henry’s prestigious legacy and the social prominence of their family estate, Smokestack Villa. During a conversation, George Romax (also referred to as Roxas or Roxane) pressures the Earl to avoid political scandals. Romax explains that they must secure oil drilling rights in Hesslovakia for a British company. To provide cover for these dealings, Prince Michael O'Paulwich will host a hunting party at the Chimney House. Romax intends to visit a nearby convent rather than attend the banquet and suggests inviting Bill Eversley to help pass on messages. He also notes the need for politically clueless outsiders to attend the event to ensure it appears legitimate.

The conversation turns to the potential danger of Staunton's memoirs being published, which could expose the vices of the House of Paulovich and jeopardize the political restoration in Hesslovakia. Romax further warns of a past "disappearance incident" that occurred at the Chimney Villa, which nearly ruined Henry's career. While the Earl is intrigued by the rumors of this unsolved mystery, Romax expresses intense anxiety that the manuscript of the memoirs might be sent to London, potentially triggering a massive scandal.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Lord Runciman informs the Marquess of Carrington that a memoir was secretly moved weeks before a man's death. The manuscripts were sent to James McGregor, a Canadian currently living in Africa, who is scheduled to arrive on the *Grangewood* this Thursday. Runciman intends to meet McGregor to request a one-month delay in the memoir's publication and to carefully edit the text. The Earl of Carringham opposes inviting McGregor to the Chimney House due to a personal dislike of Canadians living in Africa. 

Roxane suggests employing a clever, attractive woman to handle McGregor to avoid angering him. After dismissing several candidates, Runciman proposes his cousin, Virginia Rue, noting her personal charm and her familiarity with Hesse-Slav due to her husband's embassy work. The Earl of Carringham agrees, finding the prospect of seeing Virginia Ruef appealing.

Later, at Whitehall, George Roxton (referred to as George Romax) asks his assistant, Bill Everleigh, to check Mrs. Rue's telephone number at 487 Pound Street. Upon learning her phone line is out, Roxton instructs Bill to take a taxi to her home to ask if she will be available at four o'clock that afternoon for an important discussion. Bill arrives at the residence and meets Virginia Ruef, a charming twenty-seven-year-old woman. Virginia, who had lied to Bill about her phone being broken to facilitate a visit, mentions she is preparing to go to Sloane Street to purchase a new red rubber garter belt.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Virginia and Bill discuss George Romilly’s request to meet her at 4:00 pm to discuss something important. Virginia, who plans to go to Larnaca, decides to cancel her plans at Rainerch to meet him. During their conversation, Bill expresses his love for Virginia, but she maintains her independence, stating she enjoys being a "bad widow" and does not wish to marry. Bill expresses dejection, mentioning a recent dinner with Dorothy Kirkpatrick.

The narrative shifts to explain that George Romilly’s plans were compromised by a mistake made by Bill. Bill, an amiable employee at the Foreign Office who serves as George's errand runner, was sent to the Whitehall Shipping Company to inquire about the arrival of the *Granaanburgh*. Due to Bill's poor pronunciation, the clerk misheard the ship's name as the *Carnfrae* and reported it would arrive the following Thursday. Consequently, George was unaware that the *Granaanburgh* had actually docked at Southampton on Monday afternoon.

At 2:00 pm, a traveler using the name Jimmy Romilly (later identified as Anthony) arrives at the Britz Hotel via taxi from Waterloo Station. While walking near Blackfriars Bridge, Anthony is bumped into by a muscular, tanned man who stares at him intently. Upon returning to his room, Anthony observes that he has transformed from an innocent-looking boy into a gaunt, tanned man. He receives a call from the hotel clerk stating that a foreigner wishes to see him. A waiter delivers a business card for Baron Lothair Rottschläger. The Baron, described as a tall man with a fan-shaped beard, arrives and informs Anthony that he is in London as a representative of the Hesslovian royal court on serious business.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The Baron informs Anthony McGrath that restoration efforts have resumed following the death of Nicholas IV and that they support Prince Michael's ascension to the throne, a position backed by the British government. The Baron warns Anthony that his presence is problematic because the memoirs of the late Count Stapleton contain secrets that could cause European chaos and spread gossip regarding the Paulvich family. The Baron reveals that a weekly newspaper is prepared to publish the memoirs immediately upon their arrival. Despite the Baron offering Anthony increasing sums of money—reaching two thousand pounds—to abandon his task, Anthony refuses, citing his honor and his commitment to the assignment.

Following the Baron's departure, Anthony searches for a woman named Rueff. He discovers in a magazine that Mrs. Timothy Rue is actually Miss Virginia Gaussland, formerly Miss Rue. As Anthony prepares a package of letters, a menacing stranger enters his room and demands Stapleton's memoirs. The man identifies himself as a member of the Red Hand Party, presenting a paper marked with a red hand symbol. When Anthony refuses to hand over the manuscript, the stranger threatens to shoot him. Anthony reacts by stepping forward and knocking the gun out of the man's hand.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After defeating a Red Hand Party member, Anthony retreats to his room to read a memoir belonging to an old count. He orders dinner, observing that the waiter, an Italian man named Gisippo, appears intensely interested in the manuscripts on his table. Anthony finds the memoir's handwriting difficult to read and, after finishing the fourth chapter, decides to sleep. He locks his letters and the memoir's packaging in a suitcase, places a chair and a water bottle against his door as a defense, and hides the memoir under his pillow.

Four hours later, Anthony is awakened by a shadow near his window. Gisippo enters through the window and attacks Anthony with a long knife. During a struggle, Gisippo chokes Anthony, but the knife falls to the ground. Anthony attempts to block the door, but realizes Gisippo entered via the window; the intruder escapes by jumping to a neighboring balcony. While Anthony manages to retrieve the memoir from under his pillow, he discovers that Gisippo has stolen the letters from his suitcase.

Elsewhere, Virginia Rue returns to her residence on Pound Street. Her butler, Chifley, informs her that a foreign man is in the study claiming to have important information regarding her late husband, Major Timothy Rue. Virginia meets the man, a tall foreigner of flexible build, in the study. After a tense silence, the man presents Virginia with a letter, prompting her to examine whom it is addressed to.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
An unnamed man visits Virginia Rue, presenting a private letter addressed to Captain O'Neil at 15 Paris Fish Circle. Although the letter is signed "Virginia Ruff," Virginia informs the man that while it is her name, it is not her handwriting. The man reveals he found her via a newspaper clipping and claims to possess other letters—love letters written by a woman expressing fear of her husband being discovered. He demands a thousand pounds in exchange for the letters, which he characterizes as a business transaction rather than extortion. Virginia refuses the full amount but gives him forty pounds, instructing him to return the following day at six o'clock.

After the man leaves, Virginia explains the situation to George Romax, who has arrived at the house. George is horrified that Virginia is allowing herself to be blackmailed, but Virginia remains composed and amused. She explains that she is not the woman who wrote the letters and views the encounter as a "game and counter-game," believing she has effectively protected the true author by misleading the extortionist. 

Following this discussion, George asks Virginia for a favor. He explains that a Canadian man named McGregor is arriving in England the next day. George requests that Virginia meet McGregor to demonstrate the "true charm and elegance of a British lady," as the man has not previously interacted with the British upper class.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Virginia confronts George about his motive for asking her to pose as a genuine British lady for Canadian visitors. George claims his goal is to influence Mr. McGregor regarding the necessity of a monarchist restoration in Hesse to ensure European peace, specifically proposing Prince Michael O'Paulwich as the king. Virginia dismisses his explanation as a terrible lie, noting that the media is already saturated with rumors regarding the O'Paulwich dynasty and the late Nicolas IV. George, realizing Virginia is more inquisitive and less docile than he anticipated, attempts to withdraw from the arrangement. However, Virginia informs him that she intends to attend a gathering at the Chimney House over the weekend to investigate the "McGrath mystery." After George leaves, Virginia calls Miss Elinor Brent to confirm her attendance at the Chimney House.

Meanwhile, at the Bretton Hotel, Anthony McGlass deals with the disappearance of important letters. He believes a waiter named Giuseppe, possibly working for the "Red Hand League" or Royalists, stole them after mistaking them for a memoir. Anthony meets with the hotel manager to report the incident. Although Anthony initially claims only trivial items were taken to avoid a scene, he eventually admits to the manager that the stolen items are valuable letters. The manager, wishing to avoid a media scandal, agrees not to involve the police. Anthony requests information on Giuseppe, and the manager later provides details, noting that Giuseppe is a skilled waiter who has been in Britain for five years.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After investigating Giuseppe’s employment history, Anthony discovers that the Italian had been present during major thefts at two of his former hotels. Anthony suspects Giuseppe was not merely a thief but was sent by someone, noting that Giuseppe’s intense reaction to a letter on the table suggested a specific motive beyond simple theft. Despite Anthony's efforts to find Giuseppe through employee inquiries and newspaper advertisements, he finds no leads.

Anthony receives a phone call from Mr. Pottsford of Puddington Haggis Publishing. Pottsford warns that the manuscript Anthony possesses is being targeted by a dangerous group using bribery and threats. To ensure safety, Pottsford arranges for a company representative, Mr. Holmes, to collect the manuscript from Anthony's hotel and provide a £1,000 check. Following Pottsford's advice, Anthony places a fake manuscript in the hotel safe to mislead potential enemies. The following morning, Anthony hands the manuscript to Mr. Holmes and receives the payment.

After checking out of the hotel, Anthony receives a cryptic, formal letter regarding the memoirs of the Earl of Stapleton. The document requests that he consult with Mr. George Romax and invites him to visit the Chimney House as a guest of the Earl of Chatham on Friday. To protect his identity, Anthony checks into a secluded inn under the alias Anthony Kidd. He writes a letter to the Earl of Carringham, signed "Jimmy McGregor," stating he has already delivered the manuscript to the publishers and is leaving Britain. Meanwhile, Virginia Rue prepares to encounter a blackmailer by changing her persona from a simple-minded fool to someone more alert.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After arriving at her house, Virginia chats with her driver, Walton, about his wife's health before realizing she has forgotten her key. While waiting at the door, she encounters a young man selling brochures titled "Why Should I Serve My Country?" and a collection box. When her maid, Alice, opens the door, Virginia discovers that Chifford and the other servants have left for the Datchet villa following a telegram. Virginia denies sending any such message. Alice produces the telegram, which instructs Chifford to take the servants to the villa for a weekend gathering via the 5:49 train. Alice suggests the telegram might be a fraudulent attempt by criminals to rob the house. Virginia instructs Alice to continue packing her gowns for the Chimney Villa and plans to contact the police.

Seeking the telephone in the small study, Virginia finds the young man from the doorstep dead in a chair. He has a small hole in his heart, and a small gun lies on the floor. To prevent Alice from becoming hysterical, Virginia instructs her to secure the front door with a chain. Alone in the study, Virginia panics, wondering if the fake telegram and the murder are connected, and fears how the discovery might implicate her in past troubles involving George and certain letters. She attempts to call Bill for advice, but learns he left for the Chimney Villa thirty minutes prior. As Virginia stands alone with the body, the doorbell rings. She unlatches the safety chain and opens the door to find the young man from the entrance standing there.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Virginia Rue hires a young man named Anthony Kade, an Eton and Oxford graduate, to assist her with a difficult situation. Virginia reveals that a man was found shot dead in her study. She explains that the man had been blackmailing her with forged love letters bearing her signature. Upon returning home from Ranelagh, Virginia found a fake telegram and discovered that her servants had been sent away, leaving only one French maid, Alice.

Anthony and Virginia examine the body, which Anthony identifies as an Italian man he suspects is a waiter named Giseppe. Anthony notes the body is still warm, suggesting the murder occurred less than half an hour prior. He observes that because Virginia has already spoken to the maid, the timeline of the murder is complicated, as someone could claim the death occurred after Virginia's arrival.

The investigation takes a turn when Anthony examines a telegram and questions Virginia's whereabouts. He then discovers a gun on the floor. When he asks Virginia if she has ever owned a weapon, she denies it, but Anthony finds a delicate, toy-like gun with the name "Virginia" engraved on the grip. Anthony proposes two theories: either the real author of the letters killed Giseppe and fled, or someone is attempting to frame Virginia by utilizing her personal details and routines. Anthony suggests two courses of action: reporting the crime and relying on her reputation, or attempting to dispose of the body to cover up the crime. He ultimately advises a modified version of the first option, suggesting they report the death but withhold information regarding the gun and the blackmail letters.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After searching a dead man's pockets, Anthony discovers a scrap of paper containing the text: "Eleven forty-five, Thursday, Chimney House." Virginia Rue notes that she was scheduled to visit Chimney House that night. She mentions that her cousin, George Romex, does not want her to go, though she doubts he would commit murder. Anthony suggests that Virginia proceed with her plans to confuse potential conspirators and offers to handle the situation.

To facilitate the plan, Virginia sends her maid, Alice, away on a false errand. Anthony selects a large suitcase from the basement to dispose of the body. He instructs Virginia to take her luggage and the suitcase to Paddington Station, where she is to drop a luggage ticket for him to retrieve. Virginia expresses gratitude, and when Anthony mentions his friend Jimmy McGregor, Virginia reacts with surprise, suggesting she knows him.

Anthony executes the plan by retrieving the suitcase, placing Giuseppe's body in it, and driving to the outskirts of London. He obscures his car's license plate with mud, leaves the body by a roadside bend, and hides a gun in a tree crevice. He then stores the suitcase in a cloakroom at Paddington Station before driving to Chimney House.

Arriving at Chimney House at 11:30 PM, Anthony scales the wall. At 11:45 PM, he hears a gunshot coming from inside the house. After investigating the locked windows, he sees a light flicker briefly in a second-floor room before the building returns to darkness. The following morning, Inspector Bagshaw receives a phone call from "Your Lordship" that leaves him visibly affected.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The Bagginswood Inspector informs Johnson of a murder at Chimney House involving a foreign guest. The inspector notes that an open window and external footprints were discovered at the scene. Seeking to advance his career and avoid Metropolitan Police interference, the inspector enlists Dr. Carter-Laithe to assist. As they travel to the manor, they pass "The Happy Cricketer" inn, where the doctor observes a mysterious stranger. Johnson provides a list of recent arrivals via train, including Lady Elinor, an American, a young soldier, Mr. Everleigh, Mrs. Ruff, and a bald, foreign-looking man. The inspector instructs Johnson to investigate strangers at "The Happy Wanderer" upon their return.

Upon arriving at the historic Chimney House, the group is met by a butler and led to Sir Carterham, the Earl of Carrington, who is visibly distressed. The Earl explains that a maid discovered the body in the banquet hall. When questioned about the victim's identity, the Earl appears unsettled before identifying him as Stanislaus Count. Lady Elinor Brent, the Earl's daughter, arrives to announce that George Romax is on his way.

In the banquet hall, Dr. Carter-Laithe examines the body of the man lying on the floor. The inspector observes that while there are footprints on the outside steps leading to the middle window, there are no corresponding prints on the hardwood floor inside. Lady Elinor explains that the maid had already partially cleaned the floor before discovering the body. Sir Carterham expresses a desire to leave the investigation to George Romax, who is expected to arrive from Weefen Abbey.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The Earl of Carringham, feeling anxious and complaining about Romax, joins Bunder and Bondell for breakfast in the dining room. The white-haired butler, Tredwell, serves various dishes, but the Earl expresses dissatisfaction with the food and angrily rejects a dish of poached eggs. During the meal, the Earl expresses frustration regarding his social status and his involvement in a current "mess," mentioning that Dr. Abner Willis has advised him not to worry. The group discusses the recent death of an individual, with the Earl suspecting Esarhaddon, a representative of the Anglo-American Bank Group, might be responsible.

Mr. Kodges arrives at the villa in a speeding car and enters the dining room in a state of distress, calling the situation a national disaster and claiming that backup plans have been sabotaged. He insists that the matter be kept secret. George (Romax) suggests they need Inspector Batter from Scotland Yard rather than the local police. George writes a telegram to Lord Lolopryte Jeechill and instructs the Earl of Carterham to give him full control of the situation. The Earl of Carterham agrees, entrusting the chimney villa to George before slipping away.

Later, the Earl of Carringham attempts to avoid George by wandering his estate and sneaking into a secret room through a side door. However, Tredwell intercepts him and informs him that Romax is eager to see him in the study. The Earl reluctantly agrees to meet him.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The Earl of Carringham visits his study, where he finds George Roxburgh, Major McRoss, and a muscular, noble-looking man. Inspector Bartle arrives to investigate the death of "Count Stanislaus," whom Roxburgh reveals is actually Prince Michael of Hesse. The Prince had visited the villa to discuss loan terms with Herman Eschelbacher; the Prince promised oil concessions to Isakov's company in exchange for support of his ascension to the throne, a move favored by the British government.

Inspector Bartle outlines the facts: the Prince was shot with a revolver at approximately 11:45 p.m., a time indicated by his broken watch. No gunshot was heard, and the weapon is missing. Carringham notes that the house contained the Prince, his servant Boris Anschutz, Eschelbacher, an employee named Everly, and an American collector named Hiram Fisk. 

Bartle reveals that footprints near the victim's window match muddy boots belonging to a young man named Anthony Kidd, who checked into the Happy Cricketer Inn at midnight. While Roxburgh calls for Kidd's arrest, Bartle finds it suspicious that Kidd has remained at the inn rather than fleeing. During this discussion, a man calling himself Anthony Kade arrives, claiming to have urgent information regarding the tragedy. The name "Anthony Kade" causes visible surprise among the four men in the room. Kade enters and claims he has come to investigate the rumors of murder.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Anthony Kidd meets with a group of officials, including Chief Inspector Bartlett, Commissioner Mallow, and George Roxanne, to explain his presence at the Earl of Carringham's estate. Anthony admits he broke into the estate at approximately 11:40 the previous night because he heard gunshots. He explains that he came to clear his name after realizing his boots had been taken by a policeman, making him a suspect.

Anthony provides a detailed backstory, claiming he traveled from Bulawayo to England under the alias "Jimmy McGregor" to perform a favor for a friend. He states he checked into the Britz Hotel using this name to deliver a manuscript to a publisher. He claims he received a letter from Mr. Roxanne (whom he refers to as Mr. Roxby or Roxemax) regarding the manuscript and an invitation to a banquet at the Earl's residence. Anthony asserts that he had actually declined the invitation, but decided to visit the estate late at night to check on things due to concerns regarding "Chimney House" and a man named Gisippus. He describes hearing a gunshot while approaching the dark house, which led him to return to an inn for the night.

During the questioning, George Roxanne becomes visibly uncomfortable when Anthony mentions the name Jimmy McGregor. George reveals that the document in question was a letter containing the memoirs of the late Count Stapleton. Finally, George reluctantly discloses that the man shot at the estate, who had been disguised as Count Stanislaus, was actually Prince Michael of Hesse. Chief Inspector Bartle observes Anthony's testimony with apparent satisfaction.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After receiving permission from the Earl of Carringham, Anthony Kidd accompanies Chief Constable Bartlett to the room where the murder occurred. Anthony explains that the gunshot sounded as if it came from the corner room, which he initially attributed to a poacher. Bartlett reveals a discrepancy: while Anthony claims all three windows were locked from the inside, the middle window was found unlatched this morning. Anthony suggests this implies either an inhabitant of the house opened the window to frame an outsider or that he is being falsely accused of lying. Bartlett admits he suspected Anthony due to obvious footprints, but implies he is considering the possibility of an inside job.

Bartlett presents a piece of paper found under the body featuring a pattern associated with the Red Hand Brotherhood. Anthony identifies the pattern but argues that the organization is unlikely to commit murder or blend in as guests. He concludes that the open window and footprints are part of a scheme, though he denies being an informant for the group. Bartlett then leads Anthony to a sitting room to view the corpse. Anthony recognizes the deceased as Prince Michael O'Paulverich, who had previously introduced himself under the alias "Holmes," a representative from Packardson Haggikin Publishers.

Meanwhile, in the Blue Room, Mr. Roxburgh introduces Chief Constable Bartlett to Herman Eschelbacher, a wealthy financier. Roxburgh and Eschelbacher discuss the murder privately, questioning Anthony's innocence. Bartlett states he will investigate Anthony's background in South America but remains cautious. He dismisses the theory that the Red Hand Brotherhood is responsible, asserting that the printed paper was planted as a distraction. Bartlett maintains that the investigation must first determine the motive and identify who stands to gain most from Michael's death.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a discussion regarding the murder of Prince Michael, Chief Balthar and others dismiss the Red Hand Brotherhood as suspects. Batal suggests the investigation has a "mental blind spot" regarding the Hessian royalists and the potential for a new king. Roxanne reveals that Prince Nikolai, Michael's cousin, is the likely heir. Roemex describes Nikolai as an eccentric former Oxford student who recently reappeared in America. Balthazar and Isakov explain that Nikolai’s democratic views could make him a puppet for an American consortium seeking oil concessions, whereas the monarchists support Michael.

George questions why Captain Androidsi did not accompany the Prince, and Balthazar explains the Captain remained in the city to arrange a secret date for Michael. The conversation shifts to Victor King, an infamous French con artist and jewel thief believed to be in Britain. Bartholomew hypothesizes that King may have come to the Chimney House to find the "Diamond of Light Mountain" (referred to as "K") and accidentally killed the Prince, though he notes King has no history of murder.

The group decides to interview Prince Michael's servants. They summon Boris Anchukov, the Prince's personal servant. Upon hearing of the murder, Anchukov reacts with a wild howl and expresses intense grief and self-reproach for potentially being drugged and failing his master. He declares a fierce desire for vengeance, brandishing a large knife and describing a violent plan to mutilate and kill the murderer.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a man puts a knife away and leaves a room, George and Balthar the constable discuss his behavior, with George questioning if the man is truly devoted or merely performing.

Later, Virginia and Anthony walk toward a lake. Anthony recounts his experience handling a body to Virginia. He explains that while Inspector Bates believes he is innocent due to a lack of clear motive, Anthony remains cautious. Anthony questions Virginia about her past in Herschovitz, noting that she once received advances from Prince Michael O'Paulus.
