After Inspector Batteau firmly commands Miss Bondel to return to bed, she climbs back up the ivy to her room. Suspecting the Inspector may be suspicious of her, she remains awake and creeps into the corridor at 2:00 am. She discovers that the sentry post is empty and Jimmy Sycamore is missing from his post. While observing Terry O'Rourke's room, she sees a doorknob turn slowly and then back to its original position. Seeking Bill, Bondel mistakenly enters Lady Razzi's room instead. A commotion breaks out in the library below, involving the sounds of a struggle and two gunshots.

Meanwhile, Loren Wade wakes up at 9:45 pm. She prepares to leave her room, taking a flashlight and an ivory-handled gun purchased from Harrods. After leaving her dog, Rachel, behind, she drives a car to the Two-Footed Dragon Church. Upon arriving at the terrace, she finds a brown paper package that has fallen from an open window. As she sees a person descending the ivy, she flees, hearing voices arguing behind her. She runs into Detective Bartholomew, who leads her toward the library window.

Inside the library, they find Jimmy Cecil lying on the windowsill, appearing to be covered in blood. While Loren screams that he is dead, Detective Bartholomew insists he is merely unconscious from a head injury and a gunshot wound to the arm. As a crowd gathers outside the locked door, the detective and Loren work together to bandage Jimmy's arm with a handkerchief.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a loud knocking at the door, Inspector Batter opens it to a crowd of people rushing into the room. They find Jimmy Cecil lying limp on the ground amidst broken items. Jimmy regains consciousness and explains that he fought a strong thief who attempted to escape through a window by climbing down the ivy. Jimmy reveals he fired his "Leopold" blued-barrel automatic pistol once at the intruder, but the thief managed to fire back at him before escaping.

Sir Stanley Digby discovers that O'Roke was likely drugged and that important documents have been stolen. While George Lomax initially fears the files are lost, Batter reveals that Loraine Wade, the sister of Gerry Wade, has recovered a brown paper package containing the missing items. Sir Stanley and Herr Eberhard inspect the package, confirming its contents.

As George Lomax instructs Bill Everly to call Dr. Carterright to attend to O'Roke, George, Sir Stanley, and Rupert Betman leave the room to take measures. Superintendent Bartlett observes the group and wonders about the whereabouts of Osborne Kutt. The group is interrupted by the arrival of Sir Oswald, who appears at the window. Sir Oswald presents a small Mauser pistol he found on the lawn, suggesting it was dropped by the thief. Batter places the Mauser on the table next to Jimmy's pistol. Sir Oswald requests an explanation of the events, and Detective Bartholomew briefly recounts the situation.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Batters discusses the shooting of Mr. Cecil with Sir Oswald, noting that a man fled the scene after dropping a gun. Bardell explains that they only learned of the flight after Mr. Cecil informed them. Batters reveals he has men guarding the garden to catch anyone attempting to leave, though none have been caught yet. Batters expresses suspicion regarding the abundance of ivy vines at the location.

Sir Oswald is interrupted by his secretary, Rupert Bettman, who informs him that Mrs. Cutt is distressed and believes Sir Oswald might be murdered. Sir Oswald dismisses this and follows Bettman. Meanwhile, Bartlett and Jimmy discuss Bettman, whom Jimmy describes as smart but lacking a sense of humor. Miss Erin questions why Sir Oswald was wandering the garden at night, but the inspector defends Sir Oswald's character.

The investigation shifts to Miss Wade, who explains she was at the scene because she felt a need for security and had purchased a gun from Harrods. She claims she saw a tall man climbing down the ivy after a paper package landed at her feet. Detective Bates suggests the package was intentionally thrown at an accomplice. Mr. Cecil adds that the assailant was a strong, uneducated man who made growling sounds during a struggle.

Detective Balthazar discovers Countess Razkai unconscious behind a Spanish leather screen. As she regains consciousness with the help of Bender and Bondel, Bill expresses intense anxiety. The Countess assures the group of her well-being, noting her Hungarian "steel will," and directs a gentle comment toward Bill.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After being offered a cocktail, the Countess recounts her experience to Detective Bartlett, Detective Bündel, Bill, and others. She explains that unable to sleep due to the oppressive atmosphere of the house, she went downstairs to find a book using a small flashlight. While searching the bookshelf, she heard stealthy footsteps and hid behind a screen. She witnessed a struggle between two men: a man with a rough voice who repeatedly said, "Let me go," and a gentleman with a distinct British accent. During the fight, a gunshot was fired, and the Countess believed she fainted.

Inspector Bartlett investigates the scene and discovers a .45 caliber shell casing near the bookshelf rather than a bullet. He determines that the shell hit the bookshelf while the bullet ricocheted off a window frame and flew outside. Jimmy, who has a bandaged arm, admits to being the gentleman with the British accent. Inspector Bartlett explains that the incident was an attempted theft of political documents from Sir Stanley Digby, which was thwarted by the presence of a young girl named Loran.

As the Countess leaves the room leaning on Bill's arm, Bündel observes a small black mole on the Countess's right shoulder blade through her thin nightgown. Meanwhile, Inspector Batter prepares to secure the room. George Lomax and Dr. Carter arrive, with the doctor noting that Mr. Orock has been given an injection and will wake in the morning. Jimmy asks Loran to assist him as he prepares to have his gunshot wound examined.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Jimmy, Lois, and Dr. Carruthers depart, Inspector Batters (also referred to as Bartholomew, Barton, or Bartlett) pulls Irene Bondel into a private living room. Bondel recounts her entry into the Seven Dials Club and her subsequent adventures. She asserts that the woman she saw at the club, Anna, was indeed Countess Razgki, identifying her by a specific mole. She argues that the Countess's behavior—such as using a flashlight to look for a book—was suspicious and that her fainting was feigned. The Inspector acknowledges the Countess's suspicious behavior but insists on caution to avoid diplomatic issues with the embassy, noting a long-term strategy to target larger threats rather than "small fry." He asks Bondel not to raise further suspicions and mentions he must report to Sir Stanley Digby.

The following morning in the library, Inspector Battersby meets with George Lomax, Sir Osbert Cutt, and Jimmy Sceece to review evidence. The Inspector displays labeled items, including a Leopold automatic pistol and two bullets: a .45 caliber bullet from Mr. Sceges's Colt that hit a cedar tree, and a .25 caliber Mauser bullet that lodged in an armchair. The shooter wore gloves, leaving no fingerprints. Regarding a gun found near the terrace, the Inspector notes that Sir Osbert's footprints are the only ones on the lawn, aside from Miss Wade's. Based on the indentation in the grass, the Inspector and George conclude the gun was thrown from the terrace rather than dropped by a fleeing man. The meeting is interrupted by Mrs. Cutt, who brings medicine to Sir Osbert.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Cutt confesses that she only realized Sir Oswald had been out for a walk after Mr. Betman inquired about his whereabouts. Sir Oswald admits he went out for fresh air because he could not sleep, and he entered the house through a window while wearing only slippers. After Lady Cutt leaves the room, George Lomax suggests that the shooter wounded Jimmy Cecil and escaped via the gravel path along the terrace. However, Inspector Butler disputes this, noting that he arrived at the terrace less than fifty seconds after the shot and saw no one. Butler suggests the culprit escaped by climbing up the ivy. He theorizes the man used a path through Mr. Orrick’s room—which was locked from the inside—and through Stanley’s room to reach a clear exit.

Inspector Bates suggests the culprit might be an insider, which distresses George Lomax. To divert attention, Jimmy Cecil asks about a charred, oversized object on the table. Inspector Batter reveals it is a burnt glove found in a fireplace grate that appears to have been chewed by a dog. 

Eileen Bondell enters and reports that her father's servant, John Boll, has gone missing. Sir Oswald identifies Boll as the likely culprit, noting that Boll is a well-trained man who had been at the Chimney House prior to arriving at the current location. Sir Oswald concludes that Boll was targeting his confidential files regarding a valuable formula and that the man seen climbing the ivy was indeed Boll. As Inspector Batter leaves to make a phone call, Jimmy Cecil tells Eileen Bondell that he finds Bartholomew's actions suspicious, specifically regarding why Bartholomew sent Cut to throw the gun.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bondel and Jimmy discuss the events of the previous night. Jimmy proposes a theory that the Countess and Bourne were accomplices in a theft: the Countess provided O'Roke with a sleeping pill, and Bourne entered O'Roke's room via ivy to steal documents and throw them to the Countess below. Jimmy notes that the plan failed because he was waiting at the bottom, and the Countess had to hide behind a screen in the library to avoid him. Bondel disputes this, arguing that the explanation is too convenient and that Bourne was actually a scapegoat meant to divert attention from a mysterious figure known as "seven o'clock," whom she believes is a guest at the party.

Jimmy questions if Butler had a plan to use the Countess to catch someone else. They also discuss the departure of Sir Stanley Digby and O'Roke, with Jimmy suggesting O'Roke is also problematic. Bondel notes that the Bartlett Inspector was definitely not present at seven o'clock.

George Lomax approaches Bondel and Eileen, delivering a long, reactionary speech about traditional values, family, and dignity. He expresses a desire to act as a political mentor to Bondel, intending to provide her with political economics books to study. After George leaves, Bill arrives and questions why George was touching Bondel's hand. Bondel expresses concern that George's interest will trap her in political obligations, but Bill dismisses her fears, noting that George does not approve of women in parliament.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While passing through a side door near a storeroom filled with recreational items, Miss Bondell and Bill encounter Constable Butter, who is inspecting golf clubs. Inspector Bardell joins the group shortly after.

Later, Jimmy Sessage, feeling downcast and wanting to avoid discussions regarding the Santa Fe border dispute, approaches Loran Wade in the garden. Jimmy proposes marriage, but Loran declines, stating it is not the right time because they are not yet safe. Jimmy agrees, noting that while they have secured a document from the Aviation Minister, they have yet to catch the elusive "seven o'clock." Loran expresses fear regarding this figure and the death of Gerry, but Jimmy, claiming to be a genius, vows to catch him and his accomplices. Jimmy also mentions that he has gained the favor of Mrs. Kut through his charm and a recent injury.

Loran reveals that Bundel suggested she stay at the Chimney House. Jimmy encourages this so she can monitor Bundel’s unpredictable schemes. Loran suggests that Bill might be performing his own detective work, noting his fury when he saw Lomax holding Bundel's hand, though Jimmy remains skeptical of Bill's intelligence. Jimmy instructs Loran to return to the Chimney House with Bondel to prevent her from visiting the Seven Club.

Jimmy then approaches Mrs. Kut in the garden. While discussing his arm injury and the risk of septicemia, Jimmy feigns innocence to gather information. Mrs. Kut reveals that Sir Osward has rented the Duke of Alton's house in Latherbury, a large and gloomy residence. She then reminisces about her previous cheerful home in Yorkshire.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a conversation with Jimmy, Lady Cutt expresses her sorrow regarding Sir Oswald's plan to give their home to a company, noting that his taste is low and he is driven by an insatiable desire for more wealth and status. Mrs. Cutt mentions that Sir Oswald places great trust in the judgment of Mr. Bettman, a diligent worker at his factory. Jimmy recalls a pleasant weekend at the Chimney House, though he notes the sadness of Gerry's death during that time. Mrs. Cutt asks Jimmy if he is interested in a girl, specifically mentioning Vera Daventry, whom she refers to by the nickname "the socks." Jimmy expresses a desire to see Vera again, and Mrs. Cutt invites him to their house the following weekend when she is visiting.

After Jimmy leaves, Sir Oswald expresses his dislike for the boy, calling him a troublemaker. Lady Cutt informs Oswald that she has already invited Mr. Cecil to visit the following weekend. Oswald objects, revealing that Mr. Bettman warned him to be careful of Cecil, as they were former classmates. Although Lady Cutt expresses regret for the misunderstanding, she continues her knitting with a subtle smile.

In a separate setting, Lord Kettlem discusses golf with Bondel. Lord Kettlem has taken up the game with great enthusiasm but poor skill, frequently tearing up the turf. McTavish attempts to repair the damage caused by Lord Kettlem's shots. Bondel suggests that McTavish's frustration with the Cutlers is finally being met with "just desserts." Bunty remarks that golf keeps Lord Kettlem out of trouble, while Lord Kettlem claims to have achieved a hole-in-one. Bunty also observes that while Osborne Cutt's golf technique is stiff and accurate, he is unable to make short putts, causing him to lose games.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While watching golf, Lord Kettlem discusses the game with Bunty and Bondle, noting that his secretary, Mr. Bettman, has a theory that golf relies on the strength of the left arm. Bondle receives a phone call from Jimmy, who reveals he is spending the weekend at the Cutt house and asks if she knows how to obtain a universal key. Jimmy mentions he may ask Stevens for help, noting his previous interest in a blued barrel automatic pistol. Bondle warns him to be careful of Sir Oswald, while Jimmy expresses concern about a "chimpanzee" who sneaks around. Jimmy proposes a plan to Bondle and Loire to have their car break down near Lashbury between 12:15 and 12:30 to facilitate a lunch meeting. He also mentions that Terence O'Rourke is attending, whom he suspects might be involved with a secret organization and the Countess. Jimmy instructs Bondle and Loire to keep the "gorilla" and O'Rourke occupied until lunchtime.

Later, at Lesperon, Jimmy arrives to a warm welcome from Countess Kürt, though Sir Oswald appears displeased. O'Rourke attempts to confuse the guests with tall tales regarding a robbery at the Draconic Church involving masked men and Russian jewels. During the gathering, Mr. Bettman observes that O'Rourke deals cards with his left hand. After dinner, while the others play bridge, Jimmy slips into Sir Oswald's study. Using a wire to open locked drawers, he searches for information regarding Mr. Hel Eberhard's invention or the mysterious "seven o'clock," but he finds nothing of interest.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After searching a study and ensuring all drawers were locked to avoid detection by the meticulous Rupert Bettman, Jimmy leaves the room. In the hallway, he encounters Bettman, who claims he was investigating a potential thief. Jimmy notices a bulge in Bettman's pocket, suggesting he is carrying a gun, and mockingly refers to him as an "ape." Jimmy claims he is looking for cookies because a tin labeled for guests was empty. He later reveals he has hidden a heap of cookies in a dressing table drawer, intending to eat them despite finding them unappetizing. He notes that Bettman is suspicious, carries a gun, and seems to never sleep.

Later, Bondell and Loran arrive at the house after their car, a Sienna, breaks down. They are greeted by Mrs. Cutter and join O'Roke and others for lunch. Loran engages Bettman in conversation, praising his vast knowledge. A game of clockwork golf follows, featuring Bettman, Loran, O'Roke, and Bondell; Bettman and Loran emerge victorious. Jimmy joins the group, appearing confident. After lunch, Jimmy offers to drive the girls to the garage, complaining of indigestion caused by eating too many cookies. Bondell and Loran discuss their successful attempt to trick Bettman and O'Rourke. Jimmy remarks that while O'Rourke was easy to handle, Bettman is "omnipresent," appearing silently and everywhere.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jimmy, Bondel, and Bunty discuss Jimmy's recent activities. Jimmy reveals he has been searching the house for clues regarding "seven o'clock" and presents a small bottle containing a mysterious white powder, though he refuses to disclose where he found it. Bunty then brings up a nearly burned glove that Detective Batter had asked Jimmy to try on. Bondel observes that because Jimmy's right hand is bandaged, the glove—which was large—was actually worn on his left hand. Bondel concludes that Detective Batter used the distraction of the glove's size to hide the fact that the shooter was left-handed. 

Jimmy recalls that Sir Oswald Cutt is ambidextrous and notes that during a bridge game at the chimney house on the night Gary Wade died, Sir Oswald was dealing the cards left-handed. The group speculates that Sir Oswald might be connected to the "seven o'clock" mystery and the events at the Double Dragon Church, suggesting he may have orchestrated a farce to avoid suspicion. They also recall Inspector Bartel plucking ivy from the millionaire's coat, wondering if the inspector had suspected him from the start.

Meanwhile, Lord Kettlem, who is attempting to hide a wrist injury, is visited by George Lomax. After an initial attempt to avoid the visitor, Kettlem receives George. George expresses a desire to speak with Eileen (Bondel). George praises Eileen's maturity, intelligence, and ambition, eventually declaring his intention to marry her. He argues that despite their age difference, he can provide her with a respectable social position and that their union would be mutually beneficial.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
George Lomax visits Lord Kittelmann to ask permission to marry Bondel. Lord Kittelmann, advising George to reconsider to avoid potential rejection, instructs the butler, Tredewell, to find Bondel and inform her that George wishes to speak with her in the living room. After George departs, Bill Everleigh arrives seeking Bondel. Lord Kittelmann informs Bill that George is currently proposing to her. This news enrages Bill, who insults George as a shameless hypocrite and expresses regret that he never dared to propose to Bondel himself. Distraught, Bill leaves the estate and drives to London to visit Jimmy Sisge at St. James Square.

Bill tells Jimmy that he is deeply unsettled by a letter he received from the executor of Ronnie DeVille's will, which was intended to be sent two weeks after DeVille's death. While Bill initially appears bewildered and shaken, he regains his composure after drinking whiskey. Once Jimmy sends his servant, Stevens, out to buy cigarettes to ensure privacy, Bill prepares to reveal the baffling contents of the letter.

Meanwhile, Bondel returns to the garden in a state of distress. She informs Lorelai that George Lomax has proposed to her. Bondel expresses her frustration and hatred for George's stuttering, nonsensical proposal and notes that she does not know how to respond to him.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bondel expresses her disdain for George Lomax, explaining to Loraine that she fled his proposal by escaping through a window. Bondel’s father, Lord Kettlemore, joins them, and Bondel accuses him of encouraging George's advances. After a brief game of golf, the group returns to the house to find a note from George addressed to Lord Chiltern. In the note, George describes Bondel's "girlish confusion" following his proposal as attractive and expresses confidence in his eventual success in winning her over. Lord Kettlemore reacts with shock at George's persistence.

The situation shifts when Bondel receives an urgent telephone call from Jimmy, who claims that Bill has discovered something that could be the "biggest scoop of the century." Jimmy instructs Bondel and Loraine to come to the Seven Dials Club in town, advising them to use the excuse that they are taking Loraine home and to find a way to bypass the doorman, Alfred. Bondel informs her father of their departure and drives Loraine to London.

Upon arriving at 14 Hanover Street, Bondel tells Alfred that the police are looking for Mosgrove and gives him ten pounds to flee, ensuring he will not interfere with their meeting. Jimmy and Bill arrive; Jimmy leads the group into a room containing seven chairs around a table and various glassware. As Jimmy prepares to investigate a cabinet, Loraine rushes back up the stairs in a panic. She informs the group that Bill is in the car and believes he is dead because he is not moving or speaking.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After finding Bill unconscious in a car, Jimmy, Bondle, and Loran move him inside to a couch. Jimmy suspects Bill was injected with a substance by someone pretending to ask for the time. Jimmy leaves to find a doctor, leaving a handgun on a table for protection. While Bondle and Loran watch over Bill, Bondle goes upstairs to the secret meeting room to retrieve Loran's bag, which contains bromine and brandy. There, an unknown assailant strikes her with a sandbag, knocking her unconscious.

Bondle eventually regains consciousness in a dark, locked room. She discovers she is being held captive and is being held tightly by Bill. During their reunion, Bill confesses his long-standing love for her. Bondle reciprocates his feelings, and they engage in an emotional conversation about marriage. As Bondle attempts to refocus their attention on finding an escape, she hears footsteps. The door is opened by a bearded man named Mr. Mosgrove. Bill, acting with unexpected authority, tells Mr. Mosgrove that he wants to speak to him alone and instructs Bondle to go with the man, claiming he has a plan. Bondle obeys, curious about Bill's intentions.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After being locked in a musty room by Mosgolovsky, Eileen Bond is led by Mosgrove to an emergency meeting of the Seven Clocks Association. Inside the secret room, Bond finds masked individuals seated around a table. The figure at the head of the table, wearing a seven o'clock mask, reveals himself to be Detective Bartholomew. 

Bartholomew explains that the Seven Clocks Association is not a criminal organization, but a group of amateur spies founded by the late Gerry Wade to conduct secret intelligence work. He identifies the members: Mosgolovsky serves as his second-in-command; Count Andras of Hungary is five o'clock; American journalist Hayward Felphs is four o'clock; and Bill Everly is three o'clock. The two o'clock seat is vacant due to the death of Ronnie DeVille, and the one o'clock position is held by Countess Razky, who is revealed to be a top-notch actress named Barbara Saint-Moore.

Bartholomew explains that the group's purpose was to catch a dangerous thief capable of stealing secret inventions. This thief, Jimmy Sessage, was eventually caught by Bill Everly. Bartholomew clarifies that while Gerry Wade's death appeared to be a sleeping pill overdose, Mr. De Vere believed Wade was murdered by a skilled killer within their social circle. De Vere had suspected Sessage but had not yet revealed his suspicions before his own death.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Superintendent Batter explains the resolution of the mystery to Bondel. He reveals that Jimmy Sceggie poisoned Gerald Wade’s whiskey and soda with poison before Wade went to sleep, causing Wade to feel tired in a letter to Miss Lorena Wade. Cezar administered the lethal poison and later planted a bottle, a cup, and an empty chloroform bottle near the unconscious Wade to frame him or create confusion. While DeVille eventually deduced that Cezar was the thief, he was betrayed by Lorena Wade, who loved Cecil and provided him with information. DeVille was subsequently eliminated, attempting to use the seven alarm clocks to signal that Cecil was his killer.

Batter further details a complex deception involving Cecil. During an incident at the Two-Footed Dragon Church, Cecil staged a struggle to cover his theft. Cecil did not actually faint from being shot; instead, he climbed down ivy to Mr. O'Roke's room, where O'Roke had been drugged with sleeping pills. Cecil stole documents and threw them to an accomplice. To create an alibi, Cecil staged a fight, fired his Colt automatic pistol at an imaginary attacker, and then shot his own right arm with a Mauser pistol to simulate being attacked. He then threw the Mauser out the window and destroyed a tooth-marked glove in the fireplace. Batter realized the truth after finding the burnt glove and noting that the gun fell a distance inconsistent with a left-handed throw. He concluded that Lorena Wade’s presence at the terrace was not a coincidence, but that she was the intended recipient of the documents.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Bartholomew and Inspector Bartlett reveal the resolution of the mystery involving the "Seven O'Clock" killer. They explain that Mr. Cecil, previously thought to be a "brainless idiot," was actually a ruthless criminal. To catch him, Bill Everly acted as a decoy. Cecil attempted to poison Everly with a drink laced with hydrochloric morphine, but Everly poured the liquid into a jar and feigned unconsciousness. Cecil then attempted to create an alibi by driving to a golf course and visiting the Seven Dials Club, eventually hiding in a room to surprise the victims.

The conspiracy involved Lorel, a woman with a criminal past, and Jimmy, who assisted Cecil in moving the "unconscious" Everly. They intended to leave the victims together to be discovered at seven o'clock. However, the plan failed due to the intervention of Sexton and the presence of Bondell. Inspector Bartlett notes that Cecil will be hanged, while Lorel's fate remains uncertain.

Following the investigation, Bondell informs her father, Lord Kettlemere, that she intends to marry Bill Everly. Lord Kettlemere, who discusses his plans to write memoirs with a ghostwriter, reacts with satisfaction to the news, noting that Everly will make an excellent partner for his autumn golf doubles competition.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While dining in London, the narrator informs American journalist William P. Ryan that he is traveling to Treyan, a house in Falmouth, Cornwall, owned by his sister. This prompts Ryan to recount an experience from the early days of the war in 1912. Ryan, who was reporting in a Belgian village during the German army's advance, describes a strange incident involving a convent. Local farmers claimed a nun possessed special powers, specifically the ability to summon lightning to strike an "infidel savage" without harming anything else. Ryan noted that at the scene, a burnt mark on a wall resembled a giant dog, which terrified locals who called it the "death dog." Ryan eventually learned that the nun had survived and moved to the Falmouth area.

Upon arriving at his sister Kitty's house in Trin, the narrator asks if any Belgian refugees were nuns. Kitty identifies a Sister Mary Angelique, an odd character living in the village. She explains that a local physician, Dr. Ross, has arranged for the sister to live with the village nurse to receive care, as Dr. Ross is interested in studying her alleged psychic abilities and mental state. The narrator shares Ryan's story with Kitty, and they decide to visit Dr. Ross. After meeting the friendly but unsettling Dr. Ross, the narrator accompanies him to a small house inland from the village, where the nurse resides, to observe Sister Mary Angelique.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Ross and Mr. Anstruther visit Mary-Annichelle, a woman referred to as "the Auntie," who lives in a state of confusion and believes her dreams are more real than life. During the visit, Anstruther mentions a destroyed monastery and a wall marked with a "Death Hound" shape that terrifies local farmers. Upon hearing this, Mary-Annichelle becomes hysterical, claiming her memories are true. She recounts using a specific power to unleash the "death hound" on people who ignored her warnings at an altar. 

After Dr. Ross administers a liquid to calm her, Mary-Annichelle describes a circular city consisting of three rings for gods, priestesses, and priests, with a "house of crystal" at its center. As she mentions the crystal house, she traces circles on her forehead before suddenly regaining her composure and forgetting her words.

Outside, Anstruther suggests Mary-Annichelle is deranged, but Ross posits that what appears supernatural might actually be natural laws not yet understood, such as the ability to exert destructive force through willpower. Ross reveals he has been conducting experiments with crystals, noting that a previous patient reacted to a crystal by kneeling and claiming that "faith still exists" before passing out. Ross intends to conduct a further experiment the following day by having a patient gaze at a crystal, a topic that causes him to become uncharacteristically stiff and formal when questioned by Anstruther.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Rose expresses interest in Mary Annechiello as a case study for mental illness, leading the narrator to question his professional motives. The following afternoon, the narrator accompanies Dr. Rose to visit Sister Mary Angelica, a nun. During the visit, Sister Mary Angelica expresses distress over her memory loss and asks the narrator if she is insane. 

Dr. Rose suggests that Sister Mary Angelica may have a gift for seeing things in crystals. Although she initially refuses, fearing it is sinful to interpret the future, the doctor reframes the task as looking into the past. While gazing into a crystal, Sister Mary Angelica enters a trance-like state. Upon waking, she describes a dream in which the crystal is a sacred symbol and she is the "fifth miracle priestess of the Crystal House." She expresses confusion and pain regarding these visions, noting that she has dreamed of foreign cities and customs despite never traveling. She also mentions experiencing a sensation of her spirit leaving her body during prayer, which the abbess calls a "state of grace."

Dr. Rose conducts a hypnotic experiment using the crystal and a series of words. Later, at his house by the cliff, he reviews the results with the narrator. He notes that different "wonders" correspond to specific colors and symbols: the first wonder is blood, the fourth is yellow, the fifth is a bird (representing telepathy), and the sixth is a dog (representing destructive power). He observes that the seventh wonder relates to life and love, speculating that seven represents the sum of these wonders. While the narrator notes that the sixth wonder's destruction implies the seventh did not come true, Dr. Rose maintains that these phenomena must be viewed as serious medical mental disturbances.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a tense encounter where Dr. Ross expresses a desire to document a case for personal curiosity, the narrator acquires the doctor's notebook and diary. The diary entries from August reveal Dr. Ross's hypnotic sessions with Mary Angelica Sister, who spoke of "divine states" and the necessity of closing "the door" to prevent body control. Dr. Ross's notes suggest an obsession with a "sixth miracle" and a desire for power, eventually recording his decision to administer continuous morphine to Mary Angelica Nun.

The narrator also receives a letter from Mary Angelica, written in an italic hand, which describes a cycle involving "crystal guardians" and a "death hound" that rises from the sea. She identifies Dr. Ross as a "brother" who knows secrets regarding the first and second miracles but warns that he is seeking the sixth miracle prematurely. Following this, the narrator receives a letter from Gitty reporting that Dr. Ross, a nurse, and Mary Angelica the nun all perished when Dr. Ross's cliffside house was destroyed by a landslide; the wreckage on the beach resembled a giant dog. Additionally, a wealthy relative of Dr. Ross died from a suspicious lightning strike on the same night, leaving his property to the doctor. The narrator speculates that Dr. Ross may have attempted to harness the power of the sixth miracle but failed to follow the necessary steps, causing the power to backfire and "close the circle."

In a separate setting, during a dinner party attended by Mrs. Everleigh, Claire Trent, Jack Trent, and the arrogant psychiatrist Sir Arlington West, the conversation turns to the concept of a "sixth sense" and premonition.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a dinner gathering, a medical expert, Sir Arlington, discusses the nature of intuition and the subconscious with the guests. Demott West challenges Claire Trent’s dismissal of premonition by describing his "red signals"—intuitive warnings of danger. He recounts an experience in the Mesopotamian plain where a persistent feeling of danger led him to sleep outside his tent; the next morning, he discovered an Arab servant had slashed his bed where he would have been lying. Sir Arlington explains this phenomenon as the subconscious mind processing subtle external cues that the conscious mind fails to register. Demott also mentions declining an invitation to a country house due to a "red alert," only for the house to be destroyed a week later.

As the conversation continues, Demott experiences a sudden, intense "red signal" of danger. He initially struggles to identify the source, noting his complicated, painful romantic admiration for Claire Trent and his friendship with her husband, Jack Trent. However, he begins to observe the group with new scrutiny, realizing he does not truly know the Trents. He notices that Sir Arlington has been observing Claire in an extremely strange manner, causing her to appear tense and afraid. Demott also realizes the dinner is a prelude to a séance, which Sir Arlington claims to support. The medical expert concludes the discussion by noting that individuals who suppress self-deception or hallucinations can become dangerous, appearing normal while harboring a "dangerous root" that threatens others.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a gathering, Sir Arlington engages in a discussion with Mrs. Everly regarding the causes of harm and the nature of hereditary diseases. Sir Arlington explains the mechanics of gender-limited inheritance using color blindness as an example, while noting that madness is inherited with equal probability in both males and females. Claire becomes visibly distressed and pale during this conversation, pleading with the group to stop before the arrival of Mrs. Thompson. Dermot, observing Claire's anxiety and Trent's tension, feels like an outsider to a secret shared among the friends.

The group moves to the living room for a séance led by Mrs. Thompson, a medium who uses the Japanese name Kitsune. During the séance, a spirit named Tsukimi speaks through the medium, recounting dull details of her life. A second, more sinister voice then emerges, issuing a frantic warning to one of the three men present: "Don't go home. Danger! Blood!" Dermot feels the warning is directed specifically at him. After the session, a weary Mrs. Thompson remarks that the room feels filled with the "scent of death" and asks if anyone has experienced recent sudden deaths.

Following the medium's departure, Sir Arlington declines an invitation to dance and asks to speak with Dermot privately. After a brief walk toward Harley Street, Sir Arlington apologizes for taking Dermot away and instructs him to meet the others at the Grafton Gallery. Sir Arlington then searches his pockets for his keys while instructing Dermot to ring the bell for Johnson.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Sir Arlington invites his nephew, Dermot, to his study, where he confronts him regarding Dermot's feelings for Claire Trent. Sir Arlington warns Dermot that Claire is unsuitable, revealing that he is attending the evening's dinner in a professional capacity because he believes Claire is mentally ill. He cites a family history of mental illness on the mother's side and argues that Claire must be restrained to prevent a "deadly form of murderous mania." 

Dermot rejects his uncle's medical assessment and declares his love for Claire, vowing to protect her from interfering doctors. Sir Arlington threatens to disinherit Dermot and leave his property to hospitals if Dermot attempts to take Claire away. During their heated argument, the servant Johnson enters with drinks, overhearing part of the dispute. Dermot eventually apologizes for his tone but maintains his stance, stating that money cannot influence his feelings, before exiting the house and taking a taxi to the Grafton Gallery.

At the gallery, Dermot finds Claire and asks her to dance. When they move to a quiet corner, Claire confesses that she has fallen in love with him, but she urges him to leave because she believes it is "too late" for them to be together. When Dermot suggests fleeing to the South Pacific to be safe, Claire breaks free from his embrace, crying that she cannot be with him because of a sense of shame.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After being moved by a woman's plea for loyalty, Demott leaves a social gathering and walks home through the chilly evening. Upon entering his apartment, he experiences an intense sense of danger. While searching the residence, he discovers an unfamiliar revolver hidden in a drawer, noting that it has a recently fired barrel and was not there earlier that evening.

The doorbell rings, and Demott, sensing imminent danger, turns off the lights and pretends to be the servant, Milson, to intercept the visitors. Inspector Wylie of Scotland Yard arrives with a police officer, carrying an arrest warrant for Demott's master, Sir Arlington West, for the murder of an uncle. A second officer discovers the revolver in the apartment and hands it to the Inspector. Constable Cole informs Demott that the case against West is clear: West and his uncle had been arguing over a will, and a butler named Johnson found the uncle dead of a gunshot wound shortly after.

Realizing the evidence has been planted to frame him, Demott uses the pretense of making tea to escape through a window, climbing down an iron bar despite cutting his hands. He encounters Jack Trent on the street, who helps him hail a taxi. Trent expresses his belief in Demott's innocence and notes that a medium at a recent séance had warned against going home. Trent leads Demott to his own house and into a secret room on the second floor to plan their next move.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Trent reveals his involvement in a series of crimes to Demott, admitting that he killed Old West. Trent explains that he stole keys to both Old West's house and Demott's house, allowing him to kill West and then plant a revolver in Demott's drawer to frame him. Trent expresses a sinister obsession with Claire, stating his intention to use a knife on her. He claims he intends to be arrested for his actions, though he threatens to shoot Demott in the limbs to prevent escape if necessary. As Trent confesses, Demott realizes that Claire and his uncle had been plotting something, but before he can react, Claire shoots Trent. Trent collapses and dies, and Inspector Vallier confirms his death.

In a different setting, Canon Paffitt boards a first-class train carriage during a snowy night. He encounters three other passengers: Sir George Durand, a famous lawyer; Dr. Campbell Clarke, a prominent medical and psychiatric researcher; and an unremarkable, dark-skinned stranger who appears to be a foreigner. Durand and Dr. Clarke engage Canon in a discussion regarding their respective fields of expertise—the church, law, and medicine. Dr. Clarke argues that their collective perspectives overlook the viewpoint of ordinary people and the importance of human relationships. He notes that many patients suffer not from pathology, but from the psychological trauma caused by interpersonal friction. When Canon dismisses these issues as mere "nervousness," Dr. Clarke reacts sharply, criticizing the tendency to mock mental diseases with such a term.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Campbell Clark engages in a philosophical and scientific discussion with Canon Paffitt and Sir George Durand regarding the nature of the human mind and soul. Clark posits that the mind may contain multiple "occupants" or personalities, comparing the body to a house filled with silent servants or different personas shaped by emotion. To illustrate this, Clark describes a famous case of multiple personalities involving Félicité Bauer, a Breton girl who became a scientific sensation in France.

Clark details Bauer's history, noting she was raised by an English spinster named Miss Slate after her father was exiled for murdering her mother. Bauer exhibited at least four distinct personalities: Félicité 1, who was uneducated and lacked foreign language skills; Félicité 2, who was intelligent, spoke Italian and German, and played the piano; Félicité 3, who was well-bred but morally decadent and used vulgar Parisian slang; and Félicité 4, a devout and insightful but elusive personality that some suspected was a hoax created by Félicité 3. Each personality change was accompanied by severe headaches and deep sleep, with each persona being unaware of the others.

The discussion concludes with the mysterious death of Félicité Bauer. Although she was found strangled in her bed, investigations proved she had strangled herself using her own fingerprints. This feat required a superhuman level of physical strength, leaving the cause and nature of her death an unsolved mystery.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While traveling by train, a man named Raoul Le Verte joins a conversation between a doctor, Canon, and Sir George Durand regarding the case of Felicity Bault. Le Verte reveals that he was raised in a home run by a philanthropist named Miss Slade, alongside Felicie Boulanger and Annette Lavelle (also referred to as Annette Ravil). He explains that understanding Annette’s character is essential to understanding Felicity.

Le Verte describes Annette as a passionate, commanding girl who frequently bullied the physically strong but "stupid" Felicity. While Felicity resented Annette’s mockery and insults regarding her parentage, she continued to follow Annette. Le Verte recounts an incident where Annette used an early book on hypnosis to manipulate Felicity. By having Felicity stare at a spinning brass ball, Annette successfully commanded her to eat a candle in a playground, claiming it was the best cake she had ever tasted. The prank caused Felicity to experience confusion and intense hatred toward Annette, though Annette continued to dominate her.

As they grew older, Annette’s ambitions shifted from dancing to singing. Under the guidance of Miss Slade, Annette trained intensely as a singer. Le Verte notes that Miss Slade eventually expressed concern regarding Annette's health, specifically a persistent cough resulting from her excessive practice.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Raul recounts his reunion with Annette Lavoisier in Paris after five years abroad. He finds her performing successfully on stage, though she dismisses the influence of Miss Slate in favor of her own artistic expression. During a visit to her dressing room, Raul meets a handsome man acting as Annette's protector, who gifts her a ruby and diamond bracelet. As Raul leaves, he hears Annette coughing, recognizing the sign of the hereditary tuberculosis that killed her mother.

Two years later, Raul finds Annette in a garden hut at Miss Slate's, where her career has collapsed and her tuberculosis has reached a terminal stage. Despite her physical frailty and violent coughing, Annette expresses a fierce, almost manic will to survive. During this time, Felicity Bult brings Annette milk with an expression of hidden satisfaction. Enraged, Annette accuses Felicity of being glad she is dying and commands Felicity to kneel on broken glass. To Raul's surprise, Felicity obeys with a blank expression, prompting Annette to laugh before collapsing in exhaustion. Felicity leaves with a look of suppressed hatred.

After Annette dies in a desperate struggle against death, Miss Slate informs Raul that Felicity has suffered a strange mental breakdown. Raul later discovers that Felicity has become a talented piano player. When he encounters her in a reading room, she is playing a melody Annette once sang. Felicity mocks Raul, adopts a new way of speaking, and reveals that she has been playing the role of a "stupid girl" as an actress. She eventually runs out of the room, and Raul later finds her sleeping in an armchair.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Raul Wright recounts a memory of Felicia Boulter, who claimed that the deceased Annette Lavelle was tormenting her from beyond the grave. Felicia exhibited extreme terror, claiming Annette's voice was in her head and that she was being driven away. Raul notes that Felicia possessed hands with a terrifying power. After Felicia's sudden, violent death, Raul traveled to the southern United States, only learning the details of her passing through a newspaper two years later. In a train carriage, Raul suggests to a lawyer, Canon Paffitt, a doctor, and a Frenchman that Felicia was a good actress. The Frenchman, asserting that Felicia’s history is inseparable from Annette Lavelle’s, punches Canon Paffitt in the abdomen before abruptly exiting the train.

In a separate narrative, MacClannough observes his friend Dicky Carpenter’s aversion to Gypsies. MacClannough is married to Rachel Lawes, the sister of Dicky’s former fiancée, Esther Lawes. Dicky, a straightforward man in the navy, describes a recurring childhood nightmare involving a Gypsy woman who watched him with a knowing, sad expression. He recounts a real-life encounter in the New Forest where a Gypsy woman in a red headscarf warned him not to take a specific path; shortly after, the wooden bridge he crossed collapsed, nearly drowning him. Dicky claims this encounter was the beginning of a series of events, starting with his visit to the Rooses' home to see Esther, where he felt a strange sensation of being watched.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dickie Carpenter recounts a series of unsettling encounters with a mysterious, beautiful woman named Alice Thurston Howarth, who is characterized by her golden hair and a red thin scarf. Dickie describes how, during a past social gathering, she spoke to him with a gentle, regretful tone, warning him, "If I were you, I wouldn't go in." He later encountered a woman resembling her, Mrs. Haversham, in the woods, who issued a similar warning. Most recently, at Dr. Johnny's private sanatorium, Dickie encountered a nurse in a red uniform whom he identified as Mrs. Howarth. She warned him against undergoing a surgery for his torpedo-injured leg, yet a nurse at the facility claimed no one by that name worked there.

McPherson later visits Alice Howarth at her home after obtaining her address from Loose. Upon his arrival, he hears a mournful song about a gypsy woman. Alice, described as possessing Scandinavian beauty, invites him to walk with her and her husband, Morris, toward the moor. During their walk, Alice reveals that Dickie died from anesthesia during a small operation. She dismisses the idea that she was the nurse who warned him, suggesting instead that a look-alike cousin was responsible. 

Alice claims to possess a "gift" or "curse" of second sight inherited from the Fergusson family and her medium mother, Kristin. She demonstrates this by prompting McPherson to look at holes in the rocks, which he perceives as being filled with blood. Alice explains that she married Morris to protect him from the terrible things haunting him, though she was unable to save Dickie.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a conversation where a woman expresses a sense of being separated by time, Macfarlane observes the devoted love between Morris Hovells and his wife during lunch. Following their meeting, the woman suggests they may never meet again. The next morning, McAllister recalls a previous incident involving a car and a sudden fog. He visits the woman's residence, only to be met by a grieving housekeeper. She informs him that Miss Alastair Haversham has died; a captain mistakenly gave her the wrong bottle of tonic in the dark. McAllister views her body in a room illuminated by red light from stained glass. Later, at a different location, Mr. McPherson speaks with a landlady, Mrs. Rose, about local legends involving ghosts like sailors and gypsies. A young woman named Rachel arrives to see him, and he greets her with a kiss.

In a separate setting, Lady Lancaster inspects number 19, an ancient and desolate house in Westminster. Despite the house's gloomy atmosphere and low rent, the practical Lady Lancaster insists on knowing why it has remained vacant. The house agent, Mr. Radish, reluctantly reveals that the vacancy is linked to a child. He explains that thirty years prior, a man named Williams lived there with his sickly son. Williams was later identified as an escaped convict and committed suicide rather than surrendering. The young boy remained alone in the house, obeying his father's orders never to leave, while neighbors heard him crying in the deserted building at night.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Radish informs Lady Lancaster that a child once starved to death at House No. 19, and though no one has seen a ghost, some have heard a child crying. Despite this, Lady Lancaster decides to purchase the house due to its reasonable price. She moves into the home with her father, Mr. Wimborne (also referred to as Mr. Wimbourne/Wimpleton/Werner/Wulffen), and her son, Jeffrey.

While the family settles in, Mr. Wimborne hears dragging, painful footsteps on the oak staircase following Jeffrey, though he dismisses it as rain. That night, Mr. Wimborne has a dream of a city populated only by children who cry when he cannot bring "him" to them. Upon waking, he hears heartbreaking sobbing coming from above. He later hears the same sound during the day and discovers the maid believes Jeffrey is crying, though Jeffrey appears happy; Mr. Wimborne concludes the sound is the same entity that made the dragging footsteps.

Eventually, Jeffrey tells his mother about a lonely, weak little boy living in the attic who watches him play. While Lady Lancaster dismisses the boy as an impossibility, Mr. Wimborne gently encourages Jeffrey to find a way to comfort the child. When Lady Lancaster questions her father's decision to validate the boy's claims, Mr. Wimborne suggests that he and Jeffrey can perceive things she cannot, referencing a question about what lamp might guide children through the darkness.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a conversation regarding the "gift of the blind"—a childhood ability to perceive things that adults lose—Jeffrey falls seriously ill with a long-standing lung disease. As his condition worsens, Mrs. Lancaster hears the heartbreaking sobs of a child in the house. During a period of unconsciousness, Jeffrey speaks to a "little boy," expressing a desire to help him leave. Upon waking briefly, Jeffrey whispers, "Okay, I'll be right there," before falling back into a coma. Mrs. Lancaster and her father then experience a frightening supernatural event involving the sounds of triumphant laughter and the footsteps of two invisible children departing the house.

In a separate narrative, Dr. Menner advises Mrs. Hart that her heart function is weak and that she must avoid exertion and mental worry to survive. He recommends the installation of an elevator. Mrs. Hart’s nephew, Charles Richewe, also proposes installing a radio to alleviate her boredom. Despite Mrs. Hart’s skepticism and fear that radio waves and electricity will disturb her, Charles uses his technical expertise to persuade her. An elevator and a radio are subsequently installed. While Mrs. Hart finds the radio to be an ugly, buzzing device, she shows interest as Charles tunes into broadcasts from Berlin and Brussels. The passage concludes by noting that Mrs. Hart’s niece, Miriam Hart, had previously lived with her but returned to her mother's house after marrying a man her aunt disapproved of.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Disappointed in her niece Miriam, Aunt Harriet decides to name her nephew Charles as her heir due to his respectful and attentive nature. After Charles proves his worth, Harriet instructs her lawyer to draft a new will, which she signs. Charles also introduces a radio to the household, which Harriet eventually grows to enjoy during her quiet evenings.

Three months after the radio's installation, while Charles is away at a wedding, Harriet experiences a bizarre event during a folk music program. The music is interrupted by buzzing and clicking, followed by a man with an Irish accent identifying himself as Patrick, telling Mary she must be ready because he will be with her soon. Harriet, believing she heard her late husband's voice, fears this is a warning regarding her failing health. A second occurrence of the voice confirms her belief that it is not a hallucination, leading her to speculate that "lost radio waves" are transmitting the message.

Terrified, Harriet instructs her loyal maid, Elizabeth, to prepare for her funeral using items kept in a wardrobe. She subsequently contacts her lawyer to reconsider her will. During lunch, Charles mentions seeing a man in the guest room—which Harriet reveals was her husband's dressing room—before noticing the photo of Uncle Patrick on the mantelpiece. Later, while alone, Harriet waits for a third radio transmission. The voice returns, stating that Patrick will come to pick her up on Friday at nine-thirty and that there will be no pain. Trembling and pale, Harriet begins to write a response at her desk.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After hearing the voice of her late husband, Patrick, claiming he would visit her on Friday at 9:30 PM, Mary Hart prepares for her potential death. She writes a note to Dr. Menner to be delivered if she dies, instructs her maid, Elizabeth Marshall, that she is to receive an extra fifty pounds beyond her fifty-pound bequest in the will, and organizes the distribution of her personal belongings and jewelry to various relatives. Mrs. Hart also reviews her will, which leaves two five-hundred-pound checks to her sister and eldest nephew, with the remainder going to her nephew, Charles Richewright.

On Friday evening, Mrs. Hart sits alone by the fireplace, waiting for 9:30 PM. When the time arrives, she hears a rustling sound and a knock at the door. A silhouette wearing Victorian-era clothing and a beard appears; Mrs. Hart believes it is Patrick and dies from the shock. Elizabeth finds her an hour later, and although Dr. Menner and Charles are summoned, Mrs. Hart has already passed away.

Two days later, Dr. Menner reviews Mrs. Hart's note and concludes she died from the shock of a hallucination. Following this, Charles begins destroying evidence of his involvement: he burns a brown beard and hides Victorian clothing in the attic, while also disposing of radio wires. It is revealed that Charles orchestrated the event to secure his inheritance, having planned the "shock" after Dr. Menner previously suggested Mrs. Hart could have lived many years with proper care. Charles then begins efficiently managing the funeral arrangements.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Charles Richewe believes his illegal schemes to secure wealth have been successfully hidden from his deceased aunt, Mrs. Hart. However, his confidence is shaken when Mr. Hopkinson, Mrs. Hart's long-time legal advisor, arrives to discuss her will. Hopkinson reveals that Mrs. Hart had requested the will be sent to him the previous Tuesday, but it is currently missing.

Charles, having searched Mrs. Hart's documents and found nothing, summons Elizabeth, the maid, to assist. Elizabeth testifies that she saw Mrs. Hart holding a will in a long blue envelope on the morning of her death. Elizabeth also notes that she found an empty blue envelope on the table the following morning. Charles produces this empty envelope for Hopkinson. When Hopkinson asks if the fireplace has been burning, Elizabeth confirms it has been lit continuously.

Hopkinson suggests that Mrs. Hart may have instructed him to destroy the will, implying a possible conflict between her and Charles. Although Charles vehemently denies any friction with his aunt, Hopkinson appears skeptical. Charles suddenly realizes that the will may have accidentally fallen into the fireplace ashes during his aunt's death. Hopkinson informs him that if the current will is not found, a previous version from September 1920 remains valid, which leaves the entire estate to Mrs. Hart's niece, Miriam Robinson.

A telephone call from a doctor reveals that Mrs. Hart's heart condition was so severe she had only two months to live, a fact that offers Charles no comfort. Instead, Charles feels the impending shadow of imprisonment.

In a separate scene, a lawyer named Mr. Merriweather meets with his client, Leonard Walcot, who is on trial for murder. Despite Merriweather's assurances that they will clear Walcot's name, the young man remains in despair, and Merriweather privately doubts the strength of their defense.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a legal consultation, Leonard Wall maintains his innocence regarding the death of Miss Emily French, despite being in a serious financial crisis. Wall explains that he first met Miss French on Oxford Street after performing a simple act of kindness by helping her recover dropped packages. Later that evening, he encountered her at a party hosted by his friend, George Harvey. Wall claims he only learned of her wealth and eccentric lifestyle, including her residence in Crikwood and her eight cats, through Harvey.

Wall admits to visiting Miss French frequently, explaining that his motivation was not financial but rather a psychological response to her affection and his own indecisive nature. He states that after several visits, he grew fond of her and began managing her business affairs because she claimed to be inexperienced with investments. His lawyer, Mr. Mehn, challenges this by noting that Miss French’s maid, Janet McKenzie, and her bank manager described her as a capable businesswoman. However, Mehn privately considers the possibility that Miss French feigned incompetence to flatter Wall.

When Mehn asks if Wall ever embezzled funds to alleviate his financial troubles, Wall emphatically denies any underhanded dealings. Wall argues that his strongest defense is a lack of motive, asserting that if he had courted her for money, her death would have thwarted his goals. Mehn then reveals that Miss French left a will naming Wall as her primary beneficiary.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a legal consultation, the accused, Leonard Walpole (also referred to as Walcot or Wall), reacts with alarm to the news that Miss French left him money in her will. The lawyer, Mr. Mehren (also referred to as Merdinogh, Merdle, or Mergenthaler), informs him that the maid, Janet MacKenzie, testified that Miss French intended to leave the inheritance to him. Walpole dismisses MacKenzie as a jealous, suspicious woman who misunderstood Miss French's intentions or falsely believes he coerced the deceased.

Mr. Mehren reveals that MacKenzie returned to the house at 9:45 PM to retrieve a shirt sleeve pattern and heard a man and Miss French talking in the sitting room. Walpole claims this provides him with an alibi, asserting he arrived home at 9:20 PM and that his wife, Mrs. Roman (also referred to as Mrs. Walcot or Romany), can vouch for him. Walpole maintains that an intruder killed Miss French with a crowbar after forcing a window, but the lawyer notes that the stolen items were worthless and the window evidence is unconvincing.

Under questioning, Walpole's demeanor becomes hesitant. He admits he needed money for his wife but denies that Miss French believed he was a bachelor planning to marry her. After the meeting, the lawyer travels to a shabby house near Paddington Green to interview Mrs. Roman. He is met by a maid and eventually by Mrs. Roman, a calm, pale, non-English woman with high cheekbones and black hair, who asks for information regarding her husband.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Merdle, a lawyer, meets with Mrs. Roman Wall to discuss the case of Leonard Walpole. Mrs. Wall reveals she has no intention of clearing Leonard; instead, she expresses intense hatred for him, stating she would rather see him hanged. She challenges Mr. Merdle by suggesting she could testify that Leonard arrived home at 10:10 PM rather than 9:20 PM, that he knew about the incoming money, and that he confessed to the murder with blood-stained clothes. During the confrontation, she reveals she is not Leonard's wife but an actress from Vienna whose actual husband is in a mental institution. Mr. Merdle leaves feeling unsettled, doubting her testimony despite the overwhelming evidence against Leonard.

During the magistrate's court proceedings, the prosecution presents testimony from Janet McKenzie, the deceased's maid, and Roman Hergel, an Austrian woman. The defense finds the case nearly impossible to fight, with the barrister noting that the case hinges on shaking the Austrian woman's testimony. Mr. Merdle focuses on a potential discrepancy regarding who was talking to Miss French at 9:30 PM, suspecting it might have been Miss French's nephew.

On the eve of the verdict, Mr. Merdle receives an anonymous, poorly written letter suggesting that Mrs. Mogson at Shaw's Lentz, number sixteen, possesses information to prove the Austrian woman is lying. Hoping to discredit the witness, Mr. Merdle travels to a dilapidated slum to find the address. He eventually enters a filthy, dim room where he meets Mrs. Mogson, a disheveled, middle-aged, hunched woman who greets him with a strange giggle.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Mehen, a lawyer, visits an old woman to obtain information to clarify the guilt of his client, Leonard Wal. The woman reveals a severe facial scar caused by sulphuric acid and expresses a desire for revenge against a woman who "took" her lover. She demands two hundred pounds for information, but Mehen negotiates the price down to twenty pounds. In exchange, she provides a bundle of love letters written by Romany Hearleg to a man named Max. The top letter is dated the day Leonard was arrested.

The woman claims that Romany Hearleg lied in court about her whereabouts at 10:20 p.m. Mehen verifies this by visiting a cinema on Lyon Road, where a guard confirms that Romany and a man were at the theater during that time. Mehen realizes Romany fabricated her testimony out of personal animosity.

During the trial for the murder of Emily Frenchie, the defense lawyer presents the letters. The letters reveal Romany's true motive: she intended to frame Leonard to achieve revenge, even writing that she would claim he arrived home covered in blood. Upon the reading of the letters, Romany admits she fabricated the entire story and that Leonard had actually arrived home at 9:20 p.m. as he claimed.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following the collapse of Roman Helge's testimony, Ronald Walton is found not guilty in court. Mr. Merchen, a lawyer, realizes that Roman Helge is an actress who used a disguise to influence the trial. He later meets Roman face-to-face, where she confesses that she intentionally provided false testimony to save her friend. She explains that she used psychological manipulation to create a reaction favoring the defendant's release. Roman reveals that a bundle of letters was a plot, a man named Max does not exist, and she knows the defendant is actually guilty, referencing the secret of a blue ceramic jar.

In a separate location, Jack Hattersley, a twenty-four-year-old man who spends his weekends practicing golf near Stoughton Heath, hears a woman's piercing scream shouting "Murder!" while he is practicing. He runs toward a nearby elegant villa and encounters a girl with violet eyes wearing a purple linen robe. The girl, who is weeding a garden, claims she heard nothing and suggests Jack is being tricked. Confused and doubting his own senses, Jack searches the nearby woods but finds no evidence of a crime. He eventually returns home and catches his usual 8:45 train. Although he briefly wonders if he misidentified a bird call, he remains convinced he heard a woman's voice at approximately 7:25, noting the time in case the police require it.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After failing to find news of a murder in the newspapers, Jack becomes increasingly unsettled by a recurring auditory hallucination. While playing golf near a villa, he hears a woman’s voice screaming "Murdered—help! Murdered!" at exactly 7:25 a.m. When he confronts the girl weeding in the villa's garden, she claims she heard nothing and suggests he may have shell shock. This interaction causes Jack to doubt his own sanity, fearing he is experiencing a delusion.

The following morning, Jack hears the same scream at 7:25 a.m. while running toward the golf course, confirming the sound originates from the direction of the villa. During a conversation with the girl, he feels she is patronizing him regarding his mental health. At breakfast, Jack notices Dr. Lavinton, a professional-looking man with piercing gray eyes, observing him intently. Jack fears the doctor has detected his mental instability.

To test whether the screams are real or a hallucination, Jack arranges to play golf with Mr. Ravelton the next morning. At 7:25 a.m., the scream occurs again while Ravelton is playing. Jack observes that Ravelton hears nothing, confirming that the sound is only audible to Jack. The shock of the event causes Jack to stumble and collapse on the grass, leaving him disoriented as Lavinton attempts to assist him.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After nearly fainting, Jack engages in a conversation with a man named Ravelton (also referred to as Laventon or Raffles), who identifies himself as a "soul doctor" rather than a medical doctor. Jack expresses distress, claiming he is going insane because he hears voices in the morning that others cannot hear. Ravelton rejects the idea that Jack is mad, comparing his experience to seeing celestial bodies like Jupiter; he suggests that Jack may be a "signal receiver" capable of sensing phenomena that others cannot.

Ravelton analyzes the timing of the voices. By comparing Jack's watch to his own, Ravelton determines that the screams occur at 7:25 according to a consistent time standard. He concludes that the sound occupies a specific location and time at the villa, rather than being a mere psychological suggestion. Ravelton suggests that the phenomenon is governed by natural laws rather than the supernatural.

Later that evening, Ravelton shares his research regarding the history of Heather House. He explains that the villa was previously owned by the Turner couple, an English man and an exotic-looking woman of Russian descent. The Turners lived in isolation and suddenly departed early one morning, requesting that their property be sold via a letter sent to London. The villa subsequently passed to M. Maurier before being rented to a French professor and his daughter. Ravelton notes that while Mr. Turner was seen, no one saw Mrs. Turner leave. He posits that the influence of those who die tragically can be absorbed by an environment and transmitted to a suitable receiver, such as Jack.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a doctor suggests that certain events are driven by an invisible, mechanical force rather than mere coincidence, Jack conducts his own investigation but finds no new information. Later, Felice Malshaw, a young woman Jack refers to as the "violet girl," visits him at an abandoned hotel. She explains that she has been experiencing recurring nightmares for four nights involving a beautiful, fair-skinned woman in agony who attempts to hand her a blue Chinese porcelain jar. In the most recent dream, the woman screamed, "Murdered—help! Murdered!"—the exact words the doctor had spoken earlier.

Felice presents a dirty sheet of watercolor paper she found in a pantry, which depicts a tall, fair-skinned woman standing next to a table with a blue ceramic jar. Mr. Lavenham (also referred to as Lavenderton or Ralston) joins Jack and Felice to discuss the discovery. Jack recognizes the jar as a recent acquisition from his uncle, a famous collector of Chinese porcelain. Lavenham notes that the Turner couple left Heather House two months ago, suggesting the jar may have been purchased at an auction from their belongings.

Because Jack's uncle is currently traveling in Europe and cannot be reached, Lavenham proposes a plan: Jack will retrieve the porcelain jar, and they will stay at Heather House that night to confront the mystery. Lavenham suspects the jar might be double-layered with something hidden inside. The following evening, Jack retrieves the jar, though he finds no evidence of a secret compartment. Jack and Lavenham arrive at Heather House at eleven o'clock, where Felice meets them and leads them to a living room to drink coffee, warning them not to wake her sleeping father.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jack, Felicity, and Lavinton gather in a dark room to conduct a séance involving a blue porcelain jar. Lavinton instructs the group to enter a trance-like state and remove fear. During the session, Felicity expresses a sense of impending terror, and Jack experiences a suffocating sensation before losing consciousness.

Jack wakes up at 12:30 the following day lying in a shrubbery near a villa. Finding the villa empty, he returns to his hotel, where he encounters his uncle, George. George reveals he has arrived from Dover and is furious when Jack mentions the séance. George explains that the blue porcelain jar is a unique Ming Dynasty piece worth at least £10,000, belonging to his collection from an American millionaire named Hugenheim. Jack discovers a note from Lavinton, who identifies himself as "The Soul Doctor," stating that he, Felicity, and her sick father left twelve hours prior. Lavinton's note implies the supernatural experience was a deception using scientific language.

In a new narrative segment, psychologist Edward Castells receives a telegram from his friend Settle regarding the case of Sir Arthur Camichael. Settle explains that a twenty-three-year-old man, previously a normal member of the upper class, suddenly transformed into a "half-idiot" who cannot recognize anyone. As Castells and Settle travel toward Walden in a carriage, Settle suggests the issue is related to the house itself and hints at a terrifying, inexplicable presence within it. Castells, who admits to believing in the supernatural despite his profession, agrees to approach the case without prejudice.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Sattler informs Dr. Castles that Arthur inherited the Walden estate following the death of his father, Sir William. Arthur lives with his stepmother, the mysterious Lady Cammichael—who is of mixed British and Asian descent—and his eight-year-old half-brother. The estate is financially struggling. Arthur is engaged to Miss Felicity Patterson, who is currently staying at Walden.

Upon arriving at the house, Dr. Castles observes Miss Patterson walking across the lawn with a basket of roses and a gray Persian cat. Inside, Dr. Castles meets Lady Cammichael and Miss Patterson. Miss Patterson expresses distress over Arthur’s condition, claiming he is "not human anymore," while Dr. Castles suggests it may be amnesia or a secondary personality. During the conversation, Lady Cammichael reacts with hostility toward Miss Patterson and accidentally knocks over a teapot when Dr. Castles mentions the cat.

Sattler leads Dr. Castles and Miss Patterson upstairs to see Arthur. Arthur is found sitting by a window, behaving strangely. He observes the visitors stealthily but remains mostly motionless. When offered milk, Arthur approaches the table without using his hands, drinking directly from the cup in a manner Sattler describes as a "primitive state." This behavior leaves Miss Patterson trembling and convinced that the man is not Arthur.

Later, during dinner, Dr. Castles notes Lady Cammichael's mystical aura, though Sattler observes she is devoted to her son. After dinner, Dr. Castles hears a cat meowing outside and attempts to let it in, but the corridor is empty. The group stares at him intently following this occurrence. When Dr. Castles retires to his room, he tells Sattler the house seems normal, prompting Sattler to question if he truly finds the house charming.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After being disturbed by the sound of a meowing cat outside his door at night, Dr. Castles observes a gray cat creeping across the lawn the following morning. He notes the incredible sight of the cat moving through a flock of birds without frightening them. During breakfast, Dr. Castles mentions the cat to Mrs. Carmichael, who reacts with heavy breathing and an unfriendly tone, insisting she has never owned a cat.

Dr. Castles continues to examine the patient, noting that while the man appears blank and submissive, he is actually observing everything and shows a strong emotional dependence on his stepmother, Mrs. Carmichael, while ignoring Miss Patterson. Seeking clarity, Dr. Castles asks the butler about the cat. The butler reveals that Mrs. Carmichael once had a beautiful gray Persian cat that died about a week ago and was buried under a tree with purple leaves.

Dr. Castles discusses his findings with Settle, who admits to hearing the cat but not seeing it. Settle suggests the cat's voice sounds like a threat. During dinner in the green drawing room, the meowing returns, sounding angry and threatening, accompanied by the sound of fierce scratching on a copper door handle. When Settle opens the door, nothing is there. Mrs. Carmichael and Felicity Patterson are visibly terrified, while the patient, Arthur, remains calm against his stepmother's knee. Miss Patterson seeks reassurance from Dr. Castles, who believes the threat is not directed at her. Later that night, Dr. Castles is startled awake by a sound of something being fiercely torn and dragged.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After hearing a scratching sound, the narrator and Sethel discover a chair outside Mrs. Camichael's room has been torn into strips by cat claws. Suspecting Lady Camichael is in danger, the narrator attempts to warn her, but she dismisses the incident as a prank and refuses to reveal any information. To ensure her safety, the narrator and Sethel search her room and agree to patrol the path to the house in shifts.

During the night, the narrator has a dream of a gray cat leading them to a specific spot in a study. Upon investigating the study the next morning, the narrator finds the room matches the dream. They discover a missing book and a small scrap of paper caught on a nail that reads, "That cat..." Later, while sitting in the green sitting room, Arthur reacts with extreme, trembling terror to a mouse running across the floor, leading the narrator to a shocking, unstated realization.

That night, Sethel discovers Mrs. Camichael is locked in her room and hears fierce mewling and a piercing scream from Mrs. Carruthers. The narrator and Sethel break down the door to find Mrs. Camichael on her bed, bleeding from severe claw marks on her throat. After bandaging her, the narrator sends a telegram to a hospital nurse.
