The following morning, the narrator and Sethel dig under a sycamore tree and unearth the body of a cat that has been dead for a week. Sethel identifies the presence of hydrogen cyanide.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Castles and Settle discuss the hypnotic powers of Mrs. Carmichael, noting her Eastern ancestry and her motive to secure property for her son by rendering Arthur Carmichael an incurable idiot. Castles plans to have Mrs. Carmichael leave Walden on September 18th to escape a "terrifying specter."

On September 14th, a maid reports that Arthur fell into a pond after his flat-bottomed boat tipped over. Castles, Settle, and Felicity rush to the scene. After a search, they find Arthur's seemingly lifeless body on the shore. Castles performs CPR for an hour, and eventually, Arthur regains consciousness. Arthur is confused, believing the date is August 12th rather than September 14th. He recalls a terrifying dream where he turned into a cat, though the details escape him.

As they lead Arthur toward the house, Lady Carmichael appears in the hall. Upon seeing Arthur, she reacts with extreme terror and guilt, screams, and collapses. Castles determines she has died from shock, suggesting "one life for another." Castles informs Settle that Arthur was actually murdered with cyanide. 

While the public explanation will be that Arthur suffered amnesia and his wife died of a fit of madness, Castles and Settle hold secret knowledge. They note a torn chair in the hallway and discover that a missing book from the study was an ancient treatise on transforming humans into animals. Castles concludes that Felicity will keep the true secrets of the past weeks buried to protect Arthur.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
On a stormy December night, Seleras Hammel and Dick Borrow walk home from a dinner party hosted by psychiatrist Bernard Seldon. The two men represent opposing philosophies: Borrow is a lean, ascetic man driven by illusions and physical misery, while Hammel is a wealthy, self-satisfied materialist who rose from being a newsboy to seek the luxury money provides.

After parting ways at a metro station, Hammel walks alone in the cold. While waiting to cross a street, he witnesses a horrific accident where a bus strikes a man, leaving a pile of lifeless limbs in the road. Terrified by the suddenness of death, Hammel experiences an intense, uncontrollable fear. Seeking to escape his anxiety, he takes a shortcut through a narrow alley toward his home near the square.

In the dim alley, Hammel encounters a legless street performer playing a flute. The music is a strange, rising, and uplifting fragment that resembles a melody from "Rienzi." The experience is so profound that Hammel feels a ridiculous illusion of floating. He pursues the man to ask about the music. The performer is a young man with a beautiful face and a clear, non-English accent who claims to have come from "the other side of the sea" a very long time ago. When Hammel expresses sympathy for his lost legs, the man solemnly replies that his legs "were evil." Hammel gives him a shilling and returns home, unsettled by the encounter. As he lies in bed, he hears a faint, familiar sound that reminds him of the flute music, causing his heart to race.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hammerl experiences recurring, transcendental visions characterized by a joyful, ascending melody that carries him to a state of soaring flight. These experiences are followed by a violent, heavy pulling force that drags him back to reality, causing intense physical pain in his limbs and ears, along with a suffocating sense of oppression. To cope with this feeling of being trapped by walls and ceilings, Hammerl has moved to a sparsely furnished room at the top of his house to face the sky, yet he still feels the need for open countryside.

During a secret dinner, Hammerl confesses his fears of insanity to his friend Seldon, a psychiatrist. Hammerl explains that these phenomena began on a winter night following a dinner with Seldon. He describes his visions as evolving; he is developing non-physical senses that allow him to perceive light, color, and sound beyond human capability. He specifically mentions sensing the colors of magnificent wings, though he cannot describe the colors using earthly terms. Hammerl notes that he feels a constant, agonizing struggle between the "wings"—representing spiritual or transcendent states—and his worldly attachments to comfort, luxury, and pleasure, which seem to pull him in the opposite direction.

Seldon suggests several rational explanations, such as hypnosis, nervous instability, or dreams, but also acknowledges that some things remain inexplicable. He advises Hammerl to either leave London for the countryside or seek out a "cripple" whom Hammerl believes possesses supernatural powers. Hammerl refuses both options, stating he cannot afford to lose his luxuries and is afraid of the man. Seldon concludes that Hammerl must simply wait to see what happens next, though Hammerl expresses desperation regarding the internal battle he is fighting.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a conversation with Bernard Selden regarding the struggle between body and spirit, Sylas Hammel resolves to find a "cripple" whom Selden had suggested he seek. The next morning, Hammel discovers the man sitting in a dark alley, drawing lifelike landscapes with colored chalk on the pavement. When an agitated Hammel demands to know the man's identity, the man draws a picture of a figure with a beautiful face and goat legs playing a tubular instrument. The man tells Hammel that "they are evil," and Hammel is mesmerized by the face in the drawing, which he identifies as the face of Pan.

This encounter triggers a profound internal crisis. While sitting in a park, Hammel realizes that his love for wealth is the chain that binds him, preventing him from answering a "call of wings" that demands total freedom. To achieve this, he decides to sacrifice his most precious possession. Hammel travels to the East End of London to meet Boris (referred to as Bolo), a man he deems trustworthy. He informs Boris that he has spent two weeks finalizing legal documents to donate his entire fortune to the poor of East London, appointing Boris as the trustee.

After leaving Boris, Hammel experiences a sudden, painful sense of loss and fear regarding poverty, hunger, and cold. Despite his fear, he feels the pressure of his former life has been removed. Possessing only two pennies, he decides to take the metro to the park. As he descends the stairs to the metro platform, he feels a sense of evil in the heavy air and notices a man sitting alone on a chair, appearing drunk or unconscious.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
On an underground platform, a man loses his balance and falls toward an approaching train. Despite his intense fear of death, Hammel decides to sacrifice himself to save the man. He uses his remaining strength to pull the man back onto the platform, but Hammel falls onto the tracks himself. As he dies, he experiences a sense of freedom and hears the sound of Pan's flute and the flapping of wings.

Later, Lor Dobler, a thirty-two-year-old French engineer, crosses the Seine River to visit an apartment in the Caudinet. He is greeted by an elderly woman named Elise (also referred to as Eileen or Eleanor), who is a devout Catholic. Upon his arrival, Elise expresses her disapproval of the séances conducted by her mistress, Mademoiselle Simonne, claiming the practice is a deal with the devil that is making the lady pale, weak, and prone to headaches.

Raul (referred to as Lor Dobler earlier) attempts to soothe Elise, explaining that once he marries Simonne, the séances will cease. He defends Simonne’s character, describing her as the best medium in France and an honest person rather than a con artist. However, Elise remains fearful, suggesting that mediumship goes against nature and that the souls being contacted might not want to let Simonne go. The tension is interrupted by the arrival of Simonne, a tall, blonde, and elegant woman. Raul greets her affectionately, noting her extreme paleness and questioning her health.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Simonetta, a medium, expresses deep fear and exhaustion to Raul (also referred to as Laurie or Luca) while sitting on a couch. She describes a persistent feeling that something terrible will happen to her and speaks of the terrifying "darkness of nothingness" she experiences during her work. Raul attempts to comfort her, attributing her distress to the pressures of being a medium and suggesting she needs rest. He presents her with letters from Professor Loche and Dr. Gurney, who both request her services, but Simonetta insists she will never summon spirits again due to a sense of great danger. Raul promises her that she will never have to summon spirits again, though he notes that her work has been a noble contribution to science.

Simonetta expresses particular dread regarding an upcoming séance with Madame Ix (also referred to as Madame Exeche), a woman who has lost her only child. Simonetta finds the woman odd and intimidating, noting her tall, dark appearance and large, man-like hands. Raul reproaches Simonetta for her lack of sympathy toward a grieving mother, defending the success of previous séances, such as the one involving the spirit of "Little Emily." Simonetta remains anxious, noting her physical exhaustion and her fear that her work is a "deal with the devil." As she waits for the late Madame Ix, Simonetta's nervousness causes her to break a porcelain bottle. Despite her desire to cancel, Raul reminds her of the importance of the séance, emphasizing that the client is a mother seeking to see her child one last time.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Simonne, a medium, expresses her fear regarding the concept of "mother" to Raoul, noting that maternal love is a primal force capable of destruction. After Simonne rests, Raoul prepares a room for a séance, which Elise arranges with a tambourine, a horn, and writing materials. An old servant announces the arrival of Madame Iex, a tall, melancholy woman. 

Raoul attempts to manage Madame Iex's expectations by explaining the phenomena scientifically, claiming that "ghosts" are actually compressed gases from the medium's own body and warning that touching them could kill the medium. Despite Raoul's skepticism, Madame Iex is eager to see her deceased daughter, Emily. Simonne joins them, appearing pale and exhausted, but insists on proceeding with the séance.

To ensure there is no conspiracy, Madame Iex produces a rope from her handbag and methodically ties Raoul to a chair. She then locks the hallway door and removes the key. As the séance begins, Simonne’s breathing becomes heavy and eventually transitions into a moan. Suddenly, cymbals and bells sound, and a horn is thrown to the ground. A mist of vapor escapes Simonne's mouth and condenses into the figure of a small child. Madame Iex reacts with intense emotion as the figure of Emily appears, calling out "Mommy!" and stepping through the curtain toward her. Raoul watches in amazement, perceiving the manifestation as a real, living child.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a room where Raul is bound by Madame de Winter, Mrs. Exe attempts to reach a child. Raul, terrified and helpless, commands her to sit down to protect the medium, but Madame de Winter ignores him, claiming the child is her own flesh and blood. Ayesha snatches the child into her arms, followed by an anguished wail and a crunching sound from behind a curtain. Madame Exe runs downstairs to unlock a door. Raul manages to break free from his ropes just as Iris arrives. Together, they pull back the curtain to find a scene of blood; Madame is dead and appears to have shrunk to half her original size. Elise questions the cause of this phenomenon, but Raul, distraught, can only scream in confusion.

In a different setting, the Dinsmoor family prepares a dinner of eggs, beef, bread, and cheese. Mr. Dinsmoor, a burly man, maintains a dismissive attitude toward his daughter Margaret’s fears of ghosts, asserting that miracles do not happen. During the meal, which includes his wife and children Johnny and Margaret, a sudden knock at the door interrupts them.

Mortimer Cleveland, a researcher in psychosciences and parapsychology, has become stranded in the Wiltshire hills after his car tires were punctured in a storm. Seeking refuge, he follows flickering lights to a small house. Upon knocking, he hears whispers inside before a fifteen-year-old boy opens the door. Cleveland observes the family inside, noting the robust father, the pale, frightened mother, and a surprised girl at the table.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mortimer Cleveland, seeking shelter from the weather, is admitted into the home of the Dinsmoor family. The household consists of Mr. Dinsmoor, a talkative man in the construction industry; his wife; their two daughters, Charlotte and Margarete; and a boy named Johnny. Cleveland observes the distinct beauties of the sisters: Charlotte has golden-red hair and gray eyes, while Margarete possesses a dark-skinned, severe, and intimidating beauty. Despite Mr. Dinsmoor’s friendly and rambling demeanor, Cleveland senses an underlying unease and fear emanating from the family members.

After being granted permission to stay overnight, Cleveland changes into borrowed pink pajamas. While in his room, he discovers the letters "SOS" written in the dust on a cherrywood bedside table. He suspects one of the daughters wrote the distress signal, specifically noting that both girls had been near the table.

The following morning, Cleveland finds Charlotte in the garden. He attempts to test her by drawing "SOS" in the sand and asking if she knows its meaning. Initially, Charlotte denies writing the message, explaining she only knows it as a signal used by ships in distress. However, as they walk back toward the house, she suddenly whispers to him that while she did not write the specific words he asked about, she might have done something "earlier."

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Charlotte Dinsmoor expresses her fear to Mortimer on a garden path, noting that since moving into their house, her family members—her mother, father, and Maggie—have all changed. She mentions the house is considered haunted because a man killed his wife there long ago. Mortimer professionally suggests that Charlotte might be a sensitive person acting as a subconscious medium; he proposes that she wrote "SOS" the previous night by reenacting the actions of the former victim. Charlotte leaves to join her family, leaving Mortimer to reflect on whether his sudden visit caused panic in the household, noting that Johnny is the only one who did not seem afraid.

During breakfast, Mortimer observes that Johnny has stained fingers, which Johnny attributes to his interest in chemistry research, a pursuit that angers his father, who wants him to enter the building trade. Mr. Dinsmoor appears jovial but harbors animosity toward Mortimer, while Mrs. Dinsmoor greets him coldly and appears afraid of him. Maggie asks Mortimer if he slept well, appearing disappointed by his polite response. When Mortimer brings up Johnny's interest in chemistry, Mrs. Dinsmoor drops her teacup in a sudden crash.

Later, while walking in the garden, Mortimer overhears Mr. Dinsmoor discussing a fortune of nearly six thousand pounds mentioned by a lawyer. This discovery regarding money makes the situation feel clearer and more "ugly" to Mortimer. Although Mr. Dinsmoor greets him warmly, Mortimer feels a desire to leave the house and thanks him for his hospitality.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After observing that Madgeleine and Charlotte do not look alike, Mortimer Cleveland confronts Mr. Dinsmoor, deducing that one of the girls is adopted. Mr. Dinsmoor admits that Charlotte is an orphan they adopted, but claims he recently discovered through a newspaper advertisement and a lawyer that Madgeleine is actually the biological daughter of a wealthy Jewish man who intended to leave her a large inheritance. Mortimer finds the story plausible but remains suspicious. During his farewell, Mortimer notices Mrs. Dinsmoor acting fearful and sees her holding a miniature portrait of Charlotte from twenty-five years ago.

As Mortimer leaves, Madgeleine intercepts him, revealing she wrote "SOS" on a table the previous night. She expresses a deep, inexplicable sense of dread regarding her family, noting that her parents and Charlotte have all changed for the worse, while her brother Johnny remains unaffected because he did not drink the tea. Mortimer realizes the family may be being poisoned. He recalls a news report about arsenic accidentally contaminating food and connects it to the family's behavior.

Mortimer returns that evening during dinner. He intercepts the tea service and uses test tubes to demonstrate his theory. He accuses Mr. Dinsmoor of intentionally placing arsenic in the bottom of Charlotte's teacup to make her death appear to be an accident caused by contaminated canned meat or tea, thereby ensuring the biological daughter, Madgeleine, inherits the fortune. Mortimer explains that he deduced the motive after realizing the girls' identities were swapped: Charlotte is the adopted child, and the plan was to eliminate her to prevent anyone from discovering the truth about Madgeleine's lineage. Madgeleine pulls Mortimer aside, and Mortimer tells her he will keep the evidence to protect Charlotte.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During the end of the war in Egypt, Charles met Sophia Leontidis, a highly efficient twenty-two-year-old manager at a Foreign Ministry outpost. As they became close friends, Charles realized he loved her and intended to marry her upon his return to England. During a dinner at the Shepherd's restaurant, Charles expressed his intentions but declined to propose immediately, wishing to allow Sophia the freedom to assess the post-war situation independently. Sophia agreed, noting that Charles did not truly know her. She revealed she lived in a "weird little cottage" in Swindon with an extended family, including her grandfather, Aristide Leonidis, a wealthy Greek man from Smyrna.

Two years later, Charles returned to England. After landing, he read an obituary in *The Times* announcing the sudden death of Aristide Leonidis at his home in Swinley. Charles found the repetitive nature of the obituary announcements strange. He sent a telegram to Sophia expressing his condolences, and she replied that they would proceed with their planned dinner at Mario's restaurant. When they met, Sophia was dressed in prominent black mourning attire. Although they initially bypassed their awkwardness by reminiscing about their time in Cairo, Charles felt a growing sense of unease, sensing that something was not right regarding the circumstances of her grandfather's death or Sophia's calm demeanor.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a dinner at a restaurant, Charles proposes marriage to Sophia. Sophia hesitates, explaining that her grandfather’s death has complicated their future. She reveals her suspicion that her eighty-five-year-old grandfather did not die of natural causes but was murdered, a suspicion shared by a doctor who refused to issue a death certificate. Sophia notes that because Charles is a diplomat, her family's sensitive situation could impact their marriage. She warns Charles that they are being watched, identifying a man resembling a retired soldier in a blue check suit who followed her from Stewley Station. To meet Charles, Sophia had to climb out of her bathroom window and down a drainpipe. She urges Charles to seek an unbiased perspective by asking his father, an assistant to the Commissioner at Scotland Yard.

Upon returning home, Charles visits his father in his study. Charles reveals that he has met Sophia and describes the man following her. His father expresses concern, noting that while the Leonidis family is of noble lineage, the current circumstances are problematic. His father cryptically suggests that Charles should only marry her if the person who killed the grandfather is "the one." The passage ends with his father questioning how much Charles actually knows about the situation.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Charles and his father discuss a girl's refusal to provide information, noting she preferred he see things from an outsider's perspective. Inspector Taverner arrives to discuss the investigation into the Leonidis family. The father explains that Aristide Leonidis, a Greek immigrant, became a shrewd and charming businessman who built a catering empire by exploiting legal loopholes. Aristide married a country squire's daughter for love, and they lived in a house in Swinley. Of their eight children, only two survived: Roger, who is childless, and Philip, who lives at Mounted Gables with his wife, his three children (Eustace, Josephine, and Sophia), and his sister-in-law, Miss Harvillan.

Taverner confirms that Aristide was murdered via poisoning, likely with aconitine, which is a component of his own eyedrops. The father reveals that ten years ago, the seventy-five-year-old Aristide remarried a twenty-four-year-old woman from a teahouse. Taverner notes that this second wife has a young lover who serves as the tutor to Leonidis' grandsons. The investigation suggests the wife injected Aristide with a bottle containing poison instead of his required insulin. However, Taverner remains suspicious because the wife did not replace the poisoned bottle with a legitimate one, which would have made the crime much harder to detect. The presence of both full and empty insulin bottles in the house makes the trace of the specific poisoned bottle a key piece of evidence.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Charles and his father discuss the investigation into the death of Aristide Leonidis. His father explains that anyone in the house could have tampered with the insulin bottles, which were stored unlocked in a bathroom medicine cabinet. While the motive is likely financial, the young widow, Brenda, and her lover lack significant wealth. Charles recalls a nursery rhyme about a "queer-shaped house" and asks Inspector Taverna for his impression of Mrs. Leonidis; Taverna describes her as an elusive person who enjoys comfort, comparing her to a cat.

To solve the case, Charles's father insists that Charles must get inside information by getting to know the residents. Charles initially protests the idea of acting as an undercover agent against Sophia, the woman he loves. However, his father argues that unless the case is cleared of suspicion, Sophia will never be able to marry Charles. He advises Charles to be honest with Sophia about his intentions.

The following day, Charles travels to the Mansard House in Swinley with Inspector Taverna and Constable Lamb. He observes the unusual, distorted architecture of the eleven-sided manor, which resembles a magnified farmhouse. Upon arrival, Sophia meets Charles and leads him to a neglected rock garden. Charles explains his father's plan, and Sophia agrees that she needs to know the truth to find peace, admitting she was scared the previous night. While the police suspect Brenda, Charles confesses to Sophia that he does not believe she is the killer.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Sophia explains to Charles Haywood the internal dynamics of her "strange" and "cold-blooded" family. She defends Brenda Leoni, describing her as a proper lady who was likely attracted to Sophia's grandfather, Aristide, due to his charming and self-confident nature. Sophia characterizes her family members by various forms of coldness: her grandfather was a man who once stabbed two men in Smyrna without remorse; her grandmother was an arrogant woman who took responsibility for matters of life and death; her mother is a self-centered actress; Clementine, Roger's wife, is a ruthless scientist; Roger's uncle is kind but temperamental; and Sophia's father is excessively reserved. Sophia expresses a desire to quickly uncover the truth regarding Aristide's death.

Aunt Edith de Havilland, a woman in her seventies with a sharp gaze, joins them. She recognizes Charles and mentions his father. As they walk toward the house, Edith expresses her disdain for the police and discusses the reliability of old-fashioned maids. She reflects on the strangeness of Aristide being poisoned, noting that while she did not like him, his death leaves the house feeling empty. Edith reveals she has lived at the estate for forty years, having arrived after her sister Muriel married a "short, ugly foreigner." She explains that she stayed because of her love for the garden and because Philip Leonidis, a writer of obscure historical books, kept her there after his actress wife left him.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss de Havilland explains to Charles Haywood that Aristide Leonidis was so wealthy that his family members, including Roger and Sophia, were financially independent and had no immediate motive to kill him for inheritance. She suggests that Brenda, Aristide's much younger wife, might have poisoned him because she grew tired of waiting for him to die, though she notes that Aristide was a strong man who did not show signs of declining health. Charles observes Miss de Havilland's calculating demeanor and considers whether she herself might be a suspect.

The narrative shifts to a landmark building in London, where Miss de Havilland leads Charles into her brother-in-law Philip’s home. After passing through an austere hall and a blue-paneled drawing room decorated with stage photographs and a Degas painting, they enter a cold, book-filled library. There, Charles meets Philip Leonidis, a handsome, calm man around fifty who does not smoke. Philip remains listless and unemotional regarding the murder.

The meeting is interrupted by the arrival of Margda Leonidis, who enters the library wearing a peach satin nightgown and smoking a cigarette. She speaks rapidly and loudly, expressing anxiety about the public attention the murder is bringing and discussing her desire for freedom and potential acting roles inspired by the tragedy. During her frantic monologue, she drops a cigarette butt onto Philip's mahogany desk, which he removes without comment. Margda concludes by feigning a terrified expression, claiming the situation is too terrible to think about.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a brief moment of terror, Margarita Leonidis shifts into a business-like demeanor, discussing her performance of "Edith Thompson" with the narrator. Margarita suggests pointing out certain details to the inspector, but her husband, Philip Leonides, insists he will handle the communication. Sophia interrupts, instructing her mother to adopt a specific persona for the police: appearing pitiful, saying very little, and acting to protect the family. Sophia declares herself the "producer" of this performance.

Inspector Taverner arrives at the library to interview Philip. He informs Philip that his father did not die of natural causes but was killed by an overdose of belladonna (atropine), likely because the victim mistook eye drops for insulin. Taverner notes the suspicious absence of fingerprints on the empty eyedrop bottle found in the trash. Philip suggests the servant, Johnson, might be the culprit, but Taverner dismisses this, noting Johnson's loyalty and the substantial annual bonuses the deceased provided him.

During the interrogation, Philip states he spent the day in the library and only saw his father after breakfast, when Margarita was also present. Philip explains that Roger informed him of his father's sudden worsening condition. Philip called a doctor, but his father died before the doctor arrived. Regarding the family's finances, Philip explains that while his father had made the children financially independent through various assets and companies, the deceased remained a very wealthy man. Philip denies that his move to the house was due to financial difficulties, claiming it was for personal reasons and convenience.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Taverner interrogates Mr. Leonidis regarding his father’s will and family dynamics. Mr. Leonidis explains that his father made a new will in 1945, leaving his stepmother £100,000 and dividing the remainder into three equal parts for himself, his brother, and his brother's three grandchildren. He denies having a financial motive for his father's death, noting that his father provides for him and his wife. Regarding his father's marriage, Mr. Leonidis describes the couple as happy but admits he considers the marriage unwise. He also clarifies that Lawrence Brown is a private tutor for his children, Philip and Josephine, and lives on the other side of the house due to available space. He denies any knowledge of an intimate relationship between Mr. Brown and his stepmother, Brenda.

Taverner then interviews Margaret West (Mrs. Philip), a former actress with a reputation for being independent and a prankster. Margaret states that at the time of the tragedy, she was in London attending a fashion show and having drinks. She claims to have loved her husband and notes that she and Brenda do not see each other often because they have little in common. When Taverner asks about a potential romantic relationship between Brenda and Lawrence Brown, Margaret defends Brenda’s character and states she has seen no such signs, asserting that she only recognizes Brenda as her husband's wife. The Inspector concludes the interview and moves to discuss the servants.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Sophia encourages her mother, Magda Leonidis, regarding her performance, Sophia signals for the narrator to follow Inspector Taverna out of the room. The narrator asks Taverna about their specific identity and purpose in the house. Taverna advises the narrator not to explain themselves, noting that in such an unstable house, people will likely take their presence for granted. Taverna explains that his questioning is a tactic to assess the inhabitants and gather clues, adding that Margarita Leonidis will reveal much if she is not interrupted, though her credibility is low. Taverna's primary goal is to determine the motive for the cyanide poisoning.

The pair encounters a clumsy, large man who invites them into the living room and leaves to find cigarettes. In the living room, they meet Clementine Leonidis, the wife of Roger Leonidis. Clementine is described as a sharp, alert, and distinctive woman around fifty years old. When Taverna reveals the cause of death was cyanide poisoning, Clementine acknowledges it as murder. She notes that while she has a good relationship with her father-in-law, she dislikes his life goals and methods. She expresses uncertainty regarding a potential affair between Brenda Leonidis and Lawrence Brown.

Roger Leonidis returns after a phone call and reacts with intense anger, accusing a woman of being cold-blooded and suggesting she murdered his father. Roger expresses deep guilt over disappointing his father and declares a desire to strangle the woman responsible. Clementine reprimands him for his outburst, and Roger leaves the room in embarrassment. Clementine dismisses Roger's capability for violence, stating he cannot even swat a fly. During questioning, Clementine provides her whereabouts for the day of the death: Roger was at the London Boxes House catering company before returning home, and Clementine was working at the Lembert Institute on Gour Road until six o'clock.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation, Clemens explains to Taverner that she studies radioactive reactions related to nuclear fission for medical experiments, rather than destructive atomic bomb projects. Taverner then visits the home of Roger and Cleopatra Leonidis. The house is characterized by simple, hospital-like rooms until they enter the private room of Clemens's late husband. This room is personalized with a writing desk, old pipes, cigarette butts, and various photographs and watercolor sketches.

Roger, appearing to lose control of his emotions, expresses intense admiration for his wife, Cleopatra. He recounts how she devotedly cared for her first husband, a crystallographer with tuberculosis, until his death. Roger also describes the circumstances of the old man's death, noting that he had been drinking tea with him just thirty minutes before Brenda arrived to report a sudden attack. Taverner suggests they should keep an eye on Brenda, though finding evidence will be difficult.

The investigators then move to a large living room where they observe two significant portraits: a piercing, vital painting of a short old man by Augustus John, and a sarcastic, lifeless portrait of his first wife that resembles a horse's face. Detective Inspector Lamb joins Taverner after failing to get information from the servants. Finally, the second wife of Aristide Leonidis enters the room wearing expensive black mourning clothes and jewelry. Despite her beauty, she appears frightened and has been crying. When Taverner suggests she should have a lawyer present, she dismisses the idea, stating she does not like Mr. Gutschel.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Taverner informs Brenda Leonidis that her husband died from icodrine poisoning after someone deliberately switched his insulin for eye drops. Brenda denies any involvement, claiming she only gave him the usual dose before rushing to find Roger. She suggests a servant might be responsible.

Taverner, accompanied by the narrator, the Chief Constable, and Detective Inspector Lamb, moves to a room overlooking the garden to interview Lawrence Brown, a tutor who was studying Latin with Eustace. Upon their arrival, Lawrence appears visibly terrified. Eustace, the student, leaves the room after making a slicing motion across his throat toward the narrator. During the interrogation, Taverner suggests a possible motive involving a relationship between Lawrence and Brenda. Lawrence vehemently denies any such affair or murderous intent, citing his religious beliefs and his sensitive nature. He eventually flees the room in distress.

Following the interview, the investigators discuss potential suspects. Detective Inspector Lamb suggests the crime could be a form of euthanasia or a way for Lawrence to marry into Brenda's wealth, which includes significant assets and sapphires. Taverner expresses suspicion toward Brenda, noting her failure to dispose of the insulin bottle, though he acknowledges a lack of concrete evidence. He also notes a housekeeper's observation that the couple seemed close.

The narrator returns to Brenda to gather impressions. Brenda expresses intense resentment toward the family, calling them "beasts" and claiming they were mean to her. She defends her love for her husband, stating she loved him very much despite his age.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Brenda tells the narrator about her late husband, Aristide, whom she describes as a clever man who provided her with a home and happiness. She recounts their meeting at the Three-Leaf restaurant, where Aristide comforted her while she worked as a servant. She explains that she eventually discovered he was the wealthy "Leonidis Mr.," owning numerous businesses, and they married in a small London church. Brenda expresses her sadness over his death and denies killing him, claiming that others, including Roger and Philip, have treated her poorly. She notes that the family and Miss de Harvillan remained in Aristide's house despite their marriage.

Brenda also discusses Lawrence Brown, describing him as a sensitive and fragile man whom she tries to encourage. She expresses fear regarding Josephine, whom she finds odd and sneaky. When the narrator suggests that people might suspect Brenda and Lawrence of conspiring to poison Aristide due to a potential romantic motive, Brenda vehemently denies any such relationship.

Later, the narrator speaks with Sophia, expressing pity for Brenda. Sophia remains unsympathetic, arguing that Brenda is manipulative and that the family has no obligation to be kind to her. Sophia asserts that "Grandfather" was not deceived by Brenda but was instead orchestrating the situation. According to Sophia, Grandfather's plan involved the marriage and the hiring of Lawrence Brown as a tutor to provide Brenda with a controlled, "melancholic" romance to prevent her from engaging in outside scandals.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Sophia and Charles debate the motives behind the murder of Sophia's grandfather. Sophia argues that Brenda did not plot the murder, as the grandfather would have detected any conspiracy involving a tutor. She suggests Brenda is merely a troublemaker who has caused a rift between the siblings. Charles defends Brenda, noting her fear, while Sophia dismisses the attractiveness of Lawrence, whom Charles finds weak. During their argument, Charles asks why the household lacks traditional uniformed servants. Sophia explains that the grandfather prefers to hire specific staff, such as a cook, butler, and valet, and notes that previous attempts to employ servants were disrupted by Charles's mother.

Following the argument, Charles reflects on the conflicting perspectives of the Leontidis family and Brenda. He recognizes the family's resentment toward Brenda as an intruder but feels sympathy for her desire for wealth and comfort. After falling asleep in the living room of Magda Leonidis, Charles wakes to find Josephine, a young girl with a striking, "monster-like" appearance, watching him. Josephine reveals she is acting as a detective alongside Eustace, gathering evidence regarding the grandfather's poisoning by i-sec-ri-na. She mentions her family's plan to move to a house by the Thames in London following her father's financial losses related to a play titled "Red Tears on the Silk Dress." Josephine discusses the play's failure and its departure from the biblical story of Jezebel, specifically noting the ending where the character is thrown from a window.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Josephine discusses her grandfather's death and her desire to study ballet, noting that her grandfather had strongly opposed her dream. She reveals that her Uncle Roger and Aunt Clementine are planning a secret departure from the country on Tuesday. Josephine suspects Roger's departure is linked to his business in London and believes he may have embezzled money, citing an incident where Roger expressed deep disappointment to his father on the day he died.

Josephine, who admits to eavesdropping to find the truth, claims to have read love letters between Brenda and Lawrence. She expresses her intention to act as a detective and solve her grandfather's murder. Her conversation is interrupted by the arrival of Mr. Gatschil, the grandfather's lawyer, and Inspector Taverner.

A dispute arises regarding the location of the grandfather's will. Magda Leontidis suspects an "evil woman upstairs" has destroyed it, as she believes the woman would inherit everything without it. Mr. Gatschil initially suggests the will was placed in a safe, but Inspector Taverner reports that the bank has no record of any such documents, only securities. Philip suggests Roger or Edith might know the location, but Roger denies involvement, claiming his father intended to send the will to Mr. Gaston. Mr. Gaston confirms he drafted the final will in November of the previous year, which the grandfather had approved and signed, intending to send it to the bank. Roger adds that the grandfather had previously read the will aloud to the family.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation, Inspector Taverner questions the Leonidis family regarding the circumstances surrounding a will. Roger Leonidis explains that the will provided for Aunt Edith and Aunt Brenda, and divided the remaining inheritance among Roger, Philip, Sophia, and Sophia's children, Ulysses and Josephine. Clementine Leonidis testifies that the testator, Mr. Leonidis, signed the will in the presence of two servants, Johnson and Janet Walmer, who also signed as witnesses. The family maintains that the testator remained at his desk throughout the process and that a stack of papers covered the document, preventing the servants from seeing its contents.

The investigation takes a dramatic turn when Taverner presents a document found in Mr. Leonidis's safe. The lawyer, Mr. Gaskill, is shocked to discover that this document—which he identifies as the original will he prepared—is unsigned. This contradicts the family's testimony that they witnessed the signing. Philip notes that his father had glaucoma and wore high-prescription glasses while signing. Mr. Gaskill observes a specific mark on the document, resembling an airplane, and concludes that the document is not a draft but the original intended will. Taverner suggests that the signed will was switched for this unsigned version.

Following the confrontation, the group disperses. The narrator follows Taverner to the Scotland Yard assistant office, where Taverner expresses frustration over the lack of suspicious evidence regarding the family and a young tutor. The narrator encourages Taverner, claiming to have more leads.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Charles informs Taverner and the father that Roger Leonides was planning to flee abroad following a major argument with his father on the day the old man died. Based on Josephine's testimony, the group considers whether Roger embezzled company funds. Taverner suggests a theory where Roger or his wife, Clementine, switched the old man's insulin with a high concentration of isoleucin. Charles expresses a belief that Clementine is the likely perpetrator, noting her calm and capable nature, and suggests she might have acted ruthlessly to protect her husband.

The following day, Charles meets with his father and Taverner at Scotland Yard. Taverner reveals that while Roger has not committed fraud, his poor business judgment has left the family catering company on the verge of bankruptcy. To save the company, Roger needs a massive sum of money by the following Wednesday, which he could potentially secure by using his father's will to apply for a loan. It is deduced that Roger may have killed his father to ensure he could inherit the funds necessary to prevent insolvency, especially after Aristides Leonides likely refused to invest in the failing business.

The father prepares to interrogate Roger, even hiring a stenographer for the session. When Roger Leonides arrives, appearing clumsy and hurried, Charles observes his demeanor and concludes that while Roger may be involved, Clementine is the one who actually executed the poisoning. Roger enters the room talking incessantly, asking for news regarding the investigation.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an interrogation, the narrator's father and Inspector Taverner question Roger Leonidis regarding his final conversation with his deceased father, Aristide Leonidis. While Roger previously claimed their meeting was casual, the police confront him about undisclosed details. Roger admits that his catering company is facing inevitable bankruptcy and that he had planned to flee the country with his wife, Clementine, to start a new life elsewhere to avoid the shame of failure.

Roger explains that Aristide discovered the financial situation and, rather than being angry, insisted on helping to save the business. To prove this, Roger produces a crumpled letter addressed to the stockbrokers Messrs. Grethelux and Hambury. The letter, which the father inspects, contains instructions from Aristide to sell stocks to provide funds for the catering company. After Taverner takes the letter as evidence, Roger clarifies that after their meeting, he returned to his quarters to tell Clementine about his father's advice. He notes that Brenda alerted him to his father's strange condition about thirty to sixty minutes later.

When asked if he had entered the bathroom connected to Aristide's bedroom, Roger denies it. Following Roger's departure, the father suggests that the letter might be a forgery intended by Aristide to protect Roger from legal or inheritance complications. However, the father concludes that Roger and Clementine lack a motive to kill Aristide, as the old man's death would actually complicate Roger's ability to access funds and manage the company's closure.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a discussion with Taverna, the father and his son, Charles, analyze the death of Aristedes Leonides. The father notes that Aristedes died dramatically within an hour of a specific conversation, which prevented Roger Leonides from developing greater suspicions. Taverna suggests that Brenda Leonidis is the primary beneficiary of the lost will; if the new will is not signed, the old will remains effective, and if Aristedes died without a will, his widow would inherit most of the estate. The father suspects trickery is involved but cannot determine the method.

Charles asks his father for a profile of what a murderer looks like, as the case has become a dominant force in his life. The father explains that murderers often appear to be good people, such as Roger Leonides, and are driven by obsession, jealousy, or a sense of necessity rather than simple hatred. He posits that victims are often people the killer loves. He identifies "arrogance" as a universal trait of murderers, noting that their vanity often leads them to boast or discuss the case excessively to alleviate the isolation of their crime.

The father advises Charles to mingle with the family and listen for unintentional slips of the tongue or self-revelations. He discusses the potential for inherited traits, noting the Leonidis family's arrogance. Finally, the father warns Charles to be careful with "that child," leading Charles to ask if he should keep his intentions hidden from Josephine.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Taverna advises Charles to monitor Josephine, noting that she possesses dangerous knowledge regarding a killer in the house. Taverna suggests that because children often reveal information when they want to show off, Charles should avoid direct questioning and instead pretend to be ignorant to elicit more details.

Charles returns to the house, which he privately calls the "house of deformities." Although he has shared Josephine's suspicions about Roger with Taverna, he intentionally withholds information regarding love letters exchanged between Brenda and Lawrence Brown. Charles feels sympathy for Brenda, who is facing hostility from her family, and he chooses to believe her denials over Josephine's perceived cunning.

Upon arriving at the house, Charles finds Josephine sitting by a koi pond eating an apple. Josephine expresses anger toward Charles for involving the police in matters regarding Roger's uncle. She reveals her awareness of upcoming family discussions involving her parents, Roger, and Aunt Edith. Josephine shares details about Aunt Edith’s inheritance and a story regarding Edith's lover, a sailor named Evans, who stabbed Edith's husband.

When Charles attempts to press Josephine for the identity of the killer, she refuses to cooperate, comparing Charles to Watson. Charles attempts to bargain by mentioning the love letters, but Josephine claims the letters are a fabrication she created for amusement. Despite Charles offering to find an expert from the British Museum to explain a biblical reference regarding Jezebel, Josephine refuses to reveal her leads. Charles eventually accepts defeat and insists that Josephine accompany him inside to find Sophia.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Charles enters the house accompanied by Josephine, driven by a sense of urgency following the sound of a breaking branch. In the kitchen, he meets a fat, elderly nanny who provides him with tea and cookies. The nanny mentions that Josephine had been hiding and scribbling in a notebook, noting that the master had insisted on keeping her at home despite Miss Edith's wishes. When Charles asks about Josephine's whereabouts, the nanny uses the past tense regarding Mr. Phillip, later clarifying she meant the "old Leonidis."

Sophia joins them, expressing her terror and her desire to uncover the truth behind the recent death. She admits she feels as though she is dealing with a "cold, calculating poisoner." Charles questions her about who knew that the Iserlin eye drops could be lethal. Sophia explains that the method of murder was revealed by old Leonidis himself during a family gathering; he had joked that if Brenda accidentally injected the eye drops as insulin, he would die because of his heart condition. This realization leads Charles to conclude that the killer did not need a complex scheme, as the victim had provided the method. Charles believes Brenda is unlikely to be the killer, though Sophia remains skeptical.

The nanny dismisses Sophia's fears of a killer, suggesting instead that an intruder might have entered through the unlocked doors, potentially a Communist or a Catholic. Sophia then leads Charles to the living room for an early family meeting. She expresses suspicion toward Clementine, though she notes Clementine's fascination with the proletariat. Upon entering the living room, they find the Leonidis family: Philip sits expressionless in a red velvet armchair, Roger sits by the fireplace looking disheveled and agitated, and Clementine sits nearby, avoiding eye contact with the others.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a drawing room, Edith de Harvilland knits while Margaret and Eustace sit on a sofa. When Charles arrives, Philip expresses disapproval of his presence, but Sophie defends Charles, announcing that she and Sophie are getting married and want him to stay. Roger supports Charles's presence, noting that the truth will soon be known. Margaret asks several questions regarding Scotland Yard, prompting Sophie to criticize her for making the discussion feel like a mystery play.

The conversation shifts to the inheritance and Aristide's will. Edith offers to leave her portion of the inheritance to Roger once matters are cleared up, but Roger vehemently refuses any money. Philip suggests he can provide funds, but notes that stocks are falling and certain funds, including Margaret's property, are untouchable. Eustace raises the possibility of bankruptcy, a sentiment Roger and Clementine seemingly accept. Roger becomes angry, accusing the others of discussing money immediately after their father's death. Philip argues that the company's fate is tied to the family's reputation, while Roger insists his problems are personal.

After Edith ends the discussion, the group disperses. Clementine expresses her dislike for the drawing room, describing it as a "stage" for Margaret's performance. Clementine tells Charles that the family meeting was prearranged and lacked genuine communication. She argues that the family is too loyal to the late Aristide and that children should be independent. She explains that Roger has suffered for years because he lacks the business ability to live up to his father's legacy, despite his attempts to manage the banquet company. Clementine concludes by noting that Charles's previous hint to the police that Roger killed his father for money was ridiculous.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Roger realizes his company is facing inevitable bankruptcy, which brings him relief because he wishes to start a new life with Clementine Leonidis. Clementine reveals her plan to move to Barbados to live a simple, carefree life using a small inheritance from a deceased relative. She expresses her indifference toward money, noting that she was happiest when she was poor with her late husband, Richard. When the narrator suggests using money for research, Clementine dismisses the idea, claiming donations are often wasted by the wrong people. Regarding the ongoing investigation, Clementine avoids speculation but identifies Brenda Leontidis and Lawrence Brown as the recognized suspects.

Edith de Havilland approaches the narrator to discuss the family dynamics. She describes Philip as a cold, polite man who is secretly jealous of Roger and appears to be secretly pleased by Roger's failures. Edith notes that Philip failed to help Roger during his crisis. She also characterizes Clementine as an incomprehensible person who prefers an uncomfortable lifestyle. Edith mentions her care for Macey's children—Sophia, Eustace, and Josephine—but warns of the family's tradition of "blind worship."

Later, the narrator and Sophia observe Brenda Leontidis and Lawrence Brown approaching the house through the garden like "elusive spirits." Sophia expresses concern for Eustace, noting his moody and unpredictable behavior following a bout with polio, which she attributes to his parents' lack of involvement. Sophia concludes that her parents are strange people; her father is preoccupied with unverified history, while her mother creates stage personas and seeks to stir up drama.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Sophia and the narrator discuss the interconnected and emotionally tangled nature of her family, which she compares to a climbing plant. Sophia notes that while they are not dishonest, they lack independence and are deeply interdependent. 

Margaret enters the room and discusses family dynamics, mentioning Eustace’s fury and Eudora’s generosity in leaving an inheritance to Roger. Margaret disputes the narrator's suggestion that Eudora hated old Leontidis, asserting instead that Eudora loved him despite past conflicts regarding her sister's marriage. Margaret also notes that Edith was particularly opposed to Leontidis marrying Brenda. Margaret then announces her sudden decision to send Josephine to school in Switzerland to protect her from the current family mess, intending to contact Rudolf Alster in Lausanne to arrange the move. Sophia expresses frustration with her mother's impulsive nature and notes that their grandfather opposes sending Josephine away. The narrator remains skeptical of Margaret's motives, questioning why such a hasty decision is being made.

The following morning, the narrator reflects on various conversations he has had with Edith de Havilland, Clemence, Margaret, Sophia, and the housekeeper. He notes that Philip remains silent and avoids him, despite the narrator's intention to marry Sophia. The narrator contemplates Philip's psychological state, wondering if Philip's resentment toward his father and his jealousy of Roger—who was favored by their father—might have provided a motive for murder. He considers the possibility that Philip might have killed his father and attempted to frame Roger, whose company was facing bankruptcy.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The narrator reflects on his motives for investigating Sophia's father, noting that Sophia wants the truth about her suspicions to be proven or disproven to prevent uncertainty from ruining their potential marriage. While the household suspects Brenda and Lawrence of being responsible for the death of Aristide Leonidis, the narrator remains uncertain.

After breakfast, the narrator explores the Leonidis house and enters the bathroom connected to Aristide's bedroom. He observes that the bathroom is well-equipped with medical supplies, including insulin, Eserlin, aspirin, and various labeled bottles. He notes that the organized arrangement of these items would have allowed a murderer or accomplice to easily access them, complicating the police investigation. He recalls Taverna's strategy of making the suspects uneasy to provoke a mistake, though the criminal has not yet reacted.

The narrator then eavesdrops on a history lesson being given by Lawrence Brown to Josephine and Eustace in the study. He is impressed by Lawrence's ability to teach the French Revolution with vivid detail. After the lesson, the narrator encounters Eustace and Josephine. Lawrence leaves the room quickly, appearing startled by the narrator's presence. During a conversation with Eustace, the narrator learns about the boy's intellectual curiosity and his frustrations with his home life. Eustace describes Josephine as a "dimwit" regarding detective work and expresses his dislike for the suffocating atmosphere of the house, citing his parents' eccentricities and the presence of various relatives like "creepy Uncle Roger" and the young step-grandmother.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a study, the narrator questions Eustace about his grandfather. Eustace describes his grandfather as a misfit and an individualist who was obsessed with money, suggesting that his death was a necessary outcome. Lawrence Brown enters the room and instructs Eustace to return by eleven o'clock.

Lawrence then questions the narrator regarding the progress of the police investigation. The narrator, whose father is the deputy chief, maintains an air of superiority by claiming he lacks confidential information. Lawrence expresses panic and frustration, feeling targeted by a conspiracy. He complains that the Leoniadis family looks down on him because he is merely a tutor and a pacifist. He also defends Mrs. Leonidis against an insinuation made by a detective regarding her involvement in her husband's poisoning, calling the suggestion idiotic.

While walking through a hallway, the narrator encounters Josephine, who is dirty and emerges from a loft-like space near the water closet. Josephine remarks that, according to detective novels, a second murder is imminent to silence someone who knows the truth. 

Later, the narrator meets Brenda, who expresses intense fear and asks who is on her side. The narrator reflects on Lawrence's cowardice and feels guilt regarding Brenda's isolation. The narrator informs Brenda that the upcoming hearing will likely be postponed to allow for further police searches, but warns that the media may create a spectacle. The narrator advises Brenda to find a lawyer to protect her interests and guide her through procedural matters, noting that she is alone.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a conversation with a woman who expresses gratitude for his help, Charles is informed by Sophia that his father wants him to return to London to visit Scotland Yard. Charles meets his father in an oppressive office alongside Superintendent Tavener, Mr. Gaskill (also referred to as Mr. Gastellier/Gaskett/Geertz/Gekkul/Geckert), and Inspector Taverna.

The group discusses an unexpected development: Mr. Agrotopoulos, the owner of the Telfi restaurant and a longtime associate of the late Aristides Leonidis, has delivered a sealed letter and a will as instructed by Aristides over a year ago. This new will, drafted by Aristides himself, differs significantly from the previous one. Mr. Gaskill expresses anger and resentment, feeling that Aristides' actions were intended to make him look bad.

Charles learns that the new will leaves the majority of Aristides' movable and immovable property to his granddaughter, Sophia Katherine Leontides, rather than dividing it among his other relatives. Charles, who intends to marry Sophia, reads an explanatory letter written by Aristides. In the letter, Aristides explains his motive: he believes every family requires a dominant person to bear the burden of its welfare. He concludes that his sons, Roger and Philip, and his grandson, Eustace, lack the necessary business acumen, confidence, or judgment to lead. He identifies Sophia as the only heir possessing the brains, courage, and impartiality required to protect the family and his sister-in-law, Edith de Harville. Aristides admits the will is a "trap" designed to pass the family burden to Sophia without humiliating his other heirs.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Aristide Leonidas leaves a final will, which he secretly replaces with a different version during a signing ceremony witnessed by two servants to quell suspicion. In the document, he expresses gratitude for the family's loyalty and asks Sophia to protect them. Following his death, Mr. Gasteyer of the law firm Carlum and Gasteyer & Co. expresses offense at Leonidas's perceived lack of trust, leading to a confrontation with the narrator's father. Inspector Taverna notes that Leonidas was a cunning man who avoided using Gasteyer's firm for suspicious business.

The narrator, Charles, receives an urgent call from his girlfriend, who reports that Josephine has been severely injured by a blow to the head and may not recover. Charles, his father, and Taverna travel to Swinley, where Sophia informs them that Josephine has been taken to General de Macte Barcin Hospital.

Upon arriving at the property, Sophia leads them to a dilapidated courtyard containing a laundry room. A marble lion doorstop, originally from the front door, had been placed on top of the laundry room door as a trap. Sophia explains that Josephine had a habit of swaying on the door's bottom groove. Taverna observes that the trap was likely set to fall, though it was imprecise. He notes that the killer was careful, but Charles discovers dirt fragments on an old wooden chair, suggesting someone with muddy shoes stood on it. Sophia reports finding Josephine unconscious with blood on her hair around 1:15 PM. Taverna concludes the doorstop must have been placed between breakfast and 1:00 PM.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Sophia informs Mr. Charles that the house was cold because the door had been closed. She provides an account of the morning's activities: Eustace and Josephine were doing homework until 12:30, with a break at 10:30, while Philip was likely in the library. Sophia's mother emerged from the bathroom around 12:15. In the library, Mr. Charles finds Philip looking exhausted and Margaret crying. Margaret expresses intense guilt, calling Josephine her "little monster" and fearing the child will die.

Seeking the nurse, Mr. Charles finds her crying in the kitchen. She claims an evil aura exists in the house and believes someone killed the master and attempted to kill Josephine. She notes that Josephine was an eavesdropper who used secrets to threaten others, but such tactics would not work against a poisoner. Mr. Charles asks about Josephine's black leather notebook, but the nurse does not know its location.

Taverner calls Mr. Charles to Josephine's room, which has been ransacked; drawers are pulled out, sheets are torn, and photos are removed from frames. Taverner notes that anyone in the house—including Philip, Margaret, the nanny, Brenda, Lawrence, Eustace, Miss de Harvilland, or Roger—could have committed the act, as their alibis are insufficient. Recalling that Josephine once mentioned "scouting" in the attic, Mr. Charles searches the water tank room. He discovers a stack of letters wrapped in brown paper hidden behind a large water tank. The letters are from Brenda Lionides to Lawrence Brown, expressing a secret, intense love and suggesting a plan to be together. Taverner concludes that Brenda and Lawrence have been planning something for some time.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Taverner and Charles discuss the motive behind the marble lion doorstop used in the attempt on Josephine's life. Taverner concludes that Lawrence Brown likely set the trap because his character avoids direct physical violence, preferring mechanical methods like the ice axe. Taverner suggests that Brenda may have plotted the poisoning of the old man, but the mechanical trap was likely Lawrence's doing. Taverner notes that the letters found will allow the prosecutor to file a case if Josephine recovers.

Following a police request, the trial is postponed. Josephine is expected to make a full recovery, and Sophia ensures that her impulsive mother is barred from visiting the child to prevent a dramatic scene. After the postponement, Gaskill gathers the Leonidis family in Margda's living room to read Aristide Leonidis's explanatory letter and his will. 

The reading reveals that Sophia has inherited the family. The family members react with varying degrees of decorum and chaos. Margda comments on the cunning nature of the will, while Margaret behaves theatrically, asking Sophia for money. Philip remains composed, rebuking Margaret for her behavior. Margda attempts to pressure Sophia into helping Roger, but Roger declines, stating that he and Clementine intend to live a simple life in the West Indies once the company collapses. Edith de Harvillan advises Roger to accept help to protect Sophia's reputation from rumors, but Roger dismisses her concerns, asserting that he and Clementine know what they want.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following the reading of Aristide Leonidis's will, Clementine defends Roger before leaving with him. Gasteyer, expressing disgust at the situation, organizes documents and offers Sophia financial advice and an advance payment, suggesting a future meeting at the Lincoln Hotel. Gasteyer notes that Roger is unfit for business, a sentiment Aristide had previously expressed. Sophia matter-of-factly states she has no intention of reviving the catering company. After Gasteyer departs, the atmosphere among the remaining family members turns cold.

Philip reacts with icy resentment, feeling humiliated and ignored by his father despite his lifelong dedication. Edith de Harvillan attempts to soothe him, suggesting Aristide simply favored the younger generation. Eustace, Aristide's grandson, suffers an emotional outburst, expressing intense hatred for his grandfather for leaving the entire inheritance to Sophia. He flees the room in a state of hysteria, prompting Margaret to chase after him while Edith watches snidely.

Sophia remains composed, though she expresses concern regarding Eustace's grudge and her father's potential hurt. She also mentions her refusal to use her new wealth to sponsor her mother's interest in Edith Thompson's play. The narrator observes that Sophia's character closely resembles Aristide's.

Later, while Sophia, Brenda, and the narrator are in the rock garden, Detective Taverna and Sheriff Lam arrive at the house. Brenda, visibly shaken by the ongoing investigation and the presence of reporters, questions their purpose. The narrator, aware of letters found in the water tank room, suspects the authorities have been to the prosecutor's office. Taverna approaches the group and informs Sophia that he has an arrest warrant for her, charging her with the poisoning of Aristide Leonidis on September 19th.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Brenda Lionides collapses in terror, claiming she is being trapped and denying any involvement in the crime. Taverner informs her that Lawrence Brown has also been arrested. Brenda, visibly devastated, is led to a police car by Taverner, accompanied by Mr. Lawrence Brown and Sheriff Lamb. Charles observes Brenda’s expression of absolute fear, which reminds him of a story Madge Leoniadis once told about Edith Thompson. Charles speculates that Lawrence Brown might have swapped the contents of poison bottles to protect Brenda, given his unstable personality and delusions of persecution.

Charles explains to Sophia that the police arrested them based on evidence found in love letters. During their conversation, Charles suggests they should marry now that the Leonidis family is no longer under suspicion. However, Sophia reveals she had a motive: her grandfather had told her two weeks before his death that he was leaving her his entire inheritance. She had kept this secret because she feared the will would make her a suspect in the murder.

Later, Roger and Clementine approach the group. Roger expresses aggressive satisfaction regarding the arrests, stating he hopes the criminals are hanged for poisoning his father. Clementine rebukes his rudeness. As they walk toward the house, Clementine confesses to Charles her intense desire to take Roger away from the Leonidis family traditions. She explains that she has been fighting to ensure Roger can live a life of his own choosing, free from the constraints of his family's expectations.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Josephine returns home with Madge, sporting a head bandage. Despite Madge's suggestion to rest, Josephine insists on going to the pond to see her fish. The narrator follows her and discusses the recent developments, including the discovery of a will leaving all of the grandfather's assets to Sophia. Josephine admits she knew this because she had been eavesdropping. She also reveals she knew Brenda and Lawrence were involved in something suspicious because she observed Lawrence hiding things in the water closet, noting that he was not a person who typically performed plumbing repairs.

Edith de Harvilland summons Josephine and later speaks with the narrator. Appearing exhausted and dejected, Edith expresses a desire for Brenda to receive a fair legal defense, despite her previous dislike for her. She feels a sense of responsibility regarding Brenda's future but remains indifferent toward Lawrence. The narrator informs Edith of his plan to drive to London, and she asks to accompany him, requesting to be dropped off at Debenham Street and later Harley Street to avoid traveling with Margaret and Sophia.

Seeking further information, the narrator meets with Inspector Taverner. Taverner explains that while the case against Brenda and Lawrence is strong, the evidence is largely circumstantial. He notes that the incriminating love letters found behind the sink imply a conspiracy to be together after the husband's death, but a defense lawyer could argue the letters refer to the husband's expected natural death. Taverner concludes that the outcome depends on whether the jury finds the pair sympathetic or views them as a typical criminal duo.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a discussion with Taverner, who remains unsatisfied with the case outcome and doubts the guilt of the current suspects, Charles speaks privately with his father to break down the emotional barriers between them. Charles expresses his intuition that a member of the Leonidis family is the culprit and that the family members are collectively hiding the truth. He notes that while Roger is honest in his desire to see Brenda punished, the others are acting guiltily by insisting on Brenda's best defense.

Charles and his father systematically evaluate potential suspects. They discuss Philip, noting his jealousy of Roger; Margaret, who sent Josephine to Switzerland; and the possibility of a mother's irrational motives regarding Josephine. Charles dismisses the idea of Roger killing his father but discusses Clementine, noting she might have poisoned the old man to prevent him from reviving the banquet company, which would have disrupted her plans for Roger. Charles also mentions Edith de Harvilland as a potential killer who might be covering for herself by supporting Brenda. Finally, Charles suggests Eustace, noting his moody nature and hatred for his grandfather, as a fit for the killer's profile.

Charles reaches a breakthrough regarding the intruder in Josephine's room. He realizes that while he and Taverner previously believed the intruder was searching for letters, Josephine had already revealed that Lawrence had found and hidden those letters in the water closet. Consequently, Charles concludes the intruder was actually searching for Josephine's little black notebook containing her "intelligence findings," which remains missing.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Charles and his father discuss the danger facing Josephine, noting that her mother intends to send her to Switzerland for study. During this conversation, Glover informs Charles that Miss Leonides from Swinley has called with an urgent message. Sophia informs Charles via telephone that the killer is still in the house and that the housekeeper has died after accidentally drinking poisoned hot chocolate intended for Josephine.

Charles, Taverner, and others return to the "crooked house." Charles reflects on the potential innocence of Brenda and Lawrence, noting that while they were involved in a romance, they lacked the wit to escape their current predicament. He also notes that the marble doorstop used to hit Josephine was placed by an unknown person. At the house, Clementine prepares to leave for London, but Charles argues she should stay because of the housekeeper's death. Clementine defends herself and Roger, claiming they were packing upstairs and could not have poisoned the drink.

Josephine interrupts the argument, noting that the nanny and grandfather were also poisoned. She expresses a lack of affection for the deceased housekeeper. Charles hears a sigh from behind a door, suggesting someone was eavesdropping, but finds no one there. Charles takes Josephine to a private room to interrogate her. Josephine admits she knows who poisoned the hot chocolate, who poisoned her grandfather, and who hit her with the doorstop. She refuses to tell the police, believing they are wrong to suspect Brenda or Lawrence. Despite Charles's warnings that her silence makes her a target, Josephine remains defiant, comparing her situation to a detective novel and offering to tell only lies.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Josephine tells Charles that if she were to reveal a secret, she would do so dramatically by identifying a murderer. Edith de Harville interrupts, instructing Josephine to clean herself and preparing to take her to Larchmont Market for soda sundaes. Edith instructs Charles to accompany Josephine. After Josephine changes, Sophia expresses concern over Charles's presence and Josephine's plan to eat ice cream in the cold.

As they prepare to leave, Edith has Evans bring the Ford car. Charles notices boxes with blue labels in the hallway, which makes him uneasy. Before departing, Edith kisses Sophia and tells her that some things must be endured. Once Edith and Josephine drive away, Charles and Sophia discuss the danger Josephine faces. Charles reveals that a bottle of digitoxin, which Edith uses for heart disease, is empty, suggesting someone may have found the hidden key. Charles expresses a desire to prevent Roger and Clementine from leaving, prompting Sophia to describe her feeling of being trapped in a nightmare.

Charles and Sophia spend time in the garden, where Sophia recalls the deceased nanny who raised Eustace and Josephine. After an ambulance departs with the nanny's body for an autopsy, the pair returns to the house as darkness falls. They find the house occupied by a distressed maid and Margda, who questions why Edith and Josephine have been gone so long. Charles seeks out Tavener to express his concerns. While waiting, Eustace suggests they may have gone to the cinema, but Charles suspects Edith may have taken Josephine to a hotel or London for safety. At 6:45, Tavener arrives with bad news: a driver reported seeing a Ford car similar to theirs leaving the main road and heading into the woods.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a car crash at Fletchers Quarry results in the deaths of two passengers, Margarita Leonides mourns her child, Josephine. The narrator discovers concealed letters written by Edith de Havilland behind a copper teapot in an oak cabinet. One letter, addressed to Inspector Taverna, is a confession in which Edith takes full responsibility for the murders of Aristide Leonidis and the nursemaid Janet Lovell to protect Brenda Leonidis and Lawrence Brown. Edith notes that Dr. Mitchell Chaworth can confirm she has only months to live.

The narrator finds a second, thicker letter addressed to Charles, which contains Josephine's small black notebook. Upon reading the notebook, the narrator and Sophia realize that Josephine was the true killer. The notebook contains entries written in a child's handwriting, including the statement, "I killed my grandfather today." Josephine's motives were rooted in her desire to study ballet and her resentment toward family members. She had orchestrated a complex scheme, including a staged attack on herself involving a door stopper and a laundry room chair, to divert suspicion. She also poisoned the nursemaid using digitalis stolen from Edith's room to vent her anger.

The narrator reflects on Josephine's abnormal, self-centered nature, which he attributes to her family's genetic traits. Edith had discovered the notebook in an abandoned doghouse and acted out of a misguided sense of love to protect the child from the consequences of her crimes. Following the revelation, Charles proposes marriage to Sophia, promising to take her to Persia to start a new life. Charles eventually informs his father that Josephine, not Edith, was the killer, a conclusion his father admits he had already reached.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After arriving on Majorca Island via steamship from Barcelona, Mr. Parker Pyne finds the local hotels fully booked due to the peak season.
