After staying in an unsatisfactory room in the city center, he consults a Spanish taxi driver regarding hotel prices in areas like Bunyanyola and Palma. Finding the prices exorbitant, Pyne decides to seek accommodation himself. He eventually settles on the Pino d'Oro, a small seaside inn near Bolinas.

While having breakfast on the terrace, Pyne observes a British mother and her son, Basil Chester. Although the woman appears composed and well-traveled, Pyne notices a sudden, tense stiffness in her back that suggests she is struggling to suppress tears. Sensing his peaceful vacation may be interrupted, Pyne decides to obscure his identity in the hotel register, signing as "Christopher Pyn" instead of "C. Parker Pyne" to avoid being recognized by the Chesters.

Over the following days, Pyne interacts with the Chesters. He finds Basil to be a modest and polite young man, and the three British guests occasionally gather for coffee. On the fourth day, Pyne speaks privately with Mrs. Chester. While they initially discuss mundane topics like gardening and economics, Mrs. Chester eventually begins to share details about Basil’s academic successes and his character, expressing pride in how he has remained well-behaved.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After meeting Mrs. Adela Chester, Mr. Parker Pyne learns of her deep dissatisfaction with the local youth and her conservative views. The following day, Basil, Mrs. Chester's son, tells Mr. Pyne that his mother is so traditional she cannot even accept women wearing pants. Later, a flamboyant woman from the Mariposa Hotel recognizes Mr. Pyne and loudly praises his reputation as a man who can solve any problem, which causes tension with Mrs. Chester.

That evening, Mrs. Chester invites Mr. Pyne to a salon to seek his help. She reveals that Nina Whitchell suggested he could save Basil from a "disastrous marriage" to Betty Gregg. Mrs. Chester expresses disdain for the modern lifestyle of the local artists and women, specifically criticizing a girl she saw drinking cocktails and wearing flannel pants. Mr. Pyne suggests that Basil's interest in serious topics like archaeology is not a problem and that Mrs. Chester is overstepping her parental authority. He advises her to maintain her own identity as an independent person rather than defining herself solely through her son.

Mr. Pyne later speaks with Basil, who complains that his mother is narrow-minded and prejudiced against Betty. Basil explains that Betty tries too hard to appear modern when his mother is present. At Basil's request, Mr. Pyne visits Betty and her sister, Stella, at their sparse villa near the beach. There, he meets Stella, who wears no makeup, and Betty, a red-haired woman with a sharp gaze. Betty serves Mr. Pyne a cocktail and playfully asks if he has taken a side in the conflict between the young lovers and the elderly opponents.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Betty Gregg expresses her frustrations to Mr. Parker Pyne regarding Basil’s tendency to prioritize his mother’s influence and his perceived dullness due to being pampered. Mr. Pyne conducts a brief compatibility test with Betty, asking about her sleeping habits, food preferences, and bedtime; he concludes that she and Basil are well-suited. During this time, Mr. Pyne advises Mrs. Chester to act reluctantly regarding her son's engagement to maintain leverage.

After a week-long vacation in Soleil, Mr. Pyne returns to the Pinewood Hotel to find Mrs. Chester and a pale, tearful Miss Betsey drinking tea. Their conversation is interrupted by Basil, who arrives from the beach accompanied by a glamorous, dark-skinned woman named Delores (also referred to as Miss Ramona or Miss Lamorna). Delores, dressed in light blue chiffon with heavy makeup, dismisses the other women and calls Basil "darling." She subsequently leads Basil away to her hotel, leaving the remaining group in an awkward silence.

Following their departure, Betty leaves in anger, and Mrs. Chester expresses regret over the trip and her son's behavior. Mr. Pyne attempts to reassure Mrs. Chester, suggesting that Delores's presence has at least broken Basil's previous infatuation with Betty. When Basil returns, he defends his right to have friends and dismisses his mother's concerns about Betty's jealousy. A few days later, Basil breaks his scheduled plans for a picnic with Betty to go to Fontainebleau with Delores and her friends.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
On a terrace, Betty decides to end her relationship with Basil. She removes a symbolic ring given to her by Bazelton and asks Mrs. Chester to return it to him, requesting that Mrs. Chester tell him she is fine and wish him luck. When Basil returns at sunset, he and Mrs. Chester argue; Basil attributes their recent difficulties to jealousy, while Mrs. Chester fears he intends to marry a girl she does not approve of.

Seeking help, Mrs. Chester consults Mr. Parker Pyne. He agrees to intervene without Basil's knowledge. By midnight, Mr. Parker Pyne informs Mrs. Chester that he has influenced Miss Deloris Lamorna to leave Bollensa and the island shortly. He explains that he achieved this through his influence rather than money. He predicts that while Basil will be disappointed by her sudden departure, Betty will eventually forgive him.

On a steamship departing Palma, Mr. Parker Pyne reveals to Deloris Lamorna that she is actually the actress Madeline Salieri. Basil Chester approaches Mr. Parker Pyne to express his gratitude for the plan, which has resulted in his mother and Betty becoming friends. Basil asks about a Miss Salazar, but Mr. Parker Pyne informs him that she is leaving for America to assist with his business.

In a different setting, Joan Ashby and Harry Dahlhausen argue about whether a butler, Digby, rang a gong once or twice. Harry notes that his uncle has been acting strangely. Following the argument, a loud sound resembling a gunshot occurs. A dark-skinned man named Keane asks about the noise, which the butler claims was a car backfiring. However, Joan and Keane disagree on the direction of the sound, with Harry suggesting it came from the north.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At Litcham Manor, a famous British estate owned by the eccentric and temperamental Hubert Litcham Rock, guests gather in the living room. The household follows strict traditions, most notably a ceremonial dinner that begins precisely after a second bell rings; anyone arriving late is barred from the manor. Joan Ashby, a golden-haired visitor, and Harry Dallahouse, Hubert's nephew, express surprise when the dinner ceremony is delayed by ten minutes. Jeffrey Keene, Hubert's secretary, also finds the delay peculiar. Diana Clifes, Hubert's adopted daughter, mocks the delay, noting that Hubert is usually a punctual man who paces like a "hungry tiger."

When the gong finally sounds and the butler, Digby, announces dinner, the guests realize Hubert is missing. Instead of the master, Hercule Poirot enters the room. Poirot explains that his train was delayed due to an accident. While the guests, including the vague and muddled Mrs. Litcham Rock, discuss the anomaly, Digby reports that Hubert went to his study at 7:52 PM. 

The situation turns tense when Digby returns to announce that Hubert's study door is locked. Taking charge of the group, Poirot leads the guests to the study. After knocking with increasing force and receiving no response, Poirot examines the keyhole and commands the gentlemen to break down the door immediately.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Under the direction of Hercule Poirot, Jeffrey Kean and Gregory Baring break down the door to a room in Lichfield Manor. Inside, they find a tall man, identified as Hubert Lichfield Rock, slumped in a chair facing a window. A small pistol lies on the carpet near his limp hand. Poirot observes a bullet hole in the right side of the man's head, with the exit wound shattering a mirror on the left wall. A piece of paper on the desk contains the word "sorry" written in shaky handwriting. Poirot discovers the room's key in the deceased man's pocket, and Harry Delehous confirms the French window is latched. Poirot instructs the group to call the police but insists nothing be touched, noting that the gunshot was heard only fifteen minutes prior.

Poirot later speaks privately with Baring, a close friend of the deceased. Poirot reveals that Lichfield Rock had hired him in London to investigate a blackmail attempt involving a large sum of money. While Baring suggests the death was a suicide driven by eccentricity, Poirot remains skeptical. Baring mentions a new estate agent, Captain Marshall, a one-armed war veteran whom Lichfield Rock trusted. Baring dismisses rumors of Marshall swindling the estate, instead suggesting the focus should be on Diana Clives, Lady Lichfield's adopted daughter. Baring admits he is in love with Diana and had Lichfield's approval to marry her, though he describes her as a "little devil" who has had many relationships. Baring notes that Diana frequently sees Captain Marshall, leading Poirot to speculate that a connection between them might be the "family reason" Lichfield Rock wished to keep private.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Barrington informs Hercule Poirot that the nephew of the estate owner, who is the son of the lady's sister, may inherit the Lichfield-Roches estate and change his surname, as Mr. Lichfield-Roches left no heirs. Poirot then speaks with Mrs. Lichfield-Roches, who attributes the family's troubles to a curse symbolized by a mirror and notes that her husband, Hubert, had become increasingly eccentric.

Poirot questions the butler, Digby, regarding the gunshot. Digby states that Mr. Dahlhaus, Miss Ashby, and Mr. Keene were in the hall at the time, while Mrs. Lichfield-Roches, Miss Clifford, and Mr. Baring arrived later. Poirot then interviews Miss Clifford, who describes the deceased as a man with supreme authority and mental aberrations. She expresses indifference toward his death, noting he was involved in bad investments, though she admits she liked him. She also mentions she was in the garden when the shot occurred.

Next, Poirot confronts Mr. Keene about picking up an object near the study door. Although Keene initially denies it, he eventually admits to possessing a tiny silk rosebud that Poirot identifies as coming from Miss Clifford's purse. 

Finally, Major John Marshall arrives and expresses shock at the suicide. Poirot questions him regarding financial irregularities and his plan to elope with the ward, Daisy. Marshall confirms the relationship, noting that the deceased was unaware of it and that Daisy is handling the situation. Marshall also mentions he is looking for a new job to avoid losing his position if the truth were discovered.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Inspector Rives and a forensic officer conclude that the death of Mr. Litcham Rock was a suicide—noting the locked doors and windows, the deceased's strange behavior, and the discovery of a bullet below a mirror—Hercule Poirot begins his own investigation. Using a flashlight, Poirot discovers four footprints in a flower bed outside the study window. He observes that the prints belong to small, delicate shoes with high heels, leading him to question Diana Clifes.

Poirot confronts Diana in the morning room. She admits to being in the garden around 7:00 PM and again at 8:15 PM to pick flowers, noting she attempted to climb through the study window but found it locked. Poirot presents her with a tiny silk rosebud he found in Mr. Keene's pocket, which Diana claims came from her purse.

Poirot gathers the household, including Baring, Keene, Marshall, Joan Ashby, Harry Dalehouse, and the butler Mr. Digby, in the study. He rejects the suicide verdict, arguing that Mr. Rock's megalomania would prevent him from taking his own life. Poirot demonstrates that the study window's latch is loose and can be easily locked from the outside without turning the handle. He then addresses the distribution of the estate: Mrs. Litcham Rock receives an annual income and a house, while the remainder of the property goes to Diana, provided her future husband adopts the family name.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
A woman reveals that a recent codicil to a will requires Diana to marry Mr. Barrington to inherit her estate; otherwise, the property goes to her cousin, Harry Dellhouse. Hercule Poirot uses this information to debunk a theory that Diana murdered her stepfather to secure her inheritance. He explains that the crime scene was staged to look like a suicide, but the killer made several errors.

Poirot identifies the secretary, Jeffrey King (also referred to as Jeffrey Keen), as the murderer. Poirot explains that King killed the victim earlier using a silencer, then staged the body to appear as a suicide. To create a false timeline, King fired a military pistol from a living room window at 8:12 PM to mimic the sound of a gunshot. However, King failed to notice that Diana had been in the garden picking flowers, which left footprints in the flower bed. The killer had stepped on the flower bed to hide his own tracks, but the presence of only four footprints proved someone had wiped them away between Diana's visits to the garden. Furthermore, King attempted to hide a bullet he had picked up by claiming it was a silk rosebud. Upon Poirot's accusation, King collapses.

Following the resolution, Diana expresses a desire to marry Captain Marshall regardless of the inheritance, and Harry offers to split the estate with her. In a separate scene, Poirot receives a desperate, mysterious phone call from a woman at "Swan Garden" claiming she is in danger and mentioning a table with yellow irises. Upon arriving at Swan Garden, Poirot notes that only one table features yellow irises, while the others have red tulips.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At a social gathering, Poirot observes a table reserved by a wealthy American, Barton Russell, which features a specific floral request. Poirot approaches the table and meets Tony Chappel, who is drinking champagne and expressing deep melancholy over his relationship with Polly Weatherby. Tony explains that Polly is the sister-in-law of Barron Russell, a prominent businessman, and that she is currently dancing with someone else. 

Tony introduces Poirot to the other guests at the table: Barron Russell, the quiet diplomat Stephen Carter, the South American dancer Lola Valdes, and Polly Weatherby. During the introductions, Tony makes jokes about crime and international tension, which prompts Polly to criticize his ridiculous behavior. Poirot attempts to use deduction with Polly, first guessing her favorite flower is a yellow iris—which she denies, stating she prefers lilies or roses—and then correctly deducing she made a phone call, though he incorrectly guesses the recipient's initial. Polly clarifies she called a maid named Louise to mail letters.

As the evening progresses, Barron Russell insists that Polly dance with him. Stephen Carter leaves the table to greet an acquaintance. Poirot engages in conversation with Lola Valdes, learning that her favorite flowers are red carnations or red roses and that she dislikes yellow flowers. Lola describes Poirot as a dangerous person. Finally, Tony Chappel invites Lola to dance, jokingly telling Poirot to prepare for a murder that is about to happen. Poirot, finding the comment meaningful, signals to Luigi to attend to him while he reflects on the statement.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot questions a waitress named Lucie regarding guests who made phone calls. Lucie reveals that a young lady in a white dress called upon entering, and another lady made a call from a booth after exiting the cloakroom. Poirot suspects something is about to happen.

During the dinner, Barton Russell proposes a toast to his late wife, Eileen, who died four years ago today. He reveals that the dinner is a commemoration of her death, which occurred during a dinner in New York attended by himself, Stephen Carter, Anthony Chapple, Miss Valdes, and a sixteen-year-old Polly Weatherby. Barton explains that while the official verdict was suicide via cyanide, he believes Eileen was murdered by someone present at the New York dinner who planted cyanide in her handbag. He claims he is certain of the truth and intends to uncover it.

As the lights dim for a cabaret performance, a Black singer performs a mournful song. The atmosphere is noted as being strikingly similar to the night of the New York tragedy. After the performance, the lights return, and the guests discover that Polly Weatherby has slumped over the table, dead. Lola compares the death to that of "Alice" in New York. Poirot examines Polly and confirms she is dead, noting the scent of cyanide, similar to bitter almonds, in her cup. Barton Russell expresses distress, suggesting Polly may have seen something suspicious during the original incident.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals that the cyanide used in the attempted murder was not in a bag, but was instead carried by the killer. After Tony Chapple retrieves a box of cyanide from Stephen Carter's chest pocket, Barton Russell accuses Carter of poisoning Eileen to avoid a scandal. However, Poirot intervenes to expose Russell as the true culprit. 

Poirot explains that during the darkness, Russell left his seat to act as a server, pouring champagne for the guests. While doing so, he placed cyanide in Polly's glass and planted the remaining cyanide box in Carter's pocket to frame him. Poirot identifies Russell's motive as financial; as Polly's guardian, Russell faced the need to account for her property once she turned twenty-one. Polly, wishing to avoid a scandal, decides not to sue Russell but insists he leave the country. Russell departs angrily, insulting Poirot.

Polly explains to Poirot that she had suspected Russell's scheme after noticing he ordered yellow irises and an extra place setting for the dinner. She admits she had called Poirot using a forged, mysterious message to attract his attention because she felt an inexplicable sense of dread. Following the resolution, Carter leaves the gathering, and the remaining guests, including Poirot and Lola Walds, as well as Tony Chapple and Vera, move to the dance floor.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Satterthwaite, an elderly man traveling from London to Doverton Kingsburn Manor to arrive for tea by four o'clock, experiences mechanical issues with his new car. While his driver and a mechanic negotiate repairs at a service station, Mr. Satterthwaite becomes agitated, making repetitive "clucking" sounds. During this period of restlessness, he recalls seeing something vivid and colorful while driving through the countryside near a rural post office.

The memory involves a connection to a person from his past and various locations, including Corsica and Monte Carlo. Upon remembering the specific trigger, Mr. Satterthwaite rejects his driver's suggestion to take a taxi. Instead, he decides to walk to a location he identifies as the Clown's Cafe. He reaches the village of Kingsburn Darcy and finds the establishment, which is divided into a dining area and a shop selling modern ceramics. The shop window displays a "Rainbow Tea Set" with various colored tea sets, which triggers the realization that the word "Rainbow" was the source of his excitement.

This discovery leads Mr. Satterthwaite to the peculiar hope that he might encounter his old friend, Harry Quinn, in the village. He reflects on his long-standing desire to see Mr. Quinn and his history of befriending influential social figures, such as duchesses, as well as young people in need. Believing that a meeting with Mr. Quinn would signal something significant or destined is about to happen, Mr. Satterthwaite decides to enter the shop to see if there is anything interesting to discover while he waits for his car to be repaired.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While examining high-quality ceramics in a shop, Mr. Satterthwaite reminisces about the late Duchess Maria Lis, recalling her kindness toward her maid during a voyage to Corsica and her preference for brownish-purple Roquefort tea sets. After deciding to purchase a vase, the sun emerges from behind the clouds, illuminating a Gothic-style stained-glass window. The light casts a colorful mosaic onto the back of a tall, muscular man in a black suit who has just sat down in the café.

Recognizing the man's silhouette, Mr. Satterthwaite approaches him and identifies him as Mr. Quine (also referred to as Mr. Queene or Mr. Quincannon). The two men engage in a cryptic conversation regarding their past and the influence Quine has had on Satterthwaite's actions. Quine claims he is merely passing through Kingsburn Dacey to visit an old friend who is recovering from a stroke and living with family. 

Quine introduces his small black dog, Hermes, who signals a young man named Ali to bring two cups of Turkish coffee. The conversation turns to a past "tragic scene" involving a death. Quine argues that death is not always a tragedy and emphasizes that when given a mission, they strive to save lives. When Satterthwaite asks if he is being given a mission, Quine denies it, stating he does not meddle in others' affairs and that Satterthwaite will always know how to observe and act on his own.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Satterthwaite and Mr. Quinn converse over Turkish coffee served by Ali. Mr. Satterthwaite reveals his intention to visit Doveton Kingsburn, a quiet country villa and estate owned by his old friend, Tom Addison. He describes his long history with Tom, who served in the foreign service and married a Spanish woman named Pilar. 

Mr. Satterthwaite details the family's history: Tom and Pilar had two daughters, Lily and Maria. Maria died in childbirth, leaving behind a daughter named Inez, who lives in the village with her doctor father. Lily married a fighter pilot, Wing Commander Simon Gilliat; they moved to Kenya and had a son, Roland. However, Lily died in a car accident, leaving Roland as an infant. Simon subsequently remarried a widow, and they had a son named Timothy. The two boys, Roland and Timothy, were raised like brothers.

Due to circumstances in Kenya, Simon, his second wife, and the two grown sons eventually returned to England and moved to the Doveton Kingsburn manor to live harmoniously with Tom. Mr. Satterthwaite, who is Lily's godfather, expresses a desire to see Roland, whom he has not seen since the boy was twelve. He notes that Tom is aging and has a limp, but remains content. While Mr. Satterthwaite reflects on the nostalgia of visiting the estate, he admits that one thing worries him. Mr. Quinn maintains an intense interest in the family details, prompting a moment of suspicion from Mr. Satterthwaite.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Satterthwaite expresses concern to Mr. Quinn that visiting his old home might be disappointing due to changes over time. Mr. Satterthwaite insists that Mr. Quinn accompany him to visit Tom Addison, whom he describes as a hospitable man. During their conversation in a shop, a woman named Berilla Gillingham enters the china department to purchase colorful tea cups because some broke that morning. Mr. Satterthwaite recognizes her as the second wife of Simon Gillett and the wife of Tom Addison. 

Mrs. Gillingham identifies Mr. Satterthwaite as the guest expected for tea that afternoon at Doverton Grange. Mr. Satterthwaite apologizes for his delay, citing a car breakdown. He offers Mrs. Gillingham a ride to Doverton Kingsburn, but she declines, stating she must ride her motorcycle to pick up her children. Mr. Satterthwaite introduces Mr. Quinn to Mrs. Gillingham and asks if Tom Addison would welcome an extra guest, to which she agrees. However, Mr. Quinn declines the invitation, citing a prior appointment.

As Mr. Quinn prepares to depart, Mr. Satterthwaite asks for an explanation regarding their meeting. Mr. Quinn provides no direct answer but tells Mr. Satterthwaite that the word "colorblind" might be useful to him. After Mr. Quinn leaves, Mr. Satterthwaite departs in his repaired car toward Doverton Grange, repeatedly pondering the meaning of the word "colorblind." He reflects on his childhood memories of the estate's lawns, lake, and river. The passage concludes with a description of the tea setting arranged on the lawn, featuring ornate tables, garden chairs with colorful cushions, and a backdrop of a copper beech and a cedar of Lebanon tree.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
On a beautiful evening at the Doverton villa, Mr. Satterthwaite attends a lavish tea party hosted by Tom Addison. Tom, an aging but dashing man with gouty feet and mismatched red and green slippers, welcomes his guests. Among the attendees are Air Force Captain Simon Gilliat, a handsome man with red hair, and Beryl Gilt, who manages the tea service.

Mr. Satterthwaite observes the younger generation, including two half-brothers, Roland and Timothy, who are in their mid-twenties. Although they share a collaborative spirit and red hair, Timothy possesses fair skin and a fine figure reminiscent of Mr. Satterthwaite's deceased acquaintance, Lily. The group also includes Inez, a beautiful girl of Spanish heritage and daughter of the kind, retired Dr. Horton. Inez is Rowan's first cousin and lives in the house next door. Mr. Satterthwaite notes that both young men seem to have an interest in Inez.

As the party progresses with sandwiches and colorful teacups, the conversation touches on various topics, including Timothy's nacre pipe brought from Germany. While observing the scenery of the river and the nearby mill, Mr. Satterthwaite reflects on his past connections to the house and the people present. He observes that Beryl Gilt has taken the place of Simon's first wife, Lily, and concludes that while no one physically resembles her, her presence is felt at the gathering. He also notes a perceived tension between Tom Addison and Beryl.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Satterthwaite experiences a moment of intense intuition and paranoia during a tea service, sensing an impending crisis. He observes Beryl Gillett at the table, noting that she accidentally breaks a red teacup and replaces it with a light blue one from a tray, repositioning an opalite pipe next to the new cups. When Timothy approaches the table, Inez mistakenly identifies the blue cup as hers and the red one as his, but Timothy insists the cup with sugar is his.

Sensing danger, Mr. Satterthwaite rushes forward and shouts for Timothy not to drink. He explains to a bewildered Dr. Hodton that Timothy is color blind and was deceived by the cup swap. During this confrontation, Mr. Satterthwaite reveals a realization regarding parentage: he claims Timothy is Lily’s son, while Roland is the son of Bertrill and Christopher Eden. Mr. Satterthwaite alleges that a woman intentionally purchased the specific teacups to facilitate a poisoning, knowing Timothy could not distinguish the colors.

Dr. Hodton takes the blue cup from Timothy under the guise of examining a flaw in the porcelain. As the men walk toward the gate, Beryl Gillett rushes toward the house, appearing anxious and fearful. She encounters Tom Addison and sharply points out that he is wearing mismatched shoes—one red and one green—noting that he cannot see the difference. Beryl then flees the scene. As Mr. Satterthwaite and Dr. Hodton watch a motorcycle speed away, Mr. Satterthwaite concludes that Beryl's departure is likely for the best.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Satterthwaite and Dr. Horton discuss a woman's motives, concluding that her actions were driven by greed. Satterthwaite explains that she sought to secure a fortune for her son, Roland, by targeting the inheritance of Tom Edison’s grandson. Dr. Horton acknowledges the woman's true nature after observing her expression. As they speak, a straw man burns in a nearby field. After Dr. Horton departs, Satterthwaite encounters a vision of a tall, slender woman named Lily in a pearl-colored dress. He assures her that her son is safe; she smiles, kisses her hand, and walks toward the burnt scarecrow. Satterthwaite reflects that she belongs to a world of love and death. A small black dog named Hermes approaches Satterthwaite with a note from "H.Q." congratulating him.

In a different setting, Mr. Isaac Bonts, a fifty-eight-year-old man, arrives at Dartmouth on his yacht, "The Mermaid," accompanied by Leo Stein, Sir George, Madame Marlowe, Samuel Lathen, Eve Lathen, Lady Lathen, and Evan Llewellyn. After watching a rowing race, the group prepares to visit fairgrounds for various amusements. While Eve Lathen expresses a desire to visit a gypsy caravan, Mr. Bournes suggests dinner at the George Hotel. During the gathering, Leo Stein and Mr. Böhmermann engage in a game of darts. Observations are made regarding Evan Llewellyn’s distracted behavior and the alertness of Sir George and Madame Marlowe. The narrative introduces Miss Jennet Lestrange, a charming but distant woman, and a golden-haired girl named Yvonne, who approaches her and Ewan Llewellyn.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At the George King Hotel, Mr. Bohns hosts a dinner in a private second-floor room overlooking the harbor square. His guests include Mrs. Maloway, Sir George, the talkative American Lethen and his clever daughter Yvonne, the preoccupied writer Llewellyn, the intelligent Janet Lathamington, and Mr. Bohns's former partner, Leopold.

During dinner, Yvonne asks Mr. Bohns about a large diamond he showed the group the previous night. Mr. Bohns identifies the diamond as his "Morning Star," a talisman he keeps in a special inner pocket. Yvonne challenges him to a bet, claiming she has a plan to steal it. They agree on a wager: if she succeeds, Mr. Bohns must provide six pairs of high-quality nylon stockings, while she bets a new cigarette case. The potential value of the stone is discussed, with Mr. Bohns noting it could be worth at least £30,000 even if re-cut.

To facilitate the demonstration, Mr. Bohns produces the diamond and passes it among the guests, including Madame Malo, Mr. Lechner, Llewellyn, Mr. Reuelin, and Leo Stein. Suddenly, Yvonne exclaims that the diamond has been dropped. As she reaches under the table to find it, a chaotic scene ensues; Sir George, Stein, Reuelin, Lady Ratherton, and Mrs. Maloway all bend down to assist her, causing a glass to fall. While the guests scramble in confusion, Mr. Bohns remains seated, watching with a cold smile while sipping his wine.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following the disappearance of a diamond, Mr. Bunce and Mr. Bontz attempt to search Eve (also referred to as Ivy or Yvonne) for the stolen gem. Mrs. Maloway and Mrs. Rathbone conduct a thorough search of Eve behind a screen, but find nothing. During the search, Evan Llewellyn opens a window and purchases a newspaper from a newsboy passing below. The men also search the room, including a round table and the chairs where Eve had been sitting, but the diamond remains missing.

Eve eventually reveals her plan: she had previously replaced a fake diamond on her handbag clasp with the real diamond using rubber cement. She intended to hide the real stone in the empty slot on her bag, but when she attempted to retrieve it from her small black evening clutch, she discovered the diamond was gone. Despite a secondary search of the room, the diamond cannot be found. Sir George, suspecting a thief is among the group, proposes that everyone be searched. The group agrees, and the eight individuals undergo searches, yet the "Morning Star" diamond remains missing.

In a subsequent scene, Evan Llewellyn meets with Mr. Parker Paine to discuss the incident. Evan explains that Mr. Bunce had locked the door to keep servants out, meaning the diamond must have been taken by someone in the room. Evan expresses concern that his action of buying a newspaper might make it appear as though he threw the diamond to a co-conspirator outside, but Mr. Parker Paine suggests there may be other ways the gem left the room.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Llewellyn meets with Mr. Parker Pynne to discuss the theft of the "Morning Star" diamond. Llewellyn denies the theft but expresses distress over the suspicion cast upon him by others, including Sir George and Mr. Bownes. He explains his recent prosperity as luck from horse racing, which has drawn the attention of an undercover officer. During the conversation, Pynne suggests that Llewellyn's feelings regarding Lady Lymington or Mrs. Lestrange might be relevant. Pynne then asks Llewellyn to describe the physical features of everyone present on the yacht and inquires about a broken wine glass that was stepped on near the child, Ives, and Sir George Malory. Pynne requests a follow-up meeting in three days.

On Friday, Llewellyn visits Pynne’s office, where Pynne reveals that the Amalfi group has been arrested. Pynne explains that the group—consisting of a father, son, and daughter-in-law—used a sophisticated deception. Maria Amalfi, posing as a sixteen-year-old girl, used play-doh to facilitate the crime. The real diamonds were stuck to the bottom of a plate with gum, while a fake diamond was passed through the group. To hide the evidence, Ivan threw the fake diamond onto the floor, breaking a wine glass and stepping on the shards and the fake stone to make them indistinguishable. Pynne identifies Maria as the prime suspect because her youthful appearance and use of play-doh matched his criminal classifications. Llewellyn expresses his gratitude and departs for Lady Lymington's apartment.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Satterthwaite, an observant city man, is visiting the home of Colonel Melrose, a country gentleman and the county's chief police officer. While the two men are discussing horse racing in the Colonel's study, a phone call from Constable Curtis informs the Colonel that James DeWitt has been found murdered in his study. The Colonel invites Satterthwaite to accompany him to Alder Road to investigate the tricky case.

As they drive through the night, Satterthwaite recalls that the late Sir James was a stingy, angry man, while Lady DeWitt is a beautiful woman whom Satterthwaite describes as a "Renaissance type." The Colonel reveals that DeWitt was not poisoned but was beaten to death with a bronze statue. They also discuss Paul de Longueville, a man rumored to have been kicked out of the DeWitt residence a week prior after allegedly making advances toward Lady DeWitt.

Their journey is interrupted by a minor car collision at a British crossroads. The driver of the other vehicle is identified as Mr. Harry Queene. Satterthwaite becomes extremely excited upon recognizing Queene, noting that they had previously met at a hotel called "The Clock and Tweed." Satterthwaite tells the confused Colonel that Queene is a remarkable individual who previously solved the mysterious suicide of their friend, Derek Capel. Satterthwaite expresses a belief that their meeting at the crossroads was not an accident.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Colonel Melrose, Mr. Satterthwaite, Mr. Queene, and Mr. Quincannon travel by car to Alder Road to investigate a murder. During the journey, Satterthwaite recounts the history of the area and the De Winton family, specifically mentioning James Jarratt, a descendant of the wealthy De Wintons. Upon arriving at the mansion, the group meets a policeman and enters the study to find Inspector Curtis.

The victim, James Jarratt, lies dead by a desk, having been struck from behind with a skull-shattering blow. The murder weapon is a two-foot-tall bronze statue of Venus found on the floor. The inspector notes that the windows were locked and bolted from the inside and that the killer appears professional, as no fingerprints were found on the weapon. A large black enamel clock on the desk had fallen and stopped at 6:30, which the inspector suggests marks the time of the crime. However, Colonel Melrose finds this detail suspiciously convenient.

The butler, Miles, is summoned for questioning. He explains that Jarratt returned from golf at 4:45 and requested tea. Miles sent for Jarratt's valet, Jenkins, at approximately 6:15, but did not return to the study until 7:00, when he discovered the body. When Colonel Melrose asks if he saw Mrs. DeWitt earlier in the evening, the butler hesitates and admits he saw her going down the stairs, suggesting she may have been in the study. During the questioning, Satterthwaite discovers and pockets a small silver arc of glass from the fireplace.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Colonel Melrose interrogates the footman, Jenkins, regarding his whereabouts. Jenkins states he brought his master soft leather slippers and took away a pair of brogues, leaving his master after 6:15 and returning to the servants' quarters by 6:30. Curtis confirms Jenkins was in the servants' room from approximately 6:20 to 7:00, effectively clearing him of suspicion.

An anxious maid announces that Laura DeWitt wishes to see the Colonel. Laura appears in a navy blue brocade tea gown, looking unsteady. After being led into a sitting room by Colonel Melrose, she suddenly confesses to shooting Sir James de Witt downstairs. When Mr. Quincannon asks about the pistol, she becomes agitated, claiming she either dropped it or threw it out a window, though she admits she cannot remember clearly. As she leaves, the maid expresses concern that Jenkins might be suspected.

Following Laura's departure, a man named Paul Danglars arrives. He possesses an elegant, theatrical bearing. Danglars confesses to the murder, claiming he stabbed Sir James in the heart with a gem-encrusted paper knife that he snatched from a book belonging to Lady DeWitt. He explains that he entered and exited through a window around 6:30, after chatting with the gamekeeper at 6:15. Colonel Melrose notes the peculiarity of the case, informing the stunned Danglars that Mrs. DeWitt has also come forward with a confession.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Colonel Melrose, Mr. Satterthwaite, and Mr. Quin discuss the inconsistencies in the murder of James. Melrose observes that while Mrs. DeWitt admitted to shooting James and DeLange admitted to stabbing him, the actual cause of death was a crushed head. He concludes that both individuals falsely confessed because they mistakenly believed the other had committed the crime. Mr. Satterthwaite suggests the valet may have a motive, noting a maid's fearful reaction toward him.

Lady DeWitt is summoned and explains that she entered the study at 6:30 and found her husband dead on the desk. She had previously believed DeLange shot him because he had expressed a desire to do so. The police determine that James must have died by 6:25, suggesting the room's clock was manipulated to provide a false time. Laura DeWitt mentions that James carried a golf watch, which the Inspector retrieves. Colonel Melrose examines the watch and finds the hands stopped at 6:15.

Later, during dinner at Colonel Melrose's house, the men reflect on the case. Mr. Satterthwaite notes that the suspects' confessions were based on incorrect assumptions about the method of death. However, the conversation shifts when Mr. Quin suggests that instead of moving clock hands forward, someone may have moved them backward to create an alibi. This realization implies that the crime might actually point to the valet, who claimed to be with the gamekeeper during the time in question, potentially turning the entire investigation on its head.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Queene explains to Colonel Melrose and Mr. Satterthwaite that the perceived truth of a recent crime was merely a staged performance inspired by a book. Mr. Queer and Mr. Quincannon clarify that Dulwich climbed through a window to knock out James and assist a lady doctor in staging the scene. They discuss how the culprits manipulated a clock and a watch to create a false narrative, noting that the valet would have known how to manipulate the watch hands to match the clock. Mr. Queene reveals that he intends to save Jennings from the gallows, aided by a piece of evidence held by Mr. Satterthwaite: a fragment of curved glass. This glass proves the watch was not broken in James's pocket, but rather removed, opened, and tampered with before being returned.

In a separate narrative, Joyce Lambert visits an employment agency in England to seek day work, but she is rejected because she lacks the necessary credentials. Despite her panic and hunger, she maintains her composure and meets her Aunt Mary at a bar near Victoria Street. Aunt Mary, concerned about Joyce's thin appearance, informs Joyce that she cannot stay with her because she has rented her house to short-term tenants and is traveling to Harrogate for rheumatism. Joyce leaves and walks through various London streets to a dingy house on Euston Road. There, she enters an attic and is greeted by Tris, an old, coarse-haired fox terrier with cloudy eyes.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Joyce sits with her aging, twelve-year-old dog, referred to by various names including Tris, Tray, Tricky, Triffy, Trudie, Terry, Trixie, and Trix. Her landlady, Mrs. Barnes, brings Joyce a cup of hot tea and informs her that Barnes is unemployed again, creating financial uncertainty. Mrs. Barnes complains that the dog bit her earlier that morning while she was looking at Joyce's shoes, but Joyce defends the animal, explaining that it is protective of her belongings. After Mrs. Barnes leaves, Joyce expresses her deep anxiety to the dog regarding their situation.

Joyce reveals that she and the dog owe Mrs. Bunce four weeks' rent. She expresses frustration with the local men, noting that Mr. Bunce is often unemployed due to his drinking. Joyce reflects on her past, remembering happy times with the dog, her father, and Michael. She recalls Michael taking them to the countryside and how the dog supported her when she received a telegram regarding Michael's death. Joyce discloses that Michael gambled away his money, a fact she keeps secret from his aunts, who believe she is doing well.

Feeling exhausted and hungry at age twenty-nine, Joyce admits she feels much older and lacks the strength to beg for help, noting a failed attempt to visit her cousin, Charlotte Green, in Iling. She vows never to separate from the dog, despite its declining health and temperament. The passage ends when Joyce hears two knocks on the door downstairs.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After receiving a letter from Sloane and Reade stating that a painting is not an authentic work by Kip and holds no value, Joyce realizes her financial hope is gone. She calls Arthur Harley, who arrives at her house in a car. Harley, a tall man with blue eyes, expresses shock at her poor living conditions. Despite Joyce admitting she does not love him, she agrees to marry him on the condition that she can keep her dog, Trey. Harley agrees and promises to buy her a large ruby ring to contrast with a pearl ring previously given to her by Michael. As Joyce reflects on her decision, she expresses a desire to be forgotten by men and thinks of Michael.

The following morning, Trey attempts to sunbathe on a rotten windowsill and falls to the street below. Joyce rushes outside to find the injured dog. An elderly man alights from a taxi to assist her; he suspects Trey has internal bleeding. Together, they load the dog into a taxi and travel to a veterinary station. The red-faced veterinarian insists that Trey be left for a thorough examination. After leaving the dog, a heartbroken Joyce attempts to walk home, but the elderly man insists on accompanying her to ensure she cleans and bandages the wound Trey caused when he bit her during the transport.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a man helps Joyce clean a wound on her wrist caused by her dog, Tracy, he departs. Mrs. Barnes arrives to offer Joyce tea and comfort, suggesting that Joyce's husband will provide a new dog if Tracy does not recover. However, Joyce receives a phone call informing her that Tracy has died alone.

Joyce’s husband, Arthur (referred to as Harrydy), arrives and attempts to take Joyce away from her current home. Joyce informs him that she will not marry him because Tracy is dead, revealing that her motive for the marriage was merely to be with the dog. She expresses her deep hatred for Arthur, describing him as a cruel person who enjoys hurting others. After Joyce rejects his offer of financial stability, an angry Arthur leaves.

Feeling numb and contemplating suicide, Joyce visits an employment agency. Mrs. Lambert informs her that no local daytime jobs are available, so Joyce agrees to consider working abroad. She is interviewed by Mr. Alabi, who offers her a position caring for his elderly aunt and his three-year-old son. During the interview, Mr. Alabi asks about her dog. Joyce realizes that he is the same gentleman who helped her earlier. He shares that he also lost a dog two years ago, which creates a moment of profound emotional connection between them. Joyce accepts the job and signs the necessary documents. Upon returning home, Mrs. Barnes informs Joyce that Tracy's body has been delivered and that Barnes will dig a pit in the backyard for the dog's burial.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Vincent Easton waits anxiously under the big clock at Victoria Station, fearing that Theodora Darrel will not fulfill her promise to run away with him. He reflects on their recent meeting two weeks prior at Haidme Manor, where he first kissed her under a plum tree. Despite his status as an ordinary citrus grower from the Transvaal province, he had asked her to leave her luxurious life in London to be with him. Though he worries about the scandal her departure might cause and doubts her commitment, Theodora arrives at the station dressed in black.

After hiring a porter, the pair boards a train and travels through southern London. During the journey, Vincent contemplates the mysterious nature of Theodora, noting that while she is the wife of the popular and charming Richard Darrel, he knows almost nothing about her true thoughts or her marriage. He feels a sense of fear regarding her elusive aura, yet he seeks comfort through physical affection. When he asks if she will regret their decision, Theodora confidently denies it, admitting only that she is afraid of the feeling of happiness.

The couple arrives in Dover around 5 pm to stay overnight before crossing the channel. While embracing in their hotel room, they are interrupted by a knock at the door. A male tea server enters, places tea on the coffee table near Theodora, and departs after checking if they require further assistance.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After reading a newspaper headline regarding the sudden collapse and impending scandals of the company Hobson, Jekyll, and Lucas, Theo informs Vincent that her husband, Richard, is associated with the firm. Despite Vincent's attempts to dissuade her, Theo insists on returning to Richard because the company's bankruptcy means his ruin. Vincent reacts with anger, eventually hurling venomous words at her to hurt her, but Theo remains silent and leaves him to say goodbye.

Theo arrives at the Darrells' house in Chelsea, where she finds a worn-out Richard. He informs her that while the company is in crisis, she will retain her legal right to live in their house. Although Richard attempts to comfort her and claims things will improve, Theo senses he is withholding information. As Richard falls asleep with his head on her knees, Theo remains suspicious of his secrecy.

Three days later, Theo confronts Richard about the details of his situation. Richard initially deflects, claiming the matters are too complex for women to understand. He eventually admits that while his actions regarding a large property deal in South Africa were morally just, they may be legally problematic, potentially leading to criminal charges. As Richard attempts to defend himself through long-winded explanations, Theo observes his nervous behavior and realizes he is concealing the full truth. Under the pressure of the discussion and Theo's contemptuous glances, Richard eventually breaks down and buries his head in his hands.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Richard confesses to his wife, Theo, that he is a thief. To protect himself from potential disgrace and prison, he reveals that Vincent Easton possesses incriminating documents related to his illegal dealings. Richard claims he has meticulously covered his tracks but fears Easton might inadvertently destroy him if he does not realize the connection to the data. Richard pressures Theo to visit Easton immediately to retrieve the files, suggesting that Easton will not refuse her because he likes her. When Theo resists, Richard pulls a revolver from a drawer and threatens to commit suicide if she does not comply. Under this emotional duress, Theo agrees to help.

Theo travels to Easton’s residence wearing a white mink stole. She informs Easton that she is there on behalf of her husband to request the documents. Easton reveals that he has been investigating a company involving names like Hobson, Jekyll, and Lucas, and expresses surprise that Richard is at the top of such a fraudulent operation. Despite the revelation of Richard's character, Easton agrees to provide the files. Theo takes the package and burns the documents in the fireplace.

After the meeting, Theo takes a taxi through Hampstead and toward Chelsea. She experiences internal conflict, doubting herself after seeing Easton, but ultimately decides to remain loyal to Richard, rationalizing that his crimes are against society rather than her. Upon returning home, a trembling Richard asks if everything went well. Theo confirms she burned the documents herself. Exhausted and pale, Theo collapses into an armchair, wishing for eternal sleep, while Richard watches her with a mixture of guilt and embarrassment.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a room in Dover, Theo and Richard engage in a tense confrontation regarding a recent event involving Vincent Easton. Theo accuses Richard of scheming for her to meet Easton alone at night, dressed in a specific way, in the hope that Easton would make a deal for certain files. She realizes Richard was willing to sacrifice her reputation to protect his own interests and safety. Theo reveals that she had actually left with Vincent Easton three days prior, contrary to the story the servants told Richard about her going to the countryside. Although Richard attempts to forgive her, Theo refuses, citing his own infidelity and his recent betrayal. She declares she is leaving for her freedom and exits the house.

In a separate scene, a man in a dark coat arrives at a residence on Calver Street during cold, snowy weather. He asks Mrs. Casey for "Madame Lion" and whistles the tune of "Three Blind Mice" as he ascends the stairs.

Meanwhile, Molly Davis observes a new sign for "Monksville Manor Guest House." She reflects on her husband Jiles's talents as they prepare for their new venture. Following the death of Aunt Katherine, Molly inherited Monkswell Manor. Because the couple could not find a smaller residence and struggled with the bulky Victorian furniture, they decided to run the manor as a guesthouse. A lawyer notes that the house is in good condition due to extensive renovations performed by the late Miss Emery before the war.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Molly and Jules (Giles) decide to convert their house into a guesthouse to establish a permanent home. Molly plans to manage the property by raising livestock and growing vegetables, eventually hiring a maid. They place advertisements in *The Times* and local newspapers to attract tenants.

On the day the first tenants are scheduled to arrive, Jules leaves early to find barbed wire, while Molly walks to a village two miles away to buy supplies. As a blizzard approaches, Molly experiences intense loneliness and anxiety while waiting in the empty house. She inspects the decorated rooms intended for their guests: a south-facing room with redwood furniture for Mrs. Boyle, a blue room with oak furniture for Colonel Metcalf, and an east-side room with a bay window for Mr. Lane.

Jules returns late, having failed to find the necessary wire mesh. The couple discusses their upcoming guests; while Colonel Metcalf has sent word that he will arrive the following day, they expect Mr. Lane, a man Jules suspects is an artist, to arrive for dinner. Molly expresses concern that they are amateurs, but she plans to manage potential non-payment by holding guests' luggage.

While preparing a meal of Welsh cheese, Molly describes the difficulties of managing a breakfast service. The couple then listens to the radio, which reports on a stalemate in foreign affairs and a murder on Paddington Carlisle Street. Disturbed by the bad news and the weather, Molly suggests delaying the start of the bed and breakfast until spring.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Molly and Giles Davis discuss the mysterious death of Madame Lion and the police investigation into a person seen in the vicinity. Their conversation is interrupted by the arrival of Christopher Lane, a young man with light brown hair and light-colored eyes. After traveling from Wales through a severe winter, Lane displays an impatient, rapid-fire manner of speaking, expressing intense interest in Victorian-style decor, such as mahogany sideboards and four-poster beds. He identifies himself as Christopher Reine, an aspiring architect.

Giles expresses immediate dislike for Lane, referring to him as a "little bastard," but Molly insists they must remain professional because Lane is paying seven guineas a week to stay at their inn. After Lane settles into the rose-red room, he assists Molly with cleaning in the kitchen.

The following morning, the weather remains grim as Mrs. Boyle arrives via taxi. An intimidating and domineering woman, Mrs. Boyle criticizes the inn for not being a well-equipped, scientifically run establishment. Giles and Molly stand their ground against her aggressive attitude. Later that afternoon, Mrs. Boyle complains that Lane is too odd. The arrival of a local baker, dressed for extreme weather, warns the Davises that the roads are becoming blocked by snow, making his return in two days unlikely and suggesting they rely on their reserves.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Molly discusses grocery needs and reads a newspaper featuring the murder of Madame Léon. During this, Christopher Raine makes unsettling comments to Mrs. Poole regarding the strangulation of the victim, prompting her to rebuke him. The newspaper reports that police are searching for a man of medium height wearing a dark overcoat, a light-colored hat, and a woolen scarf.

At Scotland Yard, Inspector Pembleton interviews two workers, Joe and Bill, regarding the Lyon case. Joe explains that while working on a gas main on Jamman Street, they encountered a strange man wrapped tightly in clothing who appeared to have a cold. The man gave them a match and then fled, accidentally dropping a small notebook. Joe and Bill discovered the notebook contained two addresses: Luxury Farm and 74 Carlisle Street. They also noted the phrase "Three blind mice" written at the top of the page. The men recount that shortly after finding the notebook, they heard screams regarding a murder nearby.

Inspector Pembleton shows the notebook to Chief Inspector Kane. They compare the notebook to a piece of paper found pinned to the deceased victim's body, which reads "This is the first one" and features drawings of three mice and sheet music. Kane identifies "Three blind mice" as a signal song. Fingerprint reports confirm the deceased was Morine Gregg, who had been using the alias Madame Lorraine. She had been living at 74 Carlisle Street. The landlady reported that a man claiming to look for the tenant was let into the second floor; ten minutes later, she discovered the tenant had been strangled.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Pemberton and Kain discuss a well-planned killing, noting that the location, Monkswell Manor, is likely a specific house in Hapleydon, Berkshire, which Kain identifies from a newspaper crossword puzzle. Pemberton contacts the Berkshire Police Department.

At Monkswell Manor, which has become a hotel, Major Metcalf arrives with two heavy leather trunks. He is a stiff, middle-aged man with a military background in India and is a cousin of Mrs. Boyle. The innkeepers, Jeeves and Molly, are busy with service when a blizzard begins. During the night, a bearded stranger named Paravicini arrives after his car is flipped by snowdrifts. He is a foreigner with a powerful accent and a wallet full of money. Jeeves settles him into the green room. Giles warns that the heavy snow will soon leave them snowed in and cut off from the outside world, lacking deliveries and potentially telephone service.

The next morning, five feet of snow has accumulated. Mrs. Boyle eats breakfast alone, feeling dissatisfied with the establishment. She had sought a social environment to replace the busy, organized life she led during her wartime work, which ended when her house was requisitioned by the military. She finds the high quality of the food and service annoying because it prevents her from complaining. After breakfast, she encounters a red-haired young man with a mocking gaze, whom she deems mentally abnormal. She moves to the living room to claim a rose-colored chair and expresses her displeasure regarding the lukewarm radiator. As she watches snow slide off the roof, she loudly declares she will not stay long, prompting a soft chuckle from a young man named Reen.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Major Metcalf helps Jiles Davis shovel snow from the back door of Monkswell Manor. The Major, a fitness enthusiast and battle-hardened veteran, eats an early breakfast prepared by Molly Davis. After breakfast, Mr. Paravicini arrives for a simple meal, behaving flamboyantly toward Molly. While Jiles works to clear paths to the boiler room and chicken coop, Molly is interrupted by a telephone call in the library.

Constable Higgins from the Berkshire Police Department informs Molly of an emergency. He states that Sergeant Trotter is being sent to the manor, despite Molly's warning that the roads are impassable due to snow. The caller, identified as Inspector Hoggart, abruptly hangs up after instructing Molly to follow the Sergeant's directions.

A frightened Molly informs Jiles of the police call. The couple becomes anxious, speculating that they might be in trouble for minor infractions, such as using ration coupons or bringing butter from Ireland. Their conversation is interrupted by Mrs. Poole, a tenant, who complains about the cold living room. Mrs. Poole and Mrs. Poynter then criticize the guests, specifically questioning the character of the architect Christopher Raun and the arrival of the "odd foreigner," Mr. Paravicini. Mr. Paravicini enters the room quietly and reveals that he has been eavesdropping on their conversation, noting that he never forgets what he overhears.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Mrs. Bailey leaves to knit a sweater, Mr. Palladini behaves lecherously toward Molly, causing her discomfort. Mr. Paravicini advises Molly and her husband that they cannot trust people easily and questions whether they checked the references of their tenants. While observing Mr. Palladini stoking the fire, Molly realizes he uses his back to hide the fact that he uses makeup to appear younger.

Major Metcalf enters to report that a water pipe in the downstairs cloakroom has frozen. When Molly mentions that the police are sending an officer, Major Metcalf reacts with a strange, mask-like expression of fear or excitement. Jules arrives and notes that the heavy snow should make the roads impassable, but a man on skis suddenly knocks on the bay window. The newcomer is Sheriff Trotter, a young officer. Following his arrival, Mr. Paravicini angrily asks Molly why she called the police, though she denies doing so. Christopher Raine notes that the group is now completely isolated from the outside world.

Major Metcalf attempts to use the telephone but finds it broken. Jules leads Sheriff Trotter and Molly to the study to conduct separate questioning. Trotter explains that his arrival is a security measure related to the death of a woman named Madame Lyon, which occurred two days prior in London. He clarifies that the victim's real name was Molline Gregg, the wife of a farmer named John Gregg from Longridge Farm.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Chief Constable Trotter visits Jules and Molly Davis to warn them of imminent danger following the murder of Madame Gregg. Trotter explains that in 1940, three refugee children took shelter at Longridge Farm, where one child died due to neglect and abuse. The Greggs were imprisoned for the incident; Mr. Gregg died during an escape attempt, and Madame Gregg was recently released before being murdered.

Trotter reveals that a notebook found near the crime scene contains two addresses: 74 Carlisle Street, where the murder occurred, and Monkswell Manor, the Davises' location. The notebook is titled "Three Blind Mice" and includes a drawing of three mice and the nursery rhyme's sheet music. A note pinned to the victim's body stated, "This is the first one." Trotter notes that of the three children from the 1940 case, one girl was adopted and one boy, now twenty-three, was a deserting soldier described by a psychiatrist as "not right."

Trotter, acting on behalf of Inspector Hogben, intends to investigate the residents of Monkswell Manor to find a connection to the Longridge Farm case. The residents include Mrs. Poyle, Colonel Metcalf, Mr. Christopher Lane, and Mr. Paravicini. Jules suggests the killer might be Christopher Rene, but Trotter warns that the murderer could already be inside the house, as all guests arrived after the murder. Trotter also notes that a description of the suspect—a lean person in a black coat and light hat—could apply to several people in the house.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Mr. Davis briefs the authorities, Constable Trotter enters the study to confront four guests: Christopher Lawn, Mrs. Boyle, Mr. Paravicini, and Colonel Metcalf. Trotter asks if any of them have a connection to the Lonrigg Farm case, warning that one of them is in life-threatening danger. The guests deny involvement: Mr. Paravicini claims ignorance of local history, Mrs. Boyle questions her involvement in a murder, Mr. Reen claims he was a child at the time, and Colonel Metcalf states he only read about it in the paper while stationed in Edinburgh. Frustrated, Trotter leaves, warning that any deaths will be their own fault.

Christopher Lawn mocks the tension by whistling "Three Blind Mice" and making a joke about strangling Mrs. Boyle, which terrifies her. Following his departure, Colonel Metcalf reveals that Mrs. Boyle was the official responsible for arranging accommodations in 1940 and was the person who sent three children to Lonesome Farm. Mrs. Boyle denies responsibility for what happened later.

Trotter returns and clarifies he is a constable, not an inspector, intending to call Inspector Hoggart. However, Molly Davis informs him the telephone is broken. Trotter suspects the line may have been cut and goes outside to investigate, followed by Jules. While Jules checks the upstairs switchboard, Mr. Paravicini plays "Three Blind Mice" on the piano. In a bedroom, Christopher Lawn transitions from whistling to sobbing, eventually resolving to "finish it." Upstairs, Jules finds a pink bus ticket that falls from Molly’s glove, leaving him in a state of disbelief.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While preparing food in the kitchen, Molly Davis experiences a moment of disorientation and hears a whistling tune that causes her to shudder. Nearby, Colonel Metcalf descends the stairs and inspects a cabinet located under the stairs. In the study, Mrs. Boyle (also referred to as Mrs. Poole) listens to a radio program regarding the psychology of fear. A person enters the room and strangles her with a raincoat belt, turning the radio volume to full to drown out her struggles.

Following Molly's scream, Detective Trotter gathers the residents in the kitchen. Trotter questions Molly about what she heard; she mentions a whistle and a softly closing door. Trotter informs the group that Mrs. Boyle's death is connected to a notebook involving the "three blind mice" and the Lorraine Farm case. He notes that the phone lines have been cut. During the investigation, Mr. Paravicini admits he was playing "Three Blind Mice" very softly on the piano, noting that the tune was also being whistled by Mr. Lane. 

Trotter suggests the killer may have hidden in the cabinet under the stairs after the murder, noting that Colonel Metcalf's fingerprints are present there because he had recently opened it. Giles Davis suggests keeping the most obvious suspect, Christopher Rayne, under watch. Rayne reacts hysterically, claiming he is being persecuted. As the tension rises, Molly interrupts a heated exchange between Giles and Trotter to speak with the detective privately.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Jiles leaves the room, Molly Davis questions Chief Trotter regarding the Lorrach Farm case. Molly challenges the assumption that the eldest son, Christopher, is the killer, suggesting the possibility of other relatives, such as the father, who was serving overseas in the military. Trotter notes that while the boy has an unstable mental state and is a military deserter, he has considered all possibilities, including the possibility of a woman being the killer. Trotter expresses a belief that the killer is someone who enjoys their actions.

Molly retreats to the kitchen to find comfort in domestic chores. Christopher Lane enters, reporting that someone has stolen a police officer's skis from a cabinet under the stairs, an event that has made Trotter uneasy. Christopher expresses excitement about the situation, which causes Molly to shudder as she recalls the purple face of the deceased Mrs. Poole.

Molly asks Christopher to stay because she is afraid of being alone. Christopher observes that Molly is not afraid of him, despite his admission that he is the only person with a motive and a profile fitting the killer. Molly maintains her belief in other possibilities. During their conversation, Christopher admits that "Christopher Lane" is an alias and reveals that he is actually a deserter from the army. He explains that his desertion was driven by a mental breakdown following the death of his mother in an air raid, which led to a compulsion to dig her up. Molly attempts to comfort him, suggesting that he can start over.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Molly and Christopher discuss her past, specifically the death of her fiancé, Jack, which left her feeling that life is unpredictable and treacherous. Molly expresses fear and hatred toward Chief Tolt, who she claims has planted terrible ideas about her husband, Giles, in her mind. Christopher attempts to reassure her by noting that a woman was killed in London two days prior, and Giles was with Molly at the time. However, Molly reveals she found a London newspaper in Giles's pocket, suggesting he lied about being away buying wire mesh.

Giles enters the kitchen and dismisses Christopher, warning him to stay away from his wife. Once alone, Giles accuses Molly of conspiring with Christopher, suggesting they are old friends who arranged for Christopher to arrive as a stranger. He confronts her with a pink bus ticket found in her belongings, proving she traveled to London despite her claims. Molly admits she went to London but refuses to disclose her purpose. The argument escalates into mutual accusations of lying and a breakdown of trust.

The confrontation is interrupted by Mr. Paravicini, who informs them that the inspector wants everyone in the living room because the police have a lead. As Molly prepares to leave the kitchen, Mr. Palazzini approaches her to discuss culinary combinations involving foie gras and smoked meat.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Giles leads Mr. Palazzini to the living room, while Molly experiences a moment of hysterical laughter and distress after Palazzini hums "Three Blind Mice" and discusses the song's cruel lyrics. Jules attempts to calm Molly, noting that murder investigations are more pressing than cooking. In the hallway, they encounter Christopher Lane before entering the living room, where Inspector Thrope and Captain Metcalf are waiting.

Chief Constable Trotter addresses the group, noting that Mrs. Boyle is dead and will not eat again. He reveals that he has gathered everyone to conduct an experiment. Trotter explains that while he cannot verify the previous statements regarding everyone's locations at the time of the murder, he knows that four statements are true and one is false. He intends to identify the liar to find the murderer by having the individuals reenact their behaviors from the time of the crime.

Although Colonel Metcalf scoffs at the idea and Christopher Lane expresses confusion, Jules agrees to cooperate. Trotter begins the process by asking Palazzini to demonstrate his previous action of playing the piano. Palazzini plays "Three Blind Mice" on the grand piano. Trotter then instructs Molly to sit at the piano to repeat the action. He assigns specific tasks to the others to validate their statements: Lane goes to the kitchen to assist Molly, Palazzini goes to Lane's bedroom to play "Three Blind Mice," Metcalf goes to Mr. Davis's bedroom to check the phone, and Mr. Davis goes to the hall and basement. As the group departs, Palazzini remarks on the police's enjoyment of indoor games.

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Molly Davis plays the nursery rhyme "Three Blind Mice" on the piano in a drawing room, following Chief Inspector Trotter's instructions to play softly. While playing, she experiences intense fear, sensing a trap and imagining Mr. Paravicini might be a threat. Chief Inspector Trotter enters the room, claiming he has achieved his purpose by identifying a liar. He accuses Molly of having first-hand knowledge of the Lorrach Farm case and reveals he knows her true identity as a former teacher at Abbyville School. Molly confesses that her sister was the teacher involved in a tragic incident where a child died after a letter for help went unread due to her sister's illness.

The situation turns violent when the man claiming to be Trotter reveals he is actually Jim, the brother of George, a victim from Lorrach Farm. Jim, acting with a childlike demeanor, holds Molly at gunpoint with a revolver stolen from her husband. He explains his motive is revenge against those who failed to help him and his brother during their abuse at the farm. As Jim threatens her, Metcalf Major leaps from behind a sofa and tackles him. The man revealed himself to be Detective Inspector Taner, who had been working undercover with Metcalf to monitor the situation.

After the assailant is subdued, the group prepares to leave. Molly reconciles with her husband, Jules, and they discover they had both traveled to London separately to buy each other anniversary gifts. Mr. Paravicini departs to join the police, promising Molly a mysterious gift of food and nylon stockings, while Christopher Raine notes an unpleasant smell coming from the kitchen, prompting Molly to realize her pie is ruined.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In June 1935, Captain Arthur Hastings returns to London from a ranch in South America to attend to personal matters. Upon his arrival, he visits his old friend, Hercule Poirot, who has relocated to a modern, geometrically symmetrical service apartment in London. During their meeting, Hastings notices that Poirot appears younger and has darker hair; Poirot reveals he has been using a hair color restorer called "Regenerate."

The two friends discuss Poirot's retirement. While Poirot claims to have switched to growing zucchinis, he admits he cannot resist difficult murder cases to keep his "little gray cells" active. Poirot mentions that he recently narrowly escaped a situation where he almost got killed due to a killer's carelessness. He tells Hastings that he considers Hastings a "lucky charm" and believes it is Hastings's fate to stand by his side to prevent him from making the "unpardonable mistake" of ignoring the obvious.

The conversation shifts when Poirot presents Hastings with a typed letter on thick white notepaper. The letter is signed by "ABC" and challenges Poirot to solve a mystery that baffled the British police, warning him to "watch out for Andover" on the twenty-first of the month. The envelope is postmarked London WC 1. While Hastings initially dismisses the sender as a "nutcase," Poirot treats the communication with serious concern.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Captain Hastings discuss an anonymous letter that Poirot finds unsettling. While Hastings suggests the letter is merely a poorly executed prank by a drunkard, Poirot expresses deep unease, noting that his experience tells him something is wrong with it. Poirot explains to Hastings that he would find a robbery on the 21st comforting, as it would mean the letter's threat was not a murder. Meanwhile, Alexander Bonaparte Caster, a tall man with a hunchbacked and squinting appearance, sits in a shabby bedroom on Thursday, June 20th, marking a name on a printed list.

On the 21st, Inspector Japp of Scotland Yard visits Poirot and Hastings. Japp reports that the events predicted for the 21st were merely minor disturbances: a broken store window and two people being drunk and disorderly. This news relieves Poirot, who admits he may have been mistaken in taking the letter seriously. Japp dismisses such letters as common hoaxes written by people seeking a thrill. After Japp leaves, Poirot and Hastings engage in lighthearted banter regarding Hastings' thinning hair. Poirot eventually reflects on his own heightened sensitivity, comparing his concern over the letter to a blind watchdog barking at nothing. He concludes by stating that if he were to choose a type of crime to investigate, he would choose murder.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hastings and Hercule Poirot discuss the tropes of detective fiction, with Hastings suggesting sensational elements like mysterious daggers or beautiful women, while Poirot argues that the most profound crimes are often private and simple. Their conversation is interrupted by a phone call from Japp, who reports a murder in Andover. Poirot informs Hastings that an elderly tobacconist named Mrs. Asher has been killed. Although Hastings initially finds the death of a small shopkeeper unexciting, he agrees to accompany Poirot to Andover to examine an anonymous letter Poirot received.

In Andover, Poirot and Hastings meet Inspector Glen. A constable named Dover discovered Mrs. Asher's body at 1:00 a.m. in her shop; she had been killed by a severe blow to the back of the head. Based on customer sightings, the time of death is estimated to be between 5:30 and 6:05 a.m. The primary suspect is her estranged husband, Achille Asher, a German alcoholic who had previously threatened her. Inspector Glen notes that nothing was stolen from the shop. 

Upon reviewing the anonymous letter, Inspector Glen observes that the handwriting is too clear to have been written by Achille, whose body is paralyzed and prone to shaking. The inspector also notes the high quality of the paper and ink used. Poirot expresses concern regarding a coincidence in the letter, which mentions the date of the twenty-first.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
An inspector discusses the suspicious "ABC" letter with Poirot and Hastings, noting that Mary DeLaur, the niece of the deceased Mrs. Ascher, might be able to assist. The inspector reveals that Mrs. Ascher had a difficult past; she had previously left her husband, Franz Ascher, in 1922 after he pestered her for money. 

The police bring in Franz Ascher, who is found hiding in a car at a railway siding. Franz is described as a volatile and nervous man who denies killing his wife, claiming his previous threats against her were merely jokes. He provides an alibi, stating he was drinking with friends, including Dick Whillock, at the Seven Stars and the Red Dog. The superintendent takes Franz into custody as a suspect.
