Poirot investigates the crime scene and learns that an ABC train timetable—which lists stations alphabetically—was left open on the shop counter. This discovery suggests a connection to the "ABC" letter. At the morgue, Dr. Kerr explains that the murder weapon was likely a heavy object like a club or sandbag and notes that even a weak person could have wielded it. Based on the victim's position, Poirot concludes that she was likely facing a customer when attacked, which supports Franz's innocence. Poirot and Hastings then travel to Overton to interview Mary DeLaur, the victim's niece, at a large house near the village.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot interviews Mary, the niece of the murdered Mrs. Asher, regarding her aunt's death. Mary explains that she moved to London at age eleven to live with her aunt after her mother died. She describes her aunt's husband, a German man she calls "my old devil," as a cheat and a beggar who frequently threatened her aunt with violence, including threats to cut her throat. Despite this, Mary notes that her aunt was a fearless, sharp-tongued woman who often made her husband scurry away in fear. Mary reveals that her aunt had little money, only enough for funeral expenses, and had no other living relatives. Poirot asks if her aunt received anonymous letters signed "ABC," but Mary is unaware of any. After explaining he is a private detective, Poirot gives Mary his card and expresses a need for her potential future help.

Poirot and Hastings then travel to the crime scene in Andover at 5:30 PM to recreate the previous night's atmosphere. They arrive at A. Asher's shop, where a crowd has gathered. Inside the dim, dirty shop, a policeman explains that Mrs. Asher was found lying near a pile of items behind the counter. The doctor could not determine the murder weapon, though a pack of Player's cigarettes was found beside her. The policeman notes that a train schedule book was found open to the Andover-to-London page, suggesting the killer might not be local. No fingerprints were found during the initial search of the store or the counter.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Hastings visit the residence of the deceased Alice Asher, located behind her shop. A police officer informs Poirot that the fingerprints at the scene are too mixed to identify Asher's specifically. Inside the small, gloomy living quarters, Hastings observes three photographs on the mantelpiece: a cheap enlargement of Mary de la Rue, a professional portrait of an old woman in a fur collar (likely Miss Ross, who left Asher an inheritance), and an old wedding photo showing a handsome, military-bearing young man alongside a beautiful woman. Hastings notes the striking contrast between the young man in the photo and the current, disheveled state of Monsieur Archeur. Upstairs, they find Asher's bedroom containing personal items such as worn blankets, patched underwear, a cookbook, a novel titled "Oasis," and various ceramic ornaments.

Poirot then leads Hastings to a fruit and vegetable store across from the shop. Using a deceptive tactic, Poirot claims a tall, blond Russian man has been arrested for the murder, while Hastings contradicts him by suggesting the killer was a short, dark-skinned man. This provocation causes the shop employees to reveal they saw a tall, fair-haired man, though he lacked a beard. Poirot explains to a frustrated Hastings that he used these falsehoods to encourage the witnesses to speak freely.

Finally, Poirot approaches a neighboring house with "For Rent" signs. After a young boy announces their arrival, a thin-faced woman descends the stairs. Poirot poses as a reporter from the *Evening Star*, offering five pounds to write an article about Alice Asher. The woman invites them into her small room, which is furnished in the James I style.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Hastings interview Mrs. Fowler, a neighbor of the deceased Mrs. Ascher, paying her five pounds for her information. Mrs. Fowler reveals that Mrs. Ascher was a private person who suffered from the financial demands and behavior of her alcoholic husband, Franz Ascher. Mrs. Fowler notes that Franz was a "beast" who had previously threatened to ruin her. When Poirot asks about anonymous "ABC" letters or train timetables, Mrs. Fowler denies seeing any. She also suggests that Franz likely hid himself well to avoid being caught for the murder, noting the house lacks a back door.

Following this, they encounter Inspector Glen, who expresses frustration over the investigation. Glen reports that witnesses saw various groups of strangers near the shop—including tall, shifty men and short, bearded men—but no one saw a group of masked men with revolvers. No one has reported seeing Mr. Ascher.

The pair then interviews Mr. James Patrick, a thin, precise bank clerk who claims to be the last person to see Mrs. Ascher alive. Patrick states he arrived at her shop at 5:30 PM, a time he remembers by checking his watch against the church bell. He claims to know nothing of Mrs. Ascher’s personal life or her husband's alcoholism. While leaving, Poirot notices an "ABC" British Rail timetable on a shelf in Patrick's home and takes it under the pretense of checking train schedules. Finally, they attempt to speak with Albert Riddle, a hostile railway worker, but he refuses to cooperate, stating he has already told the police everything.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Hastings interview Bert Riddler, a large man who visited Mrs. Asher’s store on the night of her murder. Riddler claims he entered the closed shop shortly after six o'clock to buy "Golden Snowflake" cigarettes. He states that because no one appeared, he tapped on the counter and then left. He denies seeing the body behind the counter or touching a train schedule that was lying there covered. Riddler expresses anger at the implication of his involvement, suggesting instead that Mrs. Asher's husband was responsible.

After leaving, Poirot and Hastings take a 7:20 train from Andover. Poirot reveals he was teasing Hastings by providing a fake physical description of the killer. Regarding the actual evidence, Poirot notes that the ABC letter was left intentionally, as evidenced by the lack of fingerprints, suggesting the killer wiped it clean. Poirot speculates that the killer is a person who uses a typewriter and high-quality paper, driven by a deep-seated sense of inferiority and a desire for attention.

While Hastings looks for physical clues like ash or footprints, Poirot focuses on the psychological profile of the culprit. He dismisses Riddler as a suspect because the man's agitation contradicts the arrogance and confidence displayed in the ABC letters. Instead, Poirot considers the possibility that the killer might be someone like Mr. Patrick, who seeks attention by involving himself with the police. Although Poirot believes the killer likely originates from outside Andover, he notes that while the attack was masculine, the anonymous letter might have been written by a woman.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following the Andover incident, Poirot becomes uncharacteristically withdrawn and dismissive of the case, leading Hastings to conclude that Poirot feels defeated by the killer, ABC. Hastings observes that the media has largely ignored the murder of the elderly woman in Andover because it lacked sensationalism.

On July 25th, Hastings returns to London to find a second letter from ABC. The letter, written on high-quality paper, mentions that the Andover incident went smoothly and warns that the "game" is beginning, specifically directing attention to Bexhill-on-Sea on the twenty-fifth of the month. Poirot recognizes this as the second chapter of the ABC case.

The following morning, a high-level meeting is held involving the Chief Constable of Sussex, the Assistant Director of the CID, Inspector Glen, Chief Inspector Carter, Japp, Officer Croome, and the psychiatrist Dr. Thompson. The officials conclude the letters are from the same person responsible for the Andover murder. Poirot suggests the killer is following an alphabetical pattern, noting that the first victim, Mrs. Asher, and the next location, Bexhill, follow an alphabetical sequence. Dr. Thompson notes that the killer likely possesses a "fatal logic" characteristic of acute mania. The police decide to monitor people with names starting with "B," particularly shopkeepers, and watch for strangers in Bexhill. Inspector Glen also plans to monitor witnesses and suspects from the Andover case.

On the morning of July 25th, Poirot wakes Hastings to report that a murder occurred during the night. A girl named Elizabeth Barnard, a café waitress, was found dead on Bexhill Beach. She is believed to have died between 11:30 PM and 1:00 AM.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following the discovery of an ABC booklet open to the Bexhill train page under a murder victim, Blackstone and Poirot travel by police car toward Bexhill. They are joined by Inspector Croft, a young, educated, and somewhat arrogant detective who recently solved the Mabel Home murder involving a killer named Capers. During the journey, Croft discusses the psychological nature of serial murders and mentions his use of linguistic traps. Poirot questions Croft about the victim, a twenty-three-year-old waitress from the Turmeric Cat café, and learns she was strangled with her own thick woven belt. While Blackstone suggests the use of the belt indicates an animalistic nature, Poirot remains reserved.

Upon arriving in Bexhill, they meet Chief Inspector Carter and Inspector Kelsey. Carter provides details regarding the investigation: the victim's parents have been notified, and she has an older sister in London. The ABC booklet found at the scene has no fingerprints and was not purchased locally. The body was discovered at approximately 6 a.m. by Colonel Jerome during a morning walk with his dog. The time of death is estimated between midnight and 1 a.m. on the twenty-fifth, fulfilling the killer's stated timeline.

Inspector Croome and Inspector Kelsey begin their investigation by visiting the Turmeric Cat café. There, they meet the manager, Miss Merion, a thin woman with orange-yellow hair. While Miss Merion expresses distress over the tragedy and its impact on business, she appears excited by the attention. She reveals that the deceased girl was working her second summer at the café but claims to have no further information about her.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot, Captain Hastings, and Inspector Croome interview Miss Merrien, the owner of the Saffron Cat Cafe, regarding the death of waitress Elizabeth Barnard. Miss Merrien states that Barnard finished her shift at eight o'clock the previous night and had not mentioned any evening plans or visitors. Miss Merrien then calls over Miss Higley, a dark-haired waitress, to provide further information. 

Miss Higley describes Barnard as a quiet person who was not particularly close to the other staff. She reveals that Barnard had a handsome "friend" who worked as a clerk at the Coutts-Brenskell real estate agency near the station. Miss Higley notes that Barnard was wearing a new white dress the previous evening. Subsequent interviews with two other employees yield no further information regarding Barnard's whereabouts or plans.

The investigators then visit the home of Elizabeth Barnard's parents, Mr. and Mrs. Barnard, in a bungalow in Randarno. Mr. Barnard, a retired hardware businessman, and Mrs. Barnard, who is originally from Wales, are visibly distraught. Mr. Barnard explains that Barnard was an independent young woman who typically returned home by eleven o'clock, using a key kept under the mat. Mrs. Barnard mentions that Barnard was in love with Donald Fraser, a real estate agent at Coutts-Brenskell & Co., whom they saw approximately twice a week. The parents were unaware that Barnard would not be returning home the previous night. Inspector Croome requests permission to search Elizabeth Barnard's bedroom for items such as a diary or letters, and Mr. Barnard leads the investigators to the room.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While tying his shoelace, Hastings encounters Megan Barnard, who arrives by taxi carrying a small suitcase. Megan, described as having a fashionable but striking appearance with a short black bob, initially treats Hastings with suspicion and defiance. She asserts that her sister, Elizabeth "Betty" Barnard, was a good girl with no male friends, a statement Hastings notes was intended to mock the newspapers' tendency to idealize the dead.

Megan leads Hastings into a kitchen, where Hercule Poirot joins them. Megan recognizes Poirot as a fashionable private detective. Poirot challenges Megan's previous characterization of Betty, explaining his motive to find the truth by speaking to those who knew the deceased before her death. Megan admits that while she liked Betty, she considered her a "fool." She provides a photo of a blonde girl with a mischievous smile, noting that she herself is the "plain" sister.

Megan reveals that Betty enjoyed attention from handsome men and would banter with customers at the Yellow Cat Cafe, even if she intended to marry Donald "T" Fraser. Although Megan initially describes Donald as a calm and steady man, Poirot probes further, suggesting he may be violent or jealous. Megan eventually agrees to be truthful, admitting that Donald is naturally jealous and struggles to understand why Betty seeks the company of other men despite her affection for him.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Meghan Barnard informs Hercule Poirot that her sister, Betty, had a volatile relationship with Donald Fraser. Meghan explains that Betty frequently lied to avoid conflict with Donald, specifically regarding her outings. A month prior, Donald became furious after discovering Betty had lied about visiting a friend in Hastings when she was actually in Eastbourne with a married man. Meghan notes that Betty's habit of lying was a defense mechanism, and she suspects Betty may have avoided the man to prevent further arguments.

Meghan brings a pale and distraught Donald Fraser to the kitchen to speak with Poirot. After Poirot provides him with brandy to steady his nerves, Donald reveals that Betty had told him she was going to St. Leonards with a friend. Although he initially believed her, he later grew suspicious. He admits that he traveled to St. Leonards at eight o'clock to watch for her, but seeing no sign of her, he panicked and searched hotels, restaurants, and the harbor in Hastings. He eventually returned home around midnight.

During this conversation, Inspector Croome arrives from London. Poirot introduces Meghan and Donald to the inspector, noting that he has been gathering information to better understand the case. As Poirot retreats to the living room, he remarks to Hastings that he is surprised by the "murderer's goodness," a comment that leaves Hastings confused.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Officials, including the Deputy Commissioner, Assistant Commissioner, Dr. Thompson, Inspector Croft, and Hercule Poirot, meet to discuss whether to disclose the contents of anonymous letters to the media. The Bexhill murder case has received significantly more media attention than the Andree-Wolfe case because the victim was a young woman and the crime occurred at a popular seaside resort. Public interest has focused on the ABC train schedule, leading to a theory that the killer traveled by train.

The group debates the consequences of publicity. Croft and Poirot argue that the killer seeks infamy and public attention; therefore, making the case public would satisfy the killer's desire for notoriety. Dr. Thompson suggests that whether the police publicize the case or conduct a secret investigation, the killer will likely commit another crime to force their hand. Poirot speculates that the killer intends to follow the alphabet from A to Z, though he believes the killer will be caught before reaching the end. Inspector Croft expresses confidence that the killer will be caught before the letter F, theorizing that the criminal's overconfidence and growing carelessness will lead to mistakes.

Regarding the Bexhill case, Croft reports that a waiter at Sprand's in Eastbourne saw the victim dining with a middle-aged man in glasses on the 24th, and another witness saw her with a man resembling a naval officer near the Red Kidney Beans pub that same evening. Poirot emphasizes the need to find a motive beyond alphabetical obsession. While Dr. Thompson notes the victims are both women, Poirot questions if the killer is acting out of personal amusement or a specific psychological need. Poirot also proposes that the motive might be a personal hatred of him, either due to a past defeat or his foreign nationality.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a discussion regarding the motive of the killer known as ABC, Poirot suggests that understanding the killer's specific reasoning will reveal the next victim. While Inspector Crumb believes the killings are random, Poirot posits that the killer might be "kind-hearted," potentially acting to prevent others from suffering. Dr. Thompson disagrees, suggesting the motive is to enhance the killer's reputation. Crome suggests waiting for the next letter to make the threat public, which would alert people with names starting with "C" and potentially catch ABC.

The third letter from ABC arrives on a Friday evening at Poirot and Hastings' home. The letter, dated the 27th, mocks Poirot's abilities and states that the next crime will occur on the 30th in Churston. Upon reading the date, Poirot realizes that today is already the 30th. He discovers that the envelope was addressed to "White Horse Apartments" instead of his actual residence, "White Harbor Apartments." This error suggests the killer may have been influenced by a bottle of White Horse whisky sitting in front of him while writing.

Realizing the crime is imminent, Poirot contacts Superintendent Croome, who immediately alerts Cheltenham. Poirot and Hastings decide to take a late-night sleeper train from Paddington to reach the area. After a brief disagreement over Hastings' frantic packing, Poirot takes over the task. At Paddington Station, they meet Inspector Crumb, who confirms that despite alerting everyone with names starting with "C," no news has been received. The group concludes that the address error on the envelope was likely an unconscious slip caused by the presence of whisky.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While traveling by train, Hercule Poirot and Hastings learn from Inspector Croome that Sir Clement Clarke, a renowned throat specialist and Chinese art collector, has been found dead. Sir Clarke was struck violently on the head and discovered on a walking route near his seaside home in Devonshire. An open ABC train timetable was placed face up on his body. Poirot notes that this is the first time they have encountered a "non-private" murder involving a stranger, rather than a crime involving someone close to the victim. Poirot believes the killer's desire for publicity might lead to carelessness.

The investigators arrive at Kensington House, where they meet the deceased's brother, Franklin Clark, a resourceful man of action, and the nervous butler, Devereaux. During breakfast, Franklin expresses skepticism regarding the serial killer theory and the lack of a clear motive. Inspector Croome suggests the killer's motive may be to create a sensation and become a public figure. 

When questioned about his brother's state of mind, Franklin reveals that Sir Clarke was often agitated due to the poor health of his wife, who suffers from incurable cancer. Franklin mentions he has recently returned from the East and noticed changes in his brother. In response to a hypothetical question from Poirot regarding a body found with a revolver, Franklin states he would immediately conclude the death was a suicide.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Croome asserts that Sir Michael’s death was not a suicide, suggesting a killer scouted the area to exploit Sir Michael's nightly walking habit. Franklin Clarke explains that while the area near Chetton Fellows is usually quiet, the summer season brings many visitors to nearby Broadlands and Elbury Bay, making it easy for a stranger to blend in. The butler, Deverill, reports that no one came looking for Sir Michael yesterday and no suspicious persons were seen by the staff.

Franklin introduces Tola Grey, Sir Michael’s secretary, who manages his correspondence. She confirms that Sir Michael received no letters with the initials "ABC" and has not mentioned seeing any loiterers. Croome leads the group to the site of the crime: a grassy ridge overlooking the sea, accessible via an abandoned path leading to Elbury Bay. Franklin describes the route Sir Michael took through the fields and back home. The group reaches the location where the body was discovered near a hedge, where Croome notes the victim was likely attacked while standing in the shade of a tree.

Upon returning to the house, a doctor states that the death was instantaneous. Tola Grey expresses intense fear regarding the "D" in the ABC sequence, fearing the next murder. While Franklin expresses frustration with Poirot’s perceived incompetence, he mentions having a plan and wanting to consult the detective. Tola remains preoccupied with the whereabouts of the killer.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After watching a movie at the Athena Theatre in Tochi, Alexander Bonaparte Caste (ABC) reads a newspaper in the Princess Garden facing Tokei Harbor. The news reports the murder of Sir Charles Clarke in Chester, suggesting it is the work of a serial killer. The article links this crime to the murder of Elizabeth Barnard in Bexhill a month prior, noting that an ABC train timetable was found near Clarke's body. While reading, Mr. Cast is approached by a young man in a blue Eltex shirt who discusses the madness caused by war. Mr. Cast, appearing shaken and mentioning post-war headaches, causes the young man to leave in haste. As Mr. Cast walks toward town, he observes the public's intense speculation regarding the murder.

The murder of Sir Charles Clarke has caused a national sensation, turning the United Kingdom into an "amateur detective army." The media, including the Daily Shine, has sensationalized the case, and Hercule Poirot has become a target of both public defense and journalistic harassment. Captain Hastings notes that newspapers often distort Poirot's words, but Poirot believes this media frenzy might cause the killer to underestimate him. While Scotland Yard and local police conduct exhaustive questioning of hotel managers and transport workers, Poirot remains focused on the psychology of the killer. He explains to Hastings that while the police focus on physical clues, he is building a psychological profile. Poirot predicts that another murder will almost certainly occur, and that the killer's next crime will reveal his true identity and nature.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot informs Hastings that he intends to use a "fishing" strategy to catch the killer, focusing on the character of the person rather than their identity. Poirot expresses relief that the innocent will not have to live under the toxic atmosphere of suspicion. He proposes a plan to extract information from the victims' friends, relatives, and servants through repeated, casual conversation, believing that trivial details will eventually surface and provide clues.

Poirot shows Hastings a letter from Mary DeLaur, a clever girl who contacted the sister of a victim in Bexhill. DeLaur expresses a desire to unite with others to find the truth and mentions she is coming to London. Poirot also shows Hastings a letter from Franklin Clarke, stating he will visit the following day.

The next afternoon, Franklin Clarke visits Poirot and expresses dissatisfaction with Inspector Crook's investigation. Clarke proposes forming a special task force under Poirot's command, composed of the victims' friends and relatives, to prepare for the next murder. Clarke offers to fund the group using the inheritance left by his brother. He suggests including Megan Barnard, Donald Fraser (the deceased girl's fiancé), the niece of the lady from Anderwall, and Miss Gray. When mentioning Miss Gray, Clarke becomes visibly shy. Clarke also mentions his previous travels in China while procuring items for his brother. Poirot agrees to the plan, reiterating his belief that constant talking and exchanging opinions will lead to an insight.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Members of a special group meet at Poirot's house to discuss the investigation into a killer who murdered an old woman, a young girl, and an elderly man. The group includes Tora Grey, Megan Barnard, Mary DeLour, Franklin Clarke, and Donald Fraser. Poirot explains that the killer is likely a premeditated actor who scouted locations such as Andover and Bexhill, and who may be either a man or a woman.

Poirot begins questioning the members about their activities and observations. Franklin Clarke recounts his day, mentioning fishing, eating Irish stew, and driving to Peardon to mail letters. He recalls seeing a fat woman in a striped silk skirt, two children with a fox terrier, and a blonde girl swimming. Tora Grey states she dealt with Sir Clement's letters and performed needlework before going to bed early.

Megan Barnard recalls seeing her sister, Betty, two weeks prior. She notes that Betty mentioned being short of money after buying a hat and dresses, and expressed dislike for a café worker named Milly Higley. When Poirot asks if Betty mentioned meeting a man, Megan responds coldly. Donald Fraser, who was waiting at a café for Betty on the night of the incident, insists he does not remember seeing anyone specific on the beach.

Mary DeLour shares that she received a letter from her aunt two days before the crime, in which her aunt mentioned being bothered by an "old devil." Mary expresses grief over the lost opportunity to celebrate her birthday with her aunt. Franklin Clarke responds by reflecting on the sadness of small, lost things, citing a memory of a woman killed by a car just after buying new shoes.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Megan Barnard shares a memory of her mother's devastation following Betty's death, noting her mother bought stockings for her that she never got to use. This creates an emotional bond between Megan and Franklin Clark. Tora Grey shifts the conversation to future planning, prompting Franklin Clark to suggest that the group join forces once a fourth letter arrives.

Hercule Poirot proposes several lines of inquiry. He suggests Megan use an "offensive strategy" by arguing with a waitress named Milly Higley to provoke her into revealing her true feelings about Betty. He also suggests Donald Fraser show interest in the waitress. Poirot recommends Mary interview children near her aunt's shop, as they may have seen people entering the shop. Tora Grey notes that the third letter was postmarked from Putney (SW15), suggesting the killer, ABC, is from London. Franklin Clark proposes placing a provocative advertisement to attract ABC's attention, an idea Poirot deems worth trying despite potential danger. Franklin records the plan: Megan and Milly Higley, Donald Fraser and Milly Higley, the children of Andersson, and the advertisement.

After the group disperses, Poirot reveals to Hastings that he manipulated the conversation to give the group a sense of purpose. He observes the interpersonal tensions, noting Franklin's sudden rapport with Megan and Tora Grey's anger. Tora Grey returns to speak with Poirot privately, clarifying that she did not leave Compiegne willingly. She explains that while Franklin is loyal, it was the ill and delusional Mrs. Clarke who insisted she leave the house due to irrational suspicion.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Miss Tolla reveals her sadness regarding Mrs. Clarke's dislike of her, she departs. Poirot observes that Tolla is both brave and cunning, noting her fashionable attire. Poirot then expresses a vague, nagging impression that he and Hastings said something meaningful during a previous conversation, though he cannot immediately recall what it was.

Later, Poirot and Hastings visit Kempthorpe to see Mrs. Clarke. They are met by Nurse Capstick, who explains that while Mrs. Clarke is receiving new therapy, her condition remains poor. Capstick notes that Mrs. Clarke and her late husband, Carl, were a very happy couple. She mentions that after Carl's death, Sir Carmichael took up collecting as a consolation and worked with Miss Grey to re-catalog the museum before Miss Grey eventually left due to Mrs. Clarke's poor health.

In a second-floor living room, Poirot and Hastings find Mrs. Clarke in a dazed, physically weakened state, appearing to be under the influence of drugs that make time feel stagnant. Mrs. Clarke discusses Carl’s excellent physical fitness and expresses concern that Franklin Clarke is impulsive and easily led astray. When the topic of Carl's death arises, Mrs. Clarke asserts that the murderer must have been around the house that day. When Poirot mentions that the servants and Miss Grey claimed no strangers visited the house, Mrs. Clarke becomes agitated and calls Miss Grey a liar. She reveals a deep animosity toward Miss Grey, claiming Carl was overly sympathetic to her status as an orphan. Mrs. Clarke further discloses that she used her illness to pressure Franklin into paying Miss Grey three months' salary to ensure she would leave the house immediately.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While visiting the ailing Mrs. Clarke, Poirot and Hastings learn a significant detail regarding Miss Grey. Mrs. Clarke accuses Miss Grey of lying, claiming she personally witnessed the girl talking to a poorly dressed stranger on the front steps at eleven o'clock on the morning of Mr. Clarke's death. Poirot notes that Miss Grey had previously claimed no strangers had visited the house. During their return to London, Poirot reflects on the various family dramas surrounding the case, including the lives of the Anders, Bexhill, and Cheshire families.

Upon arriving at White's Court, Poirot and Hastings are visited by Donald Fraser, who appears extremely nervous and prone to stuttering. After being served food and drink, Fraser confesses that he has been plagued by recurring, vivid dreams for three consecutive nights. In these dreams, he is on a beach searching for a girl named Betty; he finds her, but then proceeds to strangle her with a belt. He describes the horror of realizing the girl's face is actually Megan's. A distressed Fraser asks Poirot if these dreams imply he is a killer.

The conversation is interrupted by the arrival of a fourth letter. Poirot opens the message, which mocks the police and Poirot for their inability to solve the case. The letter provides a cryptic clue, stating that the letter "T" is still far away and announcing that the next event will occur on September 11th in Doncaster, signed "ABC."

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following the receipt of a new letter, Inspector Croft of Scotland Yard arrives to manage the investigation. Franklin Clarke informs the Inspector that a private task force has been formed to handle the matter. Croft plans to announce the upcoming Wednesday's threat in Tadcaster, mobilizing police and citizens to watch anyone with a name starting with the letter "D." However, Clarke points out that the St. Leger Stakes horse race is being held in Tadcaster on that same Wednesday. This realization leads the group to conclude that the murderer, ABC, likely intends to strike at the racecourse during the event.

Tola Grey arrives with news of another letter addressed to Franklin Clarke, which specifies the location as Tadcaster on the day of the St. Leger Stakes. As the group discusses the difficulty of monitoring a massive crowd, Poirot urges them to look inward at the information they already possess. He challenges the group's claim that they know nothing about the killer.

When Poirot mentions the theme of "stockings," Tola Grey recalls a strange man who approached her at the door to sell stockings on the day Sir Michael Clarke was murdered. Poirot connects this to previous details: Meg Barndon mentioned her mother buying stockings from a door-to-door salesman, and a neighbor, Mrs. Foley, also mentioned a man selling products. Poirot concludes that a man selling stockings has been scouting the area during all three murders. Tola Grey describes the man as a bent-over individual in an old coat wearing glasses, someone who would not draw attention to himself. Poirot declares that this description reveals the murderer's true nature and identity.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Alexander Bonaparte Cast sits motionless at breakfast, appearing unwell and anxious. His landlady, Mrs. Marbury, notices he has not eaten and expresses concern. Cast insists he must go to Cheltenham for an important appointment to maintain his professional reputation. During their conversation, Mrs. Marbury mentions the ABC murder case, noting that the killer is predicted to strike in Tangcaster tomorrow during a horse race. Cast reacts with visible tension. After Mrs. Marbury leaves, Cast packs his luggage, including several flat cardboard boxes, and prepares to depart.

In the hallway, Cast speaks with Miss Lily, a girl from an adjoining room. He asks her if she believes in omens or premonitions before saying a heavy goodbye. Lily notes that he was near the site of the previous ABC murder in Torquay. After Cast departs, Lily remarks that he seems "crazy."

Meanwhile, Inspector Croome instructs his subordinates to obtain lists of all stocking manufacturers and agents, following a suggestion from Hercule Poirot. One subordinate expresses skepticism regarding Poirot's abilities.

Later, Tom Hattinger tells Lily that he saw Mr. Cast at Euston station. Tom claims he found Cast's lost ticket and asserts that Cast is traveling to Doncaster for a horse race, rather than Cheltenham. Lily expresses concern because the next predicted location for the ABC murders is Doncaster. She recalls that during the previous murder in Bexhill, Cast had gone out to the beach because he had forgotten his swimming trunks. Tom jokingly suggests that Cast might be the killer, but Lily dismisses the idea.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
On September 11th, Hastings, Polo, Clark, Fraser, Megan Barnard, Tola Grey, and Mary Dalloway gather, feeling helpless regarding the ongoing murders. They hope to identify the killer through a chance encounter, with Tola Grey being the person most likely to recognize him. Under pressure, Tola becomes hysterical and expresses fear that she will fail to recognize the killer's face. Poirot comforts her, using a roulette metaphor to explain that while the killer has had luck so far, the "law of chance" dictates that luck will eventually swing back to the investigators. Poirot believes the killer, driven by obsession and a sense of invincibility, will eventually make a mistake.

The group discusses the police response, noting that Chief Inspector Crow and Colonel Anderson are monitoring the town and racetrack with undercover officers and patrols. Poirot predicts the killer will stick to his plan because he is too obsessed to admit defeat. The group agrees to remain on high alert, patrolling streets in the morning and stationed at the racecourse in the afternoon.

While the women prepare to depart, Franklin Clarke pulls Poirot aside to discuss his sister-in-law, Charlotte. Clarke attempts to defend Tola Grey against potential prejudices held by Charlotte, suggesting that Charlotte’s suspicions regarding Tola and Clarke's brother are merely the "wild imaginings" of a woman suffering from illness and morphine use. To prove that the relationship between Tola and his brother was professional and innocent, Clarke offers Poirot a letter he received from his brother while in the Malayan Union.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Franklin presents a letter to Poirot to defend Tola Grey against accusations made by Mrs. Clarke. The letter, written by Franklin's deceased brother, describes Tola as a charming companion who provided comfort during difficult times and whom the brother considered like a daughter. Franklin expresses indignation that the brother's wife kicked Tola out of the house following his death. Poirot assures Franklin that he will use his own judgment regarding Tola.

As the group prepares for an expedition, Poirot instructs Hastings to protect Mary Deulacour. Poirot explains that because her surname begins with the letter "D," she is a potential target for the killer, ABC, who might attempt a "fourth strike" against Poirot by targeting her. After Hastings agrees, Poirot spins a roulette wheel and, seeing it land on red, expresses optimism about turning the tide.

At the Royal Cinema, a man named Mr. Leadbetter finishes watching a film titled *The Unknowing Swallow*. As the audience exits, a commotion occurs. A person previously thought to be sleeping is discovered to be dead, with blood visible and an "ABC" note found under a seat.

Meanwhile, Mr. Castor returns to his room at the Black Swan Hotel. He notices blood on his sleeve and discovers a long, thin knife in his pocket covered in red residue. After washing the blood from his sleeve in a washbasin, a girl enters the room to deliver hot water. When she notices the red water in the basin, Castor claims he cut his hand.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a woman leaves, Mr. Castor waits in silence before rushing out of a building. He navigates through a courtyard past two drivers and heads toward a street, hoping to escape via a special train at the station.

Meanwhile, Inspector Crumb and Detective Croft interview witnesses regarding a murder at a cinema. Mr. Liddell describes a tall, bald, dark-skinned man with a limp who entered the theater after the film had started. Liddell believes the man pretended to stumble to stab a victim. The doorman, Jamison, reports seeing a man collapsed in the cheap ticket section with blood on his coat. Jamison notes that several people left the area shortly before the discovery, including Mr. Jeffrey Parnell and a young man named Sam Baker.

Hercule Poirot and his companion arrive to assist the investigation. Colonel Anderson informs them that the victim is George Erskine, a barber, and that an ABC book was found near his feet. This is identified as another "ABC murder," though the method was a stabbing rather than previous methods of strangulation or blunt force trauma.

The next witness, Roger Emmanuel Downes, the headmaster of Heathfield School, recounts how he discovered the body after attempting to wake a man he thought was sleeping in the seat next to him. Colonel Anderson confronts Downes, noting that Downes has a similar height and build to the deceased and was wearing a woolen scarf. Anderson suggests the killer actually intended to stab Downes but mistakenly attacked Erskine instead. The revelation causes Downes to collapse from shock.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Mr. Duns leaves the meeting, Colonel Anderson reveals that Inspector Rice has arranged for two people to watch his house. Poirot expresses confidence that the meticulous killer, ABC, will eventually make a mistake. The investigation is interrupted by Mr. Bauer from the Black Swan Hotel, who arrives with a young woman named Mary Stroud. 

Mary reports an incident from around 4:45, where she entered a guest's room to deliver hot water and found a man washing his hands in a sink filled with red water. Her employer, Mr. Boll, adds that the man was shirtless and holding a wet sleeve. Mary describes the man as being of medium height, slightly hunched, wearing glasses, a shabby black coat, and a homburg hat. Following this report, a constable retrieves the hotel register, which contains a cramped signature reading "A.B. Case" or "ABC." The register also notes the guest brought a large box filled with silk stockings. Inspector Croome congratulates Poirot, noting his intuition was correct.

Later, at Scotland Yard, Detective Inspector Croome receives a visitor named Tom Hattinger. Hattinger claims his girlfriend Lily’s mother rents a room in Camden Town to a man named Castor, whom he describes as a mild-mannered, down-on-his-luck individual. Hattinger notes that Castor had mentioned traveling to Cheltenham and was reportedly in Chester and at the seaside during previous murders. Although Lily believes Castor is too harmless to be the killer, Hattinger expresses growing suspicion regarding the man's character.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Tom Hargreaves meets with Detective Inspector Crome to report his findings regarding the murderer known as A.B. Keith or Cash. Tom explains that he and Lily investigated whether Mr. Castor’s initials match the killer's and checked his whereabouts during the first murder in Underwall. They discovered that on June 21st, the day Bert Smith's boat docked at Southampton, Mr. Castor was out of the house because Mrs. Marbury had provided a bed for her visiting brother from Canada instead of Bert Smith.

Inspector Crome notes that while the evidence is not conclusive, he intends to interview Mr. Castor. Tom also reports that Mrs. Marbury observed Mr. Castor acting strangely, buying numerous newspapers, and talking to himself. Meanwhile, Crome reviews the Chester case file and discusses a witness named Hill, who saw a man talking to himself after watching the movie "Unrecognized Sparrow" at the Athena Theatre in Toke. Crome notes the man's behavior matches descriptions provided by Mary Stroud and Tom Hargreaves. Crome decides to have people monitor a location in Camden Town, speak with the Assistant Police Commissioner, and bring a flustered Mr. Castor to the station for a statement.

After meeting Crome, Tom tells Lily about the investigation. Later, at Mrs. Marbury's house, Mr. Castor receives a telephone call. To deflect suspicion from his strange behavior, he lies to Mrs. Marbury, claiming his sister has just had a son. This lie eases Mrs. Marbury's suspicions. As Mr. Castor prepares to leave, he reflects on the telephone call, which warned him that a detective inspector from Scotland Yard might visit him.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
An unnamed man reflects on a phone call from Lily, wondering if she knows about the approaching detective inspector. Concerned that Mrs. Mabrey might see him, he decides to leave his house and goes outside.

At Scotland Yard, a meeting is held involving the Assistant Commissioner of Police, Inspector Crumb, Poirot, and the narrator. Inspector Crumb reports on the investigation into Mr. Cast, a door-to-door sock salesman. Crumb details Cast's movements through Pitt, Bexhill, and Ashford, noting that the socks sold by Cast were identical to those belonging to Miss Drew. Crumb also reports that Cast had recently received a phone call before leaving his residence. A search of Cast's home revealed note paper, a large quantity of socks, and boxes containing eight new ABC train timetables. Most significantly, Crumb discovered a knife with dried blood hidden behind a coat rack in the hall.

While the Assistant Commissioner expresses confidence in catching the murderer, Poirot questions the motive. Inspector Crumb suggests the killer may suffer from an inferiority complex or paranoid delusions, potentially associating Poirot with the police. Poirot argues that the selection of victims, such as Betty Barnard, was not accidental but premeditated, suggesting the killer scouted locations and habits in advance. This observation causes visible anguish in Donald Fraser.

Meanwhile, Mr. Cast stands near a fruit and vegetable shop, staring at a vacant storefront. He is hungry, dizzy, and out of money. He sees a poster for the "ABC Murder Case" featuring an interview with Hercule Poirot and mutters to himself, wondering if Poirot knows his secrets, before continuing to walk.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Alexander Bonaparte Cast, an epileptic man, walks toward the Anderson Police Station, where he suddenly collapses and faints. On a clear November day, Dr. Thompson, Inspector Japp, and Hastings visit Hercule Poirot, who is suffering from bronchitis. Japp informs them that the case against Cast is going to trial. Japp notes that the prosecutor, Lucas, may attempt to use an insanity defense, though Poirot argues that an insane killer will not be acquitted.

Dr. Thompson disputes the possibility that Cast committed the Bexhill murder while in a trance-like epileptic state, noting that premeditated letters written by Cast suggest the crime was carefully planned. Poirot remains focused on the mystery of why Cast wrote those letters specifically to him. Thompson suggests that Cast’s exaggerated names, Alexander and Napoleon, indicate a desire for a grand rival, implying that Poirot is that intended opponent.

Japp discusses a significant complication: an alibi provided by a stubborn mining engineer named Strange. Strange claims he played dominoes with Cast at the White Cross Hotel in Eastbourne until midnight on July 25th. If true, Cast could not have traveled the fourteen miles to Bexhill to murder Betty Barnard between midnight and one in the morning. While Cast's guilt in the murders of Teng Carr, Chester, and Anderswal is certain due to physical evidence, the Bexhill murder remains a logistical puzzle. Poirot concludes that the case is not truly closed because they do not yet understand the paradoxical nature of Cast, whom he describes as a mixture of cunning and stupidity, and coldness and nobility.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a realization sparked by Hastings' observation regarding alphabetical order and the name Betty Bardner in Bexhill, Hercule Poirot claims to have discovered the motive behind the killer's choices, including Mrs. Asher, Sir Charles Clarke, and the staging of the murder at Tengcombe. Poirot intends to gather more information from the Special Branch before confronting the killer, "ABC," through a conversation designed to reveal the truth through lies.

Poirot spends several days investigating in Bexhill, accompanied by Hastings and a special group. His investigation includes visiting the Bardners, interviewing Mrs. Bardner about Mr. Cast, and checking the hotel where Cast stayed. He also examines the beach where Betty Bardner's body was found and observes the route from the beach to the bus station for Eastbourne. After a brief stop at the Golden Cat Cafe, where he flirts with the waitress Milly Higley, Poirot announces he is heading to Eastbourne for further investigation.

Back at the hotel, Franklin Clarke suggests Poirot is merely trying to destroy evidence of the killer's whereabouts. Poirot counters by proposing a game of "Truth or Dare," requiring everyone to swear to tell the truth. He asks Franklin Clarke a trivial question about hats at the Ascot race, which Clark answers. He asks Donald Fraser about his holiday schedule, causing Fraser visible distress. Poirot then asks Tara Grey if she would marry Sir Charles Clarke if Lady Clark were to die; Grey refuses, citing her feelings for Sir Michael. Finally, Poirot turns to Megan Bard, sharply asking her if she wants the investigation to reveal the truth.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Meghan denies a statement, Poirot asks Mary Deauville if she has a boyfriend, causing her visible discomfort. Poirot then decides to depart for Eastbourne with Hastings. While traveling along the coast road from Pevensey, they stop to visit Pevensey Castle. Nearby, Hastings observes a group of young girls singing a song about catching a fox and putting it in a cage. Poirot finds the lyrics "terrible," comparing the idea of a fox being caged to the "quick and cruel death" of a fox hunt.

Poirot decides to forgo the trip to Eastbourne to meet privately with Alexander Bonaparte Castor, using a Home Office license obtained through police connections. During their meeting, Castor appears nervous and hunched. When Poirot identifies himself as the recipient of the letters Castor allegedly wrote, Castor denies authorship, claiming a "grand conspiracy" is being orchestrated against him by his enemies.

Castor discusses his life, noting that his ambitious mother gave him his name and that he has always felt inadequate and timid. He mentions that he enjoyed the equality of the war but suffered a head injury that caused occasional lapses in memory or behavior. He describes his struggle to survive the Great Depression by working as a clerk and later selling stockings. When Poirot suggests that the company denies his claims, Castor insists he has typed letters as evidence. Poirot then reveals that the letters were typed on a specific typewriter—the one found in Castor's own room.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an interrogation, Mr. Castor (referred to as Mr. Castle and Mr. Cast in the text) defends himself against accusations regarding the ABC murders. He claims the letters received were a conspiracy and that the ABC train timetables found in a closet were mistaken for stockings. When questioned about crossing out Mrs. Ascher's name on a list, he claims he simply decided to start with her. He attempts to prove his innocence regarding the Bexhill murder by stating he was playing dominoes in Eastbourne. 

Mr. Castor recounts a somber memory of a man he met while playing dominoes who claimed that fate is written on one's hand. This man predicted that Mr. Castor would become famous but would die a violent death on the gallows. Following this recollection, Mr. Castor suffers a headache and collapses. Upon regaining a calm state, he admits to Poirot that he killed someone, though he confesses he does not know why he did it.

In his final case analysis, Hercule Poirot explains that the case was an "ABC mystery" centered on the killer's psyche rather than a traditional motive. Poirot notes that while the murders followed an alphabetical pattern, the killer's decision to write specifically to him—rather than the police or newspapers—was the central puzzle. Poirot rejects the theories of bloodlust or simple inferiority, noting that a killer driven by bloodlust would hide their tracks rather than advertise them. He concludes that the killer chose victims to avoid suspicion and used the ABC timetables as part of an obsessive pattern.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot analyzes the motives and patterns of the "ABC" killer. He notes that while the killer follows an alphabetical order, the victims—Mrs. Ascher, Betty Barnard, and Sir Charles Clarke—lack a consistent demographic profile regarding age or gender. Poirot infers the killer may be a "railway enthusiast" with a "boyish" side, evidenced by the inclusion of train timetables. Regarding Betty Barnard’s death, Poirot suggests the killer used her own belt to strangle her, implying a friendly or intimate relationship. He speculates the killer used sexual charm to lure her into a fatal jest.

Poirot discusses the murder of Sir Charles Clarke and notes that the killer's choice of locations did not follow a strict alphabetical sequence. He also mentions the Chester case, where an incorrect address on an envelope hindered progress. A key clue emerged regarding a stocking salesman seen near crime scenes, though the description provided by Miss Grey did not match Poirot's profile of Betty Barnard's killer. The fourth murder involved George Erskine, whom the killer mistakenly killed instead of a man named Duns. This error allowed the police to capture and pursue the suspect, Alexander P. Castel.

Despite Castel's capture and expected imprisonment in Broadmoor Asylum, Poirot remains unsatisfied. He presents two hypotheses to explain Castel's alibi during the Bexhill murder. First, he suggests Castel may not have committed the second murder, noting the discrepancy between Castel's personality and the killer of Betty Barnard. Second, Poirot reveals his most significant discovery: the letters sent to the police were not the work of a madman, but were sane forgeries designed to mimic a serial killer.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot confronts Franklin Clarke, revealing that the "ABC" killer is actually Franklin himself. Poirot explains that the series of murders was a calculated distraction designed to mask a single, premeditated murder. By creating a pattern of crimes linked by initials, the killer intended to divert attention away from the true motive: the inheritance of Sir Clement Clarke’s estate. Poirot notes that while Sir Clement's wife holds life tenure, Franklin stands to inherit the property upon her death.

Poirot identifies Franklin as the mastermind who manipulated Mr. Castor, a timid and indecisive man, to serve as a scapegoat. Franklin used Castor's initials and personality to frame him, even providing Castor with a typewriter and instructions to deliver letters. The motive for the murder was fear of the future; Franklin believed that if Sir Clement married Miss Tora Grey, they might have children, which would permanently deprive Franklin of his inheritance.

To execute the plan, Franklin selected victims whose names and locations matched the ABC pattern. He first killed Mrs. Ashe in Andover. For the "B" victim, he scouted cafes to find Betty Barnard, a girl he courted under the pretense of being married. Poirot concludes that Franklin orchestrated the entire scheme, using Castor as a tool to implement a series of crimes that would provide cover for his ultimate goal of securing his brother's wealth.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals that Franklin Clarke is the serial killer behind the ABC murders. Poirot explains that Clarke intentionally misdelivered the third letter to him by using a clever address error—"White Harbor - White Horse"—to ensure a private detective would receive it rather than the police. This allowed Clarke to escape the crime scene before the police could investigate. Clarke’s motive was to kill his brother, but he staged a third murder at Tancaster to prevent suspicion of an abrupt end to the case.

To frame Alexander Bonaparte Cast, Clarke staged a crime at a cinema. He stabbed a random man in the dark, placed the ABC book on the victim, and then bumped into Cast, wiping the knife on Cast's sleeve and planting it in his pocket. Due to Cast's epilepsy and memory gaps, Cast believed he had committed the murders himself. Poirot explains that he knew Cast was innocent and that Clarke had used a weighted club for the Andover and Cheltenham murders. Evidence against Clarke included witness identifications in Bexhill and, most decisively, Clarke's fingerprints on Cast's typewriter.

When confronted, Clarke attempted suicide with a revolver, but Poirot's valet had previously pickpocketed the gun and removed the bullets. Clarke is arrested by Scotland Yard detectives. In the aftermath, Poirot explains his methods to Hastings, admitting that the fingerprint evidence was a bluff to break Clarke's composure. Later, Cast visits the group, now a celebrity due to the case, and expresses his intent to sell his story to the newspapers.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Mole, a short man with a decisive chin, has a breakfast with his tall sister, Georgeina. Mr. Mole expresses frustration regarding the poor quality of his food, the incompetence of the government, and the perceived unreliability of young women. He specifically mentions Glady, who is returning to Somerset because her aunt had a stroke, and suspects Glady is being influenced by an unsuitable young man. Mr. Mole also complains about his new, rude, and incompetent employee. He notes he is busy with patients, including Miss Sainsbury-Sil, who has a toothache. Georgeina mentions that a capable dentist named Riley might be a drinker because he shakes.

At the Savoy Hotel, Mr. Amberloz reflects smugly on a plan involving a man named Dmitri and a tavern keeper named Kostasopoulos. He consults a notebook noting an appointment at 58 Charlotte Queen Street at twelve o'clock.

At the Glengowell Court Hotel, Miss Sainsbury-Sil discusses her toothache with her friend, Mrs. Boleisso. Despite the lack of current pain, Mrs. Boleisso insists she see a dentist. Miss Sainsbury-Sil agrees, noting that Mr. Mole is a careful dentist.

During a board meeting, Samuel Rosethstein observes that the chairman, Alastair Brent, appears unsettled and uncharacteristically acidic. After the meeting, Brent declines a ride from Rosethstein, stating he is going to see the dentist.

Hercule Poirot arrives at 58 Charlotte Street and is admitted into the waiting room by a red-haired doorman. Poirot finds the tastefully furnished waiting room eerie. Inside, he encounters a tanned man with a mustache who looks at him with disgust.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a waiting room, a soldier named Arlo Bondi is called away by a pageboy. Hercule Poirot observes a young man in his thirties entering the lobby, whom Poirot perceives as a dangerous and potentially murderous individual. A pageboy then leads Poirot to a second-floor suite.

Inside the dental suite, Poirot meets Mr. Morley, a professional dentist. Despite his usual self-confidence, Poirot experiences intense fear and cowardice regarding the dental procedure. As Morley prepares the chair and begins an examination, he identifies that Poirot requires fillings in two places and attention for a specific molar. Poirot experiences significant anxiety regarding the use of the dental drill.

During the procedure, Morley explains that his assistant, Miss Neville, is away in the countryside due to a sick relative, which has made his morning schedule tense. Morley discusses his upcoming appointment with Alistair Brent, a man of immense, quiet financial power in Britain who lives an unassuming life. While Morley works, he engages Poirot in conversation, discussing democratic ideals and the Belgian monarchy; Poirot corrects Morley, noting that he is Belgian rather than French. Morley mentions his ability to remember faces even when he forgets names, noting a past patient he has yet to identify. After completing the fillings, Morley concludes the appointment, and Poirot departs, remarking that while everyone in the lobby previously looked like a "bad guy," his perspective has changed.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After finishing a dental appointment with Mr. Morley at 58 Charlotte Queen Street, Hercule Poirot leaves the clinic. In the waiting room, he observes a nervous young man and a well-dressed man named Mr. Brent. As Poirot exits, he encounters a middle-aged woman in shabby, green clothing who is getting out of a taxi; he assists her by retrieving a fallen shoe buckle and her handbag.

Later, while digesting lunch, Poirot receives a phone call from Inspector Japp. Japp informs him that Mr. Morley has shot himself. Poirot returns to the clinic, where he finds Japp and a photographer examining the body. Mr. Morley lies by the fireplace with a small black hole below his right temple and a handgun near his extended right hand. Although the gun only bears Morley's fingerprints, Japp expresses suspicion because Morley was healthy, successful, and had no apparent motive for suicide. Furthermore, the gunshot was not heard, likely due to the soundproofing of the doors and street noise.

The body was discovered around 1:30 by the doorman, Alfred Biggs, after a 12:30 patient became impatient. Japp notes discrepancies in the patient records: the bellhop identified a frustrated patient as Miss Hilti, but the appointment book lists her as Miss Corby. Additionally, the last recorded buzzer press was at 12:05 to bring up a patient named Mr. Ambrosio of the Savoy Hotel. Upon hearing this, Poirot privately questions the accuracy of the page's information.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Japp and Hercule Poirot discuss the death of the dentist, Mr. Morley. Japp notes that a patient, Mr. Ambrosio, left the clinic at 12:25 and reported that the dentist appeared calm. Medical evidence from a pathologist suggests Morley was shot before 1:00. Poirot finds the theory of suicide unlikely, noting that Morley was energetic and orderly at 12:25. Japp observes that the body's position seems odd and there are marks on the carpet suggesting something was dragged.

While examining the scene, Poirot notices a door leading to a small office containing a desk and tea utensils. Japp identifies this as the office of Miss Neville, the dentist's secretary, who is absent. Japp lists several people with the opportunity to kill Morley, including his sister, servants, his partner Riley, a young man named Alfred, or various patients. Poirot emphasizes the need to find a motive, as Morley seemed a harmless man.

Poirot and Japp then interview Miss Molly George, Morley's sister. She insists that suicide is inconceivable because her brother had no reason to end his life, though she notes he was irritable that morning due to a heavy schedule and the absence of his assistant. She explains that Miss Neville, who has worked for Morley for three years, left for Somerset after receiving a telegram stating her aunt had suffered a stroke. Miss Molly suggests that Morley was upset by Neville's absence and speculates that Neville might have taken the day off to see a fiancé whom Morley disapproves of.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Japp and Hercule Poirot question Miss Molly regarding potential motives for the death of her brother, Henry. Miss Molly identifies Frank Carter, an unpleasant man who was recently laid off from an insurance company, as a person of interest. Glady Neville has lent Carter some of her savings, and Henry had previously tried to convince Glady to break off her engagement with him. Additionally, Miss Molly notes that Henry's partner, Mr. Reilly, is an Irishman who frequently argues about politics and has a drinking problem that Henry found unprofessional for a dentist. Miss Molly also reveals that Henry's will leaves the bulk of his estate to her, with only £100 going to Glady.

Glady Neville arrives in an agitated state, explaining that she received a telegram claiming her aunt had suffered a stroke. She dismisses the idea that Carter sent the telegram as a prank. Glady provides a list of patients seen by Mr. Moore and a separate list for Mr. Reilly. The latter list includes Colonel Abercrombie, whom Poirot identifies as a person of interest. Japp expresses interest in interviewing Mr. Amberloz, the last person recorded seeing Mr. Moore alive.

Poirot questions Japp's involvement, noting that Japp does not typically handle suicide cases. Japp explains that he was nearby investigating a fraud case involving Alistair Blunt, a banker. Because Blunt is a high-profile figure requiring protection from political extremists like communists or fascists, Scotland Yard is investigating the matter thoroughly. Poirot concludes that the incident may have been a failed attempt on Blunt's life and suggests a larger conspiracy involving significant financial interests.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jaap and Jeppe discuss the possibility that Morty is a pawn in a larger game involving Brunty. Meanwhile, Alfred, an employee who has worked for Mr. Morley for two weeks, speaks with Poirot and Japp. Despite feeling insecure due to previous mistakes, Alfred is pleased to be treated as an important witness.

Alfred recounts the morning's events at the dental practice. He describes a sequence of visitors: a lady with a little girl, a Mrs. Soper looking for Mr. Moore, a society lady in a Daimler, a tall soldier, Japp, and an American gentleman. The American arrived early for an eleven-thirty appointment but left before seeing the doctor because Mr. Morley did not ring the bell until approximately eleven-forty. Following this, a notable person named Mr. Brent arrived in a Rolls-Royce. After Mr. Brent left, Miss Seymour Beryl Chilwell arrived. Alfred then escorted Miss Chilwell up in the elevator. Subsequently, two men arrived: one short man with a high-pitched voice and a fat foreigner named Mr. Ambrosio. Alfred escorted Mr. Ambrosio to see Mr. Morley but did not see him leave.

After twelve o'clock, Alfred sat in the elevator reading an American detective novel titled *The Time of Death*. He notes that a lady named Miss Shady arrived and waited until one o'clock, becoming angry when Mr. Morley did not ring the bell. Alfred also mentions that around noon, Mr. Neville's young boyfriend arrived looking for Miss Neville; he entered the waiting room but subsequently disappeared. Poirot suggests that discussing murder with Alfred may help jog his memory, though Japp expresses concern about the wisdom of this approach.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Japp and Hercule Poirot interview Mr. Raleigh, a dentist whose office is located on the ground floor. Raleigh states that his partner, Henry Moody, was unlikely to commit suicide because he was a careful man with no debts, unlike Raleigh himself, who suffers from money problems. Raleigh describes his morning patients, including Betty Hughes, Colonel Abberline, and a meticulous retired civil servant named Barnes. He notes that an American patient named Howard Leeks, who called from the Holborn Palace Hotel, failed to show up for a special appointment. Regarding Miss Neville, the secretary, Raleigh claims she and Moody were "clean as a whistle," though he mentions that Moody had advised Neville to break off her engagement to Frank Carter. Raleigh expresses sadness over Moody's death but maintains he has no leads regarding murder.

Following the interview, Japp discusses the timeline of Moody's death, which occurred between 12:25 and 1:00 PM. Japp notes that Ambrosio, a new patient, was the last person to see Moody alive. Japp and Poirot plan to investigate various leads, including the American patient Mr. Leeks, Miss Neville’s telegram, and her relatives. They decide to visit Alistair Brent in Chelsea.

The narrative provides background on Alistair Brent, a reserved man who married the wealthy Rebecca Arnold. Rebecca, the sole heir to the Rothstein and Arnold fortunes following a series of family tragedies, married the much younger Alec Brent. Despite social skepticism, the marriage was successful and faithful. Japp and Poirot arrive at Brent's luxurious Gothic-style mansion in Chelsea, where Alec Brent greets them.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Japp and Hercule Poirot visit Alistair Brent at his luxurious Gothic-style mansion in Chelsea to investigate the suicide of Mr. Moller, a dentist. Brent expresses surprise at the news, stating that Moller appeared normal and joyful when Brent saw him earlier that morning. Brent mentions he has visited the clinic on Charlotte Queen Street three or four times due to dental issues but cannot recall who originally recommended the dentist to him. Poirot notes that this information might be important.

As the men depart, they are approached by Olivia Jane, a tall, slender young woman with an American accent who arrives in a sports car. She identifies herself as Brent's niece and expresses concern for his well-being. Upon learning that the police are investigating Moller's suicide, she calls the situation "absurd" and retreats into her uncle's house.

The investigators then meet Miss Sainsbury Silas at the Glengowell Court Hotel. She describes her visit to the clinic, noting that when she arrived, the only other person in the waiting room was an agitated young man with a toothache who left before she was called in. Miss Sainsbury Silas also mentions that she did not see the dentist, as she went straight to his room after fixing her hair and hat. However, she recalls seeing an unusual-looking foreigner leaving the clinic as she was ascending the stairs.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Inspector Japp interview Miss Meabelle Seale (formerly Miss Sainsbury-Silvers), a former actress and missionary who recently moved from India to the Glengowan Court Hotel. During the questioning, Japp explores whether Mr. Moly mentioned any upsetting encounters, but she denies this. Miss Seale provides an extensive personal history, detailing her career in theater, her failed marriage, and her current work teaching public speaking. Japp suggests she may need to testify in court, a prospect she accepts with enthusiasm.

Following the interview, Japp and Poirot travel to the Savoy Hotel to speak with Mr. Ambrosio, but a hotel employee informs them that Ambrosio died half an hour prior. Twenty-four hours later, Japp calls Poirot to report that the case is solved. Based on an autopsy report, Japp concludes that Moly's death was a suicide following a medical error. Japp posits that Moly accidentally administered a lethal overdose of adrenaline and procaine to Ambrosio and, unable to face the professional ruin and guilt, shot himself.

Poirot remains skeptical, questioning why a professional would commit suicide without leaving a note and doubting that a medical mistake would lead to such an extreme reaction. Japp presents alternative theories, noting Ambrosio's suspicious past involving intelligence work and blackmail in Europe and India. Japp suggests Ambrosio might have blackmailed Moly, or that Ambrosio killed Moly and then committed suicide using stolen drugs. However, Japp maintains his conviction that Moly's death was a tragic accident caused by overwork.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reviews his notes regarding several individuals: Ambrosio, a potential communist spy; Frank Carter, who recently lost his job; Howard Ricks; and Mr. Barnes. He also notes potential carpet traces at Moor's office. Poirot travels to 88 Castle Lane to visit Mr. Barnes, a retired Civil Service official.

During their meeting, Mr. Barnes reveals that he recognized Ambrosio in a waiting room and believes Ambrosio is part of a group of foreign agitators aiming to destroy the British financial system by removing Alistair Bret, a man who maintains strict financial stability. Mr. Barnes deduces that these agitators use "natural" accidents to eliminate influential figures. He cites three previous examples: a Chancellor of the Exchequer who died due to an anesthetist's mistake, an entrepreneur who died of an undiagnosed illness, and a politician who died in a car accident. In each case, the professionals involved—the anesthetist, the physician, and the driver—later achieved significant wealth and comfort.

Mr. Barnes argues that while the dentist, Morley, may not be the mastermind, he was likely eliminated because he knew too much. He suggests that an organization is behind the plot to kill Bret. Mr. Barnes speculates that Layton is the most likely person to perform the actual deed. He proposes a scheme where Molly (Layton) would provide an excuse for being unwell at the last minute, allowing Layton to perform the task. This would result in a "medical accident" that leaves the dentist appearing depressed and pitiful in court, eventually allowing him to retire with a large income.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mr. Barnes meets with Hercule Poirot and suggests that Ambrosio was a scapegoat who was set up. Barnes warns that people will target Alistair Brent to earn millions of pounds, advising that Brent's associates—such as relatives, servants, a pharmacist, or a liquor merchant—should be watched. Poirot notes that a mark on a carpet suggests a body was dragged, leading him to conclude that Mortimer was killed in his office rather than his surgery, implying the killer was someone in the house.

The following morning, Poirot calls Inspector Japp to report that Miss Mabelle Sainsbury-Sil has disappeared from the Glengowell Court Hotel. She left the hotel around 7:00 pm without her luggage, which remained in her room. Japp notes that her background in India is clear and she is not involved in the murder case. The hotel manager, Mrs. Harrison, expresses concern for the missing woman.

Poirot and Japp inspect Miss Sainsbury-Sil's room, which is tidy and contains her suitcases and various shoes. Poirot observes that her black satin evening shoes are one size smaller than her other footwear. Japp finds letters and charity receipts in her vanity, noting she has no outstanding bills. Evidence suggests she intended to return for dinner, as she had not informed the restaurant of her absence and had previously told a staff member she would return to play cards. Poirot and Japp visit the Adams family in Hampstead, who knew Miss Sainsbury-Sil from India but had not seen her for a month. Mrs. Adams provides Poirot with the address of a hotel near Russell Square and other addresses of her friends in Streatham.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Investigations into the disappearance of Miss Sainsbury West yield no results; witnesses at her hotel and in Streatham provide little information, and no hospital records match her description. The following morning, Hercule Poirot visits Mr. Howard Rix (also referred to as Lycx/Rycroft/Reix) at the Holborn Palace Hotel. Rix, a disheveled and hungry-looking young man, reacts with anger and suspicion to Poirot's presence.

Poirot reveals he remembers Rix from the dentist's waiting room three days prior. Rix becomes cautious and defensive, eventually accusing Poirot of being a private detective hired by Alistair Brent to protect him. Rix dismisses the investigation, claiming the dentist's death was a suicide resulting from a medical error involving a local anesthetic. Poirot questions Rix's whereabouts between 12:00 and 1:00 PM on the day of the death, but Rix remains uncooperative and insists that the dentist's death is irrelevant to him. Poirot concludes the meeting by noting the difference between his own concern for the cause of death and Rix's indifference.

Upon returning home, Poirot is met by Miss Gladys Neville, the secretary of the late Mr. Molley. After Poirot offers her tea to calm her nerves, Miss Neville explains that she is upset by the recent trial. She expresses a sense of responsibility for having been present, noting that she felt a woman should have accompanied Miss Molly, especially since Miss Molly disliked Mr. Reilly.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Gladys Neville meets with Hercule Poirot to express her doubts regarding the death of Mr. Morley. She argues that the conclusion that he died from an overdose of local anesthetic is unconvincing, as dentists use standardized, mechanical dosages that make accidental overdosing nearly impossible. She admits she did not share these views in court because she feared making it appear that Mr. Morley's death was intentional.

Poirot questions Gladys about a mysterious telegram that called her away from the clinic on the day of the incident. Gladys notes that the sender must have known her and her aunt, who lived at the clinic. She previously considered that Mr. Morley might have sent it to get rid of her, but dismissed the idea as far-fetched. She then discusses her friend, Frank Carter, noting his recent agitation and suspicion following his job loss. She mentions that Frank recently claimed to have found a good job, though the nature of the work is vague, involving correspondence through a London address.

Poirot arranges a lunch at the Logan Hotel for Sunday to meet Frank. During the lunch, Frank appears stylish but hostile and quick-witted. He expresses resentment toward Mr. Morley, claiming Morley treated Gladys like a slave and wanted her to leave Frank. When questioned by Poirot, Frank admits he went to the clinic around 12:15 on the day of the death to see Gladys and to inform Mr. Morley of his new job. He claims he left because he was tired of waiting in the dark, noting he saw a "slick, fat guy" in the waiting room before leaving. Poirot observes that Frank's angry outburst seemed potentially untruthful.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a conversation with Frank Carter and Glady, Hercule Poirot returns home and calls Inspector Japp to discuss the telegram sent to Miss Neville. Poirot suspects a conspiracy, noting that the telegram was cleverly sent from Ritching Bourn to mimic Ritching, Somerset. He suggests the telegram might have been a prank timed to coincide with the day Molly was scheduled to give an injection, potentially causing Molly to make a mistake due to Miss Neville's absence.
