Japp maintains that the case regarding Ambrosio is clear, as the fatal substance was injected rather than ingested, and he notes that the investigation into the missing Sylvia continues.

Later, Poirot discusses the death of his dentist, Mr. Morley, with his butler, George. While many believe Morley committed suicide, Poirot considers murder. He reviews potential suspects who were in the house: a cook and a maid, Morley's sister (who inherited his estate), a partner, a pageboy, and a Greek gentleman who is also dead. George suggests the Greek gentleman and Morley may have intended to kill each other. Poirot expands his list of suspects to include an American gentleman who was seen in the waiting room but not seen leaving, and Frank Carter, who arrived after twelve. He notes that if an American named Mr. Amberloz were the killer, the crime occurred between 12:05 and 12:20; otherwise, it was after 12:25. Poirot realizes he has omitted Mr. Barnes from his list of people present during the incident. Finally, Jane Olivia, the great-niece of Alistair Brent, calls Poirot and requests that he come to Gothic Hall to discuss something with her.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After receiving a call from Jane Olivia, Poirot arrives at a Gothic riverside house to meet Alistair Brent. In the study, Alistair is seen with his wife's niece, Mrs. Olivia, who is complaining to him. Jane Olivia reveals her motive for the meeting: she wishes to discuss the disappearance of Sainsbury Silas. 

Jane provides a specific detail regarding a past encounter involving Alistair. Three months prior, while traveling in a Rolls Royce, they stopped at number 58 on Charlotte Street. There, a middle-aged woman with artistic clothing approached Alistair, claiming to be a friend of his wife, Rebecca. Alistair, who lived in India ten years ago, does not remember her, but Jane suggests the woman was merely using the connection to solicit donations for the Zenana Missions.

Following the meeting, Jane pulls Poirot aside to confront him. Poirot reveals that he knows she summoned him to persuade him to discuss a dentist named Howard Lix. Poirot further challenges Jane, noting her suspicious reaction to the murder of a dentist named Mr. Morley and her connection to a young American named Howard Roxter. 

Jane eventually admits she was attempting to mislead him. She confesses her infatuation with Howard Roxter and explains her family dynamics: her mother, the niece of Alistair's wife, seeks to secure Alistair's inheritance. Jane expresses frustration with Alistair’s conservative, old-fashioned values, which conflict with the radical views held by Howard Roxter.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jane Olivia discusses Howard Ricks's radical views with Hercule Poirot. She explains that she arranged for Ricks to make an appointment with her Uncle Archie at the Charlotte Street dentist office because she hoped seeing Archie's kindness would mitigate Ricks's hatred toward him. She admits she became fearful of the appointment because Ricks sometimes seeks "shortcuts" to achieve his goals.

Over a month after Mr. Morley's death, Inspector Japp and Poirot discuss the disappearance of Miss Sainsbury-West. Japp maintains that Morley committed suicide, while Poirot believes he was murdered. Japp notes that Miss Sainsbury-West was on the same ship as Ambrosio when returning from India, and a waiter reported she had lunch with him at the Savoy Hotel the week before his death. Japp mentions that Ambrosio was linked to espionage and Central Europeans, but doubts Miss Sainsbury-West would be involved in such activities. Poirot notes that Reginald Barnes of Eling Castle Lodge had previously mentioned international organizations to him.

Japp reports that despite her description being published, no one has found Miss Sainsbury-West, and various sightings in Yorkshire, Liverpool, Devon, and Ramsgate have proven to be false leads. Japp suggests that if Morley was murdered, he might have told Miss Sainsbury-West something that made the killer jealous, leading to her death. Poirot concludes that such a crime would imply the involvement of a serious organization. After Japp leaves, Poirot reflects on the various pieces of information he has gathered, waiting for the final link in the chain to appear so he can organize his findings.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Japp summons Poirot to apartment number 45 in the Robbott King building, overlooking Betsy Park, to investigate a discovery. Inside a small storeroom, they find the decomposed body of Mabel Sainsbury Silvers inside a large metal fur box. The body is unrecognizable due to facial destruction, but Poirot identifies her by a shabby shoe with a tarnished buckle and a green wool coat.

Japp explains that the apartment belongs to Sylvia Chappman, a reclusive, well-dressed blonde woman. A doorman had previously seen Miss Sainsbury Silvers visiting Mrs. Chappman, but the doorman only realized her identity after seeing a classified ad and remembering her green coat and shoe buckles. Inspector Baddesley discovered that Mrs. Chappman had disappeared over a month prior, leaving only a note for her maid, Nellie, stating she was going out. Baddesley found a bloodstain on the carpet and a closed leather suitcase containing the victim's body, along with her handbag and a letter. Japp suggests the face was destroyed to cover the victim's identity or for revenge, though he notes the identity remained discoverable.

Poirot inspects Mrs. Chappman's bedroom, which is covered in dust and shows no signs of a hasty departure. He observes that the room was carefully cleaned. After examining the wardrobe, Poirot finds trendy sandals in size five and a collection of high-quality fur coats, including a gray squirrel coat from the box where the body was found. In the bathroom, Poirot notes various cosmetics and hair dyes, leading him to conclude that Mrs. Chappman likely dyes her hair to maintain her blonde appearance.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot examines a shoe from the crime scene, noting its poorly hand-sewn fastenings. Following a lead, Poirot and Inspector Japp visit Mrs. Merton at No. 82 Leopold Chambers, a close friend of the deceased, Sylvia Chapman. Mrs. Merton claims she is not particularly close to Sylvia, with whom she occasionally plays bridge or goes to the movies. She is unaware of Miss Sainsbury West, a name mentioned in recent news regarding a missing person. Mrs. Merton reveals that Sylvia’s husband is a traveling merchant for a munitions company, but she later confesses a secret: Sylvia believed her husband was actually a spy using the company as a cover.

Returning to the scene, the investigators meet Detective Inspector Bredon. Bredon reports that Sylvia’s maid believes Sylvia's husband is an open homosexual and notes that Sylvia received letters from Germany, Africa, Italy, and the Soviet Union. Sylvia's files contained only local receipts, theater programs, recipes, and a pamphlet from the Indian Women's Christian Association. The doorman does not recall seeing any suspicious men on the night of the death. A forensic expert examines the body, describing it as a healthy, middle-aged woman with dyed blonde hair and post-mortem facial injuries, though he cannot yet confirm her identity. Poirot discovers a small address book on a desk containing names of hairdressers, tailors, and doctors. This discovery leads Poirot to believe he can certainly identify the deceased. Meanwhile, Miss Morley has moved to a farmhouse near Hertford, where she lives with a soldier.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot visits Miss Morley to discuss her brother Henry’s death. Miss Morley explains that his professional papers were handled by Miss Neville and his patients were distributed among successors like Mr. Ralegh. She expresses contempt for the murder of Miss Sainsbury-Silas, a patient Henry had not previously mentioned. When Poirot asks about a patient named Mrs. Chapman, Miss Morley directs him to Miss Neville, who is now working at a dentist's in Ramstead. Miss Morley also expresses her dislike for Frank Carter, suggesting he lacks moral principles and has a short temper, though she sees no motive for him to kill Henry.

During the visit, Poirot observes Agnes, a former maid who now serves as a cook. As she departs, Agnes nervously asks if the death was truly a suicide caused by a dosage mistake, noting that Miss Morley disagrees. Poirot senses Agnes is hiding something. He recalls the layout of the Morley residence at 58 Charlotte Street, noting that the police ruled out outsiders because the house was sealed, with the only entrance being the front door managed by Alfred.

Later, Mr. Barnes visits Poirot. He reveals that Albert Chapman (also referred to as Albert Chalmers) was a spy with the code name Q.X.912, whose job involved traveling to various locations under believable pretexts. Barnes mentions rumors of Chapman's death but remains skeptical. Finally, Japp meets with Poirot and expresses a theory that the corpse found is not actually Miss Sainsbury-Silas, suggesting the victim's face was destroyed to hide her identity. Japp believes Henry Morley was killed specifically to prevent him from testifying.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following the identification of a corpse in the Bateswick Apartments, news reveals that the body previously thought to be Miss Sainsbury Syl is actually Mrs. Albert Chappman. Mr. Lestrade of 58 Charlotte Street confirmed this identity using the deceased's teeth and jawbone, which matched records from the late Mr. Morley. While Miss Sainsbury Syl's clothes and handbag were found with the body, the woman herself remains missing.

Inspector Japp discusses the case with Hercule Poirot, noting that the corpse was likely mutilated to disguise its identity. Poirot explains that the two women shared similar physical traits—both were in their forties with dyed blonde hair—which led to the confusion. Japp suggests that Mabel Sainsbury-Syl killed Sylvia, rather than the other way around.

Japp later calls Poirot to report that the official investigation has been abruptly canceled due to orders from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Japp suspects the cancellation is intended to protect the secrets of the deceased and to avoid political complications for the deceased's husband, who may hold a sensitive overseas position. Despite the official shutdown, Poirot vows to continue his own investigation.

Poirot receives a letter from Alistair Blunt requesting a meeting at his house in Chelsea. Shortly after, Poirot receives a threatening phone call from an unidentified woman. She warns him not to accept any commissions related to the case and tells him to stop interfering in matters that are not his concern. The woman dismisses Mr. Morley's death as an incidental matter. Poirot suspects the caller may be Miss Sainsbury-Syl using a disguised voice. The following morning, news breaks that the Prime Minister narrowly escaped an assassination attempt by an Indian assailant, prompting Poirot to visit Japp at Scotland Yard.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Japp informs Polly that a shooting incident occurred where a bullet intended for Alistair Blunt was missed, potentially targeting the Prime Minister instead. The perpetrator was an Indian student who was apprehended by an American man named Ricks, a patient who had previously escaped from Morley following a suicide.

Poirot visits a Gothic front where he meets Mr. Selby, a secretary for Mr. Brent. Selby delivers an invitation from Mr. Brent for Poirot to join him for a weekend at his country house, Kensington. During the visit, Poirot encounters Mrs. Olivia, a fashionable woman who expresses displeasure at Poirot's invitation, claiming Mr. Brent wanted a quiet weekend with only his family. Mrs. Olivia's daughter, Jane, approaches Poirot privately and urgently whispers that the Countess of Morcerf is dead, pleading with him not to get involved. Poirot is unsettled when he hears Mrs. Olivia arguing with Jane, noting that her voice sounds identical to a voice he heard on the phone the previous night.

The following morning, a Rolls-Royce picks up Poirot for the trip. The vehicle contains only Alistair Blunt and his secretary. During the drive, Blunt dismisses the assassination attempt as the work of incompetent, idealistic students. He reveals that he had previously received an ineffective bomb through the mail. Blunt discusses his preference for common sense and simple arithmetic over complex philosophy or politics, eventually transitioning the conversation to Poirot's detective work.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Upon arriving at Eustace Manor, Hercule Poirot encounters a cold reception from Mrs. Olivia and her daughter, Jane, who displays open hostility toward him. During dinner, Brent inquires about Helen Montgomery, but Mrs. Olivia claims Helen is resting after working in the garden. Later, Poirot overhears a private conversation in the ladies' lounge where Jane accuses her mother of neglecting Helen, while Mrs. Olivia dismisses Helen as a mere distant cousin and a burden to Uncle Alastair.

Alistair Brent summons Poirot to his room to discuss his dissatisfaction with the official decision to halt the search for a woman named Sainsbury Silas. Brent expresses suspicion regarding a bizarre case involving a dentist's suicide and a disfigured woman named Chapman. He suspects that a woman he encountered on the steps may have orchestrated a meeting to draw attention to him. Poirot suggests the woman might be dead and explores whether Brent recalls anything unusual during his dental appointment with Mr. Moly. Brent recalls a blonde girl, an Irish dentist, and a restless young man in the waiting room, but nothing suspicious.

Poirot identifies the young man as Howard Lick, a friend of Jane Olivia. He suggests that Mrs. Olivia brought Jane from America specifically to separate her from Lick, a relationship her mother disapproves of.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses Alistair Brent's personal views with him, noting Brent's dislike for a young man involved in subversive activities. Poirot asks about the distribution of Brent's assets to determine who might benefit from his death. Brent reveals that his will primarily benefits St. Edward's Hospital, the Cancer Hospital, and the Royal Blind Institute. Additionally, he leaves money to his wife's niece, Julia Olivia Lady; a trust for her daughter, Jane Olivia; and a sum to his distant cousin, Helen Montresor, who lives in a local farmhouse. Brent officially commissions Poirot to find Miss Sainsbury.

Upon leaving, Poirot is confronted by Jane Olivia, who insults him as a "meddling spy" and a "damned bourgeois detective," asserting that her uncle is perfectly safe. Poirot also encounters Lady Olivia, who treats him with disdain. While walking in the garden at night, Poirot observes Brent dictating notes to Mr. Selby. Later, in his room, Poirot reflects on various confusing elements of the case, including a telephone call, Mr. Barnes, Albert Chapman, and the maid Agnes, noting that the situation regarding Sainsbury Silas remains particularly contradictory.

The following morning, Poirot walks through the gardens and observes Helen Montresor speaking sarcastically to a gardener in a gruff Scottish voice. He then approaches a young gardener who is digging vigorously and appears reluctant to engage in conversation. Poirot finds the young man's behavior and physical presence strange and interesting, observing him from behind a garden wall.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot observes that Frank Carter, who previously held a clerical job, is now working as a gardener for Alastair Brent. While walking toward the villa, Poirot overhears a dispute between Miss Monteiro and Alastair Brent regarding the rudeness of Brent's American relative, Julia. In the dining room, Brent expresses his unhappiness to Julia and Lady Olivia regarding the tension between the women. Poirot questions Brent about his new gardener, noting that the man was hired to replace a previous gardener named Burton. Brent recalls that McAllister recommended the new man, whose name is something like "Dunning" or "Sembler," and that he is paid two pounds and fifteen pence.

During breakfast, Jane Oliveira engages in a passionate argument with Alastair Brent about social change and the status quo. Brent expresses his disdain for radical ideas and his desire for stability, leading Polly to ask what would happen if he were "eliminated." Later, Brent takes Poirot on a tour of his garden, specifically showing him his rock garden and various flowerbeds.

The peace is shattered by a gunshot coming from the laurel bushes. Howard Leeks emerges from the bushes wrestling with the gardener, Frank Carter, who is holding a revolver. Leeks claims he caught Carter red-handed after Carter shot at Brent. Carter vehemently denies the accusation, claiming he was merely trimming branches when a gun fell at his feet and he picked it up by reflex. Carter accuses Poirot of setting him up. Jane Oliveira arrives at the scene, where Howard Lacy informs her that he has just saved her uncle's life.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a gunshot, Howard Ricks intervenes to save Alastair Brent's life by disarming the shooter, Frank Carter. Jane explains that Ricks grabbed Carter and took his gun. Despite Carter's protests that the situation is a setup, Ricks drags him away to be turned over to the police. Mrs. Olivia, though angry at Ricks's presence, tends to a shaken Brent. Howard Ridges challenges Hercule Poirot to demonstrate his detective skills, noting that Poirot previously caught the wrong person during an incident involving the Prime Minister.

Poirot remains suspicious of the situation. While he believes Carter is a rogue with a flimsy story about being a spy, he finds the coincidence of Ricks being present at two consecutive shooting attempts—both times resulting in missed shots—highly unusual. During dinner, Poirot observes Ricks, suspecting the man is "wearing a sheep's skin." Later that night, Ricks visits Poirot's room to explain his actions. Ricks admits that in the previous incident involving the Prime Minister, he staged a capture to allow a boy named Ram Lal to escape. However, he insists that today was different and that Carter was truly the shooter. Ricks confesses his contradictory motive: while he believes Brent should be killed for the sake of human progress, he personally intervened because Brent is a kind gentleman.

The following morning, during a village church service, Poirot experiences a sudden epiphany. As the choir sings lyrics about being delivered from evil and avoiding traps, Poirot realizes he has almost fallen into a carefully laid, intricately woven trap.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot experiences a moment of clarity as various disparate facts—including a shoe buckle, a ten-inch stocking, a ruined face, the behavior of Ambrosio, and the role of the late Mr. Moly—align into a coherent pattern. 

Poirot encounters Mr. Raleigh at a shipping company. Raleigh, an Irishman, reveals he is leaving for America to start over and clear his debts after being abandoned by his clinic on Charlotte Street. During their conversation, Raleigh expresses his disagreement with the court's verdict regarding the death of his partner. He suggests that if Miss Morley administered a large dose to the Greek man, she must have been drunk or acting deliberately, though he notes he has never seen her drink. Poirot questions Raleigh about his movements on the day of the death, specifically regarding a visit to Mr. Moly’s office at 11:35 and his activities after Mr. Barnes left at 12:30. Raleigh denies involvement in any shooting. When Poirot asks about the maid, Agnes, Raleigh claims to feel nothing for her and suggests she has no interest in him. Raleigh then departs.

Poirot returns to Hampstead to speak with Mrs. Adams. He notes that the public was misled during the initial investigation because the body of Mrs. Chapman was mistaken for Miss Sainsbury West. Because of this error, Mrs. Adams is relieved to learn her friend is not dead, though she speculates Miss Sainsbury West may have amnesia. Poirot inquires if Miss Sainsbury West ever mentioned Albert Chapman or if she was recommended to Dr. Muriel for dental treatment. Mrs. Adams denies recommending the doctor and states that Miss Sainsbury West never mentioned the Bentlys, despite having lived in India where the bankers resided.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a conversation with Mrs. Adams, Hercule Poirot learns that Mabel Sainsbury-Silas was a kind, meticulous woman dedicated to church charity. Mrs. Adams reveals that three months prior, Mabel had expressed a desperate desire to commit a crime to secure funds for her charitable work. This information prompts Poirot to reconsider Mabel's character and her potential as a criminal. He reflects on her recent history: her return from India with Mr. Ambrosio, her intentional approach to Mr. Brent, her two visits to the Leopoldian Palace, and the discovery of a body dressed in her clothes with her handbag nearby. He also notes her sudden departure from the Glengowell Court Hotel after meeting the police.

While walking through Regent's Park, Poirot encounters Jane Olivia and Howard Lix (also referred to as Rex). Howard is visibly annoyed by Poirot's presence, and after a brief argument between the couple, Howard leaves. Jane apologizes to Poirot for previously accusing him of spying on Howard, acknowledging that Uncle Alistair had actually requested Poirot's help in investigating Mabel Sainsbury-Silas's disappearance. Poirot confirms he witnessed Howard tackle a killer to save Alistair. Jane then reveals her distress regarding Howard's demand that they marry secretly due to her perceived weakness. She seeks advice from Poirot, but he remains non-committal. Upon returning home, Poirot is visited by Inspector Japp, who expresses amazement at Poirot's investigative genius.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Japp visits Hercule Poirot to reveal new evidence regarding the death of Molly. Japp states that the gun Frank Carter used to shoot Brent on Saturday is the same weapon that killed Molly, which challenges the previous suicide theory. Japp notes that Carter lied about his whereabouts on the morning of the incident; while Carter claimed to be walking down Marlborough Road, a barman reported seeing him at 1:05 in an unusual state, with a white face and shaking hands. Poirot finds this information unsettling and doubts Carter is the killer.

Miss Gladys Neville enters the room in distress, defending Carter. She explains that while Carter is an anti-Semite, he is a "harmless young man" and denies he killed Mr. Morley. She suggests Carter went to Charlotte Street to vent his frustrations at Morley because he was depressed and had not yet secured the new job he claimed to have. Poirot questions her about whether Carter possessed two identical guns, but she denies any knowledge.

Poirot later speaks with Inspector Baddose, who confirms there is no evidence the revolver belonged to Carter prior to the shooting at Lovecourt Manor. Additionally, Carter maintains he was performing undercover anti-communist work at Lovegrove Hall, having been instructed by a woman using the code Q.H.56 to eavesdrop on gardeners. 

Finally, Poirot receives a letter from Agnes Fletcher, who expresses fear of police involvement but implies she has information regarding the day of the incident. Poirot arranges a meeting with Agnes in a Hertfordshire tea shop. During their meeting, Agnes discusses her family's upright reputation in Darington to emphasize her desire to avoid legal trouble.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Agnes tells Hercule Poirot that she and the other cooks initially believed Mr. Molli’s death was a suicide caused by a medicinal error, as reported in the newspapers. However, Agnes reveals she has been uneasy due to news reports that Frank Carter, Miss Neville’s boyfriend, had shot a man and appeared to be losing his mind. Agnes recalls that on the morning of Mr. Molli's death, around half past twelve, she saw Frank Carter standing on the stairs above the owner's office, appearing to strain to hear something. She then witnessed him run down the stairs and into the owner's consulting room. Agnes and her colleague Emma had previously withheld this information to avoid causing trouble for Frank, but Agnes now suspects he may have killed the owner.

Poirot visits Inspector Japp at Scotland Yard to request a meeting with Frank Carter. Japp suggests Poirot is acting out of sentimentality for Frank or his girlfriend, but Poirot denies this, asserting his rationality. Poirot informs Japp that he will soon provide the name and address of a witness who can testify that Frank committed the crime, though he insists on seeing Frank personally to satisfy himself.

Poirot subsequently meets with Frank Carter, who appears pale and haggard. Frank reacts with scorn and hostility toward Poirot, refusing to speak without his lawyer present. He accuses Poirot of attempting to set a trap to force a confession, while suspecting that the police are eavesdropping on their conversation.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot confronts Frank Carter in a private meeting, accusing him of lying about his whereabouts on the morning Henry Morley was killed. Poirot reveals that Agnes Fletcher, a maid at 58 Charlotte Queen Street, witnessed Carter standing on the stairs and entering Morley's room at approximately 12:06. Although Carter initially reacts with rage and claims the witness was bribed, Poirot maintains that the maid's testimony is truthful. 

Under the pressure of Poirot's intimidating presence, Carter eventually confesses. He admits he went to Morley's room to confront him regarding a woman and business issues. However, Carter claims that upon entering, he found Morley already dead from a gunshot wound to the head. Carter explains that he only touched the doorknob and Morley's hands before fleeing the scene in shock. Poirot tells Carter that his confession confirms what he already knew and promises to handle the rest of the matter.

Later, Poirot visits Mr. Ilbing (referred to as Mr. Barnes) at his home. They discuss the case, with Barnes admitting he mistakenly believed the murder was related to espionage involving Ambrosio and Alistair Blunt. Poirot compares the investigation to a magician's card trick, noting that the various political theories acted as distractions. Poirot concludes that the murder was driven by pure personal motivation rather than national interests, admitting that Barnes's professional background and insights had misled him during the investigation.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot informs Mr. Barnes that Carter saw the murderer at 12:26, despite Carter's claim that he had left the house before Mr. Morley was killed. Following several days of investigation, Poirot meets with Alistair Brent at a Gothic mansion to discuss his findings regarding Miss Sainsbury West.

Poirot explains that the identity of the deceased is a complex matter. He recounts an encounter outside a dentist's office on Charlotte Street, where he assisted Miss Sainsbury West after her new patent leather shoe, featuring a large decorative buckle, became caught in a taxi door. Later that evening, Miss Sainsbury West disappeared. When Inspector Japp called Poirot to Leopold King's apartment, they discovered a body in a fur box. Poirot noted a discrepancy: the body wore a very old, worn-out shoe with a hand-sewn buckle, whereas Miss Sainsbury West had been wearing new shoes that same morning.

Poirot details his deduction regarding the victim's identity. He initially considered if the body was Mrs. Albert Chapman, noting that the face had been deliberately destroyed. However, he observed that while Mrs. Chapman wore size 5 shoes, the deceased's shoes fit tightly, suggesting the body was indeed Miss Sainsbury West. Despite this, a doctor testified in court that the body was Mrs. Albert Chapman. Poirot concludes that the woman known as Miss Sainsbury West does not match the honest, naive persona described by her friends in India. Instead, her recent behaviors—including lunching with a spy and visiting a woman shortly before a murder—suggest she may be a cold-blooded killer or an accomplice.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses his evolving theories with Alistair Brent regarding the murders of Dr. Moore (Molly), Mr. Ambrosio, and an unknown woman. Poirot initially believed the case was a "social crime" targeting Brent due to his public status as a banker. However, he now posits that the motive is a "private crime" driven by personal gain or animosity. He considers that individuals like Frank Carter or Howard Leach—who stood to benefit from Brent's death—might be involved. Poirot also references a theory from Mr. Barnes, a retired Civil Service worker and patient of Brent, suggesting the deaths were collateral damage in a conspiracy to assassinate Brent while he was unguarded in a dentist's office.

Poirot reveals a major breakthrough regarding Mabel Sainsbury. He concludes there are two women playing the role of Mabel Sainsbury: the actual, harmless Mabel, and a clever imposter acting as an actress. Poirot reconstructs that the imposter took Mabel's place at the Leopold Hotel, wore her clothes, and eventually disappeared to the Glengowan Palace Hotel. He asserts that the corpse found in the box is the real Mabel Sainsbury, and the destruction of the body's face was a smokescreen to obscure her identity. Furthermore, Poirot explains that the dentist's evidence was compromised because the patient cards were forged to swap the names of the two women. Finally, Poirot theorizes that the real Mabel Sainsbury was murdered because she possessed a remarkable ability to remember people she had seen, making her a threat to the imposter's deception.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot reveals to Alistair Brent that Brent’s current life as a wealthy banker is built on a foundation of bigamy. Poirot explains that when Brent married Rebecca Arnold, he was already married to a woman living under the name Mrs. Albert Chatterton in the Leopold King Apartments near Chelsea. Brent used a fake identity to conceal his past, but his secret was discovered when Mabel Sainsbury Sil encountered him on Charlotte Street. This encounter was overheard by Brent's great-niece, who informed Poirot. The meeting also alerted Ambrosio, a spy and blackmailer, who recognized Brent as a valuable target for extortion.

To silence the blackmailer, Brent orchestrated a murder. Poirot details how Brent killed Molly (the dentist) and used an accomplice to move the body. Brent then impersonated a dentist to treat Ambrosio, injecting him with a lethal dose of procaine and adrenaline. To cover his tracks, Brent forged patient records for Mrs. Chattam and Miss Sainsbury West, wiped fingerprints, and staged the scene. He also attempted to frame Frank Carter by having his accomplice arrange for Carter to work as a gardener, ensuring his testimony would be dismissed.

Furthermore, Brent attempted to divert the police investigation by ensuring the body of Mrs. Chattam was found, leading authorities to search for the non-existent Mabel Sainsbury Sil instead. Brent even attempted to manipulate Poirot by having his wife—a skilled actress—impersonate others via telephone to mislead him. Finally, Brent attempted to plant a loaded gun in a laurel bush at Lovecourt to frame a man trimming the bushes, linking the weapon to the death of Muri.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot confronts Alastair Blunt, revealing that he has discovered the marriage certificate between Blunt and Gerta Grant. Poirot explains that Frank Carter saw Blunt leaving Morley's office, and that the state of Morley's body proves the murderer, not Morley, was the one who saw Amberloch's teeth. Poirot also notes that the real Helen Montresor died years ago in Canada, and Blunt had been exploiting her identity.

Blunt admits to the deception, explaining that he and Gerta Grant maintained a secret, "royal" marriage through various roles and aliases to avoid social scrutiny and maintain their mutual attraction. Blunt confesses to the murders of Mabel Sainsbury-West, Amberloch, and Morley, as well as the death of Lady Chatterley. He claims he killed Mabel by drugging her tea with barbiturates and killed Amberloch using his dental tools. Blunt justifies his actions by claiming he is a vital figure for the stability and democracy of England, and that his reputation must be protected to prevent national ruin. He argues that the lives of the victims were insignificant compared to his service to the country.

Poirot rejects this utilitarian logic, stating that every individual has a right to life and accusing Blunt of being driven by an excessive love for power. Poirot refuses Blunt's suggestion to withdraw from the case and instead has Blunt arrested. After the arrest, Poirot meets Jane Olivia and Howard Rees, telling them the matter is over. As Poirot walks home, he discusses the case with Mr. Barnes, reflecting on the moral implications of Blunt's actions. Finally, Mr. Barnes reveals that he is actually Albert Chatman, leaving Poirot in stunned silence.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After enjoying a meal of snails at the "Mère Vénardeau" restaurant, Hercule Poirot walks through Soho toward his apartment. Reflecting on his habits, Poirot expresses his devotion to a strict three-meal-a-day schedule and his disdain for afternoon tea. As he walks, he reminisces about his close friend Hastings and expresses his dissatisfaction with the modern world, which he finds lacking in order, logic, and subtlety. He notes his weariness regarding the violence and cruelty prevalent in contemporary society, dismissing a news report about the MacKentie murder case as a meaningless act of violence.

Upon arriving at his symmetrical, well-ordered apartment, Poirot is informed by his butler, George, that a gentleman is waiting for him. The visitor is Detective Inspector Spence from the Constabulary of Colchester. After Poirot settles into his living room, George serves Spence a beer and provides Poirot with a mint cordial. Spence, who is planning to retire in six months to tend to his garden, reveals that his visit is not merely social. He states he has come with a specific purpose regarding a case. He asks Poirot about the death of Mrs. McGinty, an old woman, seeking Poirot's insight into the circumstances of her death.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Spence meets with Poirot to discuss the recent conviction of James Bentley. Spence recounts the details of the murder of Mrs. McKeith, a woman who lived alone in a small house. Her head was struck with a sharp object, her room was ransacked, and approximately thirty pounds in cash was stolen. The victim's body was discovered in her living room by a neighbor after a baker arrived for a delivery and noted that Bentley, the other tenant, had not answered the door.

The evidence against Bentley was substantial: he was unemployed and owed rent, the missing money was found under a stone in the yard, and his jacket contained blood and hair matching the victim. Consequently, a jury found him guilty and sentenced him to death. Despite the irrefutable nature of the evidence and the fair conduct of Judge Stanislaus Dell and the defense lawyer, Gray Brooke, Spence believes Bentley is innocent.

Spence explains that his intuition, based on years of experience reading people, suggests Bentley is not a murderer. He notes that true murderers typically exhibit arrogance and a sense of pride in their crimes, whereas Bentley is a shy, nervous, and unremarkable man who reacts to awkwardness with lies and timidity. Although Spence personally gathered the evidence that led to the conviction, he is troubled by the prospect of an innocent man being hanged.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Superintendent Spence informs Hercule Poirot that the investigation into the death of Mrs. McKinty is being closed because Spence has been reassigned to a corruption case in Scotland. Spence admits he has found no evidence to suggest anyone other than the lodger, James Bentley, killed her, but he suggests Poirot take over the case because he believes the murder was not a suicide and that Poirot's methods might uncover what he missed. Poirot accepts the challenge, motivated by boredom and a desire to help Spence find peace of mind.

Spence provides case details regarding Mrs. McKinty, a sixty-four-year-old widow living in Broadhurst. She had £200 in a bank account and an additional £30 hidden under a loose floorboard in her bedroom, a fact known to her niece and James Bentley. The niece and her husband, a painter, are considered suspects but have no apparent motive or need for the money. 

James Bentley, a well-educated but struggling man, had been renting a room from Mrs. McKinty. He had fallen behind on his rent and was nearly out of money. On the night of the murder, November 22nd, Bentley claimed to have been out for a walk from 7:15 p.m. to 9:00 p.m. Mrs. McKinty died between 8:30 and 9:00 p.m. after being struck on the back of the head with a sharp object, possibly a butcher's knife. Although the windows were locked from the inside and there were no signs of forced entry, the bedroom floorboard had been lifted and the £30 was missing. The body was discovered the following morning by Mrs. Elliott after Bentley and a baker knocked on the door.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot and Inspector Spence discuss the murder of Mrs. McKittrick, an elderly cleaning woman and landlady. The primary suspect, James Bentley, was convicted of the crime based on evidence including stolen money hidden outside the house and a bloodstained cuff. During his trial, Bentley changed his story regarding the blood, eventually claiming he found the victim dead and touched her out of panic, which caused him to flee in terror. Spence suggests Bentley's psychological profile—noted by a waitress as someone who preferred isolated seating—might explain his suspicious behavior as a panic response rather than guilt, or perhaps it was a lawyer's fabricated alibi.

Poirot argues that because Mrs. McKittrick lived an ordinary, unremarkable life without known enemies or a mysterious past, the motive for the murder must lie with an extraordinary killer. He posits two possibilities: either the evidence against Bentley was fabricated to frame him, or Bentley was simply in the wrong place at the wrong time. Poirot suggests the investigation should focus on the character of the killer rather than the victim, questioning if the true intention was to eliminate Mrs. McKittrick or to eliminate Bentley by using her death as a means to an end. Spence provides background on Bentley, noting he is the son of a deceased doctor, attended a small public school, worked in government during the war, and lived with a domineering mother.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Detective Superintendent Spence discuss two investigative leads regarding Mrs. MacKenzie. Poirot decides his first course of action will be to meet with James Bentley, after which he intends to travel to Broadchurch to investigate the first line of inquiry—the Mrs. McGinty line—to eliminate it. Spence suggests Poirot use a false identity, such as a singer with a damaged voice, but Poirot insists on traveling under his true identity to exert influence and observe reactions, as he remains unsatisfied with the verdict in the McQuitty case.

Poirot arrives at the Long Grass Hotel, a dilapidated country courtyard, where he finds the accommodations intolerable due to dust, broken furniture, a mangy dog, and a broken door latch that allows cold wind to swirl through the room. While there, he interacts with the hostess, Molly Samuels, who is busy managing the household and dealing with hens that have entered the larder. During their conversation, Samuels mentions that her previous maid, Miss MacKnight, was murdered, which caused a local scandal. Samuels describes MacKnight as a reliable but gossiping woman. When Poirot asks about Mrs. MacKenzie, Samuels remarks that Mrs. MacKenzie's life of scrubbing floors seemed terrible. The passage concludes with Colonel Samoys appearing at the window, causing Samuels to jump up and scatter beans across the floor.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At a residence, John Samoys and Morin discuss household matters, including a dog eating chicken feed and the inadequacy of their spinach supply. Morin suggests opening a bulging can of fish. Nearby, Colonel Samson and Morley discuss Poirot, noting his French identity and suggesting they collect a seven-guinea installment from him.

Polly assists Mrs. Samoyed by picking up scattered beans. Mrs. Samoyed explains that the house is in disrepair because her husband's parents were poor and they lacked funds for repairs after returning from India. She mentions that they survive by taking in paying guests, including an elderly woman upstairs who has been bedridden since her arrival and is scheduled to leave for a niece's home the following day. Mrs. Samoyed requests the first week's rent; Poirot pays her seven one-pound notes and seven shillings. When Poirot identifies himself as a famous detective investigating the murder of Miss MacKintosh, Mrs. Samoyed is dismissive, claiming the case is closed because the renter was convicted. After she cuts her finger and blood drips onto the beans, Poirot declines to stay for lunch.

In a separate setting, Poirot interviews Mrs. Burch regarding the murder of her aunt. Mrs. Burch expresses annoyance at the police investigation and insists that James Bentley, the man convicted of the crime, is guilty. Poirot observes that Mrs. Burch is a tidy, capable woman and notes that Inspector Spence found no financial motive for the Burchs. Through questioning, Poirot learns that while the Burchs were blood relatives of the aunt, they were not close, though they knew she possessed a small fortune. He also learns that the aunt was a tidy woman who earned a daily wage doing odd jobs for the Carpenter family.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot discusses Mrs. Samuels of the Long Grass Hotel, noting that Aunt Ada Burch used to assist her. A profile of the victim, Mrs. McGinty, emerges: she was a solitary woman who knitted, polished floors, and shined brass. She was a Conservative voter who disapproved of immoral behavior and enjoyed magazines and movie star gossip, though she did not read books or watch many films.

Poirot observes Joe Burch, who arrives home for lunch. Unlike his more relaxed wife, Joe appears tense but eager to cooperate, likely because Poirot possesses a letter of introduction from Inspector Spence. Poirot contemplates whether Joe has a guilty conscience and considers the possibility that Joe attacked Aunt Ada to steal money for his wife, potentially framing James Bentley. Poirot notes that the murder weapon—described by forensics as an unusual, sharp object similar to a meat cleaver—has not been found, leading him to wonder if it is hidden within the Burch household.

Later, Poirot visits the Bredice & Scott office to interview Mr. Scott regarding James Bentley. To gain Scott's cooperation, Poirot lies, claiming he is investigating on behalf of Bentley's wealthy, powerful relatives. Mr. Scott, a talkative man, describes Bentley as a decent but ineffective junior staff member who lacked sales skills. When asked if he believes Bentley committed the murder, Scott suggests that while Bentley lacks the "nerve" for such an act, the man's mental state was unstable due to unemployment and stress, making it psychologically possible.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After speaking with Scott, who explains that James Bentley was let go due to poor business and a lack of confidence, Hercule Poirot encounters Maud Williams at the Blue Cat Cafe. Maud, a radiant blonde woman who works in the same office as Bentley, approaches Poirot to express her hope that new evidence might exonerate him. She describes Bentley as a polite, well-read, and shy man who is a devoted caretaker to his weak mother. While most people view Bentley as an unpleasant and unreliable individual, Maud’s testimony provides a different perspective on his character.

Poirot questions Maud about potential motives or enemies. She denies any knowledge of family feuds but reveals that Bentley once mentioned his landlady, Mrs. McKenty, kept her money under the floorboards. She notes that Bentley expressed concern regarding the landlady's carelessness rather than expressing a desire to steal. Poirot uses this information to reflect on how criminals often reveal themselves through careless speech.

To encourage Maud to spread word of the ongoing investigation, Poirot lies, claiming he has made significant progress. He provides her with his contact information at the Long Grass Hotel. As Poirot travels back to Brodini, he reflects on his depressing prison visit with Bentley, who believed he had no friends or enemies. Poirot concludes that Maud Williams is a friend to Bentley and observes that even an unattractive man can find a woman who admires him.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot visits the house of Mrs. McKinley to investigate a murder. He meets Mrs. Kiddell, a woman whose husband is a bricklayer and who moved into the house four months ago due to a housing shortage. Mrs. Kiddell explains that the victim, Mrs. McGinty, was killed in the living room, not the bedroom. She leads Poirot upstairs to the bedroom where the robbery occurred, noting that the room is now filled with the Kiddells' belongings because Mrs. MacKenzie's niece from Carlow took everything else. Mrs. Kiddell mentions that Mrs. Elliot was the first to find the body.

Poirot then visits Mrs. Elliot, a tall, dark-haired woman who describes the discovery of the body. She explains that Rakin, a baker, alerted her to the situation. Mrs. Elliot recounts finding the bedroom in disarray and discovering Mrs. McGinty in the sitting room with a cracked skull. She notes that the money was stolen. Mrs. Elliot claims she saw James Bentley walking down the road around eleven o'clock on the day of the murder. She expresses that she is not surprised by Bentley's arrest, characterizing him as "crazy" and suggesting that the location where he hid the money was so obvious that it seemed as though he wanted to be caught. She also confirms that the murder weapon remains a mystery.

After speaking with other neighbors who describe Mrs. MacKenzie as a respected person with no known enemies, Poirot reflects on the killer's motive: the money was left to be found to implicate Bentley, yet the weapon was hidden. Poirot concludes his investigation of the neighbors and enters a post office, where he meets a middle-aged woman named Sweetman.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot visits a local shop in Broadchurch to purchase writing paper and envelopes from Mrs. Sweetman. During their conversation, they discuss the death of Mrs. MacIntyre (also referred to as Mrs. McKenty), a woman Poirot is investigating. Mrs. Sweetman expresses doubt regarding the guilt of James Bentley, the man accused of the murder, noting that he is a shy and non-dangerous individual. She suggests that a tramp might have entered through an open window, though she questions why a thief would leave money behind.

Mrs. Sweetman and her assistant, Edna, provide details regarding Mrs. MacIntyre’s routine. Edna observes that on the Monday before her death, Mrs. MacIntyre appeared unusually excited or cheerful. Mrs. Sweetman notes that Mrs. MacIntyre had purchased a bottle of ink that day, presumably to write a letter. Poirot learns that Mrs. MacIntyre worked for various residents, including the Samoys at the Long Grass Inn, Dr. Rundle, the Upwards at The Manor, the Weatherbys, and the Carpenters.

Reflecting on the encounter, Poirot realizes that Mrs. MacIntyre rarely wrote letters, making her purchase of ink a significant clue. He seeks to determine to whom she was writing. Poirot subsequently questions Bessie Burch, Mrs. MacIntyre’s niece. Bessie denies receiving any letters from her aunt and explains that Mrs. MacIntyre found writing difficult and preferred to communicate via telephone through a neighbor, Mr. Benson, the pharmacist. Bessie notes that her aunt was a very thrifty woman.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot questions Bessie Burch regarding the personal correspondence and belongings of the deceased Mrs. McGinty. Bessie explains that Mrs. McGinty had no living close relatives, though she might receive government correspondence, which she would typically ask her relative Joe to help her manage. Bessie reveals that she packed Mrs. McGinty's personal items, including clothes and small belongings, into a suitcase stored on the top floor of the house.

Poirot requests to examine the suitcase. After Bessie goes to the kitchen, Poirot inspects the contents, which include clothing, a silver-backed mirror, a wedding photograph, a ceramic dog, a Bible, and various newspaper clippings regarding flying saucers and prophecies. He discovers two pairs of shoes wrapped in newspapers. One newspaper is the *Sunday Comet* dated November 19th, the Sunday before Mrs. McGinty was murdered on November 22nd. Poirot notices a large rectangular section has been cut out of the center of the *Sunday Comet*. He recalls that Mrs. McGinty had purchased a bottle of ink on the Monday following that newspaper's publication, leading him to suspect she may have been using it to respond to something in the paper.

Poirot examines the *Sunday Comet* article titled "Where are the women of past tragedies now?" which features stories of women involved in historical scandals. He reads about Eva Kane, the maid involved in the famous Craig case. In that case, Alfred Craig killed his wife via plant alkaloid poisoning, dismembered her, and buried her in his cellar. Eva Kane, who was pregnant at the time, reportedly fled to the New World to start a new life under a different name.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot examines four photographs and newspaper clippings belonging to the murdered Mrs. McGinty. The clippings, from a *Sunday Comet* series titled "Tragic Women," profile four women: Eva Kahn, who sought to protect her daughter from her own shameful past; Janice Corrigan, who suffered eight years of marital abuse before a young man intervened and was sentenced to five years for manslaughter; Lily Garber, who killed her cousin with a meat cleaver at age twelve and later became an exemplary citizen; and Vera Black, whose life was marked by criminal associations until she moved to a Commonwealth colony. Poirot notes that Mrs. McGinty, who rarely collected items, had recently clipped these specific articles and purchased ink, suggesting she intended to write a letter.

Poirot meets secretly with Pamela Hosford, the author of the "Tragic Women" series. He inquires about a letter Mrs. McGinty may have sent regarding the articles. Hosford initially struggles to remember the name but eventually recalls receiving a letter from a woman in "Broadwee" (possibly Broadhurst) with poor handwriting. The writer claimed to know the location of a photograph similar to one in the newspaper and inquired about payment. Hosford explains that the newspaper sent a standard, polite refusal to the wrong location. Poirot concludes that Mrs. McGinty, a woman known to be nosy, had seen an old photograph and recognized it in the newspaper, prompting her attempt to sell the information.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot questions Miss Hosford regarding the accuracy of her reports, noting errors in trial dates, names, and locations. Miss Hosford admits the articles are a "hodgepodge of romantic stories" where she uses her imagination. Poirot questions her portrayal of various women, including Eve Kane and Lily Gampel, and notes that while some subjects moved abroad, they may have returned to the country. Later, Poirot tells Spence that the "very good" nature of the people in Broadhurst might itself be a motive for murder.

Poirot visits Crossroads Farm to meet Dr. Rendell, a medical doctor. Poirot claims to be a defense lawyer seeking new evidence for an appeal regarding the Mrs. McGinty case. Dr. Rendell expresses certainty that James Bentley is the murderer. During their conversation, Dr. Rendell mentions that Mrs. McGinty performs weekly cleaning services at his home. Poirot asks if she is honest and if her statements are truthful, which makes Dr. Rendell uneasy. 

Dr. Rendell discusses several local residents, including the Upwards, the Samuels, and a young woman running a hotel. He also shares an "inside story" regarding the Cottington case, claiming his uncle was the husband's doctor. He asserts that the woman in that case was not innocent but instead manipulated a young man into committing murder, allowing her to become a wealthy widow. Poirot reflects on how such articles might make readers curious about the current whereabouts of the women mentioned in the reports.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot visits the Rundell household, where he meets Dr. Rundell and his wife, Hilda. Poirot observes that Mrs. Rundell is timid and appears to obey her husband blindly. He notes that Mrs. McGinty, the deceased housekeeper, used to work at this residence on Tuesday mornings.

Poirot then travels to Hunter's Manor, a large Victorian building. He is met by a foreign girl and led to meet Miss Dillie Henderson, the plain and wary thirty-year-old stepdaughter of Mr. Wetherby. Poirot explains that he is conducting an investigation on behalf of James Bentley's defense lawyer to gather new evidence regarding Mrs. McGinty's cause of death. Miss Henderson expresses dislike for Mrs. McGinty, describing her as a good worker who talked too much.

Dillie leads Poirot upstairs to meet her mother, Mrs. Wetherby, in a cluttered room filled with travel souvenirs. Mrs. Wetherby appears delicate and claims to be resting on doctor's orders. When questioned about Mrs. McGinty, Mrs. Wetherby expresses distress, recalling that the woman was "stupid" for keeping money under the floor instead of in a bank. She mentions that Mrs. McGinty had a "dreadful" and well-educated lodger. Mrs. Wetherby also speculates that the murder weapon, a carving knife, was thrown into a pond. Dillie interrupts, fiercely telling Poirot to stop questioning her mother due to her sensitivity. Poirot concludes the meeting by stating his motive: he is investigating because someone is scheduled to be hanged in three weeks, and he must determine if the accused is truly responsible.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Mrs. Wetherby reacts shrilly to a suggestion regarding a suspect, Hercule Poirot leaves her room. In the hall, Dierdre Henderson explains that her mother is upset by the news of the murder and the robbery. Dierdre describes her wartime service with the volunteer ambulance and women's volunteer service in Gillingham, noting that she remains home to care for her mother, who is too weak to perform housework. Poirot reflects on a drawer he saw in Mrs. Wetherby's room filled with sentimental items, contrasting it with Dierdre's lack of interest in the past.

Dierdre’s stepfather, Mr. Wetherby, enters the hall. He expresses skepticism regarding the inquiry into Mrs. McGinty, stating that the woman died months ago and the family knows nothing about her. A tense exchange occurs between Mr. Wetherby and Dierdre, characterized by mutual contempt.

Later, Poirot travels toward Goldfinch Manor, where Mrs. Upward and Robin Upward reside. On the road, he is struck by an apple core thrown from a car driven by the famous detective novelist, Mrs. Oliver. After Poirot helps her out of the vehicle, Mrs. Oliver explains she is traveling to Golden Chain Manor to meet Robin Upward, who is adapting one of her books into a play. Mrs. Oliver complains about the creative changes made to her characters. When Poirot mentions the murder of the cleaning woman, Mrs. McGinty, Mrs. Oliver confidently asserts that she will use her intuition to find the real killer, despite Poirot's caution regarding the difficulty of the case.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Robin Upward greets Ariadne Mrs. Oliver at Gold Chain Manor, embracing her and discussing a new idea for his second act. He assists with her luggage and invites her and Hercule Poirot inside to meet his mother, Laura Upward. Laura, an invalid in her sixties who uses a wheelchair, welcomes them in a luxurious living room. 

During the visit, Mrs. Oliver explains that Poirot is a detective investigating the murder of Mrs. McGinty, a cleaning lady who worked at the manor last autumn. While Robin expresses disappointment that the case is not a secret, Mrs. Oliver notes that the wrong man was convicted and faces hanging if the real murderer is not found. Robin describes the deceased cleaning lady as a very proper and tidy person. Laura Upward mentions her reliance on a faithful maid for simple cooking and expresses her dislike of needing to rely on others as her health declines.

After the social call, Poirot departs to catch a train to Gillingham to meet Superintendent Spence. As he leaves, he hears Robin making disparaging remarks about Poirot's appearance. While walking, Poirot narrowly avoids being hit by a van driven by Samson. Poirot then visits the modern home of the Carringtons, the residence of Guy Carrington, a wealthy engineer and aspiring politician. A butler refuses to let Poirot in, claiming the Carringtons are out, but Poirot encounters a young woman in a fur coat who identifies herself as Mrs. Carpenter. She remains cool and indifferent to Poirot's identity.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot visits the home of Eve and Guy Carpenter to investigate the murder of Mrs. McGinty, a cleaning lady who was killed the previous November. Eve, a beautiful woman with platinum hair and cold blue eyes, initially claims she does not know Mrs. McGinty. However, Poirot notes that Mrs. McGinty worked at the house every Friday while Eve was living at the Cottage. Eve eventually admits she remembers the murder but dismisses the victim as a "silly old woman" who was killed for hiding money under the floor.

When Poirot mentions the *Sunday Comet*, Eve becomes visibly agitated and calls for her husband. Guy Carpenter, a tall, arrogant man and public figure, enters the room. He expresses skepticism regarding Poirot's independent investigation, suggesting the case is closed and dismissing the motive as simple robbery. During the exchange, Eve makes a slip of the tongue, stating that Mrs. McGinty was a "terrible liar." Poirot identifies this as a significant clue, prompting Eve to clarify that the woman merely engaged in "silly gossip" about others.

Later, Poirot meets with Inspector Spence to discuss the case. They reflect on the obvious nature of the original robbery investigation, which overlooked a clipped newspaper and an ink bottle. Spence presents four faded original photographs from the *Sunday Comet* depicting a woman in a bell-shaped hat with roses. The two men conclude that the woman in the photographs is not Vera Black, as her presence in Broadhurst would have been widely known.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Inspector Spence discuss several women from past criminal cases to find connections to the death of Mrs. McGinnis. Spence provides details on Eva Kane, who fled the country after Craig's conviction and changed her name to Evelyn Hope. While the public viewed her as an innocent victim, Detective Inspector Trelawney suspected she was a clever actress who helped Craig dispose of his wife. They also discuss Janice Courtland, who allegedly incited her husband to violence to marry a rich man, and Lily Garfield, a girl who was sent to an orphanage after killing her aunt with a meat cleaver—the same type of weapon allegedly used to kill Mrs. McKittock.

Poirot and Spence then analyze the residents of Broadchurch. They consider the Wetherby family, where Mrs. McGinnis worked. Mrs. Wetherby is of a similar age to Eva Kane and has a daughter of a similar age to Eva's daughter. Poirot notes that the daughter hates her stepfather, suggesting motives involving family honor or secrets. They also consider Mrs. Broadhurst, who matches Eva Kane's age but is unlikely to be the killer due to her severe arthritis and domineering personality.

Finally, they examine three women in their thirties who could be Lily Garfield or Eva Kane's daughter: Dierdre Henderson, Dr. Rendell's wife, and Mrs. Guy Carpenter. Dr. Rendell's wife is blonde, matching Lily Garfield's description. Mrs. Carpenter is also blonde with captivating blue eyes, though Spence notes her disoriented, moth-like behavior when she recently fled a room.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses various theories with Inspector Spence regarding the identity of Mrs. McGinty and the potential motives for murder. Poirot suggests that Mrs. Carpenter (formerly Mrs. Sherlock) might have committed a crime to protect her social standing and her engagement to the ambitious Guy Carpenter, fearing he would reject her if he discovered she was the child of the notorious criminal Craig. Poirot also considers Mrs. Upward as a suspect, noting her domineering nature and the possibility that she might kill to prevent her son, Robin, from discovering her past identity as Eva Kane. Poirot dismisses the idea that Robin would kill for his mother's sake, suggesting instead that he is too selfish and might exploit such news for his own benefit. Spence notes that the destruction of records during the war makes verifying identities difficult.

While waiting for a train at Gilchester station, Poirot is suddenly and forcefully pushed toward the tracks by an unknown person. He is saved from being crushed by an oncoming train by a tall, burly army sergeant. Realizing that someone is attempting to kill him to prevent his investigation, Poirot calls Superintendent Spence to report the assassination attempt, viewing the event as confirmation that his investigation is on the right track. Meanwhile, at the Carpenter residence, a meter reader and Guy Carpenter's butler discuss the upcoming elections and the fact that Mr. Carpenter drives a Bentley.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
An electrician records observations in a notebook regarding the movements of several individuals. He notes that Mr. C drove home alone around 10:30 PM, while Mrs. C arrived home ten minutes before him, claiming she took the train. He also records that Dr. R made a house call toward Gilchester, and that Mrs. R was at home alone after having coffee, though the housekeeper, Mrs. Scott, did not see her again.

Meanwhile, at Chained Flower Manor, Robin Upward and Mrs. Oliver discuss a play. Robin wants to introduce gender conflict and romantic tension between the character Sven Hagen and a girl named Ingrid. Mrs. Oliver argues that Sven Hagen is sixty years old and has no interest in women, whereas Robin insists he must be thirty-five to remain a box office draw. Seeking to escape the discussion, Mrs. Oliver decides to walk to Broadhurst to assist Hercule Poirot with a real crime investigation.

While buying apples at the post office, Mrs. Oliver chats with Mrs. Sweetman. Mrs. Oliver discusses her own mystery novels, including *The Second Goldfish Mystery* and *The Death of a Maiden*. Mrs. Sweetman mentions a murder case that occurred nearby last November and notes that a "little foreigner" arrived yesterday. The conversation turns to Mrs. Weatherby, whom Mrs. Sweetman suggests is capable of standing up despite being immobile. After the post office clerk, Miss Henderson, leaves with her dog, Mrs. Oliver follows her to strike up a conversation.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Ariadne Oliver, a famous mystery novelist, discusses a dog named Ben with Deirdre (Dilly) Henderson. Deirdre recognizes Mrs. Oliver and expresses her admiration for her books, though she notes her mother dislikes detective stories. They discuss a recent murder in a nearby cottage involving an old cleaning lady. Deirdre explains that the victim's tenant, James Bentley, was arrested because she had money hidden under the floor. Deirdre expresses a strong belief in Bentley's innocence, noting that he is a lonely man who recently lost his mother. She mentions that she empathizes with him because she and her mother depend on each other, whereas she has a difficult relationship with her "heartless" stepfather, Mr. Weatherby. After Deirdre leaves for Hunter Manor, Mrs. Oliver writes in her notebook that Deirdre is not the killer.

On her way to the Long Grass Hotel, Mrs. Oliver meets Robin Upward and Eve Carpenter, whose husband is a future MP. Robin jokingly suggests that Mrs. Oliver's next murder scene should take place in Broadhurst Gardens and discusses fictional murder plots involving Eve or himself. Robin also mentions that Eve intends to invite the detective Hercule Poirot to a dinner party at her home. They encounter Molly Samson, who is cleaning a pigsty. Robin introduces Molly to Mrs. Oliver. Eve expresses interest in meeting the tenant, James Bentley, and suggests inviting him to the dinner.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
As the Carpenters' dinner concludes, Mrs. Oliver approaches Hercule Poirot and invites him to the terrace to speak privately. She slips him a note identifying Dr. Lundell as the murderer, claiming his motive was that Miss Macquart discovered he was practicing medicine illegally. Poirot expresses skepticism regarding her certainty. He informs Mrs. Oliver that someone attempted to push him onto the tracks at Gilchester station the previous night at 9:35. Mrs. Oliver notes that Dr. Lundell made a house call that night, but Poirot points out that several people's whereabouts are unverified, noting that the maid, Frieda, was at the movies and could not provide alibis for those at Hunter's Manor. Mrs. Oliver provides an alibi for Robin and his mother, stating they were playing cards with her from 8:00 to 10:30.

Poirot complains about the poor conditions and food at Mrs. Samuels' residence. Muriel Samuels joins them, appearing intoxicated from gin. During the conversation, Muriel becomes emotional while discussing adoption, revealing that she was adopted and expressing her belief that mothers should never abandon their children. After the guests depart, the group visits Mrs. Upward at the Golden Chain Garden. A discussion arises regarding genetics versus environment. Mrs. Upward asserts that bloodline and genetics are the most important factors in determining character, a sentiment supported by Johnny Sams. This leads Mrs. Oliver to question the extent of hereditary traits across generations.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a gathering, Poirot discusses the McKitty case, addressing why he does not believe the gloomy tenant was the killer. The group discusses their whereabouts on the night of November 22nd, the night of Mrs. McGinty's murder. Robin recalls being on the radio in Colpeter discussing a janitor character in a play; Hilary Rundle remembers being at her mother's house, noting that she had asked to keep her company. Mrs. Uppward mentions she had gone to bed early due to a headache in a room facing the back garden, while Mollie recalls the baker announcing the murder the following morning.

Poirot presents four photographs as clues. While the others comment on the fashion and subjects, Mrs. Uppward claims to have seen a photo of a youthful Lily Garfield recently, though she cannot specify where. After the group departs, Poirot confronts Mrs. Uppward privately. He suggests that her recognition of the photo is dangerous, noting that Mrs. McGinty also recognized a photo before her death. Mrs. Uppward remains evasive, stating that her memories are fuzzy and that she needs time to think before acting. She refuses to involve Inspector Spence, claiming it is not the right time. Poirot concludes that she is a woman who keeps things to herself and has decided to investigate the matter on her own.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While walking in the garden, Poirot is approached by a tense Mrs. Rundell. She questions his motives for being in Broadchurch, suggesting that his stated reason—investigating the death of Mrs. McGinty—is merely a convenient excuse. She also inquires about an anonymous letter, which she dismisses as a cowardly fabrication. Her reaction leaves Poirot wondering if she is attempting to mislead him or if she is actually Lily Garfield, a woman recently seen in a photograph and known to have undergone shorthand training.

Poirot later experiences a sudden intuition regarding the murder weapon. He discovers a brass object on a bookshelf at the Long Grass Hotel. Molly enters and identifies the object as a "sugar hammer" or "sugar mallet," a heavy, axe-shaped item decorated with colored stones and a bird with emerald eyes. Molly, who purchased it at a charity sale featuring items from the Weatherbys, jokingly refers to it as a "wife's best friend" for killing a husband. Poirot notices a faint stain on the blade of the hammer. Believing it to be the murder weapon, he wraps it in paper and hides it in a box in his bedroom before leaving the house.

In a separate scene at Golden Plover Manor, Mrs. Oliver and Robin discuss a character she has created. Mrs. Oliver expresses frustration with the character's traits, specifically his identity as a Finnish vegetarian.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Robin Upward and Mrs. Ariadne Oliver discuss a potential play based on a murder, with Robin suggesting a "swan song" and Mrs. Oliver expressing a desire for immediate profit. Robin, frustrated by his script and his mood, suggests they discuss casting and visit the Little Ripple Theatre to discuss a character named Cecil.

Hercule Poirot visits Hunter Manor to speak with Dierdre Henderson. He inquires about a "sugar hammer"—a copper tool decorated with a bird and colorful stones—to determine if it was originally in the house. Dierdre explains that her mother bought it in Baghdad and it was sold at a charity sale. She specifies the item was sold at the Harvest Festival at the end of September, rather than at Christmas. Poirot notes the discrepancy between this and other information, questioning why Dierdre did not ask why he was inquiring about the tool. He reflects that while Mollie Sampson might be forgetful regarding dates, the slow and clumsy Dierdre should have a more accurate memory.

Poirot later visits Mollie, who informs him that someone called him; she recorded the number in her ration book. Poirot calls the number and reaches Maud Williams at the Brace & Scott Firm. Maud mentions she is calling regarding a house Poirot had previously inquired about. When Poirot brings up the murders of James Bentley and Mrs. McGinty, Maud asks if they can help.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot arranges for Maud Williams to work as a housekeeper to assist James Bently. During a phone call, Maud expresses her willingness to quit her current job and take on the role, despite the potential for dealing with difficult people. Poirot then calls Mrs. Wetherby to recommend Maud for a trial period as a maid. Mrs. Wetherby, who is frustrated by the perceived ingratitude of foreign workers, accepts the offer, though she privately discusses the arrangement with someone in her house, noting that her husband, Roger, has an odd temper.

After leaving Mollie Samsa’s house, Poirot meets Maud at the Blue Cat café in Gilchester. He warns her that a murderer is loose in the village, but Maud remains confident in her ability to handle herself. During their conversation, Maud reveals she has previously visited Broadchurch for business, where she visited Mrs. Samuels at the Long Grass Hotel. She also admits to seeing James Bently in Broadchurch shortly before the murder of Mrs. MacKenzie, noting that she had sent him a postcard to arrange a meeting. Additionally, Maud mentions seeing Robin Upward in Broadchurch, whom she approached for an autograph. She also notes her familiarity with the Cartwrights, mentioning that Mr. Cartwright is a potential future MP.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot shows Miss Williams four photographs that were published in the *Sunday Comet* the week before Mrs. MacKenty was murdered. Miss Williams recognizes a cloche hat worn by Janice Coltrane and finds a child in the photos familiar. Poirot reveals he drew an idea from a newspaper article, and Miss Williams suggests that Eve Kene, who would now be around the age of Mrs. Upwood, might be involved or targeted by someone who hates her. The conversation briefly touches upon the famous Craig case.

Later, Mrs. Oliver attends a play with Robin. While Robin is social and enjoys the after-party, Mrs. Oliver finds the social interaction an ordeal. During the party, an actor named Michael discusses Robin's mother, noting her tendency to manipulate people, such as her treatment of Alex Rosskov. After the event, Robin drives Mrs. Oliver back to Wisteria Manor. Upon entering the dark house, Mrs. Oliver discovers Mrs. Upwood's body in the living room. She runs to the entrance hall to inform a returning Robin that Mrs. Upwood has been murdered.

Inspector Spence arrives at the scene and observes the crime. He notes that the murder was "neat and clean," explaining that Mrs. Upwood was strangled with her own scarf. He describes the method as an efficient constriction of the carotid artery that prevents the victim from struggling or crying out.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Spence and Poirot discuss the murder of Mrs. Upward, noting that the killer was likely someone she knew. Evidence at the scene included two coffee cups; while the guest's fingerprints were wiped away, lipstick traces remained on a rim. Spence notes that Mrs. Upward had recognized a photo of Lily Gambol, linking the crime to the McQuitty murder case. It is believed Mrs. Upward was playing detective and called a guest to her home at Golden Chain Manor while her son, Robin, and Mrs. Oliver were at the theater. The maid, Frieda, was in her room and did not witness the event.

Dierdre Henderson arrives to provide a statement. She claims Mrs. Upward called her to invite her for coffee between 8:30 and 9:00 PM. However, when Dierdre arrived, the house was dark and no one answered. She assumed Mrs. Upward had gone to the theater and left to go for a walk. Spence observes that Dierdre does not wear lipstick or perfume, which contrasts with the expensive perfume scent reported by Mrs. Oliver and Robin Upward at the scene. 

Spence considers whether Dierdre could be Lily Garfield, though he notes their backgrounds differ. He also contemplates Eve Carrington as a suspect, noting she can afford expensive perfume. Finally, Spence confirms that a sugar hammer is the murder weapon, matching the wounds from the McQuitty case and containing traces of human blood.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses the disorganized nature of the Somers household with Superintendent Spence, noting that the residents frequently misplace tools and lack a methodical way of living. Spence informs Poirot that James Bentley’s execution has been delayed following an application to the Home Secretary, providing time to investigate new evidence.

Poirot visits Bentley, who remains desperate and skeptical of his chances. Poirot attempts to encourage him by naming his supporters: Superintendent Spence and Maud Williams, a former colleague from Bresch and Scott. Bentley reveals he has had minor social interactions with Williams, including eating at the Blue Cat Cafe and walking up a hill with her, though he admits he was often too preoccupied with his lack of money to pursue her.

During the interview, Bentley provides details regarding the victim, Mrs. McGinty. He recalls that she frequently discussed her Sunday Comet newspaper, specifically an article regarding old murder cases. Bentley remembers Mrs. McGinty claiming that someone connected to one of those cases was living in Broadhurst. He vaguely recalls her mentioning a woman who writes plays, suggesting the subject might have been Mrs. Upward.
