Bentley mentions that Mrs. McGinty had commented on the woman's pride and noted that she was recognizable despite an old photograph. However, Bentley expresses uncertainty about whether the comments were specifically directed at Mrs. Upward, noting that he was too anxious about his own circumstances to listen closely at the time.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
James Bentley discusses local residents with Hercule Poirot, recalling comments made by Mrs. MacKenzie. Bentley mentions Mr. Carpenter’s new house and engagement to Mrs. Sherlock, and notes Mrs. MacKenzie's disdain for her. He also recalls Mrs. MacKenzie’s negative views of the Weatherby couple, describing the wife as a "fuss" maker and the husband as silent, noting it was an unhappy family. Poirot observes that Bentley is deeply engaged in thought regarding life at Hunter's Manor. Bentley reveals he helped a Silky Terrier caught in a trap and discusses his conversation with Dierdre Henderson. He mentions that Henderson's mother suffered and that her stepfather was involved in her difficulties. Bentley defends Henderson's attractiveness, causing him to blush.

In another setting, Mrs. Sweetman pressures Edna to report what she witnessed regarding the murder of Mrs. Upward. Edna, distraught and fearful of her father's reaction, refuses to speak to Bert Helling or a "foreign gentleman." Mrs. Weatherby enters the post office to buy knitting supplies and discusses her recent ill health and her dissatisfaction with her help's manners. She also comments on the local atmosphere of fear following the murder, criticizing Mrs. Samohes of the Long Grass Inn for not locking her doors. Mrs. Sweetman informs Mrs. Weatherby that Mr. Upward is staying at the Long Grass Inn due to his distress, while Janet Groom has returned to her hometown. 

Colonel Johnny Samoyski enters the post office to mail packages. Mrs. Sweetman asks for his advice regarding Edna, explaining that Edna saw something on the night of the murder. She asks the Colonel to confirm a rumor that a lady was having coffee with Mrs. Upward on the night of the crime.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Edna, a young woman, informs Johnny Samohais and Mrs. Sweetman that she witnessed a woman entering a house. Edna explains that she was watching from the shadows of a tree across the street between 8:30 and 9:00 p.m. She notes that the woman had golden hair and wore a black coat, which distinguishes her from Miss Henderson, who has dark hair. Edna observed the woman walk to the gate and enter the house while the porch light was on, noting that Mr. Robbins and a female novelist were at the theater.

Fearing her strict father's reaction, Edna becomes distressed. Mrs. Sweetman explains to Samohais that Edna had been lying about her whereabouts; she claimed to be going to the Keller place to see Reggie, but was actually waiting to meet Charlie Masterson, a married farmhand from the Colvin place. To protect Edna's reputation, Mrs. Sweetman arranges for her to be driven to the police station in Gillingchester via Samson's pickup truck to ensure discretion.

Meanwhile, at Gillingchester, Hercule Poirot meets with Inspector Spence. Poirot reflects on his distaste for the victim, James Benthall, whom he considers a self-centered and ungrateful man. Poirot reports that Benthall provided little useful information but mentioned that Mrs. McGinty claimed someone related to the "Craig case" lives in Broadhurst Gardens and would be "proud" if the truth were known. Spence notes that investigating local backgrounds is difficult due to wartime destruction of records and the transient nature of the population, mentioning families like the Samoise and Carpenter dynasties.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Inspector Spence discuss the possibility that Lily Gopel is either Evelyn Carpenter or Sheila Randall. Poirot notes that both women have motives to hide their identities. While Mrs. Carpenter appeared innocent during questioning, Mrs. Randall was notably tense, though Poirot recalls her previous claim of receiving an anonymous letter at the Long Grass Inn, which he doubts is authentic. Spence expresses concern regarding Guy Carpenter, a prominent local figure and parliamentary candidate, whose reputation would be ruined if he were found guilty or acting as an accessory.

Poirot shifts the focus to the motivation behind keeping photographs, proposing three reasons: vanity, nostalgia, or a desire for revenge. He argues that a photo of Lily Garfield—described as unattractive—would not be kept for vanity by beautiful women like Evelyn Carpenter or Sheila Randall, nor for nostalgia, as Lily was unloved. He concludes that since the late Mrs. Upward recognized one of the photos but intentionally misled Poirot by pointing to the wrong one, she likely intended to protect the person's secret or handle the matter privately to investigate their connection to Mrs. McGinty’s death. This leads Spence to suspect Deirdre Henderson and her mother.

In a separate scene, Mrs. Weatherby returns home from the post office and collapses on her sofa. She accuses her maid, Maud Williams, of spying by rummaging through her drawers. After demanding a restorative drink of milk, egg, and brandy, Mrs. Weatherby is joined by Deirdre, who arrives with her Siamese dog. Deirdre notes that Mrs. Weatherby seems angry.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At the Weatherby residence, Mrs. Weatherby discusses her fatigue and her feelings of being a burden with Dierdre (Dilly). After Dierdre accidentally knocks over a bronze statue while rushing to get eggnog, Mr. Weatherby enters and criticizes Dierdre’s clumsiness and her dog's barking. During their conversation, Mr. Weatherby asks Edith Weatherby about the investigation into Mrs. Upward's death. Edith notes that the police are at a loss regarding the motive and the identity of the killer. Mr. Weatherby suggests a vagrant might be responsible and advises Dierdre to lock the doors, while Edith mentions that Mrs. MacKenzie, a maid, worked for Mrs. Upward. After Mr. Weatherby leaves, Maud (Mrs. Moore) delivers eggnog to Mrs. Weatherby, whom she observes with an imposing presence.

Meanwhile, Hercule Poirot returns to Broadhurst, feeling exhausted by his attempts to discern a pattern in the recent murders. At the Long Grass Hotel, he observes Robin Upward struggling to write a play. Muriel Sampson enters in a panic, reporting that Johnny has not returned from the post office. Morley subsequently searches the lounge for agricultural department forms, creating a mess of various items on the floor. Disturbed by the lack of order, Poirot begins methodically tidying the scattered belongings. His work is interrupted by a phone call from Inspector Spence. Spence reveals new evidence: a witness from the Broadhurst post office saw a blonde woman, not Dierdre Henderson, entering the house on the night of the murder. This shifts the suspicion back to either Eve Carpenter or Sheila Lund, leaving Poirot in stunned silence.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot receives a phone call from Maud Williams, who requests a meeting at the post office. While walking toward the post office, Poirot encounters Sergeant Fletcher, who has been conducting a search at the Manor House for Inspector Spence. Fletcher reveals that while no secret drawers were found, he discovered an old book containing the name "Evelyn Hope" written in pencil on the title page. Poirot recognizes this as the name Eva Kane used when she left England.

At the post office, Maud Williams informs Poirot that she witnessed an individual, dressed like a man in an old felt hat, attempting to climb through Mrs. Weatherby’s window using a gardener's ladder. The intruder fled when Maud saw them. Maud also confirms that "Evelyn Hope" was the name used by a woman who went to Australia, as reported in *The Sunday Comet*. This connection leads Maud to conclude that a person named Michael was correct regarding a previous theory.

Later, Evelyn Carrington approaches Poirot to hire him. She is distressed because the police suspect her of killing Mrs. Upward. She admits to attempting to bribe the Croftes to provide a false alibi, claiming she was home listening to the radio during the crime, though the Croftes refused. Evelyn also reveals that Mrs. Upward had sent a message via Croft requesting a visit that evening, a request Evelyn had intended to ignore.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot confronts Eve Carpenter regarding her decision to pay a man to withhold a message and to provide her with an alibi. Poirot warns her that a jury will value the testimony of a servant over hers. During the exchange, Poirot observes that Eve's behavior suggests she attempts to solve all problems with money. He also makes a cryptic remark about her past, noting her resemblance to an "ugly duckling" and mentioning her former use of braces. Eve refuses to hire Poirot, stating he is working for James Bentley, the man accused of killing a janitor. After Eve leaves, Poirot reflects on the possibility that Mrs. Upward telephoned several women, including Dierdre Henderson and Evelyn Carrington.

While at a residence, Poirot discovers a photograph in a desk drawer but quickly hides it under a cushion when Moll enters the room. Moll mentions that her husband, Johnny, refuses to sell an oil painting of an ancestor who may have met a terrible end at sea. 

Later, Poirot visits Dr. Darnell's house and speaks with Mrs. Darrendell. She admits that Mrs. Upward called her around six o'clock on the night of her death, asking her to visit after nine o'clock because the house would be empty. Mrs. Darrendell explains she failed to go because she fell asleep, and she expresses guilt and fear regarding the situation.

Finally, Poirot discusses the investigation with Spence. They consider the theory that Mrs. Upward might be Eva Kahn (also known as Evelyn Hope), who may have faked her death in Australia twenty years prior to marry a wealthy industrialist. They note that Mrs. Upward's maiden name may have been Hargreaves and discuss whether Mrs. Upward killed Mrs. McIntyre to protect her secret.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Inspector Spence discuss the connection between recent deaths, identifying Evelyn Hope as a key figure. Poirot suggests that the suspects—Eva Kane, Lily Gambol, Janice Courtland, and Vera Black—may be linked to the Craig family, for whom Eva Kane served as a nanny. Poirot notes that Eva Kane was pregnant before leaving Britain following the execution of Craig, implying that her child may be involved in a motive of revenge.

Poirot places a private call to the novelist Mrs. Ariadne Oliver to gather information regarding a small circus theater and individuals named Cecil and Michael. Following this, Poirot informs Spence that he intends to visit Michael, a minor actor with the Karenquay Theater Company.

At the Long Grass Hotel, Poirot addresses a group of people to explain the murders. He uses a children's rhyme about how various women died—such as Mrs. McQuitty and Mrs. MacKenzie—to illustrate the pattern of the killings. Poirot reveals that the investigation into the death of Mrs. McKittrick, for which James Bentley was wrongly convicted, was aided by a clue involving a bottle of ink and a photograph in *The Sunday Comet*. He explains that Mrs. McKittrick recognized a photo of Eva Kane, who was connected to the Craig murder case. 

Poirot further reveals that Mrs. Upward attempted to mislead him by pointing to a photo of Lily Gambol when she actually recognized Eva Kane's photo. He notes that before Upward's wife was killed, she summoned three women—Mrs. Carpenter, Mrs. Rendell, and Miss Henderson—to see her privately. Poirot concludes that these three women share a commonality: they are approximately the same age as Eva Kane's daughter.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals that the murders of Mrs. Upward and Mrs. McKinley were committed by a man motivated by money. He begins by addressing the evidence found at Mrs. Upward's house, including two coffee cups with lipstick traces and the scent of expensive perfume. While these clues initially suggested a woman was involved, Poirot argues they were clumsy attempts to frame one of three women—Mrs. Carpenter, Mrs. Rundle, or Miss Henderson—who had received phone messages. He concludes the feminine traces were planted to mislead investigators, as the killer was actually a man.

Poirot presents a faded photograph of Eva Kane, found in a desk drawer, which has the words "My mother..." written on the back. He explains that Morley Saunders is the daughter of the notorious murderers Craig and Eva Kane, though she was adopted and unaware of her parentage. He asserts that a man, desperate to protect his lineage and ancestral home from the scandal of this connection, committed the murders.

Poirot then identifies the killer as Robin Upward. He reveals that Robin is actually the son of Evelyn Kean (who used the name Evelyn Hope), not Mrs. Upward's biological son. Robin had assumed the name Upward and used a romanticized backstory to gain the affection and wealth of Mrs. Upward. After Mrs. McGinty mentioned the photograph and the connection to the notorious Eva Kane, Robin realized Mrs. Upward, who was superstitious about heredity, would never tolerate a foster son with such a background. To protect his inheritance and conceal his true identity, Robin killed to ensure the secret remained buried.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot accuses Robin Upward of murdering Mrs. McGinty (referred to in other contexts as Miss Macquart) to prevent her from exposing the secret that he is not the biological son of Mrs. Upward. During the confrontation, Poirot threatens Robin with a sugar hammer, prompting Robin to scream that the killing was an accident and that he is not responsible due to his nature.

Poirot explains his reasoning, noting that Mrs. Samuels had provided a clue regarding Robin's adoption. Mrs. Upward, who was pathologically afraid of social disgrace, had supported Robin as a patron rather than a mother. Miss Macquart discovered a photograph of Robin's actual mother—a ballet dancer—and likely intended to seek a "generous present" from Mrs. Upward to keep the secret. To eliminate the threat, Robin stole a blunt instrument that Mrs. Samson had joked was a perfect murder weapon. He killed Miss Macquart on his way to a radio broadcast, hid her money to frame Bentley, and attempted to make the crime look like a robbery.

Poirot further reveals that Robin meticulously staged a second crime scene to frame a woman, using stolen lipstick and perfume to implicate Eve Keane. However, Robin's overconfidence led to his downfall; he kept a photograph of his biological mother and a book containing his real name. Poirot concludes that Robin attempted to plant the fatal photograph in Dierdre Henderson's belongings but failed because Mrs. Weatherby had locked the windows. Ultimately, Poirot identifies Robin as the culprit, noting that Robin's presence at the time of the Gilchester Station incident proved his involvement.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot concludes that Robin Upward killed his mother, Mrs. Uppward, noting that the killer's arrogance and the deliberate arrangement of "props" at the scene allowed the crime to occur despite Mrs. Oliver being nearby. Poirot then confronts Miss Williams, revealing that she is actually Maud Craig, the daughter of Eve Kane. Maud explains that she sought revenge after discovering that Eve Kane had not died in Australia as believed, but had instead become the wealthy and arrogant Mrs. Uppward. Maud had taken a job to investigate and had gone to the house with a gun to confront her, only to find Mrs. Uppward already dead. Poirot decides not to take action against her.

In an epilogue, Poirot and Superintendent Spence celebrate the case's resolution at a restaurant. Spence notes that Robin Upward's confession was facilitated by his own cowardice and the lack of evidence. They discuss other characters, including Mrs. Carpenter, whose shady past as a cabaret dancer led her to hide her true status, and the Weatherby family, whose conflicts are driven by money. Poirot reveals he is meddling in the lives of Dierdre Henderson and James Bentley, hoping to arrange their marriage. 

The conversation shifts to Dr. Lund, whom Spence reveals was once accused of poisoning his first wife via anonymous letters. Poirot realizes this explains why Dr. Lund attempted to push him under a train, as the doctor feared Poirot was investigating him. The passage concludes with Spence confirming that Robin Upward will be tried for murder the following week.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Every Thursday at 7:30 PM, Hercule Poirot dines at "The Happy Coffee Shop" in St. Gregory's Alley, London. While observing the staff—a waitress with "bird's nest" hair and a short, round-faced waitress—Poirot listens to a discussion regarding the temperament of certain people. His routine is interrupted when a woman wearing a light brown coat and a deep brown hat bursts into the shop, appearing to flee from someone.

The woman, identified as Jane (or Jinnie), is visibly distraught, panting, and filled with fear. She ignores Poirot's greeting and stares anxiously out the window at the dark street. A curly-haired waitress asks if she is alright, noting that Jane looks as though she has "seen a ghost." Jane requests a cup of strong tea and retreats to a distant corner table, where she continues to sit in a state of terror, frequently turning to watch the door.

Poirot observes that Jane is not wearing a wedding ring and notes her intense anxiety. He decides to approach her, sitting at her table to offer assistance. To gain her attention, Poirot reveals that he is a retired policeman. This revelation causes Jane to focus on him, though she immediately questions whether his retired status means he lacks the authority to arrest criminals in London. Poirot confirms he is merely an old man enjoying retirement. He then asks Jane directly if she feels she is in danger and if she fears that someone from outside is following her.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot meets with a woman named Jane (also referred to as Jean or Jenny) at a cafe. Jane expresses that she is in grave danger and believes her death is inevitable. She claims she deserves to die and insists that her death is the only way justice can be served. When Poirot offers to help and mentions his connection to Edward Catchpole, a detective with the Metropolitan Police, Jane becomes distressed. She accuses Poirot of being a retired policeman and a foreigner, then begs him to swear that if she dies, he will tell his police friend not to investigate the killer or solve the case. Before Poirot can respond, Jane flees into the night.

Feeling responsible for her disappearance, Poirot attempts to eat dinner but lacks an appetite. He approaches the kitchen to speak with the staff. A woman named Phyllis Sprung (who identifies herself as Euphemia) informs him that Jane is "beyond help." Poirot introduces himself as a Belgian, and Phyllis agrees to answer his questions. She reveals that Jane previously worked as a maid for a wealthy family. Phyllis notes that Jane would visit the cafe to buy coffee and cake before her mistress's parties, often speaking in a manner that mimicked a lady. Poirot observes that Jane's voice seemed fake and unnatural for a maid, a detail Phyllis admits she cannot confirm but acknowledges that Jane always spoke as if she were noble.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
At the "Joyful Coffee Shop," Poirot discusses Mrs. Jennings (also referred to as Janie or Madame Jenny) with Phryne, Phoebe, and Phyllida. Phil defends Jenny’s taste in tea, while Polly asks about the location of the house where Jenny works. Phryne explains that Jenny was deeply hurt years ago by a single, undisclosed event that broke her heart, causing her to live in her own world. Phoebe adds that Jenny is extremely self-absorbed and lacks curiosity about others or social gossip. Phyllida notes that while Jenny does not dwell on herself, she keeps her own unhappy secrets. Poirot concludes that Jenny is ensnared in danger and vows to return the next morning to find her before harm arrives, though Phryne expresses doubt that anyone can truly find her.

Edward Catchpole, a thirty-two-year-old friend of Poirot from Scotland Yard, provides a retrospective account of the events. He recounts meeting Poirot six weeks prior, when Poirot was planning a temporary stay at Mrs. Blanche’s feminine, lace-filled apartment to allow his "fast-working" brain to rest. During this time, Poirot revealed he is Belgian rather than French. On the Thursday night in question, February 7, 1929, Poirot visited the "Happy Café" because his landlady, Mrs. Eversleigh, was away. Catchpole notes that Poirot returned home late, around ten past ten, after meeting Jenny. Catchpole also observes that Mrs. Eversleigh appeared anxious upon her return, noting that Catchpole himself looked unwell.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
After returning from the Brawnsham Hotel, Carter is attended to by Mrs. Bunscombe, who offers various remedies for his distress, including an herb-stuffed pillow and a blue bottle of liquid for his bath. Carter declines these offers and waits for Poirot, who is unusually late returning from "The Joyful Cafe." When Poirot finally arrives, he reveals he has been searching for a woman named Jane, fearing she may have been murdered.

Carter, unsettled by the mention of murder, discloses that he witnessed three killings at the Brawnsham Hotel that evening: two women and one man. He describes the crime scenes as chillingly organized, noting that the three bodies were found lying neatly on the floor with their arms at their sides, palms down, and legs together. Carter further reveals a bizarre detail: each victim had an identical gold cufflink in their mouth, engraved with the letters "PIJ."

Upon hearing this, Poirot becomes agitated and recounts his own encounter with Miss Valentine (referred to as Miss Jennings/Mrs. Vane). He explains that she had instructed him that if she were found dead, he must tell his policeman friend not to look for the killer and to "not let them talk." While Carter interprets this literally as the victim wanting the killer to go unpunished, Poirot suggests a deeper connection between the instruction "don't let them talk" and the discovery of the gold cufflinks in the victims' mouths.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and the narrator debate the interpretation of a message from Miss Jenny (Jean). Poirot argues that Jean’s precise use of grammar suggests she was not merely warning against speaking to the police, but specifically instructing them not to open the mouths of the three victims to find the gold cufflinks bearing the initials "PIJ." Poirot believes Jean knew the killer would target her next and that the initials belong to an important figure. He posits that because cufflinks come in pairs, a fourth cufflink likely exists, intended for Jean's mouth. The narrator initially dismisses this as ridiculous, suggesting the killer may have only used three cufflinks, but eventually acknowledges the possibility of more victims.

Poirot insists on visiting the Bluxton Hotel the following morning to investigate the murders, intending to meet the narrator there after searching for Jean. The next morning, the narrator waits at the Browns Hotel, feeling uneasy about Poirot's presence and the responsibility of his involvement. While the narrator is conversing with the hotel manager, Luca Lazari, Poirot arrives looking downcast. He informs the narrator that he was unable to find Jean, as she failed to meet him in the café as planned.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and his companion discuss the disappearance of Miss Fay at the Blaux Han Hotel. Luca Lazari, a hotel representative, attempts to distract Poirot with descriptions of the hotel's luxury, but Poirot remains focused on the three recent murders. The victims, Mrs. Harriet Sipper, Miss Edith Gransbury, and Mr. Richard Nigges, were all guests who checked into the hotel separately on Tuesday and were found dead in their locked rooms the following day.

The narrator notes that the victims were killed alone in their respective rooms and that the room keys are missing, leading to the suspicion that the killer took them. Poirot suggests the killer may have taken the keys along with several cufflinks. The cause of death appears to be cyanide poisoning, administered through tea consumed by Sipper and Gransbury, and sherry consumed by Nigges. Forensic examinations place the time of death between 7:15 PM and 8:20 PM.

Lazari claims that at 7:15 PM, all three victims were seen by staff when their room service food was delivered. Poirot finds the coincidence of three separate guests checking in on the same day, dying on the same day, and all requesting room service at the exact same time to be impossible. To investigate these discrepancies, Poirot demands that Lazari gather all hotel staff in an empty room for questioning. Meanwhile, Poirot and the narrator proceed to the victims' rooms to conduct a thorough search.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Poirot and the narrator, Kattimer, use a universal key to inspect the rooms of three victims: Harriet Sipper (room 121), Richard Niggles (room 238), and Ada Gransbury (room 317). Poirot notes that the victims are located on consecutive floors and concludes they were not randomly selected, as they all share a connection to the initials "PIJ" and checked into the hotel on the same day.

The rooms are nearly identical, containing standard furniture, personal items, and the corpses. The bodies are found meticulously arranged on the floor with their feet facing the door and arms at their sides. Kattmer observes that the arrangement resembles how doctors position deceased patients. In the rooms of Sipper and Gransbury, teacups and saucers are present on coffee tables; however, Gransbury’s room also contains a tray with an extra teacup and crumbs, suggesting she had dinner with someone, possibly the killer. In contrast, Niggles’ room contains only a sherry glass. Poirot also notes that Gransbury brought significantly more clothing than the others.

Upon returning to Niggles' room, Poirot identifies several discrepancies. Unlike the other rooms, Niggles' window is wide open, making the room cold, and the view overlooks the hotel garden rather than "Great London." Furthermore, Poirot observes a difference in the placement of the cufflinks: while Sipper and Gransbury had their PIJ cufflinks protruding slightly from their lips, Niggles' cufflink was pushed deep into his throat. Katttimer attempts to dismiss this as a minor detail, arguing that the shared presence of the initials is the primary commonality.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Katchadourian (Carstairs) debate the details of three nearly identical murders involving victims Harry Westphal, Ada Grantsberry, and Richard Nigges. While Carstairs focuses on the similarity of the cufflinks placed in the victims' mouths, Poirot emphasizes the importance of differences, specifically noting that the window in Nigges's room was left open, unlike the closed windows in the other two crime scenes. Poirot argues that because the killer went to great lengths to arrange the bodies in identical positions, the open window is a significant inconsistency.

Carstairs proposes a sequence where the killer moved through rooms 317, 238, and 121 to facilitate a quick escape, but Poirot argues this timeline is impossible given the time constraints between the delivery of poisoned drinks at 7:15 and the discovery of the bodies. Poirot also notes the audacity of the killer, who left a note on the front desk stating, "May they rest in peace, 121, 238, 317," which was found by employee John Good at 8:20.

Lazari, the hotel manager, joins the discussion and insists that the hotel guests are of high moral character. Poirot orders that everyone present at the hotel on Thursday be gathered at Mr. Kitchbull's place to provide their whereabouts. Poirot also instructs Carstairs to investigate the victims' residences, the employees who booked their rooms, and the delivery details of their food and drinks. He further asks to check for a woman named Jeanne, though Lazari claims no such person works at the Bluxton Hotel. Later, Poirot manages to secure Room 238 alone to investigate, contemplating whether the killer escaped through the window.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot re-examines room 238 at the Brouks Hotel, where Richard Nigel’s corpse was found. Despite the room appearing tidy, Poirot feels an instinctive unease. He discovers a loose tile in the fireplace, which, when removed, reveals a hidden key labeled "238." Poirot concludes that the killer locked the door from the inside and escaped through the window, hiding the key behind the tile. He notes that while the killer took the keys to rooms 317 and 121, they left Nigel's key behind, suggesting Nigel was treated differently.

Captain Calver provides further details regarding the three victims: Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransberry, and Richard Nigel. All three traveled to London by train, and Nigel had pre-paid for their hotel rooms and separate car rentals from the station to the hotel. While Sipper and Gransberry both lived in Gleeholm in Pigeon Valley, Nigel lived in Bideford, Devon. Calver notes that Sipper arrived at the hotel two hours before Gransberry, and Nigel arrived an hour before Sipper.

Calver and Poirot theorize that Nigel may have been an unwitting accomplice who helped the killer bring the victims to the hotel without the women knowing they would be staying at the same location. Poirot suggests their next step is to investigate the victims' lives and secrets in Gleeholm. Additionally, Calver mentions that Henry Nigges, Richard's brother, is traveling from Devonshire to the hotel. Calver also notes a lingering mystery regarding a dinner order delivered to Nigel's room, as the person who ordered or delivered it remains unknown.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Pollock (Poirot) and Caderpillar (Ketteridge/Carter) decide to leave the Baskerville Hotel to change their environment. Although they initially consider walking in the hotel garden, Poirot decides they should take a bus through London to observe the scenery and potentially discover new ideas. During the bus ride, Poirot expresses his growing conviction that there is a connection between the Brownswood Hotel and Jean, despite Carter's skepticism.

Poirot argues that they should focus on the differences between the cases rather than the similarities. He highlights the peculiarities of the Richard Nigges case: the killer locked the door from the inside, hid the key behind a loose fireplace tile, and exited by climbing down a tree. Most significantly, Poirot notes that the gold cufflink in Nigges's mouth was positioned deep toward the throat, unlike the cufflinks in the other two cases, which were placed between the lips. Carter remains unconvinced, suggesting the slight difference in cufflink placement is not critical and that the key was likely hidden for convenience.

After a short ride, Poirot decides to return toward the Blaux Han Hotel to ensure they are not too far from their scheduled restaurant meeting. As they disembark, Poirot questions Carter about why he avoids looking at the three bodies in the hotel. Carter claims it is simply because he has already examined them thoroughly, but he internally reflects on a childhood memory of watching his grandfather die, which explains his reluctance to dwell on death.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Edward recalls the day his grandfather died, describing the experience of being forced by his mother to hold his grandfather's hand one last time before leaving the room.

Later, at the Brawnshan Hotel, Polo and Edward enter a lavish restaurant where Luca Lazari approaches them to apologize for misinterpreting messages and spreading rumors. A short, stout man named Raphael Bork admits to being the server responsible for the victims. Bork explains that at 7:15 PM on Thursday, he delivered "dinner-time tea" to Room 317 for Harriet Sipper, Edith Gainsborough, and Richard Nigges. The order consisted of tea, various sandwiches, a mixed cake set, and scones with jam and cream. Bork confirms that he did not deliver any sherry to Mr. Nigges.

Edward notes a discrepancy: while the tea cups in Harriet Sipple's room were consistent with what Bork delivered, a sherry glass was found in Richard Nigges' room despite no staff member admitting to delivering sherry. Lazari confirms the glass is a hotel glass, which is highly unusual.

Poirot questions Bork further about the victims' behavior. Bork describes the trio as "sneaky" and laughing, noting they were gossiping about an old woman and a young man—specifically, a story about a young man leaving an old woman to run off with someone else. Poirot observes that the three victims were friends who were unaware of their impending deaths. As Poirot reflects on this, a pale, black-haired young man at a nearby table jumps up with a terrified expression, appearing eager to speak.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
In the restaurant of the Brawnsham Hotel, Lazari introduces a new employee, Thomas Breenell. Breenell testifies that he met Richard Nigg at 7:30 p.m. near the dining room elevators. Nigg expressed concern for Mrs. Westphal and Miss Gransbury, asking Breenell to ensure their dinner bill was not charged to their accounts. Breenell notes that Nigg had just received a meal in Room 317 fifteen minutes prior, raising questions about why Nigg left his food to handle the bill.

Mr. Burbank addresses the crowd, questioning Nigg's movements and motives. A woman named Theresa Studd becomes distressed by the tension, but is calmed by a young woman and a promise from the narrator that she may return to her children soon. Poirot then presents a white piece of paper found by John Good at 8:10 p.m., which contains a killer's note: "May they rest in peace, 121, 238, 317." Despite the note, no witnesses come forward.

Later, the narrator and Polo retreat to a "secret lounge" behind the restaurant. Polo expresses anxiety regarding news of a cufflink with the initials "PIJ" found in Jenny's mouth. They discuss the inconsistencies regarding the murder victims. Rafael Bacchus had sent three teacups to Ada Gransbury's room, yet only two were found there; the third was found in Harriet Sippler's room with her body. Poirot questions the logistics of how the teacups were moved and notes that Richard Nigg was found in his room with a glass of sherry that no one had sent him, suggesting the murderer's methods were highly unusual.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Poirot and Cader discuss the possibility that the three victims argued before parting ways, though Poirot doubts a woman in an angry state would leave a half-drunk cup of tea behind. Thomas Brine, a young employee who previously saw Richard Nigges in an elevator, enters the room appearing highly distressed and sweating. Brine apologizes for failing to provide the full truth earlier, explaining that his social anxiety and a desire not to appear as though he was bragging about Nigges's praise prevented him from speaking up in the restaurant.

Brine reveals that after settling the bill, Nigges praised his efficiency and requested a glass of sherry. Brine poured the drink and gave it to Nigges, who then took it toward the elevator. Brine expresses deep regret for not mentioning the sherry when Poirot previously asked about it in the restaurant. While Cader believes Brine is being truthful and merely overwhelmed, Poirot argues that withholding information is a form of lying, as Brine failed to clear his name.

Following the encounter, Poirot decides to leave for "The Joyful Cafe" to clear his head. Cader expresses concern regarding the arrival of Henry Nigges, Richard's brother, but Poirot instructs Cader to invite Henry to the cafe to ensure his safety. Upon arriving at the cafe, Poirot encounters Phyllida Spence, who informs him that he must wait for a couple known as "Oh, Cecil" to vacate their table. She describes the husband, Cecil, as a submissive man constantly bossed around by his wife.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
At the Jolly Coffee Shop, Hercule Poirot speaks with Phyllida regarding the disappearance of Jean. Phyllida, who claims she has been thinking about Jean but cannot remember any specific actions or words that seemed unusual, expresses a cynical view toward worrying about others. Poirot remains focused on finding Jean, despite Phyllida's suggestion that his concerns might be imagined.

Later, the narrator brings Samuel Kid, a boiler maker, to the café. Kid is described as having a dirty appearance and a face that is only half-shaven. After initially mocking Poirot's accent, Kid recounts a story from the previous night. He claims that just after eight o'clock, he saw a terrified woman running from the Brooks Han Hotel. As she ran, she dropped two golden keys, which she later rushed back to retrieve. Kid notes that the keys had numbers on them.

Kid specifies that one key had a number in the one-hundreds and the other was in the three-hundreds. This detail prompts the narrator to recall that Richard Nigges' key, found by Poirot, was numbered 238, and notes that Harryte Sipper and Edith Gransberry live at addresses 121 and 317, respectively. When Poirot asks if the woman had light blonde hair, a brown hat, and a brown coat—matching Jenny's description—Kid clarifies that the woman had black, curly hair and wore a light blue hat. The narrator realizes that Kid may have nearly encountered the murderer.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
At the Joy Cafe, Samuel Kid tells Poirot that he recognized a mysterious woman who ran away, though he cannot identify her name or details beyond her cultured appearance. Following this, Inspector Stanley Bill arrives with Henry Nigges, the brother of the deceased Richard Nigges. 

Poirot questions Henry regarding the other murder victims, Harriet Seppel and Edith Gransberry. Henry reveals that he never met Edith, but knew of her through letters because she was once engaged to Richard. Henry explains that Richard was a lawyer in Pigeon Valley who lived in Glebeholme until 1913, when he broke off his engagement with Ada Gransberry and moved to Devonshire to live with Henry and his wife, Clara. 

Henry notes that Richard hated Glebeholme and became a reclusive, depressed man after moving, spending his time drinking alone and refusing social or religious engagement. He even rejected a Bible gifted by Henry's family. However, Henry observes that a few months before his death, Richard’s spirits seemed to lift; he improved his hygiene, stopped drinking, and appeared to be planning something with a new goal. Henry expresses a sense that Richard lived like a "dead man" for years and suggests that Richard may have even known he would be murdered.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Henry Nigges informs Poirot that his wife believes their village is cursed, a sentiment driven by Richard Nix's sudden desire to leave the village and break off his engagement. Henry reveals that a few months before leaving, Richard wrote a letter stating that the local young vicar and his wife died within hours of each other in a "terrible tragedy," though the specific cause was never explained.

While walking through London, Poirot and Kitcher discuss the potential connection between these past events and the recent triple murder at the Brooks Han Hotel. Poirot posits that the tragic deaths of the clergy couple sixteen years ago triggered a chain of events: Richard Nix's aversion to the church and the Bible, his decision to flee to Devonshire, his subsequent life of debauchery, and his eventual death alongside Ada Gransbury and Harriet Sipper. Poirot suggests that Richard's mental state had recently improved before he was found dead with an engraved cufflink in his mouth.

Kitcher argues that Richard's loss of faith might be a personal crisis unrelated to the priest's death, using a crossword puzzle analogy to suggest multiple plausible explanations exist for any given event. Poirot rejects this, insisting that the victims were real people rather than mere clues. He instructs Kitcher to travel to the village of Gleghorn to investigate the true identities of the victims and the cause of the clergy couple's deaths. Kitcher is also tasked with obtaining guest and staff lists from the Bluxton Hall Hotel to check for connections to the village, as well as investigating "Jane" and "PIJ."

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Desperate for assistance with a case at the Bluxton Hall Hotel, Katchup asks Poirot to help. They return to Mrs. Blanche Answorth's apartment, where Poirot conducts a methodical session of "sorting out thoughts." He has Katchup write down both known facts and unknown questions to provide clarity.

During their discussion, Poirot analyzes the timing of the murder at the Bruxelles Hotel. He notes that the three victims arrived on Wednesday and were killed on the second night. While Katchup suggests the killer might have been a meticulous planner worried about train delays, Poirot posits that the killer might have been a woman living in London who already had a connection to the victims, rather than someone traveling from out of town.

Katchup then presents a theory regarding the initials on the murder cufflinks. While Poirot previously questioned Henry Nigges about the initials "PIJ," Katchup argues that the engraving follows a common habit where the surname is placed in the middle, meaning the initials should be read as "PJI." Poirot dismisses the idea of inverting initials as ridiculous, but he instructs Katchup to travel to Gleghorn to investigate the "PJI" connection while Poirot remains in London.

Katchup travels to Glebeholme on Monday morning. After getting lost while looking for the King's Head Inn, Katchup is assisted by a friendly, freckled man with glasses. Despite the man's helpfulness, Katchup feels discomfort with the countryside and the impertinence of strangers' questions.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
After a man with glasses walks away, Mr. Kitcher (referred to variously as Cattermole and Kitcherpool) interacts with Victor Muggleton (also called Mick, McKin, or Mikkelsen), the landlord of the King's Head Inn. Initially, Muggleton asks many questions about London, but his demeanor shifts to cold contempt when Kitcher reveals he is a policeman from Scotland Yard.

Upon being shown to a simply decorated room, Kitcher decides to disclose the reason for his visit to Greyladies to satisfy the landlord's scrutiny: the murders of Lady Siphe (or Brawnshaw), Miss Gransberry, and Mr. Nigges at the Bluxton Hotel in London. Muggleton claims to know the victims but asserts they were neither friends nor enemies, merely people who "got into trouble." Kitcher notes a strange emphasis in Muggleton's words and senses suspicion in the landlord's eyes. Kitcher requests that Muggleton keep his identity a secret, but Muggleton dismissively tells him that no one in Gleghorn will provide valuable information and suggests that if Kitcher wants to ask questions, he should do so in London.

Confused by the sudden change in Muggleton's hospitality, Kitcher goes downstairs to the bar. He observes two inebriated men at a corner table: an elderly man with a white beard and a morose, muscular young man. The young man stumbles out of the inn, after which the elderly man points at Kitcher and insists he sit down. The old man begins singing a song about the "London policeman," revealing that Kitcher's profession is no longer a secret. Kitcher confronts Muggleton about the breach of confidence, but Muggleton denies saying anything.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
At the King's Head Inn, the narrator, Cutter, questions Mr. Mick about how a gentleman discovered Cutter's identity as a London policeman. Suspecting Mick is lying, Cutter moves to sit with an old man named Walter Stückri. Stückri, a man with a wine-soaked goat's beard and a garlic-scented nose, engages in a disjointed conversation about success, failure, and the cases of Harriet Sippele, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nix.

Through Stückri’s rambling, Cutter learns that Harriet Sippele's life was shaped by a "forgivable lie" and the loss of her husband, George, who died young of a disease. Stückri reveals that Harriet was approximately forty-two or forty-three years old and was deeply devoted to a wealthy man named Patrick. Cutter deduces that Harriet was twenty years older than a local "loafer" and that she chose the successful Patrick over a different path. Cutter attempts to confirm if Patrick's surname initials are PJI and if there is a woman named Jenny in Gleeholm, but Stückri's logic remains difficult to follow.

After leaving the inn, Cutter walks through a quiet, drizzly countryside, feeling the weight of the monotonous silence. He hears passersby mention "police" and "Harriet," leading him to wonder if Victor Mink has been spreading news of his presence in the village. While walking along an "S" shaped path, Cutter encounters a young man with glasses and freckles, whom he had previously met upon arriving by train.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
The narrator, Mr. Catchpole, encounters a young man with a distinctive "flying back" shape of freckles on his nose. The young man, who acts differently than he did previously, claims he knows nothing about the three murder victims and has never been to London. Catchpole attempts to ask about Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransberry, and Richard Niggles, but the young man refuses to talk, citing a fear that people do not want to become the next victims. This interaction causes Catchpole to reflect on a fourth cufflink mentioned by Bello, which he fears may be intended for a future victim.

After walking to the railway station, Catchpole realizes he has overlooked the recent murders of the local vicar and his wife. He returns to St. Peter's Church to investigate. At the cemetery, he discovers the tombstone of Patrick James Ife and his wife, Frances Maria Ife, both of whom died in 1913. Catchpole notes that the initials "PJI" on the tombstone match the silver buttons found in the mouths of the three victims at the Bluxton Arms Hotel, confirming a connection between the deaths.

While reading a poem engraved on the tombstone, Catchpole is interrupted by Margaret Ernst, a woman in her fifties who lives in a small house overlooking the cemetery. Margaret explains that she watches over the graves because people in the village have previously destroyed flowers placed there. She reveals that the poem on the tombstone was written by William Shakespeare. Catchpole reads the full poem, which discusses themes of gossip, slander, and virtue, and concludes that the text suggests Patrick and Frances were victims of defamation.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Margaret Ernst meets with Mr. Kachpur (referred to as Mr. Cattermole) at her cottage, which resembles a library filled with books and portraits of her late husband, Charles. Margaret explains that she and Charles took over the parish after the deaths of Patrick and Francis Yves. She notes that Patrick and Francis were kind people who lacked the means to engrave a full poem on their headstones, so she helped fund the epitaph. Margaret mentions that information regarding the parish was obtained from Dr. Flavoward, whom she describes as the only trustworthy person in Gleghowling.

During their conversation, Margaret reveals her defiant nature, stating that she will speak out even more if people try to silence her. She acknowledges the danger posed by the murderer of Harriet, Ada, and Richard, but asserts her willingness to talk to Kachpur regardless. Kachpur attempts to build rapport by mentioning Victor Mink, noting that Mink would be happy if Margaret ignored him. Margaret responds that she enjoys making Mink uncomfortable by rebelling against his wishes.

Kachpur asks about the history of Patrick and Francis Yves, but Margaret refuses to discuss them immediately, stating she needs time to think. Kachpur then reveals a crucial piece of evidence: gold cufflinks engraved with the initials "Patrick J. I." were found in the mouths of the three murder victims. Margaret does not seem surprised by this discovery. When Kachpur shifts the topic to the victims Harriet Cipper, Ada Grantsbury, and Richard Nix, Margaret provides extensive, detailed information about two of them without hesitation.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Margaret discusses the character of Harriet Spear with Mr. Cachupul (referred to as Mr. Cuthbert). Margaret explains that Harriet was once considered a gentle and selfless woman, but her character changed drastically after her husband, George, died of brain thrombosis in 1911. Margaret describes the current Harriet as a malicious woman who seeks out the perceived evils in others to satisfy a dark passion for condemnation. Harriet maintains a group of followers who agree with her attacks to avoid becoming her next target.

The conversation shifts to the other victims. Margaret describes Edith Gransbury as a hypocritical egomaniac who condemned others out of a sense of religious duty and fear of divine retribution, rather than for pleasure. In contrast, Margaret views Ada Gransbury as a principled woman who believed in redemption and saving sinners. Margaret notes that Ada had performed unknown harmful deeds, though she refuses to disclose details until the following day.

Regarding Richard Nigg, Margaret notes he was an influential figure in Gresholm who had previously canceled his engagement to Ada. According to the village doctor, Ambrose Flavard, Ada never spoke of Nigg after his departure, claiming she would devote herself to God. However, Mr. Cachupul reveals that Ada and Nigg were seen having tea together in a London hotel last Thursday, the same night Margaret was sitting alone looking at the cemetery. Margaret speculates that Harriet Spear was also at the hotel and suggests that the meeting between Ada and Nigg involved something significant enough to cross their established boundaries.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
At the "Happy Coffee House" in London, Polly attempts to persuade the waitress, Fay Spoon, to recall a forgotten memory regarding Jenny. Fay claims she cannot remember anything more and notes that a polite man had recently visited the shop with a policeman. During their conversation, Poirot observes that Fay believes a man had successfully imitated his voice, though Poirot maintains that every voice and rhythm is unique.

Raphael Buckle, a waiter from the Blaukenshahn Hotel, arrives at the coffee house. He explains that Luca Lazzari directed employees to find Poirot at this location. Buckle provides new recollections regarding the night of the crime, specifically a conversation between Harriet Seppel, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nix. Buckle recalls Mrs. Hepburn making eerie laughter and comments about a woman being "old enough to be his mother" and a man no longer trusting her. He also remembers Mr. Nigges disagreeing with the age comment and questioning if a certain man "has a brain."

After Buckle departs, Poirot returns to Blanche Onsworth's apartment while pondering these statements. Upon arrival, Blanche informs him that a ragged man named Samuel Kid is waiting in the sitting room. She notes that the man's face is covered in bruises, which he claimed were shaving cuts. Poirot, remembering meeting Kid previously when he had only a partially shaved beard, rushes to the parlor. He finds Samuel Kid, who has shaved his face to a state of disarray to deceive Blanche into letting him in.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Samuel Kid meets with Poirot to provide information regarding the murder at the Bluxton Hotel. Kid, who has facial injuries caused by his own shaking hands while using a razor, reveals that he recognized the woman who fled the hotel after dropping two keys. After seeing her picture in a newspaper, Kid identifies her as Nancy Drew, a well-known portrait painter.

The following day, Mr. Kitcher visits Margaret Ernst at her cottage near St. Gregory's churchyard. During their meeting, Kitcher presents Margaret with a list of guests and staff from the Blauxhan Hotel. Margaret agrees with Kid's identification, stating that Nancy Drew is the only person with the motive, ability, and means to have killed Harriet Sipper, Ada Granceberry, and Richard Nix. While Margaret finds it difficult to believe the well-mannered Nancy could commit such crimes, she notes that Nancy lived in Gleyholling until 1913, the same year Patric and Frances Ives died and Richard Nix left the village. Margaret dismisses Walter Sturridge as a suspect due to his physical infirmity, despite his connection to the deceased Frances Ives.

Kitcher requests that Margaret tell him the story of Patric and Frances Ives to help resolve the current mysteries. Margaret agrees to tell the story on the condition that Kitcher swears not to repeat any of it to anyone in the village until he leaves for London. She warns him that after hearing her account, he will likely want to find the doctor, Ambrose Flavard.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Mr. Kitcher, an investigator for Scotland Yard, questions Margaret regarding the history of the Ives couple. Margaret expresses fear and insists that Kitcher promise not to seek out Ambrose Flavard or mention Patrick and Francis Ives to anyone in the village. She explains that a tragedy in 1913 left "invisible wounds" on the residents of Glebeholme, including Nancy Duane and Richard Nigg, and that Ambrose is suffering as well. Reluctantly, Kitcher swears to keep her words secret, and Margaret begins to recount the history of the Ives family.

Sixteen years prior, a maid working for Reverend Patrick Ives spread a malicious rumor that Patrick was a fraud who used spiritualism to claim he could communicate with the deceased in order to swindle villagers out of money. The maid told only one person: Harriet Sipper, a bitter woman who enjoyed exposing the faults of others. Harriet escalated the lie, claiming Patrick had already defrauded several people. 

The rumor specifically targeted Nancy Duane, who had moved to the village in 1910 after marrying the schoolmaster William Duane. In January 1913, Harriet spread the claim that Nancy made secretive late-night visits to the vicar's house while his wife, Francis, was away. Despite the difficulty of verifying such claims in the dark, the villagers of Grellhollow condemned Patrick. This condemnation was supported by Ada Granceberry, a devout woman, and Richard Nix, a respected lawyer. Nix claimed to have personally witnessed a woman resembling Nancy entering and leaving the vicar's house during those times.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Richard Nix and Ada Granceberry, motivated by rumors of pagan behavior, conspired with Harriet Sipper to remove the parish vicar of Gregholm, Patrick Ives, from his position and expel him from the church. Patrick refused to confess, maintaining the rumors were unfounded. The village's hostility extended to Patrick's wife, Francis, who fell into a state of physical and mental collapse. Only Nancy Duane and Dr. Ambrose Flavord refused to participate in the persecution; Flavord publicly defended the couple, arguing that Francis's absence during the alleged incidents proved her innocence and accusing Harriet Sipper of spreading excessive slander.

Harriet eventually informed Victor Mickle of a secret Nancy Duane held, leading to a gathering at the King's Head Inn. Nancy Duane confessed that while Patrick did not pay her to communicate with her deceased husband, she had been meeting him secretly because she was in love with him. She maintained they had no physical contact, claiming their meetings were merely to discuss their impossible feelings. Harriet Sipper responded by vehemently condemning Patrick as a fraudulent, unfaithful pagan. Despite Nancy's attempt to stop the rumors through her confession, Ada Granceberry and Harriet led the villagers in continued condemnation.

Richard Nix became troubled by the lack of evidence and the villagers' tendency to slander the couple, causing tension in his relationship with Ada. The harassment culminated in tragedy when Francis Ives committed suicide by poisoning. Overwhelmed by guilt and suffering, Patrick Ives also took his own life. Following the deaths, Richard Nix broke his engagement and left Gleeholm, Nancy Duane moved to London, and the maid responsible for the initial rumors disappeared. New clergy, Charles and Margaret Ernst, arrived to take over the parish, hearing the tragic account from Dr. Flavord.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Margaret Ernst explains to Mr. Cattermole that she wants to engrave a poem on the tombstone of Patrick and Francis Eves to show support, despite Charles Ernst's concerns about angering villagers. Margaret discusses potential motives for murder, suggesting that Nancy Duane might have killed Richard Nigges because his withdrawal of support for Harriet Sipper and Ada Gransberry—following the lies spread by a maid—led to the villagers' hostility toward the Eves couple. When the maid is identified as Jenny Hobbs, Cattermole realizes, echoing Hercule Poirot, that Jenny may be in danger.

Margaret dismisses Walter Sturridge as a suspect, noting that the grieving father blames himself rather than others for the tragedy. She reveals that Walter, a former Cambridge scholar, acted as a guardian to Patrick and had arranged for Jenny Hobbs to supervise Patrick’s room when they were at the university. Walter blames himself for bringing Jenny to Grellingholme, which allowed her to spread the lies that led to the couple's deaths. Margaret also clarifies that the destruction of the Eves' gravestone was caused by local youths rather than Harriet Sipper.

Regarding the deaths, Margaret reveals that both Patrick and Francis died by poison from "love beans" (chicken pearl). While Margaret destroyed the suicide notes provided by Ambrose Flavard, she notes that the sequence of events can be determined from them. Margaret describes Jenny Hobbs as having been perceived as polite and harmless before she became ambitious and spread the rumors that incited the village against the Eves.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Following a discussion regarding Jeanne's sudden change in speech patterns—which Ambrose Flavard noted involved her imitating the polite tone of the well-educated Patrick and Frances Ives—the narrator, Mr. Cattermole, questions Margaret Ernst about her refusal to let him speak with Ambrose. Margaret explains that further questioning would only terrify the villagers, who already feel a contagious sense of guilt regarding their past persecution of the Ives.

The conversation shifts to theology and morality. Margaret expresses her resentment toward the hypocrisy of the devout, specifically criticizing Ada Gransebury for blaming Patrick Ives for sins rather than following church tenets regarding judgment. Margaret also defends the idea that true love can transcend religious doctrine, arguing that hate, rather than love, is the true driver of the killings. Despite her admiration for those who defy rules for love, she maintains her intolerance for murder.

After the meeting, Cattermole travels by train to London. He reflects on his decision to respect Margaret's wishes, believing that not forcing people to speak may eventually lead them to reveal information voluntarily. He also contemplates his professional relationship with Hercule Poirot, noting that while Scotland Yard officially credits him, the true credit for his investigations always goes to Poirot. During the journey, Cattermole has a vivid dream in which he is condemned for a crime he did not commit and sees his own gravestone marked with a cufflink engraved with his initials.

Meanwhile, Poirot and Inspector Stanley arrive at a villa in Belgravia to visit Nancy Drew. Upon their arrival, they are met by Nancy, a beautiful and fashionable woman approximately forty years old, who correctly anticipates the seriousness of their visit.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Poirot visits the home of artist Nancy Duane to investigate the murders of three guests at the Bluxton Hotel, which occurred the previous Thursday. The victims are identified as Harriet Sipper, Edith Gransbury, and Richard Nigg. Nancy, who claims to avoid newspapers to avoid misery, reveals that Harriet, Ada, and Richard were her former neighbors in the village of Gleeholm in Pigeon Valley before she moved to London in 1913. 

Nancy expresses intense hatred for the three victims, stating she had wished them dead many times, but she denies committing the murders. Despite her agitation, she allows Stanley Bill to conduct a search of her home. When questioned about her whereabouts during the time of the murders, Nancy claims she was at the home of her friend, Louis Wallace, from approximately 6:00 p.m. to 10:00 p.m. delivering a portrait and staying for dinner. She notes that Louis's husband, Saint John, is a snobbish artist who specializes in plants and fish.

Poirot informs Nancy that an eyewitness reported seeing her running out of the Bluxton Hotel at 8:00 p.m. on Thursday in an agitated state, during which she dropped two keys. Nancy dismisses this as a mistake and maintains her alibi. The conversation shifts when Poirot asks if she recognizes the initials PIJ or PJI from Gleeholm. Nancy reacts with visible shock and identifies the initials as belonging to Patrick James Ivy, a former clergyman.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
During an interrogation, Nancy Duane refuses to discuss the deaths of a priest and his wife, as well as Jennie Hobbs from Gleechol. She asks Poirot about the deaths of Harry, Edith, and Richard, who Poirot confirms were poisoned. Nancy mentions her late father, Algernon Johnson, and discusses an amateur painting of him that she keeps because he is smiling in it.

Police officer Stanley Bill interrupts to inform Poirot that two keys, engraved with room numbers 121 and 317 from the Bluxton Hotel, were found in the pocket of a blue fur-collared coat belonging to Mrs. Dunn. Poirot reveals that two murders occurred in those specific rooms. He notes that a witness saw Nancy dropping keys numbered 101 and 301 near the Blaxland Hotel on the night of the crime. Nancy claims she is being framed by someone who dislikes Harriet, Ada, and Richard. Poirot decides not to arrest her immediately, noting it would be illogical for a killer to keep such incriminating keys.

Poirot travels to the Wallace household to speak with Louisa Wallace. He observes the chaotic state of the house and the clumsiness of their new maid, Dorcas. Louisa shows Poirot a portrait painted by Nancy Duane. Regarding the night of the crime, Louisa states that Nancy arrived at six o'clock to deliver the painting and stayed until approximately ten o'clock. Louisa asserts that at 8:30, she, Nancy, and Saint John were all sitting together at the table, providing an alibi for Nancy.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
While visiting the home of Louisa Wallace, Hercule Poirot examines a portrait of Louisa painted by Nancy Duane. Louisa explains that Nancy and her husband, Saint John Wallace, are both talented artists with a competitive streak. Poirot observes that Nancy’s work is more expressive than Saint John’s delicate paintings, which decorate the house. During the visit, the maid, Dulcinites (also referred to as Dorcas), brings Poirot coffee, accidentally spilling some on her apron.

Poirot becomes preoccupied with a specific detail in Nancy’s portrait: a set of blue pots and bowls. He requests a tour of the house to view Saint John’s many works. While viewing a painting titled "Blue Field Bindweed: Euphorbia," Poirot realizes a connection that causes his heart to race. He eventually leads Louisa to the room where Nancy painted the portrait. There, Poirot notices a set of white pots and bowls with red, green, and white decorations on a wooden table. He asks Louisa about the blue pots and bowls depicted in the portrait.

Louisa realizes the blue items are missing and suggests that Dulcinites must have moved or broken them. She accuses the maid of theft, but Poirot defends Dulcinites' innocence. Poirot questions if Nancy Duane took the items when she left on Thursday evening, but Louisa dismisses this, noting Nancy left with nothing but a key. Despite the confusion regarding the missing items, Poirot asks to return to the portrait to continue his investigation.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Louisa Wallace and Hercule Poirot examine a painting in a drawing room, where Louisa expresses distress over the conspicuous blue pots and bowls depicted in the work, noting they were once in her house. Later, at his apartment, Poirot requests paper and pencils from Mrs. Blanche Oncleworth (referred to as Mrs. U.N. Owen). He prepares an envelope containing a drawing and a letter, instructing Mrs. Oncleworth to have Scotland Yard deliver it immediately to Inspector Stanley Bill regarding the murder at the Browns Hotel. While Mrs. Oncleworth is making the call, she receives news of another murder at the Ritz Hotel.

In a separate sequence, an unnamed narrator visits "The Happy Coffee House" in London and speaks with the waitress, Phyllis Spurling (nicknamed "Pompadour Head"). Phyllis discusses her desire to rename the establishment "The Happy Tea Room" and shares observations regarding Jenny Hobbs. Phyllis recalls a night when Miss Havisham visited; she explains that Jenny Hobbs had rushed to the window and stared at Phyllis's reflection in the glass, as if observing her. Phyllis felt that Jenny was watching her intently, as if Phyllis herself were the dangerous one. After the narrator visits Scotland Yard, they return to the apartment to find Poirot appearing uncharacteristically anxious and flushed as he prepares to leave. Mrs. Bunchworth is also acting strangely, appearing flushed and complaining about a delayed car.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Poirot and Cattermole prepare to leave for the Bluxton Hotel after being informed by Mr. Lazarus that a fourth murder has occurred at the Brawley's Hotel. Mrs. Brandonsworthy warns Cattermole to be careful, noting that three people have been killed at the Brawnsham Hotel in the past week and suggesting that guests move to less luxurious accommodations to stay alive.

During the car ride, Poirot expresses frustration over failing to prevent the fourth murder. He questions Cattermole regarding his investigation at Grellingsholme, specifically concerning Jenny and Margaret Ernst. Poirot suspects that Margaret Ernst delayed sharing information about Patrick and Francis Yve because she wanted to consult Dr. Ambrose Flavard, whom Cattermole admits she admired. Poirot argues that Margaret’s passion for the deceased Evans couple was actually a reflection of her love for Dr. Flavard. Cattermole also shares new autopsy results: the three previous victims—Harryette Sipper, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nix—all died of cyanide poisoning, but none had food in their stomachs, suggesting their afternoon tea disappeared. Poirot claims the mystery of the missing food is already solved.

Upon arriving at the Bluxton Hotel, they meet Henry Nigges, the younger brother of the victim Richard Nigges. Henry, who is unaware of the fourth murder, explains that he stayed at the Langham Hotel and has come to collect Richard's belongings. He avoids looking at the suitcase labeled "Mr. R. Negus" before departing, requesting to be kept informed of the investigation.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Following the third murder, the hotel manager Lazari appears visibly distressed and agitated. He leads Poirot and Kattarpillow to Room 402 on the fifth floor of the Browns Hotel to investigate the fourth murder. Unlike previous crime scenes, the victim, Jenny Hobbs, is missing. The room contains a large pool of blood and long bloodstains that suggest a body was dragged toward the door rather than the window.

In a corner of the room, Kattarpillow discovers Jenny Hobbs's deep brown hat, which contains a small piece of metal. Poirot identifies the object as a cufflink bearing the initials "PJI," an discovery that causes him significant emotional pain and self-reproach. While investigating the bloodstains, Poirot notes the width of the door, though Kattarpillow does not immediately grasp the significance of this detail.

Lazari recounts that Jenny Hobbs arrived at the hotel in a frantic state, appearing to be fleeing from someone. He personally escorted her to Room 402 after she demanded any available room. When Lazari returned with a master key thirty minutes later, he found the room in the state they currently observe. During the investigation, Kattarpillow looks out the window toward the hotel garden and observes the reception assistant, Thomas Brine, embracing a woman near a brown handcart. Poirot concludes the segment by questioning Lazari about whether any notes or room service requests were made regarding the fourth murder.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
After hotel staff confirm they have searched a room, Poirot discusses the description of Jenny Hobbs, noting she is approximately forty years old with light yellow hair, a brown hat, and a light brown coat. Poirot realizes this description matches a woman he met at the "Joyful Coffee Shop" the previous Thursday. He decides he must re-interview employees, including waiter Rafael Puccini, Thomas Brine, and John Good, to determine how many times they have seen Harriet Sippey, Richard Nigges, and Edith Gransbury. Carter informs Poirot that he saw a woman in a light brown coat in the hotel garden being held by Thomas Brine in a peculiar, limp posture.

Later, while Carter works on a crossword puzzle, Poirot challenges Carter's defense of Brine. Poirot suggests alternative suspects and methods for moving the body: waiter Rafael Barker might have used a laundry cart to transport the body through the grand hall, or Henry Nigges might have used a large suitcase. Poirot also notes that bloodstains indicate the body was dragged toward the door.

Poirot then recounts his findings from London, mentioning conversations with Rafael Borgia, Samuel Kid, Nancy Duane, and Louisa Wallace, though he withholds details regarding a maid named Dolores, a mysterious painting involving Stanley Bill, and the commonalities between the murder at the Bluxton Hotel and Jenny Hobbs' outburst at the cafe. The discussion is interrupted by Blanche Enderby, who announces that an anxious, tearful woman named Nancy Drew has arrived to see Poirot.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Nancy Duane visits Hercule Poirot to recount events from sixteen years ago at Glebeholme. She explains that Jenny Hobbs, motivated by jealousy over Patrick Ives's love for Nancy, fabricated a lie to defame him. Although Harriet Sipper eventually caught Jenny in the lie, Jenny fled without making amends. Nancy believes Jenny loved Patrick, noting that Jenny had previously left a lover in Cambridge to move to Gresham Holling with Patrick and Francis, even canceling a planned wedding to be with him.

Nancy discusses Richard Nigg, who later expressed deep regret for his role in the affair and his sympathy for Ada Gransbury. Poirot questions the morality of punishing people for their feelings and notes that the desire for revenge can lead to evil. Nancy describes Harriet Sipper as a former friend who transformed into a "monster" following the death of her husband, George. Nancy views Harriet as both a victim of her circumstances and a perpetrator of harm, noting that Harriet's actions were driven by a desire for revenge.

The conversation shifts to Nancy's personal history. Poirot identifies her father as Alcun Johnson, a notorious Anglo-Russian radical who believes in achieving a better world through bloodshed. Nancy contrasts her father's violent views with her late husband William Duane, whom she respected for his calm, conscientious, and decorous nature. Finally, Nancy confesses that her true passion was Patrick Ives, stating she would have given her life for him and that losing him helped her understand the profound pain Harriet felt after George's death.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Nancy Duane confesses to Hercule Poirot and Katchadourian that she betrayed her love, Patrick Eve. While she admitted to having a relationship with him, she lied to the villagers at the King's Head Inn by claiming their relationship was pure to avoid scandal and protect Francis. Nancy expresses deep guilt, stating her cowardice stained their love. She asserts that Patrick and Francis committed suicide, despite official records and doctors claiming they died in accidents. She believes Dr. Flavard, a man she considers good, falsified the accident reports to protect the reputations of Patrick and Francis from the stigma of suicide.

Following the conversation, Poirot and Katchadourian discuss the implications. They suspect Margaret Earnshaw may have tried to prevent Katchadourian from speaking to Dr. Flavard to protect the doctor's lie. Poirot notes a shift in the murders from poison to blood and questions why Jenny Hobbs's body was moved from the Bluxton Hotel. He also points out that Thomas Brineau is the only employee who saw Richard Niggles twice.

Katchadourian struggles to identify a killer, noting that while Nancy Duane is a suspect, she has an alibi. They consider motives for Henry Nigges, who might kill for money, but the connection to the other victims remains unclear. Katchadourian suggests investigating Margaret Earnshaw and Dr. Flavard as potential suspects motivated by revenge. Finally, Katchadourian reviews a list of couples to identify an "old woman and a young man" mentioned by Raphael Burbank, but finds no matches where the woman is significantly older than the man.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Poirot corrects Edward Carter’s reasoning regarding a couple mentioned by Walter Stuker and Rafael Burbank. Poirot explains that the descriptions provided by the witnesses do not match a single couple, but rather two different pairs of an old wife and a young husband. Poirot promises to take Carter to visit someone known to him the following morning.

The next day, Poirot and Carter arrive at 3 Jermyn Street. Although they expected to find the eyewitness Samuel Kid, they instead encounter Jane Hobbs, who appears terrified. Pollock, who accompanies them, reveals that Carter had misinterpreted a conversation with Walter Stukey. Carter believed Stukey said a woman "wouldn't like a child," but Stukey had actually said she "wouldn't like Semmy Kid" (Samuel Kid). This clarification reveals that Jane Hobbs was previously engaged to Samuel Kid. Poirot further suggests that Jane’s past sadness may have been caused by the death of her true love, Patrick Ivor, and that she had changed her manner of speaking to appear as his social equal.

During the visit, Jane Hobbs admits she knows Nancy Duane killed Harriet, Ada, and Richard at the Blaxland Hotel. She claims Samuel Kid witnessed Nancy Duane fleeing the hotel and dropping two keys. Poirot rejects this explanation, accusing Jane of lying and claiming the coincidence is too convenient to be true.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot confronts Jane Hobbs, accusing her and Samuel Kid of conspiring to frame Nancy Drew for the murders of Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransberry, and Richard Nigges at the Bluxton House Hotel. Poirot argues that the evidence against Nancy Drew is too convenient and illogical. He notes that the killer left a note at the front desk announcing the murders with the numbers "121, 238, 317," yet supposedly panicked and dropped two keys in front of Mr. Kid. Poirot points out that Nancy Drew had an alibi, having had dinner with Mrs. Louisa Wallace, and that the "panic" displayed by the supposed killer contradicts the calm manner required to leave a note at the desk.

Poirot identifies two major similarities between the hotel murders and a previous incident at the Browns Hotel involving a woman named Miss Jean. Psychologically, in both cases, the victims appeared to deserve death, with the perpetrator acting as an agent of justice.
