Factually, both crimes provided an excessive amount of information to the police, such as the motive for retribution and specific details like the cufflinks placed in the victims' mouths. 

Poirot reveals that Jane Hobbs targeted him at "The Happy Café" because she learned of his reputation as a meticulous former European police officer with connections to Scotland Yard. He asserts that her terrified demeanor at the café was a calculated performance designed to manipulate him into investigating the case and helping her frame Nancy Drew. Finally, Poirot questions how Jane could have known the specific, unusual detail that the killer would place cufflinks in the victims' mouths unless she was involved in the planning.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot confronts Jane Hobbs regarding her knowledge of the murders of Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nigg. Poirot notes that Jane previously claimed justice would only be served when she was dead, a statement made at 7:45, despite the bodies not being discovered at the Browns Hotel until 8:10. Overwhelmed by guilt, Jane confesses the truth behind the tragedy involving Patrick and Frances Ives.

Jane reveals that while serving Patrick at Jesus College, Cambridge, she fell in love with him. Driven by jealousy after overhearing Patrick's affection for Nancy Dunn, Jane fabricated a lie to Harriet Sipper, claiming Patrick's words to Nancy were actually intended for the late William Dunn. Harriet spread this rumor through the village, supported by Alice and Richard, leading to the social ruin of the Ives. Although Nancy attempted to defend Patrick by clarifying there was no physical affair, the village's condemnation led Frances to commit suicide, followed by Patrick. Jane also reveals that Dr. Ambrose Flavard burned the Ives' wills to protect their reputation from the villagers' zealotry.

Following Patrick's death, Jane moved to London with the help of Sam Kidd. Fifteen years later, she received a letter from Richard Nigg, who obtained her London address from Dr. Flavard. Richard visited her, asking for her help to rectify the mistakes of the past.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Richard proposes to Jenny that they seek redemption for their roles in the deaths of Patrick and Frances Ives by voluntarily choosing to be sentenced to death. Richard claims he has convinced Harriet Sipprell and Ada Gransbury to join this plan of self-sacrifice to achieve God's forgiveness. Although Jenny is initially skeptical, she meets Harriet and Ada two days later and finds that they have indeed changed, expressing remorse for their actions. The group agrees to act as judges, jury, and executioners, signing their own death warrants to correct their past mistakes.

The group's consensus is interrupted when Harriet demands that Nancy Duane also be killed. Harriet argues that Nancy is guilty because she seduced Patrick and caused the scandal that led to Francis's suicide. Harriet claims Nancy confessed to acting out of jealousy to hurt Francis. While Jenny defends Nancy and suspects Harriet is lying, Richard eventually agrees with Harriet, stating that Nancy's improper relationship and the resulting public scandal make her equally guilty. Richard concludes that they cannot sacrifice themselves while leaving a guilty person alive. Two weeks later, Richard meets Jenny alone to reveal his decision on how to proceed with their plan.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Jenny explains to Hercule Poirot the elaborate plan she and Richard Nigg devised to frame Nancy Duane for the deaths of several people. The group—consisting of Jenny, Richard, Harriet, and Ada—agreed to a series of mutual executions at a London hotel to make the deaths appear to be the work of a single killer. The plan involved Ada killing Harriet, Richard killing Ada, and Jenny killing Richard. To mislead the police, each murderer would place a cufflink engraved with the initial of Patrick Yew in the victim's mouth and arrange the scene to mimic previous deaths. They also planned to use blood from a stray cat and a note left by an accomplice, Semple, to stage Jenny's own death, making it appear as though Nancy killed all four of them for revenge. Semple was tasked with planting keys belonging to the victims in Nancy's possession to ensure her conviction.

Jenny reveals there were two versions of the plan: one known to Harriet and Ada, and a secret one known only to Richard and Jenny. While the others believed the goal was a collective execution, Jenny’s true motive was to survive to witness Nancy's hanging, after which Jenny would either commit suicide or kill Nancy if the execution failed. Richard had left the final decision regarding Nancy's fate to Jenny, believing her to be the most capable of the group. However, Jenny confesses to Poirot that she is paralyzed by a fear of death and the act of killing, which has complicated the execution of their scheme.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Jane Hobbs confesses to Hercule Poirot that she failed to participate in a planned "execution" at the Browns Hotel. Although she was part of a conspiracy involving Semmy and others to frame Nancy Duane, Jane became overwhelmed by fear and went to "The Happy Cafe" instead of meeting the group at six o'clock. She admits she feared Richard Nigges would kill her for her failure to appear.

Poirot realizes that the identical positioning of the three murder victims—Harriet Spear, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nigges—was a deliberate attempt by the conspirators to make multiple murders appear to be the work of a single, obsessive killer. The bodies were arranged upright between tables and chairs to mimic a ritualistic execution. However, Poirot deduces that Richard Nigges actually committed suicide. To maintain the illusion of a single killer and protect the plot to frame Nancy, Nigges hid a key behind fireplace tiles to simulate an escape through a window. The cufflinks in Nigges' mouth were positioned differently than the others because he died alone from poison, causing his body to convulse and the cufflink to slip. Mrs. Vane suggests Nigges had intended to end his life to escape guilt regardless of Jane's absence.

Poirot instructs Jane to stay home until he or Mr. Cattermole returns. Afterward, Stanley Bill informs Poirot that a doctor named Ambrose Flavard has gone to Scotland Yard to report that a woman named Margaret Ernst has been attacked and is dying. She has requested to see Mr. Cader before she passes.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
As Poirot and Carter hurry to catch a train, Carter expresses confusion regarding the attack on Margaret Ernst, questioning the motive if the previous suspects, Jenny Hobbs and Samuel Kid, were not the killers. Poirot dismisses the idea that the case is solved, noting that the story provided by Jenny Hobbs is a carefully crafted lie.

Poirot analyzes a previous "redemption plan" involving Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransbury, Richard Nigges, and Jenny Hobbs, where the participants intended to kill one another to atone for past sins. He uses a crossword puzzle clue regarding a six-letter word for "death" to reach a sudden realization about the truth. 

Poirot argues that Jenny Hobbs's version of events is factually impossible. He challenges the narrative that Richard Nigges committed suicide in a locked room after being persuaded by the others to take responsibility for the deaths of Patrick and Francis Ives. Poirot points out a logical inconsistency regarding the hotel keys: if Jenny Hobbs did not go to the Blaukshan Hotel as she claimed, it is unclear how Samuel Kid obtained the keys to rooms 121 and 317 to plant them in Nancy Duane's pocket. Furthermore, Poirot notes that if Nigges had committed suicide by poison, he could not have meticulously arranged his own body into a straight pose, suggesting the scene was staged by someone else.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
The narrator and Hercule Poirot discuss inconsistencies regarding food ordered by three victims, specifically why sandwiches, cakes, and scones were not eaten and why Ada Gransberry’s plate was empty. The narrator also proposes a theory regarding Samuel Kidd: that a wound on Kidd's face was not from shaving, but a scratch from a tree branch sustained while climbing into or out of Room 238. Poirot reveals he had already concluded that Kidd shaved a specific patch of stubble to disguise the wound and make it appear as a shaving accident.

The pair arrives at a vicarage, which has been converted into a makeshift hospital to treat Margaret Ernst. Dr. Ambrose Flavard explains that Margaret was attacked with a shovel by two men, Fred Krieger and Tobias Klatt, while she was attempting to stop them from desecrating the graves of the Ives. Dr. Flavard notes that the attack was triggered by the narrator's visit to Margaret, which alerted villagers to her support for the late Patrick Ives.

The narrator and Poirot visit Margaret in her bedroom, where her face is heavily bandaged and bruised. Despite her condition, Margaret remains defiant, stating she is too angry to die. Before she falls into a state where she must sleep, she indicates she has something to tell them, though she does not speak directly. Dr. Flavard asks the narrator if Margaret had almost revealed the truth regarding what happened to Patrick and Francis Ives in the village.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot confronts Dr. Ambrose Flavard regarding the deaths of Patrick and Francis Yves. While the coroner originally ruled their deaths an accidental poisoning, Flavard admits he lied sixteen years ago to protect their reputations. He reveals that the couple committed suicide using love-apple seed toxin (digitalis), which Francis had stolen from Flavard's medical cabinet. Flavard explains his motive for lying was to protect the couple from the harassment of villagers like Harriet Sipper and Ada Gransbury, whom he describes as "devils." He also confesses that his stubbornness in fighting these villagers contributed to the declining health and death of his friend, Margaret Ernst's late husband, Charles.

Margaret Ernst, who is currently ill, reveals she remembers the contents of the suicide notes. She notes that Patrick’s note contained a message for Nancy Duane, which Flavard had withheld to protect his lie. Margaret also reminds Flavard to disclose the threats made by Harriet Sipper, who had previously attempted to have the Yves couple's bodies exhumed from the churchyard on religious grounds.

Poirot decides not to expose Flavard's past lies to the authorities, allowing him to keep his medical license. Poirot also encourages Flavard to marry Margaret, noting their mutual love and their previous decision to remain apart out of guilt over Charles's death. After leaving the room, Poirot visits the graves of Patrick and Francis. He finds the headstone lying in the mud, having been disturbed by Fred Krieger and Tobias Clayton. Poirot draws a flower in the dirt as a symbolic gesture of remembrance.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
At a gravesite, Poirot cleans footprints off a tombstone and reflects on the desire for Patrick and Frances Ives to rest together. This prompts Carterpillar to contemplate the relationship between Patrick and Nancy Drew. Carterpillar shares a sudden idea with Poirot, which Poirot enthusiastically validates, claiming it makes all the pieces of the case fall into place. Poirot instructs Carterpillar to return to London to gather the personal belongings and luggage of Harry Westmacott and Adele Grant, noting that Mr. Nigges's belongings were taken by his brother, Richard Nigges.

The following day at 4:15 pm, Poirot and Carterpillar attend a gathering at the Blore House Hotel restaurant to hear the truth revealed. The staff, including John Good, Thomas Brien, and Raphael Pabk, are present. Lazarus is seen interacting with Inspector Stanley Bill, using the term "alphabet cufflinks murder case" to describe the situation. Various guests arrive, including Samuel Kid with Jenny Hobbs, and Henry Nigges. Nancy Duane arrives accompanied by Sir John Wallace, Lady Wallace, and their maid, Dorkas; she wears a scarf to hide her red eyes. 

During the gathering, Carterpillar observes Thomas Brine holding hands with a black-haired woman, which Poirot notes is a different woman from the blonde in the brown coat who previously kissed Brine in the garden. Carterpillar also spots Phyllis Spence, a waitress from the "Jolly Coffee Shop," disguised in a large hat at the back of the room. Poirot silences the crowd and prepares to reveal the truth of the investigation.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot addresses a crowd and calls upon Nancy Drew, a portrait painter, to assist in his storytelling. Nancy, wearing a scarf to cover her face, recounts her forbidden love for the late Patrick Evans. She describes how Patrick claimed to communicate with the deceased for financial gain and details her confession of their affair at the King's Head Inn. She mentions the "poisoned" deaths of Patrick and Francis Evans but omits the name of Jenny Hobbs. Poirot comforts Nancy by revealing that Patrick wrote her a letter of love before he died.

Poirot then calls upon Jenny Hobbs, Patrick’s former maid. Jenny confesses that her jealousy of Nancy led her to spread the rumors that ruined Patrick. She admits to conspiring with three accomplices—Harriet Sipper, Richard Nigg, and Ada Gransberry—to make amends for their actions. Jenny explains their plan was to frame Nancy Drew for the murders of the three accomplices. Henry Niggles testifies that his brother Richard is not the same man he once knew, leaving his involvement ambiguous.

Poirot concludes that there was no murderer; instead, the deaths were a series of suicides and killings permitted by the participants. He identifies Jenny Hobbs and Samuel Kid as criminals for conspiring to frame Nancy. Under questioning, Kid admits to placing a specific note at the Brooks Han hotel reception desk as instructed by Jenny. When Poirot questions how Kid obtained the room keys to plant on Nancy, Kid claims he borrowed a master key from an unnamed male hotel employee, but he is unable to identify the man.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
During an interrogation, Hercule Poirot challenges Samuel Kid and Jenny Hobbs regarding their conspiracy to murder Harriet Sipper, Adah Gransbury, and Richard Nigges. Kid claims he only entered room 238 and found two keys, noting a third was hidden behind a fireplace tile near Nigges's body. Poirot exposes a logical flaw in their plan: Jenny claimed she would meet him at the "Joyful Coffee House" at 7:30 p.m. to tell a prepared story, but the travel time from the Bluxton Hotel makes it impossible to commit murders and arrive by that time.

Samuel Kid attempts to defend the plan, stating the executions were originally scheduled for 6:00 p.m. to allow Jenny to arrive at the cafe by 7:30 p.m. However, Poirot questions why Kid was instructed to place a note at the hotel front desk at 8:00 p.m. if the murders were already complete. Poirot deduces that the conspirators ordered afternoon tea to Room 317 to create a false alibi, making it appear the victims were alive at 7:15 p.m. 

Poirot argues that the victims did not actually die between 7:15 p.m. and 8:10 p.m. He suggests the conspirators went to great lengths to frame the time of death during a window when Jenny had an unbreakable alibi—being with Poirot at the "Joyful Coffee House" from 7:35 p.m. to 7:50 p.m. Poirot concludes that the effort to prove Jenny's innocence during that specific timeframe suggests she is indeed the killer. Jane reacts by vehemently denying the murders and insisting the deaths occurred between 7:15 p.m. and 8:00 p.m.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals that Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nigges were already dead before 7:15 PM. He explains that the three murders were part of a conspiracy. When the waiter, Raphael Burbank, delivered afternoon tea to Room 317 at 7:15 PM, he believed the victims were alive because two accomplices were impersonating Harriet Sipper and Richard Nigges. However, the third person in the room, Edith Gransbury, was the actual corpse; she had been placed in a chair with her back to the door to hide the fact that she was dead and silent.

Poirot explains that the conspirators chose Room 317 instead of the more convenient Room 238 to avoid the risk of moving a corpse through the hotel corridors. To prevent suspicion regarding the uneaten food, the conspirators removed the tea and cakes from the scene. Poirot links the missing food to a homeless man seen earlier eating cake from a suitcase. He further deduces that the person impersonating Richard Nigges used a suitcase to transport the stolen food.

The impersonator attempted to create an alibi by discussing the bill with the receptionist, Thomas Brinell, to ensure Brinell would remember "Nigges" being alive at 7:30 PM. However, the killer made a critical error: the impersonator claimed Nigges was wealthy enough to pay for everything, unaware that Nigges actually had little income or savings, a fact known to his brother, Henry Nigges.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot explains to Ketaki, Luca Lazzari, and others that the man seen at the Blauxham hotel consuming tea and sherry was an imposter, not the real Richard Nigges. According to Poirot, the imposter checked into the hotel on Wednesday, observing the real Richard Nigges being checked in by Thomas Brinell. To mislead the police, the imposter later approached Brinell, claiming to be Nigges and asserting they had met before. Although Brinell possesses an excellent memory for faces and names, he was deceived by the imposter's confident manner and voice, leading him to believe he had simply forgotten the man's appearance.

Poirot reveals that the cyanide found in the sherry and tea was part of a staged crime scene designed to make it appear the murders occurred after 7:15 p.m. In reality, the victims—Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nigges—had already been poisoned earlier, likely by ingesting cyanide through water from the glass cups located by the sinks in their rooms. 

Poirot explains that Brinell remained silent during previous inquiries because he was confused by the discrepancy between the man he checked in and the man who later ordered sherry. Poirot concludes that the imposter was a skilled mimic. He directly accuses Samuel Kidder of being the fraud, claiming Kidder has impersonated him in the past. Phyllis Springle supports Poirot, testifying that she has heard Kidder mimic accents so accurately that she could not distinguish him from Poirot.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals that Samuel Kidder used a deceptive, unkempt appearance—including a half-shaved beard and stained clothing—to hide facial scratches sustained while climbing down from Richard Nigus's room window. Poirot clarifies that while Kidder helped the murderer, he did not kill Nigus. 

Poirot further identifies the three people present in Room 317 at 7:15 PM, when Rafael Bacard sent afternoon tea. The occupants were the deceased Edith Gransbury (who was posed on a chair to appear alive), Samuel Kidder (who was impersonating Richard Nigus), and the famous portrait painter Nancy Drew. Nancy had disguised herself as Harriet Sipper to gossip and avoid recognition by Raffles Burbach. Nancy admits that Sir John and Lady Wallace provided her with a false alibi, claiming she was not at the Bluxton Hotel on the night of the incident.

Poirot explains how he deduced the connection between the parties. He recognized the unique coffee served by a "clumsy" new maid, Dulcinites, as being from "The Happy Coffee House." He linked this to Jane Hobbs, who previously worked for Lady Wallace and frequently shopped at that coffee house. Poirot concludes that Lady Wallace attempted to deceive the investigation by presenting a portrait by Nancy Drew as a recent gift. He notes that the painting lacked Nancy's customary date and initials, "NAED," because Lady Wallace had erased the date to make the work appear newly completed to mislead the police.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals that Nancy Drew and Jeanne Hobbs were allies who conspired to frame Richard Nix. Poirot explains that he discovered a discrepancy in Nancy Drew's portrait of Lady Wallace. While the other colors in the painting reacted to light, a set of blue pots and bowls remained dull and monotonous. Poirot deduces that Nancy Drew, an experienced painter, hastily painted these items blue to hide their true color and cover up a specific motif: the emblem of Jesus College, Cambridge. 

Poirot connects this motif to Jeanne Hobbs, who previously worked as a maid for Patrick Ivory at the college. He suggests that the white jugs and bowls, which were a farewell gift from a former teacher named Walter Sturkey, were moved by Lady Wallace to a guest room. By painting them blue, Lady Wallace attempted to prevent Poirot from discovering the connection between her and Jeanne Hobbs. Poirot further accuses Mrs. Dunn of being involved in a conspiracy that resulted in three deaths, despite her claim that she acted only to save lives.

As Mrs. Dunn attempts to explain their motives to Jeanne Hobbs, Jeanne approaches her with a dark demeanor. Despite Samuel Kid's attempt to intervene, Jeanne Hobbs pulls a weapon from her handbag and stabs Mrs. Dunn through the heart. Following the murder, Jeanne Hobbs admits to the killing. The narrator reflects on the chilling nature of the crime and the meticulous way the killer handled the scene. Despite the narrator's desire to leave, Pollock insists they remain to properly handle the investigation.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
At Scotland Yard, the narrator sits with Jenny Hobbs while Samuel Kid is interrogated by Stanley Bill. Poirot reveals that Jenny Hobbs and Nancy Duane conspired, with Samuel Kid's help, to kill Harriet Westphal, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nix. During the conspiracy, Jenny pretended to be an accomplice to Richard Nix to manipulate him.

Poirot explains that Jenny’s motive was not jealousy, as she had previously claimed, but a selfless desire to protect Patrick Yves's reputation. To prevent gossip regarding Nancy Duane’s late-night visits to the vicarage, Jenny fabricated a rumor that Patrick was charging people to communicate with the deceased. She specifically targeted Harriet Westphal, who was obsessed with her late husband, George. Jenny suggested to Harriet that Patrick could facilitate communication with George for a fee, knowing Harriet would pay any amount.

Jenny admits she made this promise to Harriet without Patrick's consent to keep Harriet silent and protect the scandal from spreading. Although Patrick found the idea of pretending to channel spirits immoral and terrifying, he instructed Jenny to maintain the lie to avoid suspicion. He insisted that she tell Harriet his services were unavailable rather than admitting the communication was a fabrication.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
In a confrontation with Hercule Poirot, Jane Hobbs admits to lying about the circumstances surrounding the deaths of Harry Sipper, Harriet Sipper, and Edith Gransby. Jane explains that she previously lied to Hattie to protect Patrick Ives, who had refused Hattie's requests for unethical religious services. Jane also reveals that she sought advice from Nancy Drew, who advised her to remain in the background to avoid Hattie's defamation.

Poirot exposes Jane's elaborate fabrication: a plan where Edith Gransby killed Harriet Sipper, Richard Nigel killed Edith Gransby, and then Richard committed suicide. Poirot argues that Harriet and Edith would never have committed suicide and that the sequence was a manufactured story designed to frame them while protecting Jane and Nancy Drew from murder charges. He notes that Jane's plan was to admit to lesser wrongs to avoid being accused of conspiracy.

Poirot reveals the true motive and method: Jane lured Harriet Sipper to the Bluxton Hotel by claiming she could act as a medium to communicate with Harriet's late husband, George Sipper. This exploitation of Harriet's belief in spiritualism led to her death. Poirot deduced the truth after a clue from Mr. Cattermole regarding a word for "death" starting with the letter "D," which led him to suspect that the first murderer was not Adela Gransbury, but Jane Hobbs herself. Jane acknowledges Poirot's deduction, though she maintains that Richard Nigel was seeking his own death.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Poirot and Carter debate the linguistic interpretation of a sentence regarding a woman being "old enough to be his mother." Poirot argues that the word "now" implies a current state, whereas Carter suggests it refers to a change in the man's perception over time. Poirot clarifies the context of a previous argument between Samuel Kid (pretending to be Richard Nix) and Nancy Drew (pretending to be Harriet). The argument centered on the idea that when a person dies, their age is fixed. Therefore, if George Sipper died as a young man, his soul would remain that age, making his widow, who has aged, "old enough to be his mother" in a spiritual sense.

Poirot then reveals the mechanics of the murders at the Bluxton Hotel. He explains that Richard Nigges lured Ada Gransberry to the hotel by promising a reconciliation after sixteen years. This allowed the conspirators to prepare. Poirot concludes that Jean Hobbs killed Harriet Sipper and Richard Nigges killed Ada Gransberry, likely by slipping poison into water they drank. 

Jeanne confirms the conspiracy, stating that Richard Nigges was tormented by his past actions involving Patrick and Francis. Seeking atonement, Nigges proposed a plan where they would all die to pay for their sins, acting as an "executor" of justice. While Jeanne agreed that Harriet and Ida deserved to die, she did not want to die herself. However, she felt compelled to comply because Nigges had become a paranoid madman who would have killed her if she refused.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Poirot reveals that Jenny and Nancy Drew conspired to manipulate Richard Nix's suicide pact. Originally, Richard planned for himself, Harriet, Ada, and Jenny to die, with Richard killing the others before committing suicide. Following Nancy's advice, Jenny convinced Richard that she should be the one to kill him instead. To achieve this, Jenny lied to Richard, claiming Nancy was evil and had plotted to ruin Francis. 

Jenny and Nancy also enlisted Samuel Kid to assist with their plan. To avoid the danger of climbing out of a window in Room 238, Samuel helped them exit and impersonated Richard. While Richard believed Jenny would survive to frame Nancy for a murder at the Blaux Han Hotel, Nancy had prepared an alibi with the Wallace couple. Jenny explains her motive was survival; she believed Richard would kill her if she opposed him. She hoped the plan would result in only a short prison sentence for her and Nancy, after which she intended to marry Samuel. Poirot accuses Jenny of killing Harriet Sipper and Richard Nigges, but Jenny claims she killed Richard in self-defense.

In an epilogue set four days later at Mrs. Blanche Onsworth's house, Poirot visits Caderpillar. They discuss the Baskerville Hotel case and Poirot's methods. Poirot admits he deliberately misled Caderpillar regarding Jeanne Hobby's death and the bloodstains in Room 402 to force him to use his "grey cells." Poirot explains that the bloodstains were a deception designed to make investigators believe a body had been dragged, whereas a killer would have used a container like a suitcase or laundry cart. The conversation concludes with Poirot presenting Caderpillar with a letter from Dr. Ambrose Flavard and his wife.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bobby Jones, a 28-year-old Welshman, is playing golf with Dr. Thomas. During the game, Bobby struggles with his technique, hitting several poor shots, including one that lands in a sand trap and another that flies off to the right on the 16th hole. While searching for his ball, Bobby believes he hears someone shout, but Dr. Thomas hears nothing.

On the 17th hole, which requires hitting the ball over a chasm near a cliff's edge, Bobby hits his ball into the ravine. While walking along the ridge to investigate, Bobby discovers a dark shape about forty feet below. He and Dr. Thomas descend the rock face and find a man in his forties, unconscious with a broken spine. Dr. Thomas examines the man and determines he is dying and cannot be saved. The doctor leaves to find help, instructing Bobby to stay with the man, and predicts the man will die within twenty minutes.

As Bobby sits nearby, stunned by the situation, he observes the man's features: tanned skin, reddish-brown hair, and a strong build. Suddenly, the man opens his deep blue eyes and looks directly at Bobby. Before dying, the man speaks clearly, asking, "Why didn't they find Evans?" Immediately after speaking, the man dies.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After discovering a dead man, Bobbi uses a silk handkerchief to cover the corpse's face. While doing so, he finds a photograph of a beautiful young woman in the man's pocket; the image lingers intensely in his mind. Bobbi waits for a doctor to return, feeling anxious because he is late for his commitment to play the organ for the six o'clock evening prayer. He reflects on his father, Thomas Jones, a neurotic vicar whose digestive health suffers when he becomes flustered. Bobbi views his father with a mix of pity and frustration, feeling that their relationship is a cycle of misunderstood sacrifices.

A stranger named Basil Frenchie, a man of about thirty-five wearing golf pants, arrives on the narrow platform. Bobbi explains the situation and asks Frenchie to watch over the body so he can attend the service. Bobbi then climbs the steep path and sprints across the fields, jumping over a courtyard wall—an act observed with displeasure by the parish priest.

At the church, Bobbi plays Chopin's Funeral March on the organ. Following the service, Thomas Jones reprimands Bobbi for his lack of punctuality and responsibility. Bobbi attempts to lighten the mood by joking that he was "busy with a corpse" near the 17th hole tee box. He explains that the man fell off a cliff and died after the doctor left. Thomas is horrified by Bobbi's perceived apathy and lack of solemnity toward death. While Bobbi believes his father's reactions are due to the psychological effects of the war, Thomas feels ashamed of his son's perceived indifference. The two walk to the priest's residence, both internally making excuses for the other.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After visiting a friend who is opening an auto repair shop, Bobby catches the 11:30 train from Paddington Station to return home. He narrowly boards the first-class carriage just as it departs and discovers the only other passenger is a dark-skinned girl named Frankie (Miss Francis DeWinter). The two share a history; their families, the Joneses and the DeWinters, were childhood acquaintances, though they have grown apart due to social differences and the formality of the Jones family.

During their journey, Frankie expresses exhaustion with her life in the countryside and recounts a recent, underwhelming social outing involving the Savoy Hotel and various venues like "Puppets on Strings" and "The Bullring." The conversation shifts to a recent news report regarding a "Fatal Accident in Sea Fog." Frankie reveals she read about the man who fell off a cliff, whom Bobby and Thomas had discovered. The newspaper identifies the deceased as Alexander Prichard, a man who had recently returned from Siam. Bobby notes that Prichard was not dead when they found him but died fifteen minutes later.

As they discuss the upcoming inquest in Marigold, Bobby mentions his friend Budge Bidden. Through a series of shared childhood memories regarding Budge's stuttering and past failures—including a bankrupt chicken farm and a brief stint at a stock brokerage—Frankie realizes she knows him, confirming his history of being sent to Australia before returning to England.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Franky (Frances) and Bobby discuss Bobby's intention to invest in a business called Bajee, which is run by Baz. Bobby reveals he left the navy due to eye issues caused by intense light during overseas service, though he notes his condition is not severe enough to have prevented his continued service. Later, Franky agrees to pick up Marquis Bolt from Selhurst at 5:00 pm. During their conversation, Franky casually asks if anyone pushed a man down, suggesting the possibility of murder to make the situation more interesting.

The following day, an inquest is held regarding the death of Alexander Pritchard. Dr. Thomas testifies that the deceased had a pulse when found but died due to a broken spine after falling approximately fifty to sixty feet onto rocks. The doctor suggests the death was likely an accident caused by fog obscuring the cliff edge, though suicide remains a possibility. Robert Jones testifies that while playing golf, he heard a loud shout that sounded like someone being hit by a ball or stepping into thin air, but he found his ball about a hundred yards away from the path.

Amelia Kemm, Alexander’s sister, testifies that he had recently returned from the East and was planning a hiking trip in Wales. She describes him as cheerful and energetic, noting he did not seem to have financial difficulties. When questioned about his lack of luggage, she explains that he disliked backpacks and intended to send parcels; she mentions a parcel containing his overnight essentials was misaddressed to Derbyshire instead of Leicestershire.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At an inquest regarding the death of Alex Pritchard, Mrs. Kemann explains that she and her husband identified the deceased as her brother after seeing his name on a photograph. The coroner notes that Pritchard appeared to be in good health and spirits prior to his death, showing no signs of depression or suicidal intent. The jury concludes that the death was an unfortunate accident caused by the dangerous conditions of the sea cliff path and recommends that the town council install fencing or barriers.

Following the inquest, Bobbie returns to the parsonage, where he encounters the Kemmans in his father's study. Mr. Kemann is described as a burly, insincere man, while Mrs. Kemann appears significantly different from the woman in the old photographs, having lost her youthful, melancholy expression to heavy makeup and a coarse appearance. Mrs. Kemann asks Bobbie if Alex left any final messages, but Bobbie informs her that he did not. After the Kemmans depart, Bobbie reflects on how much Mrs. Kemann has changed, lamenting the "destructive consequences of marriage."

Bobbie is then approached by Frankie, who attended the hearing. Frankie expresses disappointment that the case was a straightforward accident rather than a murder. A disagreement arises when Bobbie defends the beauty of the woman in the photograph, while Frankie dismisses her, calling her a "painted whore." The tension subsides when Frankie suggests they play a game of golf, which Bobbie accepts.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While playing golf, Bobby suddenly remembers that the deceased man actually spoke before dying, contradicting what he previously told the Kemans. Bobby recalls the man's final words were, "Why didn't they find Evans?" Although his companion Frankie advises him that the comment is unimportant, Bobby feels a sense of guilt. That night, Robert Jones writes a letter to Mr. Leo Keiman to correct his earlier mistake. The following day, Leo Keiman replies, acknowledging the information but noting that his wife had hoped for a more significant message.

The next day, Bobby receives a letter from his friend Budge Bidden regarding a business venture. Budge has used money from his Aunt Carrie to purchase five non-roadworthy cars—one Austin, two Morris, and two Rover—intending to repair and sell them. Budge asks Bobby to help him start the business the following Monday. Bobby informs his father, a vicar, of this plan, but the vicar expresses disapproval, warning that wise people avoid such unreliable schemes.

On Wednesday, Bobby receives a second letter from a company called "Enriquez & Dolores" in Buenos Aires. The letter offers him a job with an extraordinary salary of one thousand pounds per year, noting a preference for ex-military men and implying an anonymous recommendation. Bobby is skeptical of the offer's legitimacy, calling the employers "lunatics." When his father expresses amazement at the high salary, Bobby explains he cannot accept the job because he has already committed to working with Budge. The vicar strongly opposes this, criticizing Budge as an unreliable person and dismissing the car business as nonsense.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bobbie decides to honor an agreement with an elderly man named Bajaj, despite the disapproval of a biased pastor. Consequently, Bobbie writes a letter to Enrixes & Dolos to decline a job offer, sacrificing a potential opportunity to avoid disappointing Bajaj.

Later, while playing golf, Bobbie discusses his decision with Frankie. Frankie supports his choice regarding Bajaj but warns him not to let the old man drag him down. During their conversation, Frankie notes that while she possesses significant family wealth, such as houses and servants, she personally owns very little. Frankie mentions she must go into town the following day to care for her father, whose gout is flaring up. She also suggests they might spend time together in London, but Bobbie expresses insecurity about his social standing and his upcoming work as a mechanic. This leads to a tense exchange where Frankie mocks Bobbie's inferiority complex. After a failed attempt by Bobbie to hit a ball out of a sand trap, Frankie ends the golf match and drives away in her green Bentley, leaving Bobbie feeling frustrated.

Over the next three days, Bobbie struggles with the pressure of his family life and his father's presence. On Saturday, seeking solitude, he takes sandwiches and a beer to a hillside covered in ferns. He falls asleep and wakes up at 3:30 pm, feeling a sense of liberation and grandiose ambition. As he falls back into a deep sleep, the narrative shifts to Frankie, who arrives at a large house marked "St. Assaf" in her Bentley, carrying lilies and being greeted by a nurse.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frances DeWinter visits Bobbie at a sanatorium, bringing lilies. Bobbie explains that he has become a sensation at the facility after surviving an overdose of eight morphine tablets. He reveals that the morphine was placed in a beer bottle, noting that the paper on the bottle's cap had not been sticking properly, suggesting someone spiked the drink while he was asleep. Analysis of the leftover beer confirmed it contained lethal amounts of the drug, though no source for the morphine was found at the bar.

Frankie proposes a theory connecting Bobbie’s poisoning to a previous incident where a man named Prichard was pushed off a cliff. She argues that Bobbie was not targeted for an inheritance or revenge, but because the perpetrator believed Bobbie witnessed the crime. Frankie suggests that even if Bobbie did not see the actual push, he might have noticed a distinguishing physical feature of the killer. She further links this to a strange job offer Bobbie received from a foreign company, theorizing that the perpetrators first tried to move him overseas before attempting to kill him. Bobbie remains skeptical of the connection, noting that another person, Thomas, was present at the cliff but was not poisoned. Despite his doubts, Bobbie admits the theory makes him feel important. Frankie concludes the visit by promising to return the following day.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Frankie leaves, a nurse arrives to bring Bobbie tea and remarks on how approachable Frankie is, despite her public image. Bobbie remains silent, disagreeing with the nurse's assessment. While reading the *Marblehead Gazette*, Bobbie becomes agitated and uses the call bell to demand that the nurse summon Frankie back immediately, citing an urgent matter.

When Frankie arrives, Bobbie shows her a blurred photo in the newspaper identified as Mrs. Amelia Kemm, the sister of the deceased. Bobbie realizes that this photo is not the same one he previously found and placed back into the deceased's pocket. This discovery leads them to suspect that a man named Baxton-French (also referred to as Baskerville-French or Barnett-French) switched the photos. Bobbie describes the man as a pleasant-voiced gentleman with no distinguishing features who claimed to be looking for a place to stay. Frankie suggests they verify this through the local real estate agents, Wheeler and Owen.

Frankie posits that if the death was a murder, Bobbie’s discovery of the original photo made him a target. However, Bobbie finds flaws in this theory, noting that he did not speak up immediately and that the timing of the photo's appearance in the newspaper was highly coincidental. Furthermore, Bobbie insists that when he returned the photo to the pocket, Baxton-French was not present; the man arrived several minutes later, and Bobbie could have heard him approaching from the only vantage point not obscured by the cliff. They conclude that there must be a different motive or a larger conspiracy involving the Kemm family.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frankie and Bobby discuss the murder of Prichard, concluding that the Kimmans were involved in a conspiracy to hide the true identity of the deceased, referred to as "X." Bobby recalls a strange question Prichard asked before his death: "Why didn't they look for Evans?" Bobby had previously shared this detail with the Kimmans in a letter, but they dismissed it. Shortly after, Bobby was targeted by a company attempting to buy him out and was subsequently drugged with morphine in his beer.

The pair deduces that X was deliberately pushed off a cliff, likely by "BF." To prevent X's identity from being revealed, Mrs. C placed her own photo in his pocket while removing a photo of an unknown woman. Mrs. C then falsely identified X as her brother who had returned from abroad. They conclude that X must be someone without close relatives who would report him missing, possibly someone who had recently traveled from abroad.

Frankie suggests three plans of action. First, she proposes using Bobby as bait to trap the killers, but Bobby refuses due to safety concerns. Second, they intend to investigate the name "Evans" to determine who the deceased intended to see. Third, Frankie plans to use her father’s knowledge of noble surnames to investigate the identity of Bassington-French, a man Bobby believes is innocent and likely using a legitimate reason, such as looking for a house, to remain near the scene without suspicion.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a discussion with Bobbie regarding their need for action, Frankie begins investigating the Baxton-French family. She questions her father, the Earl of Marchmont, who provides information regarding the family's branches in Hampshire, Shropshire, and Ireland. He notes that the Shropshire branch faces financial difficulties due to inheritance taxes, while the Hampshire branch is connected to a wealthy American heiress.

Frankie visits the real estate agency Wheeler and Owen to inquire about a man named Mr. Barcnton-French who was looking for property. Mr. Owen explains that the man visited on a Tuesday around 6:30 PM, the same day a man fell off a cliff. Mr. Barcnton-French reportedly stayed with the deceased until the police arrived. Although the man's behavior initially seems above suspicion, Frankie remains skeptical of his motives, questioning why he would make such a trip only to return to London the next day.

Frankie then visits Inspector Williams at the police station to gather information about the man who fell from the cliff, Prichard. The inspector clarifies that Prichard had only one photo on him—a picture of his sister—and that his pockets contained only a handkerchief, change, cigarettes, and treasury bills, contradicting rumors of spy materials or drugs. Frankie also inquires about the poisoning of the priest's son. The inspector reveals that a deep blue Talbot with license plate GG8282 was seen near the scene, which belongs to the bishop of St. Botolph's Church.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After confirming that the Bishop's car never left his garage, Frankie concludes that a false license plate was used. She attempts to research the Marquisbults' directory but finds it unhelpful, noting there are 482 individuals named Evans.

A week later, Bobby arrives in London to assist Budge with business matters. Bobby has been receiving illegible letters from Frankie instructing him to wait for her further directions. Due to a previous experience with morphine, Bobby is suspicious of food and drink and carries a military revolver. Frankie arrives in a Bentley accompanied by George Abathnot, whom she introduces as a doctor.

In Bobby's bedroom, Frankie reveals her plan to infiltrate Baskerville Hall in Stapleton, Devon, to investigate the Baskervilles. She intends to stage a car crash near the hall's gates. George will act as the witnessing doctor and insist that Frankie be taken into the house for medical observation due to a concussion. Frankie intends to use her status as the daughter of a countess to gain respect and access to the family. 

Frankie tells Bobby he must not participate in the immediate scheme but instead must grow a mustache—potentially a convincing theatrical one—and dress in a driver's uniform. She plans to have him drive a Bentley to Stapleton, reasoning that Baskingridge will not recognize him as a driver, especially since he previously only saw Bobby briefly and perceived him as a "golf-playing idiot."

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frankie, Bobbie, and George Abathnot plan to stage a fake car accident. To prepare, Frankie and Bobbie visit a garage where they meet Baz, a man with a small chin and strabismus. Frankie purchases a second-hand Standard car for ten pounds to use in the scheme. The group discusses the logistics of the plan, which involves Frankie appearing to have a concussion.

The group meets at a location about a mile from Stafeli village, near a sharp turn where the wall of the Merroway estate protrudes. The plan requires George to drive his car around the corner and wave a handkerchief to signal that the road is clear. Once Frankie waves her handkerchief from the road, Bobbie will release the brakes on the Standard, allowing it to roll down the hill and strike the wall. Bobbie intends to steer the car from the footboard before jumping clear.

During the execution, George signals that the coast is clear. Bobbie steers the accelerating car down the slope and jumps off just before it hits the wall. Frankie runs to the wreckage and sits inside to simulate an injury. George arrives and helps Frankie look more convincing by creasing her hat to simulate a concussion. A seventeen-year-old cyclist arrives at the scene and asks if Frankie is dead. George, posing as a doctor, instructs the young man to help him carry Frankie to Morrow House, the home of a magistrate named Mr. Baskingdon-French.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
George, a doctor, arrives at a residence with a young man carrying the limp body of Frankie (identified as Frances Downton) following a car accident. They are greeted by an older butler and Mrs. Baskin-French, a tall woman with red hair and light blue eyes. George places Frankie in a first-floor bedroom overlooking the garden. While the young man describes the accident to the household, George and Frankie whisper privately. George expresses concern that his professional reputation might be harmed if the true nature of the accident is revealed, but Frankie reassures him. George instructs Frankie to pretend she is a follower of Christian Science to avoid the need for further medical intervention.

George informs the house owner that Frankie has a mild concussion and needs rest. He identifies her as Miss Frances Downton, noting her connection to the DeReauks. George also claims he will handle the car repairs and manage her family notifications. 

During her stay, Frankie feels guilty for her deceptive behavior, especially as she develops a friendship with Mrs. Baskin-French. While the hostess appears calm and family-oriented, Frankie observes a hint of underlying worry in her eyes. Frankie also meets the owner, a friendly but absent-minded man, and his seven-year-old son, Tommy. Mrs. Baskin-French mentions that her brother-in-law, Roger, a charming but restless man who struggles to hold a job, is expected to visit the following day.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frankie learns from Mrs. Baskinton-French that Tommy recently suffered a fall from a swing when a rotten branch broke, though he has since recovered. Frankie observes Henry Baskinton-French during tea and dinner, noting his erratic behavior; he oscillates between nervous twitching and dazed states to sharp, sarcastic outbursts, followed by an unnaturally brilliant and rejuvenated demeanor at dinner. Frankie notices his peculiar, scary eyes and suspects he may be an addict, though she initially doubts his involvement in the murder because his brother was in Machabert when the crime occurred.

The following afternoon, Roger Baskin-French arrives for tea. He is a tall, thin man with deep blue eyes who brings a Hornby train for Tommy. Frankie’s impression of Roger as a likable man leads her to conclude that her and Bobby's initial theory—that Roger pushed Prichard off the cliff—is incorrect. However, she remains puzzled by the identity of the murderer and the source of the morphine placed in Bobby's beer.

The next day, while playing tennis, Roger confides in Frankie that he is certain Henry is using drugs, likely morphine, to treat insomnia. He notes that Henry’s pupils are as small as needle tips and observes that Henry becomes neurotic when his supply runs low, only to transform after receiving a six o'clock parcel. Roger admits he is sharing this information because he does not know how to face Sylvia. Frankie reflects on the possibility of a drug smuggling gang but remains wary, reminding herself that charming individuals can also be murderers.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Roger and Miss Frankie discuss Henry's morphine addiction and the possibility of him receiving treatment from Dr. Nicholson, a Canadian running an institution for mental illness and drug addiction nearby. They decide to keep the addiction a secret from Sylvia to avoid burdening her and to encourage Henry to seek help. During a conversation, Sylvia mentions the Nicholsons and expresses a strong dislike for Dr. Nicholson, despite noting his handsomeness. She also mentions Moira Nicholson, describing her as captivating.

Frankie decides to conduct experiments to see if Roger Baskin-French is responsible for the poisoning of Bobby, noting that Roger could have easily obtained morphine. Frankie's plan is to determine Roger's whereabouts on the 16th and to observe his reaction to a photograph of the deceased. At dinner, Frankie subtly asks Roger if he was in Claridge's on the 16th; Sylvia intervenes, stating Roger was home for a party, which leads Frankie to conclude Roger was not in Wales on the day of the poisoning.

Frankie then steers the conversation toward a man who fell off a cliff in Marchburt. Roger admits he was present at the scene until the police arrived but claims he left for London the morning after the tragedy. Frankie presents a newspaper clipping of the deceased to the group. Sylvia notes that the man in the photo resembles Allen Castles, a sentiment Roger partially agrees with. Despite her efforts, Frankie remains puzzled as the group's reactions appear natural.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frankie continues her investigation into the identity of the man who fell off the cliff with Machibault. Through a conversation with Sylvia Bassington-French, Frankie learns the man's name is Allen Castles, a Canadian naturalist, explorer, and big-game hunter. Sylvia mentions that Castles once traveled with a millionaire named John Savage. Frankie suspects Castles is the deceased man because he has no close relatives in the country and his disappearance would go unnoticed. She notes that the Rivingtons once brought Castles to lunch, providing a potential lead. Frankie also contemplates a theory involving a drug cartel and a person named Evans, wondering if Castles was killed while investigating them.

That evening, Dr. Nicholson and his wife, Moira, arrive for dinner. Frankie observes that Dr. Nicholson is a tall man with light blue eyes who drives a deep blue Talbot, noting the coincidence that Castles was also Canadian and drove a similar car. During dinner, Henry Basington-French appears subdued and anxious, while Mrs. Nicholson seems to watch her husband with a mixture of attention and potential fear. Dr. Nicholson engages Frankie in a cryptic conversation about "cultivating" bacteria in the countryside, which leaves her feeling unsettled. He then interrogates her regarding her car accident, using observations from a messenger boy named Rives to suggest that the doctor who assisted her must have turned his car around, implying an unusual route was taken. Frankie finds Dr. Nicholson's intense focus on details suspicious and perceives him as a cunning individual.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a discussion regarding Dr. Nicholson’s strong personality and his wife’s mournful appearance, Henry Basilton-French abruptly leaves the room in a state of agitation. He accuses the doctor of torturing patients by cutting off drug supplies to induce madness. Following this, Frankie and Roger discuss Dr. Nicholson’s interest in children and his upcoming absence from town.

In London, Bobby receives a letter from Frankie, delivered secretly by her maid. Frankie instructs him to travel to her, advising him to purchase chauffeur’s clothes from Harrods and grow a mustache to disguise his appearance. She tells him to bring a note from her father claiming his car is ready, and suggests he stay at the Stafly hotel because the local garage is full. Frankie urges him to gather intelligence on the suspicious Nicolson Sanatorium, noting that Dr. Nicholson drives a blue Talbot and showed unusual interest in her accident. She also claims to have discovered the real identity of a body.

Before leaving, Bobby reads a letter from his father, the vicar, which contains mundane news and a postscript about a mysterious visitor: a tall man with a slight hunchback and metal-framed spectacles who was looking for him. Suspecting this man may be an enemy, Bobby takes precautions. He leaves his Fiat in St. James Square, makes several phone calls, and then uses a Bentley to travel north toward Hampshire. He arrives at Merroway Manor, where a driver named Hawkins delivers the car and a note from Frankie instructing Bobby to stay at the Stafely Angler Arms Hotel.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bobby, disguised with a mustache, suit, and a voice change, successfully checks into the Angle Arms Inn. He adopts the persona of Edward Hawkins, the chauffeur for Miss Francis DeVenture. While at the inn, Bobby converses with the proprietor, Thomas Ashe, and other locals. Ashe reveals that a young man named Mr. Rives witnessed a recent car accident involving Miss Deveraux. 

The conversation shifts to Grantham, a local sanatorium run by an American doctor named Nicholson. The villagers express fear and suspicion regarding the facility, with a barmaid recounting a story about a wealthy young girl who attempted to escape, claiming she was being held against her will despite being diagnosed with delusions of persecution.

Seeking more information, Bobby walks toward the Grantham Sanatorium. He finds a small, unlocked door in the perimeter wall and slips inside. While walking through the grounds, he encounters a young woman who appears terrified and physically weak. She urges Bobby to leave immediately, claiming that "they" are coming and that no one can help her. As Bobby retreats, he hides in the bushes to avoid a man walking down the path. After the man passes, Bobby realizes that the woman he encountered is the same person from a mysterious missing photograph.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bobby Hawkins receives a phone call from Frances "Frankie" Davenport, who requests a car at ten o'clock to take her to London. After the call, Frankie speaks with Roger Baskerville-French, explaining that she must travel to London due to her father's fuss. Roger asks to ride into town with her, but Frankie declines, noting she will have Higgins with her and needs to shop. As Bobby drives Frankie away, Roger holds her hand and expresses concern about her safety.

Once in the car, Frankie asks Bobby if Roger might be in love with her, but Bobby shifts the conversation to a significant discovery: he found the person from the photo found in the dead man's pocket at Dr. Nicholson's private nursing home. Frankie describes Dr. Nicholson as an imposing, suspicious man who probed her about her recent accident during dinner. She suggests that Nicholson may be the head of a drug cartel, using his rehabilitation center as a cover to legally obtain and distribute drugs.

Frankie expresses a sudden intuition that Baskin-Fontaine might be innocent, despite Bobby's belief that the photo proves his guilt. They discuss the potential victim, Allen Castles, noting he was a nomadic individual who traveled to Stafford with a couple named Rivendale. They hypothesize that Castles may have been tracking Nicholson's gang or searching for the girl in the photo. Frankie reveals she used a fake letter from her father as an excuse to drive into town so she could speak with Bobby privately without being seen in Stavely's alleys.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After discussing the possibility of their garage being compromised, Frankie and Bobby decide to meet at the DeWinter city residence on Brook Street. Frankie reveals new information regarding the day Bobby was poisoned: while Barclay-French was in Stavely, Nicolson was not in London for a scheduled conference, and he drove a deep blue Talbot. Bobby notes that Nicolson could also obtain morphine, which aligns with their existing clues.

Frankie uses a phone book to search for the name "Rivington" and identifies Colonel Rivington, a Distinguished Service Cross recipient living on Tite Street, Chelsea, as a likely lead. To investigate, Frankie instructs Bobby to impersonate a new partner at a law firm. She provides him with a business card for "Spragg-Spragg-Jenkinson & Spragg, Esqs." of Bloomsbury Square, belonging to her father's lawyers. To complete the disguise, Bobby borrows a morning suit and striped trousers from the Marquess of Cheldon’s wardrobe.

Bobby travels to Tite Street and uses the fake business card to gain entry to the Rivington home. He meets Mrs. Rivington, who is easily swayed by his professional appearance and a fabricated claim that his office called ahead. Bobby pretends to represent their client, Mr. Allen Castles. Mrs. Rivington confirms that Castles is staying at the Savoy Hotel and mentions that the last time they saw him was at Stafholt, after which they visited the Bassington-Frenches. She also notes that Castles likely came to England because of a millionaire friend who died by suicide after being diagnosed with cancer.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bobby visits Mrs. Rivington to obtain Mr. Castles' address, using the pretext of handling his legal affairs and a fluctuating dollar exchange rate. During the conversation, Mrs. Rivington reveals that Castles, a Canadian, appeared downcast after visiting the Baskerville-French residence. She notes that he asked unusual and annoying questions regarding a nearby neighbor, a Dr. Nicolson and his wife. After leaving, Bobby reflects that his deception has likely ruined Dolly Martlew's reputation.

Bobby and Frankie later discuss the implications of Castles' behavior. Frankie suggests Castles' visit to the Baskerville-Frenches might have been a coincidence, while Bobby posits that Castles may have intentionally sought them out after overhearing information about their lunch plans. Following their discussion, Frankie returns to Stavely. Upon entering the study, she finds Dr. Nicolson holding Sylvia Baxton-French's hands. A distraught Sylvia rushes past Frankie in tears, stating that the doctor has just told her something terrible. Frankie observes that Dr. Nicolson is suppressing visible anger behind a polite facade. The doctor expresses a desire for Mrs. Baskin-French to persuade her husband to let him handle the situation.

Meanwhile, Bobby returns to his hotel, where he is directed to a sitting room to meet a visitor. He discovers the girl from the picture, dressed in black and visibly trembling with fear. The girl questions whether she should have come, expressing doubt about his ability to help. Bobby reassures her that she is safe and promises his assistance. The girl, noting his demeanor, realizes he is not merely a driver.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bobby meets with Moira Nicholson, who reveals that she is the wife of Dr. Nicholson. Terrified and trembling, Moira confesses her belief that her husband intends to murder her. She explains that she has experienced several "accidents" that she believes were intentional, such as being nearly run over by a vehicle and finding medications mixed up in bottles. Moira claims her husband's motive is his infatuation with Sylvia Bashington-French. She suspects Dr. Nicholson is arranging for Sylvia's husband, Henry Baxton-French, to be brought to Granchester Sanatorium as a patient. Moira believes a man named Jasper is manipulating Henry—who takes morphine—and his brother Roger to facilitate this move to the sanatorium, a place she describes as sinister where patients get worse rather than better.

Moira expresses doubt about Sylvia's true nature, wondering if the woman is merely acting. She also notes that Jasper is influencing Roger to help bring Henry to Grange. When Bobby asks if she knows Allen Castles, Moira admits she knew him before her marriage and that he visited her about a month ago without her providing his location. She has not told Castles about her fears or her unhappy marriage. Finally, Moira states that her husband is a very strange man who is extremely jealous of Bobby, viewing Moira as his property.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Moira Nicholson questions Bobbie Jones about his connection to the police. Bobbie, who is posing as a chauffeur for Francis DeWinter, explains that he needs to find Frankie as soon as possible. He instructs Moira to call Frankie and arrange a meeting at a pavilion by the river. Before leaving, Bobbie speaks with Mr. Eschew at an inn, making a casual comment about Mrs. Nicholson to avoid causing gossip that might reach Dr. Nicholson.

At the pavilion, Bobbie reveals his true identity to Moira: he is not a driver named Higgins, but Bobbie Jones from Wales. He informs her that her friend, Allen Castles, is dead, having fallen off a cliff in Marchbault on October 3rd. Bobbie notes that Allen had a photo of Moira in his pocket. Moira expresses sadness but does not reveal any prior knowledge of his death. Bobbie asks if she knows anyone named Evans, but she does not.

Frankie arrives, and the three meet to discuss the investigation. Frankie clarifies the complex situation: after an inquest involving the Kemman couple, Bobbie was targeted with eight morphine pills in his beer after refusing a job offer. The group deduces that Allen Castles was pushed off a cliff and identifies Roger Baskin-Fontaine as the prime suspect because he was at the scene and had taken Moira's photo. Frankie admits she staged a car accident to bring Bobbie to the area to investigate. Moira, fearing her husband Jasper is a suspect, is reassured by Bobbie that they had never heard of him before arriving. Frankie clarifies that their investigation is focused on Roger Baskin-Fontaine.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bobbie recounts to Moira Nicholson the circumstances surrounding a photograph taken by Roger Baskingdon-French, suggesting Roger may be involved in Allen Castles' death. Moira expresses disbelief that Roger would push Castles off a cliff, noting that the two men had only met once at lunch and lacked a motive. Moira becomes visibly distressed when Bobbie asks to share her own fears with Frankie, eventually fleeing the scene. Frankie follows Moira, calms her, and arranges a future meeting between the three of them.

Returning to Bobbie, Frankie notes two connections: she saw Nicholson holding Sylvia Baskin-French's hand followed by a glare from Nicholson, and she realizes Castles likely identified Moira's photo, which explains how he located her. Frankie and Bobbie speculate on whether Nicholson killed Castles due to a drug cartel connection or a personal motive involving Sylvia. Frankie decides to observe Roger closely to see if he can explain the photograph; if he is honest, he may become an ally in protecting Henry Baskingdon-French from being sent to Grange. The pair reflects on the confusing nature of the unfolding events and the mysterious, seemingly irrelevant role of a person named Evans.

Frankie later encounters Roger near the house. Roger informs her that Nicholson told Sylvia the truth about Henry and suggests that Henry needs treatment for drug addiction at Grange. Frankie then asks Roger directly if he ever took a photo from the pocket of the man who fell off the cliff. Roger reacts with slight annoyance and embarrassment but shows no signs of guilt or fear.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Roger admits to Frankie that he took and destroyed a photograph found in the pocket of a dead man to protect Moira Nicolson from public scrutiny, as the photo depicted her. Frankie reveals that the dead man was actually Allen Castles, a man Roger had seen at the location a week prior. Roger explains he did not recognize Castles because the man's face had been covered by a handkerchief.

Frankie attempts to convince Roger that Dr. Jasper Nicholson is a dangerous criminal, noting that Moira is terrified he is trying to kill her. She recounts the events surrounding the discovery of the body and the poisoning of Jones, though she omits the truth regarding a car accident. Roger remains skeptical of the evidence against Nicholson, arguing that the doctor's blue Talbot and the claims made by Mrs. Nicholson—regarding drugs, patients, and an affair with Sylvia—are insufficient proof of criminality. He suggests Moira's fears might stem from the unsettling environment of Granby.

Instead, Roger directs suspicion toward the Kayman couple, noting they made a false identification of the body and appeared desperate to know if the deceased had spoken. Frankie theorizes that Castles carried two photos: one of Mrs. Kayman for a "deal" and one of Mrs. Nicholson for "love." She proposes that Mr. Kayman pushed Castles off the cliff in the fog. Roger agrees that the Kaymans likely panicked when the photo was published, leading Mrs. Kayman to falsely identify the body as her brother and fake a parcel to support a hiking narrative.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frankie and Roger discuss their delayed pursuit of the Kemble couple, though Frankie secretly notes they had originally intended to pursue Roger himself. Their conversation shifts to Moira Nicolson, whom Frankie views as a terrified, helpless girl. Roger expresses a dislike for helpless people, suggesting that a person with courage would fight or kill to survive rather than remain passive. Frankie reflects on her own dislike of Moira and her slight displeasure with Bobby’s interest in her, noting Bobby's obsession with a specific photo.

The discussion turns to Henry's treatment. Roger initially expresses doubt about sending Henry to Granchester, noting that Henry's consent is necessary. However, Sylvia Baskerville-French approaches them, revealing that Dr. Nicholson told her the truth about Henry's addiction. Sylvia announces that Henry has already agreed to go to Granchester tomorrow to be under Dr. Nicholson's care. 

Roger and Frankie both protest the decision, with Roger arguing that Henry should seek treatment elsewhere to avoid staying close to home. Sylvia, however, has undergone a complete change of heart regarding Dr. Nicholson; despite her previous biases, she now views him as kind, generous, and intelligent. She becomes angry with Roger, accusing him of being inconsistent and accusing Frankie of wanting Henry far away from her. Frankie attempts to suggest that Dr. Nicholson might be a quack, but Sylvia remains a staunch supporter of the doctor. Roger eventually leaves the group to leave a message for Dr. Nicholson, leaving Sylvia confused by his sudden eagerness to contact him.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Roger and Frankie discuss the influence of Dr. Nicholson and the necessity of preventing Henry Bashington-French from going to Grange. During their conversation in the garden, they hear a gunshot coming from inside the house. They rush to the hallway, where they find a pale Sylvia Baskin-French. Finding the study door locked, Roger breaks the study window to gain entry. Inside, they discover Henry sprawled across his desk with a gunshot wound to his temple and a revolver on the floor.

Dr. Nicholson arrives shortly after the discovery. He examines the body and declares Henry dead, noting a piece of paper near Henry's elbow. The note, written by Henry, expresses his inability to fight a "deadly habit" and offers blessings to Sylvia and Tommy. Frankie finds the study key in Henry's outer coat pocket, which allows her to unlock the door from the hallway.

The following morning, Frankie meets with Bobby to discuss the event. While Frankie maintains that Henry committed suicide—noting that the door was locked and the window was shut—Bobby expresses suspicion. He suggests that Dr. Nicholson may have murdered Henry, forced him to write a suicide note, and then staged his arrival. Bobby points out that Nicholson was the one who "found" the key in Henry's pocket, suggesting the discovery could have been faked. Frankie counters that she was watching Nicholson closely and insists the key was definitely in the pocket.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bobby and Frankie discuss the possibility that Nicholson's death was not a suicide, despite Sylvia Baskington-French's testimony providing him with an alibi. Sylvia claimed that when the gunshot rang out, Nicholson arrived from the driveway and led her to the study window, meaning he could not have been the shooter. Bobby remains skeptical of alibis, but they decide to shift their investigation toward the Kemans, who reside at 17 Paddington St. Leonard's Garden. Frankie plans to visit them by pretending to be a political lobbyist to avoid being recognized.

The conversation turns to Moira, whom Bobby believes is in grave danger. He suggests that Nicholson had two obstacles to removing before marrying Sylvia, and with one gone, Moira is next. Bobby proposes moving Moira to a castle in Wales for her safety. Frankie agrees and plans to drive to Granchester at 10:30 to find Molly.

After saying goodbye to a pale and exhausted Roger at Merroway Manor, Frankie and Bobby drive to Granchester. Upon arriving at the oppressive, heavily bolted house, they are met by a nurse. Dr. Nicholson informs Frankie that Moira is in a very bad state and may not survive the night.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frankie visits Dr. Nicholson to invite his wife, Moira, to stay at her home. Dr. Nicholson informs Frankie that Moira left early that morning for London, possibly to stay at the Savoy Hotel, to seek stimulation. Frankie finds the doctor's polite, sarcastic demeanor suspicious.

After leaving the house, Frankie tells her driver, Bobby, that she believes Dr. Nicholson is lying and that Moira may have been gone since the previous night. Bobby expresses regret for letting Moira return home previously but argues that she must still be alive, suggesting she has been secretly taken somewhere or is still in Granch. To avoid suspicion in Merrow, Bobby instructs Frankie to return to London to trace the Kemans while he moves to Ambledon to use as a base of operations to find Moira.

In London, Frankie begins investigating the Kemans by visiting real estate agencies. At Gordon and Porter Real Estate Company, an agent informs her that Mr. Kemann rented 17 St. Leonard's Garden in Paddington for a quarter but has since departed, possibly to the Hilton Tower at Paddington Station. Frankie lies, claiming Kemann owes her money, to explain her interest. She then visits a second agency, where she successfully obtains a viewing permit and keys for 17 St. Leonard's Garden by claiming she wants to set up a women's boarding house. Upon inspecting the dismal, musty house, Frankie finds no personal belongings or traces of the Kemans, only an open "ABC Railway Guide" on a window seat. She concludes that the lack of evidence is a negative confirmation of their carefulness regarding any illegal activities.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After returning a key to a real estate agent, Franky travels by subway toward Piccadilly Square. While reading a newspaper, she notices a report about Sir John Milkingdon’s accident involving the Astrola, a yacht formerly owned by the late millionaire John Savage. The news mentions that the yacht's designer was killed and Savage committed suicide. Franky recalls previous mentions of Savage, specifically that Allen Castles was a friend of Savage and that Mrs. Rivenston believed Castles' return to England was linked to Savage's death. Suspecting that Savage's suicide might be connected to her current situation, Franky decides to investigate Savage's will.

Franky travels to the office of Mr. Spragg at the firm Spragg-Spragg-Jackson & Spragg, Esq. Mr. Spragg is an affable senior lawyer rumored to know the secrets of London's aristocracy. When Franky asks to view John Savage's will, Mr. Spragg expresses genuine surprise and warns her against getting involved in suspicious matters. He reveals that he has recently been the victim of a deliberate impersonation.

Franky admits to the impersonation, claiming it was a joke. To deflect blame, she implies that a young Duke was involved, which causes Mr. Spragg to soften his demeanor. During the conversation, Franky presents a letter from Mrs. Rivington. The letter states that Allen Castles mentioned traveling to Chipping Sodbury and expresses interest in the "Maltrevor's" case. Mr. Spragg expresses suspicion regarding the seriousness of the matter, specifically concerning the Maltrevor case or his client, Mr. Castles.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frankie meets with Mr. Sprague to discuss Mr. Castles, who had previously sought legal advice regarding Mr. Savage's will. Mr. Sprague explains that he advised against pursuing a lawsuit because Mr. Savage's distant relatives in Australia lack the funds to fight it. Mr. Sprague suggests that while the case involves potential undue influence by a woman, the legal reality makes a challenge difficult.

Mr. Sprague recounts that last November, Mr. Savage met a beautiful married woman, Mrs. Templeton, during a voyage from the United States to England. Under her influence, Mr. Savage began visiting her villa in Chipping Sutton frequently. Convinced he had cancer despite a distinguished expert's testimony to the contrary, a highly anxious Mr. Savage drafted a will through a renowned lawyer. The will left a £700,000 tax-free inheritance to Mrs. Templeton and the remainder to various charities. That same night, Mr. Savage committed suicide by swallowing trichloroacetate, leaving a note stating he wished to avoid a long, painful death.

A jury ruled the death a suicide caused by mental illness. Although Mr. Castles believed the will was out of character—noting that Mr. Savage typically preferred leaving money to blood relatives—the will was authenticated.
