Mr. Sprague notes that Mrs. Templeton has since left for the south of France and refuses to communicate. After the meeting, Frankie leaves, remarking that Mr. Sprague is "remarkable." Mr. Sprague is left pondering the identity of a "young duke" mentioned in conversation. Meanwhile, Bobbie worries about Moira's disappearance, considering the possibility that she is being held captive in a sanatorium.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bobbie believes that Dr. Nicolson has imprisoned Moira in a sanatorium and intends to stage her death as an accident, such as a fall from a cliff or a drowning. Believing he has a twenty-four-hour window to find her, Bobbie decides to abandon his identity as Mr. Hawkins. He travels to Amblerdorf, checks into a hotel under the alias George Parker, and rents a motorcycle.

At 10 pm, Bobbie rides the motorcycle to a secluded spot near Granchester. He hides the vehicle in a thicket and enters the Granville estate through an unlocked small door, carrying a gun for protection. While observing Dr. Nicolson through a ground-floor window, Bobbie notes the doctor's composed demeanor as he writes in a study. Bobbie then attempts to enter the house via a second-story window by climbing a tree, but a branch snaps, causing him to fall into a flower bush. Although Dr. Nicolson opens a window in response to the noise, Bobbie hides in the bushes. He overhears Dr. Nicolson speaking to a man with a coarse, hoarse voice who claims nothing is missing. As Bobbie attempts to approach the house again, he is struck from behind in the darkness and falls.

On Friday morning, Frankie arrives at the Amblesford station hotel to meet "George Parker" following a telegram regarding an inquest into the death of Henry Baskin-French. The hotel porter informs her that a Mr. Parker checked in on Tuesday evening and left his luggage behind, but has not returned.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After learning that Bobby left no message and only a telegram, Frankie drives toward Stafali in a state of turmoil, fearing Bobby has met with foul play rather than simply following a lead. Meanwhile, at a hotel, a man and a janitor speculate that a wealthy man has abandoned a woman he was meant to marry secretly.

At an inquest regarding a death ruled a suicide due to mental illness, Frankie and Roger testify. Dr. Nicholson presents a farewell letter as evidence, and the jury delivers a sympathetic verdict. Frankie notes a connection between this case and another suicide involving insanity. While the widow, Sylvia, retires to rest, Frankie informs Roger that Bobby is missing. Dr. Nicholson arrives, praising the coroner, Dr. Davidson, for the smooth proceedings. Roger confronts the doctor, asserting that his brother was murdered by someone who lured him into drug addiction. Frankie observes the doctor's reaction and begins to question the validity of his alibis, which rely on Sylvia's testimony. When Frankie informs Nicholson that her driver, Hawkins, has gone missing near Granchester, Nicholson dismisses her concerns as unreliable gossip.

Upon returning to the hall, Frankie finds a letter in Bobby's handwriting. In it, Bobby claims to have found a clue and instructs her to travel to Tudor Cottage in Chipping Sodbury by train rather than her conspicuous Bentley. He emphasizes that she must not tell anyone. Frankie realizes this aligns with her own discovery regarding Ross Emily Templeton. Although she feels relieved that Bobby is likely safe, she becomes suspicious of Roger after remembering Bobby’s heavy emphasis on secrecy, fearing Roger might have unwittingly betrayed them.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following Bobbie's advice to avoid using her conspicuous Bentley, Frankie travels via three train transfers to Chipping Sutcliffe. After arriving at the Chipping Sutton station in the rain, she follows directions from a letter she believes Bobbie wrote to find "Tudor Cottage." Upon reaching the cottage, she observes a figure in a tweed suit—whom she identifies as Bobbie—peek out of the door before retreating. Frankie enters the dark house, detects the scent of chloroform, and is ambushed from behind. An assailant presses a wet cloth over her mouth, causing her to lose consciousness.

Frankie wakes up tied on a hard floor in a dark attic containing an old iron bed, broken chairs, and a coal box. She discovers that Bobbie is also tied up in the corner with a gag. After Frankie helps him remove the gag, they realize they have been tricked. Bobbie reveals he never wrote the letter Frankie followed, and he explains he was drugged with food after arriving from Granch. Frankie recounts her journey and her encounter at the cottage. They realize that because Frankie kept the letter a secret from Roger, no one knows their location.

As they attempt to free themselves, Dr. Nicolson enters the attic. He mocks them for being easily tricked and examines their bindings. He appears satisfied with Bobbie's restraints but expresses dissatisfaction with Frankie's.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Nicholson ties Frankie to a damaged oak chair and reveals his plan to kill her and Bobby. He intends to stage a car accident on a disused road leading to a quarry, making it appear that Frankie and her driver crashed off a cliff. Nicholson notes that Bobby’s high morphine tolerance is a setback, but he plans to ensure they are found cold. Frankie attempts to bluff Nicholson by claiming she told Roger Baskington-French about his actions, but Nicholson dismisses the threat. He also reveals that his wife, Moira, is still alive, though her future is uncertain. Nicholson locks the door and leaves to perfect his plan.

While alone, Bobby and Frankie discuss the possibility that Sylvia was involved in Henry Baskington-French's death. Bobby then realizes that the man who just visited them was not Nicholson, but an imposter. Based on the appearance of the man's ears, Bobby concludes the imposter is Roger Baskington-French. Suddenly, a body falls through the skylight; it is Baxter.

To hide Baxter's presence, Bobby instructs him to throw one of Bobby's boots toward the broken glass under the skylight and hide under the bed. When the imposter enters, he sees the boot and the broken glass but assumes Bobby performed a "juggling act" to throw it. The imposter checks the ropes and leaves. Once the door is closed, Baxter emerges from under the bed and uses a folding knife to untie Frankie and Bobby.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bobby, Frankie, and Baz discuss the identity of "Nikolsen," concluding he is actually Roger Baskington-French in disguise. Baz notes that Baskington-French is a skilled actor and a dishonest man who once forged his father's signature. Frankie observes that a letter sent in Bobby's name was convincing, suggesting Baskington-French may have seen Bobby's handwriting through his involvement with the Kemans.

To confront the man, Bobby devises a plan: Frankie will sit in a chair to appear unsuspecting, while Bobby and Budge (also referred to as Barney/Bajee/Baj) will hide behind the door to ambush him. Budge explains his presence by recounting a story of debt and creditors that led him to follow Bobby's green Bentley to Stafili. Budge had previously seen the car parked outside a pub and, after a driver in a green uniform drove off, he ended up trapped in the garage at Tudor Cottage. Budge managed to escape the garage and climb onto the house's roof to reach the attic.

Bobby expresses gratitude to Budge, noting that his arrival saved them. As footsteps approach and candlelight appears under the door, the group prepares. When Baskington-French enters the room, Frankie remains slumped in a chair while Bobby and Budge pounce on him. The ambush is successful; the men knock him to the ground and bind him with rope. Bobby triumphantly addresses the captured Baskington-French, remarking that it is a lovely night for a funeral.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Roger Barnett-French is unmasked, he reveals his involvement in the recent events. He admits to forging a letter from Bobby and explains how he kidnapped Bobby at Grange by hitting him on the back of the neck with a sandbag after Bobby fell from a tree. Roger also confesses to using forgery to manipulate Moira. He admits he impersonated Dr. Nicholson to mock the group and to observe them, noting that Nicholson is actually innocent. When Frankie asks about the identity of "Evans," Roger refuses to answer, treating it as a secret.

Roger suggests they call the police and admits to kidnapping, though he claims he has no evidence for murder. Bobby retrieves a loaded gun from Roger's pocket, and the group—Bobby, Frankie, and Budge—leaves Roger tied in the attic, locking the door behind them. While searching the house, they find Moira drugged in a bedroom, accompanied by a tray containing a hypodermic syringe and an alcohol lamp.

Bobby successfully contacts the police and requests a doctor. A sergeant, an officer, and an elderly man arrive at the house. When the group leads them to the attic to confront Roger, they find the door unlocked and the room empty. Roger has escaped through a broken skylight, leaving behind a pile of cut ropes and a chair positioned under the opening. The police superintendent becomes skeptical, viewing the entire ordeal as a prank, while Bobby and Frankie struggle to explain the improbable disappearance.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After a doctor determines that Moira has been administered an opiate, he suggests she be sent to a nearby private sanatorium. Bobbie and Frankie agree and move to the Seven Stars Inn, as Inspector Hammond remains skeptical of their claims. During the night, Frankie informs Bobbie that she has evidence of being chloroformed, which she has hidden in a coal bucket.

The following morning, Bobbie informs Frankie that Inspector Hammond is treating the recent events as a prank. However, Bobbie remains determined to investigate the death of John Savage, suspecting either that his will is a forgery or that he was murdered. They suspect Roger Baskin-French is involved. Frankie notes that the witnesses to the will were the chef, Ross Chadley, and the gardener, Albert Merri, while the lawyer is from the firm Elford & Sprague.

Bobbie shows Frankie a photo of a Mr. Kemman found behind a phone. A waitress identifies the man in the photo as Mr. Templeton, the owner of Tudor Cottage, who recently moved abroad after inheriting a large fortune. Frankie and Bobbie decide to split up to investigate. Frankie visits the lawyer, Mr. Elford, using a ruse to ask about the Savage will. Elford insists the will is legitimate, as he drew it up while Savage was present and witnessed by the servants and gardener. Frankie concludes that forgery is unlikely. During their lunch meeting, Bobbie reveals that the gardener, Albert Merri, has died, though they speculate his death might have been natural due to his age.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frankie and Bobby visit Mrs. Pratt, formerly known as Ross Chaddley, a cook who previously lived with the Templeton couple. Mrs. Pratt resides in a rural cottage and recalls her time working for them. She clarifies that she stayed for two months, while the parlor maid, Gladyce, stayed for six months.

During the interview, Frankie questions Mrs. Pratt about the death of Mr. Savage, the man who left his fortune to Mrs. Templeton. Mrs. Pratt reveals that she and another worker, Albert, were called upstairs by Mrs. Templeton to witness Mr. Savage signing a document in the presence of a lawyer named Mr. Elford. Mrs. Pratt notes that Mr. Savage appeared very ill and pale. The following morning, Gladyce found Mr. Savage dead in his locked room with a letter addressed to the coroner on his bedside table. Mrs. Pratt mentions that Mrs. Templeton later helped her find a well-paying job in the north.

After leaving, Frankie and Bobby discuss the implications. Frankie suspects that the Templetons may have killed Mr. Savage to prevent him from changing his will, possibly by administering a sleeping agent, though they lack proof. Frankie questions why the household chose to use the cook and the gardener, Albert, as witnesses instead of the parlor maid. Bobby reveals that he has discovered the maid's name is Evans. They conclude that the decision to bypass Evans must be significant, as the lawyer Mr. Elford was present and likely would have preferred a more reliable witness, suggesting that Evans may have been too clever to use.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frankie experiences a sudden insight regarding the death of Mr. Savage. She theorizes that Basington-French impersonated Savage to sign a will before a lawyer and two witnesses who did not know the real man. Frankie suspects the real Savage was drugged and hidden in an attic for twelve hours, after which he was given triethylamine to ensure he would be found dead the next morning. To prove this, Frankie suggests authenticating the signature or questioning Ross Chaddle (Pratt), though she notes Pratt may have been chosen for her lack of intelligence.

To further their investigation, Bobby and Frankie seek information on Evans, a former maid at Tudor Cottage. They visit a post office where the postmistress reveals that Evans, formerly Gladys Evans, has married and moved to Wales, where she is now known as Mrs. Roberts. They discover her address is the Parsonage in Machabert, Wales. Realizing they had overlooked this information, they decide to travel home immediately. Frankie provides Bobby with money to settle Baz's debts, suggesting Frankie's father buy Baz's auto repair shop.

Driven by a sense of urgency and a hunch that something is happening, the pair drives to Medshaw Airport. Frankie arranges a plane through Donald Gold to reach their destination quickly. They arrive at the vicarage in Marchbanks as the sun sets. There, they encounter Moira, who appears distressed. Moira confirms she has also figured out the truth regarding Evans, but as Bobby invites her inside, she steps back.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Moira insists that Bobbi and Frankie meet her at an Eastern coffee shop to discuss a matter concerning her train journey to London. Once seated, Moira claims she is being followed by Baskin-French, whom she spotted with a red-haired woman. While Bobbi and Frankie search the street for him, Frankie remains suspicious. Upon returning to the table, Frankie intentionally spills coffee to create a distraction. She then collects a sample of the coffee in a vinegar bottle, revealing that Moira had poisoned the drink while the others were looking for Baskin-French. Frankie exposes Moira’s true identity, suggesting she is actually Mrs. Templeton. As Moira reacts with rage and fumbles in her handbag, Bobbi reacts by firing a gun, which misses Frankie and hits the wall.

Weeks later, Frankie receives a letter from South America. The author, a former lover of Moira, confesses to a conspiracy and reveals that Roger Baskington-French is dead. The letter explains that Moira had a criminal record and married Nicholson to evade the police and manipulate him into purchasing Granchester. While Moira continued drug trafficking under various aliases, the author worked to revive the declining Baskington-French family fortune. They orchestrated a plan involving a man named Kamen to lure Savage to Duddleswell. The author also details how they manipulated Henry into a morphine addiction to facilitate a planned overdose. The author admits that the plan was disrupted by Castles, who became suspicious of Savage’s death and the unusual wording of his will.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Roger Bashington-French writes a confession letter to Frankie, detailing his crimes and motives. He explains that Allen Castles began investigating him after recognizing Moira’s photo. To stop him, Roger pushed Castles off a cliff. To cover the crime, Roger stole Castles's identification and letters, replacing them with a photo of a gang member so the deceased would be identified as "Prichard." He also notes that Castles had attempted to contact a servant named Evans to investigate Moira's identity.

Roger admits to murdering Henry by shooting him in his study during the noise of a passing plane, then staging it as a suicide by planting a gun and a farewell letter. He also describes how Moira attempted to kill Castles by adding morphine to his beer, which failed. During a confrontation at a cottage, Moira injected herself with morphine to feign unconsciousness, allowing her to free Roger from his bonds in the attic. Roger reveals his plan to kill Frankie and Bobby Jones to secure the money he obtained through Moira.

Following the letter, Bobby Jones and Frankie discuss the case. Frankie explains she realized Moira was Templeton's wife based on how servants described her. Bobby notes that Roger's slip-ups regarding Castles's appearance helped expose him. The narrative concludes with Bobby and Frankie expressing their mutual affection and deciding to move to Kenya together, where Bobby will manage a coffee plantation. The vicar announces Bobby's engagement to Frankie to the local community.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Arthur Calgarth, a doctor, arrives at a stone jetty by a river at dusk after procrastinating through lunch and tea with friends at the "Red Wharf." He travels by hired car to the jetty to catch a ferry, intending to take another car to Dulmores once he crosses. While waiting, he feels a sense of unease and isolation.

Upon boarding the ferry, Calgarth interacts with an old ferryman. The encounter triggers a memory of a conversation from the previous day with a man whose evasive responses and suggestions regarding "fairness and justice" left Calgarth feeling angry and confused. The man had questioned if Calgarth was sure he wanted to proceed with a certain course of action, implying a potential cover-up involving a family.

After crossing the river, Calgarth asks the ferryman for directions to "Sunset Corner." The ferryman corrects him, noting that the resident, Mrs. Aigle, calls the house "Sun Angle," though locals still refer to the promontory as "Snake's Head." 

Calgarth walks through a residential area to a modern villa at the end of a road marked "Sunset Point." He observes that the house is unremarkable compared to the magnificent landscape of the river and surrounding peaks. He reflects on the Aigle family's choice of the property before pressing the doorbell. The door opens to reveal a young Irishwoman with jet-black curly hair and deep blue eyes, whose melancholy beauty gives her the appearance of a tragic figure.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Calgary arrives at the home of Mr. Ayrshire to seek a meeting, but he is met at the door by a hostile young woman, Hester Macquarrie. Hester informs him that her father does not see visitors without appointments and suggests he write a letter. Calgary attempts to engage her by mentioning her brother, Jack, which causes Hester to react emotionally, claiming that the situation regarding Jacqueline's death is over. To prove his legitimacy, Calgary presents a letter from Mr. Marshall in London. After seeing the envelope, Hester runs upstairs and returns to tell her father to let Calgary in.

Calgary is led through the house by Hester into a study, where he meets Leo Ayrshire (referred to as Leo Aggles/Agher). Leo is a thin, pale man who appears to possess a deep, quiet sense of contentment. Leo acknowledges that Calgary is there regarding Jack, whom the family calls Jacques. Leo expresses his belief that Jack is mentally unstable and should not be held responsible for his actions. Hester joins the conversation, briefly expressing frustration with Jack's past behavior before apologizing for her outburst.

When Leo asks if Calgary agrees that Jack's actions are the result of a brain issue, Calgary bluntly denies it. This denial shocks Leo and Hester. As Calgary struggles to explain the difficult truth, Hester insists on staying to hear the news. The passage concludes as an attractive young woman, whom Calgary had previously glimpsed leaving the room, re-enters the study carrying a briefcase.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Calgary, a geophysicist who recently returned from the Heseltine Expedition to Antarctica, meets with Leo Aggel, his daughter Hester, and Gwendolyn Vaughn to discuss the death of Leo's wife, Rachel. Calgary explains that his two-year absence due to the expedition has left him out of touch with recent legal matters, including the murder trial of Leo's son, Jack Aggel (also known as Jacqueline).

Calgary recounts the events of November 9th of the previous year. Jack had visited his mother to demand money, becoming rude and threatening before storming out. Later that evening, Rachel was found dead, struck by a poker that bore Jack's fingerprints. A sum of money was missing from her desk, and Jack was later arrested in Dreamead with the cash in his possession. During the trial, Jack claimed he had an alibi, stating he had hitched a ride in a dark sedan at 6:55 p.m., but the police could not corroborate this. Despite defense arguments regarding his unstable mental state, Jack was sentenced to life imprisonment and died of pneumonia six months later.

Leo confirms the facts of the case but expresses dissatisfaction with the legal system's failure to recognize Jack's mental instability under the McNodden Act. While the family maintains that Jack was guilty of the actions, Calgary shocks them by declaring that Jack Aggel is actually innocent. Leo responds by asking if Calgary believes Jack should not have been held responsible for his actions.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Calgary asserts that Jacques (identified in news reports as Jack Agar) did not kill Mrs. Aghael. Calgary explains that he was the driver of the car that picked up a hitchhiker on the night of the murder. 

Calgary recounts his movements on November 9th: after visiting an old nanny in Pergas, he drove toward Redmyn to visit Canon Pisemash. On the main road from Redmyn to Draymour, at approximately 7:05 p.m., he picked up a charming young man and drove him to the center of Dulmace. Calgary notes that since the police established the crime occurred between 7:00 and 7:30 p.m., his presence as the driver provides an alibi for the young man.

Calgary explains his failure to come forward sooner due to a concussion. After dropping the passenger, he was struck by a speeding truck while attempting to buy cigarettes. The accident caused memory loss; he could not remember the accident or his trip to London until he regained consciousness in the hospital. He subsequently traveled to Australia and Antarctica, unaware of the murder investigation.

A month prior, after returning to England, Calgary recognized the young man's face in old newspapers used for wrapping specimens. He remembered a conversation about eels, which triggered his recovered memories of the night. Calgary discovered that Jack Agar had been convicted and subsequently died of pneumonia in prison. Calgary has since presented the truth to the prosecutor through the defense lawyer, Mr. Marshall, to seek justice for the deceased man's memory.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Arthur Calgary visits the Agar family to deliver news regarding Jack Agar, who was convicted of a crime and died in prison. Calgary explains that he is a reputable witness and that medical evidence and details from Dulwich corroborate that Jack was innocent. He informs the family that justice can be achieved through a pardon from the Queen, which would allow the newspapers to report the facts and clear Jack's reputation.

The news causes significant distress among the family members. Gwendraith Vane eventually urges Calgary to leave so the family can process the shock. As Calgary prepares to depart, Miss Corston Lindström confronts him, criticizing him for forcing the family to relive their trauma and arguing that Jack was a bad man regardless of his innocence. At the door, Hester Agar reproaches Calgary, questioning the utility of justice for a dead man and stating that the news only harms the living.

After Calgary leaves for Draymour in a taxi, Hester retreats inside. Miss Lindström offers Hester brandy in her sitting room, where they discuss the suddenness of the news. While Hester acknowledges Calgary's courage in delivering the news, Miss Lindström remains skeptical and suggests there are possibilities Calgary has overlooked. Hester then goes to the study, where Leo Agar is attempting to contact Mary and Mike Darrington. Leo informs them that Dr. Calgary brought a letter from Andrew Marshall revealing that Jacqueline's account regarding her whereabouts was true.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hester Aggles calls Dr. Donald Craig to cancel their plans for a lecture, explaining that her family has received complicated news. She informs him that they have discovered her brother did not kill their mother, but she insists he keep the information secret and asks him not to visit. While Leo Aggles views the exoneration of Jacqueline as happy news, Hester finds it distressing and believes it will only cause further annoyance. Gwendolyn Vance then calls Mick, who reacts to the news of a missing witness by suggesting they meet with Marshall to seek advice. Mick jokingly wonders if one of them is the culprit, a comment that Leo finds potentially serious.

In a separate location, Mary Darland, a composed and tidy woman, attends to her husband, Philip, who suffers from muscle atrophy due to polio. Philip discusses the news regarding Jacqueline's exoneration, noting that the Marshall Law Firm is practical and likely to accept the findings. Mary expresses discomfort with the social scrutiny and the "dreadful" nature of the situation, though she notes that a certain person is dead and the matter is over. Philip observes that Mary's discreet handling of the situation was perfect. As Mary tends to Philip's physical discomfort, the phone rings; she answers it and informs Philip that the caller is Micky.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Following a phone call from her father, Mary discusses her agitation with Philip. Her father had expressed anger and warned of consequences regarding a recent situation involving the dropping of charges against Jackie. Philip explains that the situation could turn the matter back into a "cold case," potentially drawing police interest. Despite Mary's desire to avoid gossip and the discomfort of revisiting the past, Philip insists they must meet with Marshall at "the sunny corner" to discuss their situation. Mary resists the idea due to Philip's leg problems and recent break-ins at their house, but Philip maintains that the family needs to face their circumstances.

Meanwhile, at a hotel in Drearymoor, Arthur Calgary reflects on a shaken experience at the Sunny Corner. He ruminates on the unexpected and painful reactions of several people, including Hester’s contemptuous rejection of his plea for fairness, the harshness of a woman named Corston, Leo Aigle’s strange lack of reaction to his son's innocence, and the overly sympathetic behavior of Leo’s secretary. 

Calgary is interrupted by a phone call from a Mr. Aikman, but the visitor who arrives at his room is actually Michael Aigle, Leo's son. Michael, appearing bitter and resentful, tracked Calgary down by calling various hotels. Michael questions Calgary’s strength and his involvement in the Hayes-Bentley Antarctic Expedition. He reveals his motive for visiting: he is reacting to news regarding his brother. Michael challenges the accuracy of the alibi Calgary provided for Jacques, suggesting Calgary might have been mistaken about the time. Calgary firmly defends his precision, stating that his watch and the car clock were synchronized when he picked up Jacques at 6:35 and dropped him off at Dreyfus at 7:35.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a confrontation, Micky reveals to Calgary and Mike that he, Mary, Tina, Hester, and Jacques are all adopted children. Micky explains that their mother built a family through adoption because she could not have biological children, but family dynamics soured when she chose Jacques as her favorite. Micky expresses resentment toward Jacques, refusing to acknowledge him as a brother, and argues that proving Jacques's innocence has not brought relief to the family, but rather new complications.

Later, Dr. Arthur Calgary meets with a lawyer named Mr. Marshall to discuss the consequences of his actions. Calgary explains that his attempt to clear Jack Ackerley's name has not provided closure but has instead opened a "new chapter" of worry and fear for the family. Calgary feels a sense of responsibility for the situation, noting that his involvement in the case began nearly two years ago when he gave a young man a ride.

Marshall explains the family's unease: because the murder occurred in a locked house with the blinds down, the killer must have been someone known to the deceased. Marshall suggests the family would have preferred Jack Ackerley to remain the convicted murderer because his reputation as a delinquent provided a simple, albeit tragic, resolution. Now that Jack has received a posthumous pardon, the case will be retried. Marshall expresses skepticism that the police will find new concrete evidence, though he suspects they may already have a hunch about the true killer. Calgary concludes the conversation by mentioning that Hester Ager said something regarding the situation.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Arthur Calgary discusses the implications of Hester's words with Mr. Marshall, concluding that if a member of the family is guilty, the resulting suspicion will destroy the family's trust and relationships forever. Calgary expresses a motive to continue his investigation because he feels his previous actions failed to provide true justice for both the guilty and the innocent.

Marshall provides historical background on the family, explaining that Rachel Constable, the daughter of millionaire Rudolph Constable, and her husband Leo Constable established a wartime nursery at a house called Sun Angle (formerly Snake Angle) in 1940. Because Rachel could not have children, she adopted children, eventually forming a family of five: Mary, Michael, Tina, Hester, and Jack Agglethorpe. While the children were well-educated and provided for, Jack was unruly, frequently stole money, and failed in multiple business ventures. Marshall reveals that Jack was secretly married to a young dancer from Dartmouth, a fact unknown to the rest of the family; she remarried an electrician shortly after Jack's death.

Regarding the night Mrs. Aikman was killed, Marshall states that Leo Aikman, Hester, Mary Dalton, and her disabled husband were present, along with Kirsten Lindström, a Swedish nurse. Michael and Tina were absent, and Mr. Aggert's secretary, Miss Vaughn, had left before the body was discovered. Calgary notes that Miss Vaughn and Mr. Aggert were reportedly planning an engagement. When Calgary asks about a motive, Marshall explains that no one stands to gain financially from the death because Mrs. Aigle set up discretionary trusts for the children, managed by three trustees: Marshall, Leo Aigle, and an American lawyer.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Calgary questions Marshall regarding the financial motives behind Mrs. Aigle's death. Marshall explains that most of her property is held in trust, leaving little for her husband, Mr. Aigle, though she had purchased a substantial annuity insurance policy for Miss Lindström. Marshall suggests that Dr. McMaster, a retired local doctor and former wartime nursery health officer, might possess knowledge regarding the personal lives of the residents in Sunny Corner.

In a separate meeting, Colonel Finney, the Police Commissioner, and Chief Inspector Hueston discuss the necessity of reopening an investigation into a murder that occurred two years prior. They acknowledge a mistake was made in the original case involving Jackie Aggar, who was convicted based on fingerprints on a poker and money, and a reported threat against the victim. Although Aggar was considered a "bad character," it is now known he was not the murderer. The investigators note that the killer likely entered the house or was someone Mrs. Aigle trusted, given the high security of the Aigle residence.

The officials struggle to identify a motive, as Mrs. Aigle was a generous woman whose estate was structured to minimize inheritance tax. However, they consider new possibilities: Mr. Aigle is planning to marry his long-time secretary, Miss Gwendolyn Vane, suggesting a potential motive if a relationship existed or if he desired the marriage. Additionally, they consider the Swedish housekeeper, noting she might have harbored secret resentment toward Mrs. Aigle, despite having no financial incentive to kill her due to a life insurance policy provided by the deceased.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Huishe and the police chief discuss the investigation into the death of Mrs. Agglesden. Huishe notes that the crime scene appeared to be an amateurish attempt at a burglary, possibly staged by Jack Agglesden to divert suspicion. A perplexing detail involves a five-pound note found on Jack, which bore the name of Mrs. Bottley on the back; while Jack claimed it was from his mother, Mr. Agglesden and Gwendolyn Vane asserted that Mrs. Agglesden had refused to give him money that morning. Huishe proposes a theory that an unknown person ("X") overheard an argument, took the money, and tricked Jack into a trap.

The police chief reviews the potential suspects present in the house that night: Jack Agglesden, Gwendolyn Vane, Hester Aigle, the Lindstrom woman, Leo Aigle, and Mary Darlant. Based on a timetable, the police identify a window of opportunity between 6:58 PM and 7:30 PM. They also consider Michael, a brother and automobile salesman, who possessed a key and was previously uninvestigated. Huishe expresses a personal motive to solve the case because he believes Mrs. Agglesden was a good woman.

In a separate setting, Arthur Calhoun (using the name Calgary) observes Moira Craig, the widow of Jacqueline, at a cinema. The following morning, Calhoun visits Moira at her home. Moira discusses the past incident involving Jack, noting that Jack died of pneumonia in prison. She reveals that she had been considering divorcing Jack because of his imprisonment and his lack of stability.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Arthur Calgary interviews Mollie Craig, the former wife of Jackie, to discuss the circumstances surrounding Jackie's arrest for the death of his mother. Mollie reveals that her current husband, Joe, works in the power department and had warned her against Jackie, whom she nicknamed "Monkey Face." She describes Jackie as a charming manipulator who once used her money to bribe an auto repair shop owner's wife to drop charges against him.

As Calgary attempts to clear Jackie's name, he finds himself increasingly disgusted by the man's character. Mollie admits that while Jackie claimed he did not intend to kill his mother, he did hit her with a poker during an attempt to get money to avoid prison. She believes Jackie's aggressive nature and need for money led to the incident, though she expresses confusion when Calgary suggests Jackie might be innocent of the crime.

Mollie explains that Jackie kept their marriage secret to hide her from his upper-class family, claiming his mother would try to control her. She recounts a visit to Jackie's family home where a housekeeper refused to believe she was married to him. During this visit, a man named Mr. Aggles offered her a weekly allowance, which she continues to accept despite Joe's disapproval. Mollie notes that Joe provided a sizable check as a wedding gift for her current marriage, hoping it would be more successful than her previous one.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Joe Cragg, a thin-lipped blond man, argues with Calgary regarding the past case of Jack Aggell. Cragg believes that revisiting the old business is useless because Aggell is dead and his reputation was already tarnished by a history of embezzlement and swindling. Calgary maintains that Aggell had a perfect alibi, as he was in Calgary's car being driven to Dreemore when the crime occurred. After the encounter, Calgary feels agitated and angry that no one seems to care about the truth, noting that while Aggell's widow may not have loved him, others should care about his innocence.

Calgary later meets Christina Aggell, a petite, mixed-race woman raised by Mrs. Aggell, at a tea shop near a cathedral in Reddish Knolls. During their conversation, Calgary expresses his apologies for failing to prove Aggell's innocence during the original trial. Christina reveals she did not trust or like Jack and never suspected him of killing their mother. Calgary informs her that he has news that could clear Aggell of the murder charge, which may lead to a retrial. While Calgary apologizes for bringing new trouble, Christina invokes the Magna Carta, stating that no one shall be denied justice.

Finally, Calgary visits Dr. McMaster, a retired man with bushy eyebrows and shrewd grey eyes. Calgary feels a sense of camaraderie with the doctor. Calgary explains his motivation for continuing to pursue the matter, noting that despite his concussion and memory loss, he feels a logical need to address the situation rather than simply disappearing.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Calgary meets with Dr. McMaster to discuss the fallout of a message he delivered, noting that the reaction from the family lacked the expected anger or gratitude. Calgary expresses his desire to understand the family better, stating that while public facts describe the deceased, Mrs. Aggul, as a selfless and charitable woman, he perceives a disconnect regarding her adopted children, specifically a "problem child" or juvenile delinquent.

Dr. McMaster explains that Mrs. Aggul’s kindness was excessive. He observes that because she never experienced biological pregnancy or childbirth, her maternal instinct became an obsession. She consumed her life with the children, providing them with highly controlled environments, including sterilized milk, tested water, and calculated caloric intakes, while neglecting her husband. McMaster notes that the children were spoiled and lacked the benefits of simple neglect.

The doctor identifies Jake as a "loser" who was beyond redemption despite the Garr family's efforts. McMaster describes Jake as a young man without a conscience who exhibited aggressive, rage-driven behavior toward smaller children during his youth. While McMaster was surprised that Jake committed murder, he explains that Jake’s temperament—inciting others to violence during high-pressure situations like a robbery—aligns with the crime. Following this revelation, Calgary expresses a sense of confusion and realization regarding the true nature of the situation he is investigating.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Calgary discusses the murder of Mrs. Aggles with Dr. McMaster. While no obvious motives exist on the surface, Dr. McMaster suggests that Mrs. Aggles maintained a tight economic and psychological grip on her non-biological children, preventing them from making their own decisions. He details the lives of the potential suspects: Mike, a resentful car salesman who hated his biological and adoptive mothers; Hester, an unsuccessful actress who had to return home; and Mary Dalton, who married a man against her mother's wishes and faced pressure to live on the coast. Dr. McMaster also mentions a Swedish masseuse who had an aversion to Mrs. Aggles but could have left at any time, and Leo Aggles, who is planning to remarry a compatible woman.

Dr. McMaster believes the police are correct that a family member committed the crime, but he fears the truth will never be known due to a lack of evidence. He compares the situation to the historical Bravo case, noting that when a murderer escapes, the innocent are often left to suffer the consequences of suspicion.

Later, in London, Hester Agar is startled by Colston Lindstrom. Colston remarks on the "scary things" in the house and recounts a newspaper story about a woman who killed her companion under the delusion of exorcising a devil. He cryptically warns Hester that because people cannot know what occurs in a twisted mind, he might one day decide to kill her mother.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Corton Lindstrom warns Hester that she cannot trust anyone following a recent incident where a visitor clarified that Jacqueline is not the killer. Corton asserts that the killer is someone within their own circle and advises Hester to be wary of Mary, her father, and Gwendolyn Vance. Corton suggests Hester leave the house for her safety, specifically mentioning Dr. Craig, whom Hester defends. Hester expresses frustration and wishes Dr. Calgary had never visited.

Elsewhere, Leo Agher finishes signing letters for Gwendolyn Wain. Gwendolyn discusses Leo's upcoming trip to Rome and Siena to examine archives mentioned by Cardinal Machirini. She expresses a desire for Leo to leave, suggesting he would be better off away from the current situation. Gwendolyn implies that Dr. Calgary might be the killer, noting his terrible temperament. Leo expresses doubt, noting that while the doctor attacks younger children, he seemed afraid of Rachel. Gwendolyn privately contemplates that Rachel’s commanding and "always right" nature might have motivated someone to kill her with a poker.

Gwendolyn asks to move their planned March wedding to an earlier date, but Leo refuses, stating they should not rush and must be "sure." This leaves Gwendolyn upset and trembling as she leaves to post the letters. Kirsten Lindstrom enters with tea and questions Leo about upsetting Gwendolyn. After Kirsten leaves, Leo reflects on his first meeting with Rachel Constable at a social club in London's East End, remembering her earnestness and her unwavering belief in doing good.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Leo reflects on his marriage to Rachel Aigle, noting her tendency to view people as problems to be solved rather than individuals. After discovering they could not have biological children, the couple considered adoption. During a trip to New York, their car struck a blonde, blue-eyed child named Mary, who had run from a slum house. Rachel took the child to a hotel suite, provided her with new clothes, and eventually decided to adopt her. Despite Leo's unease regarding Mary’s lack of emotional connection to her past, they legally adopted her, and she became Mary Aigle.

Following the adoption, Rachel’s life became entirely centered on children. She dedicated herself to supporting orphans and children with disabilities. In 1939, at the outbreak of war, Rachel established a luxurious nursery in a remote area of England to house up to 18 children from impoverished or abusive backgrounds. She hired nurses and staff, including Kirsten Lindström, to provide specialized care. Leo cautioned Rachel that the children would eventually need to return to their original environments, but she dismissed his concerns.

During the war, Rachel faced challenges with the children's emotional well-being. When a boy named Micky expressed a desire to return to his abusive mother, Rachel struggled to accept that he could love her despite her providing a superior lifestyle. Leo observed that while Rachel provided immense care, she could not buy the children's genuine love. After the war, as children were reclaimed by relatives, Rachel proposed to Leo that they adopt several of the unclaimed children to form a permanent family.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Leo reflects on his past with Rachel, who enthusiastically pursued building an artificial family by adopting several children: Mary, Mickey, Tina, Hester, and Jackie. While Leo believed providing a home was worthwhile, he felt the children lacked the ancestral blood, ambition, and integrity of his own family. Jackie, in particular, developed into a manipulative juvenile delinquent. Despite efforts by Rachel and Leo to support him, Jackie’s academic failures and criminal tendencies led to him becoming an angry, threatening man who demanded money from them. Following Rachel's death, Leo found emotional warmth and love in his loyal secretary, Gwendolyn, though he could not marry her while Rachel was alive.

In a separate setting, Donald Craig visits Dr. McMaster to discuss his personal distress regarding his relationship with Hester Aigle. Donald expresses that he loves Hester and believes she loves him, but he is troubled by recent events involving "justice for Jackie Aigles." He suggests that the situation might have been better if certain new evidence had not surfaced. Dr. McMaster notes that many people, including the police chief and a man who recently returned from the South Pole, share this sentiment. Donald reveals that on the night Mr. Calgary visited the Aigle house, Hester called him in a distraught state, canceling their plans to attend a lecture in Dreyfus because she had heard unsettling news.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Donald Craig, a doctor, confides in Dr. McMaster regarding his girlfriend, Hester. Donald expresses concern that Hester may have killed her mother, Mrs. Aghel, with a poker after overhearing an argument between her mother and Jacqueline. Donald theorizes that Hester’s motive was not cold-blooded murder, but an impulsive act of emotional rebellion driven by a desire to escape her mother’s dominant, all-knowing, and suffocating personality. He recounts Hester's history of insecurity and her failed attempt to assert independence by joining a touring theater troupe, which ended when she became involved with a middle-aged man.

McMaster acknowledges that while the motive of seeking freedom from a dominant personality is unique, it is a possibility. He notes that Mrs. Aghel's death effectively liberated several people, including Leo, Mary, and Mickey, from her financial and personal control. Despite the psychological implications, Donald insists he needs to know the truth before marrying Hester, claiming he would still love her if she were guilty. McMaster warns that such a revelation might destroy the trust in their relationship.

In a separate discussion, Leo, Mary Darrant, and Marshall discuss the impact of a recent newspaper advertisement that is expected to reignite press interest in the case. Marshall advises a policy of "no comment" to the reporters. Leo mentions that Detective Chief Inspector Rusey intends to visit to discuss the matter. They reflect on the difficulty of reopening an investigation two years later, as local villagers may have forgotten key details. Mary Darrant notes that while the door was locked against thieves, someone could have been let in by her mother, noting her father heard the doorbell ring at seven o'clock.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a discussion regarding a recent incident, Leo Agar tells Mr. Marshall that he heard a doorbell and the sound of a door opening and closing, but heard no voices or signs of forced entry. Marshall suggests the incident might have been a robbery where an intruder used a convincing story to gain entry before knocking the owner unconscious.

As Marshall observes the gathered group, he evaluates several individuals. He notes Mary Darland’s outward confidence and her husband Philip Darland’s alertness and anxiety while sitting in a wheelchair. He observes Mickey’s cynicism and Tina’s elegant, quiet demeanor; despite Tina being a librarian living in Reddin, Marshall temporarily rules her and Mickey out as suspects. He considers Kirsten Lindström, a foreigner and employee of the victim, Jacqueline, but dismisses the idea that she would attack Jacqueline due to her attentive nature toward the children.

Marshall then contemplates the potential involvement of Leo Agar and Gwendolyn Vance. Although Leo and Gwendolyn are engaged—a fact known locally—Marshall finds it difficult to believe the reasonable Leo would attack his wife. However, he considers whether Gwendolyn Vance, who was in the house and in the study with Leo that day, might have used a poker to kill Jacqueline. He also considers Hester, a beautiful but reckless woman who had previously run away to become an actress, noting that her motives remain unknown. Marshall concludes by reflecting on whether the Aikman family knows the truth or if the situation will remain a stalemate, only to be interrupted by Mickey’s mocking gaze.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a group discussion, Mr. Marshall proposes that an unknown intruder committed the robbery and murder. Mickey challenges this stance, suggesting that the group is not being honest about their true beliefs. Mary agrees with Mr. Marshall, while Philip Darland warns Mickey that speaking too much in their current "tight spot" is dangerous. Kirsten Lindström also rebukes Mickey, telling him to mind his own business and stop acting like a reckless child. Following the tense exchange, the seven individuals retire to their rooms at Sunny Corner, though none sleep soundly.

Philip Darwent, an intelligent man who has become observant of human nature due to his physical illness, reflects on his marriage to Mary. He acknowledges that while he loves her and values her wealth, he feels a need to escape her intense devotion. He contemplates the death of his mother-in-law, Mrs. Aggles, noting that while he did not like her, her murder is a repulsive event. He suspects the perpetrator may be one of her "adopted" sons. 

As they prepare for the arrival of Detective Inspector Rush, Philip discusses the upcoming interrogation with Mary. He warns her that the detective will question her regarding her activities on November 9th. Philip advises Mary to be careful with her alibi, noting that she previously claimed to be in their room with him, but she had actually left between 7:00 and 7:30.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Philip and Mary (Polly) discuss their shared alibi regarding a period of time spent playing cards to avoid suspicion. Philip expresses a deep curiosity about the identity of the killer, noting that Micky believes the murderer is a member of their group. Mary remains indifferent and prefers to avoid the subject to maintain a sense of normalcy. Philip argues that the situation is deteriorating, citing the nervous behavior of Dr. Donald toward Hester and the potential motives surrounding Leo and Gwendolyn’s upcoming marriage. 

Mary suggests that Custon, an outsider who has suffered from headaches, might be the culprit. Philip observes that Custon is in a precarious position, as she is alone and unable to prove her innocence regarding the death of her friend and employer. Philip proposes investigating the truth by observing people's reactions to specific statements, but Mary urges him to stop setting traps and mind his own business.

Meanwhile, Michael Ainsley lies awake in bed, reflecting on his past life in the London slums. He reminisces about his mother, her drinking habits, and the excitement of the war and air raids. He compares his current life in the countryside to his former home, feeling like a prisoner eating bland food. He expresses resentment toward a woman who took him in and treats him with "drivel" and games, but he remains patient, hoping to eventually return to London.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mickey Agha reflects on his traumatic childhood following the death of his adoptive mother, Rachel Aigle. After the war, Mickey believed his biological mother had been killed by bombs, but Mrs. Airlie revealed she was alive. Mickey eventually discovered through overheard conversations that his biological mother had sold him for a hundred pounds. This realization fueled a lifelong resentment and a desire to kill the woman who had "bought" him. Although he later learned his biological mother died in a car accident, Mickey struggles with the loss of his hatred, feeling lost and afraid now that she is dead.

Kirsten Lindström sits in her bedroom, feeling anxious about the police and their potential prejudice against foreigners. She believes justice was served regarding Jacques, whom she describes as a charming but habitual liar and a cruel person. She doubts the validity of an alibi provided by a doctor from Calgary, believing Jacques is capable of manipulating time and place. Kirsten observes the dynamics of those around her, noting Mrs. Aggles's possessive maternal nature and reflecting on the potential motives of others: Mickey’s resentment, Hester’s instability, Leo and Gwendolyn’s opportunity, and the personalities of Tina and Mary.

Mary Dalton wakes from a dream of her childhood in New York. She recalls how she used affection to secure a life of luxury provided by her wealthy adoptive mother. Mary harbors contempt for the other children Rachel Aigle adopted, viewing them as undisciplined and from lower classes. She recalls her determination to marry Philip, a pilot, despite her mother's disapproval. After the war, Philip’s financial failures and a bout of polio left him paralyzed, forcing the couple to settle in Sunny Corner. Mary reveals her motive for wanting Philip entirely to herself: she did not want a child of her own and wished to escape her mother's shadow.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Philip suggests that Mary go to Sunny Corner to distract herself, noting that her father is easy to get along with. Mary feels a sense of futile anger, reflecting on her mother's influence and her own death. Meanwhile, Leo Aghart reflects on the situation involving Gwendolyn and Inspector Hueish. He recalls comforting Rachel after she reported Jacques's reckless behavior and threats. Gwendolyn leaves the house unnoticed after gathering letters. Leo notes that both he and Gwendolyn had the opportunity and motive to be involved in the case, as they were in love. Nearby, Gwendolyn lies awake, harboring hatred for Rachel Ackerley, while Rachel's voice seemingly warns her in the darkness that she will never have her husband. Hester experiences a dream where Donald Craig leaves her at an abyss, only to see Arthur Calgary reaching out to help her. Tina lies awake, reflecting on her love for the deceased Mrs. Aigle.

Inspector Hueish arrives to re-examine the case, admitting the police made a mistake in arresting Leo's son, Jacques, because they could not verify his story regarding hitchhiking. Hester expresses skepticism that the killer will ever be found, but Hueish insists on persistence. Leo Aghart provides a statement, recounting that after tea at five o'clock, he returned to the study to read while his wife, Mrs. Aigle, went to her office. They heard a doorbell but did not know who it was. Leo estimates that approximately 45 minutes later, Mrs. Aigle entered the study, reporting that Jacques had been with her, acting rude and demanding money to avoid jail, which she had refused to provide.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation, Hueish questions Mr. Aggles regarding his son Jacques's behavior. Mr. Aggles explains that he and his wife decided to stop bailing Jacques out of trouble, which led to a confrontation where Jacques left the house angrily, threatening to return for money. Mr. Aggles admits he did not fear an attack but was shocked to learn Jacques was a suspect. 

Gwendolyn Vaughn, a typist, confirms that Mrs. Aggles left the room around 6:53 PM. Gwendolyn left the house at 7:15 PM, noting that the sitting room door was ajar but she did not enter to say goodnight. On her walk home toward Red Lane, she recalls a car splashing mud on her at an intersection. Mr. Aggles mentions he may have heard a doorbell ring after his wife left the room, though he is uncertain. He also reveals he was unaware that Jacques had married, a fact that surprised him when Jacques's wife arrived the following day.

Hester Aggles is questioned about her movements on the day of the incident. She states she left the house around 7:00 PM to attend a play at the Dreymouth Theatre. She claims she went to her mother's room to ask for money for gasoline. While she initially suggests Mrs. Corsden gave her the money, she later clarifies that she took money from Coulston after Mrs. Aggles told her to do so. Hester notes that her last interaction with her mother involved saying goodnight while her mother sat at her desk. Corston Lindström adds that she saw Hester drive past her toward the main road as she was walking toward the village.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During an investigation into the death of Mrs. Aggles, Corston Lindström provides testimony regarding the events of the night. She recalls that after tea, Jacques arrived, claiming he had lost his key. He went to his mother's room, stating he was in trouble. Corston later witnessed Jacques leaving the house in an angry state, shouting a threat that his mother must have money ready for him "or else." Corston explains that she returned to the house after 7:30 PM following a flower arrangement contest and found Mrs. Aggles dead at her desk with a poker on the floor and desk drawers pulled out. Corston suggests a burglar attacked Mrs. Aggles, perhaps after being let in by her.

The Darntes, Mary and Philip, testify that they remained in their separate room playing cards until they heard Corston scream. Philip notes that his wife's composure seems suspiciously perfect. Other household members, Mike and Tiny Tina, provide alibis: Mike claims he was test-driving a car from Dulwich to Ipsley, while Tina, a library worker, states she went home to her flat in the Molkum Building and did not go out.

The investigation shifts to a suspicious five-pound note found on Jack Aggles, which bears a note on the back. Because Mrs. Aggles claimed she did not give Jack any money, the detectives question how he obtained it. Hueston questions Hester about whether she took money from her mother's desk to give to her brother. Hester becomes flustered and contradictory, initially claiming she did not know when Jacques left, before defensively asserting that she would not steal and that any money given to Jacques would have been her own.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Haversham, Huishe, and Gwendolyn Vane discuss evidence suggesting Mrs. Aigle gave money to Jack Aggell, supporting Jack's claim that his mother provided it. Following this, Colston Lindstrum attempts to calm a tense Hester Aigle, but she flees the room, followed by a loud bang at the front door. Philip Darwent suggests the truth may never be known, while Tina claims she knows nothing about the situation.

In a subsequent meeting, Inspector Rush (Huishe) reports to the Chief of Police and the Commissioner that while there are no definite results or evidence sufficient for court, the investigation has narrowed the suspects. Rush concludes that Mrs. Aigle was killed between 7:15 and 7:30 PM. He posits that the crime was a crime of passion rather than premeditated, identifying Leo Aigle and Gwendolyn Vaughn as the primary suspects with motives. Rush suggests Leo may have killed her with a poker to make it look like a robbery, or Gwendolyn may have acted on impulse to frame Jacqueline and secure her relationship with Leo.

Rush notes that while Leo seems like an unlikely killer, he might resent the love his wife shows his non-biological son. Huishe also expresses suspicion regarding Hester Aigle, noting her tension, and suggests she might be worried about a young person named Hirst. Additionally, Huishe mentions a black-haired girl from the county library who seems anxious and may know something, and a man named Mickey, who was driving a car near Mingchen Mountain at the time and whose whereabouts cannot be independently verified.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Huestis informs Colonel Finney that while the motive for a crime remains unknown, the witnesses likely know the truth but will remain silent once they realize they have said too much. Huestis plans to question Lindström to investigate potential animosity between her and Mrs. Aigle, as well as Philip Darlant, a paralyzed man whom Huestis believes may have observed something significant.

Meanwhile, Mickey takes Tina for a walk from her library to a pavilion by a riverbank to escape the cold. During their walk, Mickey expresses a sense of displacement and restlessness, noting he does not belong in his current environment and has been offered a job managing car transport for an oil company in the Persian Gulf. A tense conversation ensues regarding Mickey's biological mother. Tina expresses gratitude to Mrs. Aigle for providing her with a secure home and identity, whereas Mickey harbors resentment toward his upbringing and the luxury provided. Tina suggests that Mickey’s anger toward Mrs. Aigle is actually displaced hatred for his biological mother. She further posits that if Mickey killed Mrs. Aigle, he was actually attempting to kill the memory of his own mother. Mickey denies involvement in the murder, claiming he was giving a customer a test drive near Moor Road and Mingjing Mountain at the time. The conversation shifts when Tina points out two figures on the riverbank below, whom Mickey identifies as Hurst and her doctor friend.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
On a path leading toward a house, Mike and Tina engage in a tense confrontation. Mike asks Tina how she knows he was present on the night of her mother's murder. Tina avoids answering directly, instead expressing concern for Hurst and Don Craig. She notes that while it was previously believed Jacqueline killed the mother, it is now clear that she did not.

On a riverbank below a lookout, Don Craig attempts to discuss the morning's police questioning with Hester Hurst. Hester expresses a desire to forget the incident, but Don insists on facing reality. He asks Hester who last saw her mother alive, to which she replies it was herself around seven o'clock, just before she went to the theater. Don asks if she had a reason to kill her mother; Hester matter-of-factly admits she often had violent dreams about killing her. Don, acting with the intensity of a doctor interested in the case, pleads with Hester to tell him the truth, promising to protect her and help her if she is responsible. Hester mocks his persistence and flees up the path, leaving Don frustrated.

Elsewhere, Philip Darwent and Mary argue about whether to stay at the scene of the murder. Mary finds the location gloomy and oppressive, wanting to return home to her household duties. Philip expresses a petulant desire to stay, finding the situation interesting, though he later apologizes to Mary, lamenting how his life and ability to be his former self have changed.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Philip Darrington and his wife, Mary (referred to as Polly), engage in a tense argument regarding a murder that occurred in their house. Philip expresses a desire to treat the investigation as a "murder game" to distract himself and challenge his intellect. While Mary wishes to forget the event and return home, Philip rejects the idea that an outsider committed the crime, suggesting instead that the killer is someone within the house. He notes that while Old Marshall attempts to provide a polite explanation, no one believes it.

Philip discusses the potential motives and suspects. He mentions that for two years, Mary and others believed Jacqueline was the killer. He then turns his attention to the deteriorating relationship between Mary's father, Leo, and his partner, Gwendolyn Vance. Philip suggests their growing distance is due to either mutual suspicion or guilt. He posits that if Gwendolyn is innocent, she faces the torture of being shunned, whereas Leo faces the agony of suspecting the woman he loves. Philip also speculates that the police might suspect Leo of pushing his wife, though he does not personally believe it.

Finally, Philip questions whether Mary is avoiding the truth because she already knows the killer. He proposes a theory regarding Hester, suggesting that her motive could stem from long-term suppressed rebellion against her mother, who made Hester acutely aware of her own perceived weaknesses. Philip believes he could construct a justification for Hester's actions.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Philip and Mary discuss a murder investigation involving four people present in a house at the time of the crime. Mary eliminates the victim's father and Hester as suspects, leaving Cora and Gwen. Philip analyzes Cora, describing her as a plain woman who desires marriage and social status, suggesting she might have stayed too long in her role as a massage therapist. Philip also suspects Tina, a quiet and elegant woman, may know more about the situation than she admits, noting her strange comment about wishing she knew nothing. Philip expresses a desire to investigate Korsleigh, whom he views as simple-minded and honest. Mary, visibly upset, asks Philip to abandon his investigations and return home with her, but Philip decides to stay until the weekend to continue his thinking.

In a separate scene, Mickey tells his father, Leo Aghart, that he will be staying for a few more days and that Tina will be staying as well. Mickey expresses sudden gratitude toward Leo, reflecting on his past ungratefulness and his desire to pursue a career in vehicle maintenance rather than the oil industry his mother preferred. Leo discusses a discretionary trust fund established years ago, explaining that the money belongs to Mickey and is intended to support him as an adopted son. Mickey becomes visibly distressed and insists he does not want to touch the money, though he struggles to explain his reasoning. The conversation is interrupted by a knock at the door, and Mickey opens it to find Philip maneuvering his wheelchair into the room.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Leo and Philip discuss the family's children and the reopening of a police case. Mickey, who has recently become less hostile, leaves to find Tina. Philip questions Leo about the conscience of the children and the nature of their adoption. Leo explains that while Mary was a formal adoption, the others were brought into the family through less formal means: Jacques was an orphan left by his grandmother; Mickey was a bastard child whose mother was paid to leave him; Tina’s mother is unknown and impossible to find; and Hester is a bastard daughter whose mother, an Irish nurse, sent her to Leo and Rachel to hide her from her husband.

Leo reflects on Rachel’s desire to be a mother to all of them and expresses his belief that blood ties create an intuition that adoption cannot replicate. Philip suggests that Tina, who is of mixed race, may be withholding information that could harm someone. Leo admits he is afraid of what a full investigation might reveal, prompting Philip to wonder if Leo knows the identity of the perpetrator.

Later, Philip encounters Hester leaning out of a window. After a cynical exchange regarding suicide, Hester reveals that Mickey used to use the window as a secret escape route. Hester asks to speak with Philip, noting that Mary does not understand her. Philip invites her to his room, where he discusses his past as a pilot and his experience with disability. Hester expresses deep distress, stating that a recent situation is far worse than a simple fight and will never be resolved.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hester and Philip engage in a conversation where Hester expresses her feelings of inadequacy, her history of suicidal ideation since age fourteen, and her resentment toward her mother. Hester describes her failed attempt to live independently and her failed romantic pursuit of an older, married man who lied to her. She reveals that she discovered she was adopted at age sixteen, which contributed to her sense of insecurity. Hester also expresses frustration that her mother was always right, which made Hester feel useless.

The conversation turns to the death of Hester's mother. Hester reveals that a man named Donald Craig believes she killed her mother and has offered to marry her if she confesses. Hester vehemently denies the killing, though she notes that others, including Mary and Coulston, also suspect her. Philip suggests that Hester should not marry Donald Craig, whom he dismisses as a pompous fool. As Philip kisses Hester to cheer her up, his wife, Mary Darlton, enters the room. Mary ignores Philip's attempts to explain the kiss or reconcile, eventually leaving the room after delivering a tray of soup.

In a separate scene, a butler informs Calgary that a pretty young lady wishes to see him. Calgary is surprised but instructs the butler to bring her up. Upon her arrival, Calgary recognizes the visitor and welcomes her into his room.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hester visits Arthur Calgarth, appearing disheveled and desperate. She seeks his help, claiming that the peace of their lives was destroyed when he arrived and revealed a truth that shattered their sense of security. Calgary offers her a drink and listens as Hester describes her distress. She reveals that she has a romantic relationship with a doctor named Tang (Donald Craig) and expresses fear that he suspects her of a crime.

Hester describes a dream in which she was lost and unable to find Tang, separated from him by a bottomless pit, with Calgary on the other side reaching out to help her. Calgary assures her that he will not stand aside and intends to see the situation through. Hester explains that the group knows the perpetrator must be one of them, noting that Mr. Marshall realized a break-in was a pretense.

When Calgary asks why anyone would suspect her of killing her foster mother, Hester admits she has felt murderous impulses toward her because of the woman's condescending and "high and mighty" nature. She explains that she acted out of a desire to rebel and prove her own existence, as her foster mother's presence made her feel useless and ridiculous. Calgary empathizes with her feelings of helplessness, recalling a childhood memory of wanting to get rid of a mocking headmaster. Hester expresses regret, believing that if her foster mother had lived longer, they might have eventually become friends.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hester expresses regret to Dr. Calgary regarding her past childish behavior and her grief over her deceased mother. She declares her intention to grow up and asks for his help. Hester recounts a mysterious event that occurred, noting that the tension has ruined the potential marriage between her father and Gwendolyn. She shares various suspicions: Tina believes Micky is involved, while Colston previously suspected Hester and tried to protect her. Mary, Calgary's sister, suspected Colston.

Hester confesses to feeling fear and distrust toward everyone, including her father, Kirsten, Mary, and Gwendolyn, whom she perceives as having become cold and hateful. She wonders if the killer shares this sense of isolation. Calgary discusses the psychology of killers, suggesting they either rationalize their actions or struggle with guilt. Hester analyzes the motives of others, noting Micky’s hatred for his mother, Tina’s love for her, Colston’s loyalty to his mother, and her father’s recent emotional detachment and unapproachability. Calgary advises Hester to act like an adult and suggests she stay at the Curtis Hotel. He arranges for a taxi to take her there so she can freshen up before they meet for dinner at eight fifteen.

In a separate scene, Philip approaches Korslund Lindström to discuss the ongoing situation. Philip suggests that Leo Aghaill might not marry Gwendolyn because he either fears being a suspect or suspects Gwendolyn of murdering his first wife. Colston interrupts, telling Philip to stop asking questions and suggesting that Philip return home to his wife. Colston implies that Philip's wife is smarter than him and may be hiding truths that Philip is not prepared to discover.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Philip and Coulston engage in a tense discussion regarding the various theories surrounding a recent murder. Philip notes that different people hold conflicting suspicions: Leo suspects Gwendolyn, Gwendolyn suspects Leo, Tina knows something, Mickey knows the truth but is indifferent, and Mary suspects Hester. Philip accuses Coulston of knowing the truth. Coulston warns Philip that his pursuit of the truth is dangerous, comparing the risk to the life-threatening situations Philip faced as a former pilot, especially since Philip is now physically vulnerable in a wheelchair. Coulston reveals her intention to maintain an official story blaming Jacqueline to avoid further trouble, dismissing Philip's mention of Dr. Calgary's involvement by suggesting a concussion could have altered the facts.

After Coulston leaves, Philip feels his experiment in extracting information was successful. He begins taking notes on potential leads and identifies Tina as his next target. When his wife, Mary, enters and questions him, Philip jokingly claims he is writing to the prosecutor before telling her he is writing to Tina.

In a separate scene, Inspector Huester (also referred to as Detective Housh/Huishe) interviews a young man named Cyril Green regarding the murder of Mrs. Aigle that occurred two years prior. Cyril’s mother interjects, explaining that Cyril had previously claimed to see a Russian-made satellite fall, which she now realizes was actually a large red bubble car. Cyril recounts that on the evening of the murder, he went out to play with other children on a new path after finishing his tea. Constable Good observes the interaction, noting that the local children often engaged in minor mischief, such as stealing chrysanthemums.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After Detective Good suggests that a boy's comment about Russians might be significant, Detective Huishe decides to question Tina Aghill regarding her red bubble car. Chief Inspector Huishe confronts Tina, noting that while she previously claimed to have stayed home all evening, evidence places her car near Sunset Corner just before seven o'clock on the night of her mother's murder. Tina admits she drove to the location but claims she changed her mind and did not go inside the house. Despite Huishe's accusations that she is withholding information to protect a family member—possibly her brother Michael—Tina maintains she saw and heard nothing.

Later, Dr. Calgary meets with Huishe to gather information about the Aghill case, expressing a sense of responsibility for the trouble caused. Huishe describes Jacqueline Aghill as a small-time criminal with a history of embezzlement and scamming middle-aged or elderly women by feigning romantic interest. Huishe notes that these women rarely report him to avoid embarrassment. Calgary also inquires about the eldest daughter of the family, Mrs. Darlant, noting that he was unable to visit her because her house was locked and her family claimed she and her husband were out.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Huishe informs Dr. Arthur Calgary that Mr. Darlant, a disabled man with polio, is conducting his own detective work regarding a murder case at the Sunset Corner. Huishe notes that while Darlant is smart and intuitive, he will likely keep his findings to himself. Calgary expresses concern that the involved parties must be informed of the truth, but Huishe suggests they may already know.

Calgary later visits Molly Craig. Molly discusses rumors surrounding a murder case involving a man named Joe, who was pardoned after claiming innocence. She speculates that the other brother, Mickey, might be responsible, noting his unpredictable nature and his potential plan to escape to Persia. Molly also mentions rumors that a husband and secretary were involved, suggesting a husband might use poison.

Calgary then meets with a middle-aged woman to gather information for a book he intends to write regarding pathological personalities. He uses his experience with the Hayes-Bentley Expedition to Antarctica to build rapport with her. The woman discusses her relationship with Jacquie, whom she describes as charming but potentially motivated by money. She expresses a willingness to do anything for him, despite his death, and mentions plans they had to travel to France or Italy if his schemes succeeded.

In a separate scene, Philip Durrant wakes up feeling refreshed and anticipates a visit from Tina. His wife, Mary (Polly), informs him that Hester is coming home. This prompts a brief tension between the couple, as Philip dismisses Mary's implication that he has inappropriate feelings for Tina.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
