Philip and Mary (referred to as Polly) engage in a tense argument regarding their relationship and the ongoing murder investigation of Mrs. Aghel. Mary expresses a possessive desire for Philip, but he dismisses her, noting that they have run out of things to say. Philip reveals that he suspects Tina is lying about her whereabouts on the night of the murder. He shares gossip from a maid, Mrs. Nalcoat, stating that her son, Sir Cyril, went to the police because he saw something outside the house on the night Mrs. Aghel was killed. Philip theorizes that Tina may have witnessed or heard something and intends to use a method of questioning—asking yes or no questions—to uncover the truth.

The conversation turns to the emotional toll the investigation is taking on others, including Hester, Corston, Leo, and Gwendra. Philip becomes angry when Mary shows indifference toward these people. After Mary leaves the room, Philip reflects on his marriage, feeling a disconnect between the gentle girl he married and the cold, calculating woman Mary has become. He begins to wonder if her emotions are merely manipulations to achieve her goals.

Later, Philip is interrupted by Gwendolyn Wain, who brings him a newspaper on behalf of Leo. During their brief interaction, Philip makes a comment about "newlyweds," which causes Gwendolyn to tremble. He then pointedly asks her when she and Leo plan to marry.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Philip and Gwendolyn discuss the possibility of marriage, though Gwendolyn expresses bitterness and resentment toward Rachel Agar, noting that Leo might be forced to remain Rachel's husband.

In a separate scene, Tina visits the well-tended grave of Mrs. Aigle in a new cemetery. While she is placing carnations on the tombstone, Mickey arrives. Mickey reveals he is leaving for a job at an oil company in three weeks and wanted to say goodbye to his mother to apologize for his past lack of appreciation and his previous unkindness toward her. During their conversation, Mickey denies killing his mother, a suspicion he believes Tina holds.

Tina then reveals that she was at Sunset Point on the night of the death. She explains that while she was walking back to her car, she heard two people whispering. One person specified a time between seven and seven-thirty and told the other not to mess up, followed by a response of "You can trust me" and a final comment that "everything will be wonderful" afterward. Tina cannot identify the voices but suspects it may have been a man and a woman. She suggests the conspirators could be Leo and Gwendolyn, or perhaps Hurst and someone else, but she explicitly rules out Philip. Tina also mentions seeing a shadow walk quickly past her and hearing a car start, noting that the figure could have been Mickey due to his height and build. Mickey insists he is not the killer and urges Tina to get into his car so he can drive her to Sunny Corner.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Tina informs Micky that she received a letter from Philip, who requested a meeting to ask her a question that required only a yes or no answer, promising confidentiality. Upon arriving at the Sun Angle, Micky leaves Tina to talk to Philip while he walks in the garden to think. Inside, Tina encounters Mary, who is preparing coffee for Philip. Mary mentions that Hester is returning that day. Tina then encounters Hester on the landing, who reveals that Dr. Calgary drove her back but has since gone to Draymour.

As Tina continues toward the suite where the Dalantes live, she sees Kirsten Lindstrum approaching with a tray of coffee and cookies. Kirsten enters Philip's room and suddenly gasps, dropping the tray. Tina enters to find Philip in his wheelchair with his head slumped forward; a bright red, diamond-shaped stain is visible on his white collar. Corlston observes that Philip has been stabbed to death. Tina notices that while Philip had a pen in his hand, there is no paper in front of him, suggesting the killer took whatever he had written.

Tina and Corlston inform Leo and Gwendolyn of the murder. In the ensuing shock, Tina becomes dizzy and rushes outside to Micky, eventually fainting in his arms. Hester also collapses in shock. Micky carries Tina to Mrs. Agg's sitting room and calls for Dr. Craig. Dr. Craig arrives at the house, where Corlston confirms that Philip was murdered by being stabbed in the back of the head. Micky asks Tang to attend to the unconscious Tina.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At a house where Tina has been injured, a doctor instructs Corton to keep her warm and provide hot tea or coffee upon her waking. Mary Darland becomes agitated, shouting that she had just left Philip, who was fine and writing something, and questioning why he was so stubborn. Donald Craig enters and disputes the claim that Tina merely fainted; he declares that she was stabbed in the back and calls for an ambulance.

In a hotel room, Arthur Calgary reviews his notes, realizing he previously focused too much on Mrs. Aggles. He shifts his perspective to Jacqueline, considering various character assessments of her from Dr. McMaster, Leo Aigle, Hester, Kerstin Lindström, and Tina. He identifies a pattern involving money—specifically Mrs. Aigle’s trust fund, annuities, and bank accounts—as the "unknown factor" in the case. Calgary calls Hester, who informs him that Philip Darwent has been killed and Tina is in the hospital. Calgary promises to visit her after speaking with Chief Inspector Huishe.

Calgary meets with Inspector Huishe, who reports that Tina has woken up. Huishe explains that Philip Darwent was stabbed in the back while meddling in the murder case. He notes that the killer is among them and suggests that Tina is withholding information out of fear, possibly due to her relationship with Mike. Huishe reveals that a knife with blood on it was found in Philip's pocket. Huishe provides a timeline: at 4:00, Mary Darland was in the kitchen while Leo Aigle, Gwendolyn Vaughan, Hester Aggell, and Kerstin Lindström were in other parts of the house. At 4:05, Mickey and Tina arrived; Tina went upstairs and, along with Lindström, discovered Philip dead.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Detective Superintendent Huishe informs Dr. Arthur Calgary that Mickey has been taken into custody and will be charged within 24 hours. Huishe explains that Mickey has an alibi of being in the garden, but suggests he could have used a large magnolia tree to climb into Darnton's room to commit the murder. Huishe notes that Tina, who is recovering, provided incoherent statements: she named "Mickey," but also said, "The cup is empty" and "The pigeon is on the mast." Calgary remains unconvinced of Mickey's guilt and believes the crime is a "crime of passion."

Calgary returns to Sunset Point at dusk to resolve the situation he initiated. Upon arrival, he encounters Hester, Cressida Lindstrom, and a hostile Kirsten Lindstrom, who blames Calgary for the deaths of Philip Darwent and Little Tina. Calgary insists on speaking to everyone, eventually gathering Leo Agha, Gwendraith Vain, and Mary Darwent in the study. 

Calgary addresses the group, stating his motive is to stop the "disaster of the innocent" caused by his initial arrival. He observes that when he first arrived with news to clear Jack's name, the residents seemed satisfied with Jack's guilt because it provided a convenient explanation for the murder of Mrs. Aigle. He points out that while others justified Jack's actions through his mental state or misfortune, Kirsten Lindstrom was the only one who openly condemned him as "immoral."

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Arthur Calgary confronts Kirsten (also referred to as Corston or Caulston) Lindström regarding the murder of Mrs. Agle. While Calgary previously provided an alibi for Jacqueline (Jacques), he now posits that Jacqueline was the mastermind who manipulated Kirsten into committing the murder. Calgary explains that Jacqueline exploited Kirsten's affection for him to convince her to kill Mrs. Agle between seven and seven-thirty to facilitate a theft. Kirsten eventually breaks down, admitting her love for Jacques but claiming his actions were evil rather than her own.

Calgary further accuses Kirsten of murdering Philip Darrent and stabbing Tina. He reveals that Tina’s cryptic phrase, "the cup is empty," indicated that Kirsten had stabbed Philip while pretending to bring him coffee, leaving an empty cup on the floor. Kirsten admits to the killings, driven by fear and a desire to protect herself after realizing Jacques had deceived her. Following the revelation, Mary Darlant attacks Kirsten in a rage before leaving the room in grief.

As the group processes the truth, Leo Aghart defends Kirsten’s character, suggesting her actions stemmed from suffering, while Gwendolyn remains resentful of the torment Kirsten caused. Calgary suggests that Kirsten will likely flee to London but will eventually be caught. The passage concludes with a discussion about Tina’s recovery and her relationship with Mickey. In a moment of relief and shifting emotions, Hester expresses a sudden desire to marry Calgary.
